        The Holographic Kernel
A Verified 5D Transform Framework for Constrained Holographic State Spaces ((H^4)), Admissible Morphisms ((D^5)), and Reproducible Computation

Abstract
A Full Review of the Entire Corpus
This treatise defines a complete, implementation-faithful mathematical framework—the Holographic Kernel—for representing structured “holographic” states, transforming them via admissible programs, reconstructing them from partial observation, and verifying all claims through reproducible certificates. The system is organized as a 21-chapter manuscript with a fixed 4-lens decomposition for every major topic: Square (definitions and typing), Flower (composition and dynamics), Cloud (bounds, failure modes, impossibility), and Fractal (finite models, algorithms, and reproducible engineering). The framework is explicitly designed so that every theoretical claim corresponds to an executable object, every executable object has a verification contract, and every verification contract produces content-addressed artifacts suitable for external audit.
1. Ontology of Objects and the Kernel Contract
The corpus begins by fixing the ontology of “what exists” in the system. A state is not an untyped array; it is an element of an ambient measurable/functional space equipped with explicit regularity classes, norms, and operator domains. The treatise defines a hierarchy of function and distribution spaces (e.g., (L^p), Sobolev, BV, distributional extensions) together with deterministic naming and canonicalization rules. These preliminaries are not ancillary: they are the typing substrate that makes later reconstruction, stability, and verification statements meaningful.
A holographic state is then defined as a constrained element of an ambient space. A closed (or closable) constraint operator[C:\mathrm{Dom}(C)\subseteq \mathfrak{H}\to \mathfrak{C}]induces a closed feasible set[H^4 ;\coloneqq; \ker(C)\subseteq \mathfrak{H}.]The constraint operator is not restricted to a single “physics constraint”; it is a formal container for structural constraints (symmetries, conservation laws, bandlimits, compatibility relations), physical constraints (when mapped), and admissibility guards. This allows the treatise to unify diverse modeling requirements under a single feasibility doctrine.
A gauge group (\mathcal{G}) acts on (H^4) and identifies states that are pattern-equivalent. The quotient pattern space[\mathcal{PATT} ;\coloneqq; H^4/\mathcal{G}]is treated as the true semantic object when gauge equivalences exist. Canonicalization is formalized as an idempotent selection map (\mathrm{Can}:H^4\to H^4) that is constant on gauge orbits. This is foundational for reproducible identity: state IDs are defined by hashing canonical serialized representatives, not by fragile floating-point byte patterns.
To support computation, the corpus establishes a finite representation doctrine: every continuous object must admit a truncation family (H^{(N,P)}) (or similar), explicit basis/discretization parameters, and deterministic serialization rules. Discrete constraints (C_N) must approximate (C) with stated consistency and stability, so that “finite computation” is not an unmoored numerical approximation but a controlled morphism inside the same theory.
2. Reconstruction as Holographic Code: Restriction, Observation, and Recovery
The corpus treats observation as an operator (O) (often masked restriction or boundary extraction) applied to a hologram (H). Reconstruction is formalized via reconstruction operators (R_A) that attempt to recover global structure from partial observations (H|_A). Unlike informal “inpainting,” reconstruction is integrated into the ontology: existence, stability, and identifiability are part of the model. The treatise explicitly separates:
uniqueness modulo gauge (what can be recovered),
stability under noise and truncation (how fragile recovery is),
ambiguity classes and counterexample families (when recovery is impossible),
reconstruction envelopes (\varepsilon(A)) that quantify recoverable error given an observation regime.
A central unification is the coding view of holography: redundancy is treated as an intrinsic code structure induced by constraints and frames. Frame conditions, parity/check constraints, syndromes, and decoder families appear not as metaphors but as explicit operators. This yields self-healing dynamics: when corruption or drift occurs, correction is performed by admissible transforms designed to reduce syndromes while staying inside corridor budgets. Every decoder is required to emit feasibility and success certificates rather than merely outputting a plausible reconstruction.
3. Transformations as Admissible Morphisms and Executable Programs
The transformation layer introduces the 5D transform space (D^5): the class of admissible morphisms acting on (H^4). A transform is admissible only if it satisfies (at minimum):
constraint preservation (T(H^4)\subseteq H^4) (or preservation via certified repair),
corridor preservation (bounded invariant drift and bounded reconstruction degradation),
stability bounds (operator norms, conditioning constraints),
computability (finite executable representation),
certificateability (verification artifacts exist and are deterministic).
Transform normal forms are defined to support identity and rewriting: generator normal forms (continuous flows via exponentials), gate normal forms (discrete programs), and mixed normal forms (hybrid continuous/discrete). Costs (\mathcal{C}(T)) unify resource constraints (time/memory), distortion/risk, and verification expense into an optimization-ready functional. This enables a precise notion of “shortest program” as a proxy for geodesic or cost-minimizing transform synthesis, connecting geometry and compilation.
Primitive transforms ((T_1,T_2,T_3,T_4)) are defined as canonical operator classes—contraction/extension/rotation/manifestation—each with explicit parameterizations, generator families, and corridor constraints. These primitives serve as the kernel’s minimal “action alphabet”: higher transforms are compositions of these primitives, and admissibility is enforced stage-wise.
A key computational unification is the gate algebra and encoding layer. Transforms are representable as programs over a finite gate library. The corpus fixes a deterministic indexing discipline (including base-22 encoding and the 231 pairwise gate index set) and defines:
parsing rules,
a typed intermediate representation (IR),
rewrite rules with side conditions (commutator bounds, BCH truncation envelopes),
compiler architectures,
and program equivalence certificates (exact or approximate with certified envelopes).
This makes transform synthesis a formally governed process: a program is not acceptable unless its compilation and equivalence claims are accompanied by verifiable certificates.
4. Information, Multi-Scale Invariants, and Corridor Governance
The invariants chapter defines the information-theoretic and structural quantities that the kernel monitors and preserves. Coarse-graining operators (S_\epsilon) extract distributions (p_i(\epsilon)) and support entropy families (Shannon, Rényi/Tsallis) and mutual information across regions/scales. Structural invariants include spectral band energies, phase/winding measures, symmetry signatures, and optional topological invariants where stability is certifiable.
The invariant vector (\mathrm{Inv}(H)) is treated as a control primitive: corridor budgets (\delta) bound drift, drive early abort, and parameterize automatic repair. The corpus explicitly connects invariants to:
identifiability (what can be inferred from partial observation),
no-go results (what cannot be recovered or predicted),
and admissibility enforcement (what transforms are allowed).
Information flow and conservation equations are formalized to justify “invariant-aware” evolution and controlled dissipation. Importantly, the theory distinguishes:
exact invariants (must not drift beyond numeric tolerance),
corridor invariants (allowed bounded drift),
diagnostic invariants (useful but non-certifying),and requires each invariant’s estimator to be paired with bias/variance envelopes and deterministic test harnesses.
5. Cycles, Temporal Structure, Fractal Time, and Geometry
A central dynamical object is the quaternary engine (Q): a 4-stage closed operator cycle with closure condition (Q^4 \approx I + \varepsilon\Delta). The corpus defines equivalent realizations (differential, algebraic, discrete gate programs) and provides stability and drift control laws for iteration. Resonance, fixed points, and attractors are treated as first-class: the engine is not merely a pipeline; it is a dynamical system whose behavior must be certified.
Temporal holography extends the state model by treating time as:
a flow parameter (t),
a coordinate in the parameter manifold,
and an operator domain for dilation/rotation/reflection/coupling operators.Temporal admissibility corridors define resolution limits, uncertainty trade-offs, and well-posedness regimes. Algorithms include discrete time representations, streaming time storage, and verification suites for temporal invariants.
Fractal time and time-crystal lattices formalize self-similarity, fractal dimension estimation, period nets, resonance signatures, and synchronization operators. The corpus states detectability bounds and no-go theorems for prediction in chaotic or information-bounded regimes, and it provides algorithm suites for extraction, estimation, and confidence scoring.
Geometric structure is then layered over both state and transform spaces: metrics (energy, information-weighted, gauge quotient), connections (covariant derivatives, parallel transport), curvature/holonomy (as noncommutativity signatures), and geodesic synthesis (transform optimization as shortest path). Computational differential geometry is specified with discretizations, solvers, and regression suites, so that curvature and holonomy are not philosophical descriptors but measurable, testable quantities used for program optimization and stability diagnostics.
6. Operator Families, Recursion, Observability, and Self-Healing
The corpus explicitly defines creation, modification, and measurement operators with careful attention to domains, boundedness vs unboundedness, self-adjointness criteria, and spectral decompositions. Measurement disturbance and reconstruction from measurements are formalized, including minimal measurement sets and ambiguity certificates.
Recursion is elevated to a core computational concept: recursion operators (R^n), metadata layers (L_0,L_1,\dots,L_\infty), depth-limited extractors, and observability budgets are defined as formal objects. Nested programs and meta-operators (operators acting on operators, costs, and corridors) provide a rigorous machinery for building systems that can be monitored and controlled under depth limits. The corpus provides explicit convergence diagnostics, divergence classification, and certified impossibility regimes for observability.
Error correction and redundancy are formalized as holographic codes:
redundancy via frames and dual frames,
parity/check operators,
syndrome definitions and canonical syndrome reports,
explicit error models (erasures, additive noise, phase noise, adversarial perturbations),
decoding objectives (minimum error, ML, regularized),
iterative decoders and hybrid healing loops,
stability and drift controls under repeated healing.
Healing is integrated into (D^5): a healing procedure is an admissible transform program with cost models, normal forms, and certificates. The corpus requires explicit guard rails against overfitting to noise and against drift induced by correction loops.
7. Discretization, Data Structures, and Algorithm Catalog
Discretization is treated as a formal bridge, not an implementation detail. The corpus defines:
basis and truncation families (Fourier, wavelet, hybrid),
sampling theorems and certified sampling thresholds,
discrete constraints (C_N) with consistency (C_N\to C),
discrete invariants converging to continuous invariants,
numerical stability and round-off propagation,
corridor translation rules (continuous budgets → discrete budgets),
and counterexample templates where naive discretization fails.
Data structures are specified as first-class objects: dense, sparse, hierarchical, and streaming models with deterministic indexing, chunking, halo exchange, online monitoring, and checkpointing. Each structure is linked to:
efficient kernel implementations (apply/compose/invert),
online invariant monitoring and abort/repair triggers,
failure modes and certified error bounds,
and reproducible engineering blueprints.
Core algorithms unify the operational stack: forward evaluation, inversion, optimization, learning, program search, and certification integration. The corpus provides a coherent API surface, solver families (direct, iterative, multigrid), program search methods (shortest path, beam, A*, proof-guided pruning), and learning methods (supervised, self-supervised, RL for programs) with explicit generalization and failure regimes where possible.
8. Verification, Reproducible Builds, Prediction, Physics Mapping, Higher-Dimensional Lifts, Limits
Verification is the system’s binding constraint: the treatise specifies what must be proven vs tested, certificate formats (human-readable proofs, machine-checkable logs, hashable artifacts), determinism rules (RNG, floating point, scheduling, serialization), and evidence hierarchies (proof/verified numerics/statistics/empirics). Verification pipelines are defined for constraints, reconstruction, invariants, and transforms. Numerical trust boundaries and adversarial robustness are treated as explicit domains with warning triggers and certified uncertainty labeling. Tooling and build systems are specified so that releases are reproducible, content-addressed, and auditable.
Predictive modeling on (H^4) is then defined as forecasting/simulation/inference with calibration and uncertainty, closed-loop prediction, identifiability limits, impossibility regimes (chaos and information bounds), and corridor-aware reporting. Predictive tooling is integrated with verifiers: models produce not only predictions but also certificate-aware outputs and abstention when claims cannot be certified.
Mapping to physics is treated as a constrained embedding problem: (H) maps to field configurations, constraints map to physical constraints, invariants map to conserved quantities, gauge maps to physical gauge, and couplings between state and geometry/matter/observers must satisfy admissibility conditions (covariance, locality, well-posedness, stability, measurable outputs). The corpus defines falsifiability requirements, avoidance of non-falsifiable traps, and experimental protocol templates (synthetic and data-driven) with reproducible reporting bundles.
Higher-dimensional extensions generalize the kernel to ((H^k,D^{k+1})) families with category-theoretic framing: objects as constrained spaces, morphisms as admissible transforms, functors between representations, natural transformations as rewrite rules, and projective/inductive limits for refinement/expansion. The corpus explicitly addresses complexity growth, stability scaling laws, expressivity bounds, and verification scaling, including certified impossibility outputs when budgets or decidability fail.
Finally, ultimate limits and open problems formalize what the kernel proves and what it cannot claim: not every transform is admissible, not every target is reachable, not every state is identifiable, and not every embedding is falsifiable. The treatise defines canonical open conjectures (minimal constraint families, minimal invariant suites, minimal gate bases, minimal cost forms), a glossary of ultimate objects (fixed points, attractors, absolute bounds, certified impossibilities), and a research agenda with deliverables and publication packages.
9. Appendices as Proof-Carrying Infrastructure
The appendices are not supplemental; they are the proof-carrying infrastructure:
Notation and index conventions (deterministic typing substrate),
theorem templates and proof schemas (reusable proof scaffolds),
algorithm catalog with pseudocode and complexity,
verification and testing handbook (unit/property/benchmark/certificate standards),
data structures reference (dense/sparse/hierarchical/streaming),
reference datasets and gold states (deterministic generators and regression anchors),
encoding systems and alternative bases (compiler and equivalence certificates),
open problems and roadmap (research-grade conjectures and discriminators),
synthesis and metro navigation atlas (this corpus’ deterministic extraction router).
The corpus is therefore a complete system: formal objects, algorithms, verification, datasets, and reproducible release tooling form a single closed loop with explicit boundaries and certified uncertainty where claims cannot be strengthened.

Chapter 1 — Dimensional Ontology & Formal Preliminaries
1.1 Square — Definitions, Sets, and Canonical Language
1.1.1 Dimensional primitives (0D–5D)
1.1.1.1 Objects: point/line/plane/volume/hologram/transform
(A) 0D — Point object.A point object is a singleton measurable space[\mathbf{P}:=({\ast},{\emptyset,{\ast}}),]equipped with the counting measure (\nu({\ast})=1). A point carries no intrinsic geometry; any geometric meaning is induced by embedding (\iota:\mathbf{P}\hookrightarrow X) into an ambient space (X). The only endomorphism of (\mathbf{P}) is the identity.
(B) 1D — Line object.A line object is a 1-dimensional connected (C^k) manifold (typically (k\ge 1)) with a chosen atlas (\mathcal{A}) and a Radon measure (\lambda) absolutely continuous with respect to the 1D Hausdorff measure (\mathcal{H}^1). Canonical models include ((\mathbb{R},\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R}),dx)) and (([0,1],\mathcal{B}([0,1]),dx)). A line object supports arc-length parameterizations and 1D differential operators.
(C) 2D — Plane object.A plane object is a 2-dimensional (C^k) manifold (with or without boundary) with a Radon measure absolutely continuous with respect to (\mathcal{H}^2). Canonical models include ((\mathbb{R}^2,\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R}^2),d^2x)) and bounded Lipschitz domains in (\mathbb{R}^2). A plane object supports surface gradients, curls (in intrinsic form), and trace/normal notions when embedded.
(D) 3D — Volume object.A volume object is a measurable subset (\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^3) with:
a (\sigma)-algebra (\Sigma_\Omega) containing the Borel sets (typically the Lebesgue completion of (\mathcal{B}(\Omega))),
a reference measure (m_\Omega) (typically Lebesgue (d^3x) restricted to (\Omega)), and
when boundary is present, (\Omega) is assumed Lipschitz unless otherwise stated to guarantee trace operators and integration by parts.A volume object supports spatial differential operators, Sobolev spaces, and boundary traces.
(E) 4D — Hologram object.Fix a volume object (\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^3) and an auxiliary measurable space ((\mathcal{P},\Sigma_{\mathcal{P}},\mu)) called the parameter space. A hologram object is a measurable field[H:\Omega\times\mathcal{P}\to\mathbb{C}]belonging to a specified function class (default (L^2(\Omega\times\mathcal{P})); refined later to Sobolev/distribution classes). The hologram is a 4D object in the sense of a fibered state: for each (p\in\mathcal{P}), the slice (H(\cdot,p)) is a 3D field; the full object encodes cross-slice coupling by its dependence on (p).
(F) 5D — Transform object.A transform object is a typed map between state spaces. The primary transform types used in this manuscript are:
state transforms (T:H^4\to H^4) (admissible morphisms),
observation transforms (\mathcal{O}:H^4\to Y) into data spaces (Y),
reconstruction transforms (R:Y\to H^4), and
constraint operators (C:H^4\to Z) whose kernel defines feasibility.Transforms carry explicit domain/codomain typing, and—when computable—explicit discretization/precision regimes.
1.1.1.2 Domains/codomains and typing rules
(A) Typed universes.All objects are assigned to one of the following typed universes:
measurable spaces ((X,\Sigma_X)),
measure spaces ((X,\Sigma_X,\mu)),
normed linear spaces ((V,|\cdot|)),
Hilbert spaces ((\mathcal{H},\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle)),
operator classes (\mathcal{L}(V,W)) (bounded linear), and (\mathcal{U}(\mathcal{H})) (unitary), with extensions to unbounded operators on dense domains.
(B) Function typing.A map (f) is well-typed only when its domain and codomain are explicit:[f: A\to B,\qquad A\in\mathsf{Dom},\ B\in\mathsf{Cod}.]Equality of functions is extensional and respects almost-everywhere equivalence when the codomain is a function space over a measure space: (f=g) means (f(x)=g(x)) for (\mu)-a.e. (x).
(C) Operator typing (bounded/unbounded).A bounded linear operator is typed as[T\in\mathcal{L}(V,W)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad T:V\to W,\ |T|<\infty.]An unbounded operator is typed as a pair ((T,\mathcal{D}(T))) where (\mathcal{D}(T)\subseteq \mathcal{H}) is dense and (T:\mathcal{D}(T)\to\mathcal{H}). Composition (T\circ S) is defined only when (S(\mathcal{D}(S))\subseteq\mathcal{D}(T)).
(D) State-space typing for holograms.Let[\mathfrak{H}:=L^2(\Omega\times\mathcal{P},,d^3x,d\mu(p)).]The feasible hologram space (H^4) is typed as a subspace (H^4\subseteq\mathfrak{H}) (eventually defined as a kernel (\ker(C))). Primary transforms satisfy[T: H^4\to H^4,\qquad \mathcal{O}:H^4\to Y,\qquad R:Y\to H^4,\qquad C:H^4\to Z.]Any statement about (T) is invalid unless the typing, domain restrictions, and measurability/regularity requirements are specified.
1.1.1.3 Embedding and projection maps
(A) Embeddings.An embedding (\iota:M\hookrightarrow N) is an injective immersion that is a homeomorphism onto its image. In the measurable setting, (\iota:(X,\Sigma_X)\to(Y,\Sigma_Y)) is a measurable injection with measurable image. In Euclidean settings, embeddings are assumed (C^k) unless otherwise stated.
(B) Projections and fiber maps.Given a product space (X\times Y), canonical projections are[\pi_X(x,y)=x,\qquad \pi_Y(x,y)=y.]For holograms, the fundamental projections are:[\pi_\Omega:\Omega\times\mathcal{P}\to\Omega,\qquad \pi_{\mathcal{P}}:\Omega\times\mathcal{P}\to\mathcal{P},]and the induced slice maps:[\mathrm{Slice}_p(H):=H(\cdot,p),\qquad \mathrm{Slice}_x(H):=H(x,\cdot).]
(C) Pullback and pushforward of functions.For a measurable map (f:X\to Y), the pullback on functions is[f^\ast\phi := \phi\circ f.]For embeddings, (f^\ast) restricts functions to the embedded subspace. For projections, (f^\ast) lifts functions to constants along fibers.
(D) Compatibility with constraints and equivalence classes.If feasibility is defined by a constraint operator (C), embeddings and projections are constraint-compatible only when they commute with feasibility up to a specified tolerance:[C\circ \iota_\ast = \widetilde{\iota}\ast\circ C,\qquad C\circ \pi^\ast = \widetilde{\pi}^\ast\circ C,]where (\iota\ast,\pi^\ast) denote the appropriate action on states and (\widetilde{\iota}_\ast,\widetilde{\pi}^\ast) are induced maps on the constraint codomain. Constraint-compatibility is part of typing: if it fails, the map is inadmissible for state evolution.
1.1.1.4 Dimension-lifting and reduction operators
(A) Lifting operators.A lift is an operator that increases the representational dimension while preserving a specified invariant structure. Canonical lift types include:
fiber lift: (L_{\Omega\to\Omega\times\mathcal{P}}f(x,p)=f(x)) (constant along (\mathcal{P}));
augmentation lift: (L f(x,p)=f(x)\psi(p)) for a fixed (\psi\in L^2(\mathcal{P}));
encoding lift: (L) injects 3D structures into hologram space via a chosen coding operator (\mathcal{E}).
(B) Reduction operators.A reduction lowers dimension, typically via marginalization or slicing:
marginalization: ((\Pi_\Omega H)(x)=\int_{\mathcal{P}}H(x,p),d\mu(p)) when integrable;
energy marginal: ((\Pi_\Omega^{(2)}H)(x)=\left(\int_{\mathcal{P}}|H(x,p)|^2,d\mu(p)\right)^{1/2});
slice selection: ((\mathrm{Slice}_p H)(x)=H(x,p)) for admissible (p).
(C) Lift–reduce coherence axioms.Lift and reduction operators are constrained by coherence conditions:
idempotence on image: (\Pi\circ L = \mathrm{Id}) on the subspace of lifted objects where defined;
boundedness: (L) and (\Pi) are bounded operators between specified spaces, or their domains are explicitly stated;
compatibility with equivalence: if (H\sim H') under gauge/equivalence, then (\Pi(H)\sim \Pi(H')).
(D) Dimension-lifting as structured functor.Dimension transitions are formalized as functors between categories of structured objects (spaces with measures and constraints). A lift functor must preserve identities and composition and must map feasibility-respecting morphisms to feasibility-respecting morphisms. Reduction functors are right adjoints in settings where averaging/marginalization is the canonical universal operation.
1.1.2 Ambient measure spaces
1.1.2.1 Sigma-algebras and measurable structure
(A) Measurable spaces.A measurable space is a pair ((X,\Sigma_X)) where (\Sigma_X) is a (\sigma)-algebra on (X). The manuscript defaults to Borel (\sigma)-algebras on topological spaces and their completions under chosen measures.
(B) Completion and almost-everywhere equivalence.Given ((X,\Sigma_X,\mu)), the completion (\overline{\Sigma}_X) is obtained by adjoining all subsets of (\mu)-null sets. Functions are identified up to (\mu)-a.e. equality when placed inside (L^p) spaces.
(C) Product (\sigma)-algebras.For measurable spaces ((X,\Sigma_X)) and ((Y,\Sigma_Y)), the product (\sigma)-algebra (\Sigma_{X\times Y}) is generated by measurable rectangles (A\times B) with (A\in\Sigma_X), (B\in\Sigma_Y). Measurability of holograms (H:\Omega\times\mathcal{P}\to\mathbb{C}) is always with respect to (\Sigma_{\Omega\times\mathcal{P}}).
(D) Measurable maps and kernels.A map (f:(X,\Sigma_X)\to(Y,\Sigma_Y)) is measurable if (f^{-1}(B)\in\Sigma_X) for all (B\in\Sigma_Y). Conditional structures on (\Omega\times\mathcal{P}) are expressed via Markov kernels (K(x,\cdot)) when needed; their measurability is defined by the standard kernel conditions.
1.1.2.2 Measures ((d^3x)), ((d\mu(p))), product measures
(A) Spatial measure.On (\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^3), the reference measure is Lebesgue measure (m_\Omega), written (d^3x), restricted to (\Omega). When (\Omega) is bounded with boundary, (d^3x) denotes the interior volume measure; boundary measures are defined separately.
(B) Parameter measure.The parameter space ((\mathcal{P},\Sigma_{\mathcal{P}})) is endowed with a (\sigma)-finite measure (\mu), written (d\mu(p)). Typical choices include:
counting measure for discrete (\mathcal{P}),
Lebesgue measure for (\mathcal{P}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^k),
Haar measure for compact groups,
probability measures when (\mathcal{P}) is a random latent variable domain.
(C) Product measures and Fubini/Tonelli conditions.The product space (\Omega\times\mathcal{P}) carries the product measure (m_\Omega\otimes \mu), written (d^3x,d\mu(p)). All interchange of integrals follows Tonelli (nonnegative integrands) or Fubini (absolute integrability). Any use of (\int_{\Omega\times\mathcal{P}}) is invalid unless these conditions are met.
(D) Radon–Nikodym and densities.When (\nu\ll m_\Omega\otimes\mu), there exists a density (\rho\in L^1) such that (d\nu=\rho,d^3x,d\mu(p)). Density-based formulations are used for information functionals and for likelihood-style inference when (\mathcal{P}) is probabilistic.
1.1.2.3 Integrability classes and norms
(A) (L^p) spaces.For (1\le p<\infty),[L^p(\Omega\times\mathcal{P}) := \left{f\ \text{measurable}:\ |f|{L^p}^p=\int{\Omega\times\mathcal{P}}|f|^p,d^3x,d\mu(p)<\infty\right}.]For (p=\infty), (|f|_{L^\infty}=\mathrm{ess,sup}|f|).
(B) Hilbert structure at (p=2).[\mathfrak{H}=L^2(\Omega\times\mathcal{P})]is a Hilbert space with inner product[\langle f,g\rangle := \int_{\Omega\times\mathcal{P}} f(x,p)\overline{g(x,p)},d^3x,d\mu(p).]The induced norm is (|f|_2=\sqrt{\langle f,f\rangle}).
(C) Inequalities and operator bounds.Hölder and Minkowski inequalities govern integrability and norm bounds. For bounded linear (T\in\mathcal{L}(\mathfrak{H},\mathfrak{H})),[|T f|_2\le |T|,|f|_2.]All stability bounds later are expressed in terms of operator norms and condition numbers derived from this base.
(D) Mixed norms and anisotropic control.When separate control over (\Omega) and (\mathcal{P}) is required, mixed norms are used:[|f|{L^p\Omega L^q_{\mathcal{P}}} := \left(\int_\Omega \left(\int_{\mathcal{P}} |f(x,p)|^q,d\mu(p)\right)^{p/q} d^3x\right)^{1/p},]with the usual modifications for (p=\infty) or (q=\infty). Mixed norms encode anisotropic regularity and stability constraints.
1.1.2.4 Boundary structure ((\partial\Omega)) and traces
(A) Boundary regularity assumptions.Unless specified otherwise, (\Omega) is assumed to be a bounded Lipschitz domain when boundary traces are invoked. The boundary (\partial\Omega) carries the surface measure (dS), derived from the 2D Hausdorff measure restricted to (\partial\Omega).
(B) Trace operators.For Sobolev spaces on Lipschitz domains, the trace operator[\mathrm{Tr}:H^1(\Omega)\to H^{1/2}(\partial\Omega)]is bounded and surjective onto its range. Traces are used to define boundary conditions and to encode holographic boundary information in reconstruction constraints.
(C) Integration by parts and normal flux.For (u\in H^1(\Omega)) and vector fields (F) with suitable regularity,[\int_\Omega \nabla u\cdot F,d^3x = -\int_\Omega u,\nabla\cdot F,d^3x + \int_{\partial\Omega} (\mathrm{Tr},u),(F\cdot n),dS,]where (n) is the outward unit normal defined almost everywhere on (\partial\Omega). Boundary flux terms provide the formal backbone for conservation and reconstruction constraints.
(D) Boundary operators in hologram space.For holograms (H(x,p)), boundary traces are taken fiberwise:[\mathrm{Tr}\Omega(H)(\xi,p) := \mathrm{Tr}\big(H(\cdot,p)\big)(\xi),\qquad \xi\in\partial\Omega.]All boundary constraints must specify whether they act on (\mathrm{Tr}\Omega(H)), on normal derivatives, or on derived quantities (currents, fluxes, invariants).
1.1.3 Regularity and function classes
1.1.3.1 (L^p), Sobolev (H^s), BV, distributions
(A) Sobolev spaces.For integer (s\ge 0),[H^s(\Omega\times\mathcal{P}) := {f\in L^2:\ \partial^\alpha f\in L^2\ \text{for all multi-indices }|\alpha|\le s}]with norm (|f|{H^s}^2=\sum{|\alpha|\le s}|\partial^\alpha f|_2^2). Fractional (s) are defined by Fourier or interpolation methods on appropriate domains.
(B) BV functions.A function (u\in L^1(\Omega)) is of bounded variation, (u\in BV(\Omega)), if its distributional derivative is a finite Radon measure. BV is used to model sharp interfaces and piecewise-regular structures that occur in reconstruction and discretization.
(C) Distributions.The space of distributions (\mathcal{D}'(\Omega\times\mathcal{P})) is the dual of the test function space (\mathcal{D}=C_c^\infty). Weak derivatives and weak constraints are formulated distributionally when classical derivatives do not exist.
(D) Default regularity ladder for the manuscript.The manuscript uses a regularity ladder:[C^\infty \subset H^s \subset L^2 \subset \mathcal{D}',]and any theorem or algorithm must declare the minimal rung required for its validity. When discrete implementations are defined, the discrete analogues must be consistent with this ladder under refinement.
1.1.3.2 Smooth vs weak solutions
(A) Strong (classical) solutions.A function (u) is a strong solution of a differential equation if it possesses the derivatives required by the equation almost everywhere (or pointwise) and satisfies the equation in the classical sense.
(B) Weak solutions.A function (u) is a weak solution if it satisfies the equation after integration against test functions. For an operator equation (Lu=f), weak solutions satisfy[\langle Lu,\varphi\rangle = \langle f,\varphi\rangle]for all (\varphi) in a specified test space, where the pairing is distributional or in a duality framework.
(C) Mild solutions (semigroup form).For abstract evolution equations (\dot{u}=Au+F(u)) on a Banach/Hilbert space, mild solutions satisfy the integral equation[u(t)=S(t)u_0+\int_0^t S(t-s)F(u(s)),ds]where (S(t)) is a (C_0)-semigroup generated by (A). Mild solutions are the default for evolution in infinite-dimensional state spaces.
(D) Solution concept selection rule.A result is admissible only if the selected solution concept:
is well-posed in the stated space (existence/uniqueness/stability), and
is compatible with discretization (convergence of discrete approximations to the selected solution concept).
1.1.3.3 Compactness and embedding theorems
(A) Weak compactness.In reflexive Banach spaces, bounded sequences admit weakly convergent subsequences. In Hilbert spaces, bounded sequences admit weakly convergent subsequences by Banach–Alaoglu.
(B) Rellich–Kondrachov type compactness.For suitable bounded domains (\Omega) and (s>t), the embedding (H^s(\Omega)\hookrightarrow H^t(\Omega)) is compact. These compact embeddings underpin existence proofs for constrained minimization and convergence under refinement.
(C) Sobolev embeddings.Sobolev spaces embed into (L^q) or Hölder spaces under classical dimension-dependent conditions. Every use of pointwise evaluation, multiplication, or nonlinear composition must declare sufficient regularity to make the operation well-defined and continuous.
(D) BV compactness.Sequences bounded in (BV(\Omega)) are compact in (L^1(\Omega)) up to subsequences. BV compactness is used for interface-preserving reconstructions and for proving convergence under total-variation regularization.
1.1.3.4 Operator domains and essential self-adjointness
(A) Operator domain specification.An unbounded operator (A) on a Hilbert space (\mathcal{H}) is defined only together with a dense domain (\mathcal{D}(A)\subset\mathcal{H}). All claims about (A) must specify (\mathcal{D}(A)) and whether (A) is closed or closable.
(B) Symmetry and self-adjointness.An operator (A) is symmetric if (\langle Ax,y\rangle=\langle x,Ay\rangle) for all (x,y\in\mathcal{D}(A)). It is self-adjoint if it equals its adjoint (A^\ast) with matching domains (\mathcal{D}(A)=\mathcal{D}(A^\ast)). Self-adjointness provides spectral calculus and unitary evolution via exponentiation.
(C) Essential self-adjointness.A symmetric operator (A) is essentially self-adjoint if its closure (\overline{A}) is self-adjoint. Essential self-adjointness ensures uniqueness of the self-adjoint extension and removes ambiguity in defining (\exp(itA)).
(D) Relevance to admissible transforms.When transforms are generated by exponentials of operators ((T=\exp(tA))), the manuscript requires either:
(A) bounded, or
(A) self-adjoint (or essentially self-adjoint with specified closure), or
(A) generates a (C_0)-semigroup with explicitly stated Hille–Yosida conditions.This requirement is part of transform typing and admissibility.
1.1.4 Canonicalization and naming
1.1.4.1 Symbol table and index conventions
(A) Core symbols.(\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^3) denotes spatial domain; (\mathcal{P}) denotes parameter space; (x\in\Omega), (p\in\mathcal{P}). Holograms are (H:\Omega\times\mathcal{P}\to\mathbb{C}). State spaces are denoted by calligraphic or fraktur symbols (e.g., (\mathfrak{H}), (H^4), (D^5)).
(B) Indexing.Indices follow these rules:
Latin indices (i,j,k) denote discrete components (basis indices, gate indices).
Greek indices (\alpha,\beta) denote multi-indices or tensor components when required.
Parenthesized superscripts ((N,P,\epsilon)) denote discretization/truncation scale parameters.
(C) Equality conventions.The manuscript distinguishes:
strict equality (=) (pointwise equality on declared domains),
almost-everywhere equality (=_{\text{a.e.}}),
equivalence under a declared relation (\sim), and
equality within tolerance (\approx_{\delta}) meaning (|a-b|\le\delta) in the declared norm.
(D) Typographic conventions.Operators are uppercase Roman letters (e.g., (T,C,R,\mathcal{O})); sets are uppercase Roman (e.g., (X,Y)); spaces are calligraphic/fraktur; measures are Greek (e.g., (\mu,\nu)); invariants are (\mathrm{Inv}(\cdot)). Any deviation must be explicitly declared.
1.1.4.2 Canonical forms (normalization/phase fixing)
(A) Gauge actions and canonicalization goal.Let (\mathcal{G}) be a group acting on the state space by (H\mapsto g\cdot H). Canonicalization is a deterministic map (\mathrm{Can}) satisfying:[\mathrm{Can}(g\cdot H)=\mathrm{Can}(H),\qquad \mathrm{Can}(H)\sim H,\qquad \mathrm{Can}(\mathrm{Can}(H))=\mathrm{Can}(H).]
(B) Norm normalization.When scale is not physically meaningful, canonicalization fixes a norm, typically[|H|_2 = 1,]by mapping (H\mapsto H/|H|_2) when (H\neq 0). If (H=0), canonicalization returns (0) and records a degenerate-state flag in the serialization metadata.
(C) Phase anchoring.For complex-valued states, global phase is fixed by a deterministic anchor functional (\Phi(H)\in\mathbb{R}) and mapping[H\mapsto e^{-i\Phi(H)}H.]The anchor must be measurable and stable; canonical choices include the argument of a designated coefficient, the argument of the spatial integral (\int H), or the argument of the maximal-magnitude coefficient under a deterministic tie-breaker.
(D) Ordering and tie-breaking.When canonicalization requires selecting an extremum (e.g., maximal coefficient), ties are broken by a fixed lexicographic order on indices defined by the basis (\mathcal{B}) and discretization parameters. Canonicalization is invalid if it depends on nondeterministic iteration order or platform-dependent floating behaviors.
1.1.4.3 Equivalence relations (preview)
(A) Almost-everywhere equivalence.For (f,g\in L^p(X,\mu)), define (f\sim_{\text{a.e.}} g) iff (f=g) (\mu)-almost everywhere. All (L^p) objects are quotient objects under (\sim_{\text{a.e.}}).
(B) Gauge equivalence.Given a gauge group (\mathcal{G}), define (H\sim_{\mathcal{G}}H') iff (H'=g\cdot H) for some (g\in\mathcal{G}). The pattern space is the quotient (H^4/\mathcal{G}).
(C) Discretization equivalence.Let (H^{(N,P,\epsilon)}) denote discrete approximations. Two discrete states are equivalent within tolerance (\delta) if their canonicalized representatives satisfy[|\mathrm{Can}(H^{(N,P,\epsilon)})-\mathrm{Can}(H'^{(N,P,\epsilon)})| \le \delta,]with norm declared (default (\ell^2) on discrete coefficients). The equivalence relation must include explicit ((N,P,\epsilon)) and (\delta).
(D) Program equivalence for transforms.Two transform programs (T_1,T_2) are equivalent on a declared domain (\mathcal{D}\subseteq H^4) within tolerance (\delta) if[\sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}}\ |T_1(H)-T_2(H)| \le \delta,]with (\mathcal{D}) specified either as a finite test set with coverage guarantees or as an analytically characterized subset supporting a uniform bound.
1.1.4.4 Deterministic serialization rules (preview)
(A) Canonical byte encoding.A serialized artifact is a byte string produced by a deterministic procedure[\mathrm{Ser}:\ \text{Artifact}\to {0,1}^\ast]such that identical artifacts (under declared equivalence and canonicalization) produce identical bytes across platforms.
(B) Deterministic ordering.All maps/dictionaries/graphs are serialized with keys sorted by a declared total order. Floating arrays are serialized in fixed endianness (little-endian) and fixed IEEE-754 format unless an exact rational representation is specified. NaNs are forbidden in serialized numeric fields.
(C) Canonical text normalization.All embedded text fields are encoded UTF-8 with Unicode normalization NFC and newline normalization to LF. Trailing whitespace per line is prohibited. The serialized form includes explicit version tags for schema evolution.
(D) Hashing and identity.A cryptographic digest (\mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(\cdot))) defines artifact identity. Any manuscript-level reference to an artifact ID is defined as the digest of its canonical serialization. Any non-determinism in the serialization process invalidates identity and all dependent certificates.
1.2 Flower — Mappings Between Dimensions
1.2.1 Lifts and collapses
1.2.1.1 Inclusion functors and lift operators
(A) Category of structured domains.Define a category (\mathsf{Dom}) whose objects are tuples ((X,\Sigma_X,\mu_X,\mathcal{R}_X)) where (\mathcal{R}_X) encodes declared regularity assumptions (topological, manifold, Lipschitz boundary, etc.). Morphisms are measurable maps respecting declared structure.
(B) Lift functors.A lift functor (L:\mathsf{Dom}k\to \mathsf{Dom}{k+1}) assigns to each (k)-dimensional domain a fiber-augmented domain and to each morphism a lifted morphism preserving composition and identities. For hologram construction, the canonical lift is:[L(\Omega) := \Omega\times\mathcal{P}.]
(C) Lift operators on function spaces.Given a lift on domains (L(X)), the induced lift on functions is:[L^\sharp: L^2(X)\to L^2(L(X)),\qquad (L^\sharp f)(x,p)=f(x)\psi(p),]with (\psi) fixed as part of the lift specification. Alternative lifts define encodings (\mathcal{E}) into fiber couplings.
(D) Lift admissibility.A lift operator is admissible only if:
it is measurable and well-defined on the declared function class,
it is bounded (or has declared domain) between declared norms,
it preserves feasibility when feasibility is already defined on the source space, and
it produces states compatible with the constraint operator on the target space when feasibility is defined there.
1.2.1.2 Projection operators and marginalization
(A) Deterministic projection operators.A projection is a well-typed map (P:\mathfrak{H}\to V) for some lower-dimensional space (V). Canonical projections include fiber integrals, conditional expectations, and slices.
(B) Marginalization as integral operator.For (H\in L^1(\Omega\times\mathcal{P})), define[(\mathrm{Marg}\Omega H)(x) := \int{\mathcal{P}} H(x,p),d\mu(p).]For (H\in L^2), marginalization is defined via Bochner integration when conditions hold or via weak definitions consistent with (L^2) structure.
(C) Conditional projections.When (\mu) is a probability measure, conditional expectation defines a projection in (L^2):[\mathbb{E}[H\mid x] \in L^2(\Omega),]and satisfies orthogonality conditions. Conditional projections are the canonical reductions under probabilistic parameterization.
(D) Projection stability.Projection operators must specify stability bounds:[|P(H)| \le c,|H|]in declared norms, and must declare whether projection is information-losing or invertible on a specified subspace (e.g., on the range of a lift).
1.2.1.3 Pushforward/pullback on measures
(A) Pushforward.For a measurable map (f:X\to Y) and measure (\mu) on (X), the pushforward (f_\ast\mu) is defined by[(f_\ast\mu)(B)=\mu(f^{-1}(B)),\qquad B\in\Sigma_Y.]
(B) Pullback (change of variables).When (f) is sufficiently regular and (\nu) is a measure on (Y), a pullback (f^\ast\nu) is defined via densities and Jacobians when (\nu) is absolutely continuous. In Euclidean settings, if (f) is a diffeomorphism,[\int_Y \phi(y),d\nu(y)=\int_X \phi(f(x)),|\det Df(x)|,d(f^\ast\nu)(x).]
(C) Measure transport on product spaces.For product measures, pushforwards and pullbacks are applied componentwise when maps factorize, and generally require disintegration theorems when they do not. Any use of “marginal measure” or “conditional measure” must declare the disintegration being used.
(D) Consistency with function transforms.Measure transforms must be consistent with function pullbacks so that (L^p) norms and inner products are preserved or controlled according to declared stability. This consistency is required for interpreting transforms as unitary/energy-preserving or as dissipative under explicit cost models.
1.2.1.4 Compatibility with constraints
(A) Constraint operator typing.A constraint operator is typed as (C:\mathfrak{H}\to Z), where (Z) is a normed space of constraint residuals. Feasibility is defined as (\ker(C)) or ({H:|C(H)|\le \tau}) under declared tolerance (\tau).
(B) Constraint-commuting lifts/projections.A lift (L^\sharp) is constraint-compatible if there exists (\widetilde{C}) such that:[C\circ L^\sharp = \widetilde{L}^\sharp\circ C]on the relevant domain. A projection (P) is constraint-compatible if:[\widetilde{C}\circ P = P_C\circ C]for some (P_C) on residual space.
(C) Feasibility-preserving morphisms.A morphism (T) is feasibility-preserving if:[C(H)=0\ \Rightarrow\ C(T(H))=0.]A weaker corridor form requires (|C(T(H))|\le \gamma|C(H)|) for a contraction factor (\gamma) or (|C(T(H))|\le \tau) for a fixed tolerance.
(D) Constraint-aware composition.If (T) and (S) are feasibility-preserving, then (T\circ S) is feasibility-preserving. If one is corridor-preserving with contraction (\gamma), the composition inherits multiplicative contraction. These closure properties are mandatory for building multi-stage programs.
1.2.2 Operator composition grammar
1.2.2.1 Typed composition ((T\circ S)) rules
(A) Well-definedness.Composition (T\circ S) is well-defined only if (\mathrm{Cod}(S)\subseteq \mathrm{Dom}(T)). For unbounded operators, the domain condition strengthens to (S(\mathcal{D}(S))\subseteq \mathcal{D}(T)).
(B) Total vs partial operators.An operator is total if defined on the entire declared space; it is partial if defined on a strict subset. Partial operators must carry explicit domain predicates, and composition must propagate predicates via preimages.
(C) Measurability and continuity.For nonlinear transforms, measurability and continuity (or Lipschitz properties) must be declared. Composition is admissible only if it preserves the declared regularity class (e.g., maps (H^s\to H^s) or (L^2\to L^2)).
(D) Type-checking for program graphs.Transforms are assembled into directed acyclic graphs (or general graphs for recursion) where each edge carries type compatibility. A program is invalid if any edge violates typing, domain, measurability, or regularity compatibility.
1.2.2.2 Identity, associativity, and neutral elements
(A) Identity operators.For each typed space (V), the identity (\mathrm{Id}_V:V\to V) satisfies (\mathrm{Id}_V(v)=v). Identity is the neutral element for composition.
(B) Associativity.For compatible operators (R,T,S),[R\circ(T\circ S)=(R\circ T)\circ S]as maps with compatible domains. For partial/unbounded operators, associativity holds only on the maximal domain where both sides are simultaneously defined.
(C) Category structure.Typed spaces and admissible morphisms form a category (\mathsf{Mor}) under composition with identities. Subcategories are defined by admissibility levels (e.g., constraint-preserving, corridor-preserving, unitary, contractive).
(D) Neutral elements under program equivalence.In program representations, identity nodes may be eliminated under rewrite rules only if elimination preserves typing, domain predicates, and declared verification hooks (e.g., if identity carried a required logging checkpoint, it is not eliminable).
1.2.2.3 Inverses, pseudoinverses, and partial inverses
(A) Inverses (exact).A transform (T:V\to V) is invertible if there exists (T^{-1}:V\to V) such that (T^{-1}\circ T=\mathrm{Id}_V) and (T\circ T^{-1}=\mathrm{Id}_V). For bounded linear operators, invertibility requires boundedness of (T^{-1}) on (V).
(B) Left/right inverses.For (T:V\to W), a left inverse (L:W\to V) satisfies (L\circ T=\mathrm{Id}_V). A right inverse (R:W\to V) satisfies (T\circ R=\mathrm{Id}_W) on a specified subspace. Left/right inverses encode embeddings and surjections, respectively.
(C) Pseudoinverses.For linear operators between Hilbert spaces, the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse (T^\dagger) is defined when (T) has closed range and satisfies the Penrose equations. Pseudoinverses are used for reconstruction operators and least-squares inversion.
(D) Partial inverses under constraints.A transform may be invertible only on the feasible manifold (\ker(C)) or on a gauge-quotient. A partial inverse must declare:
the inversion domain (\mathcal{D}\subseteq V),
the equivalence class on which inversion is defined (e.g., modulo gauge), and
the error tolerance (\delta) if inversion is approximate.
1.2.2.4 Commutators and non-commutativity diagnostics
(A) Commutator definition.For linear operators (A,B) on a common domain where both products exist, the commutator is[[A,B]:=AB-BA.]
(B) Non-commutativity measures.Non-commutativity is quantified by norms of commutators:[\kappa_{A,B} := |[A,B]|]in an appropriate operator norm or induced norm on a declared subspace. For nonlinear transforms, commutators are defined via composition differences (T\circ S - S\circ T) evaluated in a declared norm on a declared test set.
(C) BCH expansion for generator exponentials.For (T=\exp(A)), (S=\exp(B)) with appropriate operator assumptions,[\log(\exp(A)\exp(B)) = A + B + \tfrac12[A,B] + \tfrac1{12}[A,[A,B]] - \tfrac1{12}[B,[A,B]] + \cdots]This expansion underpins program rewriting and approximation of composed flows.
(D) Commutator-driven curvature diagnostics.For loop compositions (T_1T_2T_1^{-1}T_2^{-1}), deviation from identity is governed by commutators. The manuscript treats such deviations as discrete curvature signatures for transformation algebras and uses them to detect interference, irreducible ordering effects, and instability under reordering.
1.2.3 Flows and evolution templates
1.2.3.1 ODE flows on Hilbert spaces
(A) Abstract evolution equation.Let (\mathcal{H}) be a Hilbert space. An ODE flow is defined by[\dot{u}(t) = F(u(t)),\qquad u(0)=u_0\in\mathcal{H},]where (F:\mathcal{D}(F)\subseteq\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{H}) is (at minimum) locally Lipschitz on bounded subsets of (\mathcal{D}(F)).
(B) Existence and uniqueness.If (F) is locally Lipschitz, then for each (u_0\in\mathcal{D}(F)) there exists a maximal interval ([0,T_{\max})) on which a unique solution exists. Global existence requires additional a priori bounds (e.g., Lyapunov or energy bounds).
(C) Feasibility invariance.For a feasible set (\mathcal{M}\subseteq\mathcal{H}) (e.g., (\ker(C))), the flow preserves feasibility if (F) is tangent to (\mathcal{M}) in the appropriate sense:[u(0)\in\mathcal{M}\ \Rightarrow\ u(t)\in\mathcal{M}\ \ \forall t,]or if a projection mechanism enforces feasibility at each time.
(D) Discretization compatibility.Any ODE flow used operationally must specify a numerical integrator and conditions ensuring convergence of discrete trajectories to the continuous flow under step refinement, with stability restrictions declared.
1.2.3.2 PDE flows and semigroup theory
(A) Abstract Cauchy problem.Let (A) be a (possibly unbounded) linear operator generating a (C_0)-semigroup (S(t)) on (\mathcal{H}). The PDE template is[\dot{u}(t) = Au(t) + f(t),\qquad u(0)=u_0.]
(B) Mild solutions.Mild solutions satisfy[u(t)=S(t)u_0+\int_0^t S(t-s)f(s),ds.]When (f) depends on (u) (semilinear), fixed point methods are used under appropriate Lipschitz and growth conditions.
(C) Hille–Yosida conditions.A linear operator (A) generates a contraction semigroup iff it is densely defined, closed, and dissipative with range conditions. All semigroup-based PDE evolutions in this manuscript must declare the generator class and the semigroup bounds.
(D) Boundary conditions and trace constraints.For PDEs on (\Omega), boundary conditions are encoded via trace operators and boundary spaces. Feasibility with respect to boundary constraints is part of the state definition when boundary-informed holography is used.
1.2.3.3 Discrete-time iterates and maps
(A) Discrete dynamics.A discrete evolution is defined by an iterate map[u_{n+1} = Q(u_n),\qquad u_0\in\mathcal{H}.]
(B) Fixed points and stability.A fixed point satisfies (Q(u^\ast)=u^\ast). Stability is defined by the behavior of (|u_n-u^\ast|) under perturbations. Contraction maps (Lipschitz constant (<1)) guarantee unique fixed points and convergence.
(C) Feasibility preservation.If feasibility is (\mathcal{M}\subseteq\mathcal{H}), the iterate is feasible if (Q(\mathcal{M})\subseteq\mathcal{M}). Corridor feasibility allows (Q) to map into a tubular neighborhood of (\mathcal{M}) followed by a feasibility projection.
(D) Computational realization.Discrete-time iterates are the canonical implementation form for transformation engines. Each iterate must include: type checks, corridor checks, and certificate emission as part of the step semantics.
1.2.3.4 Hybrid flows (event-driven operators)
(A) Hybrid state evolution.A hybrid evolution combines continuous flow with discrete jumps:[\dot{u}(t)=F(u(t))\ \text{for }t\notin{t_k},\qquad u(t_k^+)=J_k(u(t_k^-)).]
(B) Event guards.Events occur when a guard condition (G(u(t))=0) is satisfied or when a time schedule triggers a jump. Guards must be measurable and compatible with chosen solution concept.
(C) Well-posedness requirements.Hybrid systems require:
existence of continuous segments,
existence of jump maps,
exclusion of Zeno behavior or explicit Zeno handling rules, and
feasibility preservation or corridor enforcement across both flows and jumps.
(D) Hybrid admissibility.A hybrid program is admissible only if each continuous segment is admissible under its corridor and each jump map is admissible, with composition preserving corridor budgets and certificate validity.
1.2.4 Program representation of transforms
1.2.4.1 Generator parameterization ((\theta))
(A) Parametric transform families.A transform family is specified as (T_\theta) for parameter vector (\theta\in\Theta\subseteq\mathbb{R}^m) (or (\mathbb{C}^m) where appropriate). The parameter domain (\Theta) must include explicit bounds and admissibility constraints.
(B) Generator form.For generator-based transforms, parameters instantiate a generator (X_\theta) and define:[T_\theta := \exp(X_\theta),]or for flows, (T_{\theta,t}=\exp(tX_\theta)). Generator domains and self-adjointness/semigroup conditions are part of the specification.
(C) Differentiability and gradients.If optimization over (\theta) is used, differentiability of (T_\theta) (Fréchet or Gateaux) must be established or replaced by subgradient methods. The manuscript treats gradient availability as part of the program type.
(D) Parameter canonicalization.Parameters used for serialization and reproducibility must be canonicalized: fixed ordering, fixed precision, fixed rounding rules, and fixed representation of constrained parameters (e.g., log-parameterization for positivity).
1.2.4.2 Basis choice ((\mathcal{B})) and truncation parameters
(A) Basis declaration.A basis (\mathcal{B}) is a declared representation family for states (Fourier, wavelet, spline, learned dictionary, hybrid). The basis declaration includes:
basis type identifier,
parameterization (scales, bands, grid),
orthogonality/frame properties,
transform/inverse-transform algorithms and stability bounds.
(B) Truncation parameters.Finite representations require explicit truncation:[(N,P,\epsilon),]where (N) denotes spatial resolution/bandlimit, (P) denotes parameter resolution, and (\epsilon) denotes scale or error tolerance. All discrete objects are typed by these parameters.
(C) Discrete state object.A discrete state is represented as coefficients (c\in\mathbb{C}^{M}) in a declared basis with a deterministic index map (\mathrm{Idx}:{1,\dots,M}\to) basis functions. The mapping is part of the serialized artifact.
(D) Consistency and refinement.Refinement rules specify how ((N,P,\epsilon)) change and how states are lifted between resolutions. Refinement must satisfy consistency: refined approximations converge (in declared norm) to a continuous limit when such a limit exists.
1.2.4.3 Compose/optimize/rewrite transforms
(A) Program graph representation.A transform program is a typed directed graph whose nodes are primitive operators and whose edges carry typed intermediate spaces. The program denotes the composed transform obtained by topologically ordered composition (or by explicit recursion semantics for cyclic graphs).
(B) Rewrite rules.Rewrite rules are algebraic equalities or approximations with declared tolerance. A rewrite rule is admissible only if:
it preserves typing,
it preserves domain predicates,
it preserves corridor budgets (or improves them), and
it preserves certificate semantics (logs and checkpoints).
(C) Optimization objectives.Optimization selects programs minimizing a cost functional (\mathcal{C}) under constraints. Costs must include:
numerical stability penalties,
invariant drift penalties,
computational complexity costs, and
corridor violation penalties (infinite cost for violations).
(D) Correctness of rewrite/optimization.A rewrite/optimization pipeline is correct only if it emits either:
an exact equivalence certificate, or
an approximation certificate with declared tolerance bounds and verified tests supporting the bounds on a declared domain.
1.2.4.4 Minimal program normal forms
(A) Normal form definition.A normal form is a canonical representation of a transform program under a declared equivalence relation. Normal forms must be deterministic and must be unique within the declared equivalence class (or must include a deterministic tie-breaker).
(B) Minimality criteria.Minimality is defined relative to a declared cost vector (length, runtime, memory, drift). A program is minimal if no equivalent program has strictly lower cost in lexicographic order on the cost vector.
(C) Canonical serialization of normal forms.Normal forms must serialize deterministically. Serialization includes:
ordered list of primitives,
parameter values after canonicalization,
basis and truncation declarations,
versioned schema tags.
(D) Normal-form verification.A normal-form transform must carry a certificate consisting of:
typing proof (static),
corridor proof (static/analytic and/or tested),
invariant drift proof (analytic and/or tested), and
reconstruction compatibility proof where applicable.
1.3 Cloud — Corridors, Budgets, and Admissibility (Preview)
1.3.1 Error models (preview)
1.3.1.1 Noise models (additive/multiplicative/phase)
(A) Additive noise.A noisy observation or state is modeled as[\widetilde{H}=H+\eta,]with (\eta) belonging to a declared noise space (e.g., (L^2) or (H^{-s})) and carrying a declared bound (|\eta|\le \sigma) or a probabilistic law.
(B) Multiplicative noise.Multiplicative noise is modeled as[\widetilde{H}=(1+\xi)H,]with (\xi) bounded in (L^\infty) or controlled in a stochastic sense. Stability analyses must specify how multiplicative noise affects invariants and reconstruction.
(C) Phase noise.For complex-valued holograms, phase noise is modeled as[\widetilde{H}=e^{i\phi}H,]with (\phi) bounded or structured. Phase noise intersects gauge equivalence; canonicalization must distinguish physical phase from gauge phase when both are present.
(D) Composite noise.Composite noise combines the above:[\widetilde{H}=(1+\xi)e^{i\phi}H+\eta,]with declared bounds for (\xi,\phi,\eta). All corridor and verification definitions must specify which noise components are within scope.
1.3.1.2 Approximation error (truncation/quantization)
(A) Truncation error.Given continuous (H) and its discrete approximation (H^{(N,P,\epsilon)}), truncation error is[e_{\text{trunc}} := |H - \mathcal{I}{N,P}(H)|,]where (\mathcal{I}{N,P}) is the declared truncation/interpolation operator. Convergence requires (e_{\text{trunc}}\to 0) under refinement.
(B) Quantization error.Quantization maps continuous coefficients to finite precision. Quantization error is bounded by machine epsilon and scaling; numerical policies must bound rounding error accumulation under composition.
(C) Discretization mismatch.Mismatch arises when operators and invariants are discretized inconsistently. The manuscript treats mismatch as an error source separate from truncation and requires discrete operators to satisfy consistency identities (discrete analogues of integration by parts, adjointness, or unitarity where claimed).
(D) Approximation envelopes.Approximation envelopes are declared as functions (\epsilon\mapsto E(\epsilon)) bounding total approximation error at scale (\epsilon). Envelopes are required inputs to admissibility budgets.
1.3.1.3 Model error (mis-specified constraints)
(A) Constraint operator mis-specification.Model error includes selecting a constraint operator (C) that does not match the intended feasible class. This is measured by residual behavior across reference states and by systematic reconstruction failure.
(B) Bias decomposition.Model error decomposes into:
structural bias (wrong constraint family),
parametric bias (wrong parameters),
discretization bias (wrong discrete analogue),
observation bias (wrong measurement model).
(C) Identification of model error.Model error is diagnosed via:
persistent nonzero constraint residuals after repair,
invariant drift patterns inconsistent with noise/truncation,
reconstruction error not decreasing under refinement,
failure across diverse reference cases.
(D) Model correction operators.Model correction is formalized as selecting (C) from a model class ({C_\alpha}) and optimizing (\alpha) under admissibility and evidence constraints. Corrections must be versioned and certified.
1.3.1.4 Robust error envelopes
(A) Deterministic envelopes.A deterministic envelope is a bound (|e|\le E) in a declared norm. Such bounds are required for worst-case guarantees and for safety corridors.
(B) Probabilistic envelopes.A probabilistic envelope is a tail bound (\mathbb{P}(|e|>E)\le \delta). Such envelopes must declare the probability model and confidence level.
(C) Adversarial envelopes.Adversarial envelopes assume (e) is chosen by an adversary within a bounded set. Robustness requires admissibility under all perturbations in the set.
(D) Envelope propagation.Envelopes must propagate through transforms:[|T(H+e)-T(H)|\le L_T|e|]for Lipschitz (T), or via operator norms for linear (T). Envelope propagation defines how corridor budgets are updated across program steps.
1.3.2 Stability templates (preview)
1.3.2.1 Operator norm bounds
(A) Boundedness as stability.A bounded linear transform satisfies (|T|<\infty). Stability under perturbations follows from[|T(H+\eta)-T(H)|\le |T|,|\eta|.]
(B) Semigroup bounds.For flows (S(t)), stability is expressed by semigroup bounds:[|S(t)|\le M e^{\omega t}.]Contractive semigroups have (M=1,\omega\le 0).
(C) Composition bounds.For bounded transforms,[|T_n\circ\cdots\circ T_1|\le \prod_{k=1}^n |T_k|,]which motivates logarithmic cost accumulation and motivates rewriting strategies that reduce norm inflation.
(D) Stability budgets.A corridor assigns a stability budget (\mathsf{StabMax}) and requires (|T|\le \mathsf{StabMax}) for admissible transforms, optionally refined per subspace or per frequency band.
1.3.2.2 Condition numbers and ill-posedness
(A) Condition number definition.For invertible bounded linear (T),[\kappa(T):=|T|,|T^{-1}|.]Large (\kappa(T)) indicates ill-conditioning and amplification of errors in inversion.
(B) Ill-posed inverse problems.Inverse problems are ill-posed when solutions do not exist, are non-unique, or do not depend continuously on data. The manuscript encodes ill-posedness as corridor violations unless regularization is explicitly part of the transform definition.
(C) Regularization as admissible transform component.Regularized inversion defines (T_\lambda^{-1}) depending on regularization parameter (\lambda). Admissibility requires:
stability bounds for (T_\lambda^{-1}),
bias bounds introduced by (\lambda),
convergence as (\lambda\to 0) where appropriate.
(D) Conditioning diagnostics.Programs must compute or bound conditioning using analytic estimates, spectral bounds, or certified numerical estimates. Conditioning diagnostics are mandatory outputs for transforms used in reconstruction and optimization.
1.3.2.3 Lipschitz continuity and contraction maps
(A) Lipschitz transforms.A transform (T) is Lipschitz on a domain (\mathcal{D}) if:[|T(H)-T(H')|\le L|H-H'|,\qquad \forall H,H'\in\mathcal{D}.]
(B) Contractions.If (L<1), (T) is a contraction and has a unique fixed point on complete metric spaces (Banach fixed point theorem). Contractions define the canonical template for convergence guarantees.
(C) Local Lipschitz.Local Lipschitz conditions are used for existence/uniqueness of ODE flows. Domains must be declared; no global claims are permitted without global bounds.
(D) Lipschitz under discretization.Discrete implementations must preserve Lipschitz constants up to controlled factors; otherwise discrete dynamics may diverge even when continuous dynamics are stable.
1.3.2.4 Sensitivity to perturbations
(A) Forward sensitivity.Sensitivity is the map (e\mapsto T(H+e)-T(H)). Bounds are expressed by operator norms (linear) or Lipschitz constants (nonlinear).
(B) Inverse sensitivity.Inverse sensitivity is governed by (|T^{-1}|) and by regularization. Inverse sensitivity budgets are core admissibility constraints for reconstruction.
(C) Sensitivity by bands/scales.Sensitivity may vary by frequency band or scale. The manuscript allows bandwise stability budgets (|T|{\text{band}}\le M{\text{band}}) and requires transforms to declare bandwise behavior when used in multi-scale invariant control.
(D) Sensitivity reporting.Every admissibility certificate includes sensitivity reports: stability norms, conditioning estimates, and the derived propagation of error envelopes through the program.
1.3.3 Corridor semantics (preview)
1.3.3.1 Hard constraints vs soft constraints
(A) Hard feasibility.Hard feasibility is defined by (C(H)=0), i.e., (H\in\ker(C)). Hard feasibility is preserved exactly by admissible transforms.
(B) Soft feasibility.Soft feasibility is defined by (|C(H)|\le\tau) for tolerance (\tau>0). Soft feasibility supports noise and truncation. The tolerance (\tau) is part of the state type.
(C) Projection-based enforcement.Soft feasibility is enforced by projection or repair maps (\mathcal{P}:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathfrak{H}) such that (|C(\mathcal{P}(H))|\le \tau) and (|\mathcal{P}(H)-H|) is minimized under a declared metric.
(D) Penalty-based enforcement.Soft feasibility may be enforced by adding penalties to objectives:[\min_H \ \mathcal{J}(H)+\lambda|C(H)|^2,]with (\lambda) declared. Penalty methods must provide bounds connecting (\lambda) to feasibility residuals.
1.3.3.2 Budget vectors ((\delta)) (invariant drift)
(A) Invariant suite.Let (\mathrm{Inv}(H)\in\mathbb{R}^k) denote a declared invariant vector (multi-scale information, spectral moments, topology proxies, constraint residuals, reconstruction envelope parameters). Each component has declared units and scale.
(B) Drift budgets.A corridor assigns a drift budget (\delta\in\mathbb{R}^k_{\ge 0}) and requires:[|\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)|{\mathsf{w}}\le \delta]in a declared weighted norm (|\cdot|{\mathsf{w}}). Componentwise budgets are permitted and preferred for interpretability.
(C) Budget propagation.Budgets propagate through program composition by declared combination rules (additive, multiplicative, or mixed). The propagation rule is part of corridor semantics and must be conservative (never underestimates drift).
(D) Budget allocation across scales.For multi-scale invariants (I_{\epsilon_i}(H)), budgets allocate per scale. Allocation rules must be explicit and must prevent drift concentration that violates reconstruction or stability at any single scale.
1.3.3.3 Safety constraints as admissibility guards
(A) Safety constraints as hard guards.Safety constraints are predicates (\mathsf{Safe}(H)=\mathrm{true}) defining forbidden regions of state space. Admissible transforms must satisfy:[\mathsf{Safe}(H)\Rightarrow \mathsf{Safe}(T(H)).]
(B) Safety constraints in transform space.Safety constraints may restrict transforms directly: (\mathsf{Safe}(T)=\mathrm{true}) forbids transforms with excessive conditioning, norm blow-up, non-invertibility where invertibility is required, or uncontrolled drift.
(C) Guard enforcement in program execution.Program semantics require safety checks at declared checkpoints (per primitive or per stage). Violations trigger rejection or repair. Repair transforms must themselves be admissible and must emit certificates.
(D) Safety certificates.Safety compliance is certified by:
analytic bounds where available, and
verified numerical checks on declared test suites with coverage claims.Safety certificates are mandatory for publication-grade artifacts.
1.3.3.4 Failure modes and diagnostic taxonomy
(A) Numerical failures.Numerical failures include divergence, overflow/underflow, catastrophic cancellation, instability under step refinement, and nondeterminism in floating behaviors.
(B) Structural failures.Structural failures include constraint violations, loss of reconstructability, loss of identifiability, and violation of invariants beyond budget.
(C) Algorithmic failures.Algorithmic failures include non-convergence of solvers, stagnation in optimization, degeneracy in pseudoinverses, and failure of rewrite rules due to domain restrictions.
(D) Diagnostic reports.Every failure report must include:
failing predicate,
violated bound and measured value,
minimal reproducer inputs (state IDs, program IDs, seeds, discretization), and
suggested repair class (projection, regularization, reparameterization, refinement).
1.3.4 Existence/uniqueness roadmap (preview)
1.3.4.1 Fixed point schemas
(A) Contraction fixed points.If (T) is a contraction on a complete metric space, it has a unique fixed point and iterates converge geometrically.
(B) Schauder fixed points.If (T) maps a convex compact subset into itself and is continuous, a fixed point exists. Compactness conditions are enforced using Sobolev/BV compactness when needed.
(C) Monotone operator fixed points.For monotone operators in reflexive Banach spaces, existence follows from Minty–Browder frameworks. Such templates support constrained variational forms.
(D) Fixed point relevance.Fixed point schemas are used to establish existence of reconstruction operators, convergence of repair maps, existence of optimal transforms under cost, and stability of cyclic engines.
1.3.4.2 Compactness + limit arguments
(A) Weak convergence templates.Bounded sequences in Hilbert spaces admit weakly convergent subsequences. Weak limits are used to extract feasible limits under constraint bounds.
(B) Lower semicontinuity.Convex functionals are weakly lower semicontinuous; this property underpins existence of minimizers in constrained optimization.
(C) Strong convergence via compact embedding.Compact embeddings yield strong convergence from boundedness plus weak convergence. Strong convergence is required to pass nonlinear terms and to certify reconstruction properties.
(D) Discrete-to-continuous limits.Discrete approximations must be shown to be consistent and stable; then compactness yields subsequence convergence. Proof obligations include identifying the limit as a solution of the intended continuous problem.
1.3.4.3 Stability implies convergence conditions
(A) Lax-style principles.For linear consistent schemes, stability implies convergence. The manuscript adopts this principle as a template: numerical stability plus discrete consistency are mandatory for convergence claims.
(B) Energy method templates.Energy inequalities provide a priori bounds that yield stability and thus convergence for iterative schemes and PDE discretizations.
(C) Lyapunov function templates.Lyapunov functions certify monotonic decrease of an objective or residual and yield convergence of iterative repair and optimization routines under declared conditions.
(D) Corridor-controlled convergence.Corridor budgets constrain allowable drift and error growth. Convergence claims must show that corridor compliance implies boundedness and that boundedness combined with compactness implies convergence.
1.3.4.4 Counterexamples and no-go patterns
(A) Non-uniqueness under gauge.Gauge symmetry yields inherent non-uniqueness unless canonicalization or quotienting is applied. Any uniqueness claim must explicitly state “up to gauge equivalence” unless gauge is fixed.
(B) Undersampling no-go.Reconstruction from partial data fails under undersampling beyond information-theoretic limits. No-go patterns include aliasing constructions and phase retrieval ambiguity classes.
(C) Ill-conditioning no-go.Even when inverses exist, extreme conditioning makes inversion unstable; admissibility must forbid or regularize such regimes.
(D) Nonconvexity traps.Optimization over transforms is generally nonconvex; existence and uniqueness of global minimizers require coercivity and convexity-like conditions or must be replaced by certified local optimality statements.
1.4 Fractal — Implementation Standards (Preview)
1.4.1 Finite representation doctrine
1.4.1.1 Truncation parameters ((N,P)) and scale ((\epsilon))
(A) Finite state type.A finite hologram is typed as[H^{(N,P,\epsilon)}\in \mathbb{C}^{N\times N\times N\times P},]or as a coefficient vector (c\in\mathbb{C}^{M}) under a declared basis (\mathcal{B}). The tuple ((N,P,\epsilon,\mathcal{B})) is part of the state’s identity.
(B) Scale parameter.(\epsilon) denotes either target approximation error or minimal resolved scale. (\epsilon) must be declared operationally: as bandlimit, wavelet depth, grid spacing, or tolerance.
(C) Refinement policy.A refinement policy is a deterministic rule producing ((N',P',\epsilon')) from ((N,P,\epsilon)). Refinement must be monotone (increasing resolution / decreasing (\epsilon)) and must define consistent lifting of states between levels.
(D) Error tracking.Every finite state carries an error envelope (\mathsf{Err}(H^{(N,P,\epsilon)})) that upper-bounds truncation and quantization error and is updated through transforms by declared propagation rules.
1.4.1.2 Basis families and resolution ladders
(A) Basis family declaration.Each basis family (\mathcal{B}) must specify forward transform, inverse transform, stability bounds, and coefficient indexing. Orthonormal bases and frames are both permitted; frames require explicit frame bounds.
(B) Resolution ladder.A resolution ladder is a nested sequence (\mathcal{B}_0\subset \mathcal{B}_1\subset \cdots) with deterministic inclusion maps. Wavelet and multigrid ladders are canonical. Ladder compatibility is required for multi-scale invariants and reconstruction.
(C) Mixed bases.Mixed bases (e.g., Fourier in (\Omega), wavelet in (\mathcal{P})) require explicit tensor product indexing and explicit norms used for stability checks.
(D) Basis stability reporting.Basis declarations must include numerical stability diagnostics: condition estimates for transforms, bounds on reconstruction from coefficients, and sensitivity of coefficients to perturbations.
1.4.1.3 Storage formats and determinism requirements
(A) Storage format schema.Storage format must encode:
basis identifier and parameters,
truncation parameters,
coefficient arrays with deterministic ordering,
metadata for canonicalization and error envelopes,
schema version and hash.
(B) Deterministic binary encoding.Binary encoding must specify endianness, float format, compression (if any) with deterministic settings, and prohibition of platform-dependent structures.
(C) Deterministic sparse encodings.Sparse formats must serialize indices in sorted order and must define deterministic tie-breaking and thresholding rules for sparsification.
(D) Identity and hashing.Artifact identity is the hash of canonical serialization. Any non-determinism in storage invalidates identity and all downstream certificates.
1.4.1.4 Numerical precision and rounding policies
(A) Precision classes.All computations declare precision class (float32/float64/float128 where available) and specify whether mixed precision is used.
(B) Rounding policy.Rounding mode is assumed IEEE-754 default unless explicitly changed; any change must be serialized and included in reproducibility manifests.
(C) Stable summation and reductions.Reductions (sums, dot products) must use deterministic reduction order or stable summation algorithms. Parallel reductions must enforce deterministic ordering to guarantee reproducibility.
(D) Numerical error budgets.Numerical budgets are included in corridor enforcement. Programs must bound floating error contributions to invariant drift and to constraint residuals.
1.4.2 Verification doctrine
1.4.2.1 Constraint residual tests ((|C(H)|))
(A) Residual definition.Given (C:H^4\to Z), the residual is (r(H)=|C(H)|_Z). The norm (|\cdot|_Z) must be declared.
(B) Threshold policy.A state is feasible if (r(H)=0) (hard) or (r(H)\le\tau) (soft). Thresholds (\tau) are part of the state type.
(C) Residual monitoring.Residuals are computed:
after state creation,
after each transform stage,
after repair/projection, and
at program termination.All residual evaluations are logged with deterministic IDs.
(D) Residual regression tests.Regression suites include known-feasible and known-infeasible states. Constraint tests must detect regressions in feasibility logic under code changes and under precision changes.
1.4.2.2 Reconstruction error tests ((|R_A(H|_A)-H|))
(A) Mask and restriction.A reconstruction test selects a measurable region or mask (A\subseteq\Omega) and forms the restricted observation (H|_A) (or a sampled representation).
(B) Reconstruction operator.Given (R_A), reconstruction error is:[e_{\mathrm{rec}}(H;A)=|R_A(H|_A)-H|.]The norm must be declared and consistent with state typing.
(C) Test protocol.Reconstruction tests are performed over a deterministic set of masks ({A_i}) (seeded and serialized). Results include mean, max, and quantiles of (e_{\mathrm{rec}}) with confidence statements when stochastic.
(D) Acceptance criteria.Acceptance requires (e_{\mathrm{rec}}(H;A)\le \varepsilon(A)) for all required masks or for all masks in a declared coverage class. Violations trigger corridor failure or require repair.
1.4.2.3 Invariant drift tests ((|\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)|))
(A) Invariant computation.The invariant suite (\mathrm{Inv}(H)) is computed at declared scales and stored with the state artifact.
(B) Drift measurement.For transform (T), drift is:[\Delta_{\mathrm{Inv}}(H;T)=\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H).]
(C) Budget enforcement.A corridor specifies (\delta) and a norm (|\cdot|{\mathsf{w}}). Admissibility requires (|\Delta{\mathrm{Inv}}(H;T)|_{\mathsf{w}}\le \delta) on the declared domain.
(D) Drift attribution.Drift reports must attribute drift sources to: numerical rounding, truncation, noise, or transform structure. Attribution is required for debugging and for admissibility auditing.
1.4.2.4 Property-based testing templates
(A) Generator definitions.Property tests require generators for random states and transforms within declared corridors. Generators must be deterministic under seeds and must produce coverage over edge cases (sparse/dense, high-frequency/low-frequency, near-boundary, near-degenerate).
(B) Properties.Canonical properties include:
typing invariants (no domain errors),
feasibility preservation (hard/soft),
bounded drift,
bounded reconstruction error,
determinism of serialization.
(C) Shrinking and minimal counterexamples.When a property fails, the test system must shrink to a minimal reproducible counterexample under deterministic shrinking rules and must emit complete artifact IDs.
(D) Continuous integration requirements.Property tests must run under fixed environments and must be included in build gates for publication-grade artifacts. Any nondeterministic test outcome invalidates the test suite until determinism is restored.
1.4.3 Reproducibility doctrine
1.4.3.1 Deterministic seeds and sampling rules
(A) Seed hierarchy.A deterministic seed hierarchy defines seeds for:
mask generation,
noise generation,
randomized algorithm choices,
property-based test generation.Seed derivation rules must be deterministic and serialized.
