(B) Sampling determinism.All random sampling must be reproducible across platforms. Sampling must avoid nondeterministic parallel reductions and nondeterministic iteration order.
(C) Seed logging.Every artifact must record seed lineage sufficient to reproduce all stochastic choices. Seeds are treated as part of evidence, not as incidental metadata.
(D) Forbidden nondeterminism.Any reliance on system time, nondeterministic threading schedules, or hardware nondeterminism is forbidden unless explicitly controlled and recorded with a reproducibility waiver.
1.4.3.2 Reference datasets and gold states
(A) Gold states.Gold states are canonical reference holograms and transforms with stable certificates. They form the basis of regression tests and benchmark comparisons.
(B) Dataset versioning.Datasets and gold states are versioned by cryptographic hashes of canonical serialization. Any dataset update requires explicit version increment and migration notes.
(C) Coverage design.Reference suites must cover:
smooth structures,
sparse/interface structures,
high-frequency structures,
near-degenerate structures,
boundary-dominant structures,
adversarial perturbations.
(D) Evaluation protocols.Evaluation protocols specify metrics, acceptance thresholds, and confidence statements. Protocols are serialized and hashed to ensure that performance claims are reproducible.
1.4.3.3 Versioned theorem/proof artifacts
(A) Theorem artifacts.A theorem artifact includes the statement, assumptions, proof outline or full proof, and any computational verification evidence when applicable.
(B) Proof object schemas.Proof objects may be textual, symbolic, or machine-checkable. Schema and serialization rules must be explicit and deterministic.
(C) Coupling to code.Each theorem that supports an algorithm includes a traceability mapping from assumptions to code invariants and from proof steps to verification tests.
(D) Evolution and deprecation.When a theorem is refined or corrected, prior versions remain immutable; new versions must state the exact change and its impact on dependent algorithms and certificates.
1.4.3.4 Build reproducibility and audit logs
(A) Environment pinning.Builds require pinned dependency versions and deterministic compiler/interpreter configurations. Environment specifications are part of release artifacts.
(B) Deterministic builds.Build processes must be deterministic: identical inputs yield identical binaries/artifacts. Determinism is verified by hashing build outputs.
(C) Audit logs.Audit logs record:
artifact IDs,
program IDs,
test suite versions,
environment IDs,
seeds,
results and pass/fail statuses.Logs are serialized deterministically.
(D) Release integrity.Releases include signed manifests containing hashes of manuscript text, code, datasets, and certificates. Any mismatch invalidates the release.
1.4.4 Documentation doctrine
1.4.4.1 Notation index and glossary spec
(A) Symbol registry.A symbol registry defines each symbol with its type, domain, and semantic meaning. Registry entries are unique and versioned.
(B) Indexing rules.The glossary includes index conventions and forbidden ambiguities (e.g., reuse of (p) for probability and parameter without explicit disambiguation).
(C) Cross-reference discipline.Each definition and theorem has a stable identifier. Cross-references must use identifiers, not page numbers, to preserve stability under edition changes.
(D) Machine-readable glossary.Glossary entries are exportable into a machine-readable format for automated checking of symbol consistency in manuscripts and code documentation.
1.4.4.2 Theorem/algorithm formatting standard
(A) Theorem format.Every theorem includes:
statement,
assumptions (typed and minimal),
conclusion,
proof or proof reference,
applicability notes expressed as formal conditions.
(B) Algorithm format.Every algorithm includes:
typed inputs/outputs,
preconditions,
steps (pseudocode-level),
complexity bounds,
stability/error bounds,
verification tests required for acceptance.
(C) Separation of concerns.Proof obligations and algorithm obligations are separated but linked by traceability IDs. Algorithms are not permitted to introduce assumptions absent from theorems that justify them.
(D) Deterministic pseudocode.Pseudocode must specify deterministic ordering for loops over sets/maps, deterministic tie-breaking, and deterministic floating behaviors where needed.
1.4.4.3 Code-to-math traceability rules
(A) Type correspondence.Every code-level object must correspond to a typed mathematical object (space, operator, state, transform). Type mismatches invalidate correctness claims.
(B) Invariant correspondence.Every invariant used in admissibility corridors must have a code implementation and a test verifying agreement with the mathematical definition within declared tolerances.
(C) Certificate correspondence.Every certificate field corresponds to a theorem, lemma, or verification test. Certificates must be sufficient to reproduce and validate claims without external implicit knowledge.
(D) Change impact analysis.Any code change that affects a mathematical definition, invariant, constraint, or transform semantics requires:
version bump,
regeneration of affected certificates, and
rerun of dependent verification suites.
1.4.4.4 External extraction guide format
(A) Extraction units.An extraction unit is a self-contained package consisting of:
definitions used,
theorem statements used,
algorithm used,
verification tests and thresholds,
serialized artifacts and hashes.
(B) Dependency graph.Each extraction unit includes a dependency graph of required prior units. Dependencies are identified by stable IDs and hashes.
(C) Reproduction checklist.Each extraction unit includes a reproduction checklist specifying environment, seeds, datasets, and expected outputs (hashes).
(D) Publication-ready bundles.Bundles include manuscript excerpts, code snapshots, datasets, and certificates under a single manifest. The manifest is signed and is the sole authoritative reference for reproduction.
Chapter 1 — Dimensional Ontology & Formal Preliminaries
Chapter 2 — Holographic State Space ((H^4)): Constraints, Gauge, Reconstruction
2.1 Square — ((H^4)) as a Constrained Subspace
2.1.1 Ambient space ((\mathfrak{H}))
2.1.1.1 Domain ((\Omega\times\mathcal{P})) specification
(A) Spatial domain (\Omega).Let (\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^3) be a measurable set equipped with the Lebesgue (\sigma)-algebra completed under Lebesgue measure (d^3x) restricted to (\Omega). Unless otherwise stated, (\Omega) is assumed to be a bounded Lipschitz domain when boundary traces, boundary fluxes, and Green-type identities are invoked; otherwise (\Omega) may be unbounded, with growth/decay conditions specified when needed.
(B) Parameter domain (\mathcal{P}).Let ((\mathcal{P},\Sigma_{\mathcal{P}},\mu)) be a (\sigma)-finite measure space. The parameter (p\in\mathcal{P}) is a structural index encoding any declared auxiliary degrees of freedom (e.g., viewpoint, phase, frequency band, scale, lens). No physical interpretation is assumed unless a model embedding is declared; (\mathcal{P}) is treated as a measurable index set with a reference measure (\mu).
(C) Product structure and measurability.Let (\Sigma_{\Omega\times\mathcal{P}}:=\Sigma_\Omega\otimes\Sigma_{\mathcal{P}}) denote the product (\sigma)-algebra and let (d^3x,d\mu(p)) denote the product measure (m_\Omega\otimes\mu). A holographic state is a measurable map[H:\Omega\times\mathcal{P}\to\mathbb{C},]measurable with respect to (\Sigma_{\Omega\times\mathcal{P}}) and the Borel (\sigma)-algebra on (\mathbb{C}).
(D) Fiber structure and slices.For each (p\in\mathcal{P}), define the spatial slice (H_p:=H(\cdot,p)) as an element of a function space on (\Omega) whenever (p\mapsto H(\cdot,p)) is measurable in the Bochner sense. For each (x\in\Omega), define the parameter slice (H^x:=H(x,\cdot)) as an element of a function space on (\mathcal{P}) when integrability holds. Slice operations are used only under explicit integrability/regularity assumptions.
2.1.1.2 Inner products and norms
(A) Base Hilbert space.Define the ambient state space[\mathfrak{H}:=L^2(\Omega\times\mathcal{P},,d^3x,d\mu(p)).]The inner product is[\langle H_1,H_2\rangle_{\mathfrak{H}}:=\int_{\Omega\times\mathcal{P}} H_1(x,p),\overline{H_2(x,p)},d^3x,d\mu(p),]and the induced norm is (|H|{\mathfrak{H}}:=\sqrt{\langle H,H\rangle{\mathfrak{H}}}).
(B) Mixed norms (anisotropic control).When separate control over (\Omega) and (\mathcal{P}) is required, mixed norms are defined by[|H|{L^p\Omega L^q_{\mathcal{P}}}:=\left(\int_{\Omega}\left(\int_{\mathcal{P}} |H(x,p)|^{q},d\mu(p)\right)^{p/q},d^3x\right)^{1/p},]with standard modifications when (p=\infty) or (q=\infty). Mixed norms provide explicit regularity and stability budgets by separating spatial and parameter contributions.
(C) Sobolev-type norms.When differential structure is declared on (\Omega) and/or (\mathcal{P}), define Sobolev norms:
spatial Sobolev regularity:[|H|{H^s_x L^2_p}^2:=\sum{|\alpha|\le s}\int_{\Omega\times\mathcal{P}} |\partial_x^\alpha H(x,p)|^2,d^3x,d\mu(p),]
parameter Sobolev regularity (when (\mathcal{P}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^k) with Lebesgue/Haar structure):[|H|{L^2_x H^t_p}^2:=\sum{|\beta|\le t}\int_{\Omega\times\mathcal{P}} |\partial_p^\beta H(x,p)|^2,d^3x,d\mu(p),]
combined regularity:[|H|{H^{s,t}}:=|H|{H^s_x L^2_p}+|H|_{L^2_x H^t_p}.]All derivative symbols (\partial_x^\alpha), (\partial_p^\beta) are used only when the corresponding differentiable structure is declared.
(D) Weighted norms.For a measurable weight (w:\Omega\times\mathcal{P}\to(0,\infty)) with (w, w^{-1}) locally essentially bounded (or other declared conditions ensuring equivalence of norms), define[|H|{L^2(w)}^2:=\int{\Omega\times\mathcal{P}} w(x,p),|H(x,p)|^2,d^3x,d\mu(p).]Weighted norms encode anisotropic importance, multi-scale emphasis, or engineered corridor weights.
2.1.1.3 Regularity ladders for states
(A) Ladder of admissible state classes.States are classified by a regularity ladder that is explicit and monotone:[C^\infty(\Omega\times\mathcal{P})\ \subset\ H^{s,t}(\Omega\times\mathcal{P})\ \subset\ \mathfrak{H}\ \subset\ \mathcal{D}'(\Omega\times\mathcal{P}),]with inclusion interpreted as continuous embeddings when conditions hold. Each construction, theorem, or algorithm declares its minimal required rung.
(B) Spatial interface classes.When states represent sharp boundaries or discontinuities in (\Omega), BV-type structures are used fiberwise:[H\in L^2(\mathcal{P};BV(\Omega))\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad\int_{\mathcal{P}} |H(\cdot,p)|_{BV(\Omega)}^2,d\mu(p)<\infty,]or other explicitly declared BV integrability. BV control is reserved for reconstruction tasks requiring edge preservation.
(C) Compatibility with operators.Every operator (T) acting on states must declare the maximal stable domain (\mathcal{D}(T)) in this ladder. If (T) is defined by derivatives or by unbounded generators, the domain includes additional regularity conditions. If (T) is nonlinear, the domain includes closure under the nonlinear operations used.
(D) Refinement and stability.When discrete approximations (H^{(N,P,\epsilon)}) are used, refinement is valid only if:
the approximations are uniformly bounded in a declared norm, and
the approximation operators are consistent with the chosen ladder, producing convergence in a declared topology (strong (L^2), weak (L^2), or distributional convergence).
2.1.1.4 Distributional extensions
(A) Distributional state space.Let (\mathcal{D}(\Omega\times\mathcal{P})=C_c^\infty(\Omega\times\mathcal{P})) denote test functions. The distribution space (\mathcal{D}'(\Omega\times\mathcal{P})) is the continuous dual of (\mathcal{D}). A distributional hologram is (H\in \mathcal{D}'), acting via (\langle H,\varphi\rangle).
(B) Weak derivatives and weak constraints.Spatial and parameter derivatives are interpreted in the distributional sense. Constraint equations (C(H)=0) are interpreted weakly when (C) contains derivatives: (C(H)=0) means (\langle C(H),\psi\rangle=0) for all (\psi) in a declared test space, with (C) understood as an operator in the distributional sense.
(C) Gelfand triple (rigged Hilbert space).When spectral and unbounded-operator methods are used, a rigged Hilbert space is declared:[\Phi\subset \mathfrak{H}\subset \Phi',]where (\Phi) is a dense nuclear space (e.g., Schwartz-type functions on (\mathbb{R}^3\times\mathbb{R}^k) when applicable) and (\Phi') is its continuous dual. This supports generalized eigenfunction expansions and distributional reconstructions.
(D) Distribution-to-function regularization.Reconstruction pipelines may include a regularization operator (\mathcal{S}_\lambda:\mathcal{D}'\to \mathfrak{H}) (e.g., mollification or spectral cutoff) with explicit parameter (\lambda) and explicit stability/error bounds. Regularization is treated as a transform stage that must satisfy corridor constraints.
2.1.2 Constraint operator ((C))
2.1.2.1 Constraint typing and codomain
(A) Constraint operator as typed map.A constraint operator is a typed map[C:\mathcal{D}(C)\subseteq \mathfrak{H}\to \mathcal{Z},]where (\mathcal{Z}) is a normed vector space of residuals (typically a Hilbert space such as (L^2), a product of Hilbert spaces, or a dual space (H^{-s})). The domain (\mathcal{D}(C)) is declared and includes all regularity conditions required to evaluate (C(H)).
(B) Multi-constraint structure.Constraints are generally assembled as a vector operator:[C(H):=\big(C_1(H),\dots,C_m(H)\big),\qquadC_i:\mathcal{D}(C_i)\to \mathcal{Z}i,]with codomain (\mathcal{Z}:=\mathcal{Z}1\times\cdots\times \mathcal{Z}m) and product norm[|C(H)|{\mathcal{Z}}^2:=\sum{i=1}^m w_i|C_i(H)|{\mathcal{Z}_i}^2,]where weights (w_i>0) are fixed as part of the constraint specification.
(C) Hard vs soft feasibility.Hard feasibility is defined by[H\in H^4 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad C(H)=0.]Soft feasibility is defined by a tolerance (\tau\ge 0) and[H\in H^4_\tau \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad |C(H)|_{\mathcal{Z}}\le \tau,]with (\tau) declared as part of the state type when used.
(D) Constraint regularity and closedness.A constraint operator is required to be either:
bounded linear, or
closed linear (with dense domain), or
continuous nonlinear on its domain,with explicit declarations. These properties ensure that feasibility sets are closed under the chosen topology, enabling stability and convergence results.
2.1.2.2 Physical/structural constraint families
(A) Structural (holographic) constraints.Structural constraints encode the defining holographic property: reconstructability from admissible observations. Structural constraints are not asserted as entropy equalities; they are enforced through operator relations that guarantee stable inversion on (H^4). Canonical structural families include:
Frame-consistency constraints.Let ({\phi_i}{i\in I}\subset \mathfrak{H}) be a (possibly overcomplete) family and define analysis coefficients[a_i(H):=\langle H,\phi_i\rangle{\mathfrak{H}}.]Constraints enforce that (H) lies in the range of a synthesis operator (S) with declared frame bounds (see §2.2.3). Residuals encode deviations from frame-consistency or from declared redundancy relations among coefficients.
Local consistency constraints.Let (\mathcal{N}) be a neighborhood system on (\Omega\times\mathcal{P}). Constraints enforce that local patches agree under overlap operators, expressed as[C_{\mathrm{ov}}(H):={,H|{U}-\mathcal{T}{U\leftarrow V}(H|{V}),}{(U,V)\in\mathcal{E}},]where (\mathcal{T}_{U\leftarrow V}) transports representations between overlapping regions.
Boundary-coded constraints.When holography is boundary-mediated, constraints encode boundary-to-interior consistency:[C_{\partial}(H):=\mathcal{B}(H)-b,]where (\mathcal{B}) is a boundary extraction operator (trace/flux/Dirichlet-to-Neumann mapping) and (b) is the declared boundary code.
(B) Physical constraints (model-conditional).When a physical embedding is declared, constraints may encode PDE residuals, conservation laws, or gauge field conditions. Examples include:[C_{\mathrm{PDE}}(H):= \mathcal{L}(H)-f]for an operator (\mathcal{L}) (elliptic/hyperbolic/parabolic) and source (f). Such constraints require explicit domain regularity and boundary conditions and are treated as model-specific components of (C).
(C) Coding constraints (discrete or coefficient-level).Coding constraints express parity checks and redundancy relations:[C_{\mathrm{code}}(c)=A c]for coefficient vector (c) in a declared basis and a check operator/matrix (A). Continuous versions are implemented by composing coefficient extraction with parity checks.
(D) Compatibility constraints across (\mathcal{P}).When (\mathcal{P}) indexes multiple “views” or “scales,” cross-parameter constraints enforce coherent coupling:[C_{\mathrm{cross}}(H)(x,p,q):=H(x,p)-\mathcal{U}{p\leftarrow q}H(x,q),]with (\mathcal{U}{p\leftarrow q}) a declared transport/warp operator. Cross-parameter constraints are the primary mechanism ensuring that (\mathcal{P})-fibers encode a single pattern rather than unrelated slices.
2.1.2.3 Kernel definition ((H^4=\ker(C)))
(A) Feasible hologram space.Define the holographic state space as the feasibility kernel:[H^4 := \ker(C) := {H\in \mathcal{D}(C)\subseteq \mathfrak{H}\mid C(H)=0}.]When multiple constraints are used, this is the intersection of kernels:[H^4=\bigcap_{i=1}^m \ker(C_i).]
(B) Soft feasible space.When noise, truncation, or measurement disturbance is intrinsic, define the corridor-feasible space:[H^4_\tau := {H\in \mathcal{D}(C)\subseteq\mathfrak{H}\mid |C(H)|{\mathcal{Z}}\le \tau}.]All subsequent reconstruction and admissibility results must declare whether they operate on (H^4) or (H^4\tau).
(C) Tangency and invariance under transforms.A transform (T) is feasibility-preserving if[T(H^4)\subseteq H^4.]For flows generated by (\dot{H}=F(H)), feasibility invariance requires tangency:[H(0)\in H^4\ \Rightarrow\ C(H(t))=0\ \forall t,]with tangency expressed either as (DC(H)\cdot F(H)=0) in differentiable settings or via invariance theorems in weak settings.
(D) Kernel-based pattern semantics.Membership in (H^4) defines the object as a valid holographic encoding: validity is not interpreted semantically but operationally through constraints ensuring reconstructability, consistency, and admissibility under declared transformations.
2.1.2.4 Constraint compatibility conditions
(A) Closedness and well-posedness conditions.To ensure (H^4) is a stable object, constraints satisfy sufficient closedness conditions:
If each (C_i) is bounded linear, then (\ker(C_i)) is closed and (H^4) is closed in (\mathfrak{H}).
If (C) is closed linear with dense domain, then (\ker(C)) is closed in (\mathfrak{H}).
If (C) is continuous on (\mathcal{D}(C)) in a topology (\mathcal{T}), then (\ker(C)) is closed in that topology.
(B) Constraint independence and redundancy.Constraints must declare whether they are independent or redundant. A constraint family is minimal if removal of any component increases (\ker(C)) in a way that violates declared reconstruction or invariants. Redundancy is permitted, but redundancy must be recorded to avoid ill-conditioning in feasibility repair.
(C) Compatibility with gauge actions.A gauge group (\mathcal{G}) is constraint-compatible if[C(g\cdot H)=0\quad\forall H\in H^4,\ \forall g\in\mathcal{G}.]Equivalently, (\mathcal{G}) acts on (H^4). When constraints include gauge fixing, (\mathcal{G}) is reduced accordingly and the remaining gauge is recorded.
(D) Compatibility with reconstruction and observation.Observation operators (\mathcal{O}) and reconstruction operators (R_A) must be compatible with constraints:
(\mathcal{O}) must be well-defined on (H^4) and measurable/continuous under the chosen topology;
(R_A) must map observations into (H^4) or (H^4_\tau) with explicit feasibility residual bounds.These compatibility requirements are part of admissibility for any pipeline involving measurement and reconstruction.
2.1.3 Gauge group ((\mathcal{G}))
2.1.3.1 Gauge actions and invariants
(A) Gauge group definition.A gauge group is a group (\mathcal{G}) acting on (\mathfrak{H}) (and therefore on (H^4) when compatible) via an action map[\cdot:\mathcal{G}\times \mathcal{D}(\cdot)\to \mathfrak{H},\qquad (g,H)\mapsto g\cdot H,]with declared domain (\mathcal{D}(\cdot)\subseteq\mathfrak{H}), satisfying identity and associativity.
(B) Canonical gauge families.Gauge families used in this manuscript include:
global phase gauge: (g_\phi\cdot H := e^{i\phi}H), (\phi\in\mathbb{R}/2\pi\mathbb{Z});
measure-preserving reparameterization gauge on (\mathcal{P}): ((g_\psi\cdot H)(x,p):=H(x,\psi(p))) where (\psi:\mathcal{P}\to\mathcal{P}) is measurable and (\mu)-preserving;
spatial rigid-motion gauge on (\Omega) when (\Omega=\mathbb{R}^3) or when boundary conditions permit: ((g_{R,a}\cdot H)(x,p):=H(R^{-1}(x-a),p));
basis-change gauge in coefficient representations: (H\mapsto U H) where (U) is unitary or frame-preserving on (\mathfrak{H}).
Each gauge family must declare its admissible domains and whether it preserves the (\mathfrak{H})-norm.
(C) Gauge invariants.A functional (\mathcal{I}:\mathfrak{H}\to \mathbb{R}) is gauge-invariant if (\mathcal{I}(g\cdot H)=\mathcal{I}(H)) for all admissible (g). Canonical invariants include:
energy (|H|_{\mathfrak{H}}),
constraint residual (|C(H)|_{\mathcal{Z}}) when constraints are gauge-compatible,
quotient metrics defined by orbit minimization,
observation distributions when observation operators factor through the quotient.
(D) Unitary gauge actions.When the action is unitary on (\mathfrak{H}),[|g\cdot H|{\mathfrak{H}}=|H|{\mathfrak{H}},]and inner products are preserved. Unitary gauge actions ensure stable quotient constructions and simplify canonicalization by decoupling energy from gauge.
2.1.3.2 Pattern equivalence classes
(A) Orbit equivalence.Define gauge-equivalence by orbit membership:[H_1 \sim_{\mathcal{G}} H_2\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad\exists g\in\mathcal{G}: H_2=g\cdot H_1.]The equivalence class ([H]_{\mathcal{G}}:={g\cdot H:\ g\in\mathcal{G}}) is the pattern class represented by (H).
(B) Pattern semantics as quotient object.A pattern is defined as an equivalence class under (\sim_{\mathcal{G}}). All statements asserting uniqueness of reconstructed patterns are interpreted as uniqueness in the quotient space. Any claim of strict uniqueness in (\mathfrak{H}) must explicitly incorporate gauge fixing.
(C) Stabilizers and degeneracy.For (H\in\mathfrak{H}), the stabilizer subgroup[\mathrm{Stab}(H):={g\in\mathcal{G}: g\cdot H = H}]measures intrinsic symmetries of the state. Nontrivial stabilizers induce degeneracies in canonicalization and must be handled by deterministic tie-breaking rules.
(D) Equivalence under combined relations.When almost-everywhere equivalence and gauge equivalence are both relevant, the effective relation is the closure under both:[H_1 \sim H_2\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad\exists g\in\mathcal{G}\text{ such that } H_1 =_{\mathrm{a.e.}} g\cdot H_2.]All quotient objects are understood in this combined sense unless explicitly separated.
2.1.3.3 Canonical representatives (phase fixing)
(A) Canonicalization objective.Canonical representatives are selected to enable deterministic identities, hashing, and reproducible comparison. A canonicalization map (\mathrm{Can}:H^4\to H^4) is required to satisfy:[\mathrm{Can}(H)\sim_{\mathcal{G}} H,\qquad\mathrm{Can}(g\cdot H)=\mathrm{Can}(H),\qquad\mathrm{Can}(\mathrm{Can}(H))=\mathrm{Can}(H).]
(B) Energy normalization.When overall scaling is gauge or otherwise non-semantic, enforce[\mathrm{Can}(H)\ \leftarrow\ \frac{H}{|H|_{\mathfrak{H}}}\quad\text{for }H\ne 0.]If (H=0), the canonical representative is (0), and degeneracy is recorded in serialization.
(C) Phase fixing.For global phase gauge, define an anchor functional (\Phi:H^4\setminus{0}\to \mathbb{R}/2\pi\mathbb{Z}) with deterministic tie-breaking and stability, and set[\mathrm{Can}(H)\ \leftarrow\ e^{-i\Phi(H)}H.]Acceptable anchors include:
argument of a deterministic coefficient (e.g., the lexicographically first coefficient achieving maximal magnitude),
argument of the complex scalar (\int_{\Omega\times\mathcal{P}} H) when nonzero,
argument of a boundary-coded functional when boundary constraints are declared.
(D) Canonical ordering across (\mathcal{P}).When gauge includes reparameterizations of (\mathcal{P}), canonicalization must select a canonical coordinate chart or ordering. This may be realized by:
fixing a reference measure-preserving chart, or
selecting a canonical parametrization by minimizing a deterministic functional over the gauge orbit:[\mathrm{Can}(H)=\arg\min_{g\in\mathcal{G}} \mathcal{J}(g\cdot H),]with (\mathcal{J}) strictly specified and computationally realizable.
2.1.3.4 Quotient space ((\mathcal{PATT}=H^4/\mathcal{G}))
(A) Quotient definition.Define the pattern space as the quotient:[\mathcal{PATT}:=H^4/\mathcal{G}={[H]_{\mathcal{G}}:H\in H^4}.]Elements of (\mathcal{PATT}) are patterns; representatives are holograms.
(B) Quotient topology and measurability.The quotient topology is induced by the canonical projection (\pi:H^4\to \mathcal{PATT}). When a metric is defined on (\mathcal{PATT}) (see §2.1.4), it induces a Borel (\sigma)-algebra. Any probabilistic statements about patterns must use this induced measurable structure.
(C) Quotient metrics and geodesics.If (\mathcal{G}) acts by isometries on ((H^4,d)), then the quotient pseudo-metric[d_{\mathcal{G}}([H_1],[H_2]):=\inf_{g\in\mathcal{G}} d(H_1, g\cdot H_2)]is well-defined and becomes a metric on the quotient when distinct orbits have positive separation. Geodesic or shortest-path notions are then definable in (\mathcal{PATT}) by minimizing path lengths in representative space modulo gauge.
(D) Pattern-level transforms.A transform (T:H^4\to H^4) induces a pattern-level transform (\widetilde{T}:\mathcal{PATT}\to \mathcal{PATT}) if it is gauge-equivariant:[T(g\cdot H)=g'\cdot T(H)\quad\text{for some map }g'\text{ determined by }g.]If (T) is gauge-invariant (commutes with (\mathcal{G})), then (\widetilde{T}([H])=[T(H)]) is well-defined.
2.1.4 Metrics on holograms
2.1.4.1 Energy-norm distances
(A) Ambient energy metric.Define the base distance on (\mathfrak{H}) by[d_{2}(H_1,H_2):=|H_1-H_2|{\mathfrak{H}}.]When higher regularity is required, define Sobolev distances (d{H^{s,t}}(H_1,H_2)=|H_1-H_2|_{H^{s,t}}).
(B) Band-limited energy distances.Let (P_{\le \Lambda}) denote a declared low-pass projection (spatial, parameter, or joint). Define[d_{2,\Lambda}(H_1,H_2):=|P_{\le\Lambda}(H_1-H_2)|_{\mathfrak{H}},]for comparing states at a specified resolved scale, consistent with truncation ladders.
(C) Constraint-aware energy distance.When feasibility is central, define[d_{C}(H_1,H_2):=|H_1-H_2|{\mathfrak{H}}+\alpha|C(H_1)-C(H_2)|{\mathcal{Z}},]with (\alpha>0) fixed. This distance penalizes deviation in both state and constraint residual structure.
(D) Boundary-weighted energy distance.When boundary is central, define a combined interior-boundary distance:[d_{\mathrm{int}+\partial}(H_1,H_2):=|H_1-H_2|{\mathfrak{H}}+\beta|\mathrm{Tr}\Omega(H_1)-\mathrm{Tr}\Omega(H_2)|{L^2(\partial\Omega\times\mathcal{P})},]with (\beta>0) fixed and trace defined under declared regularity.
2.1.4.2 Information-weighted distances
(A) Weighted operator metric.Let (W:\mathcal{D}(W)\subseteq\mathfrak{H}\to\mathfrak{H}) be a positive self-adjoint operator (or bounded positive operator) encoding multi-scale or information emphasis. Define[d_{W}(H_1,H_2):=|W(H_1-H_2)|_{\mathfrak{H}}.]Common choices include fractional differential operators (W=(I-\Delta_x)^{s/2}) or wavelet-weighted diagonal operators in a basis.
(B) Multi-scale composite metric.Let ({P_k}) be projections onto scale bands (e.g., wavelet levels). Define[d_{\mathrm{ms}}^2(H_1,H_2):=\sum_{k} \lambda_k |P_k(H_1-H_2)|_{\mathfrak{H}}^2,]with fixed weights (\lambda_k>0). This distance enforces scale-dependent fidelity and supports corridor budgets on scale-specific drift.
(C) Information functional induced distances.Let (\mathrm{Inv}(H)) include multi-scale information summaries (I_{\epsilon_i}(H)). Define an auxiliary distance[d_{\mathrm{Inv}}(H_1,H_2):=|\mathrm{Inv}(H_1)-\mathrm{Inv}(H_2)|_{\mathsf{w}},]for a declared weighted norm. This does not replace state distances but supplements them in optimization and admissibility.
(D) Fisher-type local metrics (when probabilistic structure is declared).When (H) induces a probability density (\pi_H) by a declared map and parameters (\theta) index a parametric submanifold (H_\theta), a Fisher information metric may be defined on (\theta)-space. Such metrics are only used when the probabilistic embedding is explicitly declared.
2.1.4.3 Gauge-invariant distances
(A) Orbit-minimization distance.Given a base distance (d) on (H^4), define the gauge-invariant pseudo-distance:[d_{\mathcal{G}}(H_1,H_2):=\inf_{g\in\mathcal{G}} d(H_1, g\cdot H_2).]When (d) is (\mathcal{G})-invariant and distinct orbits are separated, (d_{\mathcal{G}}) induces a metric on (\mathcal{PATT}).
(B) Double-orbit distance.When (\mathcal{G}) does not act transitively by isometries, define[d_{\mathcal{G}}^{\pm}(H_1,H_2):=\inf_{g_1,g_2\in\mathcal{G}} d(g_1\cdot H_1, g_2\cdot H_2).]This yields maximal gauge invariance and is appropriate when canonicalization is not uniquely defined.
(C) Canonicalization-induced distance.When (\mathrm{Can}) exists and is stable,[d_{\mathrm{Can}}(H_1,H_2):=d(\mathrm{Can}(H_1),\mathrm{Can}(H_2))]is gauge-invariant by construction and is computationally preferred when it is compatible with stability requirements.
(D) Gauge-invariant distances under constraints.When constraints include gauge fixing, the gauge group is reduced. Distances are always defined with respect to the surviving gauge group; the effective gauge group must be declared as part of the state type to avoid ambiguity.
2.1.4.4 Transport-style metrics (optional)
(A) Induced probability measures.When states are normalized and interpreted as densities by a declared map,[\pi_H(x,p):=\frac{|H(x,p)|^2}{|H|_{\mathfrak{H}}^2},](\pi_H) defines a probability measure on (\Omega\times\mathcal{P}).
(B) Wasserstein transport distance.Let (c((x,p),(x',p'))) be a declared cost on (\Omega\times\mathcal{P}). Define[W_2(\pi_{H_1},\pi_{H_2}):=\left(\inf_{\gamma\in\Gamma(\pi_{H_1},\pi_{H_2})}\int c,d\gamma\right)^{1/2},]where (\Gamma) denotes couplings. This metric compares distributions of energy rather than complex phase structure and is used only when distributional comparison is intended.
(C) Hybrid energy–transport metrics.Define hybrid distances combining phase-sensitive (L^2) differences with transport distances on energy distributions:[d_{\mathrm{hyb}}(H_1,H_2):=|H_1-H_2|{\mathfrak{H}}+\lambda W_2(\pi{H_1},\pi_{H_2}),]with (\lambda\ge 0) declared. Hybrid metrics are used in reconstruction regularization and in robustness analysis.
(D) Gauge compatibility.Transport metrics based on (|H|^2) are invariant under global phase gauge but may not be invariant under other gauge actions (e.g., coordinate warps). Gauge behavior must be declared whenever transport metrics are used.
2.2 Flower — Reconstruction Operators and Holographic Code Structure
2.2.1 Restriction and observation
2.2.1.1 Region restriction ((H|_A))
(A) Measurable restriction.For (A\subseteq\Omega) measurable, define the restriction operator[\mathrm{Res}_A:\mathfrak{H}\to L^2(A\times\mathcal{P}),\qquad (\mathrm{Res}_A H)(x,p):=H(x,p)\ \text{for }(x,p)\in A\times\mathcal{P}.](\mathrm{Res}_A) is a bounded linear operator with (|\mathrm{Res}_A|\le 1).
(B) Complementary restriction and decomposition.Let (A^c:=\Omega\setminus A). Then[H = \mathrm{Ext}A(\mathrm{Res}A H)+\mathrm{Ext}{A^c}(\mathrm{Res}{A^c}H),]where (\mathrm{Ext}) denotes zero-extension back to (\Omega\times\mathcal{P}). This decomposition provides the formal basis for inpainting-style reconstruction.
(C) Restriction under boundary traces.When boundary data is used, restriction includes boundary operators:[\mathrm{Res}{A,\partial}(H):=\big(\mathrm{Res}A H,\ \mathrm{Tr}{\partial A}(H)\big),]with (\mathrm{Tr}{\partial A}) defined under regularity assumptions. Boundary-enhanced restrictions increase identifiability and improve stability.
(D) Restriction as observation primitive.Region restriction is treated as a primitive observation operator. More general observation operators are composed from restriction, sampling, compression, and noise injection.
2.2.1.2 Mask operators and sampling maps
(A) Mask operators.A mask is a measurable function (M:\Omega\times\mathcal{P}\to{0,1}) (or ([0,1]) for soft masks). The masked observation is[\mathcal{M}(H):=M\cdot H.](\mathcal{M}) is bounded linear on (\mathfrak{H}), with (|\mathcal{M}|\le|M|_{L^\infty}).
(B) Sampling maps.A sampling map is an operator (\mathcal{S}:\mathfrak{H}\to \mathbb{C}^n) defined by bounded linear functionals (\ell_i):[\mathcal{S}(H):=(\ell_1(H),\dots,\ell_n(H)).]Canonical sampling functionals include point samples (when meaningful), local averages, inner products with sensing functions, and boundary integrals. Each (\ell_i) must declare the minimal regularity required for evaluation.
(C) Randomized sampling protocols.When sampling is randomized, the sampling distribution is part of the observation type. A sampling protocol is deterministic given a seed and includes:
distribution of sensing functions or sample locations,
tie-breaking and ordering rules,
serialization of the sampled design.
(D) Observation model.A general observation operator takes the form[y = \mathcal{O}(H) + \eta,]where (\mathcal{O}) is a composition of restriction, masking, sampling, and compression, and (\eta) is noise in the observation codomain. (\mathcal{O}) must be typed and stable on the declared state class.
2.2.1.3 Boundary data extraction operators
(A) Trace extraction.Under Sobolev regularity ensuring traces, define the boundary trace operator:[\mathrm{Tr}_\Omega: H^1(\Omega;L^2(\mathcal{P}))\to H^{1/2}(\partial\Omega;L^2(\mathcal{P})),]acting fiberwise in (\mathcal{P}).
(B) Normal derivative and flux extraction.When (H(\cdot,p)) has sufficient regularity, define the normal derivative (\partial_n H) and flux functionals[\mathcal{F}{\partial\Omega}(H):=\int{\partial\Omega\times\mathcal{P}} \alpha(\xi,p),\partial_n H(\xi,p),dS(\xi),d\mu(p),]for declared test weights (\alpha). Flux extraction is central when reconstruction relies on Green identities or conservation constraints.
(C) Boundary integral operators.Define boundary integral operators of the form[(\mathcal{B}H)(\xi,p):=\int_{\partial\Omega} K(\xi,\zeta),H(\zeta,p),dS(\zeta),]with kernel (K) declared and satisfying conditions ensuring boundedness between declared boundary spaces.
(D) Boundary code semantics.Boundary extraction operators are treated as code maps: boundary data constitutes a redundancy-bearing encoding of interior structure. Compatibility with constraints requires that boundary extraction commute with feasibility and that the extracted boundary code be sufficient for stable reconstruction under declared conditions.
2.2.1.4 Observer models as operators
(A) Observer as typed operator.An observer is a tuple[\mathsf{Obs}:=(\mathcal{O},,\mathcal{Y},,\mathcal{N}),]where (\mathcal{O}:H^4\to\mathcal{Y}) is the observation operator, (\mathcal{Y}) is the observation space, and (\mathcal{N}) is the noise model on (\mathcal{Y}). The observer type includes all seeds, sampling designs, and compression settings.
(B) Observer equivalence.Two observers are equivalent if their observation operators are identical as maps (or within a declared tolerance on a declared domain) and their noise models are equivalent under declared metrics. Equivalence enables reproducible comparison of reconstruction results across experiments.
(C) Observer compatibility with gauge.An observer is gauge-invariant if (\mathcal{O}(g\cdot H)=\mathcal{O}(H)). If not gauge-invariant, the induced observation equivalence must be declared to ensure that reconstruction targets patterns ([H]) rather than specific representatives.
(D) Observer sufficiency and identifiability.An observer is sufficient for pattern recovery on a domain (\mathcal{D}\subseteq H^4) if (\mathcal{O}) is injective on (\mathcal{D}) modulo gauge, i.e.,[\mathcal{O}(H_1)=\mathcal{O}(H_2)\ \Rightarrow\ H_1\sim_{\mathcal{G}} H_2,]with stability conditions declared in §2.2.2–§2.3.
2.2.2 Reconstruction operators ((R_A))
2.2.2.1 Existence conditions for ((R_A))
(A) Reconstruction as left inverse on feasible set.Let (\mathcal{O}_A:H^4\to\mathcal{Y}_A) denote an observation operator restricted to region (A) (masking/sampling). A reconstruction operator is a map[R_A:\mathcal{Y}_A\to H^4]such that (R_A\circ \mathcal{O}_A) approximates the identity on (H^4), either exactly (noise-free ideal) or within an envelope:[|R_A(\mathcal{O}A(H)) - H|{\mathfrak{H}}\le \varepsilon(A)\quad \forall H\in\mathcal{D}\subseteq H^4.]
(B) Existence via stable injectivity (bounded inverse).If (\mathcal{O}_A) is bounded linear and there exists a constant (\alpha_A>0) such that[|\mathcal{O}A(H)|{\mathcal{Y}A}\ge \alpha_A |H|{\mathfrak{H}}\quad \forall H\in \mathcal{M},]for a subspace (\mathcal{M}\subseteq H^4) (or modulo gauge after fixing), then (\mathcal{O}_A) has a bounded left inverse on (\mathcal{M}), and a stable reconstruction exists on (\mathcal{M}).
(C) Existence via frame bounds.If (\mathcal{O}A) is an analysis operator (H\mapsto (\langle H,\phi_i\rangle)i) forming a frame for (H^4) (or a relevant subspace), i.e., there exist (0<A\le B<\infty) such that[A|H|{\mathfrak{H}}^2 \le \sum{i\in I_A} |\langle H,\phi_i\rangle|^2 \le B|H|_{\mathfrak{H}}^2,]then a bounded reconstruction exists via the dual frame.
(D) Existence under variational well-posedness.If reconstruction is defined as the unique minimizer of a strictly convex functional (\mathcal{J}A(\cdot;y)) over (H^4) (or (H^4\tau)), and (\mathcal{J}_A) is coercive and lower semicontinuous in the declared topology, then a reconstruction operator exists as the solution map (y\mapsto \arg\min \mathcal{J}_A(\cdot;y)), with stability following from strong convexity or appropriate regularity conditions.
2.2.2.2 Explicit constructions (Green/frame/wavelet)
(A) Green-type reconstruction (boundary-to-interior).Assume a model embedding yields an elliptic operator (\mathcal{L}) acting on spatial slices (H(\cdot,p)) with declared boundary conditions. Green identities yield representations of interior values in terms of boundary traces and sources. Reconstruction then takes the form:[H(x,p)=\int_{\partial\Omega} \Big(G(x,\xi),\partial_n H(\xi,p)-\partial_n G(x,\xi),H(\xi,p)\Big),dS(\xi)+\int_{\Omega} G(x,\xi),f(\xi,p),d^3\xi,]with (G) a Green’s function for (\mathcal{L}). This defines (R_A) when the observer provides boundary data and when sources are known or constrained.
(B) Frame reconstruction.Let (\Phi_A={\phi_i}{i\in I_A}) be a frame for the relevant feasible subspace with analysis operator (\mathcal{O}A(H)=(\langle H,\phi_i\rangle)i). The frame operator is[S_A(H):=\sum{i\in I_A} \langle H,\phi_i\rangle,\phi_i,]bounded and invertible on the closed span. The canonical reconstruction is[R_A(a):=S_A^{-1}\left(\sum{i\in I_A} a_i,\phi_i\right),]or equivalently[H=\sum{i\in I_A} \langle H,\phi_i\rangle,\widetilde{\phi}_i,]with ({\widetilde{\phi}_i}) the canonical dual frame. Stability constants are determined by frame bounds.
(C) Wavelet/multiresolution reconstruction.Let ({W_j}) denote multiresolution projections and let missing data correspond to masks (M_A). Reconstruction is posed as:[\min_{H\in H^4_\tau}\ |M_A(H)-y|{\mathcal{Y}}^2 + \lambda\sum{j} \omega_j |W_j(H)|_1,]where (\ell^1) penalties enforce sparsity in a wavelet basis and (\omega_j) encode scale weights. This yields stable inpainting under standard compressed sensing-type conditions and produces scale-aware reconstruction envelopes.
(D) Hybrid constructions.Hybrid reconstructions combine boundary Green terms, frame-based coefficient recovery, and wavelet regularization:
boundary data seeds a coarse reconstruction,
frame coefficients fill midbands,
wavelet regularization refines details while maintaining constraints.Hybrid constructions are admissible only if each stage is typed, stable, and compatible with feasibility repair.
2.2.2.3 Stability of reconstruction
(A) Lipschitz stability.A reconstruction (R_A) is stable on (\mathcal{Y}A) if there exists (L_A<\infty) such that[|R_A(y_1)-R_A(y_2)|{\mathfrak{H}}\le L_A|y_1-y_2|_{\mathcal{Y}_A}.]The constant (L_A) is an explicit stability certificate component and is a primary contributor to noise amplification bounds.
(B) Stability under noise and model error.Given observation (y=\mathcal{O}A(H)+\eta), stability implies[|R_A(y)-H|{\mathfrak{H}}\le|R_A(\mathcal{O}A(H)) - H|{\mathfrak{H}} + L_A|\eta|_{\mathcal{Y}_A}.]The first term is the intrinsic reconstruction envelope (\varepsilon(A)) (noise-free mismatch), the second is noise amplification. Additional terms appear under truncation/regularization and must be explicitly bounded.
(C) Stability with gauge.When recovery is defined only up to gauge, stability is measured in a gauge-invariant metric:[d_{\mathcal{G}}(R_A(y),H)\le \varepsilon(A) + L_A|\eta|.]If canonicalization is used, stability must include canonicalization stability bounds (see §2.2.4.2).
(D) Stability under discretization.A discrete reconstruction (R_{A,N}) is stable if its Lipschitz constant remains bounded uniformly over refinement (or grows under an explicitly bounded law). Stability under discretization is required for convergence claims and for consistent reconstruction envelopes across resolutions.
2.2.2.4 Algorithmic reconstruction pipelines
(A) Reconstruction pipeline as typed program.A reconstruction pipeline is a typed program[\mathcal{P}_A := \mathrm{Can}\circ \mathcal{R}_A\circ \mathcal{O}_A,]where (\mathcal{O}_A) is observation formation, (\mathcal{R}_A) is the inversion solver producing a candidate state, and (\mathrm{Can}) fixes gauge for reproducibility. Each stage declares domain, codomain, norms, and corridor checks.
(B) Constraint-integrated reconstruction.Reconstruction is performed by solving a constrained problem:[\widehat{H}:=\arg\min_{H\in \mathcal{D}}\ \mathcal{L}A(H;y)+\lambda \mathcal{R}(H)\quad \text{s.t.}\quad |C(H)|{\mathcal{Z}}\le\tau,]where (\mathcal{L}_A) is data misfit, (\mathcal{R}) is regularization, (\mathcal{D}) is a declared domain (e.g., (H^{s,t})), and (\tau) is feasibility tolerance.
(C) Standard solver stages.A canonical algorithmic pipeline includes:
preconditioning of observations (normalize units, apply inverse mask weighting),
initialization (H_0) using a coarse model (e.g., zero fill, boundary Green seed, low-pass inversion),
iterative solve using a declared method (projected gradient, proximal splitting, ADMM, or frame inversion),
feasibility projection/repair (\mathcal{P}_{\ker(C)}) or penalty refinement,
canonicalization (\mathrm{Can}) and certificate emission.
(D) Certificate output.A reconstruction program outputs:
reconstructed state (\widehat{H}),
feasibility residual (|C(\widehat{H})|),
reconstruction residual (|\mathcal{O}_A(\widehat{H})-y|),
stability estimates (empirical or analytic),
canonical hash ID for (\widehat{H}),
a reproducibility manifest (seeds, discretization, solver parameters).
2.2.3 Redundancy and coding view
2.2.3.1 Frame conditions and overcompleteness
(A) Frames as holographic redundancy.A family ({\phi_i}{i\in I}\subset \mathfrak{H}) is a frame for a closed subspace (\mathcal{M}\subseteq\mathfrak{H}) if there exist (0<A\le B<\infty) such that[A|H|{\mathfrak{H}}^2 \le \sum_{i\in I} |\langle H,\phi_i\rangle|^2 \le B|H|_{\mathfrak{H}}^2\quad\forall H\in\mathcal{M}.]Overcompleteness ((|I|>\dim \mathcal{M}) in discrete settings) encodes redundancy.
(B) Analysis and synthesis operators.Define the analysis operator (\mathcal{A}:\mathcal{M}\to \ell^2(I)) by ((\mathcal{A}H)i=\langle H,\phi_i\rangle), and the synthesis operator (\mathcal{S}:\ell^2(I)\to\mathcal{M}) by (\mathcal{S}a=\sum{i} a_i\phi_i). The frame operator is (S=\mathcal{S}\mathcal{A}), bounded and invertible on (\mathcal{M}).
(C) Dual frames and reconstruction.The canonical dual frame is (\widetilde{\phi}i:=S^{-1}\phi_i), yielding the reconstruction formula[H = \sum{i\in I} \langle H,\phi_i\rangle \widetilde{\phi}i= \sum{i\in I} \langle H,\widetilde{\phi}_i\rangle \phi_i,]with unconditional convergence in (\mathcal{M}). Stability constants depend on (A,B).
(D) Frame-based identifiability under masks.When observation corresponds to a subset (I_A\subset I) induced by region (A), identifiability is characterized by whether ({\phi_i}_{i\in I_A}) remains a frame for (\mathcal{M}) (or a sufficiently large subspace). Reconstruction envelopes (\varepsilon(A)) depend on the effective frame bounds of (I_A).
2.2.3.2 Parity/check constraints as code structure
(A) Linear code model (discrete).In discrete representations, let (c\in\mathbb{C}^M) denote coefficients. A linear constraint code is defined by a check operator (A\in\mathbb{C}^{r\times M}) and feasibility is:[A c = 0.]This is the discrete analogue of (C(H)=0). The code subspace is (\ker(A)).
(B) Syndrome and residual.The syndrome is (s(c):=Ac). Soft feasibility is (|s(c)|\le \tau). Syndromes provide localized diagnostics and enable error correction by targeting violated checks.
(C) Continuous parity constraints.Continuous constraints may be realized as:[C(H)=\mathcal{K}(H),]where (\mathcal{K}) is an integral operator that detects parity violations across overlaps or across fibers (e.g., comparing redundant encodings). Such (\mathcal{K}) is designed to be bounded/closed and to yield closed kernels.
(D) Code structure and reconstruction.When constraints define a code, reconstruction becomes decoding: given partial observations and an assumed error model, recover a codeword (feasible state) consistent with observations. This provides a unified interpretation of feasibility repair and reconstruction as code decoding.
2.2.3.3 Natural error correction mechanisms
(A) Projection decoding.Given an approximate state (\widetilde{H}\in\mathfrak{H}), a projection decoder is a map (\mathcal{P}: \mathfrak{H}\to H^4) such that[\mathcal{P}(\widetilde{H})\in H^4\quad\text{and}\quad|\mathcal{P}(\widetilde{H})-\widetilde{H}|\text{ is minimized under a declared metric.}]For linear constraints with bounded operators, projection can be realized via orthogonal projection onto (\ker(C)) when the projection is computable.
(B) Proximal decoding.For constraint residual penalties, define[\mathcal{P}\lambda(\widetilde{H}) := \arg\min_H \ |H-\widetilde{H}|{\mathfrak{H}}^2+\lambda|C(H)|{\mathcal{Z}}^2.]As (\lambda\to\infty), (\mathcal{P}\lambda) enforces feasibility when conditions ensure convergence. Proximal decoding is the canonical repair mechanism in nonlinear or non-orthogonal constraint systems.
(C) Syndrome-guided correction (discrete).For discrete codes, error correction uses syndrome (s=Ac). A correction (\Delta c) is sought such that (A(c+\Delta c)=0) and (\Delta c) is minimal under a declared norm. Standard methods include least-norm solutions to (A\Delta c=-s) and sparsity-promoting decoding when errors are sparse.
(D) Built-in redundancy as self-healing.Redundancy constraints ensure that partial data or small perturbations are detectable and correctable within corridor limits. The self-healing property is formalized by the existence of a repair map (\mathcal{P}) with stability:[|\mathcal{P}(\widetilde{H})-\mathcal{P}(\widetilde{H}')|\le L_{\mathcal{P}}|\widetilde{H}-\widetilde{H}'|,]ensuring that repair does not amplify noise uncontrollably.
2.2.3.4 Coding rate vs reconstruction quality
(A) Discrete rate definition.In finite models with coefficient dimension (M) and (r) independent linear constraints, the feasible dimension is (M-r). Define the coding rate:[\mathsf{Rate}:=\frac{M-r}{M}.]Lower rate implies higher redundancy.
(B) Reconstruction threshold trade-off.For observation of (k) coefficients or samples, feasibility restricts the solution set. Higher redundancy (lower rate) reduces the number of observations required for stable reconstruction and increases tolerance to erasures and noise. This is formalized by improved frame bounds or improved restricted injectivity constants.
(C) Conditioning trade-off.Excessive redundancy may increase constraint conditioning, making feasibility repair ill-conditioned. Constraints must be designed to maximize correction power while preserving numerical stability (controlled condition numbers of constraint operators and associated normal equations).
(D) Optimal redundancy principle.An admissible system selects redundancy such that:
reconstruction is stable under declared observation masks, and
feasibility repair is stable under declared noise/truncation budgets, and
computational complexity remains within declared resource corridors.This defines a joint optimization problem over code design and reconstruction performance.
2.2.4 Canonicalization as operator
2.2.4.1 Canon map definition ((\mathrm{Can}))
(A) Canonicalization as projection to a cross-section.Canonicalization selects a deterministic cross-section of the quotient (H^4/\mathcal{G}). Formally, (\mathrm{Can}:H^4\to H^4) satisfies:[\pi(\mathrm{Can}(H))=\pi(H),]where (\pi:H^4\to\mathcal{PATT}) is the quotient map, and (\mathrm{Can}) is constant on orbits.
(B) Canonicalization stages.A standard canonicalization is composed of:
energy normalization (when scaling is gauge),
global phase fixing (when phase is gauge),
parameter canonical ordering (when (\mathcal{P})-reparameterization is gauge),
basis canonicalization (when basis changes are gauge),
constraint-consistent rounding (when discretized).
Each stage must be deterministic and must declare tie-breaking rules.
(C) Compatibility with feasibility.Canonicalization must preserve feasibility:[H\in H^4 \Rightarrow \mathrm{Can}(H)\in H^4,]or, in soft feasibility settings, must preserve residual bounds:[|C(\mathrm{Can}(H))|\le |C(H)| + \rho_{\mathrm{Can}}(H),]with (\rho_{\mathrm{Can}}) bounded by a declared tolerance.
(D) Canonicalization domain.Canonicalization is defined on a declared domain excluding degenerate configurations that cannot be canonically fixed (e.g., zero state for phase anchoring). Degenerate cases must be mapped deterministically to canonical defaults with explicit degeneracy flags.
2.2.4.2 Stability under small perturbations
(A) Local stability definition.Canonicalization is locally stable at (H) if there exist constants (L,\delta>0) such that for (|H-H'|<\delta),[d(\mathrm{Can}(H),\mathrm{Can}(H'))\le L,d(H,H'),]where (d) is a declared base distance. Stability may fail at points with nontrivial stabilizers; such cases must be characterized.
(B) Phase-anchor stability.For phase fixing using a functional (\Phi(H)), stability requires that (\Phi) be locally Lipschitz away from its singular set (where the anchor vanishes or ties occur). Tie sets are handled by deterministic rules, but stability near ties must be characterized by explicit bounds or by declared discontinuity handling.
(C) Stability under discretization.Discrete canonicalization must be stable under precision changes: small coefficient perturbations from rounding must not induce large canonical jumps except in explicitly characterized tie-boundary regions.
(D) Canonicalization stability certificate.A canonicalization certificate includes:
the anchor functional used,
the tie-breaking order,
stability bounds away from ties, and
explicit identification of tie-boundary conditions.
2.2.4.3 Compatibility with reconstruction
(A) Commutation up to gauge.Reconstruction targets patterns; canonicalization must not alter the reconstructed pattern. For observation (y),[\pi(R_A(y))=\pi(\mathrm{Can}(R_A(y))).]Equivalently, (\mathrm{Can}) is a selector applied after reconstruction; it must not be embedded in a way that changes the optimization objective unless explicitly intended.
(B) Canonicalization as post-processing.A standard pipeline defines (\widehat{H}=R_A(y)) and returns (\mathrm{Can}(\widehat{H})). Reconstruction stability bounds must then include canonicalization stability contributions.
(C) Canonicalization in optimization.If canonicalization is embedded into reconstruction (e.g., by enforcing gauge-fixing constraints during optimization), then canonicalization becomes part of (C) and changes feasibility. This is allowed only when explicitly declared; otherwise canonicalization is a post-feasibility operator.
(D) Reconstruction equivalence tests.Compatibility is validated by verifying that reconstructed patterns match under gauge-invariant distances:[d_{\mathcal{G}}(R_A(y),\mathrm{Can}(R_A(y)))=0,]or within declared numerical tolerance for discrete approximations.
2.2.4.4 Canon-hash for reproducible IDs
(A) Canonical serialization.Given a canonical state (\mathrm{Can}(H)), define a deterministic serialization (\mathrm{Ser}(\mathrm{Can}(H))) encoding basis, truncation parameters, coefficients, and required metadata with deterministic ordering and fixed numeric representation.
(B) Canon-hash definition.Define the canonical identifier:[\mathrm{ID}(H):=\mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(\mathrm{Can}(H))).](\mathrm{ID}(H)) is invariant under gauge and under permissible representational variations covered by canonicalization.
(C) Collision resistance and versioning.Hashes are treated as cryptographic digests with negligible collision probability; nevertheless, schema versions are included in serialization to ensure that IDs are not compared across incompatible serialization versions.
(D) ID usage discipline.All experimental reports, certificates, and regression suites reference states and transforms by canonical IDs. Any artifact lacking canonical ID is not admissible for publication-grade claims.
2.3 Cloud — Bounds, Failure Modes, and Guarantees
2.3.1 Reconstruction error envelopes
2.3.1.1 ((\varepsilon(A))) definition and estimation
(A) Envelope definition (operator-theoretic).For an observation operator (\mathcal{O}_A:H^4\to\mathcal{Y}A), define the intrinsic reconstruction envelope as the smallest function (\varepsilon(A)\ge 0) such that there exists (R_A) with[\sup{H\in \mathcal{D}\subseteq H^4}\ |R_A(\mathcal{O}A(H)) - H|{\mathfrak{H}} \le \varepsilon(A).]When exact reconstruction is possible on (\mathcal{D}), (\varepsilon(A)=0).
(B) Envelope definition (variational).If reconstruction is defined by a solver (\mathcal{R}A) (algorithm-specific), define[\varepsilon{\mathcal{R}}(A):=\sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}}\ |\mathcal{R}_A(\mathcal{O}_A(H)) - H|.]This envelope is algorithm-dependent and is used for certificate claims tied to a specific implementation.
(C) Practical estimation protocol.Envelopes are estimated by:
selecting a representative set ({H_j}\subset \mathcal{D}) with coverage claims,
selecting a deterministic family of masks/observers for (A),
computing reconstruction errors (e_j=|R_A(\mathcal{O}_A(H_j))-H_j|),
reporting max and quantiles with confidence statements under declared sampling assumptions.
(D) Envelope monotonicity.When (A\subseteq B\subseteq\Omega) and observation operators are nested (more data in (B) than (A)), envelopes satisfy a monotonicity constraint:[\varepsilon(B)\le \varepsilon(A)]for optimal reconstruction, and approximately for fixed algorithms. Violations indicate implementation instability or incorrect observer nesting.
2.3.1.2 Noise-aware bounds
(A) Noise-aware error decomposition.For (y=\mathcal{O}_A(H)+\eta), reconstruction error decomposes as:[|R_A(y)-H|\le|R_A(\mathcal{O}_A(H))-H| + |R_A(\mathcal{O}_A(H)+\eta)-R_A(\mathcal{O}_A(H))|.]
(B) Lipschitz propagation.If (R_A) is Lipschitz with constant (L_A),[|R_A(y)-H|\le \varepsilon(A)+L_A|\eta|.]This is the primary noise amplification bound. Any stronger bound must specify additional structure (e.g., regularization, statistical assumptions).
(C) Regularized reconstruction bias.If reconstruction uses regularization parameter (\lambda), the bias term (b_\lambda(H)) must be bounded:[|R_{A,\lambda}(\mathcal{O}A(H))-H|\le b\lambda(H),]with (b_\lambda\to 0) as (\lambda\to 0) under declared conditions. Noise-aware bounds then become:[|R_{A,\lambda}(y)-H|\le b_\lambda(H)+L_{A,\lambda}|\eta|.]
(D) Probabilistic noise bounds.If (\eta) is random with known distribution, bounds are stated in probability or expectation. For example, if (\mathbb{E}|\eta|\le\sigma), then[\mathbb{E}|R_A(y)-H| \le \varepsilon(A)+L_A\sigma.]Tail bounds require explicit concentration assumptions.
2.3.1.3 Scale-aware bounds
(A) Resolution-indexed envelopes.At discrete resolution ((N,P)), define (\varepsilon_{N,P}(A)) as the reconstruction envelope in the discrete norm, and relate it to continuous envelopes via truncation error bounds:[\varepsilon(A)\ \le\ \varepsilon_{N,P}(A)+E_{\mathrm{trunc}}(N,P),]with (E_{\mathrm{trunc}}(N,P)\to 0) under refinement when conditions hold.
(B) Bandwise envelope decomposition.Let (P_k) be scale-band projections. Define bandwise error[\varepsilon_k(A):=\sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}} |P_k(R_A(\mathcal{O}_A(H))-H)|.]Scale-aware reconstruction is characterized by the envelope vector ((\varepsilon_k(A))_k), enabling fine-grained corridor control.
(C) Mask geometry dependence.Scale-aware envelopes depend on mask geometry: small masks degrade high-frequency recovery first. This is formalized by the interaction of (A) with sampling operators and with frame bounds on band-limited subspaces.
(D) Scale-aware admissibility.Transforms and reconstructions are admissible only if scale-aware envelopes remain within declared budgets across all required bands. This prevents “hidden” failures where average error is small but critical bands violate reconstructability.
2.3.1.4 Worst-case vs average-case guarantees
(A) Worst-case guarantees.Worst-case guarantees bound (\sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}}) errors. They require uniform stability constants and uniform injectivity/frame bounds on (\mathcal{D}).
(B) Average-case guarantees.Average-case guarantees assume a distribution (\Pi) over (\mathcal{D}\subseteq H^4). Bounds are stated in expectation:[\mathbb{E}_{H\sim\Pi}|R_A(\mathcal{O}_A(H)) - H|\le \bar{\varepsilon}(A),]or in probability with tail bounds. The distribution (\Pi) must be declared and versioned.
(C) Mixed guarantees.Mixed guarantees provide worst-case bounds on a core subset and average-case bounds on the remainder, with explicit partition and coverage claims. Such guarantees are admissible only when the partition is deterministic and externally verifiable.
(D) Guarantee certification.Guarantees are certified by a combination of analytic bounds (frame bounds, Lipschitz constants, conditioning) and verified computational evidence (regression tests, stress tests). The certificate must declare which components are analytic and which are empirical.
2.3.2 Identifiability and ambiguity
2.3.2.1 Non-uniqueness under gauge
(A) Gauge-induced non-uniqueness.If (\mathcal{G}) acts on (H^4), then for any (g\in\mathcal{G}), (H) and (g\cdot H) represent the same pattern. Therefore, no observation map that is gauge-invariant can distinguish between members of the same orbit.
(B) Pattern-level identifiability.Identifiability is stated as uniqueness in (\mathcal{PATT}):[\mathcal{O}_A(H_1)=\mathcal{O}_A(H_2)\ \Rightarrow\ [H_1]=[H_2].]Where strict identifiability in (H^4) is desired, gauge must be fixed by additional constraints or by canonicalization.
(C) Stabilizer-induced ambiguity.If (\mathrm{Stab}(H)) is nontrivial, canonicalization may be non-unique under continuous symmetries. Deterministic tie-breaking yields a representative, but local stability may fail at symmetry points; such points form a declared singular set.
(D) Gauge and reconstruction stability.Stability bounds are formulated using gauge-invariant distances. Any stability claim in (L^2) must either assume gauge is fixed or incorporate orbit minimization.
2.3.2.2 Aliasing and undersampling failures
(A) Aliasing in sampled observations.Sampling operators that do not resolve high-frequency components induce aliasing: distinct states (H_1\neq H_2) yield identical observations. Aliasing is a structural non-identifiability, not a numerical error.
(B) Undersampling in frames.If observation corresponds to a subset of frame coefficients (I_A) that does not form a frame for the target subspace, then the analysis operator is not injective and reconstruction is not uniquely determined, even in the absence of noise.
(C) Mask-induced null spaces.For mask operators (M), any state supported entirely in the masked-out region lies in the kernel of the observation. If constraints do not couple masked and observed regions, reconstruction is impossible.
(D) Phase retrieval and magnitude-only failures.If the observation discards phase information (e.g., observes (|H|) or (|\langle H,\phi_i\rangle|)), additional ambiguity arises. Identifiability then requires additional constraints, diversity of observations, or structural priors. Such regimes must be explicitly declared when used.
2.3.2.3 Counterexample families
(A) Kernel-based counterexamples.If there exists (0\neq \Delta H\in H^4) such that (\mathcal{O}_A(\Delta H)=0), then (H) and (H+\Delta H) are observationally indistinguishable. This establishes non-identifiability on any domain containing (H) and (H+\Delta H).
(B) Band-limited alias counterexamples.If (\mathcal{O}_A) only measures low-pass projections, any high-frequency perturbation in the orthogonal complement is invisible. Such counterexamples exist whenever constraints do not couple the invisible band back to visible bands.
(C) Symmetry-induced counterexamples.If constraints and observations are invariant under a symmetry group larger than the declared gauge group, additional hidden gauge exists and produces non-uniqueness in the quotient. Hidden symmetries must be identified and either fixed or incorporated as gauge.
(D) Discretization-induced counterexamples.Discrete implementations can introduce spurious null spaces (e.g., grid staggering, inconsistent discrete derivatives). Counterexamples are detected when refinement does not reduce reconstruction error or when discrete feasibility admits states not approximating any continuous feasible state.
2.3.2.4 Conditions restoring uniqueness
(A) Injectivity up to gauge.Uniqueness is restored when (\mathcal{O}_A) is injective modulo gauge on the declared domain:[\mathcal{O}_A(H_1)=\mathcal{O}A(H_2)\ \Rightarrow\ H_1\sim{\mathcal{G}} H_2.]This is typically established by frame bounds or by restricted isometry/injectivity conditions.
(B) Constraint coupling conditions.Even with partial observations, uniqueness can hold if constraints strongly couple observed and unobserved regions/bands. Sufficient conditions are expressed as:
unique continuation properties for PDE constraints,
overlap consistency constraints forming a connected constraint graph,
redundancy constraints that propagate information across (\Omega) and (\mathcal{P}).
(C) Diversity of observations.Uniqueness may require multiple observers (\mathcal{O}{A_k}) whose combined map[\mathcal{O}{\mathrm{all}}(H):=\big(\mathcal{O}{A_1}(H),\dots,\mathcal{O}{A_K}(H)\big)]is injective modulo gauge. This is the canonical approach for phase retrieval and for resolving symmetry-induced degeneracies.
(D) Canonical gauge fixing.Uniqueness in (H^4) is restored by augmenting constraints with gauge-fixing conditions (or by canonicalization with stability guarantees). Gauge fixing must preserve feasibility of the intended pattern class and must not introduce ill-conditioning.
2.3.3 Constraint feasibility diagnostics
2.3.3.1 Residual norms and thresholds
(A) Residual definition.Define the constraint residual:[r(H):=|C(H)|{\mathcal{Z}}.]For multi-constraint systems, residual includes componentwise values (r_i(H):=|C_i(H)|{\mathcal{Z}_i}) and the weighted aggregate norm.
(B) Threshold policies.Hard feasibility requires (r(H)=0). Soft feasibility uses tolerance (\tau) and requires (r(H)\le\tau). Thresholds are treated as corridor parameters and must be declared in state types and in certificates.
(C) Residual localization.When (\mathcal{Z}) is a function space over (\Omega\times\mathcal{P}), residual localization is defined by pointwise or regional norms:[r_A(H):=|C(H)|_{L^2(A\times\mathcal{P})},]enabling identification of where constraints are violated and guiding repair transforms.
(D) Residual evolution under transforms.Transforms must provide residual propagation estimates:[r(T(H))\le \alpha_T,r(H)+\beta_T,]with (\alpha_T,\beta_T) declared or empirically estimated. Residual propagation is a required component of admissibility certificates.
2.3.3.2 Constraint conditioning
(A) Linear conditioning.For bounded linear constraints (C), conditioning of feasibility repair is governed by (C) and by the projection operator onto (\ker(C)). In discrete settings, conditioning is related to singular values of the constraint matrix/operator.
(B) Nonlinear conditioning.For nonlinear constraints, local conditioning is characterized by the Fréchet derivative (DC(H)) and its singular spectrum. Poor conditioning indicates that small changes in (H) induce large changes in residuals or vice versa, leading to unstable repair.
(C) Constraint redundancy and ill-conditioning.Redundant constraints can induce near-linear dependence, increasing conditioning and causing numerical instability. Constraint families must declare redundancy structure and provide preconditioning strategies or orthogonalization in residual space.
(D) Conditioning diagnostics.Diagnostics include:
estimates of (|DC(H)|) and (|(DC(H))^\dagger|) where defined,
singular value estimates in discrete models,
observed convergence rates of repair solvers,
sensitivity of repaired state to small perturbations in input.
2.3.3.3 Feasibility repair transforms
(A) Orthogonal projection repair (linear).When (H^4=\ker(C)) is a closed subspace and orthogonal projection (P_{\ker(C)}) is computable or approximable, define repair by[\mathcal{P}(H):=P_{\ker(C)}H.]This yields the unique nearest feasible state in (\mathfrak{H}).
(B) Penalized repair.Define penalized repair:[\mathcal{P}\lambda(H):=\arg\min{U\in\mathcal{D}(C)} |U-H|{\mathfrak{H}}^2+\lambda |C(U)|{\mathcal{Z}}^2.]Under appropriate conditions (coercivity, continuity), (\mathcal{P}_\lambda) exists and is stable; as (\lambda\to\infty), solutions approach feasibility.
(C) Constrained repair with inequality constraints.If feasibility is soft or includes inequality constraints (\mathcal{K}(H)\le 0), repair is formulated as projection onto a convex set when convex, or via KKT-based methods when smooth. Nonconvex repair must declare local optimality and failure modes.
(D) Repair certification.A repair transform must output:
repaired state (\widehat{H}),
pre/post residuals (r(H), r(\widehat{H})),
repair displacement (|\widehat{H}-H|),
solver convergence data,
stability diagnostics,
canonical ID for (\widehat{H}).
2.3.3.4 Certification of feasibility
(A) Feasibility certificate object.A feasibility certificate is a structured artifact containing:
constraint version IDs and parameters,
computed residuals (r_i(H)) and aggregate (r(H)),
threshold (\tau) used,
numerical precision and rounding mode,
discretization parameters,
verification test suite IDs.
(B) Analytic certificates (when available).If feasibility follows from analytic structure (e.g., construction by known basis satisfying constraints), certificates may include a proof witness (e.g., explicit coefficients satisfying parity constraints, or a construction theorem reference).
(C) Empirical certificates (verification-based).When analytic certification is not available, feasibility is certified by verified computation: residual evaluation under deterministic conditions plus regression against gold states.
(D) Certificate validity scope.Certificates are valid only under the exact versions of constraint operators, discretization, and numeric precision indicated. Any change invalidates certificates unless a migration theorem and verified compatibility tests are provided.
2.3.4 Existence and compactness results
2.3.4.1 Closedness of ((\ker(C)))
(A) Bounded linear constraints.If (C:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathcal{Z}) is bounded linear, then (\ker(C)) is a closed subspace of (\mathfrak{H}). Consequently, orthogonal projections onto (\ker(C)) exist in (\mathfrak{H}).
(B) Closed linear constraints.If (C) is closed linear with dense domain, (\ker(C)) is closed in (\mathfrak{H}) in the graph topology and in the (\mathfrak{H})-topology when (\ker(C)\subseteq\mathfrak{H}) is interpreted as ({H\in\mathcal{D}(C):C(H)=0}).
(C) Continuous nonlinear constraints.If (C) is continuous in the (\mathfrak{H})-topology on its domain and (\mathcal{D}(C)) is closed, then (\ker(C)) is closed in (\mathcal{D}(C)). If (\mathcal{D}(C)=\mathfrak{H}), then (\ker(C)) is closed in (\mathfrak{H}).
(D) Consequences for reconstruction and repair.Closedness guarantees stability under limits: sequences of feasible states converging in the declared topology remain feasible. This supports convergence of iterative reconstruction and feasibility repair under standard boundedness assumptions.
2.3.4.2 Compact embeddings (when needed)
(A) Compactness via Sobolev regularity.If a bounded domain (\Omega) and Sobolev regularity (H^{s,t}) are declared with (s>0), then embeddings into (L^2) may be compact in the spatial variable (subject to standard conditions). Compactness yields convergence subsequences for bounded sequences in higher regularity norms.
(B) BV compactness.If states satisfy BV bounds fiberwise and are uniformly bounded in (L^1), BV compactness yields strong (L^1) convergence subsequences. This supports edge-preserving reconstruction convergence claims.
(C) Use in existence proofs.Compactness is used to establish existence of minimizers in reconstruction variational problems and to pass limits through nonlinear constraints when weak convergence is insufficient.
(D) Declared compactness regime.Any theorem invoking compactness must declare:
the exact function spaces and domains,
the topology of convergence (weak/strong),
the embedded space,
the operator continuity required to pass constraints and reconstruction relations to the limit.
2.3.4.3 Convergence of approximate solutions
(A) Approximate feasibility.Let (H_n\in\mathcal{D}(C)) satisfy (|C(H_n)|_{\mathcal{Z}}\to 0) and ({H_n}) be bounded in a space with compact embedding into (\mathfrak{H}). Then there exists a subsequence converging strongly in (\mathfrak{H}) to some (H\in\mathfrak{H}).
(B) Passing constraints to the limit.If (C) is continuous (or closed with appropriate graph convergence), then (C(H)=0), hence (H\in H^4). This yields convergence of approximate feasible states to feasible states.
(C) Convergence of reconstructions.For a family of reconstructions (\widehat{H}n=R{A,n}(y_n)) at increasing resolution with consistent observers and bounded stability constants, convergence follows by combining:
stability bounds for (R_{A,n}),
convergence of observations (y_n),
compactness of the reconstructed sequence,
limit feasibility via constraint continuity/closedness.
(D) Convergence rates.Rates require additional assumptions (smoothness, source conditions, regularization regime). Any rate claim must specify the exact regularity assumptions and the norm in which the rate holds.
2.3.4.4 Stability under limits
(A) Feasibility stability.If (H_n\in H^4) and (H_n\to H) in (\mathfrak{H}), then (H\in H^4) when (H^4) is closed in (\mathfrak{H}). This supports limit stability of feasible approximations.
(B) Gauge stability.If (H_n\sim_{\mathcal{G}}H_n') with witnesses (g_n) and (H_n\to H), (H_n'\to H'), then gauge stability depends on continuity of the action and compactness/tightness of ({g_n}). Stability under gauge requires explicit assumptions on (\mathcal{G}) and its topology.
(C) Metric stability.If distances are defined by bounded operators (energy norms, weighted norms), convergence in (\mathfrak{H}) implies convergence in the metric. If distances involve orbit minimization, lower semicontinuity of the infimum is required and must be declared or verified.
(D) Reconstruction stability under refinement.Stability under limits is validated by refinement tests: reconstructed states should converge under increasing resolution and decreasing noise. Failure indicates inconsistency in discretization, constraint implementation, or solver instability.
2.4 Fractal — Finite Models of ((H^4))
2.4.1 Truncated holograms ((H^{(N,P)}))
2.4.1.1 Grid and basis discretizations
(A) Discrete domain discretization.Let (\Omega_N) be a discrete grid or mesh approximation of (\Omega) with resolution parameter (N), and let (\mathcal{P}_P) be a discrete approximation of (\mathcal{P}) with resolution parameter (P) (finite set with weights approximating (\mu)). A discrete hologram is an array[H^{(N,P)}\in \mathbb{C}^{N_x\times N_y\times N_z \times P},]with deterministic indexing.
(B) Basis discretization.Alternatively, represent (H) in a basis (\mathcal{B}{N,P}={\psi_j}{j=1}^M) (orthonormal or frame) and store coefficients (c\in\mathbb{C}^M) such that[H^{(N,P)} \equiv \sum_{j=1}^M c_j \psi_j.]The basis, its parameters, and coefficient ordering are part of the state type.
(C) Quadrature and inner products.Discrete inner products approximate (\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathfrak{H}}) by quadrature weights (w_{x}) and (w_{p}):[\langle H_1,H_2\rangle_{N,P} := \sum_{x\in\Omega_N}\sum_{p\in\mathcal{P}_P} H_1(x,p)\overline{H_2(x,p)},w_x,w_p.]Quadrature rules must be declared and must converge under refinement.
(D) Discrete regularity proxies.Discrete analogues of Sobolev norms are defined using discrete derivatives or spectral multipliers. Consistency conditions must be declared to ensure discrete norms converge to continuous norms under refinement.
2.4.1.2 Spectral bandlimits and cutoffs
(A) Spatial bandlimits.Let (P_{\le \Lambda_x}) denote a spatial low-pass operator (FFT-based or eigenfunction-based). Band-limited holograms satisfy (H=P_{\le \Lambda_x}H). Discrete implementations enforce bandlimits by spectral truncation.
(B) Parameter bandlimits.When (\mathcal{P}) has spectral structure (e.g., (\mathcal{P}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^k) or a compact group), define parameter low-pass (P_{\le \Lambda_p}) analogously.
(C) Joint bandlimits.Joint bandlimits (P_{\le \Lambda}) act on (\Omega\times\mathcal{P}) and define the effective finite-dimensional subspace. Reconstruction guarantees often depend on bandlimits, as they determine the number of degrees of freedom.
(D) Cutoff-induced approximation error.Cutoffs introduce truncation error:[E_{\mathrm{cut}}:=|H-P_{\le \Lambda}H|.]All finite-model claims must include explicit dependence on (E_{\mathrm{cut}}) and specify how it decreases under refinement.
2.4.1.3 Memory layouts and sparse storage
(A) Dense layout.Dense arrays store all coefficients. Layout is deterministic (row-major or column-major) and explicitly declared. Dense layouts are used for reference correctness implementations.
(B) Sparse layouts.Sparse storage is used when coefficients are compressible. Sparse formats store (index,value) pairs sorted deterministically. Thresholding rules must be deterministic and included in the state type.
(C) Block-sparse and multiresolution layouts.Block sparsity groups coefficients by spatial blocks or by wavelet levels. Multiresolution layouts store coarse coefficients plus sparse fine residuals. Such layouts support reconstruction pipelines and scale-aware constraints.
(D) Streaming layouts.Streaming storage partitions holograms into deterministic chunks, supporting online reconstruction and verification. Chunk boundaries and ordering are part of the artifact identity and must remain stable under reproducibility requirements.
2.4.1.4 Precision and numerical stability
(A) Precision typing.Finite states are typed by numeric precision (float32/float64) and complex representation. Mixed precision is permitted only when explicitly declared and bounded by error budgets.
(B) Stable reductions.Inner products, norms, and invariant computations require stable deterministic reduction ordering. Parallel reductions must enforce determinism to preserve canonical hashes and regression consistency.
(C) Condition monitoring.All solvers used in reconstruction and projection must monitor conditioning of matrices/operators (explicitly or via estimates) and must enforce corridor guards (reject or regularize when conditioning exceeds thresholds).
(D) Numeric error envelopes.Finite artifacts carry numeric error envelopes tracking rounding and truncation contributions. Envelopes propagate through transforms and reconstructions via declared conservative rules and are included in certificates.
2.4.2 Discrete constraint enforcement
2.4.2.1 Discrete ((C_N)) and consistency
(A) Discrete constraint operator.Define a discrete constraint operator[C_{N,P}:\mathfrak{H}^{(N,P)}\to \mathcal{Z}^{(N,P)}]that approximates (C). The discrete feasible space is (\ker(C_{N,P})) (hard) or (|C_{N,P}(H)|\le\tau_{N,P}) (soft).
(B) Consistency requirement.Consistency requires that for sufficiently regular continuous states (H\in\mathcal{D}(C)),[|C_{N,P}(\Pi_{N,P}H)-\Pi_{N,P}^\mathcal{Z}(C(H))|{\mathcal{Z}^{(N,P)}} \to 0\quad\text{as }N,P\to\infty,]where (\Pi{N,P}) and (\Pi_{N,P}^\mathcal{Z}) are the declared discretization maps for states and residuals.
(C) Stability requirement.Stability requires uniform boundedness or controlled growth of operator norms (|C_{N,P}|) and of feasibility repair operators derived from (C_{N,P}). Uncontrolled growth invalidates convergence of feasible discrete approximations.
(D) Discrete gauge compatibility.If gauge actions are implemented discretely, discrete constraints must be gauge-compatible in the discrete sense. Any mismatch between continuous gauge and discrete gauge must be declared and treated as model error.
2.4.2.2 Projection onto ((\ker(C_N)))
(A) Linear constraints: orthogonal projection.For linear constraints (C_{N,P}), define the orthogonal projection onto (\ker(C_{N,P})) with respect to the discrete inner product. When explicit projection is not feasible, define an equivalent projection via solving a normal equation or via QR/SVD in a stable manner.
(B) Projection via least squares.Projection can be computed by minimizing (|U-H|^2) subject to (C_{N,P}(U)=0). For linear constraints (A c=0), projection is computed via:[\widehat{c} = c - A^\ast(AA^\ast)^\dagger (Ac),]under declared conditioning constraints, or via stable factorization methods.
(C) Nonlinear constraints: iterative projection.For nonlinear (C_{N,P}), projection is computed iteratively by solving[\min_U |U-H|^2 + \lambda|C_{N,P}(U)|^2,]or by sequential linearization. Convergence requires explicit conditions (local regularity, Lipschitz derivatives, step control) and is certified empirically or analytically as available.
(D) Projection certificates.Projection outputs include residual reduction, displacement norms, convergence traces, conditioning estimates, and a canonical ID for the projected state.
2.4.2.3 Penalty vs hard projection methods
(A) Hard projection methods.Hard projection enforces exact feasibility at each step. It provides strict constraint satisfaction but can be numerically expensive or ill-conditioned if constraints are poorly conditioned or if feasible sets are complex.
(B) Penalty methods.Penalty methods enforce feasibility approximately by adding (\lambda|C_{N,P}(H)|^2) to objectives. They are computationally flexible and can be stabilized by regularization but introduce feasibility bias controlled by (\lambda).
(C) Augmented Lagrangian methods.Augmented Lagrangian methods blend hard and penalty methods, maintaining multipliers and penalties to improve conditioning and enforce constraints more accurately than pure penalties.
