They are preferred when constraints are nonlinear or when hard projection is unstable.
(D) Selection doctrine.Method selection is dictated by:
conditioning of constraints,
stability under perturbations and refinement,
computational resource constraints, and
certificate requirements (hard feasibility vs corridor feasibility).
2.4.2.4 Convergence ((C_N\to C))
(A) Operator convergence regime.Convergence is stated in a declared operator topology (strong operator, graph convergence, or consistency under discretization maps). The selected regime must support passing feasibility to limits.
(B) Feasible set convergence.Discrete feasible sets should approximate continuous feasible sets:[\Pi_{N,P}(H^4)\subseteq H^4_{N,P}\quad\text{approximately, and}\quad\text{any bounded sequence }H_{N,P}\in H^4_{N,P}\text{ has limit in }H^4,]under declared assumptions and compactness.
(C) Convergence of projections and repairs.Projection/repair operators (\mathcal{P}{N,P}) converge to (\mathcal{P}) if for sufficiently regular (H),[|\Pi{N,P}\mathcal{P}(H) - \mathcal{P}{N,P}\Pi{N,P}(H)|\to 0.]This ensures that discrete feasibility repair approximates continuous feasibility repair.
(D) Convergence certification.Convergence is certified by refinement studies on gold states and by verifying that residuals, invariants, and reconstruction errors converge under controlled refinement schedules.
2.4.3 Discrete reconstruction tests
2.4.3.1 Mask sampling protocols
(A) Deterministic mask families.Mask families ({M_j}) are generated deterministically from seeds and include coverage across:
contiguous regions (A),
random masks with specified sparsity,
structured masks (stripes, blocks, multiscale omissions),
boundary-only observations.
(B) Protocol serialization.Each mask is serialized by its seed, generation rule, and explicit representation. Masks are versioned and hashed for reproducibility.
(C) Coverage requirements.Protocols declare coverage requirements: percentage of masked volume, connectedness, boundary coverage, and scale distribution. Coverage is treated as part of the guarantee scope.
(D) Mask–constraint interaction tests.Protocols include tests verifying that constraints couple observed and unobserved regions sufficiently; masks that isolate unconstrained subspaces are included as negative controls to validate failure detection.
2.4.3.2 Reconstruction solver choices
(A) Linear least squares solvers.When reconstruction is linear, solvers include QR, SVD, conjugate gradients on normal equations with preconditioning, and iterative refinement. Solver selection must satisfy conditioning requirements and determinism requirements.
(B) Convex optimization solvers.For convex objectives (e.g., (\ell^2) misfit + (\ell^1) regularization), solvers include proximal gradient, FISTA, ADMM, and primal–dual methods. Each solver must declare stopping criteria that yield certificate-compatible bounds.
(C) Nonconvex solvers.Nonconvex reconstruction (e.g., phase retrieval or nonlinear constraints) uses iterative methods with explicit initialization strategies and convergence diagnostics. Nonconvex solvers must declare local-optimality certification or empirical stability claims with explicit scope.
(D) Solver determinism.Solvers must be deterministic given seeds and must avoid nondeterministic parallel reduction ordering unless controlled. Deterministic behavior is a prerequisite for canonical IDs and regression tests.
2.4.3.3 Error reporting standards
(A) Error metrics.Error reports must include:
state error (|H-\widehat{H}|) in declared norms,
gauge-invariant error (d_{\mathcal{G}}(H,\widehat{H})) where relevant,
reconstruction residual (|\mathcal{O}_A(\widehat{H})-y|),
constraint residual (|C_{N,P}(\widehat{H})|),
invariant drift (|\mathrm{Inv}(\widehat{H})-\mathrm{Inv}(H)|) when (H) known.
(B) Scale-aware reporting.Reports include bandwise errors by projections (P_k) and summarize worst-band, mean-band, and critical-band errors. Scale-aware reporting is mandatory when multi-scale invariants are part of admissibility corridors.
(C) Statistical reporting.When tests are randomized, reports include sample counts, seeds, confidence intervals or quantiles, and explicit definitions of reported statistics. Randomness sources must be fully reproducible.
(D) Certificate linkage.Every report references artifact IDs, solver IDs, mask IDs, constraint IDs, basis/truncation parameters, and numeric precision. Reports without complete linkage are invalid for manuscript-grade claims.
2.4.3.4 Regression test suite design
(A) Gold-state suite.Define a set of gold states ({H_j}\subset H^4_{N,P}) with fixed canonical IDs and certificates. Gold states cover diverse regimes: smooth, sparse, multiscale, boundary-coded, and near-degenerate.
(B) Transform and observer suite.Define a suite of observers and transforms used to generate test observations from gold states. Observers include masks and sampling; transforms include known admissible perturbations.
(C) Pass/fail thresholds.Regression pass/fail criteria are based on:
maximum reconstruction error thresholds,
maximum constraint residual thresholds,
maximum invariant drift thresholds,
determinism checks (identical outputs and hashes).
(D) Continuous integration enforcement.Regression tests are run under pinned environments and deterministic settings. Any failure requires either a justified version increment with migration proofs or a correction restoring prior guarantees.
2.4.4 Reference implementations
2.4.4.1 Minimal kernel library spec
(A) Core data types.A minimal implementation defines:
HologramState: stores coefficients or dense arrays, basis metadata, truncation parameters, precision metadata, canonical ID fields;
ConstraintOperator: evaluates (C_{N,P}), provides residual norms, conditioning diagnostics;
GaugeAction: applies gauge transformations and canonicalization;
Observer: applies (\mathcal{O}_A), stores mask/sampling design;
Reconstructor: implements (R_A) with solver parameters;
Certificate: structured report of residuals, errors, IDs, seeds.
(B) Operator interfaces.Every operator implements:
typed domain() and codomain() descriptors,
deterministic serialize() method,
apply(state) with declared precision semantics,
optional adjoint() where applicable,
stability_estimate() where required.
(C) Determinism interface.All randomized operations accept an explicit seed and emit a deterministic design artifact with its own ID. No operation is permitted to sample without recording its seed lineage.
(D) Verification integration.The library includes a verification module implementing constraint tests, reconstruction tests, invariant drift tests, and property-based tests. Verification outputs are serialized and hashed.
2.4.4.2 Deterministic demo cases
(A) Frame-coded hologram demo.A demo defines a finite frame, constructs a feasible hologram satisfying frame-consistency constraints, applies a mask observer, reconstructs via dual frame inversion, and verifies exact or bounded reconstruction error.
(B) Boundary-coded Green demo (model-conditional).A demo defines a bounded Lipschitz domain, a declared elliptic operator with known Green’s function on a simple geometry, extracts boundary traces as observations, reconstructs interior, and verifies residual and reconstruction bounds.
(C) Wavelet inpainting demo.A demo constructs a multiscale-sparse hologram, applies structured masks, reconstructs via proximal methods with feasibility repair, and reports bandwise error convergence under refinement.
(D) Gauge canonicalization demo.A demo applies a sequence of gauge actions to a feasible state, demonstrates canonicalization invariance, and validates that canonical IDs match across gauge-equivalent representatives.
2.4.4.3 Performance benchmarks
(A) Complexity metrics.Benchmarks report runtime, memory usage, iteration counts, and conditioning statistics. Complexity is measured as a function of ((N,P)), mask sparsity, and constraint complexity.
(B) Scaling laws.Benchmarks include scaling curves:
reconstruction time vs (N) (spatial resolution),
reconstruction time vs (P) (parameter resolution),
constraint evaluation time vs constraint count,
feasibility repair time vs conditioning.
(C) Accuracy–cost trade-offs.Benchmarks report reconstruction error and residuals versus runtime and memory, providing Pareto frontiers under different solver choices and regularization regimes.
(D) Deterministic benchmarking protocol.Benchmark runs are deterministic and fully specified by environment IDs, seeds, and artifact IDs. Any nondeterminism invalidates benchmark claims for manuscript-grade reporting.
2.4.4.4 Reproducibility harness
(A) Manifest-driven execution.A reproducibility harness runs experiments from a single manifest encoding:
environment versions,
dataset IDs,
gold state IDs,
observer IDs,
solver IDs and parameters,
seeds,
expected output hashes.
(B) Artifact hashing and validation.All intermediate and final artifacts are serialized and hashed. Validation compares computed hashes to expected hashes and logs any mismatch with full provenance.
(C) Deterministic logging.Logs are deterministic, including fixed ordering of entries and stable formatting. Logs include timestamps only when explicitly permitted and when they do not affect artifact identity.
(D) Release-grade bundles.A release bundle includes the manuscript text, code snapshot, datasets, and certificates with a single signed manifest. The manifest is the authoritative reproduction reference.
Chapter 2 — Holographic State Space ((H^4)): Constraints, Gauge, Reconstruction
Chapter 3 — Transformation Space (D5)(D^5)(D5): Admissible Morphisms, Costs, Programs
3.1 Square — Definition of (D5)(D^5)(D5) and Its Algebraic Types
3.1.1 Transformation typing
3.1.1.1 Endomorphisms vs automorphisms
(A) State space and feasibility. Let H=L2(Ω×P)\mathfrak{H}=L^2(\Omega\times\mathcal{P})H=L2(Ω×P) be the ambient Hilbert space and let C:D(C)⊆H→ZC:\mathcal{D}(C)\subseteq\mathfrak{H}\to\mathcal{Z}C:D(C)⊆H→Z be a declared constraint operator with feasible hologram space H4:=ker⁡(C)⊆HH^4:=\ker(C)\subseteq\mathfrak{H}H4:=ker(C)⊆H. All transformations in this chapter are typed relative to H4H^4H4 (or a corridor-feasible variant Hτ4H^4_\tauHτ4​ when explicitly declared).
(B) Endomorphisms. A state endomorphism is a typed map
T:D(T)⊆H4→H4,T:\mathcal{D}(T)\subseteq H^4\to H^4,T:D(T)⊆H4→H4,
where D(T)\mathcal{D}(T)D(T) is an explicitly declared domain (possibly all of H4H^4H4). Endomorphisms are closed under composition when domains are compatible. The endomorphism class includes linear and nonlinear transforms, and continuous-time flow maps evaluated at a fixed time.
(C) Automorphisms. A state automorphism is an endomorphism T:H4→H4T:H^4\to H^4T:H4→H4 for which there exists an inverse endomorphism T−1:H4→H4T^{-1}:H^4\to H^4T−1:H4→H4 satisfying
T−1∘T=IdH4,T∘T−1=IdH4.T^{-1}\circ T = \mathrm{Id}_{H^4},\qquad T\circ T^{-1}=\mathrm{Id}_{H^4}.T−1∘T=IdH4​,T∘T−1=IdH4​.
For partial domains, inverse relations hold on the appropriate subsets. Invertibility is always stated either (i) strictly on H4H^4H4, or (ii) modulo gauge in PATT\mathcal{PATT}PATT with a declared quotient structure, or (iii) approximately with declared tolerances.
(D) Morphisms on quotient patterns. When a gauge group G\mathcal{G}G acts on H4H^4H4, patterns are elements of PATT=H4/G\mathcal{PATT}=H^4/\mathcal{G}PATT=H4/G. A transform T:H4→H4T:H^4\to H^4T:H4→H4 induces a pattern morphism T~:PATT→PATT\widetilde{T}:\mathcal{PATT}\to\mathcal{PATT}T
:PATT→PATT if it is gauge-equivariant (so that [T(H)][T(H)][T(H)] depends only on [H][H][H]). Pattern automorphisms require bijectivity on PATT\mathcal{PATT}PATT. Any uniqueness or invertibility claim must specify whether it is in H4H^4H4 or in PATT\mathcal{PATT}PATT.
3.1.1.2 Partial transforms on subspaces
(A) Domain-restricted transforms. A partial transform is a typed map
T:D(T)→H4,D(T)⊆H4,T:\mathcal{D}(T)\to H^4, \qquad \mathcal{D}(T)\subseteq H^4,T:D(T)→H4,D(T)⊆H4,
with D(T)\mathcal{D}(T)D(T) explicitly declared. Domain predicates may encode regularity requirements (e.g., H∈Hs,t∩H4H\in H^{s,t}\cap H^4H∈Hs,t∩H4), boundedness requirements, band-limit requirements, or corridor requirements.
(B) Subspace-restricted transforms. A common class is a transform restricted to a closed subspace M⊆H4\mathcal{M}\subseteq H^4M⊆H4, such as a band-limited subspace or a code subspace. In this case:
T:M→H4,T:\mathcal{M}\to H^4,T:M→H4,
with M\mathcal{M}M declared as part of the type. If T(M)⊆MT(\mathcal{M})\subseteq\mathcal{M}T(M)⊆M, then TTT is an endomorphism of M\mathcal{M}M. Such restrictions are used to obtain stronger stability bounds and reconstructability guarantees.
(C) Partial inverses and local diffeomorphisms. A transform TTT may admit:
a left inverse LLL on D(T)\mathcal{D}(T)D(T) if L∘T=IdL\circ T=\mathrm{Id}L∘T=Id on D(T)\mathcal{D}(T)D(T);
a right inverse RRR on Im(T)\mathrm{Im}(T)Im(T) if T∘R=IdT\circ R=\mathrm{Id}T∘R=Id on Im(T)\mathrm{Im}(T)Im(T);
a local inverse near HHH if TTT is locally bijective (e.g., via an inverse function theorem in Banach/Hilbert settings under Fréchet differentiability and invertible derivative). Any such inverse must declare its domain of validity and its stability constants.
(D) Compatibility with feasibility and gauge. If feasibility is H4=ker⁡(C)H^4=\ker(C)H4=ker(C), a partial transform is valid only if its domain is a subset of H4H^4H4 and its output remains in H4H^4H4. If gauge is present, partial transforms must declare whether they preserve gauge or whether they act on representatives with subsequent canonicalization; domain restrictions must be gauge-consistent to avoid dependence on arbitrary representatives.
3.1.1.3 Multi-stage transforms and pipelines
(A) Sequential composition pipelines. A multi-stage transform is a finite composition
T=Tn∘Tn−1∘⋯∘T1,T = T_n\circ T_{n-1}\circ\cdots\circ T_1,T=Tn​∘Tn−1​∘⋯∘T1​,
where each TkT_kTk​ is typed and the intermediate codomain matches the subsequent domain. Each stage may act on H4H^4H4 or on a corridor-feasible set Hτk4H^4_{\tau_k}Hτk​4​, provided that the pipeline includes feasibility repair operators when necessary and that the full pipeline is typed as H4→H4H^4\to H^4H4→H4.
(B) Operator graph pipelines. More generally, a pipeline is a typed directed graph whose nodes are operators and whose edges are typed intermediate objects. The pipeline denotes a deterministic evaluation order (topological ordering for acyclic graphs). Cyclic graphs correspond to recursion or fixed-point iteration and are treated only when the cycle semantics are explicitly declared (iteration rule, stopping rule, convergence conditions).
(C) Mixed operator categories. Pipelines may combine:
state transforms T:H4→H4T:H^4\to H^4T:H4→H4,
observation operators O:H4→Y\mathcal{O}:H^4\to YO:H4→Y,
reconstruction operators R:Y→H4R:Y\to H^4R:Y→H4,
feasibility repair operators P:H→H4\mathcal{P}:\mathfrak{H}\to H^4P:H→H4,
canonicalization operators Can:H4→H4\mathrm{Can}:H^4\to H^4Can:H4→H4. A pipeline is state-admissible only if its net effect is a well-typed transform H4→H4H^4\to H^4H4→H4 and it satisfies the admissibility axioms in §3.1.2.
(D) Stage-level certificates and pipeline-level certificates. Each stage TkT_kTk​ has local admissibility certificates (stability, drift, feasibility). Pipeline admissibility requires composing stage certificates via conservative propagation rules for residuals, invariants, and stability. The pipeline certificate includes (i) all stage IDs, (ii) all stage certificates, (iii) the derived global corridor guarantees, and (iv) a reproducibility manifest.
3.1.1.4 Transform equivalence and rewriting
(A) Exact functional equivalence. Two transforms T,S:D→H4T,S:\mathcal{D}\to H^4T,S:D→H4 are exactly equivalent on domain D⊆H4\mathcal{D}\subseteq H^4D⊆H4 if
T(H)=S(H)∀H∈D.T(H)=S(H)\quad \forall H\in\mathcal{D}.T(H)=S(H)∀H∈D.
Exact equivalence is a semantic property of maps, independent of program representation.
(B) Approximate equivalence under norms. Two transforms are δ\deltaδ-equivalent on D\mathcal{D}D in a declared norm ∥⋅∥\|\cdot\|∥⋅∥ if
sup⁡H∈D∥T(H)−S(H)∥≤δ.\sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}} \|T(H)-S(H)\| \le \delta.H∈Dsup​∥T(H)−S(H)∥≤δ.
When gauge is present, equivalence is stated in a gauge-invariant distance dGd_{\mathcal{G}}dG​ or after canonicalization:
sup⁡H∈DdG(T(H),S(H))≤δorsup⁡H∈D∥Can(T(H))−Can(S(H))∥≤δ.\sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}} d_{\mathcal{G}}(T(H),S(H))\le \delta \quad\text{or}\quad \sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}} \|\mathrm{Can}(T(H))-\mathrm{Can}(S(H))\|\le \delta.H∈Dsup​dG​(T(H),S(H))≤δorH∈Dsup​∥Can(T(H))−Can(S(H))∥≤δ.
(C) Program equivalence and rewrite systems. Transforms admit multiple program realizations. A rewrite rule is a syntactic replacement P⇒P′P\Rightarrow P'P⇒P′ on program representations, justified by an equivalence statement. A rewrite system is valid only if each rule preserves:
typing (domain/codomain),
feasibility preservation,
corridor invariants within declared tolerances, and
determinism and certificate semantics. Rewrite validity must be certified either by analytic proof (e.g., algebraic identities, BCH expansions) or by verified numerical evidence with explicit scope.
(D) Normalization and canonical rewriting. A rewrite system is normalizing if repeated application terminates in a normal form for any admissible program. A normal form is unique up to declared equivalence (exact or δ\deltaδ-equivalence). Normalization requires a well-founded measure decreasing with each rewrite (e.g., program length, lexicographic cost vector), explicitly declared.
3.1.2 Admissibility axioms
3.1.2.1 Constraint preservation (T(ker⁡C)⊆ker⁡C)(T(\ker C)\subseteq\ker C)(T(kerC)⊆kerC)
(A) Hard constraint preservation. A transform T:D(T)⊆H4→H4T:\mathcal{D}(T)\subseteq H^4\to H^4T:D(T)⊆H4→H4 is hard-feasible if
C(H)=0 ⇒ C(T(H))=0∀H∈D(T)⊆H4.C(H)=0 \ \Rightarrow\ C(T(H))=0 \quad \forall H\in \mathcal{D}(T)\subseteq H^4.C(H)=0 ⇒ C(T(H))=0∀H∈D(T)⊆H4.
This is equivalent to T(H4)⊆H4T(H^4)\subseteq H^4T(H4)⊆H4 when D(T)=H4\mathcal{D}(T)=H^4D(T)=H4.
(B) Soft constraint preservation (corridor-feasible). When feasibility is defined by a tolerance τ\tauτ, a transform is τ\tauτ-feasible if
∥C(H)∥≤τ ⇒ ∥C(T(H))∥≤τ′\|C(H)\|\le \tau \ \Rightarrow\ \|C(T(H))\|\le \tau'∥C(H)∥≤τ ⇒ ∥C(T(H))∥≤τ′
for a declared τ′\tau'τ′ (often τ′=τ\tau'=\tauτ′=τ or τ′=ατ+β\tau'=\alpha\tau+\betaτ′=ατ+β). A transform is contractive in residual if there exist α∈[0,1)\alpha\in[0,1)α∈[0,1), β≥0\beta\ge 0β≥0 such that
∥C(T(H))∥≤α∥C(H)∥+β.\|C(T(H))\| \le \alpha \|C(H)\| + \beta.∥C(T(H))∥≤α∥C(H)∥+β.
(C) Constraint-equivariance and tangency conditions. For differentiable settings with a constraint map CCC, a sufficient condition for invariance under a flow generated by a vector field FFF is tangency:
DC(H)⋅F(H)=0for all H∈H4,DC(H)\cdot F(H)=0\quad \text{for all }H\in H^4,DC(H)⋅F(H)=0for all H∈H4,
or more generally DC(H)⋅F(H)=L(H) C(H)DC(H)\cdot F(H)=L(H)\,C(H)DC(H)⋅F(H)=L(H)C(H) implying invariance of ker⁡(C)\ker(C)ker(C). For linear constraints and linear generators XXX, a sufficient condition is
CX=0 on H4orCX=LC on D(C),C X = 0 \ \text{on } H^4 \quad\text{or}\quad C X = L C \ \text{on } \mathcal{D}(C),CX=0 on H4orCX=LC on D(C),
with LLL declared.
(D) Repair-composed feasibility. If TTT is not feasibility-preserving but is corridor-feasible, a pipeline may restore feasibility via a repair operator P:H→H4\mathcal{P}:\mathfrak{H}\to H^4P:H→H4. The composite P∘T\mathcal{P}\circ TP∘T is feasibility-preserving if P(U)∈H4\mathcal{P}(U)\in H^4P(U)∈H4 for all UUU in the image of TTT and if P\mathcal{P}P is stable under declared norms. Repair is admissible only when its displacement and induced invariant drift remain within declared budgets.
3.1.2.2 Invariant corridor preservation
(A) Invariant suite and corridor budgets. Let Inv:H4→Rk\mathrm{Inv}:H^4\to\mathbb{R}^kInv:H4→Rk be a declared invariant vector (multi-scale information summaries, spectral moments, topology proxies, constraint residual diagnostics, reconstruction envelope parameters). Let δ∈R≥0k\delta\in\mathbb{R}^k_{\ge 0}δ∈R≥0k​ be the corridor budget and ∥⋅∥w\|\cdot\|_{\mathsf{w}}∥⋅∥w​ a declared weighted norm. Corridor preservation requires:
∥Inv(T(H))−Inv(H)∥w≤δ∀H∈D(T).\|\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)\|_{\mathsf{w}} \le \delta \quad \forall H\in \mathcal{D}(T).∥Inv(T(H))−Inv(H)∥w​≤δ∀H∈D(T).
(B) Componentwise preservation. Corridors are commonly componentwise:
∣Invi(T(H))−Invi(H)∣≤δi,i=1,…,k,|\mathrm{Inv}_i(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}_i(H)| \le \delta_i,\qquad i=1,\dots,k,∣Invi​(T(H))−Invi​(H)∣≤δi​,i=1,…,k,
which supports explicit diagnostic attribution and allows mixed strictness across invariants (hard invariants with δi=0\delta_i=0δi​=0, soft invariants with δi>0\delta_i>0δi​>0).
(C) Scale-indexed invariants. When invariants are indexed by scale ϵ\epsilonϵ (e.g., Iϵ(H)I_\epsilon(H)Iϵ​(H)), corridor preservation is enforced across a declared scale set {ϵj}\{\epsilon_j\}{ϵj​}:
∣Iϵj(T(H))−Iϵj(H)∣≤δϵj,|I_{\epsilon_j}(T(H)) - I_{\epsilon_j}(H)| \le \delta_{\epsilon_j},∣Iϵj​​(T(H))−Iϵj​​(H)∣≤δϵj​​,
preventing transforms from violating reconstructability or stability at a specific scale while remaining acceptable on average.
(D) Corridor closure under composition. For transforms T1,T2T_1,T_2T1​,T2​ with budgets δ(1),δ(2)\delta^{(1)},\delta^{(2)}δ(1),δ(2), a conservative composition rule is:
∥Inv(T2(T1(H)))−Inv(H)∥≤∥Inv(T2(T1(H)))−Inv(T1(H))∥+∥Inv(T1(H))−Inv(H)∥≤δ(2)+δ(1),\|\mathrm{Inv}(T_2(T_1(H)))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)\| \le \|\mathrm{Inv}(T_2(T_1(H)))-\mathrm{Inv}(T_1(H))\|+\|\mathrm{Inv}(T_1(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)\| \le \delta^{(2)}+\delta^{(1)},∥Inv(T2​(T1​(H)))−Inv(H)∥≤∥Inv(T2​(T1​(H)))−Inv(T1​(H))∥+∥Inv(T1​(H))−Inv(H)∥≤δ(2)+δ(1),
valid when the same invariant map is used and where each bound holds on the relevant intermediate domains. Alternative propagation rules must be explicitly declared and conservative.
3.1.2.3 Stability bounds (∥T∥,∥T−1∥)(\|T\|,\|T^{-1}\|)(∥T∥,∥T−1∥)
(A) Linear stability (operator norm). If TTT is linear and bounded on H\mathfrak{H}H, stability is governed by the operator norm
∥T∥:=sup⁡H≠0∥T(H)∥∥H∥.\|T\|:=\sup_{H\neq 0}\frac{\|T(H)\|}{\|H\|}.∥T∥:=H=0sup​∥H∥∥T(H)∥​.
A stability corridor requires ∥T∥≤M\|T\|\le M∥T∥≤M for a declared MMM. If TTT is an automorphism, stability of inversion requires ∥T−1∥≤M−\|T^{-1}\|\le M^{-}∥T−1∥≤M− for a declared M−M^{-}M−.
(B) Nonlinear stability (Lipschitz bound). If TTT is nonlinear, stability is governed by a Lipschitz constant LTL_TLT​ on a declared domain D\mathcal{D}D:
∥T(H)−T(H′)∥≤LT∥H−H′∥∀H,H′∈D.\|T(H)-T(H')\|\le L_T\|H-H'\|\quad\forall H,H'\in\mathcal{D}.∥T(H)−T(H′)∥≤LT​∥H−H′∥∀H,H′∈D.
An admissibility corridor requires LT≤ML_T\le MLT​≤M. If an inverse exists on D\mathcal{D}D, an inverse Lipschitz constant LT−1L_{T^{-1}}LT−1​ is required.
(C) Conditioning. For invertible bounded linear transforms, conditioning is
κ(T):=∥T∥∥T−1∥.\kappa(T):=\|T\|\|T^{-1}\|.κ(T):=∥T∥∥T−1∥.
Conditioning bounds control error amplification in inversion and are a mandatory certificate component whenever inversion is used operationally (reconstruction, backpropagation, inverse transforms).
(D) Stability under discretization. Discrete implementations TN,PT_{N,P}TN,P​ must satisfy stability bounds uniform in N,PN,PN,P or controlled by an explicit law:
∥TN,P∥≤MN,P,MN,P bounded or increasing under declared bounds,\|T_{N,P}\| \le M_{N,P},\qquad M_{N,P}\ \text{bounded or increasing under declared bounds},∥TN,P​∥≤MN,P​,MN,P​ bounded or increasing under declared bounds,
and similarly for inverses. Uncontrolled growth invalidates refinement convergence and certificate portability across resolutions.
3.1.2.4 Computability requirements
(A) Executability as finite program. A transform TTT is computable if it is representable by a finite program PPP over a declared operator basis, acting on finite representations H(N,P)H^{(N,P)}H(N,P) or coefficient vectors in a declared basis B\mathcal{B}B. Computability requires explicit finite parameters: basis identifier, truncation levels, precision type, and deterministic execution semantics.
(B) Resource bounds. Computability includes explicit resource bounds: time Time(T)\mathsf{Time}(T)Time(T) and memory Mem(T)\mathsf{Mem}(T)Mem(T), measured under a declared cost model (operation counts, asymptotic scaling, or empirical benchmarks). Admissibility may restrict resources by corridor:
Time(T)≤TimeMax,Mem(T)≤MemMax.\mathsf{Time}(T)\le \mathsf{TimeMax},\qquad \mathsf{Mem}(T)\le \mathsf{MemMax}.Time(T)≤TimeMax,Mem(T)≤MemMax.
(C) Determinism and reproducibility. Computable transforms must be deterministic given their serialized program parameters and seeds. Randomized transforms are permitted only if randomness is fully seeded, the sampling design is serialized as an artifact, and execution remains deterministic conditioned on the seed.
(D) Certificate production. A computable transform must emit a certificate object containing: typing information, feasibility checks, invariant checks, stability witnesses (analytic and/or measured), resource measurements, and a reproducibility manifest. A transform lacking certificate capability is not an element of D5D^5D5 in the publication-grade sense.
3.1.3 Cost and energy models
3.1.3.1 Cost functional definition (C(T))(\mathcal{C}(T))(C(T))
(A) Cost as scalarization of a cost vector. Define a cost vector
c(T):=(cstab(T), cinv(T), cenergy(T), ctime(T), cmem(T))∈R≥0m,\mathbf{c}(T):=\big(c_{\mathrm{stab}}(T),\ c_{\mathrm{inv}}(T),\ c_{\mathrm{energy}}(T),\ c_{\mathrm{time}}(T),\ c_{\mathrm{mem}}(T)\big)\in\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^m,c(T):=(cstab​(T), cinv​(T), cenergy​(T), ctime​(T), cmem​(T))∈R≥0m​,
where each component is explicitly defined. A scalar cost functional is a monotone scalarization:
C(T):=Φ(c(T)),\mathcal{C}(T):=\Phi(\mathbf{c}(T)),C(T):=Φ(c(T)),
where Φ\PhiΦ is declared (e.g., weighted sum, weighted max, lexicographic order).
(B) Stability cost. A canonical stability cost is
cstab(T):=log⁡∥T∥+log⁡∥T−1∥ (if invertible),cstab(T):=log⁡LT (if Lipschitz).c_{\mathrm{stab}}(T):=\log\|T\| + \log\|T^{-1}\|\ \ (\text{if invertible}), \qquad c_{\mathrm{stab}}(T):=\log L_T\ \ (\text{if Lipschitz}).cstab​(T):=log∥T∥+log∥T−1∥ (if invertible),cstab​(T):=logLT​ (if Lipschitz).
Logarithms convert multiplicative composition growth into additive accumulation and support pipeline cost accounting.
(C) Invariant drift cost. Given invariant corridor drift ΔInv(H;T)=Inv(T(H))−Inv(H)\Delta_{\mathrm{Inv}}(H;T)=\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)ΔInv​(H;T)=Inv(T(H))−Inv(H), define a worst-case or certified drift bound:
cinv(T):=sup⁡H∈D(T)∥ΔInv(H;T)∥w,c_{\mathrm{inv}}(T):=\sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}(T)} \|\Delta_{\mathrm{Inv}}(H;T)\|_{\mathsf{w}},cinv​(T):=H∈D(T)sup​∥ΔInv​(H;T)∥w​,
or an empirically certified upper bound over a declared test suite when analytic suprema are not available.
(D) Energy cost. Energy cost measures the transform’s effect on a declared energy functional E:H4→R≥0E:H^4\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}E:H4→R≥0​ (often E(H)=∥H∥H2E(H)=\|H\|_{\mathfrak{H}}^2E(H)=∥H∥H2​ or a weighted Sobolev energy). Define:
cenergy(T):=sup⁡H∈D(T)∣E(T(H))−E(H)∣,c_{\mathrm{energy}}(T):=\sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}(T)} \big|E(T(H)) - E(H)\big|,cenergy​(T):=H∈D(T)sup​​E(T(H))−E(H)​,
or when the transform is intended to be energy-preserving, cenergy(T)c_{\mathrm{energy}}(T)cenergy​(T) is constrained to 000 (hard invariant) or to a budget.
3.1.3.2 Regularizers (smoothness/sparsity)
(A) Smoothness regularization. For transforms expressed as T(H)=H+Δ(H)T(H)=H+\Delta(H)T(H)=H+Δ(H) with Δ(H)\Delta(H)Δ(H) in a Sobolev space, smoothness penalties include:
Rsmooth(T;H):=∥∇xΔ(H)∥L22+η∥∇pΔ(H)∥L22,\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{smooth}}(T;H):=\|\nabla_x \Delta(H)\|_{L^2}^2+\eta\|\nabla_p \Delta(H)\|_{L^2}^2,Rsmooth​(T;H):=∥∇x​Δ(H)∥L22​+η∥∇p​Δ(H)∥L22​,
or higher-order Sobolev norms. Smoothness regularizers limit oscillatory artifacts and promote stable reconstructions under partial observations.
(B) Sparsity regularization. In a coefficient basis B\mathcal{B}B, sparsity penalties include:
Rsparse(T;H):=∥CoeffB(T(H))∥1,\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{sparse}}(T;H):=\| \mathrm{Coeff}_\mathcal{B}(T(H))\|_1,Rsparse​(T;H):=∥CoeffB​(T(H))∥1​,
or penalties on increments CoeffB(T(H)−H)\mathrm{Coeff}_\mathcal{B}(T(H)-H)CoeffB​(T(H)−H). Sparsity regularizers encode compressibility and support efficient storage and fast approximate inverses.
(C) Total-variation and interface regularization. For edge-preserving regimes, use fiberwise BV/TV penalties:
RTV(T;H):=∫PTV(T(H)(⋅,p)) dμ(p),\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{TV}}(T;H):=\int_{\mathcal{P}} \mathrm{TV}\big(T(H)(\cdot,p)\big)\,d\mu(p),RTV​(T;H):=∫P​TV(T(H)(⋅,p))dμ(p),
with TV defined on Ω\OmegaΩ and used only under declared BV admissibility.
(D) Regularizers as admissibility constraints. Regularizers can be used either as part of the cost C\mathcal{C}C or as hard corridor constraints:
R(T;H)≤ρ∀H∈D(T),\mathcal{R}(T;H)\le \rho \quad \forall H\in\mathcal{D}(T),R(T;H)≤ρ∀H∈D(T),
for a declared bound ρ\rhoρ. In both cases, regularizers must be computable and must admit deterministic evaluation for certificates.
3.1.3.3 Complexity measures (time/memory)
(A) Abstract complexity measures. Define:
ctime(T):=Time(T),cmem(T):=Mem(T),c_{\mathrm{time}}(T):=\mathsf{Time}(T),\qquad c_{\mathrm{mem}}(T):=\mathsf{Mem}(T),ctime​(T):=Time(T),cmem​(T):=Mem(T),
where Time\mathsf{Time}Time and Mem\mathsf{Mem}Mem are measured under a declared model. For finite representations H(N,P)H^{(N,P)}H(N,P), complexity is typically expressed as scaling laws in (N,P)(N,P)(N,P).
(B) Operation-count time model. For a transform TN,PT_{N,P}TN,P​ implemented as a composition of kernels, define time as total arithmetic operations:
Time(TN,P):=∑kernels k#ops(k;N,P),\mathsf{Time}(T_{N,P}) := \sum_{\text{kernels }k} \#\mathrm{ops}(k;N,P),Time(TN,P​):=kernels k∑​#ops(k;N,P),
with explicit formulas (e.g., FFT kernels O(N3Plog⁡N)O(N^3P\log N)O(N3PlogN), dense matrix multiplies O(M2)O(M^2)O(M2), sparse multiplies O(nnz)O(\mathrm{nnz})O(nnz)).
(C) Memory model. Memory is the maximum allocated storage during execution:
Mem(TN,P):=max⁡t∈executionbytes_alive(t),\mathsf{Mem}(T_{N,P}) := \max_{t\in\text{execution}} \mathrm{bytes\_alive}(t),Mem(TN,P​):=t∈executionmax​bytes_alive(t),
including state buffers, temporary arrays, factorization caches, and certificate logs. Memory is measured deterministically under declared buffer reuse policies.
(D) Sample complexity for learned transforms. For learned generators, a relevant complexity measure is sample complexity: the number of training samples required to certify generalization within declared bounds. Any such measure must specify the hypothesis class and confidence parameters; absent explicit generalization theory, empirical certification scope must be declared.
3.1.3.4 Pareto trade-off surfaces
(A) Partial order on cost vectors. Define the Pareto order on cost vectors: c(T)⪯c(S)\mathbf{c}(T)\preceq \mathbf{c}(S)c(T)⪯c(S) iff ci(T)≤ci(S)c_i(T)\le c_i(S)ci​(T)≤ci​(S) for all components iii. Transform TTT dominates SSS if c(T)⪯c(S)\mathbf{c}(T)\preceq \mathbf{c}(S)c(T)⪯c(S) and strict inequality holds for at least one component.
(B) Pareto frontier. The Pareto frontier F⊆D5\mathcal{F}\subseteq D^5F⊆D5 consists of all nondominated transforms:
F:={T∈D5: ∄S∈D5 with c(S)≺c(T)}.\mathcal{F}:=\{T\in D^5:\ \nexists S\in D^5\text{ with }\mathbf{c}(S)\prec \mathbf{c}(T)\}.F:={T∈D5: ∄S∈D5 with c(S)≺c(T)}.
The frontier depends on the declared cost vector and on admissibility corridors.
(C) Scalarization and frontier exploration. A scalar cost Cλ(T)=∑iλici(T)\mathcal{C}_\lambda(T)=\sum_i \lambda_i c_i(T)Cλ​(T)=∑i​λi​ci​(T) with λi>0\lambda_i>0λi​>0 produces solutions that lie on the frontier under convexity conditions; otherwise it finds a subset of frontier points. Nonconvex trade-offs require explicit multi-objective search strategies.
(D) Certificate-aware Pareto reporting. Pareto reporting is valid only when each frontier candidate includes a full certificate: feasibility, drift, stability, and resource measurements. Frontier points lacking certificate completeness are excluded from publication-grade frontiers.
3.1.4 Normal forms for transforms
3.1.4.1 Generator normal form (Lie exponential)
(A) Generator representation. A transform is in generator normal form if represented as an exponential of a generator:
T=exp⁡(X),T=\exp(X),T=exp(X),
or as a finite product of exponentials
T=exp⁡(tnXn)⋯exp⁡(t1X1),T=\exp(t_n X_n)\cdots \exp(t_1 X_1),T=exp(tn​Xn​)⋯exp(t1​X1​),
where each XkX_kXk​ is a declared generator (bounded linear operator, self-adjoint operator, semigroup generator, or nonlinear vector field represented via flow maps) and each tkt_ktk​ is a declared step size.
(B) Lie algebra and admissible generator sets. A set of generators g\mathfrak{g}g is admissible if each X∈gX\in\mathfrak{g}X∈g produces a well-defined flow on a declared domain and preserves feasibility either exactly (tangent to ker⁡(C)\ker(C)ker(C)) or via corridor with repair. Closure properties under commutators define an associated Lie algebra structure when generators are linear and domains are compatible.
(C) Canonical ordering and BCH normalization. When transforms are products of exponentials, canonical ordering is defined by a declared ordering of generator types and indices. BCH expansions justify rewriting between equivalent products up to controlled truncation:
exp⁡(A)exp⁡(B)=exp⁡ ⁣(A+B+12[A,B]+⋯ ),\exp(A)\exp(B)=\exp\!\left(A+B+\tfrac12[A,B]+\cdots\right),exp(A)exp(B)=exp(A+B+21​[A,B]+⋯),
with truncation order and error bounds explicitly declared for approximate equivalence.
(D) Generator normal form certificates. Certificates for generator normal form include:
generator domain conditions,
existence of exponential/flow,
stability bounds for the flow map (operator norm or Lipschitz constant),
feasibility preservation/tangency conditions,
discretization consistency and error bounds for numerical exponentiation.
3.1.4.2 Gate normal form (discrete programs)
(A) Gate set and program syntax. Let Ggate={gi}i∈I⊂D5\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{gate}}=\{g_i\}_{i\in I}\subset D^5Ggate​={gi​}i∈I​⊂D5 be a fixed generating set (finite or countable) of admissible primitive transforms (“gates”). A transform is in gate normal form if written as a finite word:
T=gim∘gim−1∘⋯∘gi1,T = g_{i_m}\circ g_{i_{m-1}}\circ \cdots \circ g_{i_1},T=gim​​∘gim−1​​∘⋯∘gi1​​,
with indices ik∈Ii_k\in Iik​∈I and with a deterministic order.
(B) Gate equivalences and reductions. Gate normal forms admit reductions through relations:
gi∘gj≡gk(exact or δ-equivalent),gi∘gi−1≡Id,g_i\circ g_j \equiv g_k \quad (\text{exact or }\delta\text{-equivalent}), \qquad g_i\circ g_i^{-1}\equiv \mathrm{Id},gi​∘gj​≡gk​(exact or δ-equivalent),gi​∘gi−1​≡Id,
and higher relations encoding commutation or braid-like identities. A gate normal form is canonical when the reduction system is confluent and terminating, or when deterministic tie-breaking selects a unique reduced word among equivalent words.
(C) Gate-level admissibility. Each gate carries a local certificate (feasibility preservation, drift bounds, stability bounds, resource bounds). A gate word is admissible only if certificate propagation rules yield a global certificate satisfying corridor budgets.
(D) Gate normal form serialization. Gate programs serialize as ordered lists of gate IDs and parameters, with explicit truncation/precision metadata. Two programs are comparable only when serialized under the same schema version and when gates reference the same base definitions and certificates.
3.1.4.3 Mixed continuous/discrete normal form
(A) Hybrid composition. A transform is in mixed normal form if expressed as an alternation of continuous-flow blocks and discrete gate blocks:
T=(G(r)∘F(r))∘⋯∘(G(1)∘F(1)),T = \big(\mathcal{G}^{(r)}\circ \mathcal{F}^{(r)}\big)\circ\cdots\circ\big(\mathcal{G}^{(1)}\circ \mathcal{F}^{(1)}\big),T=(G(r)∘F(r))∘⋯∘(G(1)∘F(1)),
where F(k)\mathcal{F}^{(k)}F(k) denotes a flow map (generator exponential or ODE/PDE evolution) and G(k)\mathcal{G}^{(k)}G(k) denotes a gate-word block.
(B) Event-driven hybrid transforms. Mixed normal form includes event-driven maps:
T(H)=Jσr∘Φtr∘⋯∘Jσ1∘Φt1(H),T(H)=J_{\sigma_r}\circ \Phi_{t_r}\circ \cdots \circ J_{\sigma_1}\circ \Phi_{t_1}(H),T(H)=Jσr​​∘Φtr​​∘⋯∘Jσ1​​∘Φt1​​(H),
where Φt\Phi_{t}Φt​ are continuous evolutions and JσJ_{\sigma}Jσ​ are discrete jumps indexed by events σ\sigmaσ. Event guards and determinism requirements are part of the program type.
(C) Normalization of hybrid programs. Normalization includes: (i) canonical ordering of blocks, (ii) fusion of adjacent compatible blocks (e.g., combining Φt1∘Φt2=Φt1+t2\Phi_{t_1}\circ\Phi_{t_2}=\Phi_{t_1+t_2}Φt1​​∘Φt2​​=Φt1​+t2​​ for semigroups), and (iii) reduction of gate words within blocks. Hybrid normalization requires explicit rules and error bounds when approximations are used.
(D) Hybrid admissibility certificates. Hybrid certificates include separate analysis for flow blocks and gate blocks and a global propagation analysis. Stability and drift budgets are tracked across block boundaries with explicit envelope propagation rules.
3.1.4.4 Canonical serialization of programs
(A) Program schema. A canonical program schema includes:
type signature (domain/codomain, constraints, gauge regime),
operator graph (nodes, edges, ordering),
parameters (canonical ordering, fixed precision, bounded domains),
discretization metadata (B,N,P,ϵ\mathcal{B},N,P,\epsilonB,N,P,ϵ),
numeric regime (precision, determinism settings),
certificate hooks (required checks and thresholds),
schema version.
(B) Deterministic ordering. Graphs are serialized by deterministic topological ordering, with stable tie-breaking. All associative compositions are serialized under a declared parenthesization to preserve program identity and to avoid ambiguity in non-commutative regimes.
(C) Canonical parameter encoding. Parameters are encoded with fixed numeric representation and rounding policy. Any parameterization involving constraints (positivity, unit-norm) must serialize canonical unconstrained coordinates (e.g., log-parameters) and declare the decoding map.
(D) Hashable program identity. Define the program identity as
PID(T):=Hash(Ser(Prog(T))),\mathrm{PID}(T):=\mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(\mathrm{Prog}(T))),PID(T):=Hash(Ser(Prog(T))),
where Ser\mathrm{Ser}Ser is canonical serialization. Any admissibility certificate references PID(T)\mathrm{PID}(T)PID(T) and is invalid if the program serialization changes.
3.2 Flower — Generators, Flows, and Composition
3.2.1 Generator families
3.2.1.1 Linear generators (bounded operators)
(A) Bounded linear generators on H\mathfrak{H}H. A bounded linear generator is X∈L(H,H)X\in\mathcal{L}(\mathfrak{H},\mathfrak{H})X∈L(H,H). The exponential exp⁡(tX)\exp(tX)exp(tX) is well-defined as a norm-convergent series for all t∈Rt\in\mathbb{R}t∈R, producing a bounded linear flow map.
(B) Feasibility-tangent linear generators. A bounded linear generator XXX is feasibility-tangent to H4=ker⁡(C)H^4=\ker(C)H4=ker(C) if:
H∈ker⁡(C) ⇒ C(XH)=0,H\in \ker(C)\ \Rightarrow\ C(XH)=0,H∈ker(C) ⇒ C(XH)=0,
equivalently C∘X=0C\circ X=0C∘X=0 on ker⁡(C)\ker(C)ker(C). A stronger sufficient condition is the existence of a residual operator LLL such that
C∘X=L∘Con D(C),C\circ X = L\circ C\quad \text{on }\mathcal{D}(C),C∘X=L∘Con D(C),
implying invariance of ker⁡(C)\ker(C)ker(C) under exp⁡(tX)\exp(tX)exp(tX).
(C) Unitary generators and conservation. If XXX is skew-adjoint on H\mathfrak{H}H (X∗=−XX^\ast=-XX∗=−X) and bounded, then exp⁡(tX)\exp(tX)exp(tX) is unitary and preserves ∥H∥\|H\|∥H∥. Such generators yield energy-preserving transforms and are the canonical class for reversible, stability-maximal transforms.
(D) Band-limited and structured generators. Generators may be constructed to act on specific bands or subspaces:
X=∑kPkXPk,X = \sum_k P_k X P_k,X=k∑​Pk​XPk​,
with band projections PkP_kPk​. Such structure supports scale-aware corridors and simplifies stability analysis by separating contributions.
3.2.1.2 Nonlinear generators (vector fields on (H)(\mathfrak{H})(H))
(A) Nonlinear generators as vector fields. A nonlinear generator is a vector field
F:D(F)⊆H→H,F:\mathcal{D}(F)\subseteq \mathfrak{H}\to \mathfrak{H},F:D(F)⊆H→H,
typically locally Lipschitz on bounded subsets of D(F)\mathcal{D}(F)D(F). The associated flow is defined by the ODE:
H˙(t)=F(H(t)),H(0)=H0.\dot{H}(t)=F(H(t)),\qquad H(0)=H_0.H˙(t)=F(H(t)),H(0)=H0​.
(B) Existence and uniqueness of flows. Local existence and uniqueness follow from local Lipschitz conditions. Global existence requires a priori bounds (e.g., energy bounds, Lyapunov functions). The flow map Φt(H0):=H(t)\Phi_t(H_0):=H(t)Φt​(H0​):=H(t) defines the transform T=ΦtT=\Phi_tT=Φt​ for fixed ttt.
(C) Feasibility tangency for nonlinear flows. To preserve feasibility C(H)=0C(H)=0C(H)=0, sufficient conditions include:
DC(H)⋅F(H)=0∀H∈H4,DC(H)\cdot F(H)=0\quad \forall H\in H^4,DC(H)⋅F(H)=0∀H∈H4,
or more generally DC(H)⋅F(H)=L(H)C(H)DC(H)\cdot F(H)=L(H)C(H)DC(H)⋅F(H)=L(H)C(H) ensuring invariance of ker⁡(C)\ker(C)ker(C). In weak settings, tangency is replaced by invariance theorems formulated in terms of residual evolution inequalities.
(D) Regularity preservation. Nonlinear flows must declare the state class they preserve: Φt:Hs,t∩H4→Hs,t∩H4\Phi_t:H^{s,t}\cap H^4\to H^{s,t}\cap H^4Φt​:Hs,t∩H4→Hs,t∩H4 or Φt:H∩H4→H∩H4\Phi_t:\mathfrak{H}\cap H^4\to \mathfrak{H}\cap H^4Φt​:H∩H4→H∩H4. Without a declared preserved class, the transform is ill-typed for corridor and reconstruction compatibility.
3.2.1.3 Constraint-aware generators
(A) Projected generators. Given a raw generator X~\widetilde{X}X
that may not preserve feasibility, a constraint-aware generator may be formed by projection onto the tangent/feasible directions:
X:=ΠtanX~,X := \Pi_{\mathrm{tan}} \widetilde{X},X:=Πtan​X,
where Πtan\Pi_{\mathrm{tan}}Πtan​ is a declared projection operator mapping into directions that preserve constraints (for linear constraints, orthogonal projection onto ker⁡(C)\ker(C)ker(C); for nonlinear constraints, projection onto tangent space).
(B) Correction-term generators. Alternatively define:
X(H):=X~(H)−K(H) C(H),X(H):=\widetilde{X}(H) - K(H)\,C(H),X(H):=X(H)−K(H)C(H),
where K(H)K(H)K(H) is a correction operator selected to enforce DC(H)⋅X(H)=0DC(H)\cdot X(H)=0DC(H)⋅X(H)=0 (or to contract residual). This yields a feasibility-contracting generator when exact tangency is infeasible.
(C) Constraint-aware discretization. Constraint-aware generators must admit consistent discrete analogues XN,PX_{N,P}XN,P​ such that the discrete flow preserves discrete feasibility ker⁡(CN,P)\ker(C_{N,P})ker(CN,P​) or contracts discrete residuals. This prevents feasibility drift due to discretization mismatch.
(D) Generator admissibility certificates. Certificates include: (i) tangency proof or residual contraction bound, (ii) stability bound for the flow map, (iii) regularity preservation conditions, and (iv) discretization consistency checks.
3.2.1.4 Learned generators (parameterized)
(A) Parameterized generator families. A learned generator is a parameterized map FθF_\thetaFθ​ or XθX_\thetaXθ​ with θ∈Θ\theta\in\Thetaθ∈Θ, acting on H\mathfrak{H}H or on a finite representation. The generator is admissible only when its type, domain, stability constants, and feasibility behavior are certified or bounded on the declared domain.
(B) Certification requirements. Learned generators must provide:
Lipschitz bounds (analytic, enforced by architecture, or empirically upper-bounded with explicit scope),
feasibility preservation via projection, constraint-aware architecture, or explicit repair composition,
invariant drift bounds certified by tests across a declared coverage suite.
(C) Domain restriction and generalization scope. Learned generator admissibility is always scope-limited: D⊂H4\mathcal{D}\subset H^4D⊂H4 is explicitly declared (e.g., states within certain norms, band-limits, or training distribution support). Outside D\mathcal{D}D, no admissibility claim is made.
(D) Learned generator normal forms. Learned generators are serialized by architecture ID, parameter tensor hashes, and deterministic evaluation settings. A learned generator may be wrapped in a normal form by treating it as a primitive gate with a certificate-dependent admissibility scope.
3.2.2 Exponentials and flows
3.2.2.1 (exp⁡(tX))(\exp(tX))(exp(tX)) construction and domains
(A) Bounded operator exponentials. If X∈L(H)X\in\mathcal{L}(\mathfrak{H})X∈L(H), define
exp⁡(tX):=∑n=0∞tnn!Xn,\exp(tX):=\sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{t^n}{n!}X^n,exp(tX):=n=0∑∞​n!tn​Xn,
convergent in operator norm. Domain is all of H\mathfrak{H}H. The bound
∥exp⁡(tX)∥≤exp⁡(∣t∣∥X∥)\|\exp(tX)\|\le \exp(|t|\|X\|)∥exp(tX)∥≤exp(∣t∣∥X∥)
holds.
(B) Unbounded generator exponentials via semigroups. If XXX is unbounded but generates a C0C_0C0​-semigroup S(t)S(t)S(t) (Hille–Yosida conditions), define exp⁡(tX):=S(t)\exp(tX):=S(t)exp(tX):=S(t) for t≥0t\ge 0t≥0. If XXX generates a C0C_0C0​-group, define for t∈Rt\in\mathbb{R}t∈R. Domains and growth bounds ∥S(t)∥≤Meωt\|S(t)\|\le Me^{\omega t}∥S(t)∥≤Meωt are declared.
(C) Self-adjoint generators and unitary groups. If AAA is self-adjoint, define unitary evolution U(t)=exp⁡(itA)U(t)=\exp(itA)U(t)=exp(itA), which preserves ∥H∥\|H\|∥H∥. Self-adjointness (or essential self-adjointness with specified closure) is a required certificate component.
(D) Nonlinear exponentials (flow maps). For vector fields FFF, the transform at time ttt is the flow map Φt\Phi_tΦt​. Existence, uniqueness, and domain of Φt\Phi_tΦt​ must be explicitly declared; Φt\Phi_tΦt​ replaces exp⁡(tX)\exp(tX)exp(tX) when XXX is nonlinear.
3.2.2.2 Semigroup vs group flows
(A) Semigroup flows (irreversible). A semigroup {S(t)}t≥0\{S(t)\}_{t\ge 0}{S(t)}t≥0​ satisfies S(0)=IS(0)=IS(0)=I and S(t+s)=S(t)S(s)S(t+s)=S(t)S(s)S(t+s)=S(t)S(s) for t,s≥0t,s\ge 0t,s≥0. Semigroups model irreversible transformations (dissipation, projection, smoothing). Inverses need not exist; admissibility in D5D^5D5 does not require invertibility unless explicitly required by the application.
(B) Group flows (reversible). A group {G(t)}t∈R\{G(t)\}_{t\in\mathbb{R}}{G(t)}t∈R​ satisfies the semigroup property for all real ttt and includes inverses G(−t)=G(t)−1G(-t)=G(t)^{-1}G(−t)=G(t)−1. Group flows represent reversible transforms and support symmetric stability analysis with ∥G(t)−1∥\|G(t)^{-1}\|∥G(t)−1∥ bounded.
(C) Corridor distinctions. Semigroup transforms are admissible when they preserve feasibility and corridor invariants within budgets; group transforms must additionally satisfy inverse stability bounds when invertibility is required. The corridor includes explicit flags indicating whether invertibility is required.
(D) Discrete approximations to flows. Semigroup/group flows are implemented via numerical exponentiation, splitting schemes, or time-stepping. Discrete implementations must preserve semigroup/group identities up to declared tolerances and must include stability envelopes quantifying time-discretization drift.
3.2.2.3 Splitting methods and BCH expansions
(A) Lie–Trotter splitting. For generators A,BA,BA,B with suitable conditions, the Lie–Trotter formula states:
exp⁡(t(A+B))=lim⁡n→∞(exp⁡(tnA)exp⁡(tnB))n.\exp(t(A+B)) = \lim_{n\to\infty} \left(\exp\left(\tfrac{t}{n}A\right)\exp\left(\tfrac{t}{n}B\right)\right)^n.exp(t(A+B))=n→∞lim​(exp(nt​A)exp(nt​B))n.
Finite nnn yields an approximation with error depending on commutators and domain regularity.
(B) Strang splitting. A second-order symmetric splitting is:
exp⁡(t(A+B))≈exp⁡(t2A)exp⁡(tB)exp⁡(t2A),\exp(t(A+B)) \approx \exp\left(\tfrac{t}{2}A\right)\exp(tB)\exp\left(\tfrac{t}{2}A\right),exp(t(A+B))≈exp(2t​A)exp(tB)exp(2t​A),
with leading error terms governed by [A,[A,B]][A,[A,B]][A,[A,B]] and [B,[A,B]][B,[A,B]][B,[A,B]]. Error orders and constants must be declared in admissibility certificates.
(C) BCH expansions for rewriting. BCH provides:
log⁡(exp⁡(A)exp⁡(B))=A+B+12[A,B]+112[A,[A,B]]−112[B,[A,B]]+⋯\log(\exp(A)\exp(B)) = A+B+\tfrac12[A,B]+\tfrac{1}{12}[A,[A,B]]-\tfrac{1}{12}[B,[A,B]]+\cdotslog(exp(A)exp(B))=A+B+21​[A,B]+121​[A,[A,B]]−121​[B,[A,B]]+⋯
Truncated BCH yields approximate rewrite rules. Truncation order and remainder bounds must be explicitly declared, including domain constraints under which commutators are well-defined.
(D) Constraint-aware splitting. When constraints must be preserved, splitting is admissible only if each split flow preserves feasibility or if feasibility repair is inserted between substeps. If splitting introduces constraint drift, drift must be bounded and repaired within corridor budgets.
3.2.2.4 Event-driven discontinuous flows
(A) Hybrid evolution definition. Event-driven flows combine continuous evolution Φt\Phi_tΦt​ with discrete jumps JσJ_\sigmaJσ​. A hybrid transform has the form
T=Jσm∘Φtm∘⋯∘Jσ1∘Φt1.T = J_{\sigma_m}\circ \Phi_{t_m}\circ \cdots \circ J_{\sigma_1}\circ \Phi_{t_1}.T=Jσm​​∘Φtm​​∘⋯∘Jσ1​​∘Φt1​​.
(B) Event guards and schedules. Events occur either at predetermined times or when a guard predicate becomes true:
σk triggers when Gk(H(t))=0 (or Gk(H(t))≤0).\sigma_k \text{ triggers when } G_k(H(t))=0 \text{ (or }G_k(H(t))\le 0\text{)}.σk​ triggers when Gk​(H(t))=0 (or Gk​(H(t))≤0).
Guard predicates must be measurable and compatible with the chosen solution concept, and must be computable under finite representations.
(C) Well-posedness and Zeno exclusion. Hybrid systems must exclude Zeno behavior (infinitely many jumps in finite time) or must include explicit Zeno handling rules (e.g., merging jumps, minimum dwell time). Without such rules, the hybrid transform is ill-defined and not admissible.
(D) Hybrid admissibility. Hybrid admissibility requires: feasibility preservation (or corridor feasibility with repair), invariant drift bounds across both flows and jumps, stability bounds for each segment, and deterministic event evaluation rules (including deterministic tie-breaking when multiple guards trigger simultaneously).
3.2.3 Composition and commutation
3.2.3.1 Associativity and parenthesization
(A) Associativity in typed composition. For transforms R,T,SR,T,SR,T,S with compatible domains,
R∘(T∘S)=(R∘T)∘SR\circ(T\circ S)=(R\circ T)\circ SR∘(T∘S)=(R∘T)∘S
as maps on the maximal domain where both sides are defined. For partial transforms, parenthesization affects domain constraints and is therefore part of program typing.
(B) Parenthesization as semantic choice in non-commutative regimes. Even though composition is associative, the intermediate domains for partial transforms may differ under different parenthesizations. Therefore parenthesization is treated as part of program semantics and must be serialized deterministically.
(C) Stability propagation under composition. For bounded linear transforms, ∥T∘S∥≤∥T∥∥S∥\|T\circ S\|\le \|T\|\|S\|∥T∘S∥≤∥T∥∥S∥. For Lipschitz transforms, LT∘S≤LTLSL_{T\circ S}\le L_T L_SLT∘S​≤LT​LS​. Stability costs are therefore additive in log scale and must be computed and reported in certificates.
(D) Corridor propagation under composition. Constraint residual and invariant drift propagate through composition via conservative rules declared in the corridor semantics. Pipeline admissibility requires verifying that propagated budgets remain within corridor limits.
3.2.3.2 Commutator diagnostics ([X,Y])([X,Y])([X,Y])
(A) Commutator for linear generators. For linear operators X,YX,YX,Y with compatible domains,
[X,Y]:=XY−YX.[X,Y]:=XY-YX.[X,Y]:=XY−YX.
Commutator norms ∥[X,Y]∥\|[X,Y]\|∥[X,Y]∥ quantify non-commutativity and determine splitting error and rewrite validity.
(B) Commutator for nonlinear transforms. For nonlinear transforms T,ST,ST,S, define a commutation defect:
ΔT,S(H):=T(S(H))−S(T(H)),\Delta_{T,S}(H):=T(S(H)) - S(T(H)),ΔT,S​(H):=T(S(H))−S(T(H)),
and quantify non-commutativity by sup⁡H∈D∥ΔT,S(H)∥\sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}} \|\Delta_{T,S}(H)\|supH∈D​∥ΔT,S​(H)∥ or a certified bound over a declared test suite.
(C) Diagnosing ordering sensitivity. If ∥[X,Y]∥\|[X,Y]\|∥[X,Y]∥ or ∥ΔT,S∥\|\Delta_{T,S}\|∥ΔT,S​∥ is small within corridor limits, reordering may be admissible with bounded error. If large, ordering is semantically significant and must not be altered by rewriting unless explicit error budgets and proofs are provided.
(D) Commutators and invariant drift. Non-commutativity often induces invariant drift in split approximations. Corridor enforcement requires tracking drift attributable to commutator terms and bounding them via commutator norms or empirical drift measurements.
3.2.3.3 Curvature as non-commutativity
(A) Loop holonomy transforms. Given invertible transforms T,ST,ST,S, define the loop (commutator) transform:
HT,S:=T∘S∘T−1∘S−1.\mathcal{H}_{T,S}:=T\circ S\circ T^{-1}\circ S^{-1}.HT,S​:=T∘S∘T−1∘S−1.
Deviation of HT,S\mathcal{H}_{T,S}HT,S​ from identity quantifies curvature-like effects in transform space.
(B) Generator-level curvature. For small-step exponentials exp⁡(ϵX)\exp(\epsilon X)exp(ϵX), exp⁡(ϵY)\exp(\epsilon Y)exp(ϵY), the loop deviation satisfies:
Hexp⁡(ϵX),exp⁡(ϵY)≈exp⁡(ϵ2[X,Y]+O(ϵ3)),\mathcal{H}_{\exp(\epsilon X),\exp(\epsilon Y)} \approx \exp(\epsilon^2 [X,Y] + O(\epsilon^3)),Hexp(ϵX),exp(ϵY)​≈exp(ϵ2[X,Y]+O(ϵ3)),
under standard domain assumptions. This provides a computable diagnostic for non-commutative geometry of generator families.
(C) Curvature diagnostics in program graphs. In program graphs, loops represent order-sensitive interactions. Measuring loop holonomy provides a systematic method to identify where rewriting is unsafe and where ordering constraints must be preserved.
(D) Curvature-aware optimization. Optimization over D5D^5D5 can include curvature penalties to prefer commuting or near-commuting operator families when stability is paramount. Such penalties must be explicitly defined and must be computable under the program representation.
3.2.3.4 Cycle detection in program graphs
(A) Cycles as recursion or iteration. A program graph cycle corresponds to an iterative process:
Hn+1=F(Hn),H_{n+1} = \mathcal{F}(H_n),Hn+1​=F(Hn​),
or a fixed-point solve embedded into the transform. Cycle semantics are invalid unless iteration rules and convergence conditions are declared.
(B) Cycle admissibility conditions. A cycle is admissible if:
the iterate map is well-typed on a declared invariant set D\mathcal{D}D,
the map is contractive or otherwise provably convergent on D\mathcal{D}D,
the stopping rule is deterministic and yields a certified tolerance,
feasibility and invariant corridors remain satisfied across iterations.
(C) Cycle certificates. Cycle certificates include: contraction constants or Lyapunov proofs, iteration counts, residual decay traces, final tolerance achieved, and sensitivity to perturbations. Certificates must identify failure modes when convergence is not guaranteed (divergence, oscillation, chaos).
(D) Cycle normalization rules. Cycle normalization may replace a convergent iteration by an equivalent implicit solver or by a closed-form transform when available. Such replacements require explicit equivalence proofs and must preserve corridor budgets.
3.2.4 Optimization over (D5)(D^5)(D5)
3.2.4.1 Variational formulation of transformation goals
(A) State-targeted transformation objective. Given a target state H⋆∈H4H_\star\in H^4H⋆​∈H4 and initial state H0∈H4H_0\in H^4H0​∈H4, define a transform-selection problem:
min⁡T∈D5 J(T;H0,H⋆):=Dist(T(H0),H⋆)+λC(T),\min_{T\in D^5}\ \mathcal{J}(T;H_0,H_\star):=\mathsf{Dist}(T(H_0),H_\star)+\lambda \mathcal{C}(T),T∈D5min​ J(T;H0​,H⋆​):=Dist(T(H0​),H⋆​)+λC(T),
where Dist\mathsf{Dist}Dist is a declared metric (energy, information-weighted, gauge-invariant), and C\mathcal{C}C is the transform cost. Constraints (feasibility, invariants, stability) are either embedded in D5D^5D5 membership or imposed explicitly.
(B) Distributional objectives. For a distribution Π\PiΠ over states (e.g., dataset), define:
min⁡T∈D5 EH∼Π[Dist(T(H),T(H))]+λC(T),\min_{T\in D^5}\ \mathbb{E}_{H\sim\Pi}\big[\mathsf{Dist}(T(H),\mathcal{T}(H))\big]+\lambda\mathcal{C}(T),T∈D5min​ EH∼Π​[Dist(T(H),T(H))]+λC(T),
where T(H)\mathcal{T}(H)T(H) is a desired mapping (supervised target) or where Dist\mathsf{Dist}Dist encodes desired invariant shaping.
(C) Constraint-aware objectives. When feasibility must be enforced softly, include residual penalties:
J(T):=Dist(T(H),H⋆)+λC(T)+μ∥C(T(H))∥2,\mathcal{J}(T) := \mathsf{Dist}(T(H),H_\star)+\lambda\mathcal{C}(T)+\mu\|C(T(H))\|^2,J(T):=Dist(T(H),H⋆​)+λC(T)+μ∥C(T(H))∥2,
with μ\muμ declared. Hard-feasible transforms set μ=∞\mu=\inftyμ=∞ in the sense of restricting to TTT satisfying feasibility preservation.
(D) Multi-objective form. Optimization may be multi-objective over the cost vector c(T)\mathbf{c}(T)c(T). Solutions are Pareto-optimal within admissibility corridors; scalarization must be declared and must preserve the intended trade-off semantics.
3.2.4.2 Gradient flows and adjoints
(A) Differentiable parameterization. If transforms are parameterized TθT_\thetaTθ​ with θ∈Θ⊆Rm\theta\in\Theta\subseteq\mathbb{R}^mθ∈Θ⊆Rm and J(θ)\mathcal{J}(\theta)J(θ) is differentiable, gradient-based optimization uses:
θk+1=θk−ηk∇θJ(θk),\theta_{k+1}=\theta_k - \eta_k \nabla_\theta \mathcal{J}(\theta_k),θk+1​=θk​−ηk​∇θ​J(θk​),
with step control ηk\eta_kηk​ and domain constraints on θ\thetaθ.
(B) Adjoint method for state-dependent objectives. When J\mathcal{J}J depends on Tθ(H)T_\theta(H)Tθ​(H), gradients are computed via adjoint operators. For linear transforms, the adjoint T∗T^\astT∗ satisfies ⟨T(H),Y⟩=⟨H,T∗(Y)⟩\langle T(H),Y\rangle=\langle H,T^\ast(Y)\rangle⟨T(H),Y⟩=⟨H,T∗(Y)⟩. For nonlinear transforms, adjoints correspond to derivatives of the transform and require Fréchet differentiability in declared norms.
(C) Gradient flows in function space. For generator-based transforms exp⁡(tXθ)\exp(tX_\theta)exp(tXθ​), optimization may operate on generator parameters by differentiating through the exponential or through numerical integrators. Differentiation must be consistent with discretization and must include stability bounds to avoid exploding gradients.
(D) Corridor-aware gradients. When admissibility includes corridor constraints, gradients must incorporate constraint handling: projection onto feasible parameter sets, penalty methods, or constrained optimization (KKT) frameworks. Any gradient-based method must emit certificates verifying that intermediate iterates remain within admissible corridors or must include reject/repair actions.
3.2.4.3 Constraints via projection/penalty
(A) Projection methods. Projection-based constrained optimization uses a projection Π\PiΠ onto an admissible set Θadm⊆Θ\Theta_{\mathrm{adm}}\subseteq\ThetaΘadm​⊆Θ:
θk+1=ΠΘadm(θk−ηk∇J(θk)).\theta_{k+1}=\Pi_{\Theta_{\mathrm{adm}}}\big(\theta_k-\eta_k\nabla\mathcal{J}(\theta_k)\big).θk+1​=ΠΘadm​​(θk​−ηk​∇J(θk​)).
Projection must be deterministic and stable, and must include certificate outputs (distance to admissible set, post-projection admissibility checks).
(B) Penalty and barrier methods. Penalty methods incorporate constraints as penalties:
Jλ(θ)=J(θ)+λV(θ),\mathcal{J}_\lambda(\theta)=\mathcal{J}(\theta)+\lambda \mathcal{V}(\theta),Jλ​(θ)=J(θ)+λV(θ),
where V\mathcal{V}V measures violations (constraint residuals, invariant drift). Barrier methods use V(θ)→∞\mathcal{V}(\theta)\to\inftyV(θ)→∞ near boundary of feasibility. Penalty schedules must be explicit and deterministic.
(C) Augmented Lagrangian methods. Augmented Lagrangian methods combine multipliers with penalties for equality constraints such as feasibility preservation in parameter space. They require explicit multiplier updates and convergence conditions and are favored when constraints are stiff.
(D) Certificate-aware constraint handling. Any constraint-handling method must guarantee that the produced transform TTT satisfies admissibility axioms. If intermediate iterates violate admissibility, they must be labeled as non-admissible transient artifacts and excluded from any certificate claiming T∈D5T\in D^5T∈D5 until final admissibility is verified.
3.2.4.4 Global vs local optima
(A) Convex regimes and global optimality. Global optimality can be guaranteed when optimization is convex in the relevant representation and when admissible sets are convex. This may occur for restricted linear transforms with convex costs and convex constraints.
(B) Nonconvex regimes and local optimality. In general, D5D^5D5 optimization is nonconvex (composition, nonlinear constraints, discrete gates). Solutions are typically local optima, stationary points, or certified approximate solutions. The certificate must state the optimality notion used (global, local, stationarity, approximate).
(C) Initialization dependence and basins. Nonconvex optimization depends on initialization. Certificates must record initialization strategy and provide empirical evidence of robustness (multiple runs with controlled seeds) when claiming practical reliability.
(D) Lower bounds and impossibility. For global claims, lower bounds on J\mathcal{J}J are required. In their absence, only local claims are admissible. Any claim of global Pareto optimality must include either convexity proof or exhaustive finite search with proven completeness on the discretized space.
3.3 Cloud — Guarantees, Failure Modes, and Corridor Enforcement
3.3.1 Stability and conditioning
3.3.1.1 Condition number bounds for transforms
(A) Linear condition number. For invertible bounded linear TTT,
κ(T)=∥T∥∥T−1∥\kappa(T)=\|T\|\|T^{-1}\|κ(T)=∥T∥∥T−1∥
governs inversion sensitivity:
∥ΔH∥∥H∥≲κ(T)∥ΔY∥∥Y∥,Y=T(H).\frac{\| \Delta H\|}{\|H\|} \lesssim \kappa(T)\frac{\|\Delta Y\|}{\|Y\|}, \quad Y=T(H).∥H∥∥ΔH∥​≲κ(T)∥Y∥∥ΔY∥​,Y=T(H).
Condition number bounds are required whenever T−1T^{-1}T−1 is applied operationally.
(B) Nonlinear conditioning via local derivatives. For differentiable nonlinear transforms TTT, conditioning near HHH is measured by the derivative DT(H)DT(H)DT(H) and its inverse when locally invertible:
κT(H):=∥DT(H)∥ ∥(DT(H))−1∥.\kappa_T(H):=\|DT(H)\|\ \|(DT(H))^{-1}\|.κT​(H):=∥DT(H)∥ ∥(DT(H))−1∥.
Local conditioning is used to bound sensitivity to perturbations and is recorded as part of certificates on representative test suites.
(C) Conditioning under discretization. Discrete transforms may have conditioning that worsens with resolution. Admissibility requires either uniform conditioning bounds or explicit resolution-dependent bounds included in corridors.
(D) Conditioning diagnostics and thresholds. A transform certificate includes conditioning diagnostics: ∥T∥\|T\|∥T∥, ∥T−1∥\|T^{-1}\|∥T−1∥, κ(T)\kappa(T)κ(T) (or Lipschitz/inverse Lipschitz bounds), and explicit thresholds. Exceeding thresholds triggers reject/repair or enforces regularization.
3.3.1.2 Stability under composition
(A) Norm growth. For bounded linear TiT_iTi​,
∥Tn∘⋯∘T1∥≤∏i=1n∥Ti∥.\|T_n\circ\cdots\circ T_1\|\le \prod_{i=1}^n \|T_i\|.∥Tn​∘⋯∘T1​∥≤i=1∏n​∥Ti​∥.
For inverses, ∥(Tn⋯T1)−1∥≤∏i∥Ti−1∥\|(T_n\cdots T_1)^{-1}\|\le \prod_i \|T_i^{-1}\|∥(Tn​⋯T1​)−1∥≤∏i​∥Ti−1​∥ when each is invertible. Stability budgets are therefore multiplicative and best tracked in log scale.
(B) Lipschitz growth. For Lipschitz transforms,
LTn∘⋯∘T1≤∏i=1nLTi.L_{T_n\circ\cdots\circ T_1}\le \prod_{i=1}^n L_{T_i}.LTn​∘⋯∘T1​​≤i=1∏n​LTi​​.
Admissibility corridors constrain products to remain below stability thresholds; this motivates rewriting to reduce stability blow-up.
(C) Drift accumulation. Invariant drift typically accumulates additively under conservative bounds. For corridor budgets δi\delta_iδi​ per stage, a conservative global bound is δtot≤∑iδi\delta_{\mathrm{tot}}\le \sum_i \delta_iδtot​≤∑i​δi​. More refined bounds may exploit cancellations but require explicit proofs and are not assumed.
(D) Repair insertion effects. Feasibility repairs inserted between stages change stability and drift properties. Repair operators must have stability bounds and drift bounds; otherwise repair may dominate total drift. Repair usage must be justified by corridor feasibility requirements and must be included in cost accounting.
3.3.1.3 Sensitivity to noise/truncation
(A) Noise propagation. For a Lipschitz transform TTT,
∥T(H+η)−T(H)∥≤LT∥η∥.\|T(H+\eta)-T(H)\|\le L_T\|\eta\|.∥T(H+η)−T(H)∥≤LT​∥η∥.
For pipelines, the effective Lipschitz constant bounds noise amplification. Certificates must compute or bound this amplification factor.
(B) Truncation propagation. Let ΠN,P\Pi_{N,P}ΠN,P​ denote discretization and let TN,PT_{N,P}TN,P​ denote discrete implementation. Truncation error is controlled by:
∥T(H)−ΠN,P−1TN,P(ΠN,PH)∥≤ET(N,P),\|T(H)-\Pi^{-1}_{N,P} T_{N,P}(\Pi_{N,P}H)\|\le E_T(N,P),∥T(H)−ΠN,P−1​TN,P​(ΠN,P​H)∥≤ET​(N,P),
with ET(N,P)→0E_T(N,P)\to 0ET​(N,P)→0 under refinement for consistent implementations. This bound must be declared or empirically certified for transform portability across resolutions.
(C) Mixed noise–truncation envelope. When observation noise and truncation both exist, total error is bounded by the sum of propagated noise and truncation bounds, with explicit constants:
∥TN,P(HN,P+ηN,P)−T(H)∥≤LTN,P∥ηN,P∥+ET(N,P)+∥TN,P(HN,P)−ΠN,PT(H)∥.\|T_{N,P}(H_{N,P}+\eta_{N,P}) - T(H)\| \le L_{T_{N,P}}\|\eta_{N,P}\| + E_T(N,P) + \|T_{N,P}(H_{N,P})-\Pi_{N,P}T(H)\|.∥TN,P​(HN,P​+ηN,P​)−T(H)∥≤LTN,P​​∥ηN,P​∥+ET​(N,P)+∥TN,P​(HN,P​)−ΠN,P​T(H)∥.
(D) Corridor enforcement under perturbations. Admissibility requires that feasibility and invariants remain within corridor budgets under declared perturbation envelopes. This yields robust admissibility: transforms remain valid for noisy or approximate states within specified bounds.
3.3.1.4 Robustness theorems
(A) Robust feasibility preservation. If TTT is residual-contractive:
∥C(T(H))∥≤α∥C(H)∥+β,\|C(T(H))\|\le \alpha \|C(H)\| + \beta,∥C(T(H))∥≤α∥C(H)∥+β,
then for corridor-feasible HHH with ∥C(H)∥≤τ\|C(H)\|\le \tau∥C(H)∥≤τ, feasibility remains bounded by ατ+β\alpha\tau+\betaατ+β. Choosing τ\tauτ such that ατ+β≤τ\alpha\tau+\beta\le\tauατ+β≤τ yields invariance of the corridor-feasible set.
(B) Robust invariant drift control. If invariants are Lipschitz with constant LInvL_{\mathrm{Inv}}LInv​ in a chosen norm and TTT is Lipschitz with constant LTL_TLT​, then for perturbation η\etaη,
∥Inv(T(H+η))−Inv(T(H))∥≤LInvLT∥η∥.\|\mathrm{Inv}(T(H+\eta))-\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))\| \le L_{\mathrm{Inv}} L_T \|\eta\|.∥Inv(T(H+η))−Inv(T(H))∥≤LInv​LT​∥η∥.
This provides a computable drift amplification bound used in corridor budgets.
(C) Robust reconstruction compatibility. If TTT preserves reconstructability envelopes (see §3.3.2.3) and is stable, then reconstruction after transformation remains stable under noise and truncation, with bounds derived from stability constants of TTT, reconstruction operators, and the coupling between them.
(D) Robustness under composition. Robustness constants propagate through composition multiplicatively (stability) and additively (drift/residual bounds). Robust admissibility is therefore maintained by tracking these propagation laws and enforcing corridor thresholds at pipeline design time and at runtime verification checkpoints.
3.3.2 Admissibility testing
3.3.2.1 Constraint residual propagation
(A) Residual propagation model. Let r(H)=∥C(H)∥Zr(H)=\|C(H)\|_{\mathcal{Z}}r(H)=∥C(H)∥Z​. A transform TTT is admissible only if it provides a certified propagation bound:
r(T(H))≤αTr(H)+βT∀H∈D(T),r(T(H))\le \alpha_T r(H)+\beta_T \quad \forall H\in \mathcal{D}(T),r(T(H))≤αT​r(H)+βT​∀H∈D(T),
with αT,βT\alpha_T,\beta_TαT​,βT​ declared and certified (analytic or empirical).
(B) Stage-wise residual checks. For a pipeline T=Tn⋯T1T=T_n\cdots T_1T=Tn​⋯T1​, residual checks are performed after each stage. Certificates record:
pre-stage residual,
post-stage residual,
predicted bound,
observed value,
pass/fail decision.
(C) Residual localization. When residuals are spatially or parametrically localized, testing includes regional residual norms rA(H)r_A(H)rA​(H). Localization supports targeted repairs and identifies which constraints are violated.
(D) Residual repair protocol. If residual exceeds corridor threshold, the pipeline must either reject the transform application or apply a declared repair transform P\mathcal{P}P with its own admissibility certificate. Repair itself must be bounded in displacement and invariant drift.
3.3.2.2 Invariant drift bounds across scales
(A) Drift measurement. For invariant suite Inv\mathrm{Inv}Inv, compute drift:
ΔInv(H;T)=Inv(T(H))−Inv(H).\Delta_{\mathrm{Inv}}(H;T)=\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H).ΔInv​(H;T)=Inv(T(H))−Inv(H).
Drift is tested against componentwise or weighted budgets.
(B) Scale-indexed drift testing. For invariants IϵjI_{\epsilon_j}Iϵj​​, drift testing is performed at each required scale ϵj\epsilon_jϵj​. The test suite must include scales corresponding to the discretization ladder and to reconstruction-critical bands.
(C) Drift attribution. Testing must attribute drift to:
transform semantics (intended changes),
numerical rounding,
truncation/discretization mismatch,
noise amplification. Attribution requires logging intermediate states and computing partial contributions when possible.
(D) Drift reject/repair. If drift violates budget, the system must reject, adjust parameters (e.g., reduce step sizes ttt), increase regularization, or apply a repair map designed to restore invariants. Repair must be admissible and must not introduce larger violations.
3.3.2.3 Reconstruction-compatibility checks
(A) Reconstruction envelope preservation. Let ε(A)\varepsilon(A)ε(A) denote the reconstruction envelope for observation region/mask AAA. A transform TTT is reconstruction-compatible if, for declared observers OA\mathcal{O}_AOA​ and reconstructions RAR_ARA​, the post-transform state remains reconstructable within corridor:
sup⁡H∈D∥RA(OA(T(H)))−T(H)∥≤εT(A),\sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}} \|R_A(\mathcal{O}_A(T(H))) - T(H)\| \le \varepsilon_T(A),H∈Dsup​∥RA​(OA​(T(H)))−T(H)∥≤εT​(A),
with εT(A)\varepsilon_T(A)εT​(A) bounded by declared budgets and not exceeding reconstructability thresholds.
(B) Commutation tests with observation–reconstruction pipelines. A stronger compatibility condition tests approximate commutation:
T∘RA≈RA∘OA∘T∘RA,T\circ R_A \approx R_A\circ \mathcal{O}_A\circ T\circ R_A,T∘RA​≈RA​∘OA​∘T∘RA​,
on observation-consistent states. This ensures that transforming and then reconstructing yields consistent outcomes with reconstructing and then transforming.
(C) Mask family coverage. Compatibility must be tested over a declared mask family {Ai}\{A_i\}{Ai​} representing expected observation regimes. Certificates record worst-case and quantile envelope values over the family.
(D) Compatibility under discretization. Compatibility must be tested at each relevant resolution (N,P)(N,P)(N,P) or under a declared scaling law. Failure at higher resolutions indicates discretization inconsistency or instability and invalidates reconstruction compatibility claims.
3.3.2.4 Reject/repair strategies
(A) Reject strategy. If a transform application violates admissibility (feasibility, invariants, stability, reconstructability), the system must reject and return a certificate stating which predicate failed, with measured values and thresholds.
(B) Repair strategy via feasibility projection. If constraint violation is the primary issue, apply a feasibility repair P\mathcal{P}P to project back to H4H^4H4 or to Hτ4H^4_\tauHτ4​. Repair is admissible only if its displacement and induced drift are bounded.
(C) Repair strategy via parameter adjustment. If drift or stability violations arise, adjust transform parameters (reduce step size ttt, increase regularization, shorten program length) under deterministic rules and rerun checks. Such adaptive repair must be deterministic and included in program semantics.
(D) Repair strategy via program rewriting. If violations arise from an unstable ordering or from non-commutativity effects, rewrite the program to an equivalent or approximately equivalent form with improved stability (e.g., symmetric splitting). Rewriting is admissible only with explicit rewrite certificates and with re-validation of corridor constraints.
3.3.3 Invertibility regimes
3.3.3.1 Exact inverse vs approximate inverse
(A) Exact inverses. Exact inversion is required for automorphisms and for reversible pipelines. A transform TTT has exact inverse T−1T^{-1}T−1 if it is bijective on the declared domain and both TTT and T−1T^{-1}T−1 are admissible with bounded stability constants.
(B) Approximate inverses. An approximate inverse SSS satisfies:
sup⁡H∈D∥S(T(H))−H∥≤δinv,\sup_{H\in\mathcal{D}} \|S(T(H)) - H\| \le \delta_{\mathrm{inv}},H∈Dsup​∥S(T(H))−H∥≤δinv​,
with δinv\delta_{\mathrm{inv}}δinv​ declared. Approximate inversion is used when TTT is not exactly invertible (due to dissipation, truncation) but can be inverted up to a known envelope.
(C) Gauge-relative inversion. When states are defined modulo gauge, inversion may be defined at the pattern level:
dG(S(T(H)),H)≤δinv.d_{\mathcal{G}}(S(T(H)),H)\le \delta_{\mathrm{inv}}.dG​(S(T(H)),H)≤δinv​.
Inversion certificates must specify whether inversion is strict in H4H^4H4 or modulo gauge in PATT\mathcal{PATT}PATT.
(D) Stability of inversion. Approximate inverses must include stability bounds:
∥S(y)−S(y′)∥≤LS∥y−y′∥,\|S(y)-S(y')\|\le L_S\|y-y'\|,∥S(y)−S(y′)∥≤LS​∥y−y′∥,
ensuring that inversion does not amplify noise beyond corridor limits.
3.3.3.2 Pseudoinverse and regularization
(A) Pseudoinverses for linear maps. For linear maps T:H1→H2T:\mathcal{H}_1\to\mathcal{H}_2T:H1​→H2​ between Hilbert spaces with closed range, the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse T†T^\daggerT† is defined by the Penrose equations and yields least-norm solutions for inverse problems.
(B) Regularized inverses. When TTT is ill-conditioned or non-invertible, define regularized inversion:
Sλ:=(T∗T+λI)−1T∗,S_\lambda := (T^\ast T + \lambda I)^{-1}T^\ast,Sλ​:=(T∗T+λI)−1T∗,
or more general Tikhonov/proximal regularizers. Regularization introduces bias controlled by λ\lambdaλ, which must be bounded and included in certificates.
(C) Constraint-aware pseudoinverses. When inversion must remain in H4H^4H4, pseudoinverse solutions must be projected or constrained:
H^:=arg⁡min⁡H∈H4∥T(H)−y∥2+λR(H),\widehat{H} := \arg\min_{H\in H^4} \|T(H)-y\|^2 + \lambda \mathcal{R}(H),H:=argH∈H4min​∥T(H)−y∥2+λR(H),
with feasibility explicitly enforced. Such inverses are generally nonlinear even if TTT is linear on H\mathfrak{H}H.
(D) Certificate requirements. Pseudoinverse and regularized inverse certificates include conditioning estimates, regularization parameters, bias bounds, noise amplification bounds, and empirical validation on gold test suites.
3.3.3.3 Domain restriction and partial invertibility
(A) Invertibility on submanifolds/subspaces. A transform may be invertible only on a subset M⊆H4\mathcal{M}\subseteq H^4M⊆H4 (e.g., band-limited subspace, constrained code subspace). In this case, T:M→T(M)T:\mathcal{M}\to T(\mathcal{M})T:M→T(M) is bijective and an inverse exists on T(M)T(\mathcal{M})T(M), with explicit stability bounds.
(B) Local invertibility. If TTT is Fréchet differentiable and DT(H)DT(H)DT(H) is invertible at HHH, then TTT is locally invertible near HHH. Local inverses are admissible only when the neighborhood is explicitly declared and stability bounds hold uniformly on that neighborhood.
(C) Partial inversion in pipelines. Pipelines may require only partial inversion of certain stages (e.g., invertible preprocessing followed by non-invertible smoothing). The pipeline type must specify which parts require inversion and which are lossy. Certificates must match the declared inversion regime.
(D) Partial invertibility certificates. Certificates must specify: inversion domain, inversion method, inversion error envelope, stability of inversion, and any dependence on discretization parameters (N,P)(N,P)(N,P).
3.3.3.4 No-inverse conditions and certificates
(A) Structural non-invertibility. Transforms that collapse degrees of freedom (projections, averaging, thresholding, dissipative semigroups) are not invertible. Non-invertibility is structural and must be declared. Attempting inversion without regularization is inadmissible.
(B) Observational non-invertibility. If a transform discards phase or maps multiple patterns to the same observation signature, inversion is ambiguous. Such ambiguity classes must be characterized; inversion is defined only as set-valued or as a selected representative via priors and canonicalization.
(C) Certified no-inverse statements. A no-inverse certificate consists of:
identification of a nontrivial kernel or non-injectivity witness (e.g., ΔH≠0\Delta H\neq 0ΔH=0 with T(H)=T(H+ΔH)T(H)=T(H+\Delta H)T(H)=T(H+ΔH)), or
a proof that TTT is not surjective onto the codomain (e.g., range is a proper subset), or
a conditioning blow-up certificate indicating that any inverse would be unstable beyond corridor thresholds.
(D) Operational consequence. If no-inverse is certified, pipelines must use reconstruction/estimation frameworks that operate under ambiguity (regularization, priors, feasibility constraints) and must present outputs as pattern-level or corridor-feasible approximations with explicit uncertainty envelopes.
3.3.4 Existence/uniqueness of optimal transforms
3.3.4.1 Coercivity and lower semicontinuity
(A) Direct method setup. Consider an optimization problem over transforms in a feasible class A⊆D5\mathcal{A}\subseteq D^5A⊆D5:
min⁡T∈AJ(T),\min_{T\in\mathcal{A}} \mathcal{J}(T),T∈Amin​J(T),
where J(T)\mathcal{J}(T)J(T) includes cost and misfit terms and where A\mathcal{A}A is closed under a declared topology.
(B) Coercivity. A sufficient condition for existence is coercivity: J(T)→∞\mathcal{J}(T)\to\inftyJ(T)→∞ when TTT leaves every bounded subset of the transform space under the chosen topology (e.g., when stability norms or complexity costs diverge). In practice, coercivity is enforced by explicit penalties on ∥T∥\|T\|∥T∥, ∥T−1∥\|T^{-1}\|∥T−1∥, and program length.
(C) Lower semicontinuity. Lower semicontinuity of J\mathcal{J}J under the chosen topology ensures that limits of minimizing sequences do not increase cost. For convex costs in linear operator spaces, weak lower semicontinuity is typical; for nonconvex costs, lsc must be established by explicit arguments or replaced by approximate existence claims.
(D) Closed admissible set. The admissible set A\mathcal{A}A must be closed under the chosen convergence notion (e.g., strong operator convergence on a declared bounded domain, or convergence of program parameters with fixed program structure). Closure fails when admissibility corridors depend discontinuously on TTT (e.g., due to non-deterministic branching); such cases require explicit restrictions.
3.3.4.2 Compactness arguments for minimizers
(A) Compactness in parameterized families. When transforms are parameterized by θ∈Θ⊂Rm\theta\in\Theta\subset\mathbb{R}^mθ∈Θ⊂Rm, compactness is obtained by restricting Θ\ThetaΘ to compact sets or by coercivity ensuring bounded minimizing sequences. Then any minimizing sequence has a convergent subsequence.
(B) Compactness in gate programs. For discrete gate programs, compactness corresponds to finiteness of the search space under bounded length and bounded parameter quantization. Existence is then trivial by finite minimization but must include completeness of the search procedure.
(C) Compactness in operator topologies. For infinite-dimensional operator spaces, compactness is rare; existence results therefore rely on restricting to compact parameter families or using weak compactness in reflexive parameter spaces plus continuity of induced transforms on bounded domains.
(D) Minimizer certification. Any existence claim must produce not only a transform but also a certificate that the produced transform lies in the admissible set and achieves the claimed cost relative to declared competitor class (global or local optimality claims).
3.3.4.3 Uniqueness conditions
