For invariants that are not Lipschitz (e.g., topological signatures), noise-aware bounds require smoothing or robust summaries; without robustness, only empirical envelopes are admissible.
(D) Concentration under stochastic noise (ensemble statements).If (\eta) is random with declared distribution and independent of (H), bounds may be stated in probability or expectation. Such statements must declare: noise distribution, independence assumptions, and whether bounds are conditional on (H) or averaged over an ensemble. Corridor enforcement for safety-critical invariants must not rely solely on probabilistic bounds; it must include worst-case envelopes on declared noise budgets.
4.3.1.3 Bounds under reconstruction masks
(A) Masking as information loss operator.Let (\mathcal{O}_A) be a mask/sampling operator observing only part of the domain or coefficients. Masking reduces available information about (H). Entropy of the induced distribution (\pi_H) is not directly bounded by masking unless a specific reconstruction or conditional model is declared; therefore, bounds under masks are formulated for identifiability and for reconstructible invariant ranges.
(B) Observable invariant bounds.Define the observable invariant set at mask (A):[\mathcal{S}_A(H):={\mathrm{Inv}(H'):\ H'\in H^4,\ \mathcal{O}A(H')=\mathcal{O}A(H)}.]Then for any invariant component (k), define mask-induced uncertainty:[\mathrm{Width}{A,k}(H):=\sup{v\in\mathcal{S}A(H)} v_k - \inf{v\in\mathcal{S}_A(H)} v_k.]Large width indicates that the mask fails to determine the invariant, even if the state can be reconstructed only up to ambiguity.
(C) Bounds from stability/frames.When (\mathcal{O}_A) is stable on a subspace (frame bounds, restricted injectivity), reconstruction error (\varepsilon(A)) bounds invariant error via Lipschitz constants:[|\mathrm{Inv}_k(\widehat{H})-\mathrm{Inv}_k(H)|\le L_k,|\widehat{H}-H|\le L_k,\varepsilon(A).]Thus mask-induced invariant uncertainty is controlled in regimes with stable reconstruction; otherwise, uncertainty may be unbounded.
(D) Mask sharpness via saturating examples.To certify sharpness, one must exhibit families (H_1,H_2\in H^4) with identical observations but differing invariants, demonstrating lower bounds on (\mathrm{Width}_{A,k}(H)). Such examples are part of no-go certification (§4.3.3) and guide corridor design by identifying invariants that cannot be reliably controlled under given observation regimes.
4.3.1.4 Sharpness and saturating examples
(A) Sharpness definition.An upper bound on an invariant deviation is sharp on a domain (\mathcal{D}) if there exists a sequence of admissible perturbations or approximations for which the bound is achieved asymptotically (up to constants). Sharpness is not required for safety corridors but is used to avoid overly conservative budgets in optimization.
(B) Truncation sharpness examples.For spectral truncation, the maximal change in spectral entropy occurs when energy is concentrated in removed bands. A saturating example is a state (H) with (|P_{\mathrm{low}}H|\ll |P_{\mathrm{high}}H|), yielding dramatic changes after truncation. Such examples calibrate the necessity of including truncation envelopes in invariant corridors.
(C) Noise sharpness examples.For entropy under additive noise, near-sparse distributions (most mass concentrated in a few bins) can have high sensitivity: small perturbations can redistribute small probabilities substantially relative to their baseline. These examples justify smoothing policies and/or minimal-probability floors to stabilize Rényi/Tsallis computations.
(D) Mask sharpness examples.For masking, a saturating ambiguity example is any nonzero (\Delta H\in H^4\cap\ker(\mathcal{O}_A)). Then (H) and (H+\lambda\Delta H) share observations for all (\lambda), yet can have arbitrarily different invariants unless constraints couple (\Delta H) to observed regions/bands. Such examples are used to certify impossibility and to motivate additional coupling constraints or richer observers.
4.3.2 Identifiability from partial observations
4.3.2.1 Necessary conditions
(A) Injectivity modulo gauge.A necessary condition for pattern identifiability from observation (y=\mathcal{O}(H)) is injectivity modulo gauge on the domain:[\mathcal{O}(H_1)=\mathcal{O}(H_2)\ \Rightarrow\ H_1\sim_{\mathcal{G}} H_2.]If this fails, no algorithm can uniquely recover patterns from observations in the worst case.
(B) Dimensional counting in finite models.In finite-dimensional truncations where (H) is represented by (M) degrees of freedom and constraints reduce to an effective dimension (d_{\mathrm{eff}}), a necessary (not sufficient) condition for generic identifiability is that observation dimension (\dim(\mathcal{Y})) is at least (d_{\mathrm{eff}}) after accounting for gauge degrees of freedom. This condition is used as a diagnostic; it is not a guarantee.
(C) Information lower bounds under probabilistic models.Under an ensemble model (\mathbf{H}\sim\Pi), identifiability of (\mathbf{H}) from (\mathbf{Y}=\mathcal{O}(\mathbf{H})+\eta) requires sufficient mutual information (I(\mathbf{H};\mathbf{Y})). If a decoder must choose among (M) equiprobable hypotheses, a necessary condition is that (I(\mathbf{H};\mathbf{Y})) be large enough to distinguish them within target error probability; formal thresholds depend on the declared decoding criterion and hypothesis model.
(D) Constraint coupling necessity.If (\ker(\mathcal{O})\cap H^4) contains nontrivial directions, identifiability fails unless additional constraints or multiple observations remove that intersection. Therefore, a necessary structural condition is that constraints provide coupling across unobserved degrees of freedom in a way that eliminates observational null modes on the declared domain.
4.3.2.2 Sufficient conditions
(A) Stable injectivity (bi-Lipschitz) on a domain.A sufficient condition for robust identifiability is the existence of constants (0<\alpha\le \beta<\infty) such that[\alpha,d_{\mathcal{G}}(H_1,H_2)\le |\mathcal{O}(H_1)-\mathcal{O}(H_2)|{\mathcal{Y}}\le \beta,d{\mathcal{G}}(H_1,H_2),]for all (H_1,H_2) in the declared domain. This yields both uniqueness and stability, and implies reconstruction error bounds under noise.
(B) Frame conditions for coefficient observations.If observations are frame coefficients and the observed subset forms a frame for the target subspace, then stable reconstruction exists and thus identifiability holds. The frame bounds provide explicit stability constants that propagate to invariant stability via Lipschitz bounds.
(C) Unique continuation under PDE constraints (model-conditional).When constraints include PDE residuals with unique continuation properties, boundary or partial interior observations may uniquely determine the solution under sufficient regularity and boundary conditions. Such results must be stated with explicit operator assumptions, domain regularity, and observation geometry; they are not generic and are used only when the embedding is declared.
(D) Multi-observer sufficiency.If a set of observers ({\mathcal{O}{A_k}}{k=1}^K) yields a combined map (\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{all}}) that is injective modulo gauge (and stable), identifiability is restored. This is the canonical mechanism for resolving aliasing or phase ambiguities; sufficiency is certified by analytic bounds or by verified discrete injectivity tests on gold-state families.
4.3.2.3 Ambiguity classes under gauge
(A) Orbit ambiguity.Gauge ambiguity is unavoidable: all observations that are gauge-invariant cannot distinguish (H) from (g\cdot H). Therefore, ambiguity classes are at least gauge orbits ([H]_{\mathcal{G}}), and identifiability is defined on (\mathcal{PATT}=H^4/\mathcal{G}).
(B) Hidden symmetries and enlarged ambiguity.If both constraints and observations are invariant under a larger group (\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{hidden}}\supsetneq\mathcal{G}), then the true ambiguity classes are orbits of (\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{hidden}}). Hidden symmetries must be explicitly detected and incorporated as gauge or fixed by additional constraints; otherwise, identifiability claims are invalid.
(C) Invariant-equivalence ambiguity.If (\mathrm{Inv}) is used as a proxy identifier, then invariant-equivalence classes[\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{Inv}}(H):={H'\in H^4:\ \mathrm{Inv}(H')=\mathrm{Inv}(H)}]define an ambiguity class. A suite is admissible for identification only if the diameters of these classes (modulo gauge) are bounded within declared tolerances on the intended domain.
(D) Practical ambiguity certificates.Ambiguity is certified by constructing explicit pairs or families (H_1,H_2) with identical observations (and possibly identical invariants) but differing states beyond gauge. Such certificates are stored and referenced as “no-go witnesses” for specific observer/invariant configurations.
4.3.2.4 Practical identifiability tests
(A) Null-space search (discrete).In finite models, test identifiability by searching for nontrivial (\Delta H) satisfying both feasibility and observational null constraints:[C_{N,P}(\Delta H)=0,\qquad \mathcal{O}_{A,N,P}(\Delta H)=0.]Any nonzero solution certifies non-identifiability. The search must be deterministic and must report conditioning and numerical tolerances.
(B) Local injectivity via Jacobians (parameterized families).In parameterized families (H_\theta), test local identifiability by rank of the Jacobian of the observation map (and/or invariant map). Rank deficiency indicates local ambiguity. Jacobian tests must be repeated across representative (\theta) values to avoid false conclusions due to local degeneracies.
(C) Perturbation-and-reconstruct stress tests.Given a gold state (H), generate perturbations (H+\Delta H) within feasibility corridors (via feasibility repair), observe, reconstruct, and compare pattern-level distances and invariant drift. Persistent ambiguity across perturbations indicates non-identifiability or instability.
(D) Certificate structure for identifiability tests.Each identifiability test produces a certificate containing:
observer IDs and parameters,
discretization and constraint IDs,
discovered null modes (if any) with canonical IDs,
stability/injectivity metrics (frame bounds, condition numbers),
pass/fail decision with tolerance policies,
reproducibility manifest (seeds, solver versions).
4.3.3 Information-theoretic no-go results
4.3.3.1 Undersampling impossibility results
(A) Deterministic undersampling (linear).If (\mathcal{O}) is linear and (\dim(\ker(\mathcal{O})\cap \mathcal{M})>0) on a subspace (\mathcal{M}\subseteq H^4), then (\mathcal{O}) is not injective on (\mathcal{M}) and exact recovery on (\mathcal{M}) is impossible in the worst case. This is the fundamental undersampling obstruction.
(B) Constraint-limited degrees of freedom.Even with constraints, if the effective degrees of freedom exceed observation dimension (after accounting for gauge), then generic identifiability fails unless additional structure (e.g., sparsity with known support patterns) is imposed. Any such additional structure must be explicitly encoded as constraints or priors; it cannot be assumed implicitly.
(C) Adversarial construction principle.Given any observation operator (\mathcal{O}), adversarial ambiguity can be constructed by selecting two feasible states that coincide on the observed subspace and differ in an unobserved subspace that is not constrained by coupling constraints. This principle is used to generate no-go witnesses and to guide the design of coupling constraints.
(D) No-go certification artifact.An undersampling no-go certificate includes:
explicit pair (H_1,H_2\in H^4) (or (\Delta H\neq 0)) with identical observations,
proof or verified computation that both are feasible within tolerance,
demonstration that they are not gauge-equivalent,
quantified separation in a gauge-invariant metric.
4.3.3.2 Phase retrieval limits
(A) Magnitude-only observation ambiguity.If observations discard phase (e.g., (|\langle H,\phi_i\rangle|) or (|\widehat{H}|)), then (H) and (e^{i\theta}H) are always indistinguishable, and additional nontrivial ambiguities may exist (conjugate symmetry, reflection ambiguities, or more general phase-flip constructions) depending on the sensing family.
(B) Necessary diversity of measurements.Phase retrieval is only possible under sufficiently diverse measurements (e.g., multiple masks, coded diffraction patterns, or nonlinear constraints coupling phase). The framework treats this as a structural requirement: a single magnitude-only observation is insufficient except on restricted domains with additional constraints.
(C) Constraint-based phase fixing.Feasible phase retrieval regimes must explicitly incorporate phase-fixing constraints or additional observables that render phase identifiable modulo a reduced gauge. Any claim of phase identifiability must specify the exact constraint set and the observation diversity used.
(D) Phase retrieval no-go certificates.A phase no-go certificate provides:
two feasible states with identical magnitude observations,
non-gauge-equivalence (beyond global phase),
proof that constraints and observations are insufficient to break ambiguity on the intended domain,
minimal additional observation/constraint required to restore uniqueness (as a design recommendation).
4.3.3.3 Adversarial ambiguity constructions
(A) Null-mode injection.If (\Delta H\in H^4\cap\ker(\mathcal{O})), then (H) and (H+\Delta H) are observationally indistinguishable. Construct (\Delta H) to maximize change in targeted invariants (entropy, topology, symmetry defect) while keeping feasibility, yielding worst-case invariant ambiguity under a given observer.
(B) Mask-localized adversaries.For region masks, construct (\Delta H) supported in the unobserved region and ensure constraints do not force it to be zero (lack of coupling). Such adversaries demonstrate that local observations cannot bound global invariants unless coupling constraints propagate information.
(C) Scale-localized adversaries.For band-limited observations, construct (\Delta H) supported in unobserved spectral bands. If constraints do not couple bands, invariants depending on fine scales are unidentifiable. These adversaries are used to justify scale-coupling constraints and multi-band observation design.
(D) Certified adversarial suite for regression.Adversarial constructions are included in a regression suite: a set of certified ambiguous pairs that must remain ambiguous under unchanged observers/constraints. This prevents false claims of identifiability caused by implementation artifacts or insufficient testing.
4.3.3.4 Certifying impossibility
(A) Constructive certificate requirements.An impossibility claim is admissible only if supported by a constructive witness:
either a null mode (\Delta H\neq 0) satisfying feasibility and zero observation,
or a pair (H_1,H_2) with identical observations and proven non-equivalence,
or a proven lower bound showing observation cannot separate a declared hypothesis class.
(B) Numerical certification with tolerances.When proofs are impractical, impossibility may be certified numerically with deterministic tolerances:[|C(H_i)|\le \tau,\quad|\mathcal{O}(H_1)-\mathcal{O}(H_2)|\le \tau_{\mathrm{obs}},\quadd_{\mathcal{G}}(H_1,H_2)\ge \gamma_{\min}.]All tolerances must be stated and must be conservative relative to floating-point error envelopes.
(C) Scope restriction and domain declaration.Impossibility is always scoped to a declared domain (\mathcal{D}\subseteq H^4) and to declared observers/constraints. A certificate must explicitly state scope; it does not generalize beyond that scope unless additional arguments are provided.
(D) Design consequence extraction.Every impossibility certificate must include a minimal design consequence: what must change (observer diversity, constraint coupling, gauge fixing, resolution) to remove the witness. This converts no-go results into actionable framework refinement.
4.3.4 Corridor design for information control
4.3.4.1 Drift vector budgets ((\delta))
(A) Budget vector definition.For invariant vector (\mathrm{Inv}\in\mathbb{R}^m), define a corridor budget (\delta\in\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^m) specifying allowable drift magnitude for each invariant component under admissible transforms.
(B) Budget sources and decomposition.Budgets decompose into contributions:[\delta = \delta_{\mathrm{model}}+\delta_{\mathrm{disc}}+\delta_{\mathrm{noise}}+\delta_{\mathrm{algo}},]accounting respectively for modeling mismatch, discretization/truncation, noise, and algorithmic approximation. Each component must be estimated or bounded; corridor enforcement must use the sum.
(C) Absolute vs relative budgets.Some invariants require relative budgets (scale-free), e.g.,[\frac{|\Delta_k|}{|\mathrm{Inv}_k(H)|+\epsilon_0}\le \delta_k^{\mathrm{rel}},]with a fixed (\epsilon_0>0) to avoid division by zero. The budget type (absolute/relative) is part of the corridor specification.
(D) Budget witness artifacts.Budgets must be justified by witness artifacts: refinement studies, noise stress tests, solver error traces, and stability bounds. Budgets without witnesses are not admissible for external guarantees.
4.3.4.2 Multi-scale budget allocation
(A) Scale-weighted allocation.For multi-scale entropies (H_{\epsilon_k}), allocate budgets by weights (w_k) reflecting criticality and expected numerical sensitivity:[\delta_k^{\mathrm{Sh}} = \delta_{\mathrm{tot}}\cdot \frac{w_k}{\sum_j w_j}.]Weights are fixed by the corridor policy and must be justified (e.g., reconstruction sensitivity at scale (k)).
(B) Coupled budgets for dependent invariants.When invariants are coupled (e.g., band energies sum to 1), independent componentwise budgets can be inconsistent. Use coupled constraints such as:[|\Delta|{W}\le \Delta{\max}\quad\text{and}\quad\sum_k \Delta_k = 0 \ \text{(when normalization requires)}.]Coupled budgets reduce false violations caused by dependent components.
(C) Region-aware allocation.Allocate separate budgets per region or per region pair for mutual information invariants:[\delta_{A,B,k},]ensuring that corridor policies can protect critical couplings (e.g., boundary-to-interior coupling) more strongly than incidental couplings.
(D) Allocation under resource constraints.Budget enforcement has computational cost (especially topological invariants). Allocate budgets to a tiered invariant set: cheap invariants monitored continuously, expensive invariants monitored periodically or on-demand, with conservative budgets bridging the intervals.
4.3.4.3 Automatic budget tightening/loosening
(A) Adaptive calibration rule.Budgets may be adapted based on observed stability envelopes under controlled tests. Define an update rule:[\delta^{(t+1)} = \mathrm{Clip}\big(\alpha,\delta^{(t)} + (1-\alpha),\widehat{\delta}{\mathrm{obs}}\big),]where (\widehat{\delta}{\mathrm{obs}}) is an empirically estimated envelope, (\alpha\in[0,1]) is a smoothing parameter, and (\mathrm{Clip}) enforces hard lower/upper bounds to prevent unsafe loosening.
(B) Tightening triggers.Tighten budgets when:
regression tests reveal drift beyond predicted envelopes,
numerical stability worsens (conditioning increases),
reconstruction error increases for gold states under the same transforms,
invariants show increased sensitivity under noise stress tests.
(C) Loosening triggers with safety guards.Loosen budgets only when:
stability certificates improve (better conditioning, tighter analytic bounds),
refinement studies demonstrate reduced discretization envelopes,
solver accuracy improves with verified determinism,and only up to a cap determined by safety and reconstruction prerequisites.
(D) Versioned corridor policies.Any budget change constitutes a corridor policy version change. Policies must be versioned, hashed, and linked to all admissibility certificates. Comparisons across experiments are valid only under identical corridor policy versions.
4.3.4.4 Failure reporting structure
(A) Minimal failure record.A corridor failure record contains:
transform ID and parameters,
input state ID and output state ID,
invariant suite ID,
budgets (\delta),
drift vector (\Delta),
the set of violated components and violation magnitudes.
(B) Localization and scale attribution.Failures must be localized by:
scale index (k) (for multi-scale invariants),
region pair ((A,B)) (for MI),
band index (for spectral invariants),
filtration threshold range (for topological summaries).Localization is required to support deterministic repair strategies.
(C) Root-cause classification.Each failure is classified into a diagnostic taxonomy:
truncation envelope exceeded,
noise envelope exceeded,
algorithmic nonconvergence,
transform instability (conditioning),
constraint violation coupling into invariants,
nondeterminism/serialization mismatch.Classification rules are deterministic and documented.
(D) Repair recommendation output.Failure reports must include a deterministic repair recommendation, such as:
increase resolution,
increase smoothing parameter for robust invariants,
apply feasibility repair before invariant computation,
switch to a more stable solver,
tighten transform step size or regularization schedule,
reject transform as inadmissible.Recommendations must map directly to implementable pipeline actions in §4.4.3.
4.4 Fractal — Computing and Verifying Invariants
4.4.1 Practical estimators
4.4.1.1 Histogram and kernel estimators
(A) Histogram estimator for (p(\epsilon)).Given discrete state (H^{(N,P)}) and partition (\mathcal{B}\epsilon), compute[\widehat{p}i(\epsilon)=\frac{\sum{z\in B_i^\epsilon} |H(z)|^2 w(z)}{\sum{z} |H(z)|^2 w(z)}.]Implementation must enforce deterministic iteration order and stable summation (fixed reduction ordering).
(B) Kernel density estimation (KDE) for (\rho_\epsilon).Given samples (z_j) from (\pi_H) (or grid points with weights), define[\widehat{\rho}\epsilon(z)=\sum_j \omega_j K\epsilon(z,z_j),]with deterministic weights (\omega_j). KDE requires explicit bandwidth selection and boundary correction rules; bandwidth is a corridor parameter that affects entropy estimates.
(C) Entropy estimation from histograms/KDE.Compute Shannon entropy from (\widehat{p}) directly. For continuous entropy from (\widehat{\rho}\epsilon), compute (-\int \widehat{\rho}\epsilon\log\widehat{\rho}_\epsilon,dm) via deterministic quadrature. Any bias correction method must be deterministic and versioned.
(D) Error bars and conservative envelopes.Estimators must output uncertainty measures:
sampling variance estimates (if sampling used),
discretization bias proxies (compare adjacent (\epsilon) or refinement levels),
numerical rounding bounds.Corridor checks must use conservative envelopes combining these uncertainties.
4.4.1.2 Wavelet energy estimators
(A) Wavelet decomposition determinism.Wavelet estimators require fixed wavelet family, boundary extension method, decomposition depth, and coefficient ordering. All are part of the invariant suite ID.
(B) Band energy computation.Compute (E_j(H)=|W_j H|^2) in the discrete inner product. If the wavelet transform is not exactly orthonormal under the discrete inner product, report leakage bounds and use corrected normalization.
(C) Spectral entropy from band energies.Compute normalized band distribution (q_j=E_j/\sum E_j) and apply entropy functionals. For corridor control, store both raw energies (E_j) and normalized (q_j) to disambiguate drift due to scaling changes vs redistribution.
(D) Multi-scale consistency checks.Verify that (\sum_j E_j(H)) matches (|H|^2) within tolerance. Verify monotone behavior under known smoothing transforms (e.g., diffusion decreases high-(j) energies). These checks serve as internal validation of estimator correctness.
4.4.1.3 Spectral estimators
(A) Fourier-based estimators (when applicable).When (\Omega) supports FFT (periodic or padded), compute (\widehat{H}(k,p)) and define spectral density (|\widehat{H}|^2). Band energies are computed by summing over frequency shells/bins with deterministic bin definitions.
(B) Eigenfunction-based estimators (general domains).When (\Omega) is a general bounded domain, estimate spectral content via eigenfunctions ({\psi_n}) of a declared operator (e.g., Laplacian). Compute coefficients (c_n(p)=\langle H(\cdot,p),\psi_n\rangle) and band energies via grouped indices. The eigenbasis and truncation are part of the invariant type.
(C) Spectral invariants beyond energy.Compute additional invariants such as spectral centroid, bandwidth, and higher-order spectra (bispectrum-like invariants) only when stable under discretization and when computational cost is justified by corridor requirements.
(D) Conditioning and numerical stability.Spectral estimators must monitor numerical issues: aliasing, leakage, windowing artifacts, and discretization inconsistencies. Estimators must output diagnostic metrics (e.g., energy conservation error, leakage ratios) included in invariant computation certificates.
4.4.1.4 Bias/variance trade-offs
(A) Scale selection bias.Coarse scales (\epsilon) reduce variance but increase bias; fine scales increase variance and discretization sensitivity. The ladder ({\epsilon_k}) must be selected to balance these effects, consistent with finite resolution and corridor sensitivity.
(B) Smoothing and positivity floors.Deterministic smoothing reduces variance and stabilizes Rényi/Tsallis computations but introduces bias. Smoothing parameters must be set by corridor policy and validated by gold-state studies; they must not be tuned per-instance unless explicitly allowed and fully logged.
(C) Ensemble vs single-state reporting.For single-state invariants, report deterministic point estimates plus conservative envelopes. For ensemble claims (average-case bounds), report means/quantiles with deterministic sampling and fixed seeds; sampling schemes must be reproducible.
(D) Bias/variance as a corridor design input.Corridor budgets must account for estimator bias/variance:[\delta_{\mathrm{est}} = \delta_{\mathrm{bias}}+\delta_{\mathrm{var}}+\delta_{\mathrm{num}}.]Estimator-induced uncertainty is not optional; it is a required budget component for any invariant used in admissibility decisions.
4.4.2 Invariant test harness
4.4.2.1 Unit tests for invariants
(A) Sanity bounds and invariance identities.Unit tests must verify:
(0\le H_\epsilon(H)\le \log N(\epsilon)),
(q_k(H)\ge 0), (\sum_k q_k(H)=1) within tolerance,
symmetry defect (\mathrm{Def}_G(H)\ge 0),
invariants are unchanged under declared gauge actions (within tolerance).
(B) Consistency under trivial transforms.Verify invariants are unchanged under identity transform and under deterministic canonicalization:[\mathrm{Inv}(\mathrm{Can}(H))=\mathrm{Inv}(H)]when invariants are designed to be gauge-invariant and canonicalization preserves feasibility.
(C) Monotonicity tests for known smoothing.For a known smoothing transform (S) (diffusion/low-pass), test expected monotonic behavior:
high-frequency band energies decrease,
coarse-grained entropy does not decrease (under declared model),within tolerance. These tests validate estimator correctness and sign conventions.
(D) Boundary and degenerate-case tests.Test handling of:
near-zero probabilities,
near-zero states (must reject or return null invariants deterministically),
phase singularities (must stabilize via current-based measures or smoothing),
topological tie cases (deterministic ordering and stable summaries).
4.4.2.2 Property-based randomized tests
(A) Random feasible state generation.Generate random states (H\in H^4_{N,P}) by deterministic seeded procedures:
sample coefficients,
enforce constraints via projection/repair,
canonicalize gauge,
verify feasibility residual within tolerance.Generators are versioned and produce canonical IDs.
(B) Random transform generation (admissible subset).Generate admissible transforms (T) from a deterministic family and apply to random states. Verify corridor drift bounds and invariance identities where applicable. This tests invariant robustness against broad perturbations.
(C) Metamorphic properties.Test metamorphic relations such as:
invariants of disjoint unions under block-diagonal composition,
invariants under deterministic relabeling of bins (when invariants are permutation-invariant),
invariants under controlled scaling (when invariants are scale-normalized).
(D) Statistical thresholds and determinism.Property-based tests must be deterministic given seeds and must have fixed pass/fail thresholds. Any test relying on random sampling must fix sample sizes and seeds; failures must be reproducible exactly.
4.4.2.3 Regression baselines and gold states
(A) Gold invariant baselines.For each gold state (H_j), store baseline invariant vectors (\mathrm{Inv}(H_j)) with suite ID and environment ID. These baselines are the authoritative regression targets.
(B) Multi-resolution baselines.Store baselines across multiple resolutions ((N,P)) to detect discretization regressions. Expected cross-resolution variation must be bounded by stored envelopes; exceeding envelopes triggers failure.
(C) Baseline drift management.If an invariant definition changes (suite version update), regenerate baselines under the new suite and archive old baselines. Cross-version comparisons must not mix suites; suite IDs enforce this constraint.
(D) Regression failure triage outputs.When regression fails, output:
which invariant components violated,
which gold states violated,
the minimal reproduction manifest,
diagnostic traces (leakage, conditioning, estimator sanity metrics).
4.4.2.4 Tolerance policies
(A) Layered tolerance decomposition.Define tolerances as sums of components:[\tau = \tau_{\mathrm{float}}+\tau_{\mathrm{disc}}+\tau_{\mathrm{est}}+\tau_{\mathrm{algo}},]where each component is justified and versioned.
(B) Relative tolerances for scale-dependent quantities.For quantities spanning orders of magnitude (band energies), use relative tolerance:[|a-b|\le \tau_{\mathrm{abs}}+\tau_{\mathrm{rel}}(|a|+|b|).]Tolerance parameters must be fixed and included in suite IDs.
(C) Tie-boundary tolerance handling.For invariants with discontinuities at ties (e.g., top-(K) selection), define a tie-boundary margin: if values lie within a margin of equality, treat ordering changes as non-failures but require additional diagnostic logging. The margin is deterministic and conservative.
(D) Fail-closed policy for safety guards.For guard invariants used for admissibility/safety, tolerances must be fail-closed: if an invariant is indeterminate or unstable under the current numeric regime, the system must reject rather than accept. Indeterminacy handling is part of the execution harness.
4.4.3 Invariant-aware transform execution
4.4.3.1 Online invariant monitoring
(A) Streaming invariant computation.During transform execution (iterative solvers, multi-stage pipelines), compute a subset of cheap invariants periodically:
norm and band-energy proxies,
coarse entropies at a small set of scales,
constraint residual norms.Streaming computations must be deterministic and must not change the transform output (pure monitoring).
(B) Drift prediction and early warning.Maintain predicted drift envelopes using per-step bounds (from operator stability estimates). If predicted drift exceeds budgets, trigger early warning and switch to conservative execution mode (smaller step size, stronger feasibility projection, or increased smoothing for robust invariant computation).
(C) Monitoring under canonicalization.If canonicalization is applied intermittently, invariants must be computed on canonicalized states to avoid gauge-induced drift artifacts. Monitoring must record whether values are pre- or post-canonicalization.
(D) Monitoring logs as certificates.Monitoring logs are serialized and hashed. They include: time/iteration index, invariant snapshots, residuals, step sizes, and abort/repair events. Logs are part of transform admissibility certificates.
4.4.3.2 Early abort on corridor violation
(A) Abort criterion.Abort when any guard invariant violates its corridor:[|\Delta_k|>\delta_k \quad \text{for any guard component }k,]or when feasibility residual exceeds its corridor, or when numerical stability diagnostics exceed thresholds (conditioning, divergence, NaNs). Abort criteria are deterministic.
(B) Grace periods and hysteresis.For non-guard invariants, allow a deterministic grace policy: a limited number of violations within a bounded overshoot may be tolerated if subsequent repair is guaranteed and certified. Guard invariants do not allow grace periods.
(C) Partial output and failure certificates.On abort, output:
last valid state (\widehat{H}_{\mathrm{safe}}) (if available),
failure report (§4.3.4.4),
monitoring log,
recommended repair action.Abort outputs are complete artifacts, not partial logs.
(D) Deterministic retry policy.If automatic retry is enabled, it must follow a deterministic retry schedule (e.g., reduce step size by fixed factor, increase smoothing by fixed factor) with a fixed maximum retry count. Each retry is logged and hashed.
4.4.3.3 Automatic repair attempts
(A) Feasibility-first repair.If corridor violation is driven by constraint residual growth, apply feasibility repair (projection or penalized repair) before recomputing invariants. Repair must be deterministic and must output its own certificate.
(B) Invariant-stabilization repair.If violation is due to estimator instability (e.g., phase singularities, topological ties), apply deterministic smoothing or robustification:
enforce probability floors,
increase coarse-graining bandwidth (\epsilon) within allowed ladder,
switch to robust summaries (e.g., persistence entropy rather than full diagram).Such repairs change the invariant type only if they change suite parameters; therefore they must remain within the predeclared suite configuration or trigger an explicit suite change.
(C) Transform-parameter repair.If drift indicates transform instability, repair by adjusting transform parameters within admissible bounds:
reduce step size,
increase regularization,
enforce commutation-preserving approximations (e.g., symmetric splitting).Parameter repair rules are deterministic and must be recorded.
(D) Repair success certification.A repair is successful only if:
all guard invariants satisfy budgets,
feasibility residual is within tolerance,
monitoring diagnostics return to stable regime,
repaired state passes regression sanity checks (when required).Repair output includes a certificate linking pre/post states and all applied actions.
4.4.3.4 Report generation
(A) Transform run report schema.A transform execution report includes:
input state ID, output state ID,
transform program ID and parameters,
invariant suite ID and computed invariant vectors,
corridor budgets and pass/fail results,
feasibility residuals and reconstruction compatibility checks (when required),
monitoring log ID and repair event IDs.
(B) Human-readable summary.Provide a deterministic summary listing:
maximum invariant drift component,
most violated component (if any),
scale/region attribution,
feasibility status,
resource usage summary (time/memory if measured deterministically).
(C) Machine-readable manifest.Emit a canonical manifest linking all artifacts by hash ID and including environment versions. The manifest is the authoritative reference for reproducibility.
(D) External audit bundle.For external use, package:
report + manifest,
invariant vectors and drift/budget tables (serialized deterministically),
optional compressed logs (content-hashed),
minimal reproduction instructions encoded as manifest fields (no free-text dependence).
4.4.4 Reference implementations
4.4.4.1 Minimal invariant library API
(A) Core interfaces (typed).A minimal invariant library exposes:
ProbabilityExtractor: implements (H\mapsto p(\epsilon;H)) and/or (H\mapsto \rho_\epsilon),
EntropyFamily: computes Shannon/Rényi/Tsallis, MI, conditional variants,
SpectralInvariants: computes band energies and spectral entropies given declared filters,
PhaseInvariants: computes current-based phase invariants and winding measures with robust policies,
TopologicalInvariants: computes persistent summaries with deterministic pipelines,
InvariantSuite: aggregates components into (\mathrm{Inv}(H)) with canonicalization and hashing.
(B) Deterministic serialization and hashing.Every object provides deterministic serialization of parameters and outputs. InvariantSuite must provide suite_id() as a hash of its full parameterization and inv_id(H) as a hash of canonicalized invariant outputs.
(C) Error and indeterminacy handling.The API must distinguish:
valid numeric result,
indeterminate due to domain violations (e.g., (|H|=0)),
unstable due to tie boundaries beyond tolerance,
numerical overflow/underflow.Indeterminacy is handled by fail-closed policy for guard invariants.
(D) Traceability to manuscript definitions.Each API component must reference the exact mathematical definition implemented (by identifier) and provide unit tests that demonstrate conformity. This ensures code-to-math traceability required by the manuscript’s verification doctrine.
4.4.4.2 Performance benchmarks
(A) Benchmark dimensions.Benchmarks measure runtime and memory as functions of:
resolution ((N,P)),
ladder size (K),
number of bins (N(\epsilon)),
number of spectral bands,
topological computation complexity (grid size, filtration steps).
(B) Deterministic benchmarking protocol.Benchmark inputs are fixed gold states and fixed suites with fixed seeds. Benchmarks must run with deterministic settings and stable reduction ordering; nondeterministic timing variability is handled by reporting deterministic operation counts (where feasible) and bounded timing intervals measured under controlled environments.
(C) Accuracy–cost profiles.Report Pareto profiles: estimator bandwidth vs bias/variance, ladder size vs stability, topological summary complexity vs robustness. Profiles are used to justify suite design and corridor enforcement feasibility.
(D) Benchmark regression.Performance baselines are versioned. Any significant change triggers a performance regression report and must be justified as an intended trade-off (e.g., increased robustness) with corresponding suite version increment.
4.4.4.3 Numerical stability tests
(A) Precision sensitivity.Test invariants under float32 vs float64 (and other declared precisions) on the same gold states. Differences must remain within declared numeric envelopes; otherwise, the suite must require higher precision or apply stabilization.
(B) Stress tests near singularities.Test phase invariants near zeros of (H), topological invariants near tie thresholds, and entropy variants near sparse distributions. Suites must demonstrate stable behavior under deterministic smoothing policies.
(C) Conditioning diagnostics for spectral methods.Test spectral estimators under aliasing and leakage scenarios. Verify energy conservation errors and leakage metrics remain within tolerances; otherwise, revise filter definitions or require higher resolution.
(D) Stability under refinement.Run invariants at multiple resolutions and verify convergence (or bounded variation) consistent with the declared invariant semantics (renormalized vs raw). Nonconvergence beyond envelopes indicates discretization inconsistency or estimator bias, and must be resolved before external claims are made.
4.4.4.4 Reproducibility packaging
(A) Complete suite manifests.Each invariant suite is packaged with:
suite parameter manifest,
test harness manifest,
baseline invariant records for gold states,
environment manifest (library versions),all content-hashed and linked.
(B) Reproduction scripts as deterministic workflows.Reproduction is a deterministic workflow: given a manifest, compute (\mathrm{Inv}(H)) and verify hashes match baselines. Any deviation is a reproducibility failure and must be reported with diagnostic logs.
(C) Artifact provenance chains.Invariant artifacts include provenance links to:
source state artifacts,
transform artifacts that produced states,
observation/reconstruction artifacts if applicable,
corridor policy versions.This enables end-to-end audit from transforms to invariant outcomes.
(D) External distribution discipline.External packages include only:
manifests,
invariant outputs,
minimal necessary gold states or synthetic generators,
verification harness.No unverifiable or environment-dependent artifacts are used for claims; all outputs must be reproducible from the package.
Chapter 4 — Information, Entropy, and Multi-Scale Invariants
Chapter 5 — The Quaternary Engine ((Q)): Closed Operator Cycles
5.1 Square — Formal Definition of the 4-Stage Cycle
5.1.1 Cycle object definition
5.1.1.1 Define stage operators ((Q_1,Q_2,Q_3,Q_4))
Let (H^4\subseteq \mathfrak{H}) denote the holographic state space defined as a constraint kernel (H^4=\ker(C)) (or a corridor-feasible relaxation thereof), equipped with a declared Banach/Hilbert topology ((H^4,|\cdot|_{H^4})) induced from (\mathfrak{H}). Let (\mathcal{PATT}:=H^4/\mathcal{G}) denote the pattern quotient by the declared gauge group (\mathcal{G}), and let (\mathrm{Can}:H^4\to H^4) denote a canonicalization map selecting representatives of gauge orbits whenever defined.
A quaternary engine stage is a (generally nonlinear) operator[Q_k:\mathcal{D}(Q_k)\subseteq H^4 \longrightarrow H^4,\qquad k\in{1,2,3,4},]together with a declared operator type:
linear bounded: (Q_k\in \mathcal{B}(H^4));
linear unbounded: (Q_k:\mathcal{D}(Q_k)\to H^4) is linear, densely defined, closed (or closable) in the sense of closed operators;
nonlinear closed-graph: (Q_k) has closed graph in (H^4\times H^4) (equivalently: (H_n\to H), (Q_k(H_n)\to Y) implies (H\in \mathcal{D}(Q_k)) and (Q_k(H)=Y));
locally Lipschitz: for each bounded set (B\subset \mathcal{D}(Q_k)) there exists (L_{k,B}<\infty) with (|Q_k(H)-Q_k(\widetilde{H})|\le L_{k,B}|H-\widetilde{H}|).
The quaternary engine cycle operator is the composition[Q^{[4]} := Q_4\circ Q_3\circ Q_2\circ Q_1,]with domain[\mathcal{D}(Q^{[4]}):=\Big{H\in\mathcal{D}(Q_1);:; Q_1(H)\in\mathcal{D}(Q_2),; Q_2Q_1(H)\in\mathcal{D}(Q_3),; Q_3Q_2Q_1(H)\in\mathcal{D}(Q_4)\Big}.]When a “single operator” notation is required, (Q) denotes the full-cycle map (Q:=Q^{[4]}). When the four stages are identical or parameter-shared in a declared way, the notational identification (Q^{[4]}=Q^4) is permitted and must be stated as an explicit convention of the engine instance.
A quaternary engine instance is the data[\mathsf{Q}:=\Big(H^4,|\cdot|,; (Q_1,Q_2,Q_3,Q_4),; \mathcal{D}(Q^{[4]}),; \mathrm{Inv},; \delta,; \mathrm{Can},; \mathcal{G}\Big),]where (\mathrm{Inv}) is a declared invariant suite and (\delta) a corridor budget vector for admissibility.
5.1.1.2 Typed domains and codomains
Each stage (Q_k) is typed as an admissible morphism acting on feasible states:[Q_k:\mathcal{D}(Q_k)\subseteq H^4 \to H^4,]with the following mandatory typing constraints.
(A) Feasibility typing.For hard constraints (C(H)=0), stage admissibility requires[H\in\mathcal{D}(Q_k)\cap H^4 \ \Longrightarrow\ Q_k(H)\in H^4,]i.e., (Q_k(H^4\cap \mathcal{D}(Q_k))\subseteq H^4).For soft feasibility corridors, stage admissibility is expressed as[|C(Q_k(H))|\le \tau_k\quad\text{whenever}\quad |C(H)|\le \tau_{\mathrm{in}},]with declared tolerances (\tau_{\mathrm{in}},\tau_k).
(B) Gauge compatibility typing.If the engine is intended to act on patterns (\mathcal{PATT}), each stage must admit a well-defined induced action on the quotient, either by gauge-equivariance[Q_k(g\cdot H)=g\cdot Q_k(H)\qquad(\forall g\in\mathcal{G}),]or by being followed by canonicalization within the stage definition:[Q_k \equiv \mathrm{Can}\circ \widetilde{Q}_k,]where (\widetilde{Q}_k) is an operator on (H^4) and (\mathrm{Can}) fixes the gauge.
(C) Regularity typing.Let ({X_s}{s\in\mathcal{S}}) be a declared regularity ladder (Sobolev/variation/distributional levels). Each stage must declare an input and output level:[Q_k: X{s_k^{\mathrm{in}}}\cap H^4 \to X_{s_k^{\mathrm{out}}}\cap H^4,]together with a verification rule ensuring that if the stage is applied outside its stated input regularity, the engine rejects or repairs deterministically.
(D) Reconstruction typing.If reconstruction operators (R_A) (Chapter 2) are to remain valid throughout iteration, each stage must preserve a declared reconstructibility condition:[\mathsf{Rec}(H)\ \Longrightarrow\ \mathsf{Rec}(Q_k(H)),]where (\mathsf{Rec}) is a predicate defined by reconstruction error envelopes (\varepsilon(A)), conditioning thresholds, or frame bounds.
5.1.1.3 Closure condition ((Q^4=I+\epsilon\Delta))
The defining feature of the quaternary engine is near-closure: the four-stage cycle is an almost-identity map with a controlled increment.
Definition (Quaternary near-closure).An engine instance satisfies quaternary near-closure on a domain (\mathcal{U}\subseteq \mathcal{D}(Q^{[4]})) if there exists (\epsilon\ge 0) and an increment operator (\Delta:\mathcal{U}\to H^4) such that[Q^{[4]}(H)=H+\epsilon,\Delta(H)\qquad\forall H\in\mathcal{U}.]When the notational convention (Q^{[4]}=Q^4) is adopted, this relation is written[Q^4 = I+\epsilon\Delta\quad\text{on }\mathcal{U}.]
The increment (\Delta) is required to be compatible with feasibility and gauge semantics:[H\in H^4 \ \Rightarrow\ \Delta(H)\in T_H(H^4) \ \text{(or in a declared tangent/feasible direction set)},]and, if operating on patterns,[\Delta(g\cdot H) \sim_{\mathcal{G}} \Delta(H),]or equivalently (\Delta) is well-defined on canonical representatives.
Local and global interpretations.
If (\epsilon) is small and (\Delta) is regular, the cycle approximates a step of a continuous-time flow generated by (\Delta).
If (\epsilon) is not small, near-closure is interpreted as a corridor constraint on the deviation from identity, with (\Delta) serving as a diagnostic decomposition of net change.
Closed-cycle requirement.When stages are (possibly unbounded) closed operators, the engine must ensure that (Q^{[4]}) is a well-defined closed-graph map on (\mathcal{U}). Sufficient conditions include: each (Q_k) has closed graph and maps bounded sets into sets on which the next stage is closed-graph with compatible domains, or the composition is implemented through a deterministic graph-closure procedure with explicit domain checks.
5.1.1.4 Increment operator ((\Delta)) classification
The increment (\Delta) is a typed object with a declared structural class, enabling proof templates, stability bounds, and implementation strategies.
(A) Linear bounded increment.(\Delta\in \mathcal{B}(H^4)) and[|\Delta(H)|\le |\Delta|{\mathrm{op}}|H|,\qquad |\Delta|{\mathrm{op}}<\infty.]This regime supports direct operator-norm drift bounds and spectral stability analysis.
(B) Linear unbounded increment (generator class).(\Delta) is a densely defined closed operator on (H^4). A common instance is (\Delta=X) where (X) generates a strongly continuous semigroup (or group) on (H^4). In this regime, near-closure is interpreted on (\mathcal{U}\subseteq \mathcal{D}(X)) and must include domain stability under iteration.
(C) Nonlinear Lipschitz increment.(\Delta:\mathcal{U}\to H^4) is locally Lipschitz:[|\Delta(H)-\Delta(\widetilde{H})|\le L_\mathcal{U}|H-\widetilde{H}|\quad (H,\widetilde{H}\in \mathcal{U}).]This supports discrete-time stability and continuous-time embedding bounds via standard dynamical systems estimates.
(D) Structured increments.The engine must declare whether (\Delta) belongs to one or more of the following structural subclasses, each implying additional invariance or monotonicity properties:
Hamiltonian/skew-adjoint type: (\Delta(H)=XH) with (X^\ast=-X) (conservative norm-preserving regime).
Gradient/descent type: (\Delta(H)=-\nabla \Phi(H)) for a declared functional (\Phi) (Lyapunov/decrease regime).
Projected type: (\Delta(H)=\Pi_{T_H}(\widetilde{\Delta}(H))), with (\Pi_{T_H}) a feasible-direction projection enforcing constraints.
Splitting-commutator type: (\Delta) decomposes into a sum plus commutators produced by stage splitting (BCH structure), enabling explicit leading-order error classification.
Noise/approximation type: (\Delta=\Delta_{\mathrm{det}}+\xi) with deterministic envelopes for (\xi) induced by truncation/noise.
5.1.2 Two equivalent realizations
5.1.2.1 Differential realization (derivative/integral ladder)
A differential realization models stages as a closed operator ladder built from differentiation-like and integration-like operators with feasibility repair and canonicalization.
Let (D:\mathcal{D}(D)\subseteq H^4\to \mathfrak{K}) be a closed (possibly unbounded) differential operator into a Banach space (\mathfrak{K}), and let (J:\mathcal{D}(J)\subseteq \mathfrak{K}\to H^4) be a right-inverse or parametrix (integration-like operator) satisfying[J\circ D = I + \mathcal{R},]on a declared domain, with residual (\mathcal{R}:H^4\to H^4) bounded or corridor-controlled.
Let (P:H^4\to H^4) be a feasibility projection (exact projection onto (\ker(C)) or a penalty-based projection), and let (\mathrm{Can}) be canonicalization. A canonical 4-stage differential ladder is:[Q_1 := D_{\mathrm{lift}},\quadQ_2 := J_{\mathrm{collapse}},\quadQ_3 := \mathrm{Can},\quadQ_4 := P,]where (D_{\mathrm{lift}}) and (J_{\mathrm{collapse}}) are typed versions of (D) and (J) that map within (H^4) by embedding auxiliary outputs into feasible states (when necessary) using declared lift/collapse operators. The full cycle satisfies[Q^{[4]}(H)=P(\mathrm{Can}(J(D(H)))) = H + \epsilon,\Delta(H),]where (\Delta) collects the ladder residual (\mathcal{R}), projection/canonicalization non-identity effects, and discretization terms.
A differential realization is admissible only if:
domains are stable across stages: (H\in\mathcal{U}\subseteq \mathcal{D}(D)) implies (D(H)\in \mathcal{D}(J));
feasibility repair is well-defined and corridor-certified;
the combined residual (\Delta) satisfies declared stability and drift bounds.
5.1.2.2 Algebraic realization (generator exponentials)
An algebraic realization models stages as exponentials (or resolvents) of generators in transformation space.
Let (X_k) be (possibly unbounded) generators on (H^4) for (k=1,\dots,4), each generating a strongly continuous semigroup ({e^{tX_k}}{t\ge 0}) (or group ({e^{tX_k}}{t\in\mathbb{R}})) on a declared invariant domain. Let (\epsilon>0) be a step size, and define[Q_k := e^{\epsilon X_k}.]The full cycle is then[Q^{[4]} = e^{\epsilon X_4}e^{\epsilon X_3}e^{\epsilon X_2}e^{\epsilon X_1}.]When the (X_k) commute, (Q^{[4]}=e^{\epsilon (X_1+X_2+X_3+X_4)}) exactly. In the noncommuting case, (Q^{[4]}) admits a Baker–Campbell–Hausdorff effective generator (X_{\mathrm{eff}}(\epsilon)) on a common domain:[Q^{[4]} = e^{\epsilon X_{\mathrm{eff}}(\epsilon)},\qquadX_{\mathrm{eff}}(\epsilon)=\sum_{k=1}^4 X_k ;+;\frac{\epsilon}{2}\sum_{i<j}[X_j,X_i];+;O(\epsilon^2),]where commutators are understood as operators on a declared core domain and (O(\epsilon^2)) collects higher nested commutators. The near-closure form (Q^{[4]}=I+\epsilon\Delta+O(\epsilon^2)) is obtained by expanding the exponential:[Q^{[4]}(H)=H+\epsilon,(X_{\mathrm{eff}}(0)H)+O(\epsilon^2),]so that, to leading order, (\Delta(H)=X_{\mathrm{eff}}(0)H).
This realization is admissible only when:
each exponential map preserves feasibility and gauge semantics (or is followed by canonicalization within the stage);
commutators required for the declared order of approximation are well-defined and bounded on the declared domain;
the engine reports the truncation order used and the resulting certified error envelope.
5.1.2.3 Discrete realization (gate programs)
A discrete realization models each stage as an executable program built from a finite set of primitive gates acting on the finite hologram model (H^{(N,P)}).
Let (H^{(N,P)}) denote the finite representation space, and let (\mathsf{Gate}) denote a finite set of primitive operators with typed signatures[g:\mathcal{D}(g)\subseteq H^{(N,P)}\to H^{(N,P)},]including linear kernels, pointwise nonlinearities, projections, canonicalizers, and sampling/reconstruction subroutines.
A stage program is a directed acyclic operator graph (\mathsf{Prog}k) whose evaluation defines[Q_k^{(N,P)} := \llbracket \mathsf{Prog}k\rrbracket : H^{(N,P)}\to H^{(N,P)}.]The full cycle program is the concatenation/composition:[Q^{[4]}{(N,P)} := Q^{(N,P)}4\circ Q^{(N,P)}3\circ Q^{(N,P)}2\circ Q^{(N,P)}1.]Near-closure is enforced in the discrete model as a certified property:[|Q^{[4]}{(N,P)}(H)-H-\epsilon\Delta{(N,P)}(H)|\le \eta{(N,P)}(H),]with deterministic error envelope (\eta{(N,P)}) including floating-point, truncation, and solver errors. The discrete increment (\Delta{(N,P)}) must be computed deterministically (e.g., via difference quotients or explicit program extraction of the increment channel).
5.1.2.4 Equivalence theorems between realizations
Theorem (Differential–algebraic equivalence at first order).Assume:
(X) is a (possibly unbounded) generator of a strongly continuous semigroup on (H^4);
the differential ladder realization defines a map (Q^{[4]}\epsilon:H^4\supseteq \mathcal{U}\to H^4) satisfying[Q^{[4]}\epsilon(H)=H+\epsilon XH + r_\epsilon(H),]with (|r_\epsilon(H)|\le M\epsilon^2) uniformly on bounded subsets of (\mathcal{U});
the algebraic realization defines (E_\epsilon(H):=e^{\epsilon X}H) on (\mathcal{U}) with the semigroup Taylor remainder bound[|e^{\epsilon X}H-(H+\epsilon XH)|\le M'\epsilon^2\quad(H\in\mathcal{U}).]Then there exists (C<\infty) such that[|Q^{[4]}_\epsilon(H)-e^{\epsilon X}H|\le C\epsilon^2\qquad(H\in\mathcal{U}).]
Theorem (Algebraic–discrete equivalence under consistent discretization).Assume:
(\Pi_{N,P}:H^4\to H^{(N,P)}) is a consistent projection and (\iota_{N,P}:H^{(N,P)}\to H^4) is an embedding, with (\iota_{N,P}\Pi_{N,P}\to I) in the strong topology on a dense subset;
each stage satisfies a consistency estimate[|\iota_{N,P}Q_k^{(N,P)}\Pi_{N,P}H - Q_kH|\le \eta_k(N,P;H),]with (\eta_k(N,P;H)\to 0) as (N,P\to\infty) for each fixed (H) in a declared regularity class.Then, for the full cycle,[|\iota_{N,P}Q^{[4]}{(N,P)}\Pi{N,P}H - Q^{[4]}H|\le \sum_{k=1}^4 \eta_k(N,P;\widetilde{H}_k),]where (\widetilde{H}_k) denotes the intermediate states of the continuum cycle, provided all intermediate states remain in the declared regularity class.
Corollary (Unified increment convergence).Under the hypotheses above, discrete increments (\Delta_{(N,P)}) converge to the continuum increment (\Delta) in the sense that[|\iota_{N,P}\Delta_{(N,P)}\Pi_{N,P}H - \Delta(H)|\to 0,]whenever the increment is defined as the first-order term of the near-closure expansion and the remainder is (O(\epsilon^2)) uniformly on bounded sets.
5.1.3 Cycle invariants
5.1.3.1 Invariant suite preserved by ((Q))
Let (\mathrm{Inv}:H^4\to\mathbb{R}^m) be a declared invariant suite (including multi-scale information invariants, spectral invariants, symmetry defects, and feasibility residuals), and let (\delta\in\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^m) be a corridor budget.
Definition (Cycle admissibility by invariants).The cycle (Q^{[4]}) is ((\mathrm{Inv},\delta))-admissible on (\mathcal{U}\subseteq \mathcal{D}(Q^{[4]})) if[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(Q^{[4]}(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)\big|\preceq \delta\qquad\forall H\in\mathcal{U},]componentwise, with the additional requirement that each stage is stage-admissible with budgets (\delta^{(k)}) satisfying (\sum_k \delta^{(k)}\preceq \delta) if stage-wise monitoring is required.
Gauge-invariant enforcement.If invariants are pattern-level quantities, admissibility is enforced after canonicalization:[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(\mathrm{Can}(Q^{[4]}(H)))-\mathrm{Inv}(\mathrm{Can}(H))\big|\preceq \delta.]
Feasibility invariants.The invariant suite must contain feasibility guards, at minimum:[\mathrm{Inv}_{\mathrm{feas}}(H):=|C(H)|,]with a strict bound (|C(Q^{[4]}(H))|\le \tau) for a declared (\tau), and stage-wise feasibility checks if required by the engine instance.
5.1.3.2 Drift bounds under ((Q)) iteration
Define the discrete-time trajectory generated by the cycle:[H_{n+1}=Q^{[4]}(H_n),\qquad n\in\mathbb{N},\quad H_0\in \mathcal{U}.]
Proposition (Invariant drift accumulation bound).If (Q^{[4]}) is ((\mathrm{Inv},\delta))-admissible on (\mathcal{U}) and (H_n\in\mathcal{U}) for (0\le n\le N), then[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(H_N)-\mathrm{Inv}(H_0)\big|\preceq N,\delta.]If a weighted norm corridor (|\cdot|_W) is used, then[|\mathrm{Inv}(H_N)-\mathrm{Inv}(H_0)|_W \le N,\delta_W,]with (\delta_W) the per-cycle bound in (|\cdot|_W).
Proposition (Near-closure drift bound in state norm).Assume near-closure (Q^{[4]}(H)=H+\epsilon\Delta(H)) on (\mathcal{U}) and assume (|\Delta(H)|\le M) on (\mathcal{U}). Then for (H_n\in\mathcal{U}),[|H_N-H_0|\le N,\epsilon,M.]If (\Delta) is Lipschitz with constant (L) and (\epsilon L<1), then the increment map is a perturbation of identity with controlled stability:[|H_{n+1}-\widetilde{H}_{n+1}| \le (1+\epsilon L)|H_n-\widetilde{H}_n|,]yielding[|H_N-\widetilde{H}_N|\le (1+\epsilon L)^N|H_0-\widetilde{H}_0|.]
Theorem (Contractive regime and bounded drift).Assume (Q^{[4]}) is a contraction on ((\mathcal{U},d)) for a declared metric (d):[d(Q^{[4]}(H),Q^{[4]}(\widetilde{H}))\le \lambda,d(H,\widetilde{H}),\qquad 0\le \lambda<1,]and (\mathcal{U}) is complete in (d) and invariant under (Q^{[4]}). Then:
there exists a unique fixed point (H^\star\in\mathcal{U}) with (Q^{[4]}(H^\star)=H^\star);
for any (H_0\in\mathcal{U}), (d(H_n,H^\star)\le \lambda^n d(H_0,H^\star));
invariant drift relative to (H^\star) converges:[\mathrm{Inv}(H_n)\to \mathrm{Inv}(H^\star),]whenever (\mathrm{Inv}) is continuous on (\mathcal{U}).
5.1.3.3 Resonance conditions
Resonance characterizes periodic amplification, phase-locking, or cycle-closure enhancements caused by spectral or nonlinear commensurability.
(A) Linear resonance (spectral).Assume (Q^{[4]}) is linear and bounded on (H^4). A mode (u\neq 0) is a resonance mode of order (m\in\mathbb{N}) if[(Q^{[4]})^{m}u = u,]equivalently if (u) lies in an eigenspace with eigenvalue (\lambda) satisfying (\lambda^m=1). In a quaternary context, the distinguished resonant eigenvalues are the 4th roots of unity:[\lambda\in{1,-1,i,-i}.]Resonance diagnostics consist of estimating spectral radii near the unit circle and computing cycle multipliers on declared invariant subspaces (e.g., spectral bands).
(B) Near-resonance and Floquet multipliers.In the algebraic realization with (Q^{[4]}=e^{\epsilon X_{\mathrm{eff}}(\epsilon)}), near-resonance arises when (X_{\mathrm{eff}}) has purely imaginary spectrum near commensurate frequencies. Define Floquet multipliers (\mu) as eigenvalues of (Q^{[4]}) on a finite-dimensional reduction; near-resonance occurs when (|\mu|\approx 1) and (\arg(\mu)) is rationally related to (2\pi).
(C) Nonlinear resonance (cycle response).For nonlinear (Q^{[4]}), define the (m)-cycle set[\mathsf{Per}_m := {H\in\mathcal{U}: (Q^{[4]})^m(H)=H}.]A resonance regime is present if (\mathsf{Per}_m) contains a robust (structurally stable) manifold or if the linearization at periodic points has multipliers of modulus (\approx 1), indicating persistent periodic or quasi-periodic behavior.
(D) Resonance constraints as corridor guards.When resonance leads to undesirable amplification, the engine declares guard constraints limiting resonance diagnostics:[\max_{k\in\mathcal{K}}\mathrm{Amp}_k(H)\le \Gamma_k,]where (\mathrm{Amp}_k) measures growth in designated spectral bands, phase winding, or mutual-information couplings under iteration.
5.1.3.4 Fixed points and attractors
(A) Fixed points and cycle-fixed patterns.A fixed point is (H^\star\in\mathcal{U}) satisfying[Q^{[4]}(H^\star)=H^\star.]A pattern-fixed point is a gauge orbit ([H^\star]{\mathcal{G}}) satisfying (Q^{[4]}(H^\star)\sim{\mathcal{G}} H^\star), equivalently:[\mathrm{Can}(Q^{[4]}(H^\star))=\mathrm{Can}(H^\star),]under a well-defined canonicalization.
(B) Attractors.An attractor is a compact invariant set (\mathcal{A}\subseteq \mathcal{U}) such that there exists a neighborhood (\mathcal{N}) with[\mathrm{dist}(H_n,\mathcal{A})\to 0\quad\text{for all }H_0\in\mathcal{N}.]When (\mathcal{A}) is a fixed point, this reduces to contraction-based convergence. When (\mathcal{A}) is a periodic orbit, it represents a stable cycle distinct from the trivial fixed point.
(C) Increment-based fixed point characterization.Under near-closure (Q^{[4]}(H)=H+\epsilon\Delta(H)), fixed points satisfy[\Delta(H^\star)=0.]Thus the quaternary engine fixed point problem reduces to solving (\Delta(H)=0) within feasibility corridors. If (\Delta=-\nabla\Phi), fixed points coincide with stationary points of (\Phi).
(D) Attractor certification.Attractor claims must be supported by a certificate based on one of the proof templates in §5.3.4: contraction mapping, Lyapunov decrease, or energy method, and must include explicit constants and domain conditions ensuring invariance of (\mathcal{U}) under iteration.
5.1.4 Cycle normal forms
5.1.4.1 Canonical stage ordering
A quaternary cycle is a 4-tuple of stages ((Q_1,Q_2,Q_3,Q_4)) with composition (Q^{[4]}). Since a cycle has inherent rotational symmetry, equivalence under cyclic permutation is often admissible.
Definition (Cycle-rotation equivalence).Two stage tuples ((Q_1,\dots,Q_4)) and ((\widetilde{Q}_1,\dots,\widetilde{Q}_4)) are rotation-equivalent if there exists (r\in{0,1,2,3}) such that[(\widetilde{Q}1,\widetilde{Q}2,\widetilde{Q}3,\widetilde{Q}4) = (Q{1+r},Q{2+r},Q{3+r},Q{4+r}),]with indices taken modulo (4). Rotation-equivalent cycles define conjugate full-cycle operators when interpreted as time-origin shifts in the stage sequence.
Canonical ordering rule.A canonical stage ordering is defined by a deterministic minimization over rotations using a declared stage identity order (e.g., lexicographic order of stage program hashes):[(Q_1^{\mathrm{can}},\dots,Q_4^{\mathrm{can}}) := \arg\min_{r\in{0,1,2,3}} \big(\mathrm{ID}(Q_{1+r}),\dots,\mathrm{ID}(Q_{4+r})\big),]where (\mathrm{ID}(Q_k)) is a deterministic stage identifier.
5.1.4.2 Gauge-normalized cycle
Gauge degrees of freedom must not create spurious distinctions between cycles intended to act on patterns.
(A) Induced operator on the quotient.If each stage is gauge-equivariant, it induces an operator (\overline{Q}_k) on (\mathcal{PATT}). The full cycle induces (\overline{Q}^{[4]}) and is the primary object of pattern-level dynamics.
(B) Canonicalization insertion.If stages are not gauge-equivariant but canonicalization is available, define gauge-normalized stages[Q_k^{\mathrm{gn}} := \mathrm{Can}\circ Q_k,]or, when stage outputs must remain in canonical gauge,[Q_k^{\mathrm{gn}} := \mathrm{Can}\circ Q_k\circ \mathrm{Can}.]The chosen rule is part of the engine specification and must be used consistently for admissibility and hashing.
(C) Gauge-normalized near-closure.Near-closure is enforced in gauge-normalized form:[\mathrm{Can}(Q^{[4]}(H))=\mathrm{Can}(H) + \epsilon,\Delta_{\mathrm{gn}}(H),]where (\Delta_{\mathrm{gn}}) is computed after canonicalization and interpreted in the tangent of canonical representatives.
(D) Gauge-normalized equivalence.Two cycles are gauge-normal equivalent if their induced actions on (\mathcal{PATT}) coincide (exactly or within declared corridor tolerances on a domain), and their stage identities coincide after canonical stage ordering and canonical serialization.
5.1.4.3 Minimal gate decomposition
In a discrete realization, each stage is a program composed of primitive gates. The engine must define a minimal normal form for stage programs and for the full cycle.
(A) Gate set and rewrite system.Let (\mathsf{Gate}) be the primitive gate set and let (\mathsf{Rewrite}) be a confluent, terminating rewrite system (or a deterministic best-effort reducer) specifying algebraic identities, cancellation rules, and commutation rules under typing constraints.
(B) Stage minimality.A stage program (\mathsf{Prog}k) is in minimal form if no rewrite reduces its cost under a declared cost functional (\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{prog}}) (gate count, depth, or weighted cost). Minimality is defined relative to (\mathsf{Rewrite}) and (\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{prog}}).
(C) Cycle minimality.The full cycle program (\mathsf{Prog}^{[4]}:=\mathsf{Prog}_4\circ\cdots\circ\mathsf{Prog}_1) is reduced by allowing cross-stage rewrites (e.g., (g\circ g^{-1}\to I)) subject to domain constraints and corridor semantics. The reduced program must preserve admissibility certificates: any rewrite that changes floating-point order, stochasticity, or tolerance policies is prohibited unless it is provably semantics-preserving.
(D) Minimal decomposition certificate.A minimal decomposition is accompanied by a certificate containing: original program IDs, rewrite rules applied, final program ID, and equivalence checks (exact algebraic or regression-based within tolerance). The certificate is part of the cycle identity.
5.1.4.4 Cycle identity hashing
A cycle identity must be deterministic and reproducible across environments.
(A) Canonical serialization.Serialize the cycle in canonical form:[\mathrm{Ser}(\mathsf{Q}) := \mathrm{Ser}\Big(\text{stage-order-canonical }(Q_1,\dots,Q_4),;\text{suite IDs},;\text{corridor policy IDs},;\text{precision policies}\Big),]with explicit ordering, explicit parameter normalization, and explicit rounding rules.
(B) Identity hash.Define the cycle identity[\mathrm{ID}_{\mathsf{Q}} := \mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(\mathsf{Q})).]
(C) Behavioral hash (optional).A behavioral hash may be defined using a fixed set of gold states ({H_j}) and recording canonicalized outputs (\mathrm{Can}(Q^{[4]}(H_j))) and invariant drift vectors. This hash is permitted only as a supplement; it cannot replace structural hashing.
(D) Versioned semantics.Any change to: gate semantics, rewrite rules, invariant suite parameters, corridor budgets, canonicalization rules, or precision policies changes (\mathrm{ID}_{\mathsf{Q}}) by construction.
5.2 Flower — Dynamics Generated by ((Q))
5.2.1 Iteration dynamics
5.2.1.1 Discrete dynamical system definition
Given an engine instance and a valid initial state (H_0\in\mathcal{U}\subseteq \mathcal{D}(Q^{[4]})), define the orbit:[H_{n+1} = Q^{[4]}(H_n),\qquad n\ge 0.]Define the (n)-step iterate (Q^{[4]n}) recursively by composition on its maximal domain:[Q^{[4]0}=I,\qquad Q^{4}=Q^{[4]}\circ Q^{[4]n}.]
Define the admissible orbit condition:[H_n\in\mathcal{U}\ \text{for all }n\le N,]for an execution horizon (N). Execution is valid only if admissible orbit conditions hold under deterministic checks (feasibility residuals, invariant corridors, regularity typing, reconstructibility predicates).
5.2.1.2 Stability under repeated application
Let (d) be a declared metric on (H^4) (e.g., induced by (|\cdot|) or gauge-invariant distance after canonicalization).
Definition (Lipschitz stability).The cycle is Lipschitz-stable on (\mathcal{U}) if there exists (L\ge 0) such that[d(Q^{[4]}(H),Q^{[4]}(\widetilde{H}))\le L,d(H,\widetilde{H})\qquad(H,\widetilde{H}\in\mathcal{U}).]Then[d(H_n,\widetilde{H}_n)\le L^n d(H_0,\widetilde{H}_0)]whenever both trajectories remain in (\mathcal{U}).
Definition (Nonexpansive and contractive regimes).
nonexpansive: (L\le 1);
contractive: (L<1).
In the contractive regime, fixed point and attractor results follow from §5.1.3.2. In the nonexpansive regime, boundedness and invariant corridor control are the primary stability tools.
5.2.1.3 Periodic/aperiodic regimes
Definition (Periodic orbit).A state (H\in\mathcal{U}) is periodic of period (m\ge 1) if[Q^{[4]m}(H)=H.]It is pattern-periodic if[Q^{[4]m}(H)\sim_{\mathcal{G}} H,\quad\text{equivalently}\quad \mathrm{Can}(Q^{[4]m}(H))=\mathrm{Can}(H).]
Definition (Aperiodic orbit).An orbit is aperiodic if it does not satisfy the periodicity condition for any (m) on the execution horizon, and if it fails approximate periodicity within declared numerical tolerances:[d(\mathrm{Can}(H_{n+m}),\mathrm{Can}(H_n))>\tau_{\mathrm{per}}\quad\text{for all }m\in[1,M_{\max}]]over a declared test window.
Periodic regime diagnostics.The engine must compute and report:
minimal observed return time (m) under a declared threshold,
invariants along the orbit (\mathrm{Inv}(H_n)),
stage-wise residuals and drift logs.
5.2.1.4 Chaos diagnostics (if present)
Chaos diagnostics are admissible only as formally defined quantities in the declared metric setting.
(A) Maximal Lyapunov exponent (Fréchet differentiable regime).Assume (Q^{[4]}) is Fréchet differentiable on (\mathcal{U}) with derivative (DQ^{[4]}(H)). Define the maximal Lyapunov exponent along an orbit by[\lambda_{\max}(H_0):=\limsup_{n\to\infty}\frac{1}{n}\log \left|DQ^{[4]}(H_{n-1})\cdots DQ^{[4]}(H_0)\right|.]A positive value indicates sensitive dependence in the linearized sense.
(B) Metric sensitivity (non-differentiable regime).Define (\delta)-sensitivity as: there exists (\eta>0) such that for any neighborhood of (H) there exists (\widetilde{H}) with (d(H,\widetilde{H})<\delta) and some (n) such that (d(H_n,\widetilde{H}_n)>\eta). This is a purely metric definition requiring no derivatives.
(C) Entropy-like growth proxies.In finite models, growth of effective complexity may be proxied by increases in multi-scale entropy or spectral entropy beyond corridor-expected trends. Such proxies are diagnostics only; admissibility decisions rely on corridor checks and failure mode definitions (§5.3.3).
(D) Certificate requirement.Any declared chaotic regime must include: the metric used, the differentiability assumption (if any), the computed exponent estimate procedure, and reproducibility manifests.
5.2.2 Continuous-time embedding
5.2.2.1 Flow embedding ((Q \approx \exp(\epsilon X)))
Assume near-closure:[Q^{[4]}(H)=H+\epsilon\Delta(H)]with (\Delta) regular enough to define an ODE/PDE on (H^4):[\frac{dH}{dt} = \Delta(H),\qquad H(0)=H_0.]Define the time-(\epsilon) flow map (\Phi_\epsilon) of this differential equation on its maximal existence interval. A first-order embedding is the approximation[Q^{[4]}(H)\approx \Phi_\epsilon(H).]If (\Delta=XH) is linear and (X) generates a semigroup, then (\Phi_\epsilon(H)=e^{\epsilon X}H) and the embedding becomes exact up to the splitting error induced by stage decomposition.
5.2.2.2 BCH/Floquet analysis
In the algebraic realization,[Q^{[4]} = e^{\epsilon X_4}e^{\epsilon X_3}e^{\epsilon X_2}e^{\epsilon X_1}.]Define the effective generator via BCH:[Q^{[4]}=e^{\epsilon X_{\mathrm{eff}}(\epsilon)}.]To second order,[X_{\mathrm{eff}}(\epsilon)=\sum_{k=1}^4 X_k + \frac{\epsilon}{2}\sum_{i<j}[X_j,X_i] + O(\epsilon^2).]This decomposition identifies:
the intended generator (\sum_k X_k),
the cycle curvature (noncommutativity) (\sum_{i<j}[X_j,X_i]),
higher-order nested commutator corrections.
If stages vary periodically with iteration index (n) (cycle modulation), the resulting discrete-time system is a nonautonomous periodic system. Floquet analysis applies to the linearized dynamics:[H_{n+1}=A_n H_n,\qquad A_{n+M}=A_n,]with monodromy operator (A_{M-1}\cdots A_0) playing the role of (Q^{[4]}) over one macro-period.
5.2.2.3 Splitting schemes
A quaternary cycle is a four-way splitting integrator for an underlying generator. Standard splitting orders arise from stage symmetry.
(A) First-order Lie splitting.[Q^{[4]}_{\mathrm{Lie}}(\epsilon)=e^{\epsilon X_4}e^{\epsilon X_3}e^{\epsilon X_2}e^{\epsilon X_1}]approximates (e^{\epsilon (X_1+X_2+X_3+X_4)}) with local error (O(\epsilon^2)) when commutators are controlled.
(B) Symmetric (Strang-type) quaternary splitting.A symmetric four-stage arrangement yields higher order:[Q^{[4]}_{\mathrm{sym}}(\epsilon)=e^{\frac{\epsilon}{2}X_1}e^{\frac{\epsilon}{2}X_2}e^{\epsilon X_3}e^{\frac{\epsilon}{2}X_2}e^{\frac{\epsilon}{2}X_1},]or other symmetric compositions. Symmetry cancels odd-order error terms, typically yielding local error (O(\epsilon^3)) under sufficient regularity.
(C) Constraint-projected splitting.If feasibility projection (P) is inserted between exponentials,[H\mapsto P(e^{\epsilon X_k}H),]the method becomes a projected integrator; error analysis must include projection nonlinearity and feasibility drift.
(D) Discrete gate splitting.In a gate-program realization, splitting corresponds to program factorization into commuting or nearly commuting subgraphs; error corresponds to commutator-like discrepancies measurable by invariant drift and operator residual tests.
5.2.2.4 Error bounds for embeddings
Assume (\Delta) is Lipschitz on (\mathcal{U}) with constant (L), and assume the local truncation error of the cycle relative to the flow (\Phi_\epsilon) satisfies[|Q^{[4]}(H)-\Phi_\epsilon(H)|\le K\epsilon^{p+1}\qquad(H\in\mathcal{U}),]for some order (p\ge 1). Then standard discrete-to-continuous bounds yield a global error estimate over time (T=N\epsilon):[|H_N - \Phi_{T}(H_0)|\le \frac{K}{L}\big(e^{LT}-1\big)\epsilon^{p},]provided the orbit remains in (\mathcal{U}). When (L=0) (constant increment), the bound becomes (|H_N-\Phi_T(H_0)|\le K T \epsilon^p).
For algebraic splitting of generators (X_k), the constant (K) is controlled by commutator norms (on the declared domain), e.g. (K\sim \sum_{i<j}|[X_i,X_j]|) for first-order splittings.
5.2.3 Control via cycle modulation
5.2.3.1 Stage parameterization ((\theta_k))
Let each stage be parameterized:[Q_k = Q_k(\theta_k),\qquad \theta_k\in\Theta_k,]with a declared parameter space (\Theta_k) (finite-dimensional or structured). The full cycle depends on (\theta=(\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3,\theta_4)):[Q^{[4]}\theta := Q_4(\theta_4)\circ Q_3(\theta_3)\circ Q_2(\theta_2)\circ Q_1(\theta_1).]Near-closure becomes[Q^{[4]}\theta(H)=H+\epsilon,\Delta_\theta(H),]where (\Delta_\theta) is the drift field induced by cycle parameters.
Parameter typing requires:
deterministic serialization of (\theta_k),
parameter-domain checks ensuring (Q_k(\theta_k)) remains admissible (stability, feasibility, computability).
5.2.3.2 Adaptive stage scheduling
A scheduling policy selects parameters (\theta^{(n)}) at each iteration:[H_{n+1}=Q^{[4]}_{\theta^{(n)}}(H_n).]A scheduler is admissible only if:
it is deterministic given declared inputs (state, invariant measurements, and fixed seeds if any),
it preserves corridor constraints at every step via explicit checks,
it produces complete logs enabling reproduction.
Scheduling may be:
time-driven: (\theta^{(n)}) is a deterministic function of (n),
state-driven: (\theta^{(n)}) depends on (H_n) through measured invariants and residuals,
hybrid: time-driven with state-based correction.
5.2.3.3 Feedback from invariants
Let (\mathrm{Inv}) be the invariant suite and define the drift:[\Delta\mathrm{Inv}n := \mathrm{Inv}(H{n+1})-\mathrm{Inv}(H_n).]Feedback control adjusts (\theta^{(n)}) to enforce:[|\Delta\mathrm{Inv}_n|\preceq \delta.]
A canonical feedback law is a projected correction step:[\theta^{(n+1)} = \Pi_{\Theta}\Big(\theta^{(n)} - \eta,G(H_n,\theta^{(n)})\Big),]where (G) is a deterministic estimator of the sensitivity of invariant drift to parameters (analytic gradients when available, otherwise deterministic finite differences), and (\Pi_{\Theta}) is a deterministic projection onto admissible parameter sets.
Feedback is required to be fail-closed: if sensitivity estimates are unstable or indeterminate, the scheduler must default to a conservative admissible parameter set or abort with a certificate.
5.2.3.4 Optimal control of drift ((\Delta))
Define an optimal control objective that shapes drift while respecting feasibility and invariant corridors.
(A) Drift minimization.Minimize the norm of the increment:[\min_{\theta\in\Theta}\ \mathbb{E}{H\sim \Pi}\big[|\Delta\theta(H)|^2\big]\quad\text{s.t.}\quad Q^{[4]}_\theta\ \text{admissible on }\mathcal{U},]where (\Pi) is a declared distribution over states (gold states or domain samples), or remove expectation and enforce pointwise constraints on a finite test set.
(B) Targeted drift shaping.Given a target drift field (\Delta^\star(H)) or target invariant shaping direction, minimize[\min_{\theta\in\Theta}\ \mathbb{E}{H\sim\Pi}\big[|\Delta\theta(H)-\Delta^\star(H)|^2\big]\quad\text{s.t. corridor and feasibility constraints.}]
(C) Constrained invariants as hard constraints.Enforce[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(Q^{[4]}_\theta(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)\big|\preceq \delta]as a hard constraint, with optimization operating only over admissible (\theta). If infeasible, the solver must output an infeasibility certificate identifying violated necessary conditions.
(D) Gradient and adjoint structure (when differentiable).If (Q^{[4]}_\theta) is differentiable in (\theta), compute gradients by chain rules with declared adjoint operators for each stage. The engine must define adjoint kernels for program stages and certify correctness via finite-difference checks on gold states within tolerance.
5.2.4 Cycle composition and nesting
5.2.4.1 Nested cycles ((Q^{(m)}))
A nested cycle is a macro-operator formed by composing multiple quaternary cycles or embedding cycles as stages of a larger cycle.
(A) Iterated macro-cycle.Define the (m)-macro-cycle[Q^{(m)} := (Q^{[4]})^m,]with domain restricted to states for which all intermediate iterates are defined and admissible.
(B) Recursive quaternary composition.Define a higher-level quaternary engine (\mathsf{Q}^{\uparrow}) whose stages are themselves cycles:[Q^{\uparrow}k := (Q^{[4]}{(k)})^{m_k},\qquad k=1,\dots,4,]with full macro-cycle (Q^{\uparrow[4]}=Q^{\uparrow}_4\circ\cdots\circ Q^{\uparrow}_1). This yields hierarchical control and multi-timescale dynamics.
(C) Near-closure across nesting.If (Q^{[4]}=I+\epsilon\Delta) and (\Delta) is regular, then[(Q^{[4]})^m = I + m\epsilon \Delta + O(m\epsilon^2),]on domains where higher-order terms remain controlled. Nested near-closure must be re-certified; it cannot be assumed.
(D) Nested corridor allocation.Budgets must allocate across levels: per-stage budgets for inner cycles and aggregate budgets for macro-cycles, with deterministic rules preventing budget leakage across nesting layers.
5.2.4.2 Multi-cycle interference
Consider two cycles (Q) and (R). Alternating execution defines[H_{n+1}=\begin{cases}Q(H_n),& n\ \text{even},\R(H_n),& n\ \text{odd}.\end{cases}]Interference is measured by the noncommutativity defect:[\mathsf{Def}_{\mathrm{comm}}(H):=|Q(R(H)) - R(Q(H))|.]In algebraic realizations with generators (X) and (Y),[e^{\epsilon X}e^{\epsilon Y}=e^{\epsilon(X+Y)+\frac{\epsilon^2}{2}[X,Y]+O(\epsilon^3)},]so interference is controlled by commutators. In discrete realizations, interference is controlled by program rewrite commutation diagnostics and measured by invariant drift discrepancies under swapped execution order.
5.2.4.3 Synchronization conditions
Synchronization refers to phase-locking or period alignment between cycles or between cycle execution and invariant monitoring windows.
(A) Period synchronization.Two cycles synchronize with ratio (m:n) on a state (H) if[Q^m(H)\sim_{\mathcal{G}} R^n(H),]within declared tolerances after canonicalization, and if this alignment persists under small perturbations (structural stability).
(B) Resonant synchronization.In linear reductions, synchronization corresponds to commensurate Floquet multipliers and shared invariant subspaces. In nonlinear regimes, synchronization is detected by recurring invariant signatures at commensurate iteration indices.
(C) Corridor-based synchronization.Synchronization can be enforced by corridor constraints requiring invariant profiles to match at designated cycle boundaries:[\mathrm{Inv}(H_{t_j}) \approx \mathrm{Inv}(H_{t_{j+M}}),]for a declared monitoring period (M).
(D) Synchronization certificates.Synchronization is reported with: ratio, tolerance, invariant traces, and phase/alignment diagnostics computed deterministically.
5.2.4.4 Cycle graph representations
A quaternary engine is represented as a directed typed multigraph.
Nodes: typed state spaces or intermediate representations (including canonicalized states).
