Edges: stage operators (Q_k) with declared parameterization and certificates.
Subgraphs: nested cycles represented as hyperedges or grouped subgraphs.
A cycle graph representation is valid only if it is:
acyclic within each stage program (unless explicitly modeling feedback),
deterministically serializable,
type-checkable against declared domains/codomains,
equipped with a rewrite system preserving semantics and certificates.
Graph-level identities correspond to program normal forms and determine cycle identity hashing.
5.3 Cloud — Stability, Corridors, and Failure Maps for ((Q))
5.3.1 Bounding ((\epsilon\Delta))
5.3.1.1 Operator norm bounds
Assume near-closure (Q(H)=H+\epsilon\Delta(H)) on (\mathcal{U}).
(A) Uniform increment bound.If (|\Delta(H)|\le M) on (\mathcal{U}), then single-step deviation satisfies[|Q(H)-H|\le \epsilon M.]
(B) Linear bounded increment.If (\Delta\in\mathcal{B}(H^4)), then[|Q-I|{\mathrm{op}} \le \epsilon|\Delta|{\mathrm{op}}.]If (\epsilon|\Delta|{\mathrm{op}}<1), then (Q) is invertible with Neumann-series inverse on the range where the bound holds:[Q^{-1} = (I+\epsilon\Delta)^{-1} = \sum{n=0}^\infty (-\epsilon\Delta)^n.]
(C) Lipschitz increment.If (\Delta) is Lipschitz with constant (L), then[|Q(H)-Q(\widetilde{H})|\le (1+\epsilon L)|H-\widetilde{H}|.]
(D) Stage-induced bounds.If stage-wise deviations satisfy (|Q_k(H)-H|\le \epsilon M_k) (on stage domains), then a conservative bound for the full cycle is[|Q^{[4]}(H)-H|\le \epsilon\sum_{k=1}^4 \widetilde{M}_k,]where (\widetilde{M}_k) accounts for propagation through subsequent stage Lipschitz constants. These constants must be computed or bounded and included in certificates.
5.3.1.2 Scale-dependent drift bounds
When multi-scale invariants (Chapter 4) are used as guards, drift must be bounded per scale index (k).
Let (\mathrm{Inv}^{(k)}) denote invariants at scale (\epsilon_k), and define budgets (\delta^{(k)}). A scale-dependent drift bound is:[\big|\mathrm{Inv}^{(k)}(Q(H))-\mathrm{Inv}^{(k)}(H)\big|\preceq \delta^{(k)}\qquad\forall k.]If drift is induced by a linear increment (X), and (\mathrm{Inv}^{(k)}) is Lipschitz with constant (L_k), then[\big|\mathrm{Inv}^{(k)}(Q(H))-\mathrm{Inv}^{(k)}(H)\big|\le L_k,\epsilon,|\Delta(H)|,]providing a constructive budget allocation rule. When (L_k) depends on resolution, this dependence must be explicitly reported.
5.3.1.3 Noise-dependent drift bounds
Let the executed cycle be perturbed by noise or approximation errors:[\widetilde{Q}(H)=Q(H)+\xi(H),]with deterministic envelope (|\xi(H)|\le \eta(H)). Then[|\widetilde{Q}(H)-H|\le \epsilon|\Delta(H)|+\eta(H).]For invariant drift, if (\mathrm{Inv}) is Lipschitz with constant (L_{\mathrm{Inv}}),[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(\widetilde{Q}(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(Q(H))\big|\le L_{\mathrm{Inv}}\eta(H),]so budgets must include a noise/approximation component:[\delta_{\mathrm{total}} \succeq \delta_{\mathrm{model}} + \delta_{\mathrm{disc}} + \delta_{\mathrm{noise}},]with (\delta_{\mathrm{noise}}) derived from (\eta(H)) and Lipschitz envelopes.
5.3.1.4 Certified drift reporting
A drift certificate for a cycle execution on input (H) consists of the deterministic record:
cycle ID (\mathrm{ID}_{\mathsf{Q}}),
input state ID and output state ID (canonical state hashes),
near-closure residual:[r(H):=Q(H)-H-\epsilon\Delta(H),]and its norm bound (|r(H)|\le \eta(H)),
invariant drift vector:[\Delta\mathrm{Inv}(H):=\mathrm{Inv}(Q(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H),]with componentwise comparison against budgets,
stage-wise logs (optional or mandatory depending on policy) documenting intermediate states and per-stage drift.
Certificates must be deterministically serializable and hashed into an execution ID.
5.3.2 Admissibility of cycle stages
5.3.2.1 Stage-wise corridor tests
Each stage (Q_k) must pass:
feasibility test: (|C(Q_k(H))|\le \tau_k),
invariant drift test on guard invariants:[\big|\mathrm{Inv}{\mathrm{guard}}(Q_k(H))-\mathrm{Inv}{\mathrm{guard}}(H)\big|\preceq \delta_{\mathrm{guard}}^{(k)},]
regularity typing test: (Q_k(H)\in X_{s_k^{\mathrm{out}}}),
numerical stability test: finite values, conditioning thresholds, solver convergence where applicable.
Stage-wise tests are fail-closed: failure triggers reject or deterministic repair (§5.3.2.4).
5.3.2.2 Composition corridor tests
Even if each stage is individually admissible, the composition may violate corridor constraints due to cumulative effects. Therefore the full cycle (Q^{[4]}) must pass:[|C(Q^{[4]}(H))|\le \tau,\qquad\big|\mathrm{Inv}(Q^{[4]}(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)\big|\preceq \delta,]and, when near-closure is certified,[|Q^{[4]}(H)-H-\epsilon\Delta(H)|\le \eta(H).]Composition tests must be evaluated on canonicalized states when pattern-level semantics are enforced.
5.3.2.3 Reconstruction compatibility
Let (R_A) be a reconstruction operator (Chapter 2) and let (\varepsilon(A;H)) denote a reconstruction error envelope estimate at state (H). The cycle is reconstruction-compatible if, for all required observation regimes (A),[\varepsilon(A;Q^{[4]}(H))\le \varepsilon_{\max}(A),]and optionally if each stage preserves a weaker per-stage reconstructibility:[\varepsilon(A;Q_k(H))\le \varepsilon_{\max}^{(k)}(A).]Reconstruction compatibility must be logged and included in certificates when the engine is used in pipelines requiring reconstruction guarantees.
5.3.2.4 Reject/repair logic
A deterministic reject/repair policy is defined by:
guard failure (\Rightarrow) reject immediately (no output accepted),
feasibility drift (\Rightarrow) apply feasibility repair (P) and re-evaluate invariants,
estimator instability (\Rightarrow) apply declared robustification (smoothing floors, coarse-graining adjustment within suite) and re-evaluate,
solver nonconvergence (\Rightarrow) deterministic retry schedule (step size reduction, iteration cap adjustments) with fixed maximum retries,
persistent failure (\Rightarrow) emit failure certificate containing violated constraints and recommended design changes.
Repair must never change invariant suite IDs or corridor policy IDs unless the engine explicitly enters a different declared operating mode with a different ID.
5.3.3 Failure modes
5.3.3.1 Divergence modes
Divergence occurs when trajectory norms or guard quantities grow unbounded or exceed allowed envelopes.
Diagnostics.
norm blow-up: (|H_{n}|>\Gamma),
feasibility blow-up: (|C(H_n)|>\tau),
conditioning blow-up: (\kappa_n>\kappa_{\max}) where (\kappa_n) is a declared conditioning measure (e.g., Jacobian condition estimate).
Certified detection rule.Divergence is declared when any guard exceeds its threshold at any step, with deterministic ordering and no heuristic exceptions.
5.3.3.2 Mode collapse and oversmoothing
Mode collapse refers to loss of diversity in representation (e.g., spectral energy concentrates into low bands) beyond corridor intent; oversmoothing refers to excessive suppression of fine-scale structure.
Diagnostics.
spectral concentration: (q_{\mathrm{low}}(H_n)\ge 1-\eta) for too large (\eta),
entropy saturation: coarse entropies approach extrema inconsistent with baseline,
mutual-information collapse: designated coupling invariants drop below thresholds.
Certified detection rule.Define a guard vector (\mathrm{Inv}_{\mathrm{collapse}}) (spectral entropy, band ratios, MI), and declare failure when any component violates its directional corridor constraints.
5.3.3.3 Aliasing-amplification modes
Aliasing amplification occurs when high-frequency or undersampled components are driven into observable bands, increasing reconstruction error or creating spurious invariant drift.
Diagnostics.
growth of high-band energy relative to resolution cutoff,
increased reconstruction residual (\varepsilon(A)),
increased discrepancy between discrete and refined invariants beyond truncation envelopes.
Certified detection rule.Define a resolution guard invariant (\mathrm{Inv}_{\mathrm{alias}}) and require it to remain within precomputed truncation envelopes; violations indicate aliasing amplification.
5.3.3.4 Adversarial drift modes
Adversarial drift refers to systematic corridor-satisfying changes that accumulate to alter unmonitored aspects of the state, or to drift that exploits estimator weaknesses (tie boundaries, phase singularities).
Diagnostics.
repeated near-threshold oscillations in guard invariants with steady growth in non-guard measures,
instability concentrated in invariant components known to be discontinuous or fragile,
divergence between behavioral hash and structural hash expectations on gold states.
Certified detection rule.Adversarial drift is declared when:
non-guard diagnostic invariants exceed secondary thresholds while guard invariants remain within corridors, and
the pattern distance (gauge-invariant) from baseline grows beyond an allowed envelope:[d_{\mathcal{G}}(H_n,H_0)>\Gamma_{\mathrm{adv}},]on a declared horizon.
5.3.4 Proof templates
5.3.4.1 Contraction mapping proofs
A contraction proof certifies convergence to a unique fixed point.
Template requirements:
a complete metric space ((\mathcal{U},d)),
invariance: (Q(\mathcal{U})\subseteq\mathcal{U}),
contraction constant (\lambda<1) with[d(Q(H),Q(\widetilde{H}))\le \lambda d(H,\widetilde{H}),]
explicit fixed point (H^\star) existence and uniqueness by Banach theorem,
explicit convergence rate (\lambda^n).
5.3.4.2 Lyapunov function proofs
A Lyapunov proof certifies stability or convergence via monotone decrease of a functional.
Template requirements:
a functional (V:H^4\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}) coercive on (\mathcal{U}),
a decrease condition:[V(Q(H))\le V(H)-\alpha W(H),]with (\alpha>0) and (W\ge 0),
boundedness of orbits in (\mathcal{U}),
identification of invariant set where (W(H)=0) and characterization of limit points.
This template applies directly when (\Delta=-\nabla\Phi) or when stages implement proximal/regularization steps.
5.3.4.3 Energy method proofs
Energy methods certify boundedness and stability of norms and invariant energies under iteration.
Template requirements:
a norm/energy (E(H)) (e.g., (|H|^2), band energies, constraint residual energy),
a per-step bound:[E(Q(H)) \le (1+\epsilon a)E(H)+\epsilon b,]
Grönwall-type iteration yields:[E(H_n)\le e^{a n\epsilon}E(H_0)+\frac{b}{a}(e^{a n\epsilon}-1),]with explicit constants.
Energy methods link directly to scale-dependent drift envelopes and stability certificates.
5.3.4.4 Counterexample framing proofs
Counterexample proofs certify impossibility or failure regimes by constructing explicit states or mode families.
Template requirements:
explicit state family ({H_\lambda}\subseteq \mathcal{U}),
explicit demonstration that stage or cycle violates a desired property (e.g., invariance, stability, reconstructibility),
certification that violation persists under allowed tolerances (not a numerical artifact),
extraction of necessary design changes to remove the counterexample (observer enrichment, coupling constraints, stricter stage admissibility, modified budgets).
5.4 Fractal — Implementing the Engine
5.4.1 Engine execution API
5.4.1.1 State object interface
A state object (\mathsf{State}) represents (H\in H^{(N,P)}) (or a continuum proxy) and must implement:
data access[\mathsf{State.data} \in \mathbb{K}^{M}\quad (\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{R}\text{ or }\mathbb{C}),]with declared memory layout and deterministic iteration order;
inner product and norm[\langle H,\widetilde{H}\rangle,\quad |H|,\quad \text{consistent with discretization weights;}]
constraint evaluation[\mathsf{State.residual} := C_{N,P}(H),]with deterministic residual norms and thresholds;
canonicalization[\mathsf{State.can()} := \mathrm{Can}_{N,P}(H),]producing a canonical representative with stable serialization;
serialization[\mathsf{State.serialize()} \to \text{bytes},]deterministic under fixed precision policies, enabling content hashing.
5.4.1.2 Stage operator interface
A stage operator (\mathsf{Stage}) implements:
apply[\mathsf{Stage.apply}(H) \to H',]deterministic, typed, with domain checks;
admissibility check[\mathsf{Stage.check}(H,H') \to \mathsf{CheckResult},]verifying feasibility, guard invariants, stability diagnostics;
certificate emission[\mathsf{Stage.cert}(H,H') \to \mathsf{Cert},]including stage ID, parameters, drift diagnostics;
optional adjoint/gradient[\mathsf{Stage.adjoint}(),\quad \mathsf{Stage.grad}(\cdot),]required when optimization/control is enabled and must be verified by deterministic tests.
5.4.1.3 Monitoring hooks
Monitoring hooks compute invariants and diagnostics during execution.
A hook (\mathsf{Hook}) specifies:
a sampling schedule (every step, every (m) steps, stage boundaries),
a set of computations: feasibility residuals, invariant suites, reconstruction checks, conditioning estimates,
an abort policy and repair triggers.
Hooks must be pure observers unless explicitly declared to alter execution (repair mode), in which case all alterations are logged and hashed.
5.4.1.4 Deterministic execution rules
Execution determinism requires:
fixed evaluation order for reductions and floating-point summations;
fixed precision policies (float32/float64/mixed) declared by the engine ID;
fixed seeds for any stochastic components (if permitted at all);
fixed solver tolerances and maximum iterations;
fixed tie-breaking policies in discontinuous invariants (topological summaries, rank selections).
Any nondeterminism invalidates cycle identity hashing and certificate reproducibility.
5.4.2 Numerical methods
5.4.2.1 Discrete differential operators
Differential stages require discrete operators consistent with the model’s inner product and boundary policies.
Required properties:
discrete operator consistency: (D_{N,P}\to D) in a declared sense on smooth cores;
stability: discrete operator norms bounded or controlled by step sizes;
boundary handling: deterministic stencils (Dirichlet/Neumann/periodic) with explicit semantics;
adjoint consistency: if adjoints are used, (D_{N,P}^\ast) matches the discrete inner product.
5.4.2.2 Stable integration schemes
When stages implement flow steps (e^{\epsilon X_k}) implicitly or explicitly, stable integrators are required.
Examples of admissible schemes (to be chosen and fixed by the engine instance):
implicit Euler for dissipative generators,
Crank–Nicolson for balanced stability/accuracy,
symplectic or unitary integrators for conservative/skew-adjoint generators,
Strang splitting for multi-generator decompositions.
Each scheme must declare:
local truncation error order,
stability region,
deterministic solver strategy (linear/nonlinear solve),
stopping criteria and residual thresholds.
5.4.2.3 Spectral vs spatial methods
Stages may be executed in spectral or spatial form.
Spectral methods require:
FFT/eigenbasis determinism,
band cutoff policies,
leakage monitoring and energy conservation checks.
Spatial methods require:
stencil determinism,
CFL-like step constraints (when applicable),
boundary condition enforcement.
The engine must declare the representation per stage, and must include representation-transfer diagnostics ensuring invariants are comparable across representation changes.
5.4.2.4 Error control policies
Error control is a mandatory engine component.
Policies include:
step size control: (\epsilon) adapted by deterministic rules based on residual estimates;
tolerance budgeting: numerical tolerances allocated into invariant corridors;
estimator robustness: probability smoothing floors and coarse-graining adjustments within suite;
fail-closed escalation: if error cannot be controlled, abort and emit failure certificates.
5.4.3 Engine certificates
5.4.3.1 Stage logs
Each stage produces a log record containing:
stage ID and parameter hash,
input state ID and output state ID,
feasibility residuals pre/post,
guard invariants pre/post,
numerical diagnostics (iterations, conditioning, convergence flags),
time/memory profiling if deterministically measured.
Stage logs are serialized deterministically and content-hashed.
5.4.3.2 Invariant drift logs
Invariant drift logs contain:[\Delta\mathrm{Inv}n := \mathrm{Inv}(H{n+1})-\mathrm{Inv}(H_n),]together with:
budgets (\delta),
pass/fail flags,
maximal normalized violation metric,
scale/region attribution for multi-scale invariants.
All logs are emitted both per stage (if configured) and per full cycle.
5.4.3.3 Reconstruction compatibility logs
When reconstruction compatibility is required, logs contain:
mask/sampling protocol IDs,
reconstruction operator IDs,
reconstruction errors (\varepsilon(A)),
conditioning metrics,
pass/fail flags and envelope comparisons.
These logs link Chapter 5 execution to Chapter 2 guarantees and are part of admissibility certificates.
5.4.3.4 Reproducibility manifests
A reproducibility manifest contains:
engine ID (\mathrm{ID}_{\mathsf{Q}}),
invariant suite ID and corridor policy ID,
discretization parameters ((N,P)) and basis IDs,
precision policies,
solver parameters and iteration caps,
seed policies (if any),
hash links to all logs and artifacts.
The manifest is necessary and sufficient to reproduce the run within declared numeric tolerances.
5.4.4 Engine benchmarks
5.4.4.1 Canonical test patterns
Canonical test patterns are fixed gold states (H_j\in H^{(N,P)}) (and their refinements) designed to exercise:
feasibility constraints,
gauge and canonicalization,
multi-scale invariant signatures,
reconstruction stability regimes.
Each pattern must include a canonical state hash, invariant baseline vector, and expected drift envelopes under declared reference cycles.
5.4.4.2 Stress tests
Stress tests probe stability boundaries:
noise injection at declared amplitudes,
truncation stress (lower resolutions, aggressive cutoffs),
adversarial drift tests (constructed null-mode perturbations),
long-horizon iteration tests for accumulation behavior.
Stress tests produce pass/fail outcomes with full certificates and are versioned as part of the engine validation suite.
5.4.4.3 Performance metrics
Performance metrics include:
runtime per stage and per cycle,
memory footprint per stage,
invariant computation overhead,
reconstruction check overhead,
throughput under batch execution on gold states.
Performance is reported deterministically where possible (operation counts) and otherwise as bounded intervals under controlled environments. Performance regressions are treated as versioned changes requiring justification.
5.4.4.4 Regression suite
A regression suite is the authoritative test harness enforcing that:
invariants match gold baselines within declared envelopes,
near-closure residuals remain within certified bounds,
feasibility and reconstructibility are preserved,
determinism holds (hashes stable across reruns).
Any regression failure emits a failure certificate including minimal reproduction manifests and localized diagnostics (stage index, scale index, region index, and offending invariants).
Chapter 5 — The Quaternary Engine ((Q)): Closed Operator Cycles
PART II — TRANSFORMATIONS & ENCODING (Ch. 6–10)
Chapter 6 — Primitive Transformations ((T_1)–(T_4)): Contraction/Extension/Rotation/Manifestation
6.1 Square — Definitions and Canonical Forms of the Four Primitives
6.1.1 ((T_1)) Contraction (Tzimtzum) as operator class
6.1.1.1 Domain restriction and energy compaction
Let ((H^4,\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle,|\cdot|)) denote the holographic state space equipped with a fixed inner product and induced norm. Let (C:H^4\supseteq\mathcal{D}(C)\to \mathcal{Y}) be the constraint operator with (H^4=\ker(C)) (or a corridor-feasible relaxation), and let (\mathrm{Inv}:H^4\to\mathbb{R}^m) be the declared invariant suite with budgets (\delta\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}). Let (\mathrm{Can}:H^4\to H^4) denote the canonicalization operator selecting gauge representatives when required.
A contraction primitive is a (possibly nonlinear) operator[T_1:\mathcal{D}(T_1)\subseteq H^4\to H^4]designed to reduce effective support, effective dimensionality, or effective description length while maintaining feasibility and corridor bounds. Contraction is formalized as an operator class characterized by compaction of a declared energy decomposition.
Let (\mathcal{B}) be a declared analysis basis or frame for (H^4) with analysis map (\mathcal{A}{\mathcal{B}}:H^4\to \ell^2(\Lambda)) and synthesis map (\mathcal{S}{\mathcal{B}}:\ell^2(\Lambda)\to H^4). Let coefficients be (a=\mathcal{A}{\mathcal{B}}(H)). Let (\Lambda) admit a partition into index blocks (\Lambda=\bigsqcup{r\in\mathcal{R}}\Lambda_r) representing spatial regions, frequency bands, scales, or other resolution atoms. Define block energies[E_r(H):=\sum_{\lambda\in\Lambda_r} |a_\lambda|^2,\qquad E(H):=\sum_{r\in\mathcal{R}}E_r(H)=|a|{\ell^2}^2,]with the convention that (|a|{\ell^2}) is equivalent to (|H|) via the declared frame bounds.
A contraction is said to perform domain restriction if it reduces the mass of the state outside a declared active index set (\Lambda_{\mathrm{act}}\subseteq \Lambda), measured by[\mathrm{Out}(H;\Lambda_{\mathrm{act}}):=\sum_{\lambda\notin \Lambda_{\mathrm{act}}}|a_\lambda|^2.]A contraction is said to perform energy compaction if it increases the fraction of energy contained in the top-(K) coefficients or top-(K) blocks, quantified by a compaction functional (\mathsf{Comp}) such as[\mathsf{Comp}K(H):=\frac{\sum{\lambda\in\Lambda_K(H)}|a_\lambda|^2}{\sum_{\lambda\in\Lambda}|a_\lambda|^2},]where (\Lambda_K(H)\subseteq\Lambda) denotes indices of the (K) largest (|a_\lambda|) (ties resolved deterministically).
Admissible contraction requirement. A contraction primitive (T_1) must satisfy, for all (H\in\mathcal{U}\subseteq \mathcal{D}(T_1)\cap H^4),[T_1(H)\in H^4,\qquad\big|\mathrm{Inv}(T_1(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)\big|\preceq \delta^{(1)},]for a declared per-primitive budget (\delta^{(1)}), and must additionally satisfy a compaction target[\mathsf{Comp}K(T_1(H))\ge \mathsf{Comp}K(H) - \eta{\mathrm{comp}},]with deterministic tolerance (\eta{\mathrm{comp}}\ge 0) and declared (K) (or a schedule (K(\epsilon)) in multi-scale regimes).
6.1.1.2 Sparsification and compression forms
Contraction primitives are implemented through one of the following canonical forms, each of which is a typed template specifying the operator as a composition of analysis, coefficient transformation, synthesis, and feasibility repair.
(A) Hard-threshold contraction.Given basis/frame (\mathcal{B}), threshold (\tau\ge 0), and deterministic tie-breaking, define the coefficient map[(\mathcal{T}{\tau}^{\mathrm{hard}}(a))\lambda :=\begin{cases}a_\lambda, & |a_\lambda|\ge \tau,\0, & |a_\lambda|<\tau,\end{cases}]with ties at (|a_\lambda|=\tau) resolved by a fixed ordering on (\Lambda). The raw contracted state is[\widetilde{H}:=\mathcal{S}{\mathcal{B}}(\mathcal{T}{\tau}^{\mathrm{hard}}(\mathcal{A}{\mathcal{B}}(H))).]The admissible contraction is the feasibility-repaired and canonicalized output[T_1(H):=\mathrm{Can}\circ \mathsf{Proj}{H^4}(\widetilde{H}),]where (\mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}) is an exact projection onto (\ker(C)) when available, or a deterministic penalty/proximal repair producing a state whose constraint residual is within the declared tolerance.
(B) Soft-threshold (proximal) contraction.Given (\lambda>0), define[(\mathcal{T}{\lambda}^{\mathrm{soft}}(a))\lambda :=\max{|a_\lambda|-\lambda,0},\frac{a_\lambda}{|a_\lambda|},]with (\frac{a_\lambda}{|a_\lambda|}:=0) when (a_\lambda=0). Then define (\widetilde{H}) and (T_1(H)) as above. This form corresponds to the proximal map of an (\ell^1) regularizer in coefficient space and is the canonical contraction form for convex sparsification.
(C) Block-sparsity contraction.With blocks ({\Lambda_r}), define block norms (|a|{r}:=\left(\sum{\lambda\in\Lambda_r}|a_\lambda|^2\right)^{1/2}). For threshold (\tau\ge 0),[(\mathcal{T}^{\mathrm{block}}{\tau}(a)){\lambda} :=\begin{cases}a_\lambda,& |a|{r(\lambda)}\ge \tau,\0,& |a|{r(\lambda)}<\tau,\end{cases}]where (r(\lambda)) is the unique block containing (\lambda). This contraction preserves grouped structure and is appropriate when invariants couple within blocks.
(D) Low-rank contraction.When (H) admits an operator/matrix representation (M(H)) in a declared representation, contraction may be defined via spectral truncation:[M(\widetilde{H}) := \sum_{j=1}^K \sigma_j u_j v_j^\ast,]where (\sigma_j) are singular values of (M(H)) in decreasing order with deterministic tie-breaking, and (u_j,v_j) are corresponding singular vectors. The map (H\mapsto \widetilde{H}) is the inverse representation (M^{-1}) applied to the truncated factorization, followed by feasibility repair and canonicalization.
Each contraction form must include a deterministic compression report:
retained index set (\Lambda_{\mathrm{act}}) (or retained rank (K)),
energy retained fraction (\mathsf{Comp}_K(H)),
invariant drift and constraint residual drift,
reconstruction-compatibility diagnostics if required.
6.1.1.3 Constraint-preserving contraction
Contraction must preserve feasibility (C(H)=0) (hard) or corridor feasibility (soft). A contraction is strongly constraint-preserving if[C(H)=0 \ \Longrightarrow\ C(T_1(H))=0]for all admissible inputs. It is corridor constraint-preserving if[|C(T_1(H))|\le \tau_1\quad\text{whenever}\quad |C(H)|\le \tau_{\mathrm{in}},]for declared (\tau_{\mathrm{in}},\tau_1).
A canonical method to ensure constraint preservation is projected contraction:[T_1 := \mathrm{Can}\circ \mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}\circ \widetilde{T}_1,]where (\widetilde{T}1) is any contraction map on (\mathfrak{H}) (or on a relaxation of (H^4)), and (\mathsf{Proj}{H^4}) is a deterministic projection/repair map.
When (\mathsf{Proj}{H^4}) is defined as a minimizer of a convex penalty, a canonical form is:[\mathsf{Proj}{H^4}(Z) := \arg\min_{H\in \mathcal{D}}\ \frac12|H-Z|^2 + \alpha,|C(H)|^2,]with fixed (\alpha>0) and deterministic solver rules. When hard feasibility is required and direct projection is available, use:[\mathsf{Proj}{H^4}(Z) := \arg\min{H\in H^4}\ |H-Z|.]
6.1.1.4 Canonical parameterization
A contraction primitive is parameterized by a canonical parameter tuple[\theta_1 := (\mathcal{B}, \pi_{\mathcal{B}}, \kappa, \tau, \alpha, \mathrm{ProjID}, \mathrm{CanID}, \mathrm{PolicyID}),]where:
(\mathcal{B}) is the basis/frame identifier;
(\pi_{\mathcal{B}}) is the deterministic index ordering on (\Lambda);
(\kappa) is the retention level (e.g., (K) or block budget);
(\tau) is threshold(s) (scalar or blockwise);
(\alpha) is projection/repair strength when penalty-based;
(\mathrm{ProjID}) identifies the constraint repair method and solver tolerances;
(\mathrm{CanID}) identifies the canonicalization rule;
(\mathrm{PolicyID}) identifies determinism, precision, and tie-breaking policies.
Canonicalization of parameters requires:
normalization of thresholds under basis scaling;
deterministic serialization of all floating parameters (fixed precision rounding rules);
explicit declaration of the compaction objective (top-(K), threshold, low-rank, blockwise) so that parameter equivalence is decidable.
6.1.2 ((T_2)) Extension (Kav) as operator class
6.1.2.1 Support expansion mechanisms
An extension primitive is an operator[T_2:\mathcal{D}(T_2)\subseteq H^4\to H^4]designed to enlarge support, continue a state from a restricted region, increase effective bandwidth under controlled regularity, or fill missing data under constraints, while preserving admissibility corridors.
Let (\Omega) be the ambient domain and let (A\subseteq \Omega) be an observed/active subset. Let (\mathsf{Res}_A:H^4\to \mathsf{Obs}(A)) denote the restriction/observation operator. Extension aims to map a state to a new state whose observable content expands from (A) to a larger set (B\supseteq A), or to the full domain (\Omega).
A canonical notion of support expansion is defined via an energy decomposition in physical space. Let (\chi_A) denote a mask and define masked energy:[E_A(H):=|\chi_A H|^2,\qquad E_{\Omega\setminus A}(H):=|\chi_{\Omega\setminus A}H|^2.]Extension expands the admissible energy in (\Omega\setminus A) while preserving invariants and constraint feasibility:[E_{\Omega\setminus A}(T_2(H))\ge E_{\Omega\setminus A}(H) - \eta_{\mathrm{ext}}]with declared tolerance (\eta_{\mathrm{ext}}), and while maintaining corridor bounds on artifacts (defined below).
6.1.2.2 Interpolation and continuation
Extension is defined through continuation from partial data. Let (y:=\mathsf{Res}A(H)) be observation data on (A). A canonical extension form is a constrained variational continuation:[\widetilde{H}:=\arg\min{Z\in \mathcal{Z}} \ \mathcal{R}(Z) \quad\text{s.t.}\quad \mathsf{Res}_A(Z)=y,]where (\mathcal{R}) is a declared regularization functional enforcing smoothness, sparsity in a chosen basis, or consistency with a generative prior, and (\mathcal{Z}) is the typed regularity class (e.g., (H^s\cap\mathcal{D}(C))).
Canonical families include:
harmonic/elliptic continuation: (\mathcal{R}(Z)=|\nabla Z|^2) with boundary conditions and PDE constraints;
Sobolev continuation: (\mathcal{R}(Z)=|Z|_{H^s}^2);
frame continuation: (\mathcal{R}(Z)=|\mathcal{A}{\mathcal{B}}(Z)|{\ell^1}) or mixed norms;
constraint-driven continuation: include (|C(Z)|^2) as a hard or soft constraint.
The admissible extension is:[T_2(H):=\mathrm{Can}\circ \mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}(\widetilde{H}),]with deterministic solver policies and explicit feasibility certification.
6.1.2.3 Constraint-preserving extension
Extension must preserve feasibility and must not introduce constraint-incompatible artifacts. The extension is constraint-compatible if the continuation problem is posed in a feasible set:[\widetilde{H}\in H^4 \quad\text{or}\quad |C(\widetilde{H})|\le \tau_2,]and the final output satisfies hard or corridor feasibility.
If constraints include boundary traces or gauge conditions, the extension must incorporate them at the level of the continuation constraints. In particular, if (C) includes a divergence-free or integrability condition, extension must enforce it globally rather than only on (A).
A canonical constraint-aware continuation is:[\widetilde{H}:=\arg\min_{Z}\ \mathcal{R}(Z)\quad \text{s.t.}\quad \mathsf{Res}_A(Z)=\mathsf{Res}_A(H),\quad C(Z)=0,]with (C(Z)=0) relaxed to (|C(Z)|\le \tau) when necessary and certified.
6.1.2.4 Canonical parameterization
An extension primitive is parameterized by[\theta_2 := (A\hookrightarrow B,\ \mathsf{ObsID},\ \mathcal{R}\mathrm{ID},\ s,\ \alpha,\ \beta,\ \mathrm{BCID},\ \mathrm{SolverID},\ \mathrm{ProjID},\ \mathrm{CanID},\ \mathrm{PolicyID}),]where:
(A\hookrightarrow B) encodes the expansion (mask identifiers, geometry encoding, deterministic ordering);
(\mathsf{ObsID}) identifies the observation/restriction operator family;
(\mathcal{R}\mathrm{ID}) identifies the regularizer functional class;
(s) indicates regularity level (Sobolev index, wavelet scale, etc.);
(\alpha,\beta) encode weights for penalty terms (constraint penalty, prior penalty);
(\mathrm{BCID}) specifies boundary conditions for PDE continuations;
(\mathrm{SolverID}) specifies deterministic solver and tolerances;
(\mathrm{ProjID},\mathrm{CanID},\mathrm{PolicyID}) specify feasibility repair, canonicalization, and determinism.
Two parameter tuples are considered equivalent only if they induce identical serialized continuation problems and identical deterministic solver semantics.
6.1.3 ((T_3)) Rotation (Igulim) as operator class
6.1.3.1 Phase rotation and symmetry actions
A rotation primitive is an operator[T_3:\mathcal{D}(T_3)\subseteq H^4\to H^4]representing symmetry actions, unitary/orthogonal transforms, and phase-space rotations that preserve designated invariants exactly or within corridor bounds.
Let (\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{rot}}) be a declared rotation/symmetry group acting on (H^4) by a group action (\rho:\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{rot}}\times H^4\to H^4), written (g\cdot H). A rotation primitive is of the form[T_3(H)=\rho(g,H),]for a parameter (g\in \mathcal{G}{\mathrm{rot}}), possibly followed by canonicalization and feasibility projection if required:[T_3 := \mathrm{Can}\circ \mathsf{Proj}{H^4}\circ \rho(g,\cdot).]
A special case is phase rotation in a complex representation, defined by multiplication by a unimodular phase:[T_3(H)=e^{i\varphi}H,]or by a spatially varying phase compatible with constraints:[T_3(H)(x)=e^{i\varphi(x)}H(x),]when (\varphi) is constrained so that (C(e^{i\varphi}H)=0) whenever (C(H)=0), or when followed by constraint repair.
6.1.3.2 Orthogonal/unitary transforms
A rotation primitive is unitary (or orthogonal in the real case) if it preserves the inner product:[\langle T_3(H),T_3(\widetilde{H})\rangle=\langle H,\widetilde{H}\rangle\quad\forall H,\widetilde{H}\in\mathcal{D}(T_3),]hence preserves energy:[|T_3(H)|=|H|.]
Canonical unitary rotations include:
Fourier-domain rotations: (T_3 = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\circ U\circ \mathcal{F}) where (U) is unitary on coefficient space;
spatial coordinate rotations: (H(x)\mapsto H(R^{-1}x)) for a rotation matrix (R) with appropriate boundary handling;
frame rotations: (a\mapsto Ua) with unitary (U) in coefficient domain, followed by synthesis.
When (H^4) is defined by constraints (C(H)=0), a unitary operator is constraint-preserving if[C\circ T_3 = 0\quad\text{whenever}\quad C=0,]equivalently (T_3(\ker C)\subseteq \ker C). For linear constraints, sufficient is commutation:[C\circ T_3 = C \quad\text{on the domain,}]or (CT_3 = C) as operators when properly typed.
6.1.3.3 Manifold rotations in parameter space
Rotation may act not only on the state representation but also on a parameter manifold (\mathcal{M}) indexing families of states (H(\eta)) with (\eta\in\mathcal{M}). A manifold rotation is:[T_3(H(\eta)) := H(\Psi(\eta)),]where (\Psi:\mathcal{M}\to\mathcal{M}) is a diffeomorphism (or a group action) preserving a declared metric or measure on parameter space.
This form supports reparameterizations and gauge-compatible reindexing. Admissibility requires:
(\Psi) is deterministic and invertible on its declared domain;
invariants are preserved under pullback:[\mathrm{Inv}(H(\Psi(\eta)))=\mathrm{Inv}(H(\eta))]for exact invariants, or within corridor bounds.
6.1.3.4 Canonical parameterization
A rotation primitive is parameterized by[\theta_3 := (\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{rot}}\mathrm{ID},\ g,\ \mathrm{RepID},\ \mathrm{BCID},\ \mathrm{ProjID},\ \mathrm{CanID},\ \mathrm{PolicyID}),]where:
(\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{rot}}\mathrm{ID}) specifies the rotation group and representation;
(g) is the group element (e.g., Euler angles, axis-angle, unitary matrix, or Lie algebra coordinates);
(\mathrm{RepID}) specifies the coordinate/basis representation in which (g) acts;
(\mathrm{BCID}) specifies boundary conventions for coordinate transforms;
(\mathrm{ProjID},\mathrm{CanID},\mathrm{PolicyID}) specify feasibility repair, canonicalization, and determinism.
Canonicalization requires normalizing group parameters (e.g., angle wrapping into a fixed interval, canonical axis-angle sign conventions, deterministic decomposition of unitary matrices).
6.1.4 ((T_4)) Manifestation (Yosher) as operator class
6.1.4.1 Projection into explicit forms
A manifestation primitive is an operator[T_4:\mathcal{D}(T_4)\subseteq H^4\to H^4]that maps implicit or latent structure into explicit, reportable, renderable, or measurable forms while preserving admissibility. Manifestation includes controlled projection to structured submanifolds, explicit coordinate representations, or physically interpretable measurement embeddings.
Manifestation is formally distinguished from contraction/extension/rotation in that it may change representation class while remaining within (H^4) by composing with canonical representation embeddings. A canonical manifestation template is:[T_4 := \mathrm{Can}\circ \mathsf{Proj}{H^4}\circ \mathcal{M}{\mathrm{render}},]where (\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{render}}) is a deterministic rendering/projection operator into a declared explicit form family (\mathcal{E}\subseteq \mathfrak{H}) (e.g., piecewise-constant fields, quantized grids, sparse explicit feature decompositions), followed by feasibility repair and canonicalization.
6.1.4.2 Quantization and discretization mapping
Manifestation includes discretization maps from continuum-like representations to discrete explicit representations. Let (H^{(N,P)}) denote a finite model space. Let (\Pi_{N,P}:H^4\to H^{(N,P)}) be a deterministic discretization operator and let (\iota_{N,P}:H^{(N,P)}\to H^4) be the embedding. A manifestation step may take the form:[\widetilde{H} := \iota_{N,P}\circ Q_{\mathrm{quant}}\circ \Pi_{N,P}(H),]where (Q_{\mathrm{quant}}) is a quantizer mapping coefficients to a finite alphabet under fixed rounding and saturation rules.
Quantization error is quantified by[e_{\mathrm{q}}(H):=| \widetilde{H}-H|,]and must be bounded within the manifestation corridor (§6.3.4). If manifestation changes the norm equivalence (e.g., through non-orthonormal sampling), the error must be measured in declared energy and information-weighted norms.
6.1.4.3 Rendering/measurement operators
Manifestation includes deterministic observation operators (M) and renderers (R) that produce explicit measurements while retaining a state object in (H^4) (e.g., embedding the measurement record into an augmented state or producing a canonical representational substitute).
Let (\mathsf{Obs}) denote an observation space and (M:H^4\to \mathsf{Obs}) a deterministic observation operator. Manifestation can be defined as the state-level transform that makes the observation explicit via a declared embedding (\iota_M:\mathsf{Obs}\to H^4):[T_4(H):=\mathrm{Can}\circ \mathsf{Proj}{H^4}\big(H \oplus \iota_M(M(H))\big),]where (\oplus) denotes a declared direct-sum embedding or augmentation operator with typed semantics. When augmentation is not permitted, manifestation is instead defined as projection to a submanifold (\mathcal{E}\subseteq H^4) parameterized by explicit features (f):[T_4(H):=\arg\min{Z\in \mathcal{E}}\ |Z-H|^2\quad\text{(with deterministic tie-breaking),}]again followed by feasibility repair and canonicalization if (\mathcal{E}) is only approximately feasible.
6.1.4.4 Canonical parameterization
A manifestation primitive is parameterized by[\theta_4 := (\mathrm{FormID},\ \Pi_{N,P}\mathrm{ID},\ Q_{\mathrm{quant}}\mathrm{ID},\ \mathrm{ObsID},\ \iota_M\mathrm{ID},\ \mathrm{ProjID},\ \mathrm{CanID},\ \mathrm{PolicyID}),]where:
(\mathrm{FormID}) identifies the explicit form manifold/family (\mathcal{E});
(\Pi_{N,P}\mathrm{ID}) specifies discretization grid/basis and embedding conventions;
(Q_{\mathrm{quant}}\mathrm{ID}) specifies quantization (bit-depth, rounding, saturation);
(\mathrm{ObsID}) specifies measurement/rendering operator;
(\iota_M\mathrm{ID}) specifies observation embedding semantics;
(\mathrm{ProjID},\mathrm{CanID},\mathrm{PolicyID}) specify feasibility repair, canonicalization, and determinism.
6.2 Flower — Generator Algebras for the Primitives
6.2.1 Lie generators
6.2.1.1 Define generators ((X_1,\dots,X_4))
Assume a differentiable regime in which transformation families can be expressed as flows generated by vector fields on (H^4). A generator is a (possibly unbounded) operator or vector field (X:\mathcal{D}(X)\subseteq H^4\to H^4) such that the initial value problem[\frac{dH}{dt}=X(H),\qquad H(0)=H_0\in\mathcal{D}(X),]admits a flow map (\Phi^X_t(H_0)) on a declared time interval, with (\Phi^X_0=\mathrm{Id}).
The primitive transformations admit canonical generator classes:
Contraction generator (X_1) (dissipative/compacting): a vector field for which there exists a compaction functional (\mathsf{Comp}) such that along the flow (t\mapsto \Phi^{X_1}_t(H)),[\frac{d}{dt}\mathsf{Comp}(\Phi^{X_1}_t(H))\ge 0]in the weak sense appropriate to the chosen functional (or within tolerances).
Extension generator (X_2) (continuing/expanding): a vector field that increases support-energy outside a region or increases a continuation functional while preserving feasibility:[\frac{d}{dt}E_{\Omega\setminus A}(\Phi^{X_2}_t(H))\ge 0]subject to invariant corridor constraints.
Rotation generator (X_3) (skew-adjoint/symmetry): in linear regimes, (X_3) is skew-adjoint on (H^4),[X_3^\ast=-X_3,]implying unitary flow (e^{tX_3}) and energy preservation.
Manifestation generator (X_4) (projection/measurement drive): a vector field (often gradient-like) that decreases a manifestation objective (\Psi(H)) measuring distance to explicit form family (\mathcal{E}):[X_4(H)=-\nabla \Psi(H),]with (\Psi(H)=\mathrm{dist}(H,\mathcal{E})^2) or a surrogate, combined with feasibility projection when required.
In discrete realizations, generators are represented by parameterized update rules whose first-order increments approximate these vector fields.
6.2.1.2 Commutator table
Let (\mathfrak{g}) denote the Lie algebra generated (formally) by ({X_1,X_2,X_3,X_4}) under commutator ([X,Y]). The commutator structure controls noncommutativity errors in primitive compositions and the curvature of composite programs.
A canonical commutator table is defined by structure decompositions:[[X_i,X_j] = \sum_{k=1}^4 c_{ij}^k X_k + R_{ij},]where (c_{ij}^k\in\mathbb{R}) are declared structure constants (possibly zero) and (R_{ij}) is a residual vector field lying in a declared complement (e.g., higher-order correction space, constraint-repair direction space, or a residual class controlled by corridor bounds).
The framework requires the commutator semantics be specified at one of the following levels:
Exact closed algebra: (R_{ij}=0) for all (i,j). This is a strong regime enabling algebraic normal forms and exact BCH truncations within the primitive span.
Controlled residual algebra: (R_{ij}\neq 0) but satisfies a deterministic bound[|R_{ij}(H)|\le \gamma_{ij},\Xi(H),]for a declared functional (\Xi) and constants (\gamma_{ij}), on the admissible domain (\mathcal{U}).
Empirical commutator regime (discrete): commutators are defined operationally by finite differences on gold states:[X_i,X_j\approx \frac{1}{\epsilon^2}\big(T_i^\epsilon T_j^\epsilon(H)-T_j^\epsilon T_i^\epsilon(H)\big),]with deterministic (\epsilon) and certification envelopes.
In all regimes, the commutator table must support deterministic diagnostics of program noncommutativity and be included in the program certificate when BCH-based approximations are invoked.
6.2.1.3 Exponentiation to flows
For each generator (X_i), define the associated primitive flow (T_i(t)) by[T_i(t)(H):=\Phi^{X_i}t(H),\quad t\in \mathbb{R}{\ge 0}\ \text{(semigroup) or }t\in\mathbb{R}\ \text{(group),}]on the maximal domain where solutions exist and remain admissible. In linear regimes where (X_i) is a closed operator generating a (C_0)-semigroup (or group),[T_i(t)=e^{tX_i}.]
Admissibility of flows requires:
feasibility invariance:[H\in H^4\Longrightarrow T_i(t)(H)\in H^4;]
invariant corridors:[|\mathrm{Inv}(T_i(t)(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)|\preceq \delta_i(t);]
continuity and stability of the flow map in the declared topology.
6.2.1.4 BCH approximations
For small parameters, compositions of exponentials admit BCH expansions. For two generators (X,Y),[e^{\epsilon X}e^{\epsilon Y}=e^{\epsilon(X+Y)+\frac{\epsilon^2}{2}[X,Y]+O(\epsilon^3)},]where (O(\epsilon^3)) is controlled by nested commutators ([X,[X,Y]]), ([Y,[X,Y]]), etc., on a declared common domain.
For primitive compositions, BCH provides:
an effective generator for a program fragment,
explicit leading-order noncommutativity corrections,
principled step-size bounds for approximation validity.
Any use of BCH must be accompanied by:
the truncation order,
the commutator bounds used to certify the remainder,
the domain on which the approximation is certified.
6.2.2 Composition programs
6.2.2.1 Primitive sequencing grammars
A primitive program is a finite composition of primitive instances:[\mathsf{Prog} := T_{i_L}(\theta_{i_L})\circ \cdots \circ T_{i_2}(\theta_{i_2})\circ T_{i_1}(\theta_{i_1}),]where each (i_\ell\in{1,2,3,4}) and each (\theta_{i_\ell}) is a parameter tuple in canonical form.
A canonical grammar for primitive programs is specified by:
typed nonterminals representing admissible subspaces and representation modes;
production rules restricting sequences to those that preserve feasibility and canonical representation constraints;
normalization rules inserting canonicalization and feasibility repair at declared boundaries:[\cdots \circ T_i \circ \mathrm{Can}\circ \mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}\circ T_j\circ \cdots,]when required by typing.
A minimal admissible grammar must include:
a rule for contraction–extension alternation (to prevent unbounded growth/shrink),
a rule placing rotation stages where invariants require symmetry alignment,
a rule placing manifestation stages at extraction/report boundaries.
6.2.2.2 Minimal decompositions for target transforms
Let (\mathcal{T}\subseteq (H^4\to H^4)) denote a declared class of admissible target transforms (e.g., transforms in transformation space), equipped with a cost functional (\mathcal{C}(\cdot)) capturing program length, compute cost, and corridor tightness.
A primitive decomposition of (T\in\mathcal{T}) is a program (\mathsf{Prog}) such that[\sup_{H\in\mathcal{U}{\mathrm{test}}} d(\mathsf{Prog}(H),T(H))\le \eta{\mathrm{approx}},]for a declared test set (\mathcal{U}{\mathrm{test}}) and tolerance (\eta{\mathrm{approx}}), and such that (\mathsf{Prog}) is admissible (corridor-preserving) on (\mathcal{U}_{\mathrm{test}}).
A decomposition is minimal if it minimizes (\mathcal{C}(\mathsf{Prog})) among all decompositions meeting the approximation and admissibility requirements:[\mathsf{Prog}^\star \in \arg\min{\mathcal{C}(\mathsf{Prog}) : \mathsf{Prog}\approx T,\ \mathsf{Prog}\ \text{admissible}}.]
6.2.2.3 Program rewriting rules
A rewriting system (\mathsf{Rewrite}) is a set of typed rewrite rules preserving semantics and admissibility. Canonical rewrite families include:
rotation fusion: (T_3(g_2)\circ T_3(g_1)\Rightarrow T_3(g_2g_1)) with canonical group multiplication and parameter normalization;
manifestation idempotence: (T_4\circ T_4 \Rightarrow T_4) when (T_4) is a projection onto (\mathcal{E}) (or approximate idempotence within certified tolerance);
contraction–extension simplifications: rules that cancel redundant contraction immediately followed by extension when the net effect is identity within corridor tolerance, producing a shorter equivalent program with a certificate;
projection/canonicalization commuting rules: (\mathrm{Can}\circ \mathsf{Proj}{H^4}\Rightarrow \mathsf{Proj}{H^4}\circ \mathrm{Can}) only when provably equivalent under the declared gauge and constraint structure; otherwise prohibited.
Each rewrite rule must:
preserve typing and determinism,
preserve admissibility corridors (or adjust budgets deterministically and certify),
update program identity hashes.
6.2.2.4 Cycle embedding of primitives
Primitive programs embed naturally into the quaternary engine cycles by assigning the four primitive archetypes to the four stages:[Q_1 \equiv T_1,\quad Q_2 \equiv T_2,\quad Q_3 \equiv T_3,\quad Q_4 \equiv T_4,]or by allowing each stage (Q_k) to be a composite program predominantly of the (k)-th primitive type:[Q_k := \mathsf{Prog}_k(T_k;\theta_k),]with deterministic stage IDs and admissibility budgets allocated stage-wise:[\delta \succeq \delta^{(1)}+\delta^{(2)}+\delta^{(3)}+\delta^{(4)}.]
Near-closure constraints (Q^{[4]}\approx I+\epsilon\Delta) become design constraints on primitive parameters, enabling controlled cycles and systematic drift shaping.
6.2.3 Multi-scale versions
6.2.3.1 Scale-indexed primitives ((T_k^{(\epsilon)}))
Let (\epsilon\in\mathcal{E}) index resolution (grid scale, wavelet scale, spectral cutoff). A scale-indexed primitive is a family[T_k^{(\epsilon)}:\mathcal{D}(T_k^{(\epsilon)})\subseteq H^4 \to H^4,]such that the action is consistent with coarse-graining operators (S_\epsilon) and refinement embeddings where defined.
For contraction, scale-indexing may mean threshold schedules (\tau(\epsilon)) or retention budgets (K(\epsilon)). For extension, scale-indexing may mean continuation strength or PDE regularization at scale. For rotation, scale-indexing may mean band-limited unitary actions. For manifestation, scale-indexing may mean quantization depth or render resolution.
6.2.3.2 Cross-scale coupling rules
Cross-scale coupling requires that primitive actions commute with coarse-graining up to certified error:[S_\epsilon(T_k^{(\epsilon')}(H)) \approx T_k^{(\epsilon)}(S_\epsilon(H)),]where (\epsilon) and (\epsilon') are related by the resolution ladder, and “(\approx)” is an equality within a declared envelope in a declared norm.
A deterministic coupling certificate must specify:
the commutation defect functional,
bounds under truncation/noise,
test sets on which the defect is certified.
6.2.3.3 Scale-consistent compositions
A multi-scale composition is scale-consistent if it preserves the hierarchy of invariants (\mathrm{Inv}^{(\epsilon)}) across scales within allocated budgets:[|\mathrm{Inv}^{(\epsilon)}(\mathsf{Prog}(H))-\mathrm{Inv}^{(\epsilon)}(H)|\preceq \delta^{(\epsilon)}.]Scale-consistency requires budget allocation across scales and deterministic reporting of which scales govern accept/reject decisions.
6.2.3.4 Adaptive scale scheduling
Adaptive scheduling selects (\epsilon_n) based on deterministic monitoring of invariant drift and reconstruction error:[(H_{n+1},\epsilon_{n+1}) := \mathsf{Step}(H_n,\epsilon_n,\mathrm{Inv}(H_n),\varepsilon(\cdot;H_n)).]Scheduling is admissible only if:
the policy is deterministic,
scale changes are bounded (no unbounded oscillation),
each step remains within corridor budgets,
all scheduling decisions are logged with hashes.
6.2.4 Control and optimization
6.2.4.1 Fit primitives to constraints/invariants
Given a target constraint/invariant objective (\mathcal{J}) (e.g., desired invariant changes, reconstruction profile, compaction profile), primitive parameters are fitted by solving:[\min_{\theta}\ \mathcal{J}(\mathsf{Prog}\theta(H_0))\quad\text{s.t.}\quad\mathsf{Prog}\theta\ \text{admissible on }\mathcal{U}{\mathrm{fit}}.]Here (\mathsf{Prog}\theta) is a program defined by primitive parameters (\theta=(\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3,\theta_4,\dots)), and admissibility is enforced by hard constraints or by barrier penalties encoding corridor violations.
A canonical admissibility barrier is:[\mathcal{B}(\theta):=\sum_{H\in\mathcal{U}{\mathrm{fit}}}\sum{j=1}^m\max\left{0,\ \frac{|\mathrm{Inv}j(\mathsf{Prog}\theta(H))-\mathrm{Inv}_j(H)|}{\delta_j}-1\right}^2,]and the constrained problem is implemented as minimizing (\mathcal{J}+\lambda \mathcal{B}) with deterministic (\lambda) schedules and hard abort if violations persist.
6.2.4.2 Parameter estimation methods
Parameter estimation is conducted by one of the following certified methods:
adjoint gradient method (differentiable programs): compute exact gradients via declared adjoint kernels for each primitive, verify by deterministic finite-difference spot checks on gold states;
proximal/alternating minimization: particularly for contraction and extension defined by convex objectives, alternating between coefficient shrinkage (contraction) and feasibility projection;
closed-form parameter solves: e.g., for rotation group elements that align phases or match invariant signatures, compute (g) via deterministic alignment rules;
grid/beam search over program structure: for discrete gate programs, search over primitive sequences under a cost bound, pruning by admissibility checks and heuristics that are deterministic and declared.
All methods must output:
parameter tuple (\theta) in canonical serialization,
admissibility certificates,
objective value and validation metrics.
6.2.4.3 Optimal primitive programs
An optimal primitive program is defined as a Pareto-optimal solution under multi-objective cost:[\mathcal{C}(\mathsf{Prog}) := \big(\mathrm{time}(\mathsf{Prog}),\ \mathrm{memory}(\mathsf{Prog}),\ \mathrm{drift}(\mathsf{Prog}),\ \mathrm{approx}(\mathsf{Prog})\big),]with a deterministic ordering for tie-breaking. The optimization goal is to identify programs on the Pareto frontier subject to admissibility constraints.
A program is admitted as Pareto-optimal only if:
each objective is computed deterministically with declared measurement conventions,
admissibility holds on a declared validation set,
the frontier is reported with program IDs and certificates.
6.2.4.4 Robust control under noise
Robust control requires parameter choices stable under declared noise and truncation families. Let (\mathcal{N}) denote a noise model and (H^\xi) denote noisy input. Robust admissibility requires:[\mathbb{P}{\xi\sim\mathcal{N}}\left[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(\mathsf{Prog}(H^\xi))-\mathrm{Inv}(H^\xi)\big|\preceq \delta\right]\ge 1-\alpha,]for declared risk (\alpha), or the worst-case version:[\sup{\xi\in\Xi}\big|\mathrm{Inv}(\mathsf{Prog}(H^\xi))-\mathrm{Inv}(H^\xi)\big|\preceq \delta,]for a deterministic uncertainty set (\Xi).
Implementation uses:
worst-case envelopes for quantization and truncation errors,
noise-aware thresholds (contraction) and regularizers (extension),
conservative unitarity tolerances (rotation),
quantization budgeting (manifestation).
6.3 Cloud — Admissibility Corridors for Each Primitive
6.3.1 Contraction corridor
6.3.1.1 Max allowable energy compaction
Define a compaction measure (\mathsf{Comp}) (e.g., (\mathsf{Comp}K)) and a maximum compaction increment per contraction step (\Gamma{\mathrm{comp}}) to prevent pathological collapse:[\mathsf{Comp}_K(T_1(H))-\mathsf{Comp}K(H)\le \Gamma{\mathrm{comp}}.]This bound prevents contraction from producing degenerate states that trivially satisfy compaction objectives but violate reconstruction or structural invariants.
6.3.1.2 Information loss bounds
Let (I_\epsilon(H)) denote scale-dependent information measures (entropy families, mutual information, spectral entropy). Contraction must satisfy directional information budgets:[I_\epsilon(T_1(H)) \ge I_\epsilon(H) - \delta^{I}{1}(\epsilon),]for declared (\delta^{I}{1}(\epsilon)). If the intended contraction is information-reducing, the corridor must specify a lower bound and an upper bound on reduction to avoid catastrophic loss.
A canonical information-loss certificate includes:
pre/post invariant vectors (\mathrm{Inv}(H)), (\mathrm{Inv}(T_1(H))),
scale-indexed (I_{\epsilon_k}) values,
normalized losses (\frac{I_{\epsilon_k}(H)-I_{\epsilon_k}(T_1(H))}{\delta^{I}_{1}(\epsilon_k)}).
6.3.1.3 Reconstruction preservation bounds
Let (R_A) be reconstruction operators and (\varepsilon(A;H)) the reconstruction error envelope for the observation set (A). Contraction must preserve reconstructibility:[\varepsilon(A;T_1(H))\le \varepsilon(A;H) + \delta^{\mathrm{rec}}{1}(A),]for declared bounds. When contraction is used as preconditioning for reconstruction, stricter bounds may require contraction to improve reconstruction conditioning:[\kappa{\mathrm{rec}}(T_1(H))\le \kappa_{\mathrm{rec}}(H),]where (\kappa_{\mathrm{rec}}) is a declared reconstruction conditioning diagnostic.
6.3.1.4 Failure modes and detectors
Canonical contraction failure modes:
oversparsification: excessive coefficient elimination leading to irrecoverable structural loss, detected by violation of information-loss or reconstruction-preservation bounds;
constraint damage: contraction pushes state out of feasibility, detected by (|C(T_1(H))|>\tau_1);
aliasing injection: contraction in coefficient domain introduces ringing/aliasing in physical domain, detected by high-frequency energy growth or scale-invariant distortions;
gauge instability: contraction changes gauge-sensitive degrees in a way that destabilizes canonicalization, detected by discontinuous jumps in canonical representatives beyond tolerance.
Detectors must output a deterministic diagnostic vector and classify failure mode by a deterministic rule set.
6.3.2 Extension corridor
6.3.2.1 Stability of continuation
Extension must be stable with respect to perturbations in observed data. Let (d_{\mathrm{obs}}) be a metric on observation space. A Lipschitz stability corridor is:[|T_2(H)-T_2(\widetilde{H})|\le L_{\mathrm{ext}},|\mathsf{Res}A(H)-\mathsf{Res}A(\widetilde{H})|{\mathrm{obs}}]for declared (L{\mathrm{ext}}), on a declared admissible set. If continuation is ill-posed, the corridor must specify regularization strength sufficient to keep (L_{\mathrm{ext}}) below a threshold.
6.3.2.2 Artifact prevention bounds
Artifacts include spurious oscillations, boundary discontinuities, and hallucinated structures inconsistent with invariants. Define an artifact functional (\mathsf{Art}(H)) (e.g., total variation excess, high-band energy excess, boundary trace mismatch). Extension must satisfy:[\mathsf{Art}(T_2(H))\le \mathsf{Art}(H)+\delta^{\mathrm{art}}_2.]
6.3.2.3 Reconstruction consistency bounds
Extension must preserve consistency between restriction and reconstruction:[\mathsf{Res}_A(T_2(H))=\mathsf{Res}_A(H)]for exact continuation, or[|\mathsf{Res}_A(T_2(H))-\mathsf{Res}A(H)|{\mathrm{obs}}\le \eta_A]when numerical solvers introduce residual error. Additionally, extension must not worsen reconstruction envelopes for required observation masks.
6.3.2.4 Failure modes and detectors
Canonical extension failure modes:
blow-up continuation: continuation unstable, detected by norm growth beyond corridor or conditioning blow-up;
boundary ringing: discontinuities at (A)-boundary, detected by trace mismatch norms;
phantom structure: extension introduces features inconsistent with invariant suite, detected by invariant drift beyond budgets at specific scales/regions;
constraint mismatch: extension violates global constraints, detected by (|C(T_2(H))|>\tau_2).
6.3.3 Rotation corridor
6.3.3.1 Unitarity/orthogonality tolerance
Rotation must preserve energy and inner products up to tolerance when implemented numerically. Define unitarity defect:[\Delta_{\mathrm{uni}}(T_3):=\sup_{|H|=1}\big||T_3(H)|^2-1\big|.]The corridor requires:[\Delta_{\mathrm{uni}}(T_3)\le \tau_{\mathrm{uni}}.]In discrete settings, (\Delta_{\mathrm{uni}}) is evaluated on a fixed deterministic test set spanning representative modes.
6.3.3.2 Phase wrapping and aliasing bounds
Rotation in frequency/phase space must avoid aliasing under discretization. Define phase wrap defect (\mathsf{Wrap}(H)) and aliasing defect (\mathsf{Alias}(H)) (e.g., energy leakage beyond bandlimit). The corridor requires:[\mathsf{Alias}(T_3(H))\le \mathsf{Alias}(H)+\delta^{\mathrm{alias}}3,]and phase wrapping constraints in canonical phase conventions:[|\mathrm{Phase}(T_3(H))-\mathrm{Phase}(H)-\varphi|{\mathrm{mod}\ 2\pi}\le \tau_{\mathrm{phase}}.]
6.3.3.3 Symmetry preservation checks
Rotation must preserve declared symmetry signatures (e.g., topological invariants, symmetry fingerprints). Let (\mathrm{Inv}{\mathrm{sym}}\subseteq \mathrm{Inv}) denote symmetry invariants. Corridor requires:[|\mathrm{Inv}{\mathrm{sym}}(T_3(H))-\mathrm{Inv}_{\mathrm{sym}}(H)|\preceq \delta^{\mathrm{sym}}_3.]If rotation is itself a symmetry action, (\delta^{\mathrm{sym}}_3) is typically zero (exact), with numerical tolerances specified.
6.3.3.4 Failure modes and detectors
Canonical rotation failure modes:
non-unitary drift: energy leakage due to numerical instability, detected by (\Delta_{\mathrm{uni}}) exceeding threshold;
aliasing amplification: rotation moves energy across band limits, detected by leakage metrics;
symmetry breaking: invariants not preserved, detected by (\mathrm{Inv}_{\mathrm{sym}}) drift;
constraint breakage: rotation violates constraints, detected by (|C(T_3(H))|>\tau_3) when not exactly constraint-preserving.
6.3.4 Manifestation corridor
6.3.4.1 Quantization error budgets
Manifestation via quantization must satisfy:[|T_4(H)-H|\le \delta^{\mathrm{q}}_4,]with possible scale-dependent budgets (\delta^{\mathrm{q}}_4(\epsilon)). If manifestation is intended to be lossy (explicit rendering), the corridor must specify which invariants may change and by how much.
A canonical quantization certificate includes:
bit-depth and rounding mode,
observed quantization residual norms in declared metrics,
invariant drift attributable to quantization (by deterministic difference decomposition).
6.3.4.2 Rendering stability budgets
Rendering operators must be stable under perturbations:[|T_4(H)-T_4(\widetilde{H})|\le L_{\mathrm{rend}}|H-\widetilde{H}|,]with declared (L_{\mathrm{rend}}). If manifestation projects to a nonconvex explicit manifold (\mathcal{E}), the corridor must include conditions under which the projection is single-valued (or a deterministic selection rule) and stable.
6.3.4.3 Measurement disturbance budgets
If manifestation includes measurement modeling, the measurement disturbance must be bounded. Define disturbance as the deviation between pre- and post-manifestation canonical representatives:[d_{\mathcal{G}}(\mathrm{Can}(T_4(H)),\mathrm{Can}(H))\le \delta^{\mathrm{dist}}4,]in a gauge-invariant metric (d{\mathcal{G}}). If manifestation is intended to alter the state, the corridor must specify allowable disturbance bands.
6.3.4.4 Failure modes and detectors
Canonical manifestation failure modes:
quantization collapse: overly coarse quantization destroys essential structure, detected by invariant drift and reconstruction failures;
render instability: small perturbations cause large output changes, detected by empirical Lipschitz checks on gold perturbations;
constraint violation: manifestation produces non-feasible explicit forms, detected by constraint residual;
non-deterministic tie selection: projection to explicit forms yields nondeterminism, detected by hash instability across deterministic reruns (treated as hard failure).
6.4 Fractal — Implementations and Test Suites for ((T_1)–(T_4))
6.4.1 Reference algorithms
6.4.1.1 Contraction algorithms (sparse/wavelet)
Algorithm 6.1 (Projected soft-threshold contraction).Input: (H\in H^{(N,P)}), basis (\mathcal{B}), threshold (\lambda), projection/repair (\mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}), canonicalizer (\mathrm{Can}).Output: (H' = T_1(H)).
(a\leftarrow \mathcal{A}_{\mathcal{B}}(H)).
(a'\leftarrow \mathcal{T}^{\mathrm{soft}}_{\lambda}(a)) (deterministic arithmetic, fixed rounding policy).
(\widetilde{H}\leftarrow \mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{B}}(a')).
(Z\leftarrow \mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}(\widetilde{H})) (deterministic solver).
(H'\leftarrow \mathrm{Can}(Z)).
Emit certificate: retained energy fraction, constraint residual pre/post, invariant drift pre/post.
Complexity: (O(\mathrm{cost}(\mathcal{A}{\mathcal{B}})+\mathrm{cost}(\mathcal{S}{\mathcal{B}})+\mathrm{cost}(\mathsf{Proj}))), with explicit constants required for the chosen basis and solver.
6.4.1.2 Extension algorithms (inpainting/continuation)
Algorithm 6.2 (Constraint-aware variational extension).Input: (H\in H^{(N,P)}), mask (A), regularizer (\mathcal{R}), constraint penalty weight (\alpha), solver (\mathsf{Solve}), projection (\mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}), canonicalizer (\mathrm{Can}).Output: (H'=T_2(H)).
(y\leftarrow \mathsf{Res}_A(H)).
Define objective:[\mathcal{J}(Z):=\mathcal{R}(Z)+\alpha|C_{N,P}(Z)|^2\quad \text{s.t.}\quad \mathsf{Res}_A(Z)=y.]
(\widetilde{H}\leftarrow \mathsf{Solve}(\mathcal{J},\mathsf{Res}_A(Z)=y)) with deterministic initialization and stopping.
(Z\leftarrow \mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}(\widetilde{H})) if needed.
(H'\leftarrow \mathrm{Can}(Z)).
Emit certificate: data-consistency residual, constraint residual, artifact measures, invariant drift, reconstruction envelope.
6.4.1.3 Rotation algorithms (FFT/unitary)
Algorithm 6.3 (Unitary rotation in coefficient space).Input: (H\in H^{(N,P)}), analysis map (\mathcal{A}{\mathcal{B}}), synthesis (\mathcal{S}{\mathcal{B}}), unitary matrix/operator (U) in coefficient space with canonical parameterization.Output: (H'=T_3(H)).
(a\leftarrow \mathcal{A}_{\mathcal{B}}(H)).
(a'\leftarrow U a).
(\widetilde{H}\leftarrow \mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{B}}(a')).
If constraint-preserving is not exact, apply (Z\leftarrow \mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}(\widetilde{H})); else (Z\leftarrow \widetilde{H}).
(H'\leftarrow \mathrm{Can}(Z)) if required.
Emit certificate: unitarity defect estimate, aliasing/leakage metrics, invariant drift.
6.4.1.4 Manifestation algorithms (projection/render)
Algorithm 6.4 (Quantized manifestation to explicit form).Input: (H\in H^{(N,P)}), discretizer (\Pi), quantizer (Q_{\mathrm{quant}}), explicit form projector (\mathsf{Proj}{\mathcal{E}}), feasibility projection (\mathsf{Proj}{H^4}), canonicalizer (\mathrm{Can}).Output: (H'=T_4(H)).
(h\leftarrow \Pi(H)).
(h_q\leftarrow Q_{\mathrm{quant}}(h)) with fixed rounding/saturation.
(\widetilde{H}\leftarrow \mathsf{Proj}_{\mathcal{E}}(h_q)) interpreted in (H^{(N,P)}) then embedded to (H^4) if required.
(Z\leftarrow \mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}(\widetilde{H})).
(H'\leftarrow \mathrm{Can}(Z)).
Emit certificate: quantization residual, explicit-form distance, invariant drift, measurement disturbance metrics.
6.4.2 Verification per primitive
6.4.2.1 Constraint residual propagation tests
For each primitive (T_k), define residual propagation:[r_{\mathrm{in}}:=|C(H)|,\qquad r_{\mathrm{out}}:=|C(T_k(H))|.]A constraint propagation test suite must include:
exact-feasibility tests on states with (r_{\mathrm{in}}=0),
corridor-feasibility tests on states with (0<r_{\mathrm{in}}\le \tau_{\mathrm{in}}),
worst-case tests on adversarial perturbations (H+\Delta H) where (\Delta H) is chosen from a deterministic uncertainty set.
Pass conditions are (,r_{\mathrm{out}}\le \tau_k) and bounded amplification (r_{\mathrm{out}}\le \alpha_k r_{\mathrm{in}}+\beta_k) with declared constants when applicable.
6.4.2.2 Invariant drift tests
Invariant drift for primitive (T_k) is:[\Delta\mathrm{Inv}_k(H):=\mathrm{Inv}(T_k(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H).]The test suite must include:
unit tests verifying exact invariants for rotation (and any other exact invariants),
scale-indexed drift checks for information invariants,
regression checks comparing to gold baseline drifts.
Pass conditions: (|\Delta\mathrm{Inv}_k(H)|\preceq \delta^{(k)}) for each required invariant component, with deterministic tolerances.
6.4.2.3 Reconstruction tests
Where reconstruction operators (R_A) are required, the suite must test:[|R_A((T_k(H))|A) - T_k(H)|\le \varepsilon{\max}(A),]and must compare reconstruction envelopes before/after primitive application. For contraction and manifestation, reconstruction tests are primary safety checks.
6.4.2.4 Performance tests
Each primitive must be benchmarked under fixed deterministic workloads:
time per application,
memory footprint,
sensitivity to precision (float32/float64/mixed) under declared policies,
scaling with ((N,P)) and with basis choice.
Performance tests must be integrated into regression to detect unintended algorithmic complexity growth.
6.4.3 Program libraries
6.4.3.1 Primitive program catalogs
A catalog is a versioned collection of canonical primitive instances ({T_k(\theta)}) with:
unique IDs (hashes of canonical serialization),
admissibility certificates and domain conditions,
intended use tags (preconditioning, denoising, continuation, symmetry alignment, rendering),
reference performance metrics and validity scales.
Catalog entries must declare whether they are safe for composition under corridor budgeting and whether they preserve reconstruction compatibility.
6.4.3.2 Rewriting/normalization engines
A normalization engine applies the rewrite system (\mathsf{Rewrite}) to produce canonical program normal forms:[\mathsf{Prog}\ \Rightarrow\ \mathsf{NF}(\mathsf{Prog}).]The normalization engine must be deterministic, terminating (or bounded with deterministic fallback), and must emit rewrite certificates that preserve admissibility.
6.4.3.3 Composition optimizers
Composition optimization searches over program structures and parameters to meet target objectives while minimizing cost:[\min_{\mathsf{Prog}\in\mathcal{L}}\ \mathcal{C}(\mathsf{Prog})\quad\text{s.t.}\quad \mathsf{Prog}\ \text{admissible},\ \mathsf{Prog}\approx T_{\mathrm{target}}.]Optimizers must:
enforce admissibility as hard constraints or certified barriers,
be deterministic given a seed and fixed search ordering (or fully deterministic without seeds),
output Pareto frontiers with program IDs and certificates.
6.4.3.4 Deterministic packaging
Packaging rules require:
canonical serialization of program graphs,
complete dependency closure (basis IDs, solver IDs, precision policies),
stable hashing of programs and certificates,
reproducibility manifests sufficient to rerun and validate outputs on gold states.
6.4.4 Benchmark datasets
6.4.4.1 Synthetic hologram families
Synthetic families must span:
smooth states, oscillatory states, multi-scale fractal-like states,
states near constraint boundaries (high conditioning),
states with known invariant signatures across scales,
gauge-equivalent families for canonicalization stress.
Each synthetic family must include:
generator specification,
gold states at multiple resolutions,
invariant baselines and allowable drift envelopes per primitive.
6.4.4.2 Noise families
Noise families must include deterministic parameterized models:
additive noise (Gaussian-like surrogates with fixed seeds),
multiplicative noise,
phase noise,
structured adversarial perturbations aligned to nullspaces of observation operators.
Noise datasets must include exact reproducibility seeds and uncertainty set specifications for worst-case tests.
6.4.4.3 Mask/reconstruction families
Mask families define observation subsets (A) and sampling operators for reconstruction tests:
random masks with fixed seeds,
structured masks (blocks, rings, bands),
boundary-only masks for continuation stress.
Each mask family must include reconstruction operators (R_A) (or solver configurations), conditioning diagnostics, and envelope baselines.
6.4.4.4 Regression baselines
Regression baselines include:
gold outputs (T_k(H_j)) for canonical states (H_j),
gold invariant drift vectors,
gold constraint residual trajectories,
performance baselines.
All baselines must be versioned and tied to primitive IDs and policy IDs so that changes are traceable and deliberate.
Chapter 6 — Primitive Transformations ((T_1)–(T_4)): Contraction/Extension/Rotation/Manifestation
Chapter 7 — Encoding & Gate Algebras: Base-22, 231 Gates, Coordinate Independence
7.1 Square — Encoding as a Coordinate Chart on Operator Space
7.1.1 Base-((n)) numeral systems for operators
7.1.1.1 Define symbol set and mapping rules
Let (n\in\mathbb{N}) with (n\ge 2). Let (\Sigma_n={\sigma_0,\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_{n-1}}) be a fixed alphabet of (n) symbols equipped with a fixed digit map[\mathrm{dig}:\Sigma_n\to{0,1,\dots,n-1},\qquad \mathrm{dig}(\sigma_k)=k,]and inverse map (\mathrm{sym}:{0,\dots,n-1}\to\Sigma_n).
Define (\Sigma_n^\star) as the set of finite strings over (\Sigma_n). A base-(n) integer encoding is the injective map[\mathrm{EncInt}n:\mathbb{N}\to\Sigma_n^\star]defined by the standard positional representation: for (m\in\mathbb{N}), choose the unique length (L\ge 1) and digits (d_0,\dots,d{L-1}\in{0,\dots,n-1}) such that[m=\sum_{\ell=0}^{L-1} d_\ell n^\ell,\qquad d_{L-1}\neq 0 \ \text{unless } m=0,]and define[\mathrm{EncInt}n(m):=\mathrm{sym}(d{L-1}),\mathrm{sym}(d_{L-2})\cdots \mathrm{sym}(d_0).]Define the inverse decoding map (\mathrm{DecInt}n:\Sigma_n^\star\to\mathbb{N}\cup{\bot}) by[\mathrm{DecInt}n(s)=\begin{cases}\sum{\ell=0}^{L-1}\mathrm{dig}(s{L-\ell}),n^\ell, & s\in\Sigma_n^L,\ s_1\neq \sigma_0 \ \text{or } L=1,\\bot, & \text{otherwise},\end{cases}]where (s_k) denotes the (k)-th symbol of (s), and (\bot) denotes an invalid parse.
To encode signed integers, define a sign symbol (\sigma_{+},\sigma_{-}\in\Sigma_n) as two designated digits (fixed once and for all by policy). Define[\mathrm{EncZ}n(z)=\begin{cases}\sigma{+},\mathrm{EncInt}n(z), & z\ge 0,\\sigma{-},\mathrm{EncInt}_n(|z|), & z<0,\end{cases}]and decode correspondingly.
To encode bounded real parameters (\theta\in[\theta_{\min},\theta_{\max}]\subset\mathbb{R}) deterministically, fix a parameter quantization policy[\mathsf{Q}:=(L_\theta,\ \theta_{\min},\ \theta_{\max},\ \mathsf{round},\ \mathsf{clamp}),]where (L_\theta\in\mathbb{N}) is the fixed digit length, (\mathsf{round}) is a deterministic rounding rule, and (\mathsf{clamp}) is a deterministic saturation rule.
Let (M:=n^{L_\theta}-1). Define the quantized integer[q(\theta):=\mathsf{clamp}\Big(\mathsf{round}\Big(M\cdot\frac{\theta-\theta_{\min}}{\theta_{\max}-\theta_{\min}}\Big),\ 0,\ M\Big)\in{0,\dots,M}.]Define the fixed-width encoding[\mathrm{EncPar}{n,\mathsf{Q}}(\theta):=\mathrm{EncFixed}n(q(\theta);L\theta),]where (\mathrm{EncFixed}n(\cdot;L)) is base-(n) encoding padded with leading (\sigma_0) symbols to length (L). Decode by[\mathrm{DecPar}{n,\mathsf{Q}}(s)=\theta{\min}+(\theta_{\max}-\theta_{\min})\cdot\frac{\mathrm{DecFixed}n(s;L\theta)}{M},]with (\mathrm{DecFixed}n) rejecting strings not of length (L\theta).
A base-(n) operator encoding scheme is a tuple[\mathsf{EncScheme}_n := (\Sigma_n,\ \mathrm{dig},\ \mathcal{G},\ \mathsf{Grammar},\ \mathsf{Sem},\ \mathsf{Policy}),]where (\mathcal{G}) is a gate set, (\mathsf{Grammar}\subseteq \Sigma_n^\star) is a syntactic language of valid programs, (\mathsf{Sem}:\mathsf{Grammar}\to \mathrm{End}(H^4)) is the semantic decoding map, and (\mathsf{Policy}) fixes all fixed-width lengths, parameter quantizers, tie-breaking, and deterministic evaluation order.
The present chapter specializes to base-22 with a fixed alphabet (\Sigma_{22}) and a gate family indexed by unordered pairs ({i,j}\subseteq{1,\dots,22}), yielding ( \binom{22}{2}=231) elementary gates.
7.1.1.2 Indexing operators by symbols
Let (\mathcal{T}\subseteq \mathrm{End}(H^4)) denote a declared class of admissible transforms (operators) acting on holographic states. Let (\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{gate}}={g\alpha}{\alpha\in\mathcal{A}}\subseteq \mathcal{T}) denote a fixed gate family indexed by a finite index set (\mathcal{A}). A gate program is a finite sequence[\pi = (g{\alpha_1}(\theta_1),\dots,g_{\alpha_L}(\theta_L)),]with parameters (\theta_\ell) in gate-dependent parameter domains. Its semantic operator is[\mathsf{Sem}(\pi):=g_{\alpha_L}(\theta_L)\circ \cdots\circ g_{\alpha_1}(\theta_1),]with composition order fixed by policy.
To index gates by base-(n) symbols, one fixes an injective map from indices into digit strings:[\mathrm{EncIdx}n:\mathcal{A}\to \Sigma_n^{w{\mathrm{idx}}},]with fixed width (w_{\mathrm{idx}}). Gate parameters are encoded by (\mathrm{EncPar}{n,\mathsf{Q}\alpha}) for each gate type (or by a shared policy when uniform). A fixed-width instruction word has form[\mathrm{Instr}(g_\alpha(\theta)) := \mathrm{EncIdx}n(\alpha)\ \Vert\ \mathrm{EncPar}{n,\mathsf{Q}_\alpha}(\theta),]where (\Vert) denotes concatenation.
A program encoding is then the concatenation of instruction words preceded by a fixed-width header:[\mathrm{EncProg}n(\pi) := \mathrm{Hdr} \ \Vert\ \mathrm{Instr}(g{\alpha_1}(\theta_1)) \ \Vert\ \cdots \ \Vert\ \mathrm{Instr}(g_{\alpha_L}(\theta_L)),]where (\mathrm{Hdr}) encodes (at minimum) schema version, basis/representation identifiers, policy identifiers, and length (L). The header format is fixed by (\mathsf{Policy}) and must be self-sufficient to unambiguously parse the body.
Define the decoding map (\mathrm{DecProg}_n) by deterministic header parsing and instruction splitting into fixed-width blocks, rejecting any program not in (\mathsf{Grammar}). The decoded program semantics is[\mathsf{Sem}(\mathrm{DecProg}_n(s))\in\mathrm{End}(H^4),]and program validity requires, beyond syntactic validity, that all type checks and admissibility checks pass (see §7.3.3).
Encoding a general operator (T\in\mathcal{T}) requires a canonical decomposition strategy:[\mathsf{Decomp}:\mathcal{T}\rightharpoonup (\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{gate}}\text{-programs}),]which may be partial. A canonical encoding map is then[\mathrm{EncOp}_n(T) := \mathrm{EncProg}_n(\mathsf{NF}(\mathsf{Decomp}(T))),]where (\mathsf{NF}) is a deterministic normal-form reducer on programs (see §7.1.3.4, §7.2.1.4).
7.1.1.3 Encoding invariants (what must persist)
An encoding scheme is not merely a syntactic representation; it is required to preserve specified invariants of operator identity, admissibility, and semantics.
Let (\sim_{\mathrm{op}}) denote the operator equivalence relation induced by the framework (including gauge semantics, corridor tolerances, and canonicalization policy). An encoding scheme must specify the set of invariants that are preserved exactly and those preserved within certified tolerance.
(A) Structural invariants (exact).The following must be preserved exactly by encoding/decoding:
schema version and policy identifiers (including fixed-width lengths, rounding rules, evaluation order);
representation identifiers (basis IDs, chart IDs, discretization parameters where required);
program instruction sequence after normalization, as a sequence of gate IDs and quantized parameters;
determinism-critical metadata: tie-breaking rules, parenthesization rules, checkpoint policy.
(B) Typing invariants (exact).Each gate carries a typed domain/codomain declaration and admissibility policy. Encoding must preserve:
gate type signature (including required regularity class);
parameter domain and quantization policy;
declared constraint-compatibility mode (exact-preserving vs repair-required).
A decoded program that fails typing checks is invalid and must be rejected.
(C) Semantic invariants (exact or certified).Define the semantic operator of a decoded program (s) by[\mathsf{Sem}(s):=\mathsf{Sem}(\mathrm{DecProg}n(s)).]The encoding must satisfy:[\mathsf{Sem}(\mathrm{EncProg}n(\pi))=\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)]as an exact equality when gates are exact symbolic operators, and as a certified equality within an operator-norm envelope when gates are executed numerically. In numerical regimes, the scheme must provide a certified bound[|\mathsf{Sem}{\mathrm{num}}(s)-\mathsf{Sem}{\mathrm{ideal}}(s)|_{\mathrm{op}}\le \eta(s),]with (\eta(s)) computed deterministically from per-gate error envelopes and composition rules (§7.3.2).
(D) Admissibility invariants (corridor).Let (\mathrm{Inv}:H^4\to\mathbb{R}^m) be the invariant suite and (\delta) the corridor budget vector. Decoded programs must preserve the admissibility contract:[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(\mathsf{Sem}(s)(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)\big|\preceq \delta\quad\text{for all }H\in\mathcal{U}{\mathrm{cert}}(s),]for a declared certified domain (\mathcal{U}{\mathrm{cert}}(s)). The encoding must preserve the corridor policy ID and the certified domain descriptor.
(E) Canonical identity invariants (hash-stability).A canonical program identity hash (\mathrm{ID}(s)) is defined by hashing the canonical serialization of the decoded program and policy identifiers. Encoding must preserve (\mathrm{ID}) under decode–re-encode:[\mathrm{ID}(s)=\mathrm{ID}(\mathrm{EncProg}_n(\mathrm{DecProg}_n(s)))]for all valid program strings (s).
7.1.1.4 Decoding and invertibility conditions
A decoding map (\mathrm{DecProg}_n:\Sigma_n^\star\to \mathsf{Programs}\cup{\bot}) is valid only if it is:
total on grammar: defined for every syntactically valid string (s\in\mathsf{Grammar});
deterministic: identical outputs across environments under the same policy;
type-safe: rejects any string whose decoded gates violate typing requirements.
Let (\pi=\mathrm{DecProg}_n(s)) and (T=\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)). The program is semantically valid if:
every gate application is defined on the declared certified domain (domain stability);
the composed operator is admissible on its certified domain.
A program string (s) is invertible if the decoded operator (T) is invertible as a map on its declared domain and its inverse can be expressed within the same gate family or within a declared larger family.
