For a gate family in which each gate is invertible and the inverse is representable by parameter negation (as for exponential gates),[g_\alpha(\theta)^{-1}=g_\alpha(-\theta),]the inverse program is defined by reversal and inversion:[\pi^{-1}:=\big(g_{\alpha_1}(-\theta_1),\dots,g_{\alpha_L}(-\theta_L)\big)\ \text{in reverse order},]so that[\mathsf{Sem}(\pi^{-1})=\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)^{-1}]as an exact equality in the ideal semantics, and within a certified envelope in numerical semantics.
Invertibility is declared at the level of the encoding scheme as a property:[\mathsf{InvOK}(s)\in{\mathrm{true},\mathrm{false}}]computed deterministically from gate types, parameter domains, and admissibility constraints. If any gate is non-invertible (e.g., projection/quantization), (\mathsf{InvOK}(s)=\mathrm{false}) unless a pseudoinverse mode is explicitly declared (with a separate typing signature and certificate).
7.1.2 The 231 gate set as pairwise generators
7.1.2.1 Gate indexing ((g_{ij})) schema
Fix the index set[\mathcal{I}:={1,2,\dots,22}.]Define the unordered pair index set[\mathcal{A}:={(i,j)\in\mathcal{I}\times\mathcal{I}:\ 1\le i<j\le 22},]with (|\mathcal{A}|=\binom{22}{2}=231). The 231 gate family is indexed by (\mathcal{A}):[\mathcal{G}{231}:={g{ij}(\theta): (i,j)\in\mathcal{A},\ \theta\in\Theta_{ij}}.]
A canonical index encoding in base-22 uses two digits, one for (i) and one for (j). Fix a bijection[\mathrm{symIdx}:\mathcal{I}\to \Sigma_{22},\qquad i\mapsto \sigma_{i-1},]and define[\mathrm{EncIdx}{22}(i,j):=\mathrm{symIdx}(i)\ \Vert\ \mathrm{symIdx}(j)\in \Sigma{22}^2.]Decoding recovers ((i,j)) and rejects any pair not satisfying (i<j). Canonicalization of indices enforces sorting: any encoded instruction with indices ((j,i)) is invalid under the base policy, unless a separate policy explicitly allows ordered pairs and normalizes them.
7.1.2.2 Generator association ((g_{ij}=\exp(\theta E_{ij})))
Let (V) be a real or complex vector space of dimension (22) with a fixed orthonormal basis ({e_1,\dots,e_{22}}). Define the elementary skew-adjoint (real case: skew-symmetric) generators (E_{ij}\in\mathrm{End}(V)) for (i<j) by[E_{ij}e_i = e_j,\qquad E_{ij}e_j = -e_i,\qquad E_{ij}e_k = 0\ \text{for }k\notin{i,j}.]Equivalently, in the basis ({e_k}), (E_{ij}) is the matrix with entries ((E_{ij}){i,j}=-1), ((E{ij})_{j,i}=1), and zeros elsewhere.
Let (G_{22}) be the connected Lie group generated by these generators (real case: (G_{22}=\mathrm{SO}(22)); complex unitary representation case: a subgroup of (\mathrm{U}(22)) determined by the representation). Define the plane-rotation element[R_{ij}(\theta):=\exp(\theta E_{ij})\in G_{22}.]Fix a representation (action) of (G_{22}) on the holographic state space:[\rho: G_{22}\to \mathrm{Aut}(H^4),]where (\rho) is a group homomorphism into admissible automorphisms (or into admissible endomorphisms when restricted).
The gate semantics are defined by[g_{ij}(\theta) := \rho(R_{ij}(\theta)) = \rho(\exp(\theta E_{ij})).]The parameter domain (\Theta_{ij}\subset\mathbb{R}) is a declared bounded interval compatible with canonical angle wrapping. A canonical choice is (\Theta_{ij}=(-\pi,\pi]) with the identification (g_{ij}(\theta)\equiv g_{ij}(\theta+2\pi k)) for (k\in\mathbb{Z}) in exact group semantics, and with deterministic wrap-to-((-\pi,\pi]) in encoded semantics.
The gate family thus induces a coordinate chart near the identity in operator space by mapping small parameter vectors (\theta) to products of exponentials. Fix a deterministic ordering (\preceq) on (\mathcal{A}) and define the ordered exponential coordinate map[\Phi({\theta_{ij}}{(i,j)\in\mathcal{A}}):=\prod{(i,j)\in\mathcal{A}} g_{ij}(\theta_{ij})\quad\text{(product taken in the fixed order (\preceq)).}]On a sufficiently small neighborhood of (\theta=0), (\Phi) is locally surjective onto a neighborhood of the identity in the connected subgroup generated by ({E_{ij}}), and provides a local coordinate chart for admissible automorphisms in the image of (\rho).
7.1.2.3 Gate typing and domains
Each gate (g_{ij}(\theta)) is typed as an admissible transform on holographic states:[g_{ij}(\theta):\mathcal{D}_{ij}\subseteq H^4\to H^4.]The type includes:
domain stability: (\mathcal{D}{ij}) is a declared subset stable under the action:[H\in \mathcal{D}{ij}\ \Longrightarrow\ g_{ij}(\theta)(H)\in \mathcal{D}{ij},]for all (\theta\in\Theta{ij});
constraint preservation: for hard feasibility (C(H)=0),[H\in H^4\cap \mathcal{D}{ij}\ \Longrightarrow\ C(g{ij}(\theta)(H))=0,]or, for corridor feasibility, (|C(g_{ij}(\theta)(H))|\le \tau_{ij});
gauge compatibility: if pattern-level semantics are used, gates must be gauge-equivariant or followed by canonicalization. A gate is gauge-equivariant if[g\cdot g_{ij}(\theta)(H)=g_{ij}(\theta)(g\cdot H)\qquad(\forall g\in\mathcal{G}),]on the declared domain.
regularity typing: for a ladder ({X_s}),[g_{ij}(\theta):X_s\cap H^4\to X_s\cap H^4,]for the declared regularity level(s) (s).
In the unitary/orthogonal regime (where (\rho) is norm-preserving),[|g_{ij}(\theta)(H)|=|H|\quad\text{and}\quad \langle g_{ij}(\theta)H, g_{ij}(\theta)\widetilde{H}\rangle = \langle H,\widetilde{H}\rangle,]so each gate is a bounded isometry on (H^4).
7.1.2.4 Canonical gate parameter storage
For each gate family ((i,j)), fix a quantization policy (\mathsf{Q}{ij}) of the form in §7.1.1.1. A canonical choice for rotation-like gates is:[\theta{\min}=-\pi,\quad \theta_{\max}=\pi,\quad \text{wrap-to-}\Theta_{ij}=(-\pi,\pi],\quad L_\theta\in\mathbb{N}\ \text{fixed}.]Define the canonical stored parameter (\theta^{\mathrm{store}}) of (\theta) by[\theta^{\mathrm{store}}:=\mathrm{DecPar}{22,\mathsf{Q}{ij}}(\mathrm{EncPar}{22,\mathsf{Q}{ij}}(\mathrm{wrap}(\theta))).]The stored parameter is thus the unique quantization representative induced by policy; it is a function of (\theta) alone and is deterministic.
A gate instruction is canonical only if:
indices satisfy (i<j);
parameter digits equal (\mathrm{EncPar}{22,\mathsf{Q}{ij}}(\theta)) for a (\theta\in\Theta_{ij});
any equivalent parameter under periodicity is normalized via (\mathrm{wrap}).
The canonical storage scheme must specify:
rounding rule (ties);
saturation behavior at endpoints;
angle wrapping convention;
numerical precision and serialization encoding (fixed-width base-22 digits).
7.1.3 Gate graph structure
7.1.3.1 Vertices, edges, and path programs
Define the gate graph as the complete simple graph[\Gamma_{22}:=(V,E),\qquad V=\mathcal{I},\quad E={{i,j}:i<j}.]Each edge ({i,j}\in E) corresponds to a one-parameter family of gates (g_{ij}(\theta)). A path program is a finite sequence of oriented edges (with an orientation convention fixed by (i<j)) together with parameters:[\pi=\big((i_1,j_1,\theta_1),\dots,(i_L,j_L,\theta_L)\big),\quad (i_\ell,j_\ell)\in\mathcal{A}.]The path length is (L), and the semantic operator is[\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)=g_{i_L j_L}(\theta_L)\circ\cdots\circ g_{i_1 j_1}(\theta_1).]
A program can be viewed as a walk on (\Gamma_{22}) with labeled edges. This viewpoint supports:
connectivity-based generation theorems (§7.2.3.3, §7.2.3.4),
shortest-path compilation (§7.2.4.1),
loop diagnostics (holonomy and curvature; §7.2.2).
7.1.3.2 Path composition semantics
Let (\pi) and (\pi') be programs. Their concatenation is[\pi'\circ \pi := \big((i_1,j_1,\theta_1),\dots,(i_L,j_L,\theta_L),(i'1,j'1,\theta'1),\dots,(i'{L'},j'{L'},\theta'{L'})\big).]Semantics satisfy[\mathsf{Sem}(\pi'\circ\pi)=\mathsf{Sem}(\pi')\circ \mathsf{Sem}(\pi),]as an exact equality in ideal semantics and under deterministic evaluation in numerical semantics.
The identity program (\varnothing) (empty sequence) has semantics (\mathsf{Sem}(\varnothing)=I). The inverse program (when defined) is[\pi^{-1} = \big((i_L,j_L,-\theta_L),\dots,(i_1,j_1,-\theta_1)\big),]with semantics (\mathsf{Sem}(\pi^{-1})=\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)^{-1}) in the ideal unitary regime.
Define program equivalence (\pi\equiv \pi') if (\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)=\mathsf{Sem}(\pi')) as operators, or if they are equal in the pattern-level sense after canonicalization and within corridor tolerances (as specified by policy).
7.1.3.3 Minimal path problems
Fix a cost model assigning a nonnegative cost to each gate instance:[\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{gate}}(i,j,\theta)\in\mathbb{R}{\ge 0},]and define the program cost[\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{prog}}(\pi):=\sum{\ell=1}^L \mathcal{C}{\mathrm{gate}}(i\ell,j_\ell,\theta_\ell),]or a multi-objective vector cost (time, memory, corridor slack, numerical risk).
Given a target operator (T\in\mathcal{T}) and a metric (d_{\mathrm{op}}) on operator space (e.g., operator norm on a certified domain, or action-metric on a gold set), define the ((\eta,\delta))-feasible approximation class[\Pi(T;\eta,\delta):=\left{\pi:\ d_{\mathrm{op}}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi),T)\le \eta\ \text{and }\ \pi \text{ admissible under corridor }\delta\right}.]A minimal path program is a minimizer[\pi^\star\in\arg\min_{\pi\in \Pi(T;\eta,\delta)} \mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{prog}}(\pi),]with deterministic tie-breaking.
When the target operator is itself an element of the gate-generated group, one may set (\eta=0) and seek exact synthesis.
Minimal-path problems are computationally nontrivial because:
gate parameters are continuous (or high-resolution discrete after quantization);
equivalence relations create many syntactically distinct but semantically identical programs;
admissibility imposes constraints coupling parameter choices across the chain.
A compiler must therefore combine algebraic rewriting (to reduce search) with certified approximation methods (§7.2.4, §7.4.1).
7.1.3.4 Normal forms for paths
A program normal form is a deterministic map[\mathsf{NF}:{\text{valid programs}}\to{\text{valid programs}}]such that:
(\mathsf{Sem}(\mathsf{NF}(\pi))\equiv \mathsf{Sem}(\pi)) (semantic equivalence under the declared equivalence relation);
(\mathsf{NF}(\mathsf{NF}(\pi))=\mathsf{NF}(\pi)) (idempotence);
if (\pi\equiv \pi') and both are within the certified domain of (\mathsf{NF}), then (\mathsf{NF}(\pi)=\mathsf{NF}(\pi')) (canonical representative property), either exactly (confluent rewrite system) or within a declared syntactic canonicalization domain.
A canonical normal form for gate paths is built from the following deterministic reductions:
(A) Local fusion.Replace consecutive identical-index gates by one gate:[(i,j,\theta_1),(i,j,\theta_2)\ \Rightarrow\ (i,j,\mathrm{wrap}(\theta_1+\theta_2)),]with wrap-to-(\Theta_{ij}), and drop zero-angle gates.
(B) Local cancellation.Cancel inverse-adjacent gates:[(i,j,\theta),(i,j,-\theta)\ \Rightarrow\ \varnothing.]
(C) Commuting swaps.If a commutation criterion holds (e.g., disjoint index pairs under the Lie algebra commutation rule; §7.2.1.2), swap to enforce a fixed lexicographic order on gate indices.
(D) Canonical checkpoint insertion (policy-controlled).Insert canonicalization and feasibility repair checkpoints at specified boundaries if required by admissibility policy:[\cdots \Rightarrow \cdots \circ \mathrm{Can}\circ \mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}\circ \cdots,]treated as explicit typed operators if the gate set is extended to include them, or as an execution policy attached to program evaluation.
The normal form must be compatible with canonical encoding: program identity hashes are computed after applying (\mathsf{NF}) and after parameter quantization canonicalization.
7.1.4 Coordinate independence
7.1.4.1 Change-of-basis maps between encodings
Let (V) be the 22-dimensional coordinate fiber used to define (E_{ij}), with a basis (B={e_i}). Let (B'={e'_i}) be another basis related by an invertible linear map (U:V\to V) such that (e'_i=Ue_i). In orthonormal regimes, (U\in \mathrm{O}(22)) (real) or (U\in \mathrm{U}(22)) (complex).
The generator basis transforms by conjugation:[E'{ij} := U E{ij} U^{-1},]and hence[\exp(\theta E'{ij}) = U \exp(\theta E{ij}) U^{-1}.]
If the holographic action is represented by (\rho), and if (\rho) is compatible with basis change via a corresponding operator (\rho(U)) on (H^4), then gate semantics obey:[g'{ij}(\theta) = \rho(\exp(\theta E'{ij})) = \rho(U), g_{ij}(\theta), \rho(U)^{-1}.]
A coordinate change map on programs is a deterministic map[\mathsf{Trans}{B\to B'}:\ {\text{programs in basis }B}\to {\text{programs in basis }B'}]such that[\mathsf{Sem}{B'}(\mathsf{Trans}{B\to B'}(\pi))=\mathsf{Sem}{B}(\pi)]as operators on (H^4), or equivalently as operators on patterns after gauge canonicalization.
When (U) is itself representable by gate programs (or by a declared basis-change operator in the extended IR), (\mathsf{Trans}{B\to B'}) is computable by conjugation compilation:[\mathsf{Trans}{B\to B'}(\pi) := \pi_U^{-1}\ \circ\ \pi\ \circ\ \pi_U,]where (\mathsf{Sem}(\pi_U)=\rho(U)).
7.1.4.2 Invariant preservation under re-encoding
Let (\mathrm{Inv}:H^4\to\mathbb{R}^m) be the invariant suite used for admissibility. Coordinate independence requires that invariants are representation-independent in the sense that if a basis change corresponds to a symmetry action on (H^4), then[\mathrm{Inv}(\rho(U)H)=\mathrm{Inv}(H)\quad\text{for all }H\in\mathcal{U},]for all allowed (U) in the coordinate-change class (or within corridor tolerances when numerical approximations intervene).
For program semantics, if (\mathsf{Trans}{B\to B'}) preserves the operator, then for any admissible input (H),[\mathrm{Inv}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)(H))=\mathrm{Inv}(\mathsf{Sem}(\mathsf{Trans}{B\to B'}(\pi))(H)).]If program execution includes canonicalization and feasibility projection, invariants are evaluated after these operations in a policy-consistent manner; the coordinate-change map must preserve these policies exactly.
7.1.4.3 Equivalence of gate bases
Let (\mathfrak{g}\subseteq\mathrm{End}(V)) be the Lie algebra generated by ({E_{ij}}). In the standard construction, (\mathfrak{g}\cong \mathfrak{so}(22)) (real) or a unitary-representation counterpart.
Two gate bases ({E_{ij}}) and ({\widetilde{E}{ij}}) are equivalent if there exists an invertible (U) such that (\widetilde{E}{ij}=UE_{ij}U^{-1}) for all (i<j). Equivalent bases generate the same Lie algebra and thus the same connected Lie group (up to representation).
At the level of holographic state space, two gate bases are operator-equivalent if their induced representations on (H^4) generate the same subgroup of admissible automorphisms:[\langle \rho(\exp(\theta E_{ij}))\rangle = \langle \rho(\exp(\theta \widetilde{E}_{ij}))\rangle,]with equality understood as equality of generated subgroups in (\mathrm{Aut}(H^4)) under the declared topology (exact or closure).
7.1.4.4 Portability theorems
Theorem 7.1 (Portability under representable basis changes).Assume:
(U) is an allowed coordinate change with (\rho(U)\in\mathrm{Aut}(H^4));
(\rho(U)) is representable by a program (\pi_U) in the gate IR (or by a typed basis-change primitive);
admissibility corridors and invariant evaluation are (U)-invariant:[\mathrm{Inv}(\rho(U)H)=\mathrm{Inv}(H),\quad C(\rho(U)H)=0\iff C(H)=0.]Then for any gate program (\pi) admissible on (\mathcal{U}), the transformed program[\pi' := \pi_U^{-1}\circ \pi \circ \pi_U]is admissible on (\rho(U)\mathcal{U}), and satisfies[\mathsf{Sem}(\pi')=\rho(U)^{-1}\circ \mathsf{Sem}(\pi)\circ \rho(U).]In particular, (\pi) and (\pi') implement the same pattern-level action on (\mathcal{PATT}=H^4/\mathcal{G}) whenever (\rho(U)) is gauge-compatible.
Theorem 7.2 (Encoding portability as atlas transition).Let (\mathsf{EncScheme}^{(B)}) and (\mathsf{EncScheme}^{(B')}) be two encoding schemes using equivalent gate bases (B,B'). Assume each scheme provides a canonical decomposition map (\mathsf{Decomp}^{(B)}), (\mathsf{Decomp}^{(B')}) and a normal form map (\mathsf{NF}) that is semantics-preserving. Then there exists a deterministic transition map (\mathsf{ReEnc}{B\to B'}) such that for all operators (T) in the intersection of the decomposition domains,[\mathrm{DecOp}{B'}(\mathsf{ReEnc}{B\to B'}(\mathrm{EncOp}{B}(T))) \equiv T,]and admissibility certificates are preserved up to the declared corridor mapping between policy IDs.
7.2 Flower — Gate Composition, Interference, and Algebra
7.2.1 Gate composition algebra
7.2.1.1 Associativity and parenthesization
Let (\mathcal{G}{231}) be the gate family ({g{ij}(\theta)}) and let (G:=\langle \mathcal{G}_{231}\rangle) be the subgroup of (\mathrm{Aut}(H^4)) generated by these gates (in the ideal semantics). Composition in (\mathrm{Aut}(H^4)) is associative:[(A\circ B)\circ C = A\circ (B\circ C),]so program semantics are well-defined independent of parenthesization in exact operator algebra.
In numerical execution, finite precision can make evaluation order observable through rounding. A deterministic policy must therefore fix:
a canonical evaluation order for a program of length (L) (left-fold or right-fold);
checkpoint insertion points for canonicalization and feasibility projection if required;
deterministic accumulation order for any reductions within gate kernels.
The operator algebra of programs is thus defined as a semantics-with-policy map:[\mathsf{Sem}_{\mathsf{Policy}}:\mathsf{Programs}\to \mathrm{End}(H^4),]and all equivalence claims are made relative to (\mathsf{Policy}).
7.2.1.2 Non-commutativity and commutators
Define the group commutator of two invertible operators (A,B) by[[A,B]_{\mathrm{G}} := A\circ B\circ A^{-1}\circ B^{-1}.]For gate families arising from exponentials (A=\exp(X)), (B=\exp(Y)) in a Lie group, the group commutator measures non-commutativity and is close to identity when ([X,Y]) is small.
At the Lie algebra level, define the Lie commutator[[X,Y] := X\circ Y - Y\circ X.]For the generators (E_{ij}) on (V), commutators satisfy explicit structure relations. In the standard (\mathfrak{so}(22)) basis,[[E_{ij},E_{kl}]=\begin{cases}0, & {i,j}\cap{k,l}=\varnothing,\E_{il}, & j=k,\ i<l,\ \text{up to sign determined by index order},\E_{jk}, & i=l,\ \text{up to sign},\\text{(linear combination of one basis element)}, & \text{when exactly one index matches},\0, & (i,j)=(k,l).\end{cases}]Under the holographic representation (\rho), the commutator structure is transported to (H^4) via the derived representation of the Lie algebra.
Non-commutativity is thus localized: gates with disjoint index pairs commute in the ideal model, and gates sharing an index generally do not.
7.2.1.3 BCH expansions for composed gates
Let (X,Y) be elements of a Lie algebra of generators with a common domain on which commutators up to the required order are well-defined. For small (\epsilon),[\exp(\epsilon X)\exp(\epsilon Y)=\exp(\epsilon Z(\epsilon)),]where (Z(\epsilon)) is given by the Baker–Campbell–Hausdorff expansion:[Z(\epsilon)=X+Y + \frac{\epsilon}{2}[X,Y] + \frac{\epsilon^2}{12}\big([X,[X,Y]]+[Y,[Y,X]]\big)+O(\epsilon^3).]For gate composition, take (X=\theta E_{ij}), (Y=\phi E_{kl}). If ([E_{ij},E_{kl}]=0), then BCH collapses to (Z=X+Y) exactly, yielding exact commutativity.
BCH truncation becomes a certified approximation method when commutator norms are bounded on the declared domain. A certified truncation of order (p) must provide a remainder bound[\big|\exp(\epsilon X)\exp(\epsilon Y)-\exp(\epsilon Z_p(\epsilon))\big|_{\mathrm{op}}\le \eta_p(\epsilon;|X|,|Y|),]with (\eta_p) computed deterministically from commutator bounds.
7.2.1.4 Simplification and cancellation rules
A gate rewrite system (\mathsf{Rewrite}) is a set of deterministic rewrite rules preserving semantics (exactly or within certified tolerance). Canonical rules for the 231 gate family include:
(A) Same-edge fusion.[g_{ij}(\theta_1)\circ g_{ij}(\theta_2)\ \Rightarrow\ g_{ij}(\mathrm{wrap}(\theta_1+\theta_2)).]
(B) Inverse cancellation.[g_{ij}(\theta)\circ g_{ij}(-\theta)\ \Rightarrow\ I.]
(C) Disjoint commutation.If ({i,j}\cap{k,l}=\varnothing), then[g_{ij}(\theta)\circ g_{kl}(\phi)\ \Rightarrow\ g_{kl}(\phi)\circ g_{ij}(\theta),]allowing deterministic sorting by a fixed total order on indices.
(D) Conjugation transport (index-sharing).When index-sharing commutators are used for structured synthesis, conjugation identities produce derived gates. In the ideal Lie group, one has identities of the form[g_{1i}(\alpha)\circ g_{1j}(\beta)\circ g_{1i}(-\alpha) \approx g_{ij}(f(\alpha,\beta)),]with (f) determined by the group structure; in exact algebra, these are expressed via commutators and BCH in small-angle regimes. Such rules may be used only when accompanied by explicit approximation envelopes and admissibility checks.
Any rewrite system must specify:
applicability conditions (typing, parameter ranges);
exactness vs approximation status;
deterministic tie-breaking when multiple rewrites apply;
a termination strategy (confluent normal form or bounded best-effort reduction with certificate).
7.2.2 Interference as non-commutative geometry
7.2.2.1 Define commutator curvature measures
Interference is defined as the measurable effect of non-commutativity of gate sequences. A fundamental curvature proxy is the norm of the group commutator:[\kappa(A,B):=|[A,B]{\mathrm{G}}-I|{\mathrm{op}},]or, in pattern-level semantics, the gauge-invariant distance between ([A,B]_{\mathrm{G}}) and identity.
For gates (A=g_{ij}(\theta)), (B=g_{jk}(\phi)) sharing an index, (\kappa(A,B)) is generally nonzero and scales as (O(|\theta\phi|)) for small angles, reflecting the leading commutator term.
A Lie-algebra curvature proxy in small-parameter regimes is[\kappa_{\mathrm{Lie}}(X,Y):=|[X,Y]|_{\mathrm{op}},]where (X,Y) are the corresponding generators in the derived representation on (H^4). Certified curvature bounds require explicit operator-norm estimates on the declared domain.
7.2.2.2 Loop holonomy and cycle signatures
Let (\pi) be a closed loop program (a word of gates whose index sequence forms a cycle in (\Gamma_{22})). Define its holonomy as the semantic operator[\mathrm{Hol}(\pi):=\mathsf{Sem}(\pi).]In a commuting regime, holonomy of a closed loop would be identity. In noncommutative regimes, holonomy captures the net “rotation” induced by interference.
When (\mathrm{Hol}(\pi)) is sufficiently close to identity in operator norm, define the cycle signature as the principal logarithm:[\mathrm{Sig}(\pi):=\log(\mathrm{Hol}(\pi)),]in the Lie algebra of the represented group, with domain restricted to the neighborhood where (\log) is single-valued and stable. The signature is a noncommutative analogue of integrated curvature over the loop.
For numerical regimes, define a deterministic approximate signature using BCH-truncated logs, together with a certified remainder bound.
7.2.2.3 Resonant gate sequences
A program (\pi) is resonant (of order (m)) on a declared invariant subspace (W\subseteq H^4) if the restriction of (\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)) to (W) has spectral multipliers that are (m)-th roots of unity, or sufficiently close under a declared tolerance. Formally, in finite models:[\exists,\lambda\ \text{eigenvalue of } \mathsf{Sem}(\pi)|_W\ \text{such that}\ \lambda^m\approx 1.]In infinite-dimensional settings, resonance is defined via spectral measures or via finite-dimensional projected diagnostics.
Resonance is detected by:
repeated-application periodicity tests:[|\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)^m(H)-H|\le \tau_{\mathrm{res}}]on a gold set (H\in\mathcal{U}_{\mathrm{gold}});
spectral diagnostics on finite-dimensional reductions;
invariant oscillation signatures consistent with periodicity.
Resonant sequences are admissible only if resonance is compatible with corridor budgets; otherwise they are classified as failure modes (cf. Chapter 5 resonance-induced drift).
7.2.2.4 Anti-resonant sequences and damping
An anti-resonant (or damping) construction is a program transformation (\pi\mapsto \pi_{\mathrm{damp}}) designed to reduce holonomy and commutator curvature while preserving target action within tolerance.
Canonical anti-resonant mechanisms include:
symmetric composition: replace (\pi) by a palindromic sequence[\pi_{\mathrm{sym}} := \pi_{\mathrm{half}}\circ \pi_{\mathrm{half}}^{-1}]or by Strang-like symmetric rearrangements that cancel odd-order BCH terms, reducing interference.
commutator cancellation blocks: insert blocks whose holonomy approximates the inverse of an estimated commutator drift:[\pi_{\mathrm{damp}} := \pi \circ \pi_{\mathrm{corr}},\qquad \mathrm{Hol}(\pi_{\mathrm{corr}})\approx \mathrm{Hol}(\pi)^{-1}\ \text{on drift-dominant modes}.]
index-disjoint scheduling: reorder commuting gates to cluster disjoint pairs and reduce interaction density, lowering cumulative noncommutativity without changing semantics.
Anti-resonant transformations must be implemented as certified rewrites: they must produce an equivalence certificate showing that the new program either is semantically identical in exact algebra or differs by at most a declared approximation envelope in the chosen operator metric, and that corridor budgets remain satisfied.
7.2.3 Category system for generators (mother/double/simple)
7.2.3.1 Define generator partitions
Let (\mathcal{S}:={E_{ij}:i<j}) denote the full set of simple generators (the pairwise basis elements), (|\mathcal{S}|=231). Fix a connected spanning tree (\mathcal{T}\subseteq E(\Gamma_{22})) with (|\mathcal{T}|=21). Define the mother generator set as the generators corresponding to tree edges:[\mathcal{M}:={E_{ij}:{i,j}\in\mathcal{T}}.]Define the double generator set as the complement within the simple set:[\mathcal{D}:=\mathcal{S}\setminus \mathcal{M}.]Thus (\mathcal{S}) (simple generators) is partitioned into (\mathcal{M}) (mother) and (\mathcal{D}) (double), where “double” denotes generators that are obtained from multi-step interactions (commutators) of mother generators within the Lie algebra generated by (\mathcal{M}).
A canonical choice of (\mathcal{T}) is the star tree rooted at (1):[\mathcal{T}{\star} := {{1,k}:2\le k\le 22},]yielding[\mathcal{M}\star={E_{1k}:2\le k\le 22},\quad |\mathcal{M}\star|=21,]and (\mathcal{D}\star={E_{ij}:2\le i<j\le 22}), (|\mathcal{D}_\star|=210).
7.2.3.2 Interaction tensors
Define structure constants for the Lie algebra generated by simple generators by[[E_{ab},E_{cd}] = \sum_{(e,f)\in\mathcal{A}} f_{(ab)(cd)}^{(ef)} E_{ef},]where coefficients (f_{(ab)(cd)}^{(ef)}\in{-1,0,1}) in the standard (\mathfrak{so}(22)) basis (up to sign conventions induced by index ordering). The tensor (f) is the interaction tensor of the simple generator basis.
For the star mother set (\mathcal{M}\star), an explicit closure identity holds:[[E{1i},E_{1j}] = E_{ij}\qquad (2\le i<j\le 22),]with sign determined by the ordering convention defining (E_{ij}). This identity exhibits how pairwise interactions of mother generators generate the full double set.
Transporting to holographic operator space via (\rho) yields derived generators (X_{ij}) acting on (H^4) (formally (X_{ij}=d\rho(E_{ij})) when (d\rho) exists). Their commutators satisfy[[X_{ab},X_{cd}] = \sum_{(e,f)} f_{(ab)(cd)}^{(ef)} X_{ef}]on the common domain where the commutators are defined, providing the algebraic backbone for BCH-based compilation and interference diagnostics.
7.2.3.3 Closure properties
Let (\mathfrak{g}(\mathcal{E})) denote the Lie subalgebra generated by ({E_{ij}:{i,j}\in \mathcal{E}}) for some edge set (\mathcal{E}\subseteq E(\Gamma_{22})).
Theorem 7.3 (Graph connectivity ⇔ Lie closure).Let (\mathcal{E}\subseteq E(\Gamma_{22})). Then[\mathfrak{g}(\mathcal{E}) = \mathfrak{so}(22)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad(V,\mathcal{E})\ \text{is a connected graph.}]
A connectivity-based proof proceeds by showing that if ((V,\mathcal{E})) is connected, then for any two vertices (p\neq q) there exists a path (p=v_0!-!v_1!-\cdots-!v_m=q). Commutator chaining produces the generator (E_{pq}) from successive commutators of adjacent edge generators along the path, yielding all basis elements and hence full (\mathfrak{so}(22)). Conversely, if the graph is disconnected, the generators preserve a nontrivial decomposition of (V) into invariant coordinate subspaces, so the generated Lie algebra cannot be all of (\mathfrak{so}(22)).
At the gate level, the connectedness of (\mathcal{T}) implies that the subgroup generated by ({\exp(\theta E_{ij}):{i,j}\in\mathcal{T}}) is dense in (and in fact equals the connected group) (\mathrm{SO}(22)) under appropriate parameter ranges, supporting synthesis of any gate (g_{ij}(\theta)) from mother gates via commutator constructions.
7.2.3.4 Minimal generating sets
Restrict attention to generating sets drawn from the simple generator basis (\mathcal{S}).
Corollary 7.4 (Minimal mother size within simple generators).Any subset (\mathcal{M}\subseteq \mathcal{S}) that generates (\mathfrak{so}(22)) via Lie closure must contain at least (21) elements. Moreover, any spanning tree edge set yields such a generating set of size (21).
This follows because a graph on (22) vertices must have at least (21) edges to be connected, and by Theorem 7.3 connectivity is necessary and sufficient for Lie closure.
Within the broader operator algebra (allowing arbitrary linear combinations of generators), fewer generators may suffice, but within the constrained vocabulary of simple basis elements, (21) is the minimal cardinality for full generation.
This establishes a canonical hierarchy:
mother generators (tree edges): minimal connectivity basis;
double generators: generated by commutator closure of mothers;
simple generators: full pairwise basis of 231 atomic directions.
7.2.4 Gate-program optimization
7.2.4.1 Shortest path transforms under costs
Let (\mathcal{P}) be the set of admissible programs in the encoding grammar and let (\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{prog}}) be a scalar or vector cost. Let (T{\mathrm{target}}) be a target operator. Define an approximation constraint (d_{\mathrm{op}}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi),T_{\mathrm{target}})\le \eta), and admissibility constraints (corridors) (\pi\in \mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{adm}}(\delta)).
The optimization problem is:[\min_{\pi\in\mathcal{P}{\mathrm{adm}}(\delta)}\ \mathcal{C}{\mathrm{prog}}(\pi)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad d_{\mathrm{op}}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi),T_{\mathrm{target}})\le \eta.]
This can be framed as a shortest-path search on a Cayley graph or on an implicit state-space graph where nodes represent partial products (or equivalence classes under normal form) and edges correspond to appending one gate instruction. Heuristic search (A(^*)) is admissible when:
the heuristic lower bounds the remaining cost to reach the target;
equivalence-class canonicalization preserves optimality under the chosen pruning rules.
In continuous-parameter regimes, shortest-path search must be coupled with parameter solving. A canonical approach is:
restrict to a discrete parameter lattice induced by encoding quantization for synthesis search;
refine parameters via local continuous optimization under fixed structure, with admissibility re-certified after refinement.
7.2.4.2 Constraint-aware rewriting
Constraint-aware rewriting reduces program cost while preserving feasibility with respect to (C(H)=0) or corridor feasibility. A rewrite (\pi\Rightarrow \pi') is constraint-aware admissible only if it satisfies:[|C(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi')(H))|\le \tau\quad\text{whenever}\quad|C(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)(H))|\le \tau,]on the declared certified domain.
For purely constraint-preserving gates (e.g., unitary symmetry gates commuting with constraints), the rewrite system may ignore constraint checks beyond typing. For mixed systems where constraint repair is inserted, rewrites must preserve the repair schedule and must not reorder repair steps unless the equivalence is certified by explicit residual bounds.
Constraint-aware rewriting therefore operates on an IR that includes explicit barrier operators (projection/repair) or explicit stage boundaries with invariants and residual checks attached.
7.2.4.3 Invariant-aware rewriting
Invariant-aware rewriting preserves the invariant corridor budgets while simplifying programs. For a rewrite (\pi\Rightarrow \pi'), the admissibility requirement is[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi')(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)\big|\preceq \delta\quad\text{for all }H\in\mathcal{U}_{\mathrm{cert}},]together with the same bound for (\pi).
Invariant-aware rewrites include:
commutation sorting for disjoint gates (preserves invariants in exact symmetry regimes);
cancellation and fusion (exact);
symmetry-alignment rewrites that reduce invariant drift by transforming program order into symmetric compositions, certified by BCH remainder bounds;
replacement of a long subword by a shorter equivalent subword selected from a catalog of certified identities, each identity carrying a proof object (rewrite trace or verified equivalence on a basis of states).
The rewrite engine must maintain a running corridor budget ledger and reject any rewrite whose certified drift envelope exceeds remaining budget.
7.2.4.4 Multi-objective program search
Let the cost be vector-valued:[\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{prog}}(\pi)=\big(\mathrm{time}(\pi),\mathrm{memory}(\pi),\mathrm{risk}(\pi),\mathrm{drift}(\pi),\mathrm{length}(\pi)\big).]A program (\pi) Pareto-dominates (\pi') if (\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{prog}}(\pi)\preceq \mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{prog}}(\pi')) componentwise and strictly in at least one component.
A multi-objective compiler produces a Pareto set (\mathcal{F}\subseteq\mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{adm}}) such that no element of (\mathcal{F}) is dominated by another feasible program meeting the approximation constraint.
Search procedures must:
use deterministic pruning rules based on dominance and admissibility;
maintain certificates for each frontier element (admissibility + approximation error);
provide deterministic tie-breaking for output ordering.
When a scalar output is required, a deterministic scalarization policy is fixed:[\mathcal{C}\lambda(\pi):=\sum{k} \lambda_k,\mathcal{C}_k(\pi),]with fixed weights (\lambda_k\ge 0) and fixed normalization of each cost component.
7.3 Cloud — Limits, Ambiguity, and Verification of Encoded Programs
7.3.1 Ambiguity and degeneracy
7.3.1.1 Multiple programs for the same transform
Program ambiguity arises because the gate algebra satisfies relations beyond trivial cancellation and fusion, including commutations, conjugation identities, and higher-order relations in the group.
Define program equivalence (\pi\sim \pi') if (\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)=\mathsf{Sem}(\pi')) as operators (ideal equality), or if they are equivalent under pattern semantics and within corridor tolerances (numerical/corridor equality).
Ambiguity is intrinsic: for any nontrivial group, there exist infinitely many distinct words representing the identity (relations), hence infinitely many representations of the same operator by insertion of identity words.
To manage ambiguity, the framework requires a canonicalization pipeline:[\pi\ \mapsto\ \mathsf{NF}(\pi)\ \mapsto\ \mathrm{EncProg}_{22}(\mathsf{NF}(\pi)),]so that program identity is defined by its normal form.
7.3.1.2 Gauge-induced degeneracy
If the effective semantics are defined on patterns (\mathcal{PATT}=H^4/\mathcal{G}), then programs differing by gauge conjugations can be observationally equivalent:[\mathsf{Sem}(\pi') = G^{-1}\circ \mathsf{Sem}(\pi)\circ G,\qquad G\in \mathcal{G}{\mathrm{lift}},]where (\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{lift}}\subseteq \mathrm{Aut}(H^4)) is a represented gauge subgroup (or its canonicalization closure).
Gauge-induced degeneracy is resolved by:
canonicalizing states before and after program execution when evaluating equivalence;
defining program equivalence in the quotient sense:[\mathrm{Can}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)(H))=\mathrm{Can}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi')(H))]on a certified domain, with deterministic tolerances.
If a gate program includes explicit gauge-fixing steps, they become part of the program semantics and therefore part of the identity hash.
7.3.1.3 Identifying canonical programs
A canonical program is defined as the unique representative of its equivalence class under a declared rewrite-and-normalization system:[\pi^{\mathrm{can}} := \mathsf{NF}(\pi),]provided (\mathsf{NF}) is confluent on the relevant domain, or as a deterministic best-effort canonicalization when confluence is not guaranteed.
Canonical identification requires:
a fixed total order on gate indices and parameters (after quantization);
deterministic commutation sorting rules where allowed;
deterministic elimination of neutral and cancelling subsequences;
deterministic resolution of periodic parameter equivalences (angle wrapping).
The canonical program identity hash is computed from the canonical serialization of (\pi^{\mathrm{can}}) together with policy identifiers.
7.3.1.4 Certificates of equivalence
A program equivalence certificate is an object (\mathsf{CertEq}(\pi,\pi')) that validates (\pi\sim\pi') under the declared semantics. Two certificate types are required.
(A) Algebraic certificate (rewrite trace).A deterministic sequence of valid rewrite steps[\pi=\pi_0 \Rightarrow \pi_1 \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow \pi_K=\pi']where each step is an allowed rule with verifiable side conditions (typing, parameter range). This yields exact equivalence in the ideal algebra.
(B) Analytic certificate (metric bound).A deterministic bound of the form[d_{\mathrm{op}}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi),\mathsf{Sem}(\pi'))\le \eta,]together with a bound on invariant drift differences on the certified domain:[\sup_{H\in\mathcal{U}{\mathrm{cert}}}\big|\mathrm{Inv}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi')(H))\big|\preceq \delta{\mathrm{eq}}.]Analytic certificates are required when equivalence is approximate (e.g., BCH truncations, numerical approximations).
In all cases, certificates are deterministically serializable and hashable, and they bind program IDs, policy IDs, and certified domains.
7.3.2 Error propagation in gate chains
7.3.2.1 Numerical error accumulation bounds
Let (\widetilde{g}\ell) denote the numerically executed approximation of the ideal gate (g\ell), with operator error[|\widetilde{g}\ell - g\ell|{\mathrm{op}}\le \eta\ell.]Let the ideal program operator be[T := g_L\circ\cdots\circ g_1,]and the executed operator be[\widetilde{T} := \widetilde{g}L\circ\cdots\circ \widetilde{g}1.]Assume each ideal gate is bounded with (|g\ell|{\mathrm{op}}\le M_\ell), and each executed gate is bounded with (|\widetilde{g}\ell|{\mathrm{op}}\le \widetilde{M}\ell) (often (M\ell=\widetilde{M}\ell=1) in unitary regimes). Then a telescoping bound yields:[|\widetilde{T}-T|{\mathrm{op}}\le \sum_{\ell=1}^{L}\eta_\ell\prod_{k=\ell+1}^{L}\widetilde{M}k \prod{k=1}^{\ell-1} M_k.]In the unitary/isometric regime (M_k=\widetilde{M}k=1), the bound simplifies to[|\widetilde{T}-T|{\mathrm{op}}\le \sum_{\ell=1}^{L}\eta_\ell.]This bound is computable deterministically from per-gate kernel error envelopes and is used as the global semantic error certificate.
7.3.2.2 Stability of long programs
Long programs can be stable or unstable depending on whether gates amplify perturbations. In unitary regimes, operator norms remain bounded and error accumulation is at most linear (in worst case). In non-unitary regimes (if the IR is extended to include contraction/extension/manifestation gates), stability depends on condition numbers and Lipschitz constants.
Let (L_\ell) be a Lipschitz constant for gate action on states in the declared state metric:[d(g_\ell(H),g_\ell(\widetilde{H}))\le L_\ell, d(H,\widetilde{H}).]Then for the composed map,[d(T(H),T(\widetilde{H}))\le \left(\prod_{\ell=1}^{L} L_\ell\right)d(H,\widetilde{H}).]Stability of long programs requires explicit bounds or adaptive segmentation:
enforce (L_\ell\le 1+\epsilon_\ell) with (\sum \epsilon_\ell) bounded;
insert canonical checkpoints (projection/canonicalization) to prevent uncontrolled drift;
enforce maximum length (L) consistent with corridor budgets and error accumulation bounds.
7.3.2.3 Drift under truncation
When programs are executed on finite models (H^{(N,P)}), each gate (g_{ij}) is approximated by a discrete operator (g_{ij}^{(N,P)}). Define consistency error[\eta^{\mathrm{disc}}{ij}(N,P;H):=|\iota{N,P} g_{ij}^{(N,P)}(\Pi_{N,P}H) - g_{ij}(H)|.]For a program (\pi), truncation drift accumulates by composition:[|\iota_{N,P}\mathsf{Sem}^{(N,P)}(\pi)\Pi_{N,P}H - \mathsf{Sem}(\pi)H|\le \sum_{\ell=1}^L \eta^{\mathrm{disc}}{\alpha\ell}(N,P;H_\ell)\cdot \prod_{k>\ell} L_k,]where (H_\ell) are intermediate states along the ideal execution and (L_k) are Lipschitz constants of subsequent gates on the relevant domain.
A truncation policy must therefore define:
a resolution ladder ((N,P));
a certified truncation envelope per gate and per scale;
a maximum program length per resolution tier, ensuring truncation drift remains within corridor budgets.
7.3.2.4 Guard rails and cutoffs
A valid encoded program must carry guard rails enforcing safe execution and verifiable correctness. Mandatory guard rails include:
length cap: a maximum allowed length (L\le L_{\max}) per policy and per resolution tier;
checkpointing: periodic evaluation of constraint residuals (|C(H)|), invariant drift components, and reconstruction compatibility metrics at declared steps;
early abort: abort execution if any guard invariant violates corridor budgets, if numerical instabilities occur (NaNs, divergence), or if the accumulated error envelope exceeds a policy threshold;
cutoff strategy: if a program exceeds allowed risk, either reject or deterministically truncate with a certificate describing the induced approximation error and corridor impact (truncation is admissible only under an explicit approximation contract).
Guard rails are part of the semantics-with-policy and must be included in the program’s reproducibility manifest.
7.3.3 Admissibility in encoded form
7.3.3.1 Gate-level admissibility tests
Each decoded gate instruction ((i,j,\theta)) must pass:
parameter domain test: (\theta\in\Theta_{ij}) after decoding and canonical wrapping;
typing test: gate signature matches declared input/output regularity classes;
constraint-compatibility test: either exact constraint-preservation is known (by design), or a deterministic feasibility repair is attached by policy;
numerical stability test: kernel evaluation must be stable under the declared precision policy; unitary defect and leakage metrics must be below gate-level tolerances when applicable.
Gate-level admissibility produces a per-gate certificate record including gate ID, parameter value, quantization representation, and local diagnostics.
7.3.3.2 Chain-level corridor tests
A gate chain (\pi) must satisfy corridor constraints at the chain level:[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)\big|\preceq \delta\quad\text{for }H\in\mathcal{U}{\mathrm{cert}}(\pi).]Policies may require stricter stage-wise checks:[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(H{\ell})-\mathrm{Inv}(H_0)\big|\preceq \delta^{(\ell)}\quad\forall \ell,]where (H_\ell) are intermediate states.
Chain-level tests must incorporate:
accumulated numerical error envelopes (§7.3.2.1);
truncation drift envelopes (§7.3.2.3);
worst-case vs average-case corridor interpretations as declared.
7.3.3.3 Reconstruction compatibility checks
When the framework requires reconstructibility (Chapter 2), encoded programs must preserve reconstruction compatibility. Let (R_A) be reconstruction operators and (\varepsilon(A;H)) reconstruction error envelopes. The chain must satisfy:[\varepsilon(A;\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)(H))\le \varepsilon_{\max}(A)]for all required observation regimes (A) and for certified inputs (H).
If reconstruction checks are too costly to run at every step, the policy must specify:
at which checkpoints reconstruction checks are computed;
conservative bounds relating invariant drift proxies to reconstruction error, enabling early abort without full reconstruction.
7.3.3.4 Reject/repair protocol
A deterministic reject/repair protocol is defined as follows.
Reject on parse/type failure.Any invalid string parse, invalid index pair, invalid parameter decode, or typing failure yields immediate rejection.
Reject on infeasible gate.If a gate-level admissibility test fails and no repair mode is declared for that gate type, reject.
Repair by feasibility projection (if declared).If feasibility repair is permitted, apply the deterministic repair operator (\mathsf{Proj}_{H^4}) at declared repair points, then re-evaluate invariants and residuals.
Abort on corridor violation.If any corridor constraint is violated at a checkpoint, abort and emit a failure certificate identifying the first violating step, the violating invariants, and the error envelopes at that step.
Certified fallback.If a fallback mode is declared (e.g., replace a gate by a smaller-angle quantized approximation), the fallback must come with a certificate and must be logged as a program-mode change with a distinct mode ID.
Repair protocols are part of the policy and therefore part of the semantics; they must be stable under re-encoding and must appear in reproducibility manifests.
7.3.4 No-go results for finite bases
7.3.4.1 Expressivity limits
The 231-gate family (\mathcal{G}{231}) generated by (\exp(\theta E{ij})) yields a subgroup of automorphisms that is, in the ideal model, norm-preserving and invertible (unitary/orthogonal regime) in the represented coordinate fiber. Consequently:
purely unitary gate sets cannot represent non-invertible transforms (e.g., hard projections, quantization) without extending the IR;
purely symmetry gate sets cannot change invariants that are exactly preserved by the represented group action.
Formally, if (I:H^4\to\mathbb{R}) is an invariant preserved by all gates in (\mathcal{G}{231}),[I(g{ij}(\theta)H)=I(H)\quad\forall (i,j),\theta,H,]then no program over (\mathcal{G}_{231}) can alter (I). Any target operator (T) that changes (I) is therefore not representable.
7.3.4.2 Approximation bounds
Let (G=\langle \mathcal{G}{231}\rangle) be the generated group and let (d_G) be a metric on (G) compatible with operator topology. Fix a finite parameter quantization policy so that each gate has at most (Q) distinct encoded parameter values. Then the number of distinct encoded programs of length at most (L) is bounded by[N_L \le 1 + \sum{\ell=1}^{L} (231\cdot Q)^\ell \le \frac{(231Q)^{L+1}}{231Q-1}.]Therefore any (\varepsilon)-net of reachable operators realizable by length-(\le L) programs has cardinality at most (N_L). If the metric entropy (covering number) of a target subset (\mathcal{K}\subseteq G) satisfies (N(\mathcal{K},\varepsilon)>N_L), then there exist elements of (\mathcal{K}) that cannot be approximated within (\varepsilon) by programs of length (\le L).
This yields a lower bound on required program length:[L \ \ge\ \frac{\log N(\mathcal{K},\varepsilon)}{\log(231Q)}\ -\ 1,]whenever approximation is required uniformly over (\mathcal{K}).
7.3.4.3 Lower bounds on program length
Lower bounds arise from both information-theoretic counting (above) and from geometric constraints imposed by the gate set.
A geometric lower bound can be obtained by assuming each gate step is bounded in metric displacement:[d_G(I,g_{ij}(\theta))\le \Delta_{\max}\quad\text{for all allowed encoded }\theta.]Then any program of length (L) lies within radius (L\Delta_{\max}) of the identity in the induced path metric. If a target operator (T) has (d_G(I,T)>!L\Delta_{\max}), it cannot be reached by length-(L) programs.
In numerical and corridor-constrained regimes, an additional bound is imposed by admissibility: if each gate consumes a minimum corridor slack (\underline{\delta}>0) in some invariant component, then any admissible chain must satisfy (L,\underline{\delta}\le \delta), yielding[L\le \min_{k}\left\lfloor \frac{\delta_k}{\underline{\delta}_k}\right\rfloor,]and thus certain operators cannot be realized within corridor constraints.
7.3.4.4 Certified impossibility cases
A certified impossibility case is a proof object (\mathsf{NoGo}(T)) establishing that no admissible encoded program exists for target (T) under the current gate basis, parameter quantization, and corridor policy.
Canonical no-go certificates include:
invariant obstruction: exhibit an invariant (I) preserved by all gates but changed by (T);
component obstruction: show (T) lies outside the connected component of the identity reachable by the generated group (relevant when the target includes determinant/sign changes not reachable by (\mathrm{SO}(22))-type gates);
budget obstruction: prove any program approximating (T) must violate corridor budgets, using lower bounds on necessary drift or minimum length;
precision obstruction: show quantization limits prevent achieving the required approximation tolerance (\eta) within admissibility constraints, by bounding the best-possible approximation error of the quantized gate set.
A no-go certificate must reference policy IDs, the metric used for approximation, and the certified domain of the claim.
7.4 Fractal — Implementing Encodings and Gate Compilers
7.4.1 Gate compiler architecture
7.4.1.1 Parsing and IR generation
A base-22 program string (s\in\Sigma_{22}^\star) is parsed deterministically into:
a header (\mathrm{Hdr}(s)) containing schema version, basis ID, policy ID, and program length (L);
a body partitioned into (L) fixed-width instruction blocks.
Parsing must enforce:
correct total length consistent with (L) and fixed widths;
valid index pairs (i<j);
valid parameter digits per gate quantization policy;
rejection of any extraneous data.
The output of parsing is a typed intermediate representation (IR):[\mathsf{IR}=(\text{list of typed gate nodes},\ \text{deterministic evaluation order},\ \text{attached policies}).]The IR is the canonical internal object on which rewriting, cost evaluation, and certificate generation operate.
7.4.1.2 Rewrite engine
The rewrite engine applies (\mathsf{Rewrite}) rules (§7.2.1.4) to the IR to produce (\mathsf{NF}(\mathsf{IR})). Required properties:
determinism of rule application order;
typed validity preservation;
explicit tracking of semantic equivalence status (exact vs approximate);
emission of a rewrite certificate:[\mathsf{CertRewrite}:\ \mathsf{IR}\Rightarrow \mathsf{IR}',]including the list of applied rewrites and associated side-condition validations.
If approximate rewrites are used, the engine must additionally compute an operator error envelope and propagate it into corridor budgets and final certificates.
7.4.1.3 Cost evaluation and search
Cost evaluation computes (\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{prog}}) for a program under fixed policies. Costs include:
gate count and depth;
estimated runtime and memory under selected kernel implementations;
numerical risk metrics (condition estimates, error envelope growth);
corridor slack consumption.
Search procedures (shortest path, Pareto search) operate on normal-form program states and must:
use deterministic priority ordering;
prune by admissibility and dominance;
cache by canonical program hashes to avoid redundant exploration;
produce certificates for each candidate and final selection.
7.4.1.4 Output program serialization
Serialization emits a base-22 program string:[s := \mathrm{EncProg}_{22}(\pi^{\mathrm{can}}),]where (\pi^{\mathrm{can}}) is the canonical normal form program, including canonical quantized parameters.
Serialization must include:
the exact policy IDs that fix widths, rounding, and evaluation order;
optional embedded checksums/hashes (encoded in base-22 fixed fields) for integrity checks;
deterministic ordering of all header fields.
The serialized output is the canonical external artifact and the basis for program identity hashing.
7.4.2 Gate execution kernels
7.4.2.1 Dense kernels
Dense kernels represent the 22-dimensional gate action as dense matrix multiplication on a 22-coordinate fiber (or on a dense block of the full state representation). For a gate (g_{ij}(\theta)), the dense kernel applies a 2D rotation block embedded in a 22×22 identity matrix.
Dense kernels are appropriate when:
the action is already represented in a compact 22-dimensional coordinate subspace;
batching and vectorization amortize overhead.
Determinism requires fixed floating-point reduction order and fixed BLAS/LAPACK configuration or equivalent deterministic kernel implementations.
7.4.2.2 Sparse kernels
Sparse kernels exploit that each (g_{ij}(\theta)) acts nontrivially only on two coordinates in the 22-dimensional fiber (or on two channels/modes in a larger representation). The kernel updates only the affected coordinates:
[
\begin{pmatrix}x_i'\ x_j'\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}x_i\ x_j\end{pmatrix}.]Sparse kernels are canonical for Givens-style rotations and minimize memory movement. Determinism requires fixed evaluation order of gate updates and fixed implementations of (\sin,\cos) (or fixed polynomial/rational approximations with deterministic rounding).
7.4.2.3 Streaming kernels
Streaming kernels apply gate sequences to data streams or tiled state representations without materializing the full state at once. They require:
deterministic tiling and traversal order;
checkpointing policies for invariants and residuals;
bounded memory usage guarantees.
Streaming execution must preserve semantic equivalence to dense/sparse execution up to certified numerical envelopes.
7.4.2.4 Mixed precision kernels
Mixed precision execution uses lower precision for intermediate computations while maintaining higher precision accumulators where needed. A mixed precision policy must specify:
which operations are in low precision (e.g., gate parameter trig evaluation, local updates);
which accumulations are in high precision (e.g., invariant computations, constraint residual norms);
deterministic casting and rounding rules;
certified error envelopes per gate and for the full chain.
Mixed precision is admissible only when the accumulated error envelope remains within corridor budgets and reconstruction tolerances.
7.4.3 Gate-program certificates
7.4.3.1 Invariant drift proofs
A program certificate must include the invariant drift contract:[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)\big|\preceq \delta,]together with a method of verification.
Two admissible certificate modes are required:
analytic bounds: derive drift bounds from invariance properties of gates (exact invariants) and from Lipschitz bounds on invariants plus state error envelopes;
gold-set verification: evaluate invariants on a fixed set of canonical gold states, provide worst-case observed drift and a certified generalization envelope (only allowed if the policy declares the gold-set certificate as sufficient for the intended deployment scope).
Certificates must include policy IDs and the exact invariant suite definition used.
7.4.3.2 Stability bound proofs
Stability proofs certify that perturbations do not blow up under execution:[d(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)(H),\mathsf{Sem}(\pi)(\widetilde{H}))\le L_\pi, d(H,\widetilde{H}),]with a certified bound on (L_\pi), computed either by:
product of gate Lipschitz constants on the certified domain, or
tighter bounds exploiting unitarity/isometry in unitary regimes.
The certificate must also include numerical error accumulation bounds (|\widetilde{T}-T|_{\mathrm{op}}) (§7.3.2.1).
7.4.3.3 Constraint preservation proofs
Constraint preservation is certified by:
structural proofs (commutation with constraint operator, known invariance);
residual propagation bounds under approximate gates;
explicit feasibility repair steps with residual thresholds.
A constraint certificate includes:
pre/post constraint residual norms on gold states,
worst-case residual amplification bounds,
explicit repair checkpoints and solver tolerances.
7.4.3.4 Reproducibility manifests
A reproducibility manifest is a deterministic record containing:
program string (s) and program ID hash;
encoding scheme identifiers (alphabet version, policy IDs);
basis/chart identifiers and any basis-change programs applied;
kernel identifiers and precision policies;
checkpoint schedules and guard rail thresholds;
seed policies (if any) and confirmation of determinism constraints;
pointers (hashes) to all certificates: rewrite trace, invariant drift, stability, constraint, reconstruction.
The manifest is necessary and sufficient to reproduce execution outcomes within declared numerical tolerances.
7.4.4 Benchmarks for encodings
7.4.4.1 Program length distributions
Benchmarks must measure the distribution of program lengths produced by compilers for representative target families:
exact synthesis targets from (G);
approximate synthesis targets with fixed (\eta);
constraint-sensitive targets requiring strict corridor adherence.
Reported statistics include:
mean/median length, tail percentiles,
length vs approximation tolerance curves,
length vs corridor tightness curves.
All distributions are tied to policy IDs and gate catalogs.
7.4.4.2 Runtime scaling curves
Runtime benchmarks must evaluate scaling with:
program length (L),
discretization size ((N,P)) when acting on finite holograms,
kernel choice (dense vs sparse vs streaming),
precision choice.
Scaling curves are reported as deterministic operation counts where possible (gate kernel FLOP counts) and as measured runtimes only under controlled deterministic environments, with the environment encoded in the benchmark manifest.
7.4.4.3 Error vs cost curves
Error–cost benchmarking measures trade-offs between:
approximation error (d_{\mathrm{op}}(\mathsf{Sem}(\pi),T)),
invariant drift magnitude,
numerical error envelopes,
compute cost.
The benchmark must produce Pareto frontiers for each target family and provide reproducibility manifests for frontier programs.
7.4.4.4 Regression suite
A regression suite is a versioned set of tests ensuring stability of:
parsing and decoding correctness,
normal form determinism,
semantic equivalence of rewritten programs,
admissibility and corridor preservation on gold states,
reconstruction compatibility when required,
kernel-level determinism and numerical error envelopes.
Regression failures must emit a failure artifact containing: program IDs, step of failure, violated invariant components, and all relevant policy IDs.
Chapter 7 — Encoding & Gate Algebras: Base-22, 231 Gates, Coordinate Independence
Chapter 8 — Temporal Holography: Time as Parameter, Dimension, Operator
8.1 Square — Time Models and State Extension
8.1.1 Classical time vs holographic time
Let ((\mathfrak{H},\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathfrak{H}})) be the (complex) Hilbert space of admissible holograms. A temporal hologram is a map[t\ \mapsto\ H_t \in \mathfrak{H},\qquad t \in \mathcal{T},]where (\mathcal{T}\subseteq\mathbb{R}) is a time domain (typically (\mathbb{R}), (\mathbb{R}{\ge 0}), a finite interval ([t{\min},t_{\max}]), or a periodic circle (\mathbb{T}_P \cong \mathbb{R}/P\mathbb{Z}) of period (P>0)).
Temporal holography distinguishes three roles of “time”:
Time as a flow parameter: an external index (t) that orders evolution.
Time as a coordinate/dimension: an additional axis along which holographic fields live.
Time as an operator: a family of transformations acting on temporal holograms.
These roles are compatible but not identical; the formalism must support all three simultaneously.
8.1.1.1 Parameter time ((t)) for flows
A parameter-time flow on (\mathfrak{H}) is a family ({\Phi_t}{t\in\mathcal{T}}) of maps (\Phi_t:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathfrak{H}) satisfying[\Phi_0=\mathrm{Id},\qquad \Phi{t+s}=\Phi_t\circ \Phi_s,]whenever the composition is defined (semigroup on (\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}), group on (\mathbb{R})). The trajectory of an initial hologram (H_0\in\mathfrak{H}) is (H_t=\Phi_t(H_0)).
A linear time-homogeneous flow is generated by an (unbounded) operator (A) on (\mathfrak{H}),[\frac{d}{dt}H_t = A H_t,\qquad H_0\in\mathrm{Dom}(A),]with solution (H_t = e^{tA}H_0) where ({e^{tA}}_{t\ge0}) is a (C_0)-semigroup when (A) is the infinitesimal generator.
A time-inhomogeneous (non-autonomous) evolution is given by[\frac{d}{dt}H_t = A(t)H_t + b(t),]with evolution operator (U(t,s)) satisfying (H_t = U(t,s)H_s + \int_s^t U(t,\tau),b(\tau),d\tau).
8.1.1.2 Holographic time coordinate ((p_t \in \mathcal{P}))
Let (\mathcal{P}) be a parameter manifold of latent coordinates used to index or encode holographic structure. A holographic time coordinate is a map[\iota:\mathcal{T}\to\mathcal{P},\qquad t\mapsto p_t,]used when time is not merely an external ordering but a coordinate in a representation space.
A hologram may then be represented as a section over (\mathcal{P}),[\widetilde{H}(p)\in\mathfrak{H},\qquad p\in\mathcal{P},]with the temporal slice given by[H_t = \widetilde{H}(p_t).]
Reparameterization invariance. If (\varphi:\mathcal{T}\to\mathcal{T}) is a monotone (C^1) reparameterization, the representation is invariant when there exists an action (\Gamma_\varphi) on (\mathcal{P}) such that[p_{\varphi(t)} = \Gamma_\varphi(p_t),\qquad\widetilde{H}(p_{\varphi(t)})=\widetilde{H}(\Gamma_\varphi(p_t)).]This separates “physical time” from “coordinate time” and permits gauge fixing (Section 8.1.4).
8.1.1.3 Time-indexed holograms ((H(\cdot,\cdot;t)))
Let ((\mathcal{X},\nu_{\mathcal{X}})) and ((\mathcal{Y},\nu_{\mathcal{Y}})) be measurable spaces of holographic arguments, and let[H(\cdot,\cdot;t)\in L^2(\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{Y}, \nu_{\mathcal{X}}\otimes\nu_{\mathcal{Y}})\ \cong\ \mathfrak{H}.]A time-indexed hologram is the field[H:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{Y}\times\mathcal{T}\to\mathbb{C},\qquad (x,y,t)\mapsto H(x,y;t),]with (H_t(x,y)=H(x,y;t)). The temporal hologram space is the Bochner space[\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}} \ :=\ L^2(\mathcal{T}, \rho(t),dt;\ \mathfrak{H}),]where (\rho) is a time density (Section 8.1.2). Elements of (\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}) are equivalence classes of measurable maps (t\mapsto H_t) with finite norm.
8.1.1.4 Canonical time conventions
A temporal holography framework must specify conventions sufficient to make comparisons and operator actions unambiguous:
Domain convention. Specify (\mathcal{T}) and whether it is open/closed, semi-infinite, or periodic.
Origin convention. Choose a canonical (t_0\in\mathcal{T}) (often (t_0=0)).
Unit convention. Fix a time unit (dimensionful scaling) via a map (\kappa:\mathcal{T}\to\mathbb{R}) or a scale constant (s_t).
Resolution convention. Specify admissible discretizations and refinement structure.
Sexagesimal–sextine canonicalization (synchronization backbone). For periodic or cyclic time axes, a canonical coarse partition is the sixfold (sextine) backbone:[\mathcal{T}\cong \mathbb{T}_P,\qquad \text{with sextine seams at}\quad t = \frac{kP}{6},\ k=0,\dots,5.]This is the minimal nontrivial partition that is simultaneously compatible with the two refinement operators:[6 \xrightarrow{\times 2} 12,\qquad 6 \xrightarrow{\times 3} 18.]In general, for any refinements (N_1=6a) and (N_2=6b) with (\gcd(a,b)=1), the shared seam set is the base (6)-partition; the induced synchronization operator is a “handshake” across multiple clocks (Sections 8.3.1, 8.4.1).
8.1.2 Time density and measures
8.1.2.1 Define ((\rho(t))) and normalization
Let (\rho:\mathcal{T}\to(0,\infty)) be measurable. Define the time measure[d\mu(t) := \rho(t),dt,]where (dt) is Lebesgue measure (or Haar measure on (\mathbb{T}_P)). Two normalizations are used:
Probability normalization: (\int_{\mathcal{T}}\rho(t),dt = 1).
Energy normalization: (\rho(t)=1) on finite (\mathcal{T}) (or constant on (\mathbb{T}_P)).
The choice is dictated by whether temporal averaging represents expectation or total energy.
8.1.2.2 Time weighting in inner products
For (H,G\in \mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}), define the weighted inner product[\langle H,G\rangle{\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}}\ :=\ \int{\mathcal{T}} \langle H_t, G_t\rangle_{\mathfrak{H}}\ \rho(t),dt,]with induced norm[|H|{\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}}^2\ =\ \int_{\mathcal{T}} |H_t|_{\mathfrak{H}}^2 \rho(t),dt.]If (\rho) is bounded above and below away from (0) on compact (\mathcal{T}), this norm is equivalent to the unweighted (L^2) temporal norm.
8.1.2.3 Temporal marginalization operators
Define the temporal marginal (weighted mean hologram)[\mathcal{M}\rho(H)\ :=\ \int{\mathcal{T}} H_t\ \rho(t),dt\ \in\ \mathfrak{H},]interpreted as a Bochner integral.
Define the centered field[\widetilde{H}t := H_t - \mathcal{M}\rho(H),]and the temporal covariance operator (\mathcal{K}_H:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathfrak{H}) by[\mathcal{K}H u := \int{\mathcal{T}} \langle u,\widetilde{H}t\rangle{\mathfrak{H}}\ \widetilde{H}_t\ \rho(t),dt.]Then (\mathcal{K}H) is self-adjoint, positive semidefinite, and trace-class when (t\mapsto |H_t|{\mathfrak{H}}^2) is integrable and (\mathfrak{H}) is separable.
Define temporal projection and compression via eigenpairs ((\lambda_k,\phi_k)) of (\mathcal{K}_H):[\widetilde{H}t \approx \sum{k=1}^r a_k(t)\phi_k,\qquada_k(t) = \langle \widetilde{H}t,\phi_k\rangle{\mathfrak{H}},]with optimality in the Hilbert–Schmidt sense for fixed rank (r).
8.1.2.4 Boundary and initial condition formalism
When time is a parameter for evolution, boundary/initial conditions are imposed on (H_t):
Initial value: (H_{t_0} = H^0) at (t_0\in\mathcal{T}).
Terminal value: (H_{t_1} = H^1) at (t_1\in\mathcal{T}).
Periodic boundary: (H_{t+P} = H_t) on (\mathbb{T}_P).
Mixed boundary: conditions on operators applied to (H_t) (e.g., (\partial_t H) at endpoints).
When time is treated as a dimension, boundary conditions become constraints on the admissible space:[\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}^{\mathrm{BC}} := {H\in \mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}:\ \mathcal{B}(H)=0},]for a boundary operator (\mathcal{B}) (trace operator on Sobolev spaces in time).
8.1.3 Temporal constraints
Let (\mathcal{C}:\mathfrak{H}\to \mathfrak{Z}) be a constraint operator (with (\mathcal{C}(H)=0) defining admissible holograms). Temporal admissibility requires compatibility of time evolution and time operators with (\mathcal{C}).
8.1.3.1 Causality-style constraints (formal)
Causality constraints are implemented as restrictions on temporal dependence of observables or reconstructions. Let (\mathcal{O}t) be an observation operator producing measurements (y(t)=\mathcal{O}t(H{\le t})). A causal operator (\mathcal{O}) satisfies:[H|{(-\infty,t]} = G|_{(-\infty,t]}\quad\Rightarrow\quad\mathcal{O}_t(H)=\mathcal{O}t(G).]For linear time-invariant filtering on (\mathcal{T}=\mathbb{R}), a causal convolution operator (F) has impulse response (k(\tau)=0) for (\tau<0):[(FH)t = \int{0}^{\infty} k(\tau),H{t-\tau},d\tau.]For discrete time, causality is enforced by lower-triangular operator structure.
8.1.3.2 Smoothness and bounded variation in time
Define temporal Sobolev regularity:[H\in W^{m,2}(\mathcal{T};\mathfrak{H})\quad\Leftrightarrow\quad\partial_t^k H \in L^2(\mathcal{T};\mathfrak{H}),\ k=0,\dots,m,]with norm[|H|{W^{m,2}}^2 = \sum{k=0}^m \int_{\mathcal{T}} |\partial_t^k H_t|_{\mathfrak{H}}^2,\rho(t),dt.]
Define bounded variation in time for (\mathfrak{H})-valued functions on an interval:[\mathrm{TV}(H) := \sup_{\Pi} \sum_{j} |H_{t_{j+1}}-H_{t_j}|_{\mathfrak{H}} < \infty,]where (\Pi) ranges over partitions. BV control is a sufficient condition for stable reconstruction under sampling and interpolation with quantified error bounds.
8.1.3.3 Temporal reconstruction constraints
Temporal reconstruction constraints specify conditions under which (H) is recoverable from partial temporal samples or compressed measurements. A prototypical condition is temporal bandlimiting in the sense of a temporal Fourier transform.
Assume (\mathcal{T}=\mathbb{R}) and (H\in L^2(\mathbb{R};\mathfrak{H})). Define the (Bochner) Fourier transform[\widehat{H}(\omega) := \int_{\mathbb{R}} H_t,e^{-i\omega t},dt\in\mathfrak{H}.](H) is temporally bandlimited to ([-\Omega,\Omega]) if (\widehat{H}(\omega)=0) for (|\omega|>\Omega).
Sampling at step (\Delta t < \pi/\Omega) yields stable reconstruction (Section 8.3.1). More general constraints include sparsity of temporal coefficients in a frame ({\psi_k}), or low-rank temporal covariance (Section 8.1.2.3), enabling compressed sensing or subspace tracking.
8.1.3.4 Compatibility with ((\mathcal{C}(H)=0))
Define the temporally lifted constraint (\mathcal{C}\mathcal{T}:\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}\to L^2(\mathcal{T};\mathfrak{Z})) by[(\mathcal{C}\mathcal{T}H)(t) := \mathcal{C}(H_t).]Temporal admissibility requires[\mathcal{C}(H_t)=0\quad\text{for a.e. }t,]and operator admissibility requires for each temporal operator (U) that[\mathcal{C}\mathcal{T}(UH)=0 \quad\text{whenever}\quad \mathcal{C}_\mathcal{T}(H)=0.]Sufficient conditions:
If (\mathcal{C}) is linear and (U) satisfies (\mathcal{C}\circ U_t = 0) whenever (\mathcal{C}=0), then preservation holds.
If (\mathcal{C}) defines a closed subspace (\mathfrak{H}_0\subseteq\mathfrak{H}), and (U_t(\mathfrak{H}_0)\subseteq\mathfrak{H}_0), then invariance is immediate.
If (\mathcal{C}) is nonlinear, invariance is checked by verifying (U) is a symmetry of the constraint manifold (Lie derivative vanishes along constraint gradients), or by enforcing projection:[H_t \leftarrow \Pi_{\mathcal{C}=0}(H_t)\quad\text{after each operation,}]with (\Pi_{\mathcal{C}=0}) the constraint projection (exact or proximal).
8.1.4 Temporal canonical forms
Temporal canonicalization fixes redundant degrees of freedom and yields stable identities for comparison, compression, and operator application.
8.1.4.1 Phase alignment across time
Assume (H_t\in\mathfrak{H}) is complex-valued and defined up to a global phase (e^{i\phi(t)}). Define a reference functional (R:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathbb{C}) (nonzero on admissible states) and set phase by[\phi(t) := -\arg(R(H_t)),\qquadH_t^{\mathrm{aligned}} := e^{i\phi(t)}H_t.]A common choice is (R(H)=\langle H,H_{\mathrm{ref}}\rangle_{\mathfrak{H}}) for a fixed reference (H_{\mathrm{ref}}), or (R(H)=\int H(x,y),w(x,y),d\nu) for a weight (w).
This canonicalization is stable when (|R(H_t)|) is bounded below by a positive constant on the time range of interest.
8.1.4.2 Time-shift gauge and fixing
If time is defined modulo shift, i.e. (H_t\sim H_{t+\tau}) for some unknown (\tau), define the temporal correlation functional[\Gamma(\tau) := \int_{\mathcal{T}} \langle H_t, H_{t+\tau}\rangle_{\mathfrak{H}}\ \rho(t),dt,]with appropriate periodic extension if (\mathcal{T}=\mathbb{T}P). A canonical shift (\tau^\star) is chosen by[\tau^\star := \arg\max{\tau\in\mathcal{T}} \Re,\Gamma(\tau),]and define the gauge-fixed field[(H^{\mathrm{fix}})t := H{t+\tau^\star}.]FFT-based evaluation of (\Gamma) is available in discrete time (Section 8.4.1.2).
8.1.4.3 Canonical time origin selection
When an intrinsic event marker exists, define a scalar observable (s(t)=\mathcal{S}(H_t)\in\mathbb{R}) (e.g., energy, entropy proxy, constraint residual norm). A canonical origin is selected as[t_0^\star := \arg\min_{t\in\mathcal{T}} s(t),\quad\text{or}\quadt_0^\star := \arg\max_{t\in\mathcal{T}} s(t),]depending on whether the origin is an “anchor minimum” or “anchor maximum.”
For periodic domains, a sextine origin convention sets (t_0^\star) to the nearest sextine seam (kP/6) to enforce compatibility across refinement grids.
8.1.4.4 Time-normalized IDs
Define a time-normalized identifier map (\mathrm{ID}:\mathfrak{H}_{\mathcal{T}}\to\mathcal{I}) by a composition of canonicalizations:[\mathrm{ID}(H) := \mathrm{Hash}\Big(\mathrm{Quant}\big(\mathcal{N}(\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{A}(H)))\big)\Big),]where:
(\mathcal{A}): phase alignment,
(\mathcal{G}): time-shift gauge fixing,
(\mathcal{N}): amplitude/energy normalization (e.g., (|H|{\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}}=1)),
(\mathrm{Quant}): deterministic quantization,
(\mathrm{Hash}): collision-resistant digest of the canonical representation.
Stability requires Lipschitz continuity of (\mathcal{A},\mathcal{G},\mathcal{N}) on the admissible set and a quantization tolerance matched to the noise model (Section 8.3.2–8.3.3).
8.2 Flower — Temporal Operators
Temporal operators act on (\mathfrak{H}_{\mathcal{T}}) (and potentially on joint spaces (\mathfrak{H}) with spatial coordinates). Operators are specified at three levels:
Action on time (warp (t\mapsto \varphi(t))),
Amplitude correction (Jacobian factors preserving norms),
Constraint preservation ((\mathcal{C}) invariance or projection).
8.2.1 Time dilation operators
8.2.1.1 Define dilation ((t\mapsto t^\alpha))
Assume (\mathcal{T}=\mathbb{R}{>0}). Let (\alpha>0). Define the nonlinear dilation map (\varphi\alpha(t)=t^\alpha), with Jacobian (\varphi_\alpha'(t)=\alpha t^{\alpha-1}).
Define the dilation operator (D_\alpha) on (\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}) by[(D\alpha H)t\ :=\ \Big(\frac{\rho(\varphi\alpha(t))\ \varphi_\alpha'(t)}{\rho(t)}\Big)^{1/2}\ H_{\varphi_\alpha(t)}.]Then (D_\alpha) is an isometry on (\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}):[|D\alpha H|{\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}}=|H|{\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}}.]
For constant (\rho), this reduces to[(D_\alpha H)t = (\alpha t^{\alpha-1})^{1/2} H{t^\alpha}.]
8.2.1.2 Generator representation
Let (u=\log t), so (t=e^u). Then (\varphi_\alpha(t)=t^\alpha) corresponds to (u\mapsto \alpha u). In log-time coordinates, the operator becomes a scaling operator, and the generator can be derived by differentiating at (\alpha=1).
For (\rho\equiv 1), define (f(u):=e^{u/2}H_{e^u}) (the standard unitary transport between (L^2(\mathbb{R}{>0},dt)) and (L^2(\mathbb{R},du))). Then[(D\alpha f)(u) = \alpha^{1/2} f(\alpha u),]and the infinitesimal generator (G) satisfies[\left.\frac{d}{d\alpha}\right|{\alpha=1} D\alpha f = G f,\qquad(Gf)(u)=\frac{1}{2}f(u) + u f'(u).]Transporting back to (t)-space yields the generator acting on (H) as a first-order differential operator with logarithmic coefficient.
8.2.1.3 Effect on invariants
Let (I:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathbb{R}) be an invariant of holographic states (e.g., a constraint-compatible energy, a topological index, or a conserved quantity under a dynamics). Under dilation,[t\mapsto I((D_\alpha H)t)]is generally reparameterized. A time-reparameterization invariant functional is one that depends only on the image set ({H_t:t\in\mathcal{T}}) up to measurable reindexing, equivalently:[I{\mathrm{traj}}(H) := \int_{\mathcal{T}} \psi(I(H_t))\ \rho(t),dt]for some (\psi), which remains invariant under (D_\alpha) when the Jacobian correction is used.
8.2.1.4 Composition laws
For (\alpha,\beta>0),[D_\alpha\circ D_\beta = D_{\alpha\beta}.]This holds because ((t^\beta)^\alpha = t^{\alpha\beta}) and Jacobian factors multiply accordingly. The inverse is (D_{\alpha^{-1}}).
8.2.2 Time rotation and phase operators
8.2.2.1 ((t\mapsto e^{i\omega t}))-type actions
Define the modulation operator (M_\omega) by[(M_\omega H)t := e^{i\omega t} H_t,\qquad \omega\in\mathbb{R}.]This is unitary on (\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}) when (\rho) is real and positive.
8.2.2.2 Spectral interpretation
For (\mathcal{T}=\mathbb{R}), modulation corresponds to frequency translation:[\widehat{M_\omega H}(\xi) = \widehat{H}(\xi-\omega).]Thus (M_\omega) is the temporal analogue of a phase ramp; it changes carrier frequency without altering magnitude spectrum.
8.2.2.3 Resonance conditions
Let (H) satisfy a temporal linear response (y(t)=\langle u,H_t\rangle_{\mathfrak{H}}). If (y) has dominant frequency (\omega_0), then modulation by (\omega) produces a shift to (\omega_0+\omega). A resonance condition for an operator (T) coupled to time is expressed as spectral overlap:[\mathrm{supp}(\widehat{H})\cap \mathrm{supp}(\widehat{T H})\neq\varnothing,]with stability controlled by separation of support (Section 8.3.2–8.3.3).
8.2.2.4 Stability bounds
For modulation, stability is exact:[|M_\omega H - M_\omega G|{\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}} = |H-G|{\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}}.]For approximate modulation implemented in discrete time by complex exponentials sampled on a grid, stability depends on phase quantization error and floating-point precision (Section 8.4.1.4).
8.2.3 Time reflection and reversal
8.2.3.1 Define reflection about ((t_0))
Define time reflection (R_{t_0}) by[(R_{t_0}H)t := \Big(\frac{\rho(2t_0-t)}{\rho(t)}\Big)^{1/2} H{2t_0-t}.]For symmetric density (\rho(t)=\rho(2t_0-t)), the operator is unitary.
8.2.3.2 Invertibility and admissibility
(R_{t_0}) is involutive:[R_{t_0}^{-1} = R_{t_0},\qquad R_{t_0}^2 = \mathrm{Id}.]Admissibility requires that (t\mapsto 2t_0-t) maps (\mathcal{T}) into itself; for (\mathcal{T}=\mathbb{R}_{\ge0}), reflection about (t_0>0) is only admissible on a truncated interval ([0,2t_0]) unless extension conventions are defined.
8.2.3.3 Constraint preservation issues
If (\mathcal{C}(H_t)=0) for all (t), then reflection preserves constraints pointwise provided (\mathcal{C}) is time-local:[\mathcal{C}((R_{t_0}H)t)=\mathcal{C}(H{2t_0-t})=0.]If constraints involve temporal derivatives (e.g., causality, monotonicity of invariants), reflection may violate admissibility. Such constraints are enforced either by restricting the admissible operator set or by post-operation repair (Section 8.3.3.4).
8.2.3.4 Diagnostics for reversal artifacts
Define diagnostic measures:
Constraint residual: (r(t):=|\mathcal{C}(H_t)|_{\mathfrak{Z}}).
Causality residual: a functional (\kappa(H)) measuring violation of causal structure (e.g., prediction error using only past).
Temporal monotonicity break: detect sign flips in (\frac{d}{dt}I(H_t)) when (I) is expected monotone.
Reversal artifacts are flagged when residuals exceed certified thresholds derived from noise and discretization bounds (Section 8.3.1.4, 8.3.3.1).
8.2.4 Space-time coupling operators
8.2.4.1 Shear operators ((t\mapsto t+\beta x))
Let (H=H(x,y;t)). Define a shear operator (S_\beta) acting on time by[(S_\beta H)(x,y;t) := H(x,y;\ t+\beta,\chi(x,y)),]where (\chi) is a chosen spatial coordinate or scalar field, and (\beta\in\mathbb{R}).
If (\chi) depends only on (x) (or a single spatial axis), this is a space-time shear. In the simplest case with (\chi(x,y)=x),[(S_\beta H)(x,y;t) = H(x,y;t+\beta x).]
8.2.4.2 Coupling constraints
To preserve well-posedness and prevent foldovers in discretization, require a Lipschitz corridor:[|\beta|,|\nabla \chi|_\infty \le \Lambda,]where (\Lambda) is set by the temporal sampling resolution (\Delta t) and the spatial resolution (\Delta x). A sufficient discrete constraint preventing temporal aliasing under shear is[|\beta|,\Delta x \le \frac{1}{2}\Delta t,]when linear interpolation is used and the signal is near Nyquist.
8.2.4.3 Covariant treatment preview
Let (z=(x,t)) be a space-time coordinate. A covariant approach treats couplings as actions of an affine group on (z),[z \mapsto A z + b,\qquad A\in \mathrm{GL}(d+1),]with induced pullback on fields (H(z)). Constraint preservation and unitarity correspond to structural restrictions on (A) (e.g., measure-preserving, or preserving a chosen metric). The present chapter uses implementable subclasses (shear, dilation, reflection, modulation).
8.2.4.4 Implementable kernels
Implementing (t\mapsto t+\delta) with non-integer shifts requires interpolation. Define a temporal kernel (k_\delta) such that[H(t+\delta) \approx \sum_{n} k_\delta[n],H(t+n\Delta t).]Standard choices:
Sinc kernel (bandlimited assumption): ideal but infinite support.
Windowed-sinc: finite support with controlled error.
Cubic/linear interpolation: efficient, lower accuracy, stability constraints tighter.
Kernel design is validated by reconstruction and stability tests (Section 8.4.3).
8.3 Cloud — Temporal Uncertainty, Resolution, and Limits
8.3.1 Temporal resolution limits
8.3.1.1 Sampling theorem analogues
Let (H\in L^2(\mathbb{R};\mathfrak{H})) be temporally bandlimited to ([-\Omega,\Omega]). For sampling step (\Delta t < \pi/\Omega), define samples (H[n]=H_{n\Delta t}). Then reconstruction holds in (\mathfrak{H})-norm:[H_t = \sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}} H[n]\ \mathrm{sinc}\Big(\frac{t-n\Delta t}{\Delta t}\Big),]where the series converges in (L^2(\mathbb{R};\mathfrak{H})). This is the Bochner-space extension of Shannon sampling.
8.3.1.2 Bandlimit vs time resolution
