Temporal resolution (\Delta t) and bandlimit (\Omega) satisfy the Nyquist condition:[\Delta t \le \frac{\pi}{\Omega}.]When the temporal spectrum is only approximately supported, (\Omega) is replaced by an effective bandwidth determined by tail energy:[\int_{|\omega|>\Omega} |\widehat{H}(\omega)|_{\mathfrak{H}}^2,d\omega \le \varepsilon^2.]This yields a reconstruction error bound proportional to (\varepsilon) plus interpolation truncation error.
8.3.1.3 Aliasing conditions
For discrete-time sampling at rate (f_s=1/\Delta t), aliasing occurs when the spectrum overlaps under folding:[\widehat{H}(\omega)\ \text{and}\ \widehat{H}(\omega+2\pi k f_s)\ \text{overlap for some}\ k\neq 0.]In multi-rate temporal holography (simultaneous 12/18/60 grids), aliasing manifests as inconsistent cross-indexing. Let (\Pi_{N}) denote projection onto an (N)-bin partition of a period (P). Consistency requires that coarse-to-fine projections agree on sextine seams:[\Pi_{12}\circ \Pi_{60}\ \text{and}\ \Pi_{18}\circ \Pi_{60}\quad\text{agree on}\quad kP/6.]Violations quantify aliasing-induced incoherence.
8.3.1.4 Certified resolution bounds
Let (\widehat{H}) be supported in ([-\Omega,\Omega]) and suppose reconstruction uses a windowed-sinc kernel of half-width (W). Then the uniform reconstruction error admits a bound:[|H_t - \widehat{H}^{(W)}t|{\mathfrak{H}}\ \le\ c_1,\varepsilon\ +\ c_2,\frac{1}{W},|H|_{W^{1,2}(\mathbb{R};\mathfrak{H})},]for constants (c_1,c_2) depending on the window. This yields certified guard rails for algorithmic choices (Section 8.3.2.4).
8.3.2 Chronological uncertainty principles
8.3.2.1 Formal uncertainty statements
Let (h\in L^2(\mathbb{R})) with (|h|2=1). Define time variance[\sigma_t^2 := \int (t-\mu_t)^2 |h(t)|^2,dt,\qquad \mu_t=\int t|h(t)|^2,dt,]and frequency variance (\sigma\omega^2) analogously from (|\widehat{h}(\omega)|^2). Then[\sigma_t,\sigma_\omega \ge \frac{1}{2}.]For (\mathfrak{H})-valued (H), apply to the scalar energy density (e(t)=|H_t|_{\mathfrak{H}}^2) when (e) is integrable and normalized, or to projections (\langle u,H_t\rangle) for (u\in\mathfrak{H}).
8.3.2.2 Trade-offs between time/frequency precision
Any attempt to localize temporal events (sharp time support) increases spectral spread, increasing sensitivity to sampling and phase operators. Conversely, strong bandlimiting enforces temporal delocalization, limiting achievable temporal resolution beyond Nyquist considerations.
A practical corollary: for reconstruction problems with measurement noise (\eta) and temporal filtering (F), the estimation error lower bound scales with the effective time-frequency concentration of the chosen representation.
8.3.2.3 Noise amplification regimes
For inverse problems (y=\mathcal{A}H+\eta) with (\mathcal{A}) involving temporal downsampling or warps, the stability constant is controlled by the smallest singular value of the induced operator on the admissible subspace:[|H-\widehat{H}| \le \sigma_{\min}^{-1}(\mathcal{A}|_{\mathrm{adm}})\ |\eta|.]Noise amplification increases as sampling approaches Nyquist, as warps create local compression (effective (\Delta t) increases), or as coupling shears introduce interpolation errors.
8.3.2.4 Guard rails
A guard rail policy specifies admissible operator parameters to maintain stable inversion and bounded error:
Sampling guard: (\Delta t \le \pi/\Omega_{\mathrm{eff}}).
Warp guard: (\sup_t |\varphi'(t)| \le \gamma_{\max}) with (\gamma_{\max}) set by interpolation accuracy.
Coupling guard: (|\beta|,\Delta x \le \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta t).
Regularization guard: choose (\lambda) in Tikhonov/proximal inversion so that (\kappa(\lambda)) bounds noise gain.
8.3.3 Temporal admissibility corridors
8.3.3.1 Drift bounds in time invariants
Let (I:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathbb{R}^m) be invariants expected to drift slowly. Define the drift rate[d_I(t) := |\partial_t I(H_t)|.]An admissibility corridor is specified by bounds[d_I(t)\le \delta_I(t),]where (\delta_I) is determined by model error and measurement noise. Operators are admissible when they preserve corridor feasibility:[H\in \mathrm{Corr}(\delta_I)\ \Rightarrow\ U H \in \mathrm{Corr}(\delta_I').]
8.3.3.2 Reconstruction preservation across time
Given a reconstruction operator (\mathcal{R}) and a forward operator (\mathcal{A}), define temporal reconstruction error[E(t) := |H_t - (\mathcal{R}\mathcal{A}H)t|{\mathfrak{H}}.]Preservation requires (E(t)) remain within certified bounds for all (t) (or on a weighted set under (\rho)), and that operator actions do not create localized spikes beyond corridor thresholds.
8.3.3.3 Stability of time operators
An operator (U) on (\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}) is Lipschitz-stable if[|UH-UG|{\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}} \le L_U |H-G|{\mathfrak{H}_{\mathcal{T}}}.]Unitary operators satisfy (L_U=1). Interpolation-based shears and nonlinear warps typically satisfy (L_U>1), with (L_U) controlled by kernel condition numbers and sampling resolution.
8.3.3.4 Reject/repair for time transforms
Define a repair operator (\mathcal{P}) projecting onto admissible constraints:[\mathcal{P}(H) := \arg\min_{G\in \mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}:\ \mathcal{C}\mathcal{T}(G)=0} |G-H|{\mathfrak{H}{\mathcal{T}}}^2 + \lambda \mathcal{R}(G),]where (\mathcal{R}) is a regularizer enforcing smoothness/corridor constraints.
Reject/repair policy:
Apply (U) to obtain (H' = UH).
If diagnostics exceed thresholds, set (\widehat{H} := \mathcal{P}(H')), else (\widehat{H}:=H').
Record diagnostics to support regression and reproducibility.
8.3.4 Existence and well-posedness
8.3.4.1 Initial value problems in ((\mathfrak{H}))
Consider the IVP[\frac{d}{dt}H_t = A H_t + b(t),\qquad H_{t_0}=H^0.]A mild solution is[H_t = e^{(t-t_0)A}H^0 + \int_{t_0}^t e^{(t-s)A},b(s),ds.]
8.3.4.2 Semigroup well-posedness
If (A) generates a strongly continuous semigroup ({e^{tA}}_{t\ge 0}), then the IVP is well-posed: existence, uniqueness, and continuous dependence on data hold. Sufficient conditions include (A) being closed and densely defined and satisfying Hille–Yosida bounds.
8.3.4.3 Weak solution existence
When (A) is only defined weakly (e.g., via bilinear forms), weak solutions are defined through test functionals (\ell\in\mathfrak{H}^\ast):[\frac{d}{dt}\ell(H_t) = \ell(AH_t) + \ell(b(t)),]in the distributional sense. Existence follows under coercivity and boundedness of the associated form, with energy estimates derived from Grönwall-type inequalities in (\mathfrak{H}).
8.3.4.4 Counterexamples
Ill-posedness arises when:
(A) fails to generate a (C_0)-semigroup (e.g., spectrum in the right half-plane without dissipativity),
temporal inversion requires division by near-zero spectral components (severe noise amplification),
warps violate monotonicity or create foldovers in discrete implementations.
Such cases motivate admissibility corridors and guard rails rather than unrestricted operator algebras.
8.4 Fractal — Temporal Algorithms and Tests
8.4.1 Discrete time representations
8.4.1.1 Time grids and adaptive stepping
Let (t_0<t_1<\dots<t_{N-1}) be a grid. Uniform grids use (t_n=t_0+n\Delta t). Adaptive grids are defined by error-controlled stepping for an evolution (\dot{H}=F(t,H)):
Algorithm (adaptive stepping, abstract).
Input: (H^{(0)}\in\mathfrak{H}), (t_0), (t_{\max}), tolerance (\epsilon).Output: grid ({t_n}), states ({H^{(n)}}).
Set (n\leftarrow 0), (t\leftarrow t_0), (H\leftarrow H^{(0)}), choose (\Delta t).
Predict (H_{\mathrm{pred}}) with a method of order (p).
Estimate error (e=|H_{\mathrm{pred}}-H_{\mathrm{corr}}|_{\mathfrak{H}}).
If (e\le\epsilon): accept step ((t,H)\leftarrow (t+\Delta t,H_{\mathrm{corr}})), store; else reject.
Update (\Delta t \leftarrow \Delta t \cdot (\epsilon/e)^{1/(p+1)}) (with safety factors).
Repeat until (t\ge t_{\max}).
Constraint preservation can be enforced by projecting (H\leftarrow\Pi_{\mathcal{C}=0}(H)) after each accepted step.
8.4.1.2 Time-frequency transforms
For discrete-time sequences (H[n]\in\mathfrak{H}):
STFT of projections: choose (u_k\in\mathfrak{H}), compute (h_k[n]=\langle u_k,H[n]\rangle), then apply STFT to (h_k).
Operator-valued spectrum: compute (\widehat{H}[\omega]) via FFT in time on coefficients in a fixed (\mathfrak{H})-basis.
Windowing policies are selected to satisfy uncertainty guard rails (Section 8.3.2).
8.4.1.3 Streaming time storage
For long temporal holograms, store as chunked blocks:
chunk length (B),
overlap (O) for filter continuity,
metadata includes time indices, density (\rho), canonicalization parameters, and hash IDs (Section 8.1.4.4).
Streaming invariants are tracked via incremental updates:[\mathcal{M}\rho(H) \leftarrow \mathcal{M}\rho(H) + \Delta \mathcal{M},\quad\mathcal{K}_H \leftarrow \mathcal{K}_H + \Delta \mathcal{K},]using numerically stable online covariance updates.
8.4.1.4 Precision policies
Define a precision policy (\pi) specifying:
floating-point type (FP32/FP64),
complex phase representation,
accumulation strategy (Kahan / pairwise sums),
quantization rules for IDs.
A certified policy ensures that rounding error stays below corridor tolerances:[\mathrm{err}{\mathrm{fp}} \le \epsilon{\mathrm{corr}}.]
8.4.2 Temporal operator implementations
8.4.2.1 Dilation kernels
Discrete nonlinear dilation (t\mapsto t^\alpha) requires resampling on a warped grid. Let uniform samples (H[n]=H_{n\Delta t}). Define target indices[n' = \varphi_\alpha(n\Delta t)/\Delta t = (n\Delta t)^\alpha/\Delta t,]generally non-integer. Implement[(D_\alpha H)[n] \approx w[n]\sum_{m} k(n'-m),H[m],]where (k) is an interpolation kernel and (w[n]) is the discrete Jacobian correction consistent with (\rho).
Complexity: (O(NK)) for kernel support (K), or (O(N\log N)) for FFT-based bandlimited methods on periodic domains.
8.4.2.2 Rotation kernels
Modulation is implemented exactly:[(M_\omega H)[n] = e^{i\omega n\Delta t} H[n],]with phase computed using stable recurrence to reduce drift:[e^{i\omega(n+1)\Delta t} = e^{i\omega\Delta t},e^{i\omega n\Delta t}.]
8.4.2.3 Reflection kernels
For reflection about sample index (n_0),[(R_{n_0}H)[n]=H[2n_0-n],]with boundary handling via truncation, padding, or periodic wrap depending on (\mathcal{T}).
8.4.2.4 Coupling kernels
For shear (t\mapsto t+\beta x), in a discretized spatial grid (x_i=i\Delta x), implement a per-(x_i) fractional delay:[(S_\beta H)[i,n] \approx \sum_m k(\delta_i - m),H[i,n+m],\qquad \delta_i=\beta x_i/\Delta t.]Kernel selection is constrained by coupling guard rails (Section 8.3.2.4).
8.4.3 Verification suite
8.4.3.1 Time invariants tests
Given invariants (I(H_t)), verify drift corridors:
Test: For all (n),[|I(H[n]) - I(H[n-1])| \le \delta_I[n].]Failures are categorized by operator source (warp, coupling, interpolation) using attribution logs.
8.4.3.2 Temporal reconstruction tests
For bandlimited synthetic fields, test sampling-reconstruction consistency:[\max_n |H[n]-\widehat{H}[n]|{\mathfrak{H}} \le \varepsilon{\mathrm{rec}}.]For compressed sensing scenarios, evaluate recovery under sparsity/low-rank priors and compare against certified bounds.
8.4.3.3 Stability regression tests
For each operator (U), test Lipschitz stability numerically:[\frac{|U(H+\Delta)-U(H)|}{|\Delta|} \le L_U + \epsilon,]over randomized perturbations (\Delta) consistent with the noise model.
8.4.3.4 Performance profiling
Measure:
throughput (samples/sec),
memory footprint (bytes/sample),
kernel latency,
scaling vs (N), spatial size, kernel width.
Require reproducible profiling conditions (fixed seeds, pinned precision policy, deterministic kernels).
8.4.4 Reference experiments
8.4.4.1 Synthetic time-signals
Construct (H_t) using controlled temporal spectra:[H_t = \sum_{k=1}^K a_k e^{i\omega_k t}\Phi_k,\qquad \Phi_k\in\mathfrak{H},]with known bandlimit and known invariants for validation.
8.4.4.2 Known resonant cases
Choose (\omega_k) matching operator-induced resonances (modulation, periodic boundary conditions, sextine seams on (\mathbb{T}_P)). Validate that predicted spectral shifts and stability bounds match observed behavior.
8.4.4.3 Stress tests under noise
Add noise (\eta_t) with controlled covariance:[\mathbb{E}\eta_t=0,\qquad \mathbb{E}\langle u,\eta_t\rangle\overline{\langle v,\eta_s\rangle} = \Sigma(u,v),k(t-s),]and measure:
reconstruction error vs SNR,
corridor violation frequency,
repair operator effectiveness.
8.4.4.4 Reproducible reports
A reference report includes:
full specification of (\mathcal{T}), (\rho), discretization, precision policy,
operator parameters and guard rails,
invariant definitions and thresholds,
deterministic seeds and versioned kernels,
outputs: metrics tables, failure attribution logs, and hashed canonical IDs.
Chapter 8 — Temporal Holography: Time as Parameter, Dimension, Operator
Chapter 9 — Fractal Time & Time-Crystal Lattices: Scaling, Resonance, Period Nets
Let (\mathcal{T}\subseteq\mathbb{R}) be a time domain equipped with a (\sigma)-algebra (\mathcal{B}(\mathcal{T})) and a finite, nonnegative Borel measure (\nu). A time-indexed holographic state is a map[H:\mathcal{T}\to H^4\subseteq\mathfrak{H},]measurable with respect to (\mathcal{B}(\mathcal{T})) and the canonical (\sigma)-algebra induced by (\mathfrak{H}), and satisfying the constraint (C(H(t))=0) for (\nu)-a.e. (t). When required, assume (H\in L^2(\mathcal{T};\mathfrak{H},\nu)) or (H\in H^s(\mathcal{T};\mathfrak{H})) in the Bochner sense.
A time-crystal lattice is defined here as an equivalence class of multi-scale periodic structures extracted from (H) (or from an associated observable (\mathcal{O}(H))) under a chosen gauge (including time-shift gauge and phase gauge), together with admissible operators acting on that structure with certified stability and detectability properties.
9.1 Square — Fractal Time Definitions
9.1.1 Self-similarity definitions
9.1.1.1 Scaling law (\big(T(t)=T(\lambda t)\big)) formalization
Let (X) be a measurable space of signal values (typically (X=\mathbb{R}^m), (\mathbb{C}^m), or (\mathfrak{H})). A map (T:\mathcal{T}\to X) is strictly self-similar with scaling factor (\lambda>0) if[T(t)=\mathcal{A}\lambda,T(\lambda t)\quad\text{for all }t\in\mathcal{T}\cap \lambda^{-1}\mathcal{T},]where (\mathcal{A}\lambda:X\to X) is a deterministic scaling operator. If (X) is normed, (\mathcal{A}_\lambda) is assumed bounded and invertible unless stated otherwise.
For (\mathfrak{H})-valued (T), the operator (\mathcal{A}\lambda) is required to be compatible with the constraint manifold:[T(t)\in H^4 \ \Rightarrow\ \mathcal{A}\lambda T(\lambda t)\in H^4,]equivalently (C\circ \mathcal{A}\lambda|{H^4}=0) whenever (C|{H^4}=0). In particular, (\mathcal{A}\lambda) must map (\ker C) to (\ker C).
Define log-time coordinate (\tau=\log t) on (\mathcal{T}\cap(0,\infty)). Under this change of variables, scaling (t\mapsto \lambda t) becomes translation (\tau\mapsto \tau+\log\lambda). Self-similarity is equivalently:[\widetilde T(\tau)=\mathcal{A}_\lambda,\widetilde T(\tau+\log\lambda),\quad \widetilde T(\tau):=T(e^\tau).]This representation is canonical for lattice constructions because it converts multiplicative scale recursion into additive group actions.
A weaker, corridor-compatible formulation is approximate self-similarity: for a tolerance (\eta\ge 0),[|T(t)-\mathcal{A}_\lambda T(\lambda t)|_X \le \eta \quad \text{for } \nu\text{-a.e. }t.]When (X=\mathfrak{H}), the norm may be any of the admissible metrics of Chapter 2, including gauge-invariant distances after canonicalization.
9.1.1.2 Discrete vs continuous self-similarity
Let (\Lambda\subseteq(0,\infty)) be a multiplicative subgroup (scale group). A signal (T) is:
Continuously self-similar if the scaling law holds for all (\lambda) in an interval ((\lambda_-,\lambda_+)) with (\lambda_-<1<\lambda_+), with (\mathcal{A}\lambda) forming a representation:[\mathcal{A}{\lambda_1\lambda_2}=\mathcal{A}{\lambda_1}\mathcal{A}{\lambda_2},\qquad \mathcal{A}_1=I.]
Discretely self-similar if the scaling law holds for (\lambda\in\Lambda) where (\Lambda={\lambda_0^k:k\in\mathbb{Z}}) for some (\lambda_0>1). In log-time, this is periodicity of (\widetilde T(\tau)) up to (\mathcal{A}_{\lambda_0}) with period (\log\lambda_0).
Statistically self-similar if equality holds in distribution (or in correlation structure) rather than pointwise. For random processes (T), this means:[T(\cdot)\overset{d}{=}\mathcal{A}_\lambda T(\lambda\cdot),]interpreted in the appropriate product (\sigma)-algebra.
Discrete self-similarity is the default for finite computational models and for time-crystal lattices because it yields a countable scale-index set with an explicit renormalization recursion. Continuous self-similarity demands a continuous parameterization (\lambda\mapsto \mathcal{A}_\lambda) (often a semigroup) and is treated as a limit regime.
9.1.1.3 Scale index sets
A scale index set is a countable or measurable set (\mathcal{K}) indexing resolution levels. Two canonical forms are used:
Geometric scale ladder: (\epsilon_k=\epsilon_0\lambda_0^{-k}) for (\lambda_0>1), (k\in\mathbb{Z}) or (k\in\mathbb{N}).
Log-time lattice: (\tau_k=\tau_0 + k\Delta\tau) with (\Delta\tau=\log\lambda_0) and (t_k=e^{\tau_k}).
A scale-indexed family (T^{(k)}) represents coarse-grained or band-limited versions of a base signal (T). Formally, let (S_{\epsilon}) be a coarse-graining operator (e.g., low-pass filter, wavelet truncation, or conditional expectation on a partition). Define[T^{(k)} := S_{\epsilon_k}T.]For (\mathfrak{H})-valued data, (S_{\epsilon}) must be bounded on the relevant Bochner space and must respect constraints in the sense that either (S_{\epsilon}(\ker C)\subseteq \ker C) or is followed by a constraint projection (P_{\ker C}).
An index set is admissible if the induced family of transforms and projections is stable across scales, i.e., there exists (M<\infty) such that[\sup_k |S_{\epsilon_k}|<M,\qquad \sup_k |P_{\ker C}\circ S_{\epsilon_k}|<M.]
9.1.1.4 Canonical scaling parameters
A canonical scaling parameterization is a tuple[\Theta_{\mathrm{scale}} := (\lambda_0,\epsilon_0,\tau_0,\mathcal{A}_{\lambda_0},\mathcal{K})]satisfying:
(\lambda_0>1) and (\mathcal{K}\subseteq \mathbb{Z}) or (\mathbb{N}) is specified deterministically.
(\epsilon_k=\epsilon_0\lambda_0^{-k}) is used for all multi-resolution computations.
The operator (\mathcal{A}_{\lambda_0}) is fixed by gauge normalization (including phase and time-shift gauge) so that scale comparisons are coordinate-independent.
In finite models, (\lambda_0) is chosen so that the induced frequency bands align with the chosen FFT/wavelet grids (to avoid systematic aliasing at boundaries).
A canonical choice is the smallest (\lambda_0>1) (within a permitted set) that minimizes scale-boundary leakage under the selected discretization scheme, subject to admissibility corridors (stability and constraint preservation).
9.1.2 Fractal dimension of time
9.1.2.1 Hausdorff dimension definition
Let (E\subseteq\mathcal{T}) be a Borel set (e.g., the support of an event measure or the set of times where a thresholded observable holds). For (s\ge 0), define the (s)-dimensional Hausdorff measure:[\mathcal{H}^s(E)=\lim_{\delta\downarrow 0}\ \inf\left{\sum_{i}(\mathrm{diam},U_i)^s:\ E\subseteq\bigcup_i U_i,\ \mathrm{diam},U_i<\delta\right}.]The Hausdorff dimension is[\dim_H(E)=\inf{s:\mathcal{H}^s(E)=0}=\sup{s:\mathcal{H}^s(E)=\infty}.]
For signal graphs, let (T:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}) (or into a normed space). The graph is[\Gamma(T)={(t,T(t)):\ t\in[0,1]}\subseteq \mathbb{R}^{1+m}.]Define (\dim_H(\Gamma(T))) as above. When (T) is (\alpha)-Hölder, classical bounds yield (\dim_H(\Gamma(T))\le 2-\alpha); in general, the framework treats (\dim_H) as a measurable invariant to be estimated, not assumed.
For (\mathfrak{H})-valued (T), the graph is embedded via a finite feature map (\Phi:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathbb{R}^m) (canonicalized and fixed), and the dimension is defined for (\Gamma(\Phi\circ T)). The feature map (\Phi) is part of the reproducibility manifest.
9.1.2.2 Box-counting dimension definition
For bounded (E\subseteq\mathbb{R}), let (N(\epsilon)) be the smallest number of intervals of length (\epsilon) required to cover (E). The upper and lower box-counting dimensions are[\overline{\dim}B(E)=\limsup{\epsilon\downarrow 0}\frac{\log N(\epsilon)}{\log(1/\epsilon)},\qquad\underline{\dim}B(E)=\liminf{\epsilon\downarrow 0}\frac{\log N(\epsilon)}{\log(1/\epsilon)}.]When they coincide, the common value is (\dim_B(E)).
For empirical time series, (E) is typically obtained from a thresholding rule applied to an observable (y(t)=\mathcal{O}(H(t))), e.g.,[E_\theta={t:\ y(t)\ge \theta},]or from event timestamps ({t_i}) treated as a discrete set with a thickening rule (E_\epsilon=\bigcup_i [t_i-\epsilon/2,t_i+\epsilon/2]).
The box-counting estimator is canonical in finite models because it is directly computable from discretized time grids. It is treated as an operational dimension invariant with explicit bias and stability analysis.
9.1.2.3 Estimation procedures
Let sampled data be available on a grid ({t_n}_{n=0}^{N-1}) with spacing (\Delta t) (not necessarily uniform). Define a multi-scale family of bins (\epsilon_k) satisfying (\epsilon_k\ge \Delta t) and (\epsilon_k \to 0) over the admissible range (k=0,\dots,K).
Procedure A (box-counting on event set):
Construct event set (E) from data (threshold crossings, local maxima, zero-crossings, etc.) under deterministic rules.
For each (\epsilon_k), compute (N(\epsilon_k)) by counting non-empty bins in a partition of (\mathcal{T}).
Fit slope of (\log N(\epsilon_k)) vs (\log(1/\epsilon_k)) over a certified scaling window ([k_-,k_+]).
Procedure B (wavelet scaling of increments):Let (y(t)) be a real-valued observable. Define wavelet coefficients (W_y(a,b)) at scale (a) and location (b). For (q>0), define structure functions[S_q(a)=\int |W_y(a,b)|^q,db.]Scaling laws of the form (S_q(a)\asymp a^{\zeta(q)}) imply multifractal structure; a single-fractal dimension is obtained from (\zeta(q)) at chosen (q) or from (q=2) energy scaling.
Procedure C (graph dimension proxy):Estimate (\dim_B(\Gamma(y))) via box-counting in (\mathbb{R}^2) using the discretized curve ((t_n,y_n)) and a square grid at resolution (\epsilon_k). This is stable under time-shift gauge and amplitude normalization, provided canonicalization is applied.
Each procedure produces not only an estimate (\widehat D) but also a certificate containing: admissible (\epsilon)-range, regression residuals, and sensitivity to perturbations (noise, subsampling, window shifts).
9.1.2.4 Convergence and bias
Let (E) be the true set and (E_N) an estimator derived from (N) samples under a deterministic procedure. A dimension estimator (\widehat D_N) is consistent for (D) if (\widehat D_N\to D) in probability (or almost surely) as (N\to\infty) under the model class.
Bias sources are categorized as:
Discretization bias: finite (\Delta t) enforces (\epsilon\ge \Delta t), truncating the small-scale regime.
Finite-window bias: limited observation length (T_{\mathrm{obs}}) truncates large scales.
Noise thickening: additive noise expands event sets and inflates (N(\epsilon)) at small (\epsilon).
Rule bias: the event extraction rule may not be stable under coordinate changes unless canonicalized.
A certified scaling window ([k_-,k_+]) is admissible if:[\epsilon_{k_+}\gg \Delta t,\qquad \epsilon_{k_-}\ll T_{\mathrm{obs}},\qquad\text{and regression residual }R^2\text{ exceeds threshold}.]When noise variance (\sigma^2) is known or bounded, a lower cut (\epsilon_{k_+}\ge c\sigma) is enforced to avoid noise-dominated scales. When (\sigma) is unknown, it is estimated and included in the certificate with confidence bounds.
9.1.3 Period nets and lattices
9.1.3.1 Define base period sets
Let (y:\mathcal{T}\to\mathbb{C}) be an observable derived from the hologram (H) via a fixed, canonical (\mathcal{O}) (e.g., energy density in a band, a projection coefficient, or a gauge-invariant statistic). Define the continuous-time Fourier transform over an observation window (W\subseteq\mathcal{T}):[\widehat y_W(\omega)=\int_W y(t),e^{-i\omega t},d\nu(t),]with the convention (d\nu(t)=dt) unless a time density is required.
A base frequency set is a finite set (\Omega_0={\omega_1,\dots,\omega_m}\subset\mathbb{R}_+) extracted by a deterministic peak selection rule from (|\widehat y_W(\omega)|) or from a multi-resolution spectrum. The corresponding base period set is[\mathcal{P}_0={P_j=2\pi/\omega_j:\ \omega_j\in\Omega_0}.]Base sets may also be specified axiomatically (domain-driven) and then verified against data; in either case, the selection rule and tolerances are part of the reproducibility manifest.
Base elements are canonicalized by ordering (ascending (P)), amplitude tie-breakers, and tolerance clustering: periods within relative distance (\le \tau_P) are merged into a representative using amplitude-weighted averaging, with the merge operation deterministic.
9.1.3.2 Lattice construction rules
A period lattice is constructed from (\mathcal{P}_0) by closure rules encoding harmonic generation and scale recursion. Two equivalent constructions are used.
Construction 1 (multiplicative lattice in periods).Let (\mathbb{Q}_+) be positive rationals. Define the rational closure[\mathcal{L}P = \overline{{ q,P_j:\ q\in\mathbb{Q}+,\ P_j\in\mathcal{P}_0}},]where (\overline{\cdot}) denotes closure under the adopted tolerance and truncation corridor (bounded by admissible min/max periods).
Construction 2 (additive lattice in log-period).Let (u_j=\log P_j). Define integer span lattice[\mathcal{L}u=\left{\sum{j=1}^m n_j u_j:\ n_j\in\mathbb{Z}\right},]truncated to a bounded window ([u_{\min},u_{\max}]). Map back by (P=\exp u). This construction directly supports renormalization updates because scaling becomes translation.
A period net is the finite, graph-structured representation of the truncated lattice at a given resolution. Vertices are period nodes, and edges encode admissible generative relations, including:
harmonic relation (P\leftrightarrow kP) (integer (k)),
subharmonic relation (P\leftrightarrow P/k),
beat-derived relation (P_b) induced by (P_1,P_2) (defined in §9.1.4.2).
Edges store type tags, confidence scores, and drift bounds.
9.1.3.3 Period mixing and aliasing
Let observations be discrete with sampling interval (\Delta t) and sampling frequency (f_s=1/\Delta t). Frequencies (\omega) and (\omega') are observationally aliased if[\omega' \equiv \omega \ \ (\mathrm{mod}\ 2\pi f_s),]or, in linear frequency (f=\omega/2\pi), if (f'\equiv f \ (\mathrm{mod}\ f_s)), with the usual Nyquist restriction (|f|\le f_s/2) defining an identification interval.
Period mixing occurs when multiple base components generate intermodulation terms under nonlinear observation maps or under nonlinear dynamics. If (y(t)\approx \sum_j a_j e^{i\omega_j t}) and a quadratic nonlinearity is present, then frequencies (\omega_j\pm \omega_k) appear. More generally, under polynomial nonlinearity of degree (d), integer combinations up to order (d) appear:[\omega=\sum_{j=1}^m n_j\omega_j,\qquad \sum_j |n_j|\le d.]A period lattice is admissible only when its generation order and truncation bounds are explicitly specified, because otherwise the lattice is infinite and not computationally stable.
Aliasing is treated as an identifiability obstruction: distinct lattices can induce identical sampled spectra. The certificate of a period net must record the sampling regime and the induced alias classes for each node.
9.1.3.4 Canonical lattice IDs
A canonical lattice ID is a deterministic identifier for a period net/lattice object, invariant under permitted gauges and stable under small perturbations within corridor tolerances.
Let (\mathcal{N}) be a period net with vertex set (V) and edge set (E). Canonicalization proceeds:
Canonicalize base periods (\mathcal{P}_0) by deterministic clustering, ordering, and normalization (unit choice fixed).
Canonicalize vertex list by sorting primary key (\log P), secondary keys (confidence, amplitude, generation depth).
Canonicalize edges by sorting (type tag, source vertex ID, target vertex ID) with deterministic tie rules.
Serialize to a fixed byte format with explicit endianness, float quantization policy, and version tag.
Hash the serialization with a fixed cryptographic hash (or a deterministic non-cryptographic hash if mandated), producing (\mathrm{ID}(\mathcal{N})).
Equivalence under time-shift gauge and phase gauge is enforced by constructing (\mathcal{N}) from gauge-invariant spectral magnitudes or from canonically aligned phases. Any non-invariant features used for node annotation must not enter the hash.
9.1.4 Resonance signatures
9.1.4.1 Standing wave criteria
A standing wave signature in an observable (y(t)) is defined as a persistent, narrowband concentration of spectral energy around a set of frequencies (\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}_+) with stable phase relationships over time.
Let (W_1,\dots,W_L) be a partition of the observation window into subwindows. For each (\omega), define windowed complex amplitudes[A_\ell(\omega)=\int_{W_\ell} y(t)e^{-i\omega t},dt.]A standing wave at (\omega) is certified if:
Energy concentration: (|A_\ell(\omega)|) exceeds threshold for most (\ell).
Phase coherence: (\mathrm{Var}\ell(\arg A\ell(\omega))) is below threshold after canonical phase unwrapping.
Band isolation: the energy in a small band around (\omega) dominates adjacent bands by a specified ratio.
Stability: the inferred (\omega) drifts by at most (\delta_\omega) across subwindows.
These conditions are defined with explicit thresholds and produce a certificate including drift bounds and coherence scores.
9.1.4.2 Beat frequency rules
Given two oscillatory components with frequencies (\omega_1,\omega_2), the beat frequency is (\omega_b=|\omega_1-\omega_2|). In time-crystal lattices, beat relations are treated as generative edges in the net.
Formally, define a beat operator (\mathrm{Beat}) on frequency nodes:[\mathrm{Beat}(\omega_1,\omega_2)=|\omega_1-\omega_2|.]In periods, (P_b=2\pi/\omega_b) when (\omega_b>0). If (\omega_1\approx \omega_2), then (P_b) becomes large and may exceed the admissible window; such edges are truncated and recorded as “out-of-range beats.”
In finite observation windows, (\omega_b) is resolvable only if the window length (T_{\mathrm{obs}}) satisfies (T_{\mathrm{obs}}\gtrsim 2\pi/\omega_b). Beat edges therefore carry a resolvability certificate: a lower bound on (T_{\mathrm{obs}}) and a flag indicating whether the current data suffices.
9.1.4.3 Phase locking conditions
Let phases (\theta_j(t)) be extracted for components (\omega_j) via analytic signal methods or complex demodulation:[z_j(t)=y(t)e^{-i\omega_j t},\qquad \theta_j(t)=\arg(z_j(t)).]Two components are (p!:!q) phase-locked if the phase mismatch[\Delta_{p,q}(t)=p,\theta_1(t)-q,\theta_2(t)]remains bounded modulo (2\pi) and has small variation:[\mathrm{Var}{t\in W}\big(\mathrm{wrap}(\Delta{p,q}(t))\big)\le \tau_{\mathrm{lock}},]for coprime integers (p,q) in a bounded search range. The lock ratio (p:q) is chosen by minimizing the residual variation under deterministic tie-breaking.
Phase-locking is admissible only when the phase extraction pipeline is gauge-fixed (time-shift and global phase) and stable under noise. The lock certificate records (p,q), the residual, and robustness under resampling.
9.1.4.4 Resonance invariant definitions
A resonance signature is a structured invariant extracted from a period net that encodes commensurability, coherence, and stability. It is defined as[\mathrm{Res}(y)=\big(\Omega^\star,\ \mathcal{R},\ \kappa,\ \delta\big),]where:
(\Omega^\star) is the canonically selected set of resonant frequencies (standing-wave certified).
(\mathcal{R}) is the set of rational relations (p\omega_i\approx q\omega_j) and beat relations (\omega_k\approx|\omega_i-\omega_j|) with bounded denominators, each with a confidence score.
(\kappa) is a coherence vector (phase coherence, amplitude persistence, cross-window stability).
(\delta) is a drift budget vector (frequency drift, phase drift, beat drift) consistent with corridor semantics.
(\mathrm{Res}) is defined to be invariant under time-shift gauge and global phase gauge by construction. Under admissible transforms acting on time (Chapter 8 operators), resonance invariants evolve within certified drift budgets; the corresponding inequalities are formalized in §9.3.2.
9.2 Flower — Operators on Time Crystals
9.2.1 Period multiplication/division operators
9.2.1.1 Scaling operators on periods
Define period scaling operators (\mathcal{M}_a) for (a>0) by[(\mathcal{M}_a P)=aP,]acting on period nodes and extending to lattices by acting componentwise on their log-coordinates:[u=\log P \ \mapsto\ u+\log a.]On the time domain, (\mathcal{M}_a) corresponds to time dilation (t\mapsto at) when acting on signals (y(t)) (with the reciprocal action on frequency).
For period nets, (\mathcal{M}_a) is admissible only when:
the resulting periods remain within truncation bounds ([P_{\min},P_{\max}]),
alias constraints are not violated under the sampling regime,
drift budgets remain within corridor tolerances.
When (a) is rational (a=p/q), (\mathcal{M}_a) corresponds to harmonic/subharmonic relations and is represented as a typed edge generator.
9.2.1.2 Generator forms
In log-period coordinates (u=\log P), scaling is translation:[u\mapsto u+s,\quad s=\log a.]Thus (\mathcal{M}a) is generated by the derivative operator (\partial_u) in the sense[\mathcal{M}{e^s}=\exp(s,\partial_u),]interpreted on suitable function spaces over the period axis. This provides a canonical generator representation used by compiler layers: scale updates become exponentials of a fixed generator.
On frequency ( \omega = 2\pi/P), scaling acts as (\omega\mapsto \omega/a). The corresponding generator in (\log\omega) is (-\partial_{\log\omega}).
9.2.1.3 Composition laws
The operators satisfy:[\mathcal{M}_a\circ \mathcal{M}b=\mathcal{M}{ab},\qquad \mathcal{M}_1=I,\qquad (\mathcal{M}a)^{-1}=\mathcal{M}{1/a}.]On truncated nets, invertibility is partial because nodes pushed out of range are dropped; thus the inverse exists only as a partial inverse with a truncation certificate listing lost nodes.
Compositions on lattices are canonicalized by multiplying parameters and reducing to a normal form (a\in\mathbb{Q}_+) when possible, else storing (a) as a normalized floating parameter with bounded relative error.
9.2.1.4 Admissibility constraints
A scaling operator (\mathcal{M}_a) is corridor-admissible for a net (\mathcal{N}) if:
Range admissibility: (\mathcal{M}a(V)\subseteq [P{\min},P_{\max}]) up to allowed truncation fraction (\tau_{\mathrm{drop}}).
Stability: confidence scores and coherence (\kappa) do not degrade beyond (\delta)-budgets.
Identifiability: alias classes do not collapse distinct nodes beyond tolerance.
Constraint compatibility: if (\mathcal{N}) is derived from (H\in H^4), any induced operator on (H) (e.g., time dilation) must preserve (C(H)=0) in the sense of Chapter 8 admissibility.
Admissibility produces a certificate: node drop list, alias warnings, and quantitative drift bounds.
9.2.2 Interference operators
9.2.2.1 Beat construction operators
Define (\mathcal{B}) acting on a pair of frequencies:[\mathcal{B}(\omega_1,\omega_2)=\big(|\omega_1-\omega_2|,\ \omega_1+\omega_2\big),]with the second component included when nonlinear mixing is permitted by the observation model.
On a period net, (\mathcal{B}) induces candidate nodes and edges with types: (\mathrm{beat}) and (\mathrm{sum}). Each candidate is validated by resolvability and by observed spectral support. Validation is deterministic: a candidate is accepted if it reduces reconstruction residual in a local model (defined below) beyond threshold.
Local validation model: on a window (W),[y(t)\approx \sum_{j\in J} a_j e^{i\omega_j t},]with (J) including the candidate. Accept if the least-squares residual decreases by at least (\Delta_{\min}) and parameters remain stable under window splitting.
9.2.2.2 Phase mixing operators
Phase mixing is modeled as an operator acting on phase trajectories (\theta(t)). Define the mixing operator (\mathcal{P}\gamma) for (\gamma\in\mathbb{R}) by[(\mathcal{P}\gamma(\theta_1,\theta_2))(t)=\mathrm{wrap}(\theta_1(t)+\gamma,\theta_2(t)).]This models coupling where one component’s phase modulates another. The operator is admissible only when resulting phase trajectories remain sufficiently smooth (bounded variation) and do not induce unstable frequency drift beyond corridor budgets.
In practice, (\mathcal{P}_\gamma) is inferred by minimizing phase residuals against observed analytic-signal phases and is used to propose coupling edges in synchronization graphs.
9.2.2.3 Spectral filtering operators
Let (F) be a spectral filter acting on (y) by multiplication in frequency space:[\widehat{(F y)}(\omega)=\chi(\omega)\widehat y(\omega),]where (\chi) is a bounded measurable mask (ideal, soft, or wavelet-band). Filters define the band-limited observables used to build multi-scale nets.
Admissibility requires stability bounds:[|Fy|{L^2(\mathcal{T})}\le |\chi|{L^\infty}|y|_{L^2(\mathcal{T})},]and corridor constraints specify maximum allowed energy removal or concentration per band to prevent spurious fractality (over-filtering) or spurious resonance (ringing).
9.2.2.4 Stability and drift control
Interference operators are assessed by propagation of uncertainty. If frequencies are estimated with errors (|\widehat\omega_j-\omega_j|\le \eta_j), then beat errors satisfy:[\big||\widehat\omega_1-\widehat\omega_2|-|\omega_1-\omega_2|\big|\le \eta_1+\eta_2.]This inequality defines a canonical drift budget update rule for beat-generated nodes.
For phase locking, if (\theta_j) have perturbations bounded in (L^\infty) by (\eta_\theta), then mismatch (\Delta_{p,q}) perturbs by at most ((p+q)\eta_\theta), giving lock robustness thresholds.
These bounds are integrated into the net certificate and into operator rejection/repair logic.
9.2.3 Synchronization operators
9.2.3.1 Phase locking mechanisms
Define a synchronization operator on a set of (m) phase oscillators (\theta\in\mathbb{T}^m) by the flow:[\dot\theta_i = \omega_i + \sum_{j=1}^m K_{ij},\sin(\theta_j-\theta_i),\qquad i=1,\dots,m,]with coupling matrix (K) (not necessarily symmetric). This is a canonical formal model for time-crystal synchronization in the net: resonant components are treated as coupled oscillators.
A locking state is defined by existence of constants (\Omega) and (\phi_i) such that[\theta_i(t)=\Omega t+\phi_i + \xi_i(t),\qquad \sup_t|\xi_i(t)|<\infty,]with bounded deviation (\xi). The lock certificate records (\Omega), phase offsets (\phi), and boundedness estimates for (\xi).
9.2.3.2 Coupled oscillator formalism
Synchronization edges are induced by inferred couplings. Given observed phases (\theta_i(t)), estimate (K) by minimizing[\int_W \sum_i \left|\dot\theta_i(t)-\omega_i-\sum_j K_{ij}\sin(\theta_j(t)-\theta_i(t))\right|^2 dt]under regularization constraints (sparsity or smoothness of (K)). Discrete-time variants are used when phases are sampled.
The resulting coupling graph is integrated with the period net: nodes are frequencies/periods; edges represent inferred coupling. This yields a time-crystal lattice with coupling, a richer object that supports bifurcation diagnostics and control operations.
9.2.3.3 Bifurcation analysis templates
Let (K(\alpha)) depend on a scalar control parameter (\alpha). Define the locked set (\mathcal{L}(\alpha)) as the set of indices participating in stable phase-locking at (\alpha). A bifurcation occurs when stability changes; in practice, certify via eigenvalue crossings of the linearized dynamics around a locked solution.
Template:
Compute candidate locked solution (\theta^\star) from data or model.
Linearize to obtain Jacobian (J(\alpha)).
Certify stability if (\mathrm{Re},\lambda(J(\alpha))<-\gamma) for margin (\gamma>0).
Detect bifurcation when margin crosses zero beyond tolerance.
Certificates record numerical conditioning and discretization parameters to ensure reproducibility.
9.2.3.4 Control parameters
Control acts by modifying:
intrinsic frequencies (\omega_i),
coupling strengths (K_{ij}),
noise level (effective temperature),
external forcing terms (f_i(t)).
In the operator framework, these are encoded as admissible transforms acting on the time-crystal object rather than on raw (H). Control admissibility requires that induced transforms on (H) (if implemented at the hologram level) remain corridor-admissible with respect to constraints and invariants.
9.2.4 Multi-scale time evolution
9.2.4.1 Scale-coupled flows
Let (y^{(k)}=S_{\epsilon_k}y) be the multi-scale observables. A scale-coupled flow is a recursion:[\frac{d}{dt}y^{(k)} = F_k\big(y^{(k)}, y^{(k+1)}\big),]or, in discrete update form,[y^{(k)}_{n+1} = \Psi_k\big(y^{(k)}_n, y^{(k+1)}_n\big),]with stability constraints uniform in (k). The coupling models information transfer across scales: fine-scale dynamics influences coarse-scale resonances and vice versa.
This is the canonical dynamical substrate for “fractal time” in the lattice: evolution is not single-scale but governed by an explicit hierarchy.
9.2.4.2 Renormalization style updates
Define a renormalization operator (\mathcal{R}) mapping fine-scale structures to coarse-scale structures:[\mathcal{R}:\ \mathcal{N}^{(k+1)} \mapsto \mathcal{N}^{(k)},]where (\mathcal{N}^{(k)}) is the period net extracted at scale (\epsilon_k). (\mathcal{R}) is implemented as:
rescaling of frequencies/periods to a canonical scale,
merging nodes/edges within tolerance,
updating confidence/coherence metrics by deterministic aggregation.
A fixed point (\mathcal{N}^\star) of (\mathcal{R}) represents scale-invariant time-crystal structure. Approximate fixed points are those satisfying (d(\mathcal{R}\mathcal{N},\mathcal{N})\le \eta) under a chosen net metric.
9.2.4.3 Stability under scale recursion
Scale recursion is stable if the induced map on net features is a contraction or is bounded with controlled drift. Let (\mathbf{v}^{(k)}) be the feature vector representation of (\mathcal{N}^{(k)}) (canonicalized). Stability requires existence of constants (L<\infty) and drift (\delta_k) such that[|\mathbf{v}^{(k)} - \mathcal{F}(\mathbf{v}^{(k+1)})|\le \delta_k,\qquad|\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{u})-\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{v})|\le L|\mathbf{u}-\mathbf{v}|.]When (L<1), repeated renormalization converges; when (L\ge 1), admissibility corridors must bound accumulated drift.
Stability certificates are produced by empirical Lipschitz estimation under perturbations and by analytic bounds when filters and estimators admit them.
9.2.4.4 Efficient computation strategies
Efficiency requires multi-resolution computation:
Use wavelet packet or multirate filter banks to obtain (y^{(k)}) in (O(N)) or (O(N\log N)) time.
Extract spectral peaks using FFT per scale with short windows; reuse computations via overlap-add.
Build nets incrementally: (\mathcal{N}^{(k)}) initialized from (\mathcal{R}(\mathcal{N}^{(k+1)})) and then refined by scale-specific evidence.
Maintain streaming summaries for long time series: sketch-based spectral estimators, phase trackers, and incremental regression for dimension.
All computation steps are required to be deterministic under fixed seeds and fixed floating policies; certificates include full parameter logs.
9.3 Cloud — Bounds and Detectability
9.3.1 Detecting fractality under noise
9.3.1.1 Robust dimension estimators
Let observed signal be (y_{\mathrm{obs}}(t)=y(t)+\xi(t)) with noise (\xi) satisfying (|\xi|{L^2}\le \sigma) or a probabilistic bound. A dimension estimator (\widehat D) is robust if there exists (K(\sigma)\to 0) as (\sigma\to 0) such that[|\widehat D(y{\mathrm{obs}})-\widehat D(y)|\le K(\sigma)]with high probability.
Robustification strategies:
Use coarse scales (\epsilon\ge c\sigma) to avoid noise-dominated bins.
Use median-of-means regression across subwindows for slope estimation.
Use wavelet shrinkage before feature extraction with deterministic thresholds.
Use bias-corrected estimators calibrated on noise-only baselines.
Robustness is certified by perturbation experiments built into the test harness: inject synthetic noise within specified envelopes and verify bounded estimator drift.
9.3.1.2 False positive control
Define null model class (\mathcal{H}_0) consisting of non-fractal signals (e.g., smooth signals plus noise, or stationary short-memory processes) and alternative (\mathcal{H}_1) consisting of fractal-scale signals (e.g., signals with power-law scaling in increments).
A detection rule (\varphi) outputs “fractal” if a statistic (S(y)) exceeds threshold. False positive control requires[\sup_{y\in\mathcal{H}_0}\mathbb{P}(\varphi(y)=1)\le \alpha.]Operationally, (\mathcal{H}_0) is represented by a finite reference suite of generators, and (\alpha) is controlled by empirical quantiles with Bonferroni or FDR correction across multiple scales. The certificate records the null suite version and computed p-values.
9.3.1.3 Confidence intervals
Let (\widehat D) be obtained by linear regression on ((x_k,y_k)=(\log(1/\epsilon_k),\log N(\epsilon_k))) over a certified scaling window. Under standard regression assumptions for the selected window, a confidence interval is[\widehat D \pm t_{1-\alpha/2},\mathrm{SE}(\widehat D).]Because regression errors across scales are correlated, the framework treats this as an approximate interval and augments it with a bootstrap interval computed from window resampling. Deterministic bootstrap is implemented via fixed pseudo-random seeds and recorded.
The final confidence report includes: window, residual diagnostics, bootstrap distribution summary, and sensitivity to window shifts.
9.3.1.4 Certified detection thresholds
A threshold (\tau_D) for declaring fractality is certified if it satisfies:
Achieves specified false positive bound (\alpha) on the null suite.
Achieves specified power (1-\beta) on the alternative suite at target effect sizes.
Is stable under small parameter perturbations (binning offsets, window shifts, small changes in (\epsilon)-grid).
Certification output includes: suite versions, achieved (\alpha,\beta), and robustness margins.
9.3.2 Resonance stability bounds
9.3.2.1 Perturbation bounds on resonances
Let (\omega_i) be true frequencies and (\widehat\omega_i) estimates with (|\widehat\omega_i-\omega_i|\le \eta_i). For a rational relation (p\omega_i\approx q\omega_j), define resonance residual[r_{ij}^{p,q}=|p\omega_i-q\omega_j|.]Then the estimated residual satisfies[\big|\widehat r_{ij}^{p,q}-r_{ij}^{p,q}\big|\le p\eta_i+q\eta_j.]A resonance is certified if (\widehat r_{ij}^{p,q}\le \tau_{p,q}) and the right-hand bound ensures (r_{ij}^{p,q}\le \tau_{p,q}+p\eta_i+q\eta_j). Thresholds (\tau_{p,q}) are corridor parameters that can depend on denominators to penalize spurious high-order resonances.
9.3.2.2 Drift bounds under transforms
Let (T) be an admissible time operator acting on (y) (e.g., dilation, shear, filtering). Resonance drift is measured by[\Delta_{\mathrm{Res}} = d\big(\mathrm{Res}(y),\mathrm{Res}(T y)\big),]where (d) is a canonical metric on resonance signatures (e.g., matching distance on frequency sets plus weighted differences of coherence and relation sets). A corridor specifies budget (\delta_{\mathrm{Res}}) such that admissibility requires (\Delta_{\mathrm{Res}}\le \delta_{\mathrm{Res}}).
For dilation (t\mapsto at), frequencies scale (\omega\mapsto \omega/a), thus relations (p\omega_i\approx q\omega_j) are preserved exactly in the noiseless idealization; drift arises only from estimation error and truncation. Certificates explicitly separate:
analytic invariance component,
numerical estimation component,
truncation/alias component.
9.3.2.3 Lock/unlock conditions
Let (p:q) locking be detected with mismatch variance (V_{p,q}). Define lock margin[m_{p,q}=\tau_{\mathrm{lock}}-V_{p,q}.]A lock is stable under perturbation bounded by (\eta_\theta) if[m_{p,q} > c(p+q)\eta_\theta,]for a specified constant (c) determined by the phase unwrapping and variance functional. Unlock occurs when the margin becomes negative under perturbation or under operator-induced drift.
In coupled oscillator models, stability is further certified by a Jacobian margin (\gamma>0) (real parts of eigenvalues bounded away from zero). A lock is operationally accepted only when both phase-mismatch margin and Jacobian margin are satisfied.
9.3.2.4 Failure modes
Failure modes are classified and recorded:
Spurious resonance: caused by window leakage or spectral side-lobes; mitigated by tapering and cross-window validation.
Alias resonance: caused by sampling; detected by alias-class analysis and reported as non-identifiable unless additional data resolves it.
Transient locking: phase locking appears only in short subwindows; rejected unless persistence exceeds threshold.
Noise-induced coherence: coherence metrics inflated by preprocessing; mitigated by null-suite calibration.
Each failure mode has a deterministic detector and is included in the resonance certificate report.
9.3.3 Lattice identifiability limits
9.3.3.1 Minimal observation windows
To resolve two frequencies separated by (\Delta\omega), any windowed spectral estimator must observe for time (T_{\mathrm{obs}}) satisfying[T_{\mathrm{obs}}\gtrsim \frac{c}{\Delta\omega},]for a constant (c) depending on the estimator and window shape. This is treated as an operational identifiability bound: beat periods and near-commensurate resonances are not certified unless the observation window supports the required resolution.
For fractal dimension estimation, the effective scale range is bounded by (\Delta t) and (T_{\mathrm{obs}}), restricting the number of reliable decades of scaling. Certificates always report the usable scaling depth.
9.3.3.2 Aliasing impossibility results
Under uniform sampling at frequency (f_s), any two continuous-time frequencies (f) and (f+k f_s) induce identical discrete-time samples. Therefore, period lattices differing by such shifts are observationally indistinguishable without additional assumptions (bandlimit, prior bounds). This yields a no-go: unique lattice identification is impossible unless the admissibility corridor includes a bandlimit constraint or additional sensors.
The framework records this as an ambiguity class in the lattice certificate: each frequency node is associated with an equivalence class modulo aliasing.
9.3.3.3 Ambiguity class certificates
Given observed net (\widehat{\mathcal{N}}), define the set of compatible nets:[\mathcal{A}(\widehat{\mathcal{N}})={\mathcal{N}:\ \mathrm{Obs}(\mathcal{N})\approx \widehat{\mathcal{N}}\ \text{within tolerances}},]where (\mathrm{Obs}) is the observation-and-extraction pipeline. If (|\mathcal{A}(\widehat{\mathcal{N}})|>1), uniqueness fails. A certificate of ambiguity provides:
a canonical parameterization of (\mathcal{A}) (e.g., alias shifts, gauge variants),
minimal modifications that transform one compatible net to another,
conditions under which (\mathcal{A}) collapses to a singleton (additional data, tighter corridors).
9.3.3.4 Practical identifiability tests
Practical tests for identifiability:
Holdout stability: recompute net on disjoint subwindows; require consistent canonical ID up to allowed drift.
Resampling stability: subsample or jitter sampling; require net invariants remain within tolerance.
Model residual test: fit local oscillatory model; require residual below threshold and parameter stability.
Alias stress test: map nodes through alias equivalence and measure ambiguity; if large, mark as non-identifiable.
These tests are mandatory for “certified” nets and are included in regression suites.
9.3.4 No-go theorems for prediction
9.3.4.1 Limits under chaotic regimes
If the underlying phase dynamics has positive maximal Lyapunov exponent (\lambda_{\max}>0), then prediction error grows at least exponentially:[|\delta(t)|\gtrsim |\delta(0)|e^{\lambda_{\max} t},]for perturbations (\delta) in the relevant state/phase variables. In operational terms, any forecast beyond horizon (T_{\mathrm{pred}}\approx \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}\log\frac{\epsilon_{\mathrm{tol}}}{\epsilon_0}) cannot be certified at tolerance (\epsilon_{\mathrm{tol}}) given initial uncertainty (\epsilon_0).
Time-crystal lattices in chaotic regimes may still yield stable statistical invariants (e.g., distribution of periods, coarse-grained resonance counts), but not pointwise phase forecasts. Certificates must distinguish predictive claims: pointwise vs statistical.
9.3.4.2 Information-bounded prediction bounds
Let (\mathcal{I}) be the information budget extractable from observation under noise and sampling constraints (e.g., mutual information between past and future). If (\mathcal{I}) is finite and small relative to the model complexity required to forecast, then prediction beyond a horizon is information-theoretically limited. Operationally, if a family of futures remains consistent with past within observational corridors, unique prediction is impossible.
The framework encodes this as: if ambiguity class size remains above threshold under all admissible additional computations on the same data, then prediction claims must be downgraded to set-valued or distributional forecasts.
9.3.4.3 Adversarial counterexamples
For any finite observation window and finite resolution, there exist signals with distinct future behavior that match identically on the observed window while sharing the same extracted net within tolerances. Constructively, one may splice two signals that coincide on ([0,T_{\mathrm{obs}}]) and diverge afterward while preserving local spectral statistics up to a chosen order. Therefore, without additional structural assumptions (bandlimit, analytic continuation class, or dynamical model constraints), deterministic long-horizon prediction is not certifiable.
Certificates record the assumed structural class; absent such a class, only bounded claims are permitted.
9.3.4.4 Certified impossibility
An impossibility certificate is produced when one of the following holds:
alias ambiguity cannot be resolved under given sampling constraints,
required resolution exceeds available (T_{\mathrm{obs}}) by a computed bound,
chaos horizon bound is below target forecast horizon,
ambiguity class remains non-singleton under stability tests.
Impossibility certificates are first-class outputs: they are stored alongside nets and resonance signatures and used by downstream pipelines to prevent overcommitment beyond the corridor.
9.4 Fractal — Algorithms and Benchmarks
9.4.1 Period net extraction algorithms
9.4.1.1 Spectral peak extraction
Algorithm 9.4.1 (Deterministic Peak Extraction).Input: samples ((t_n,y_n){n=0}^{N-1}), windowing rule, frequency grid (\Omega), peak tolerance (\tau\omega), top-(m) budget.Output: base frequency set (\Omega_0) and certificate.
Canonicalize time grid (sort, deduplicate, enforce deterministic interpolation if needed).
Apply deterministic taper (w_n) (specified by name/version).
Compute discrete spectrum (\widehat y(\omega)) on (\Omega) (FFT if uniform, NUFFT otherwise).
Compute magnitude (S(\omega)=|\widehat y(\omega)|^2).
Find local maxima of (S) under deterministic neighborhood rule.
Cluster maxima within (\tau_\omega) (merge deterministically by max magnitude, then amplitude-weighted refine).
Select top (m) clusters by magnitude; set (\Omega_0) to their centers.
Produce certificate: (\Omega), taper, clustering parameters, noise estimates, and stability under window splitting.
Complexity: (O(N\log N)) for FFT, (O(N\log N + |\Omega|)) for standard implementations; NUFFT complexity depends on chosen kernel and accuracy.
9.4.1.2 Multi-resolution extraction
Algorithm 9.4.2 (Multi-Resolution Period Net Seed).Input: (y), scale ladder (\epsilon_k), band filters (\chi_k), peak extractor from 9.4.1.Output: initial period nets (\mathcal{N}^{(k)}) across scales.
For each (k):
Compute (y^{(k)} = F_{\chi_k} y).
Extract (\Omega_0^{(k)}) via Algorithm 9.4.1.
Construct base periods (\mathcal{P}_0^{(k)}).
Build truncated lattice and edges (harmonics, beats) up to specified order (d).
Store (\mathcal{N}^{(k)}) with certificates.
This yields a scale stack of nets supporting renormalization and stability checks.
9.4.1.3 Phase tracking
Algorithm 9.4.3 (Phase Tracker with Lock Diagnostics).Input: (y), resonant frequencies (\Omega^\star), demodulation bandwidth (b), smoothing parameter (\eta).Output: phase trajectories (\theta_j(t)), coherence (\kappa), lock candidates.
For each (\omega_j\in\Omega^\star), compute demodulated signal (z_j(t)=\mathrm{LPF}_b(y(t)e^{-i\omega_j t})).
Define (\theta_j(t)=\arg(z_j(t))) with deterministic unwrap rule.
Smooth (\theta_j) with a deterministic BV-regularized smoother (parameter (\eta)).
For bounded (p,q), compute mismatch (\Delta_{p,q}) and variance scores; select candidates.
Output phases and lock report with robustness tests under subwindow recomputation.
9.4.1.4 Complexity analysis
Period net extraction complexity is dominated by spectral estimation:
uniform sampling: (O(N\log N)) per scale,
multi-scale: (O(KN\log N)) naive, reduced via filter bank reuse to (O(N\log N)) total plus extraction overhead.
Graph construction for a net with (V) nodes:
harmonic edges up to (k_{\max}): (O(k_{\max}V)),
beat edges among nodes: (O(V^2)) naive; reduced to (O(V\log V)) using sorted frequency lists and bounded beat window.
All complexity bounds must be recorded in benchmark reports with actual measured constants and hardware metadata.
9.4.2 Fractal dimension estimation suite
9.4.2.1 Box counting implementation
Algorithm 9.4.4 (Box Dimension Estimator on Event Set).Input: event times ({t_i}) or event indicator (e_n) on grid, scale list (\epsilon_k), window ([k_-,k_+]).Output: (\widehat D_B), CI, certificate.
For each (\epsilon_k), partition (\mathcal{T}) into bins of size (\epsilon_k) deterministically aligned to (\tau_0).
Count non-empty bins (N(\epsilon_k)).
Compute regression slope of (\log N) on (\log(1/\epsilon)) over ([k_-,k_+]).
Compute CI by deterministic bootstrap on subwindows.
Output (\widehat D_B) and certificate (window, residuals, stability tests).
9.4.2.2 Wavelet-based estimation
Algorithm 9.4.5 (Wavelet Scaling Estimator).Input: (y), wavelet family (\psi), scales (a_k), moment (q), regression window.Output: scaling exponent (\zeta(q)), inferred dimension proxy, certificate.
Compute wavelet coefficients (W_y(a_k,b)).
Compute (S_q(a_k)=\int |W_y(a_k,b)|^q,db) numerically.
Regress (\log S_q(a_k)) on (\log a_k) to estimate (\zeta(q)).
Map (\zeta) to a dimension proxy per specified rule (fixed in manifest).
Validate by surrogate null data and perturbation tests.
9.4.2.3 Robust estimators
Robust estimators include:
trimmed regression (exclude outlier scales),
median-of-means on subwindows,
Huber regression on log-log data,
noise-calibrated scale selection ((\epsilon\ge c\sigma)).
Each robust estimator is deterministic given fixed parameters and produces a comparable certificate. The suite defines an aggregation rule: if estimators disagree beyond tolerance, the output is “non-certified” with an ambiguity report rather than a single dimension number.
9.4.2.4 Validation tests
Validation consists of:
Synthetic calibration: known fractal constructions with known dimension proxies.
Null calibration: smooth signals + noise; ensure false positive control.
Stability under resampling: subsample/jitter; bound estimator drift.
Scale-window sensitivity: shift ([k_-,k_+]); require stable slope.
These tests are integrated into continuous regression pipelines for the manuscript’s reference implementations.
9.4.3 Resonance detection suite
9.4.3.1 Locking detection
Algorithm 9.4.6 (Lock Detector).Input: phase trajectories (\theta_i(t)), search bounds (p_{\max},q_{\max}), threshold (\tau_{\mathrm{lock}}).Output: lock relations (\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{lock}}) with certificates.
For each pair ((i,j)), for each coprime ((p,q)) within bounds:compute (V_{p,q}=\mathrm{Var}(\mathrm{wrap}(p\theta_i-q\theta_j))).
Select ((p,q)) minimizing (V_{p,q}).
Accept if (V_{p,q}\le \tau_{\mathrm{lock}}) and robustness tests pass.
Record relation, residual, and confidence.
9.4.3.2 Beat detection
Beat detection uses the beat operator plus validation:
Compute candidate (\omega_b).
Check resolvability bound from window length.
Check spectral support at (\omega_b) across subwindows.
Accept and record confidence.
9.4.3.3 Confidence scoring
Confidence is computed from a weighted combination of:
persistence across windows,
coherence margin,
SNR margin,
robustness under resampling,
model residual improvement.
Weights are fixed and versioned. Scores are monotone in evidence and used to select canonical resonance sets (\Omega^\star).
9.4.3.4 Regression tests
Regression tests include:
fixed synthetic resonant constructions,
stress tests with controlled drift,
aliasing scenarios (ensure correct ambiguity flags),
numerical stability across float precisions.
Each regression test produces a deterministic report: detected resonances, mismatches vs ground truth, and failure mode classifications.
9.4.4 Reference datasets
9.4.4.1 Synthetic time crystals
A synthetic time-crystal generator produces signals with:
specified base frequencies (\Omega_0),
specified coupling (K),
specified noise model,
specified drift laws across scales.
Generator outputs both raw signal and ground-truth lattice structure for evaluation. Seeds and parameters are deterministic and serialized.
9.4.4.2 Known resonant constructions
Reference constructions include:
exact harmonic stacks,
exact rational lock families (p:q),
controlled beat hierarchies,
discretely self-similar log-periodic signals.
Each construction includes a proof certificate of its resonance relations and scaling properties under the adopted definitions.
9.4.4.3 Noise stress families
Noise families are specified by envelopes:
additive Gaussian (white/colored),
impulsive noise,
multiplicative amplitude noise,
phase noise (random walk).
Each stress dataset includes calibrated (\sigma) (or bounds) and ensures coverage of false-positive failure modes.
9.4.4.4 Reproducible evaluation
A reproducible evaluation protocol specifies:
exact preprocessing steps and versions,
exact parameter sets for extraction and estimation,
deterministic seed policies,
standardized outputs: net IDs, resonance signatures, dimension estimates, impossibility certificates, and performance metrics.
All evaluations produce audit logs suitable for external verification.
Chapter 9 — Fractal Time & Time-Crystal Lattices: Scaling, Resonance, Period Nets
Chapter 10 — Geometry of Transformations: Metrics, Connections, Curvature, Tensors
10.1 Square — Geometry on ((\mathbb{H}^4)) and ((\mathbb{D}^5))
This section fixes a canonical geometric setting in which states and transformations admit: (i) metrization, (ii) intrinsic differentiation via connections, and (iii) curvature/holonomy diagnostics of non-commutativity and obstruction. The “Square” refers to the choice of a four-dimensional intrinsic state geometry together with a bounded five-dimensional transformation parameter domain that is sufficiently expressive to expose nontrivial metric, connection, and curvature phenomena while remaining computationally implementable.
10.1.0 Global setting and objects
Let:
(\mathfrak{H}) denote the admissible state manifold, assumed to be a smooth connected (4)-manifold. In the canonical model of this section,[\mathfrak{H};\cong;\mathbb{H}^4,]the (4)-dimensional hyperbolic space of constant sectional curvature (-\kappa^2) (often (\kappa=1) by normalization).
(\mathcal{D}) denote the transformation parameter domain, assumed to be a smooth (5)-manifold with boundary. In the canonical model,[\mathcal{D};\cong;\mathbb{D}^5 ;=;{,\theta\in\mathbb{R}^5:|\theta|<1,},]equipped with a metric encoding transformation cost/complexity.
A transformation family is a smooth map[\Phi:\mathcal{D}\times \mathfrak{H}\to \mathfrak{H},\qquad (\theta,x)\mapsto \Phi_\theta(x),]where each (\Phi_\theta) is an admissible transformation (often a diffeomorphism onto its image; in group settings (\Phi_\theta\in \mathrm{Diff}(\mathfrak{H})) or in a finite-dimensional Lie subgroup). The differential with respect to (x) is (D_x\Phi_\theta:T_x\mathfrak{H}\to T_{\Phi_\theta(x)}\mathfrak{H}); the differential with respect to (\theta) is (D_\theta\Phi_\theta(x):T_\theta\mathcal{D}\to T_{\Phi_\theta(x)}\mathfrak{H}).
Constraints are encoded by a smooth map (c:\mathfrak{H}\to \mathbb{R}^m) (equality constraints) and/or inequality constraints (h:\mathfrak{H}\to \mathbb{R}^p) (with (h(x)\le 0)). The feasible state manifold is[\mathfrak{H}_{\mathrm{feas}}={x\in\mathfrak{H}:c(x)=0,; h(x)\le 0},]assumed to be a smooth embedded submanifold on the active set when regularity conditions hold.
Gauge structure is encoded by a Lie group (G) acting smoothly on (\mathfrak{H}) (and possibly on (\mathcal{D})) by (G\times \mathfrak{H}\to \mathfrak{H}), ((g,x)\mapsto g\cdot x). Physical/semantic equivalence is modeled as the quotient (\mathfrak{H}/G) when appropriate.
The remainder of (\S 10.1) constructs metrics on (\mathfrak{H}) and on transformation space, then defines compatible connections, and finally develops curvature/holonomy diagnostics.
10.1.1 State-space metrics
10.1.1.1 Riemannian metrics on ((\mathfrak{H})) (when admissible)
A Riemannian metric on (\mathfrak{H}) is a smooth assignment (x\mapsto g_x) where each (g_x) is an inner product on (T_x\mathfrak{H}). Admissibility of a metric in this manuscript means:
Smoothness: (x\mapsto g_x(u,v)) is smooth for all smooth vector fields (u,v).
Positive-definiteness: (g_x(u,u)>0) for all (u\neq 0).
Constraint compatibility (local): on (\mathfrak{H}{\mathrm{feas}}), the restriction (g|{\mathfrak{H}{\mathrm{feas}}}) is nondegenerate on tangent spaces (T_x\mathfrak{H}{\mathrm{feas}}) and yields stable projections (formalized in \S 10.1.1.4).
Transform-compatibility (optional but typical): the action (\Phi_\theta) is at least locally Lipschitz with respect to the induced distance (d_g), and preferably differentiably compatible (pushforward/pullback relations), enabling geometric optimization and curvature diagnostics.
Canonical model: (\mathfrak{H}\cong \mathbb{H}^4)
Use the Poincaré ball model: (\mathbb{H}^4={x\in\mathbb{R}^4:|x|<1}) with metric[g_x ;=;\lambda(x)^2,\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathbb{R}^4},\qquad\lambda(x) ;=;\frac{2}{1-|x|^2},]so that in coordinates (x=(x^1,\dots,x^4)),[g_{ij}(x)=\lambda(x)^2,\delta_{ij},\qquadg^{ij}(x)=\lambda(x)^{-2},\delta^{ij}.]
The associated hyperbolic distance admits the closed form
[
d_{\mathbb{H}}(x,y)
\operatorname{arcosh}!\left(1+\frac{2|x-y|^2}{(1-|x|^2)(1-|y|^2)}\right),]which is the canonical intrinsic notion of separation for state comparisons in (\mathbb{H}^4).
Geodesics and energy
Given a smooth curve (\gamma:[0,1]\to \mathfrak{H}), define length and energy:[L(\gamma)=\int_0^1 \sqrt{g_{\gamma(t)}(\dot\gamma(t),\dot\gamma(t))},dt,\qquadE(\gamma)=\frac12\int_0^1 g_{\gamma(t)}(\dot\gamma(t),\dot\gamma(t)),dt.]A (constant-speed) geodesic locally minimizes (L) and is a critical point of (E); it satisfies the geodesic equation[\nabla_{\dot\gamma}\dot\gamma=0,]where (\nabla) is the Levi–Civita connection of (g) (constructed in \S 10.1.3).
10.1.1.2 Information-weighted metrics
State geometry is frequently determined by what is observable or decision-relevant rather than by ambient coordinates. Let (\mathcal{Y}) be an observation space and (p(y\mid x)) a statistical model for observations given state (x\in\mathfrak{H}). The Fisher information metric on (\mathfrak{H}) is
[
g^{\mathrm{FIM}}_{x}(u,v)
\mathbb{E}_{y\sim p(\cdot\mid x)}\Big[\big\langle \nabla_x\log p(y\mid x),u\big\rangle,\big\langle \nabla_x\log p(y\mid x),v\big\rangle\Big],\qquad u,v\in T_x\mathfrak{H},]where (\nabla_x) denotes differentiation in a chosen chart, and the resulting bilinear form is interpreted intrinsically (chart-independently) as a tensor field provided regularity holds.
More generally, for any smooth positive semidefinite operator field (W_x:T_x\mathfrak{H}\to T_x\mathfrak{H}) that encodes sensitivity/priority, an information-weighted metric takes the form[g^{W}_x(u,v)=g_x(W_x u, v),]and is Riemannian when (W_x) is symmetric positive-definite relative to (g_x).
Natural-gradient compatibility
When optimization is performed over states with objective (J:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathbb{R}), the natural gradient direction (\mathrm{grad}_g J) is defined by[g_x(\mathrm{grad}_g J,,u)=dJ_x(u)\quad\forall u\in T_x\mathfrak{H},]which in coordinates becomes ((\mathrm{grad}_g J)^i=g^{ij}\partial_j J). Choosing (g=g^{\mathrm{FIM}}) yields geometry aligned with the local KL divergence:[\mathrm{KL}(p(\cdot\mid x),|,p(\cdot\mid x+dx))=\frac12,g^{\mathrm{FIM}}_x(dx,dx)+o(|dx|^2).]
Algorithm: Monte Carlo Fisher metric estimation
Assume (i) sampling from (p(\cdot\mid x)) is available and (ii) score vectors (\nabla_x\log p(y\mid x)) can be computed.
Input: state (x), sample count (N).Output: estimated Fisher matrix (\widehat{G}) representing (g^{\mathrm{FIM}}_x) in a chart.
For (k=1,\dots,N): sample (y_k\sim p(\cdot\mid x)).
Compute score (s_k=\nabla_x\log p(y_k\mid x)\in\mathbb{R}^4) (chart representation).
Return (\widehat{G}=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^N s_k s_k^\top).
Regularize as (\widehat{G}_\varepsilon=\widehat{G}+\varepsilon I) if needed, with (\varepsilon>0) chosen to satisfy numerical conditioning guarantees consistent with constraint compatibility (\S 10.1.1.4).
10.1.1.3 Gauge-quotient metrics
Suppose a Lie group (G) acts freely and properly on (\mathfrak{H}), so the quotient (\mathfrak{M}=\mathfrak{H}/G) is a smooth manifold and the projection (\pi:\mathfrak{H}\to \mathfrak{M}) is a principal (G)-bundle. A metric (g) on (\mathfrak{H}) induces a quotient metric on (\mathfrak{M}) once a horizontal distribution is specified.
Vertical and horizontal subspaces
At (x\in\mathfrak{H}), define the vertical subspace[\mathcal{V}_x=\ker(d\pi_x)\subset T_x\mathfrak{H},]which equals the tangent to the orbit (G\cdot x). Choose a (g)-orthogonal complement[\mathcal{H}_x=\mathcal{V}_x^{\perp_g}\subset T_x\mathfrak{H},\quadT_x\mathfrak{H}=\mathcal{V}_x\oplus \mathcal{H}_x.]For (u\in T_x\mathfrak{H}), denote by (u^{\mathrm{H}}) its horizontal projection.
Quotient metric
For (\bar{x}=\pi(x)\in\mathfrak{M}) and (\bar{u},\bar{v}\in T_{\bar{x}}\mathfrak{M}), choose horizontal lifts (u^{\mathrm{H}},v^{\mathrm{H}}\in \mathcal{H}x) satisfying (d\pi_x(u^{\mathrm{H}})=\bar{u}), (d\pi_x(v^{\mathrm{H}})=\bar{v}). Define
[
\bar{g}{\bar{x}}(\bar{u},\bar{v})
g_x(u^{\mathrm{H}},v^{\mathrm{H}}).]This is well-defined (independent of the representative (x) and of lift choices) under the standard invariance conditions: (g) must be (G)-invariant and (\mathcal{H}) must be chosen equivariantly (the orthogonal complement construction satisfies this when (g) is (G)-invariant).
Practical implication
Gauge-quotient metrics formalize the principle: distance and curvature should not respond to gauge reparameterization. Computationally, all differential quantities must be computed using horizontal projections (or with gauge-fixing plus invariance checks).
10.1.1.4 Metric compatibility with constraints
Constraints define feasibility manifolds and admissible motions. Metric compatibility here means: the metric and induced connection support stable, intrinsic projection onto feasible directions, and geodesic/gradient flows can be made constraint-preserving.
Equality constraints and induced metric
Let (c:\mathfrak{H}\to \mathbb{R}^m) be smooth with full rank Jacobian on the constraint set, so[\mathfrak{H}_c={x:c(x)=0}]is a smooth embedded submanifold of codimension (m). The induced metric on (\mathfrak{H}_c) is the restriction[g^{(c)}x = g_x\big|{T_x\mathfrak{H}_c\times T_x\mathfrak{H}_c}.]
Orthogonal projection onto feasible tangents
Let (J_c(x):T_x\mathfrak{H}\to\mathbb{R}^m) be the differential (dc_x). Define the (g)-adjoint (J_c(x)^{!}:\mathbb{R}^m\to T_x\mathfrak{H}) by
[
g_x(J_c^{!}\lambda,,u)=\langle \lambda,,J_c u\rangle_{\mathbb{R}^m}.
]
Then the (g)-orthogonal projector onto the tangent (T_x\mathfrak{H}_c=\ker(J_c)) is
[
P_x
I
J_c^{!},(J_c J_c^{!})^{-1},J_c,]where (J_c J_c^{!*}\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}) is invertible under the full-rank assumption.
This formula is the geometric core of constraint-compatible algorithms: it is chart-independent once the metric (g) is fixed.
Constrained (projected) Levi–Civita connection
Let (\nabla) be the Levi–Civita connection of (g) on (\mathfrak{H}). The intrinsic Levi–Civita connection (\nabla^{(c)}) on (\mathfrak{H}_c) satisfies, for tangent vector fields (X,Y) on (\mathfrak{H}c),[\nabla^{(c)}X Y = P(\nabla{\tilde{X}}\tilde{Y})\Big|{\mathfrak{H}_c},]where (\tilde{X},\tilde{Y}) are any smooth extensions to a neighborhood in (\mathfrak{H}) and (P) is the orthogonal projection onto (T\mathfrak{H}_c). This yields a connection that is torsion-free and metric-compatible with the induced metric (g^{(c)}).
Constraint-preserving geodesics
A curve (\gamma(t)\in\mathfrak{H}c) is a geodesic in (\mathfrak{H}c) if
[
\nabla^{(c)}{\dot\gamma}\dot\gamma = 0.
]
Equivalently, in (\mathfrak{H}) it satisfies a constrained Euler–Lagrange equation with a Lagrange multiplier (\lambda(t)\in\mathbb{R}^m):
[
\nabla{\dot\gamma}\dot\gamma
J_c^{!*}\lambda,\qquadc(\gamma)=0.]
Algorithm: one-step constraint-compatible state update
Given a proposed tangent update (u\in T_x\mathfrak{H}) (e.g., a gradient direction), define the feasible tangent direction (u_{\mathrm{feas}}=P_x u), then take an intrinsic step using a retraction (R_x:T_x\mathfrak{H}\to\mathfrak{H}):
(u_{\mathrm{feas}}\leftarrow P_x u).
(x^+\leftarrow R_x(\eta u_{\mathrm{feas}})) for step size (\eta>0).
If required, perform a correction (x^{++}\leftarrow \Pi_{\mathfrak{H}_c}(x^+)) by solving (c(x)=0) locally (Newton on constraints with metric-aware Jacobian conditioning).
The retraction may be chosen as the exponential map (R_x(v)=\exp_x(v)) when tractable, or as a second-order approximation stable in the chosen chart.
10.1.2 Transformation-space metrics
Let (\mathcal{T}) denote a transformation manifold. Two canonical realizations are used:
Parameter-domain realization: (\mathcal{T}=\mathcal{D}\cong \mathbb{D}^5) with transformations indexed by (\theta\in\mathcal{D}).
Group realization: (\mathcal{T}\subset \mathrm{Diff}(\mathfrak{H})) or (\mathcal{T}\subset G) a finite-dimensional Lie group acting on (\mathfrak{H}). In this case (\theta\mapsto \Phi_\theta) is a chart on (\mathcal{T}).
A metric on (\mathcal{T}) can be defined intrinsically, or induced from the action on (\mathfrak{H}).
10.1.2.1 Operator norm metrics
When transformations act linearly on a Hilbert space (or on tangent spaces), metrics can be defined via operator norms.
Right-invariant metric on a matrix Lie group
Suppose (\mathcal{T}) is a matrix Lie group (e.g., a subgroup of (\mathrm{GL}(n))). Let (\mathfrak{t}) be its Lie algebra. Fix an inner product (\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathfrak{t}}) on (\mathfrak{t}). Define a right-invariant Riemannian metric by
[
g_T(U,V)
\left\langleU T^{-1},V T^{-1}\right\rangle_{\mathfrak{t}},\qquad U,V\in T_T\mathcal{T}.]Common choices include:
Frobenius/Hilbert–Schmidt: (\langle A,B\rangle=\mathrm{tr}(A^\top B)),
Weighted variants (\langle A,B\rangle=\mathrm{tr}(A^\top W B)) for (W\succ 0).
This construction generalizes to infinite-dimensional operator groups when functional-analytic hypotheses guarantee smoothness.
Metric from differential action on states
For a diffeomorphism group acting on (\mathfrak{H}), an infinitesimal transformation is a vector field (v) on (\mathfrak{H}). An operator-norm-type metric can be defined by
[
|v|_{\mathcal{T}}^2
\int_{\mathfrak{H}} g_x(v(x),v(x)),\rho(x),d\mathrm{vol}_g(x),]with weighting density (\rho) selecting the region of state relevance. This yields an (L^2)-type (Sobolev variants possible) geometry on the transformation group.
10.1.2.2 Cost-induced metrics
A transformation metric is often best defined as the second-order structure of a cost functional or as an action-induced pullback.
Hessian metric from a cost
Let (C:\mathcal{D}\to \mathbb{R}) be a smooth cost on parameters (e.g., program length, energy, resource use). If (\nabla^2 C(\theta)) is positive-definite, define[g^{C}\theta(u,v)=u^\top \nabla^2 C(\theta),v,\qquad u,v\in T\theta\mathcal{D}\cong \mathbb{R}^5.]This produces a local geometry in which geodesics correspond to least-cost deformation paths consistent with the second-order approximation of (C).
Pullback metric from state action
Fix a reference distribution (\rho) on states and a base metric (g) on (\mathfrak{H}). Define the action differential (J_\theta(x)=D_\theta\Phi_\theta(x)). The induced metric on parameters is
[
g^{\Phi}_\theta(u,v)
\int_{\mathfrak{H}}g_{\Phi_\theta(x)}!\big(J_\theta(x)u,;J_\theta(x)v\big),\rho(x),d\mathrm{vol}_g(x).]This metric assigns transformation directions large norm when they cause large state displacement in regions weighted by (\rho).
Algorithm: stochastic pullback metric estimation
Input: (\theta), sample count (N), state sampler (x_k\sim \rho).Output: estimated (5\times 5) matrix (\widehat{G}_\theta).
For (k=1,\dots,N): sample (x_k\sim \rho).
Compute (y_k=\Phi_\theta(x_k)).
Compute Jacobian (J_k = D_\theta \Phi_\theta(x_k)\in\mathbb{R}^{4\times 5}) in local charts.
Compute state metric matrix (G_y\in\mathbb{R}^{4\times 4}) representing (g_{y_k}).
Accumulate (\widehat{G}\theta \leftarrow \widehat{G}\theta + J_k^\top G_y J_k).
Return (\widehat{G}_\theta/N) (optionally regularized).
This metric is canonical for “transformation-space optimization”: it makes the geometry of transformation parameters reflect actual induced motion on (\mathfrak{H}).
10.1.2.3 Path length and geodesics
Given a Riemannian metric (h) on (\mathcal{T}), the length and energy of a transformation path (\theta(t)) are[L(\theta)=\int_0^1 \sqrt{h_{\theta(t)}(\dot\theta(t),\dot\theta(t))},dt,\qquadE(\theta)=\frac12\int_0^1 h_{\theta(t)}(\dot\theta(t),\dot\theta(t)),dt.]Geodesics satisfy (\nabla^{\mathcal{T}}_{\dot\theta}\dot\theta=0), where (\nabla^{\mathcal{T}}) is the Levi–Civita connection of (h).
In local coordinates (\theta^\alpha) ((\alpha=1,\dots,5)), the geodesic equation is
[
\ddot\theta^\alpha + \Gamma^\alpha_{\beta\gamma}(\theta),\dot\theta^\beta \dot\theta^\gamma=0,
\qquad
\Gamma^\alpha_{\beta\gamma}
\frac12,h^{\alpha\delta}\big(
\partial_\beta h_{\gamma\delta}
+
\partial_\gamma h_{\beta\delta}
\partial_\delta h_{\beta\gamma}\big).]
Canonical special case: Euclidean (\mathbb{D}^5)
If (h) is the Euclidean metric restricted to (\mathbb{D}^5), geodesics are straight lines (\theta(t)=\theta_0+t(\theta_1-\theta_0)) (while remaining inside (\mathbb{D}^5)); if boundary constraints are active, the admissible geodesic becomes a constrained variational problem.
10.1.2.4 Equivalence under reparameterization
Let (\theta:[0,1]\to\mathcal{T}) be a path and let (\varphi:[0,1]\to[0,1]) be a smooth increasing diffeomorphism with (\varphi(0)=0), (\varphi(1)=1). Then the reparameterized path (\tilde\theta(t)=\theta(\varphi(t))) satisfies[L(\tilde\theta)=L(\theta),]while (E(\tilde\theta)) generally changes unless (\theta) is parameterized with constant speed. Thus, for uniqueness and numerical stability, geodesics are typically normalized by constant-speed parameterization:[h_{\theta(t)}(\dot\theta(t),\dot\theta(t))=\text{constant}.]This property underpins stable discretizations and fair comparisons of transformation programs: length is intrinsic; time-parameterization is a gauge.
10.1.3 Connections and covariant derivatives
10.1.3.1 Define connection on state bundles
Let (E\to \mathfrak{H}) be a smooth vector bundle (e.g., (E=T\mathfrak{H}), a feature bundle, or an associated bundle under a gauge group). A connection on (E) is a map[\nabla:\Gamma(E)\to \Gamma(T^*\mathfrak{H}\otimes E)]satisfying for all smooth functions (f) and sections (s):[\nabla(fs)=df\otimes s + f\nabla s.]
