A projection-based decoder is[H^{(t+1)} ;=; \Pi{\mathcal{A}m}\circ \cdots \circ \Pi{\mathcal{A}_1}(H^{(t)}),]with projections realized as exact metric projections when convex, and as deterministic approximate projections when nonconvex.
The foundational convergence template is: if the composed operator is a contraction on a closed subset of (\mathfrak{H}), then (H^{(t)}) converges to a unique fixed point (H^*) satisfying all constraints.
13.2.1.4 Hybrid decoding
A hybrid decoder combines: (i) coarse localization by syndromes, (ii) local correction injection, (iii) global variational refinement, and (iv) canonicalization and corridor enforcement at each iteration. The canonical hybrid iteration is[H^{(t+1)} ;=; \mathrm{Can}\Big(\mathcal{R}{\mathrm{refine}}\circ \mathcal{I}{\mathrm{inject}}\circ \mathcal{L}{\mathrm{localize}}\big(H^{(t)},y\big)\Big),]where (\mathcal{L}{\mathrm{localize}}) returns a localized error model, (\mathcal{I}{\mathrm{inject}}) applies a correction supported on the localized region, and (\mathcal{R}{\mathrm{refine}}) performs a global constrained optimization step. Determinism requires fixed ordering, fixed random seeds (if any), and fixed numeric tolerances.
13.2.2 Redundancy-driven reconstruction
13.2.2.1 Multi-region reconstruction
Let ({A_j}{j=1}^J) be a family of observation regions or masks, with associated observations (y_j=\mathcal{O}{A_j}H+\xi_j). A redundancy-driven reconstruction aggregates multiple partial views by solving[\widehat{H} ;\in; \arg\min_{G\in \mathcal{A}}\sum_{j=1}^J \omega_j,\mathsf{d}j(\mathcal{O}{A_j}G,y_j),]with weights (\omega_j\ge0) chosen deterministically. In frame-coded representations, the ({A_j}) are selected so that the union of retained coefficients forms a stable subframe, yielding explicit lower bounds on recoverability.
13.2.2.2 Adaptive mask selection
An adaptive strategy selects subsequent observation regions (A_{t+1}) as deterministic functionals of current syndrome and uncertainty estimates:[A_{t+1} ;=; \mathsf{Select}\Big(\mathrm{SynReport}(H^{(t)}),;\mathrm{Unc}(H^{(t)}),;\delta\Big),]where (\mathrm{Unc}) returns a canonical uncertainty map (e.g., posterior variance proxy, residual sensitivity, or frame coefficient instability), and (\delta) is the corridor budget vector guiding exploration.
Admissibility requires that the selection rule itself be reproducible and that each acquired region meets declared safety and feasibility guards.
13.2.2.3 Error localization
Localization is formalized as constructing a support estimate (\widehat{\Gamma}\subseteq \Omega\times\mathcal{P}) or an index estimate (\widehat{\Lambda}\subseteq\mathcal{K}) such that the error is concentrated in (\widehat{\Gamma}) or (\widehat{\Lambda}). A canonical localization operator is[\widehat{\Lambda} ;=; \arg\max_{\Lambda\in\mathcal{L}} ;\mathcal{E}(\Lambda; s_{\mathrm{local}}),]where (\mathcal{L}) is a declared family of admissible index sets (e.g., connected blocks in a multiscale tree) and (\mathcal{E}) is a deterministic score such as energy concentration of residual coefficients, check violations, or instability under small perturbations.
Localization is certified when it yields a decrease in syndrome norm under a bounded correction step.
13.2.2.4 Correction injection
Given a localization (\widehat{\Gamma}), a correction injection operator (\mathcal{J}{\widehat{\Gamma}}) constructs (\Delta H) supported on (\widehat{\Gamma}) (or localized in coefficient blocks) by solving a local constrained problem:[\Delta H ;\in; \arg\min{\Delta\in \mathfrak{H}}\Big(|\mathcal{K}(H_{\mathrm{obs}}+\Delta)|{\mathcal{S}}^2+|\mathcal{O}(H{\mathrm{obs}}+\Delta)-y|^2\Big)\quad \text{s.t. } \mathrm{supp}(\Delta)\subseteq \widehat{\Gamma}.]The healed candidate is (H_{\mathrm{cand}}=H_{\mathrm{obs}}+\Delta H), followed by projection onto (H^4) and canonicalization.
13.2.3 Stability and convergence of healing
13.2.3.1 Contraction criteria
Let (\mathcal{D}:H^4\to H^4) denote one full healing iteration (including corridor checks and canonicalization). Healing is contractive on a closed set (\mathcal{U}\subseteq H^4) if there exists (0<L<1) such that[|\mathcal{D}(H)-\mathcal{D}(G)|{\mathfrak{H}} ;\le; L|H-G|{\mathfrak{H}}\qquad\forall,H,G\in\mathcal{U}.]Then (\mathcal{D}) has a unique fixed point (H^\in\mathcal{U}) and (H^{(t)}\to H^) for any initial state in (\mathcal{U}). A sufficient condition is strict decrease of a strongly convex Lyapunov functional.
13.2.3.2 Lyapunov criteria
Let (\mathcal{L}:H^4\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge0}) be a Lyapunov functional satisfying:
(\mathcal{L}(H)=0) iff (H\in\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{hol}}) and (\mathcal{O}H=y) within tolerance;
(\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{D}(H))\le \mathcal{L}(H)-\sigma(\mathcal{L}(H))) for a strictly positive function (\sigma) on ((0,\infty)).
Then (\mathcal{L}(H^{(t)})) decreases monotonically, and convergence to the feasible set follows under standard compactness or coercivity assumptions. A canonical Lyapunov choice is a weighted sum of constraint, check, and observation residual energies, augmented by corridor penalty terms.
13.2.3.3 Stop conditions
A deterministic termination rule is defined as the first iterate (t) such that all are satisfied:[|C(H^{(t)})|\le\tau_C,\quad |\mathcal{K}H^{(t)}|\le\tau_K,\quad\mathsf{d}(\mathcal{O}H^{(t)},y)\le\tau_O,\quad|\mathrm{Inv}(H^{(t)})-\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{ref}})|_\infty\le\delta,]or the first iterate at which the decrease in Lyapunov functional falls below a declared tolerance for a declared number of consecutive iterations.
If early abort occurs due to corridor violation, the output must be a certified failure report rather than an uncertified hologram state.
13.2.3.4 Failure detection
A failure detector is a deterministic predicate (\mathsf{Fail}(H^{(t)},\mathrm{log}^{(t)})) triggered by:
Residual non-decrease over a declared window;
Growth of invariant drift beyond budget;
Detection of ill-conditioning (e.g., exploding step sizes, unstable projections);
Inconsistency across redundant reconstructions (e.g., high disagreement between region reconstructions).
Failure classification must map to the global diagnostic taxonomy: erasure insufficiency, noise blow-up, phase ambiguity, adversarial non-identifiability, or numerical instability.
13.2.4 Integration with ((D^5))
13.2.4.1 Healing as admissible transforms
A healing transform is an element (T_{\mathrm{heal}}\in D^5) acting on (H^4) such that:
Constraint preservation: (T_{\mathrm{heal}}(H^4)\subseteq H^4);
Corridor preservation: (\mathrm{Inv}(T_{\mathrm{heal}}H)\in \mathrm{Inv}(H)\pm \delta) for declared budgets;
Reconstruction improvement: the reconstruction error functional decreases: (\mathsf{d}(\mathcal{O}T_{\mathrm{heal}}H,y)\le \mathsf{d}(\mathcal{O}H,y)) up to tolerance.
Iterative healing is represented as a program (T_{\mathrm{heal}} = T_m\circ \cdots \circ T_1) with stage-wise certificates. The program normal form is required to be canonically serializable and hash-identifiable.
13.2.4.2 Healing cost models
Healing is optimized under a cost functional[\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{heal}}(T) ;=; \alpha,\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{comp}}(T) ;+; \beta,\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{drift}}(T) ;+; \gamma,\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{misfit}}(T),]where (\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{comp}}) quantifies computational resources, (\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{drift}}) quantifies invariant drift, and (\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{misfit}}) quantifies residual observation mismatch. The trade-off surface is declared and the chosen point is recorded as part of the certificate.
13.2.4.3 Healing program normal forms
A canonical healing program is decomposed into four typed blocks:[T_{\mathrm{heal}} ;=; \mathrm{Can}\circ \Pi_{H^4}\circ \mathcal{U}\circ \mathcal{E},]where (\mathcal{E}) performs error estimation/localization, (\mathcal{U}) performs correction and refinement, (\Pi_{H^4}) enforces holographic feasibility, and (\mathrm{Can}) fixes gauge and serialization. Each block admits a declared intermediate representation (IR) with deterministic rewrite rules and a unique serialization.
13.2.4.4 Healing certificates
A healing certificate is a structured object containing:
Program hash and version identifiers;
Stage-wise residual logs for (C), (\mathcal{K}), and (\mathcal{O});
Invariant drift trace (\mathrm{Inv}(H^{(t)}));
Conditioning and stability estimates;
Deterministic seeds, numerical precision settings, and build manifest.
A continuous-time healing model adopted as a canonical reference is the semigroup form[H_{\mathrm{healed}}(\tau) ;=; e^{-\tau L_{\mathrm{heal}}},H_{\mathrm{damaged}},\qquadL_{\mathrm{heal}} ;=; -\nabla^2 ;+; V_{\mathrm{reconstruct}}[H],]interpreted as a dissipative smoothing-plus-potential operator that drives the state toward reconstructable feasibility.
13.3 Cloud — Guarantees and Limits
13.3.1 Correctable region bounds
13.3.1.1 Minimum observation thresholds
Let (\mathcal{O}:H^4\to \mathcal{Z}) be the observation operator and (\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{hol}}\subseteq H^4) the code space. A necessary and sufficient condition for unique recovery (up to gauge) in the noiseless case is:[\mathcal{O}H_1=\mathcal{O}H_2 ;\Rightarrow; H_1\sim{\mathcal{G}} H_2\quad \text{for all } H_1,H_2\in\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{hol}}.]Equivalently, (\ker(\mathcal{O}|{\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{hol}}})) equals the gauge-null directions. This defines the minimum observation threshold in operator-theoretic form.
For erasures under a frame representation, a computable sufficient condition is that the retained subframe has strictly positive lower frame bound (A_\Lambda>0). The minimum observation threshold is then the smallest (|\Lambda|) over admissible erasure families such that (A_\Lambda>0).
13.3.1.2 Maximum erasure rates
Define an erasure family (\mathcal{E}q) as the set of masks that erase at most fraction (q) of coefficients (or spatial mass). The maximum correctable erasure rate (q^) is[q^ ;:=; \sup{q\in[0,1):\ \inf{\Lambda\in\mathcal{E}q}A\Lambda>0}.]A positive lower bound on (q^) follows from overcompleteness and coherence properties of the frame; tight frames with low mutual coherence maximize (q^) under fixed redundancy.
13.3.1.3 Noise thresholds
Under additive observation noise (y=\mathcal{O}H+\xi), a decoder (\mathcal{D}) achieves a noise stability guarantee if[|\mathcal{D}(y)-H|{\mathfrak{H}} ;\le; \kappa,|\xi|{\mathcal{Z}}]for a finite constant (\kappa) depending on (\mathcal{O}) restricted to (\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{hol}}) and on the frame/check conditioning.
A normalized signal-to-noise threshold used as an operational corridor guard is[\mathrm{SNR}[H] ;>; \mathrm{SNR}_{\mathrm{crit}} ;=; \frac{2\pi}{e},]interpreted as the condition for maintaining coherence of code-structured holograms under the adopted SNR definition.
13.3.1.4 Certified correction bounds
A certified correction bound is a deterministic statement of the form:[|H-\widehat{H}|{\mathfrak{H}} ;\le; \mathcal{B}\big(\varepsilon,\delta,\kappa,\mathrm{cond}(\mathcal{O}),A\Lambda,B_\Lambda\big),]where (\varepsilon) is the observation noise bound, (\delta) is the invariant budget, (\kappa) is the algorithmic stability constant, and ((A_\Lambda,B_\Lambda)) are subframe bounds. The bound is accepted only if all constants are computed or upper-bounded by reproducible procedures and recorded in the certificate.
13.3.2 Impossibility regimes
13.3.2.1 Phase retrieval limits
If the observation operator discards phase information (e.g., magnitude-only measurements), then non-identifiability arises whenever there exist distinct (H_1,H_2\in\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{hol}}) such that (|\mathcal{O}H_1|=|\mathcal{O}H_2|). A certified impossibility statement is produced by constructing an explicit ambiguity family (e.g., conjugate-reflection pairs, phase-wrapped solutions, or gauge-twisted solutions) contained within corridor budgets.
13.3.2.2 Underdetermined reconstruction limits
If (\dim(\mathcal{Z})) is insufficient relative to the intrinsic degrees of freedom of (\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{hol}}/\mathcal{G}), then uniqueness fails. A functional impossibility certificate is:[\exists,0\neq \Delta H\in \mathcal{T}H(\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{hol}})\ \text{with}\ \mathcal{O}\Delta H=0\quad\Rightarrow\quadH \text{ not locally identifiable}.]Here (\mathcal{T}_H(\cdot)) denotes an appropriate tangent or feasible direction set (depending on regularity).
13.3.2.3 Adversarial limits
Under adversarial perturbations bounded only by energy, no nontrivial correction guarantee can exceed the radius at which error balls of distinct codewords overlap under observation. Certified impossibility is produced by exhibiting (H_1,H_2\in\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{hol}}) and (e_1,e_2\in\mathcal{E}) such that[\mathcal{O}(H_1+e_1)=\mathcal{O}(H_2+e_2),]with both candidates satisfying corridor constraints, thus proving that any decoder must err on at least one instance.
13.3.2.4 Certified impossibility
A certified impossibility report contains:
Explicit ambiguity constructions or null directions;
Verification that constructed examples lie in (H^4) (and (\ker\mathcal{K}) if relevant);
Verification that observation outputs coincide within declared tolerances;
A canonicalization step ensuring the ambiguity is not merely gauge;
Deterministic serialization of the construction parameters and verification logs.
13.3.3 Stability under repeated healing
13.3.3.1 Error accumulation bounds
Let (H^{(t+1)}=\mathcal{D}t(H^{(t)},y)) be a sequence of healing steps with possibly time-varying decoder maps. Suppose each step is stable in the sense that[|H^{(t+1)}-H^*|{\mathfrak{H}} ;\le; L_t|H^{(t)}-H^|_{\mathfrak{H}} + b_t,]where (H^) is a target feasible state, (0\le L_t<1), and (b_t) is a bias term capturing modeling and discretization error. Then[|H^{(t)}-H^| ;\le; \Big(\prod_{j=0}^{t-1}L_j\Big)|H^{(0)}-H^|;+;\sum_{k=0}^{t-1}\Big(\prod_{j=k+1}^{t-1}L_j\Big)b_k,]giving a certified accumulation bound when the products and sums are bounded deterministically.
13.3.3.2 Drift under correction loops
Repeated healing may introduce systematic drift in invariants even while decreasing observation misfit. Drift is controlled by enforcing corridor checks at each iteration and by using correction steps constrained in invariant directions:[\langle \nabla \mathrm{Inv}_\ell(H^{(t)}),\ \Delta H^{(t)}\rangle \approx 0 \quad \text{for protected invariants (\ell)}.]Certified drift control requires logging invariant increments and halting when cumulative drift approaches budget.
13.3.3.3 Overfitting to noise
Overfitting is formalized as a regime where (\mathsf{d}(\mathcal{O}H^{(t)},y)) continues to decrease while (|H^{(t)}|{\mathfrak{H}}) or a smoothness penalty (\mathcal{R}(H^{(t)})) increases beyond admissible bounds, or where cross-mask validation error increases. A deterministic overfitting detector uses held-out masks ({A{\mathrm{val}}}) and compares reconstruction residuals across train/validation masks.
13.3.3.4 Guard rails
Guard rails are enforced by:
Maximum iteration counts per corridor level;
Monotonicity constraints on Lyapunov functionals;
Minimum step-size and maximum step-size policies;
Early abort on conditioning blow-ups;
Mandatory re-canonicalization at declared intervals to prevent gauge drift accumulation.
All guard rail triggers must be logged and serialized into the certificate.
13.3.4 Corridor integration
13.3.4.1 Healing within invariant budgets
Define the invariant budget vector (\delta\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge0}) and require, at every healing step,[|\mathrm{Inv}(H^{(t)})-\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{ref}})| ;\preceq; \delta.]If a candidate update violates the budget, the repair policy is to project the update onto the tangent space of protected invariants or to reduce step size deterministically until compliance is restored.
13.3.4.2 Healing within reconstruction budgets
Let the reconstruction budget (\varepsilon(A)) be the permitted error envelope for region (A). Healing is admissible only if[|R_A(H^{(t)}|_A)-H^{(t)}| ;\le; \varepsilon(A)]for all declared critical regions (A). Violations trigger either (i) localized correction injection targeted at the violated region, or (ii) rejection of the candidate transform.
13.3.4.3 Automatic corridor tightening
Tightening is defined as a deterministic update rule (\delta_{t+1}=\mathsf{Tighten}(\delta_t,\mathrm{log}^{(t)})) that decreases budgets when healing stabilizes (residuals consistently low) and increases budgets only under explicit declared conditions (e.g., external measurement changes). Tightening must be monotone unless an explicit exception certificate is produced.
13.3.4.4 Audit logs
An audit log is an append-only deterministic record containing:
Observations and their hashes;
Decoder program hash;
Iteration-wise residuals (|C(H^{(t)})|), (|\mathcal{K}H^{(t)}|), (\mathsf{d}(\mathcal{O}H^{(t)},y));
Invariant drift vectors;
Guard rail events and reasons;
Final certification or impossibility certificate.
13.4 Fractal — Implementations and Tests
13.4.1 Decoder implementations
13.4.1.1 Projection decoders
A canonical alternating-projection decoder in truncated space (H^{(N,P)}) is specified by the iteration:
Algorithm 13.1 (Alternating Projection Decoder).Input: observation (y), operators (\mathcal{O},C,\mathcal{K}), canonicalization (\mathrm{Can}), tolerances (\tau_C,\tau_K,\tau_O), max iterations (T_{\max}).Initialize: (H^{(0)}:=\mathrm{Can}(\mathcal{O}^\dagger y)).For (t=0,\dots,T_{\max}-1):
(H^{(t+\frac{1}{3})} := \Pi_{{G:\mathsf{d}(\mathcal{O}G,y)\le\tau_O}}(H^{(t)})).
(H^{(t+\frac{2}{3})} := \Pi_{\ker(\mathcal{K})}(H^{(t+\frac{1}{3})})).
(H^{(t+1)} := \mathrm{Can}(\Pi_{H^4}(H^{(t+\frac{2}{3})}))).
If certification conditions hold, terminate and return (\widehat{H}=H^{(t+1)}).Output: (\widehat{H}) or failure certificate.
Each projection must be implemented as a deterministic solver with declared tolerances and deterministic stopping rules.
13.4.1.2 Optimization decoders
A canonical proximal-gradient decoder solves the regularized program[\min_{H\in H^4}\ |\mathcal{O}H-y|^2 + \lambda \mathcal{R}(H) + \mu|\mathcal{K}H|^2,]using deterministic step schedules and proximal kernels. Constraint enforcement (\Pi_{H^4}) may be implemented as: (i) exact projection in linear constraints, (ii) penalty continuation, or (iii) augmented Lagrangian updates, provided each variant yields a reproducible certificate trace.
13.4.1.3 Hybrid decoders
A hybrid decoder combines:
syndrome-based localization (\widehat{\Gamma});
local correction injection (\Delta H) supported on (\widehat{\Gamma});
global variational refinement;
corridor enforcement and canonicalization.
The hybrid implementation is required to serialize intermediate artifacts: (\widehat{\Gamma}), local correction coefficients, refinement objective values, and canonical hashes.
13.4.1.4 Streaming decoders
In streaming settings, observations arrive as (y^{(t)}) with incremental operators (\mathcal{O}^{(t)}). A streaming decoder maintains a state estimate (H^{(t)}) and updates by a single deterministic incremental step:[H^{(t+1)} ;=; \mathrm{Can}\Big(\Pi_{H^4}\big(H^{(t)} - \alpha_t \nabla |\mathcal{O}^{(t)}H-y^{(t)}|^2\big)\Big),]with periodic check enforcement (\Pi_{\ker\mathcal{K}}). Streaming correctness requires bounded drift and bounded cumulative numerical error; these are enforced by corridor audit logs.
13.4.2 Test suite
13.4.2.1 Erasure tests
Erasure tests enumerate deterministic mask families:
contiguous-region erasures;
multiscale block erasures aligned with the resolution hierarchy;
random-but-seeded coefficient erasures;
adversarially chosen erasures maximizing subframe degradation.
Each test asserts: (i) certification passes when theoretical conditions predict correctability, and (ii) impossibility certificates are produced when (\inf A_\Lambda=0) under the test family.
13.4.2.2 Noise tests
Noise tests include:
bounded-energy additive noise at multiple SNR levels;
structured noise concentrated in bands;
correlated noise consistent with declared covariance operators;
phase noise with controlled (|\theta|_{L^\infty}) and controlled smoothness.
Each test reports reconstruction error scaling versus noise level and verifies monotonicity with predicted bounds.
13.4.2.3 Adversarial tests
Adversarial tests construct perturbations by:
worst-case gradient ascent on decoder loss (within declared norm budget);
ambiguity class constructions (two distinct feasible solutions with identical observations);
targeted syndrome spoofing (perturbations that minimize syndrome while violating invariants);
truncation exploit tests (errors that concentrate beyond represented bands).
Each adversarial test is required to output either a certified failure classification or a certified success with explicit stability margins.
13.4.2.4 Regression tests
Regression tests pin:
deterministic seeds;
gold-state holograms and observations;
expected certificate hashes and residual traces (within tolerance);
performance budgets (time/memory) under fixed hardware abstraction.
A regression failure is defined as any change in certification status, any corridor violation not previously present, or any hash drift not explained by declared version updates.
13.4.3 Benchmark datasets
13.4.3.1 Structured holograms
Structured families include:
band-limited analytic holograms with known invariants;
topologically structured support manifolds when defined;
symmetry-structured states (unitary orbit families);
multi-region correlated holograms designed for redundancy demonstration.
Each structured family includes closed-form ground truth invariants and known correctable erasure regimes.
13.4.3.2 Random holograms
Random families are generated by:
sampling coefficients in a canonical basis with seeded pseudorandom generators;
projecting samples onto (H^4) and (\ker\mathcal{K}) via deterministic solvers;
canonicalizing gauge;
computing and storing invariant vectors as metadata.
Random families are used to estimate empirical thresholds and failure probabilities under noise and erasures.
13.4.3.3 Mask families
Mask families include:
uniform random masks at fixed retain-rate;
multiscale tree masks;
boundary-only masks (holographic boundary reconstructions);
adversarial masks produced by maximizing condition numbers of restricted operators.
Each mask family carries a deterministic identifier, generating seed, and a declared compatibility class with reconstruction operators.
13.4.3.4 Noise families
Noise families include:
i.i.d. Gaussian noise (seeded);
band-limited noise;
heavy-tailed noise;
phase noise families generated by band-limited random fields.
Noise generation is required to be deterministic under declared seeds and to serialize its parameters into the certificate.
13.4.4 Reporting
13.4.4.1 Success metrics
Canonical success metrics include:
(|H-\widehat{H}|_{\mathfrak{H}}) when ground truth is known;
observation residual (\mathsf{d}(\mathcal{O}\widehat{H},y));
check residual (|\mathcal{K}\widehat{H}|);
invariant drift (|\mathrm{Inv}(\widehat{H})-\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{ref}})|_\infty);
wall-clock and memory costs under declared benchmarking protocol.
13.4.4.2 Failure taxonomy
Failures are classified into mutually exclusive canonical categories:
Non-identifiable observation: ambiguity class detected;
Insufficient redundancy: subframe lower bound collapses;
Noise threshold exceeded: corridor violation under SNR;
Phase ambiguity: gauge/phase non-uniqueness not resolvable under available observations;
Numerical instability: conditioning blow-up or nonconvergence;
Adversarial drift: certified adversarial perturbation exceeds correctable budgets.
Each failure report includes the minimal witness set (operators, masks, perturbations) that certifies the classification.
13.4.4.3 Performance profiling
Profiling outputs include:
per-iteration complexity counts;
kernel runtimes per operator component;
memory residency per data structure (coefficients, wavelets, sparse supports);
stability diagnostics (condition numbers, step-size histories).
Profiling is deterministic and tied to reproducibility manifests.
13.4.4.4 Reproducibility artifacts
A reproducibility artifact bundle contains:
serialized program IR and hashes;
dataset and mask IDs;
noise generator seeds and parameters;
full audit logs;
certificate object;
canonical renderings of (H) in declared basis and resolution.
Companion artifact linkage:
Chapter 13 — Error-Correction and Redundancy: Holographic Codes and Self-Healing
Chapter 14 — Discretization & Truncation: Finite Models of Infinite Structure
14.1 Square — Discretization Definitions
14.1.1 Bases and truncations
14.1.1.1 Fourier basis truncation
Let (\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^3) be a bounded domain or a periodic cell, and let (\mathcal{P}) be a parameter manifold equipped with a reference measure. In the canonical setting, a holographic state is a complex field (H:\Omega\times\mathcal{P}\to\mathbb{C}) belonging to an ambient Hilbert space (\mathfrak{H}) with inner product (\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathfrak{H}}). A canonical model consistent with earlier chapters is (\mathfrak{H}=L^2(\Omega\times\mathcal{P})), with (H^4\subset\mathfrak{H}) defined by feasibility constraints and completeness properties.
Assume (\Omega) is periodic (or impose boundary conditions yielding a complete orthonormal spectral basis). Let ({e_k(x)}{k\in\mathbb{Z}^3}) be the Fourier basis on (\Omega), (e_k(x)=|\Omega|^{-1/2}e^{i k\cdot x}). For a fixed truncation radius (K\in\mathbb{N}), define the spectral index set[\mathcal{K}K := {k\in\mathbb{Z}^3:\ |k|\infty \le K}](or any equivalent norm choice with explicit mapping). The spatial Fourier truncation operator is the orthogonal projector[(\Pi^{x}K H)(x,p)=\sum{k\in\mathcal{K}K} \hat{H}(k,p),e_k(x),\quad\hat{H}(k,p):=\langle H(\cdot,p),e_k\rangle{L^2(\Omega)}.]The truncation error is[e^{x}{K}(H):=|H-\Pi^{x}K H|{\mathfrak{H}},]and is controlled by the tail energy in Fourier space.
If (\mathcal{P}) is itself a torus or admits a Fourier-type eigenbasis of a canonical Laplace–Beltrami operator, one may similarly define a parameter-space truncation (\Pi^{p}P) and combined truncation (\Pi{K,P}:=\Pi^{x}K\otimes \Pi^{p}P), yielding the finite subspace (\mathfrak{H}{K,P}:=\mathrm{ran}(\Pi{K,P})).
14.1.1.2 Wavelet basis truncation
Assume (\Omega) supports a multiresolution analysis (MRA) ({V_j}{j\in\mathbb{Z}}) with scaling functions (\phi{j,m}) and wavelets (\psi_{j,m}), where (j) is scale and (m) indexes translation. Let[H(x,p)=\sum_{m} a_{J_0,m}(p)\phi_{J_0,m}(x)+\sum_{j\ge J_0}\sum_{m} b_{j,m}(p)\psi_{j,m}(x),]with convergence in (L^2(\Omega)) for each (p) (and in (\mathfrak{H}) under standard measurability assumptions).
For a depth (J\ge J_0), define the wavelet truncation operator[(\Pi^{x,\mathrm{wav}}{J}H)(x,p):=\sum{m} a_{J_0,m}(p)\phi_{J_0,m}(x)+\sum_{j=J_0}^{J}\sum_{m} b_{j,m}(p)\psi_{j,m}(x).]This truncation is localized in both space and scale. The truncation error[e^{x,\mathrm{wav}}{J}(H):=|H-\Pi^{x,\mathrm{wav}}{J}H|_{\mathfrak{H}}]is controlled by the decay of wavelet coefficients and is naturally aligned with sparse representations and bounded-variation regimes.
A parameter-space wavelet/MRA truncation may be defined analogously when (\mathcal{P}) admits such structure, producing a combined truncation (\Pi_{J,Q}) with discrete indices ((j,m)) and ((q,n)).
14.1.1.3 Hybrid basis selection
Pure Fourier truncation is global and optimal for analytic or globally smooth states; pure wavelet truncation is optimal for localized singularities, edges, and sparsity. A hybrid basis is defined by selecting two bases (\mathcal{B}1,\mathcal{B}2) (e.g., Fourier and wavelet) and defining a decomposition[H = H{\mathrm{global}} + H{\mathrm{local}},]where (H_{\mathrm{global}}\in \mathrm{span}(\mathcal{B}1)) and (H{\mathrm{local}}\in \mathrm{span}(\mathcal{B}_2)), with a constraint that the decomposition is canonical (unique) under a chosen rule.
A mathematically controlled hybrid scheme is given by minimization:[(H_{\mathrm{global}},H_{\mathrm{local}})\in\arg\min_{u\in U_K,\ v\in V_J}\Big(|H-u-v|{\mathfrak{H}}^2+\lambda_1 |u|{\mathcal{R}1}^2+\lambda_2 |v|{\mathcal{R}_2}\Big),]where (U_K=\mathrm{ran}(\Pi^{x}K)), (V_J=\mathrm{ran}(\Pi^{x,\mathrm{wav}}{J})), (\mathcal{R}_1) is a smoothness regularizer (e.g., Sobolev), and (\mathcal{R}_2) is a sparsity-promoting regularizer (e.g., (\ell^1) on wavelet coefficients). This produces a deterministic, corridor-compatible finite representation once ((K,J,\lambda_1,\lambda_2)) are fixed.
14.1.1.4 Basis-change operators
Let (\mathcal{B}={b_i}{i\in I}) and (\mathcal{B}'={b'j}{j\in J}) be two complete families spanning the same closed subspace of (\mathfrak{H}). Define the analysis operators[\mathcal{A}{\mathcal{B}}:\mathfrak{H}\to \ell^2(I),\quad(\mathcal{A}{\mathcal{B}}H)i := \langle H,b_i\rangle{\mathfrak{H}},]and the synthesis operators[\mathcal{S}{\mathcal{B}}:\ell^2(I)\to\mathfrak{H},\quad\mathcal{S}{\mathcal{B}}(c):=\sum{i\in I} c_i b_i,]with unconditional convergence in (\mathfrak{H}) for orthonormal bases, and in the frame sense for redundant systems.
When both bases are orthonormal on the relevant truncation subspace, the basis-change operator is unitary:[U_{\mathcal{B}\to\mathcal{B}'} := \mathcal{A}{\mathcal{B}'}\circ \mathcal{S}{\mathcal{B}}.]For frames, the canonical change uses the dual frame (\tilde{\mathcal{B}}) and becomes[U_{\mathcal{B}\to\mathcal{B}'}:=\mathcal{A}{\mathcal{B}'}\circ \mathcal{S}{\tilde{\mathcal{B}}}.]A finite discretized implementation must store the truncation indices, the Gram matrices, and the chosen dual construction (canonical dual, oblique dual, or stabilized dual via Tikhonov).
14.1.2 Sampling theorems
14.1.2.1 Nyquist-type conditions
Assume (H(\cdot,p)) is bandlimited for each (p) in the sense that (\hat{H}(k,p)=0) for (|k|\infty>K). Let (h=\pi/K) be the canonical grid spacing associated with the bandlimit. Define the lattice (\Lambda_h:=h\mathbb{Z}^3\cap\Omega) and samples (y{n}(p):=H(x_n,p)), (x_n\in\Lambda_h).
The sampling operator (\mathcal{M}h: H \mapsto {H(x_n,\cdot)}{x_n\in\Lambda_h}) is injective on the bandlimited subspace, and the Shannon-type reconstruction operator (\mathcal{R}_h) satisfies (\mathcal{R}_h\circ \mathcal{M}_h = I) on that subspace under standard periodic/boundary assumptions. In practice, (\mathcal{R}_h) is realized as inverse FFT on the truncated spectral lattice, giving a stable reconstruction in the presence of bounded noise with error controlled by the operator norm of (\mathcal{R}_h).
14.1.2.2 Compressed sensing conditions (optional)
Let (H) admit a sparse representation in a dictionary (\mathcal{D}) on the discretized grid: (H\approx \Psi \alpha) where (\alpha) is (s)-sparse. Let measurements be linear (y=A\Psi\alpha+\eta) with (\eta) noise and (A) a sampling matrix induced by masks, subsampling, or projections. Sufficient recoverability is obtained when (A\Psi) satisfies a restricted isometry property (RIP) on (s)-sparse vectors, or equivalently when coherence-based bounds are satisfied.
The canonical decoder is basis pursuit denoising:[\alpha^\star \in \arg\min_{\alpha}|\alpha|_1\quad\text{s.t.}\quad|A\Psi\alpha-y|_2\le \sigma,\qquadH^\star:=\Psi\alpha^\star.]A finite treatise requires that the RIP/coherence criterion be checkable or upper-bounded in the given operator family; when exact certification is intractable, the corridor system must explicitly encode a probabilistic certification regime (confidence bounds and failure probability).
14.1.2.3 Masked sampling conditions
Let (M_A) be a mask operator selecting a measurable region (A\subset\Omega) or selecting a sampling pattern on a grid. The observation model is[y = \mathcal{O}_A(H) := M_A H + \eta,]where (\eta) is measurement noise. For a fixed model class (\mathcal{M}\subset\mathfrak{H}) (e.g., bandlimited, sparse-wavelet, or constraint-feasible states (H^4)), masked identifiability requires that (\mathcal{O}A) be injective on (\mathcal{M}/\mathcal{G}) (modulo gauge) and stable:[|H_1-H_2|{\mathfrak{H}}\le\kappa(A),|\mathcal{O}_A(H_1)-\mathcal{O}A(H_2)|{\mathcal{Y}}\quad\forall H_1,H_2\in\mathcal{M}.]The constant (\kappa(A)) is the reconstruction condition number; a certified sampling condition is an explicit upper bound on (\kappa(A)) and a lower bound on the smallest singular value of the restricted forward operator.
14.1.2.4 Certified sampling thresholds
Define a sampling design (\pi) (grid density, mask family, measurement operator class) and a model class (\mathcal{M}). A certified sampling threshold is a function[m_{\min}(\epsilon,\delta;\pi,\mathcal{M})]such that whenever the number of samples (m\ge m_{\min}), there exists a reconstruction operator (\widehat{R}\pi) satisfying[\mathbb{P}\big(|,\widehat{R}\pi(y)-H,|{\mathfrak{H}} \le \epsilon\big)\ge 1-\delta\quad\text{for all }H\in\mathcal{M}.]In deterministic regimes (bandlimited, noiseless), (\delta=0) and (m{\min}) is structural (Nyquist count). In stochastic regimes, (m_{\min}) must be paired with an explicit concentration argument, and the reporting format must output ((m,\epsilon,\delta)) as part of the reproducibility manifest.
14.1.3 Discrete constraints
14.1.3.1 Discrete constraint operator ((C_N))
Let (C:\mathfrak{H}\to \mathcal{Y}) be a (possibly vector-valued) constraint operator whose feasible set defines the admissible subspace (or manifold) of states, typically (H^4:=\ker(C)) in the linear case. The discretization must define:
a finite-dimensional state space (\mathfrak{H}_N\subset\mathfrak{H}),
an embedding (E_N:\mathfrak{H}_N\hookrightarrow\mathfrak{H}),
a restriction/measurement (D_N:\mathcal{Y}\to \mathcal{Y}_N),and a discrete operator[C_N := D_N\circ C\circ E_N:\mathfrak{H}_N\to \mathcal{Y}_N.]When (C) is defined by differential operators, (C_N) is induced either by finite differences (strong form) or by Galerkin projection (weak form). For integral constraints, (C_N) is induced by quadrature rules consistent with the measure on (\Omega\times\mathcal{P}).
14.1.3.2 Consistency of ((C_N\to C))
Consistency is defined by the requirement that for every sufficiently regular (H\in\mathrm{dom}(C)),[|C_N(\Pi_N H) - D_N(C(H))|_{\mathcal{Y}_N}\to 0\quad\text{as }N\to\infty,]where (\Pi_N:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathfrak{H}_N) is the canonical projection associated with the chosen discretization.
A rigorous framework requires explicit rates: there exists (p>0) and (K(H)) such that[|C_N(\Pi_N H) - D_N(C(H))|_{\mathcal{Y}_N}\le K(H),h_N^{p},]with (h_N) the resolution parameter (grid spacing, spectral cutoff (K^{-1}), wavelet scale (2^{-J}), or digit-lift depth (4^{-k}) depending on the selected family). The rate constant must be stated in terms of norms of (H) in the regularity class required by (C).
14.1.3.3 Discrete feasibility definitions
Define the discrete feasible set in the linear case as[H^4_N := \ker(C_N)\subset \mathfrak{H}_N.]In corridor form (soft feasibility), define a budget vector (\delta_N) and the corridor-feasible set[H^{4,\delta_N}_N := {h\in\mathfrak{H}N:\ |C_N(h)|{\mathcal{Y}_N}\le \delta_N}.]The corridor budgets (\delta_N) must be chosen to dominate both numerical error and modeled admissibility slack. This choice is formalized in §14.3.4.
14.1.3.4 Projection onto discrete feasible set
For linear constraints and an inner product on (\mathfrak{H}N), the orthogonal projection (P_N:\mathfrak{H}N\to H^4_N) is defined by:[P_N := \arg\min{u\in H^4_N}|u-h|{\mathfrak{H}_N}.]If (C_N) has full row rank, a closed form is[P_N = I - C_N^\ast (C_N C_N^\ast)^{-1} C_N,]where (C_N^\ast) is the adjoint under the chosen discrete inner products.
For nonlinear constraints, projection is defined as a local solver:[P_N(h) \in \arg\min_{u\in\mathfrak{H}N} \frac12|u-h|{\mathfrak{H}_N}^2\quad\text{s.t.}\quadC_N(u)=0,]implemented via SQP, augmented Lagrangian, or proximal splitting under explicit convergence criteria and corridor safeguards.
14.1.4 Discrete invariants
14.1.4.1 Discrete information measures
Let (I_\epsilon(H)) denote an information functional computed after coarse-graining at scale (\epsilon). A discretization supplies (\epsilon=\epsilon_N) and a discrete coarse-graining operator (S_{\epsilon_N}) producing a finite histogram/probability vector (p(\epsilon_N)). Define discrete Shannon entropy:[I_{\epsilon_N}^{(N)}(h):= -\sum_{i=1}^{m_N} p_i(\epsilon_N)\log p_i(\epsilon_N),]with conventions (0\log 0:=0). Rényi/Tsallis variants are defined analogously with explicit parameter domains and continuity at the limiting parameters.
A rigorous treatment requires explicit bias/variance decomposition for the chosen estimator, and a statement of conditions under which (I_{\epsilon_N}^{(N)}(h)\to I_\epsilon(H)) as (N\to\infty) with (\epsilon_N\to 0) under an admissible scaling schedule.
14.1.4.2 Discrete spectral measures
Let (A) be a continuous operator whose spectral invariants enter (\mathrm{Inv}(H)) (e.g., Laplacian-like generators, covariance operators, or reconstruction kernels). A discretization yields a matrix/operator (A_N) on (\mathfrak{H}_N). Define spectral invariants:
band energies (E_{B}(A_N)) from eigenvalue bands,
spectral gaps (\lambda_{2}(A_N)-\lambda_1(A_N)),
traces (\mathrm{tr}(f(A_N))) for admissible (f).
When the framework uses a Square/Flower/Cloud/Fractal pipeline with coarse-grain maps (S,R) and defects such as (\Delta_{SR}=|A_k-S A_{k+1}R|), the discretization must treat these as first-class spectral-invariant carriers and provide consistent estimators across resolution levels.
14.1.4.3 Discrete topological measures (optional)
When topological invariants are defined (e.g., winding numbers of phase, discrete homology on level sets, or persistent homology of amplitude magnitude), the discretization must specify:
the simplicial/cubical complex construction from grid data,
the filtration parameter schedule,
stability bounds under perturbations (bottleneck distance or equivalent),
convergence conditions as grid is refined.
A topological invariant is admissible only when it is stable under the discretization’s noise and truncation envelope; otherwise it must be moved into a “diagnostic” invariant class with explicit non-guarantee tagging.
14.1.4.4 Convergence to continuous invariants
Let (\mathrm{Inv}:\mathfrak{H}\to \mathbb{R}^m) be an invariant map. A discrete invariant map (\mathrm{Inv}_N:\mathfrak{H}_N\to\mathbb{R}^m) is convergent if for all (H) in the admissible regularity class,[|\mathrm{Inv}N(\Pi_N H)-\mathrm{Inv}(H)|{\mathbb{R}^m}\to 0.]A complete treatment requires:
explicit rates in (h_N),
explicit robustness to noise (\eta_N),
explicit behavior under gauge canonicalization (invariant or gauge-quotiented invariants).
14.2 Flower — Numerical Methods for ((H^4)) and ((D^5))
14.2.1 Discrete operator kernels
14.2.1.1 Finite difference operators
Let (L) be a differential operator acting on (H) (e.g., gradient, Laplacian, divergence, constraint PDE operator). A finite difference discretization on a grid of spacing (h) defines (L_h:\mathfrak{H}_N\to\mathfrak{H}_N) via stencil operators. Consistency order (p) is defined by:[|L_h(\Pi_h u)-\Pi_h(Lu)|\le C(u)h^p.]Boundary conditions must be encoded as part of the operator definition (Dirichlet, Neumann, Robin, periodic) and must be treated as constraint components when the feasibility set requires them.
Stability is operator-dependent; for time-evolution kernels, a CFL-type restriction or implicit methods must be specified explicitly and turned into admissibility guard rails.
14.2.1.2 Spectral operators
Spectral operators represent (H) in a Fourier basis and apply (L) by multiplication in frequency space when (L) is a constant-coefficient differential operator or a pseudodifferential operator with known symbol. For example, (\Delta) becomes multiplication by (-|k|^2). Spectral differentiation yields high accuracy for smooth states but requires explicit anti-aliasing policies (2/3-rule or equivalent) when nonlinearities are present.
A rigorous implementation requires:
explicit index ordering for FFT,
explicit normalization conventions,
explicit truncation and de-alias filters,
explicit equivalence mapping to physical space norms.
14.2.1.3 Wavelet operators
Wavelet operator kernels apply (L) via sparse operator representations on multiresolution coefficients. For operators with local structure, the wavelet matrix is compressible and can be truncated to achieve (O(N)) or (O(N\log N)) complexity.
A complete specification requires:
chosen wavelet family,
boundary-adapted wavelets or extension rules,
coefficient threshold schedule as a function of corridor budgets,
proof of stability of the thresholding policy for the intended invariant suite.
14.2.1.4 Mixed operators
Mixed operators combine:
spectral treatment in smooth/global directions,
wavelet/local treatment in singular directions,
finite differences in irregular coordinate charts,with operator splitting:[L \approx L^{(1)} + L^{(2)} + \cdots,\qquade^{tL}\approx \prod_j e^{tL^{(j)}} + \mathcal{O}(t^q).]A mixed method must specify:
a splitting order (q),
a commutator defect estimator (for non-commuting components),
a stability policy that couples time-step selection to commutator magnitude and corridor budgets.
14.2.2 Projection and penalty methods
14.2.2.1 Hard projection methods
Given an unconstrained update (\widetilde{h}{n+1}\in\mathfrak{H}N), the hard-feasible update is[h{n+1} := P_N(\widetilde{h}{n+1})\in H^4_N.]For composite constraints (C_N=(C_N^{(1)},\dots,C_N^{(r)})), alternating projection schemes are admissible:[h^{(i+1)} := P_N^{(i)}(h^{(i)}),]with explicit conditions for convergence (convexity or regularity assumptions) and explicit failure detection when constraints are incompatible.
14.2.2.2 Penalty formulations
Penalty methods replace feasibility by minimization:[h^\star \in \arg\min_{h\in\mathfrak{H}_N}\Big( \mathcal{J}N(h) + \frac{\lambda}{2}|C_N(h)|{\mathcal{Y}_N}^2 \Big),]where (\mathcal{J}_N) is the discrete objective (reconstruction loss, cost functional, transformation goal). A rigorous specification requires:
a rule for selecting (\lambda) as a function of corridor budget (\delta_N),
a proof (or certified bound) that penalty solutions satisfy (|C_N(h^\star)|\le \delta_N),
an explicit conditioning analysis as (\lambda\to\infty).
14.2.2.3 Augmented Lagrangian methods
Augmented Lagrangians stabilize penalties:
[
\mathcal{L}_\rho(h,\nu)
\mathcal{J}_N(h) + \langle \nu, C_N(h)\rangle + \frac{\rho}{2}|C_N(h)|^2,]with updates alternating between minimizing in (h) and updating multipliers (\nu). The method must specify:
multiplier initialization and determinism,
update rules (step sizes, stopping criteria),
feasibility repair when updates increase constraint residual beyond corridor thresholds.
14.2.2.4 Convergence criteria
Convergence criteria must be stated as a vector of tests:
primal residual: (|C_N(h_k)|\le \delta_N),
objective stationarity: (|\nabla \mathcal{J}_N(h_k)+C_N^\ast \nu_k|\le \eta_N),
iterate stability: (|h_k-h_{k-1}|\le \epsilon_N),
invariant drift compliance: (|\mathrm{Inv}_N(h_k)-\mathrm{Inv}_N(h_0)|\le \Delta_N).The tolerances ((\delta_N,\eta_N,\epsilon_N,\Delta_N)) must be explicitly derived from §14.3.4 corridor translation.
14.2.3 Solvers
14.2.3.1 Linear solvers
For linear systems (A_N h=b), admissible solvers include:
conjugate gradient (CG) for symmetric positive definite (A_N),
MINRES for symmetric indefinite,
GMRES/BiCGSTAB for nonsymmetric.
A rigorous manuscript must specify:
termination conditions in terms of backward error,
preconditioning requirements and validity,
determinism of floating-point reductions (reproducible BLAS settings or deterministic reductions).
14.2.3.2 Nonlinear solvers
For nonlinear systems (F_N(h)=0), admissible solvers include:
Newton and damped Newton:[h_{k+1}=h_k - \alpha_k (J_F(h_k))^{-1}F_N(h_k),]
quasi-Newton (BFGS/L-BFGS) on (\nabla \mathcal{J}_N),
fixed-point iteration (h_{k+1}=\Phi_N(h_k)) under contraction certificates.
Each solver must include:
a globalization strategy (line search/trust region),
Jacobian/Hessian approximation rules,
certified failure modes (divergence, ill-conditioning, nonphysical solutions).
14.2.3.3 Iterative refinement
Iterative refinement is mandatory when corridor budgets require tight residual control under finite precision. For a computed solution (h^{(0)}), compute residual (r=b-A_N h^{(0)}) in higher precision, solve (A_N \Delta h=r) approximately, and update (h^{(1)}=h^{(0)}+\Delta h). This continues until residual falls below corridor-defined tolerances or stagnates.
A complete specification requires:
the precision ladder (fp32/fp64/fp128),
the stopping rule linked to certified stability (§14.3.2),
a reproducibility policy for mixed-precision kernels.
14.2.3.4 Preconditioning strategies
Preconditioning must be treated as part of the discrete model, not an implementation detail. Admissible preconditioners include:
multigrid (geometric or algebraic),
diagonal/Jacobi and block-diagonal,
incomplete factorizations (ILU/IC),
spectral preconditioning using approximate diagonalization when available.
The manuscript must specify how preconditioning affects:
conditioning bounds,
stability under refinement (N\to\infty),
determinism (ordering dependence in sparse factorizations).
14.2.4 Stability control
14.2.4.1 Step size control
For time-stepped or iterative flows (h_{n+1}=\Psi_{\Delta t}(h_n)), step size (\Delta t) must be chosen to guarantee stability:
explicit schemes: (\Delta t\le \Delta t_{\max}) (CFL-type),
implicit schemes: solve tolerance must scale with (\Delta t) to prevent hidden drift.
A stability controller is defined by:[\Delta t_{n+1} = \mathrm{clip}\Big(\Delta t_n \cdot \Big(\frac{\mathrm{tol}}{\mathrm{err}n}\Big)^{1/q},\ \Delta t{\min},\ \Delta t_{\max}\Big),]where (\mathrm{err}_n) is an a posteriori estimate, and (\mathrm{tol}) is derived from corridor budgets.
14.2.4.2 Regularization control
Regularization parameters (penalty (\lambda), Tikhonov (\alpha), sparsity weight (\beta)) must be coupled to corridor requirements. A canonical rule is discrepancy matching:[|C_N(h^\star(\lambda))| \approx \delta_N,]or equivalently selecting (\lambda) such that feasibility residual meets the constraint corridor while minimizing objective distortion. This rule must be made deterministic and logged.
14.2.4.3 Conditioning control
Conditioning control includes:
scaling of variables and operators (normalize rows/columns),
orthonormalization of bases (stabilize Gram matrices),
gauge-fixing before solving (remove null directions),
reparameterization to reduce curvature in optimization problems.
Condition numbers must be estimated and reported; thresholds define when to switch solvers or refine discretization.
14.2.4.4 Error tracking control
Error tracking is the explicit propagation of three error classes:
truncation error (e_{\mathrm{trunc}}(N)),
discretization error (e_{\mathrm{disc}}(N)),
numerical error (e_{\mathrm{num}}(N)).
A compliant implementation must provide an upper envelope[e_{\mathrm{tot}}(N)\le e_{\mathrm{trunc}}(N)+e_{\mathrm{disc}}(N)+e_{\mathrm{num}}(N),]and must tie corridor admissibility to (e_{\mathrm{tot}}) via §14.3.4.
14.3 Cloud — Error Bounds and Convergence
14.3.1 Truncation error
14.3.1.1 Error vs bandlimit bounds
Assume (H(\cdot,p)\in H^s(\Omega)) for (s>0) uniformly in (p), with (|H|{L^2(\mathcal{P};H^s(\Omega))}<\infty). Then Fourier truncation satisfies:[|H-\Pi^{x}K H|{\mathfrak{H}}\leC,K^{-s},|H|{L^2(\mathcal{P};H^s(\Omega))},]with (C) depending on (\Omega) and normalization conventions. For analytic (H), the decay is exponential in (K), yielding exponentially small truncation error.
14.3.1.2 Error vs wavelet depth bounds
Assume (H(\cdot,p)) lies in a Besov space (B^s_{q,r}) appropriate to the chosen wavelet system. Then wavelet truncation to depth (J) satisfies:[|H-\Pi^{x,\mathrm{wav}}{J}H|{\mathfrak{H}}\leC,2^{-Js},|H|{L^2(\mathcal{P};B^s{q,r}(\Omega))},]with (C) determined by the wavelet family and boundary handling. This bound formalizes the advantage of wavelets for limited regularity (edges, BV structure).
14.3.1.3 Error vs sparsity bounds
Let (c(H)) denote coefficients of (H) in a dictionary (\mathcal{D}). Define the best (s)-term approximation error[\sigma_s(H) := \inf_{|c|0\le s}|H-\mathcal{S}{\mathcal{D}}(c)|{\mathfrak{H}}.]If coefficients decay as (|c|{(n)}\le C n^{-a}), then (\sigma_s(H)\le C' s^{-(a-1/2)}). A finite model based on sparsity is admissible only when (s) is chosen so that (\sigma_s(H)) is below the corridor error envelope.
14.3.1.4 Certified truncation envelopes
A certified truncation envelope is an explicit computable function (E_{\mathrm{trunc}}(N)) satisfying[|H-\Pi_N H|{\mathfrak{H}}\le E{\mathrm{trunc}}(N).]Admissible constructions include:
tail-energy estimators in Fourier space:[E_{\mathrm{trunc}}^2(K)=\sum_{|k|>K}|\hat{H}(k,\cdot)|_{L^2(\mathcal{P})}^2,]approximated by oversampled spectra and monotone bounds;
coefficient-decay bounds for wavelets using a boundedness assumption on the coefficient norm.
The envelope must be logged and attached to every discrete artifact as part of the reproducibility manifest.
14.3.2 Numerical stability
14.3.2.1 Condition number bounds
Let (A_N) be the discrete operator defining a solver step (constraint projection, reconstruction, transform application). Stability requires bounding (\kappa(A_N)=|A_N||A_N^{-1}|) on the relevant subspace (or pseudo-inverse norm on quotient space when gauge nulls exist). Corridor feasibility requires that numerical error be bounded by:[e_{\mathrm{num}} \lesssim \kappa(A_N),\epsilon_{\mathrm{mach}},|A_N|,|h|,]up to method-dependent constants, where (\epsilon_{\mathrm{mach}}) is machine precision.
14.3.2.2 Round-off error propagation
For an iterative chain (h_{n+1}=T_N(h_n)) computed in floating point, define the computed map (\widetilde{T}N=T_N+\Delta T_N). Error recursion is bounded by:[|h{n+1}-\widetilde{h}_{n+1}|\le|DT_N(h_n)|\cdot |h_n-\widetilde{h}_n| + |\Delta T_N(h_n)|,]and yields exponential amplification when (|DT_N|>1). Stability certification requires either contraction regimes or explicit bounding of amplification.
14.3.2.3 Catastrophic cancellation regimes
Cancellation occurs when subtracting nearly equal quantities (e.g., computing constraint residuals as differences of large terms). A rigorous implementation must:
choose numerically stable formulations (e.g., compute residuals in transformed coordinates),
use compensated summation for large reductions,
log when effective precision drops below corridor thresholds.
Where cancellation is unavoidable, the corridor translation must incorporate an inflation factor in (\delta_N) to prevent false infeasibility flags.
14.3.2.4 Stability certificates
A stability certificate is a structured object containing:
estimated (\kappa(A_N)) or upper bounds,
precision regime and rounding mode,
backward error bound (|A_N \widetilde{h}-b|),
reproducible seeds/configuration for randomized estimators.
If the broader system uses multi-lens stability terms (e.g., renormalization defects (\Omega_{SR}), semigroup coarse-grain defect (\Omega_{CR}), coupling defect (\Omega_{SF}^{\mathrm{coup}})), the certificate must report these with budgets and normalization.
14.3.3 Convergence theorems
14.3.3.1 Consistency proofs
For each operator component (L) used in constraints, reconstruction, or transforms, define discrete approximations (L_N). Consistency requires:[|L_N(\Pi_N u)-\Pi_N(Lu)|\to 0]for (u) in a dense subset of (\mathrm{dom}(L)). Proofs must specify the regularity class (Sobolev/Besov) and the discretization family (finite difference order, spectral cutoff, wavelet depth).
14.3.3.2 Stability implies convergence proofs
For linear evolution problems and linear constraints, a Lax-type principle applies: for a well-posed continuous problem, if the discrete method is stable and consistent, it converges. The treatise must state stability in operator-norm form:[\sup_{N}\sup_{0\le n\le T/\Delta t_N}|\Psi^{(N)}_{\Delta t_N}{}^{,n}|\le M_T<\infty,]and must identify the topology in which convergence holds (e.g., (L^2), (H^s), or weak-*).
14.3.3.3 Convergence rates
Convergence rates must be stated explicitly and tied to:
approximation order (p) (finite differences),
spectral decay (Fourier),
coefficient decay (wavelet/sparse),
solver tolerance schedule (iterative methods),
corridor scaling schedule ((\delta_N)).
A canonical complete statement takes the form:[|H - H_N|{\mathfrak{H}}\leE{\mathrm{trunc}}(N)+E_{\mathrm{disc}}(N)+E_{\mathrm{solve}}(N),]with each term explicitly upper-bounded and logged.
14.3.3.4 Counterexamples
The manuscript must include explicit counterexample templates showing failure of convergence when:
aliasing occurs under nonlinear operations without de-alias filters,
constraints are discretized inconsistently (spurious kernels),
projection operators are computed with unstable pseudo-inverses,
corridor budgets are chosen smaller than unavoidable discretization bias.
Each counterexample must be paired with a detectable signature (diagnostic invariant or residual pattern) and a prescribed mitigation (refinement, basis change, regularization increase, or rejection).
14.3.4 Corridor translation
14.3.4.1 Continuous corridor (\to) discrete corridor mapping
Let the continuous corridor be specified by budgets (\delta^{\mathrm{cont}}=(\delta_C^{\mathrm{cont}},\delta_{\mathrm{Inv}}^{\mathrm{cont}},\delta_{\mathrm{Rec}}^{\mathrm{cont}},\dots)) constraining:
feasibility residuals (|C(H)|),
invariant drift (|\mathrm{Inv}(H')-\mathrm{Inv}(H)|),
reconstruction residuals (|R_A(H|_A)-H|),under transforms and computations.
Define a discretization bias envelope (b_N) that bounds unavoidable discrepancy:[b_N :=\sup_{H\in\mathcal{M}}\Big(|C_N(\Pi_N H)-D_N(C(H))|+|\mathrm{Inv}_N(\Pi_N H)-\mathrm{Inv}(H)|+\cdots\Big).]Then the discrete corridor budgets must satisfy[\delta^{(N)} \ge b_N + \delta^{\mathrm{model}}_N,]where (\delta^{\mathrm{model}}_N) is any intentional slack budget (e.g., physical tolerance). Conversely, to ensure discrete certification implies continuous admissibility, one must enforce[\delta^{(N)} \le \delta^{\mathrm{cont}} - b_N,]which is possible only when (\delta^{\mathrm{cont}}>b_N). When (\delta^{\mathrm{cont}}\le b_N), the discretization level is formally insufficient and must be refined.
In the multi-lens certificate setting where coarse-graining maps (S,R) and defects (\Delta_{SR}=|A_k-S A_{k+1}R|) are used to certify stability across resolution ladders, the corridor translation must also include these renormalization defects as first-class budget items.
14.3.4.2 Budget scaling rules
Budget scaling requires an explicit schedule as (N\to\infty). If a discretization yields bias (b_N = O(h_N^p)), then a canonical tightening schedule is:[\delta^{(N)} = \delta^{\mathrm{cont}} - c,h_N^p,]with (c) chosen as a certified upper bound. For multi-scale discretizations indexed by level (k) (e.g., (N=4^k) ladders), a canonical schedule is:[\delta^{(k)} = \delta^{\mathrm{cont}} - c,4^{-kp}.]When the system explicitly uses lift and coarse-grain checks across ((d,k)\to(d,k+1)) or phase-locked lifts, the budgets must also be stable under lifting; in particular, renormalization-spine defects must not increase with (k) beyond the permitted envelope.
14.3.4.3 Confidence reporting rules
When corridor tests are computed from estimators (entropy estimators, spectral estimators, stochastic sampling), each reported corridor check must include:
point estimate (\widehat{\Omega}),
bound or interval ([\widehat{\Omega}-\Delta,\widehat{\Omega}+\Delta]),
confidence level (1-\alpha),
method provenance (bootstrap, concentration bound, analytic variance bound).
A corridor pass is declared only when the upper confidence bound satisfies the budget:[\widehat{\Omega} + \Delta \le \epsilon,]where (\epsilon) is the relevant budget component. In deterministic tests (linear algebra residuals, exact arithmetic regimes), (\Delta=0).
14.3.4.4 Failure handling rules
Failure handling must distinguish:
model insufficiency (discretization too coarse: (b_N) dominates),
numerical instability (conditioning or solver tolerance too weak),
true infeasibility (state/transform violates constraints beyond continuous corridor).
A certified protocol is:
If (|C_N(h)|>\delta^{(N)}) but decreases systematically under refinement (N\mapsto N'), classify as model insufficiency and refine.
If residuals oscillate or increase with refinement and conditioning indicators worsen, classify as numerical instability and switch solvers/preconditioners/precision regimes.
If residuals persist across refinement and stability indicators remain bounded, classify as true infeasibility and reject or apply admissible repair transforms.
All failures must emit an audit log: discretization parameters, solver parameters, condition estimates, corridor budgets, and the minimal reproducing test case.
14.4 Fractal — Implementation and Benchmarks
14.4.1 Discretization library
14.4.1.1 Basis modules
A compliant basis module must provide:
basis identifier (\mathrm{BasisID}) (family, parameters, normalization),
truncation descriptor (\mathrm{Trunc}(N)) (indices, ordering),
transforms (\mathrm{Analysis}(h)), (\mathrm{Synthesis}(c)),
basis-change (\mathrm{ChangeBasis}(c,\mathrm{BasisID}\to\mathrm{BasisID}')).
Determinism requires fixed index ordering, fixed normalization conventions, and explicit handling of boundary extensions.
14.4.1.2 Operator modules
Operator modules implement discrete kernels (L_N) and their adjoints:
(\mathrm{Apply}(L_N,h)),
(\mathrm{ApplyAdjoint}(L_N,h)),
(\mathrm{Assemble}(L_N)) when a matrix form is required,
(\mathrm{EstimateNorm}(L_N)) and (\mathrm{EstimateCond}(L_N)).
When operators include multi-lens constructs (Square operator (A_{d,k}), diagonalization (U\Lambda U^{-1}), semigroup (e^{-tA}), coarse-grain (S,R)), the module must expose each artifact explicitly so that certificate checks are first-class operations.
14.4.1.3 Constraint modules
Constraint modules implement:
(C_N) evaluation,
(C_N^\ast) evaluation,
feasibility projection (P_N),
corridor feasibility checks and repair suggestions (projection, penalty tuning, refinement recommendation).
Constraints must integrate with invariants and reconstruction checks so that admissibility is globally certified.
14.4.1.4 Solver modules
Solver modules implement:
linear solvers with preconditioners,
nonlinear solvers with globalization,
projection and augmented Lagrangian loops,
mixed-precision iterative refinement,with a unified reporting interface that returns a solver certificate.
14.4.2 Test suite
14.4.2.1 Convergence tests
Convergence tests require:
a family of reference states (H_{\mathrm{ref}}) with known projections (analytic Fourier coefficients, known wavelet coefficients, manufactured solutions),
a refinement ladder (N_0<N_1<\cdots),
measured errors (e(N_i)),
verification of expected convergence rate (e(N_i)\approx C h_{N_i}^p).
Tests must be automated and produce machine-readable artifacts.
14.4.2.2 Stability tests
Stability tests require:
long-chain application of operators and transforms,
monitoring of residuals and invariant drift,
condition number tracking,
pass/fail under corridor budgets.
Where the framework uses renormalization-spine defects and multi-lens budgets (e.g., (\Omega_{SR},\Omega_{CR},\Omega_{FR})), tests must include lift steps and verify that defects do not grow beyond budgets.
14.4.2.3 Accuracy tests
Accuracy tests include:
reconstruction accuracy under masks (compare (R_A(h|_A)) with (h)),
constraint satisfaction accuracy,
invariant accuracy (compare with high-resolution baseline).
Each accuracy test must report both absolute and relative error, normalized by appropriate backbone scales.
14.4.2.4 Regression tests
Regression tests require:
frozen gold artifacts (inputs/outputs),
deterministic seeds and fixed versions of dependencies,
tolerance policies with explicit rationale (tight for hard-lock identities, looser for soft corridor terms).
Regression must include performance regression where runtime and memory are part of the acceptance criteria.
14.4.3 Benchmark protocols
14.4.3.1 Runtime scaling
Runtime scaling benchmarks must report:
complexity as a function of (N) (or (K,J,k)),
breakdown by module (basis transforms, operator application, solver iterations),
parallel scaling if applicable.
Complexity claims must match measured scaling within bounded deviation on the benchmark hardware configuration recorded in the manifest.
14.4.3.2 Memory scaling
Memory benchmarks must report:
peak resident memory,
representation size of state, operators, preconditioners,
sparsity ratios and compression ratios.
Sparse formats must be fully specified (CSR/CSC, block sparse, wavelet coefficient storage) with deterministic ordering.
14.4.3.3 Error scaling
Error scaling must report:
error vs resolution,
error vs truncation parameters,
error vs noise level,
error vs corridor budgets (trade-off curves).
For each curve, the benchmark must specify the measurement norm and any normalization constant.
14.4.3.4 Reproducible reporting
Every benchmark output must include:
code version identifiers,
parameter manifest,
deterministic seed manifest,
environment manifest (compiler, BLAS, precision mode),
hash of the benchmark suite.
14.4.4 Reference cases
14.4.4.1 Smooth cases
Smooth reference families include:
analytic Gaussian-modulated plane waves,
finite Fourier sums (exactly bandlimited),
smooth separable products (H(x,p)=f(x)g(p)) with known spectra.
These cases certify spectral truncation and high-order finite differences.
14.4.4.2 Sparse cases
Sparse reference families include:
piecewise smooth states with jump discontinuities,
wavelet-sparse constructions with known coefficient decay,
localized features embedded in smooth backgrounds.
These cases certify wavelet truncation, thresholding policies, and hybrid decompositions.
14.4.4.3 Highly oscillatory cases
Oscillatory reference families include:
chirps and localized high-frequency packets,
multi-band superpositions with separated scales,
near-aliasing regimes that stress sampling thresholds.
These cases certify anti-alias policies, stability of reconstruction, and correctness of spectral indexing.
14.4.4.4 Adversarial cases
Adversarial families include:
near-singular operator regimes (ill-conditioned constraints),
boundary layers and stiff dynamics,
noise-corrupted undersampled masks near identifiability thresholds.
These cases certify failure classification, refinement triggers, solver switching, and corridor-based rejection logic.
Chapter 14 — Discretization & Truncation: Finite Models of Infinite Structure
Chapter 15 — Data Structures for Holographic Computation: Sparse, Hierarchical, Streaming
This chapter is a practical “systems layer” for holographic computation: how you store, move, and transform holographic state so that reconstruction, inversion, and invariant enforcement remain fast, stable, and auditable.
Throughout, assume the canonical holographic field (or reconstruction state) is a 4D tensor with three spatial dimensions plus a feature/channel axis:
Field: (X \in \mathbb{C}^{N \times N \times N \times P}) (or real-valued when appropriate)
The last axis (P) covers channels such as polarization, wavelength bins, time-slices, latent features, basis coefficients, or multi-plane stacks.
We’ll structure the chapter with the four lenses in your outline:
Square: what the data is (models)
Flower: what we do to it (operators)
Cloud: what can go wrong and what can be guaranteed (stability/failure)
Fractal: how to build it as real code (architecture/testing/repro)
15.1 Square — Data Models
15.1.1 Dense tensor model
Dense is the “baseline truth”: every voxel exists, every channel exists, and you pay full memory/time cost. Dense is unbeatable when your kernels are bandwidth-coalesced and your data is not meaningfully sparse.
15.1.1.1 Shape conventions ((N\times N\times N\times P))
Choose one canonical axis naming and stick to it everywhere:
Spatial axes: ((x,y,z)) or ((i,j,k))
Channel axis: (p \in [0, P))
Recommended semantic contract:
(N): reconstruction resolution per axis
(P): operator-local feature channels (basis, wavelengths, etc.)
A key discipline: never let “channels” become “a fourth spatial axis” in code. Channels have different locality and transform behavior than space.
15.1.1.2 Memory layouts
Dense layout is where most performance is won or lost. The main choice is which axis is contiguous (“unit stride”).
Common layouts:
SoA (Structure of Arrays): separate arrays per component (real/imag, or per-channel)
Good for SIMD/vectorization, and many GPU kernels.
AoS (Array of Structures): interleave components (complex packed, or channel-packed)
Good for small (P), poor when you want coalesced loads per channel.
Typical contiguous orders (row-major examples):
XYZP contiguous in P: X[x,y,z,p] with p fastest
Best when kernels operate across channels at a fixed voxel (feature mixing, per-voxel ops).
PXYZ contiguous in X: X[p,x,y,z] with x fastest
Best when you stream through space for a fixed channel (FFT per channel, stencils).
Engineering rule:
If your dominant kernels are 3D spatial transforms (FFT, stencils, wave propagation), make x (or the innermost spatial axis) contiguous.
If your dominant kernels are per-voxel channel mixing, make p contiguous.
Also consider:
Padding: align inner strides to 32/64 bytes (CPU) and to warp-friendly segments (GPU).
Tiling: store as blocked tiles (e.g., (8^3) or (16^3) voxels per tile) to raise cache reuse.
Complex storage: interleaved complex can be fine; for GPU, sometimes split real/imag improves throughput depending on kernel.
15.1.1.3 Access patterns
Dense access patterns usually fall into:
Stencil/local neighborhood
e.g., finite difference updates, wave steps
Dominant cost: memory bandwidth + cache reuse
Needs tiling (block loops) and halo handling
Global transforms
FFTs, global convolutions, dense linear operators
Dominant cost: global memory traffic and transpose/shuffle overhead
Per-voxel channel mixing
small dense matrix multiply at each voxel: (P\times P)
Dominant cost: arithmetic, but also register pressure
A discipline that prevents “mystery slowness”:
Declare every kernel’s intended locality: voxel-local, block-local, global.
Then store/tiling/ordering must match that declared locality.
15.1.1.4 Performance bounds
Dense performance is usually bounded by the roofline:
Total elements: (N^3 \cdot P)
Memory traffic dominates if arithmetic intensity is low (stencils, simple per-voxel ops).
You want:
High effective bandwidth (coalesced + cache-friendly)
Reduced passes (fuse operators when safe)
Avoid unnecessary transposes (especially 3D FFT pipelines)
A simple sanity bound (per pass):
Reading + writing complex (X): (\approx 2 \cdot N^3P \cdot \text{sizeof(complex)}) bytesIf you do 10 passes, you did 10× that traffic no matter how clever you are.
15.1.2 Sparse tensor model
Sparse is for when holographic state is naturally localized (e.g., sparse scatterers, compressive features) or when you’re willing to approximate.
Sparse buys memory/time, but introduces indexing cost and stability risk.
15.1.2.1 Sparse encodings (COO/CSR-like)
For 3D+channels, you typically store sparsity over space and keep channels dense:
Nonzeros indexed by voxel coordinate ( (x,y,z) )
Values are vectors (v \in \mathbb{C}^P)
Encodings:
COO: list of coordinates + values
Best for construction/streaming writes, worst for repeated reads.
CSR-like (compressed by one axis):
E.g., compress (z) slices, or compress linearized Morton index.
Hashed sparse: map from key→value
Great for dynamic updates, expensive for deterministic performance.
Practical rule:
Prefer a sorted COO (by a deterministic linear index) for build,
Then convert to a compressed format for repeated kernels.
15.1.2.2 Block sparsity
Block sparsity is usually the “sweet spot”:
Partition volume into blocks (e.g., (8^3))
Only store blocks that contain meaningful signal
Inside a block, store dense (8^3 \times P)
Benefits:
Better locality
Lower index overhead per value
SIMD/GPU friendly kernels on dense blocks
Block format also meshes naturally with hierarchical and streaming models.
15.1.2.3 Multi-resolution sparsity
Multi-resolution sparsity combines:
coarse coverage everywhere,
fine detail only where needed.
Two common approaches:
Sparse pyramids: store a downsampled dense field + sparse residual at higher levels
Thresholded wavelets: store significant coefficients across scales
This is where “holographic computation” tends to like sparsity most: you get global coherence at low-res and precision locally.
15.1.2.4 Stability implications
Sparse models distort physics/operators unless handled carefully:
Aliasing: missing support can create false structure under transforms
Bias: thresholding favors certain structures
Non-unitary transforms: sparse truncation breaks orthogonality → energy drift
Countermeasures:
Always track a conserved scalar (e.g., norm/energy proxy) and log drift
Use regularization and reprojection steps (see 15.2.4 and 15.3.4)
Treat sparsity as a modeling choice, not just a compression trick
15.1.3 Hierarchical model
Hierarchical structures are about scales: holography often has global low-frequency structure plus localized high-frequency detail.
15.1.3.1 Octrees / wavelet trees
Octree: recursively subdivide 3D space where detail exists
Great for localized detail and adaptive sampling.
Wavelet tree: coefficients across scales, usually with good compression
Great for invertible multiresolution transforms.
Common requirement: you must define a refinement criterion that is tied to reconstruction error or invariant deviation, not just magnitude.
15.1.3.2 Multigrid ladders
Multigrid is a hierarchy designed for solving inverse problems efficiently:
Coarse grids remove low-frequency error quickly
Fine grids refine detail
For holographic inversion (phase retrieval, deconvolution, wave equation inverse), multigrid-like ladders often dominate naive single-grid iterations.
15.1.3.3 Coarse/fine coupling
This is the hard part: moving information between levels without creating artifacts.
Key operators:
Restriction (R): fine → coarse
Prolongation (P): coarse → fine
Coupling patterns:
Additive correction: (x \leftarrow x + P(\delta x_c))
Residual correction: operate on residuals and correct fine state
You need to log and monitor:
mismatch energy injected at boundaries,
consistency of invariants across levels.
15.1.3.4 Deterministic indexing
Hierarchies become un-debuggable if indexing is not deterministic.
Use deterministic schemes:
Morton/Z-order keys for octree nodes
stable sorting for node lists
canonical child order
Determinism matters for:
reproducibility,
stable caching,
regression testing,
audit trails (15.4.4).
15.1.4 Streaming model
Streaming is mandatory when (N^3P) is too large for memory or when you need online results.
Streaming is not a “data format”; it’s a contract: only a window of the field is resident.
15.1.4.1 Chunking strategies
Chunk designs:
Spatial slabs (stream by z): easy for many PDE-like kernels
Bricks/tiles (e.g., (B^3)): better locality, harder boundary management
Frequency chunks: if operations are spectral-domain
Always decide:
chunk shape,
halo depth,
overlap strategy (especially for convolution/FFT).
15.1.4.2 Online reconstruction support
Online reconstruction typically requires:
incremental updates,
partial inversions,
checkpointed state,
monotonic improvement metrics.
Design pattern:
each chunk has a local state + boundary summary (a “boundary condition packet”)
local reconstruction integrates boundary packet, then emits an updated packet onward
15.1.4.3 Online invariant monitoring
Streaming demands invariants that can be estimated locally and aggregated:
chunk-local energy/norm estimates
boundary mismatch metrics
drift detectors (moving averages, robust stats)
Invariant monitoring is not “extra”; it’s what prevents silent divergence.
15.1.4.4 Checkpointing
Checkpointing must capture:
chunk states,
boundary packets,
operator schedules (what was applied, in what order),
random seeds (if stochastic steps exist),
invariant logs up to that point.
Use periodic checkpoints plus event-driven checkpoints (e.g., drift spike).
15.2 Flower — Operations on Structures
15.2.1 Apply transforms efficiently
We assume transforms are operators (T) acting on (X): linear, nonlinear, or composite.
15.2.1.1 Dense kernels
Dense-friendly kernels:
FFT-based propagation/convolution
separable transforms
batched GEMM over channels
stencils and PDE stepping
Dense optimization priorities:
fuse passes when safe,
tile space for cache,
avoid format conversions,
keep memory contiguous for dominant access axis.
15.2.1.2 Sparse kernels
Sparse-friendly kernels:
sparse gather/scatter + local per-voxel mixing
sparse convolution with neighbor lookup
sparse operator application (SpMV-like)
Priorities:
sort indices for coherence,
block sparsity whenever possible,
minimize indirection inside inner loops.
15.2.1.3 Hierarchical kernels
Hierarchical-friendly kernels:
wavelet analysis/synthesis
multigrid V-cycle operators
adaptive refinement/coarsening
Priorities:
stable restriction/prolongation
consistent boundary handling across levels
deterministic scheduling (same input → same traversal)
15.2.1.4 Streaming kernels
Streaming-friendly kernels:
overlap-add convolution
streaming FFTs per chunk with overlap
chunkwise stencils with halos
Priorities:
deterministic chunk boundaries,
explicit halo exchange format,
local invariant estimation + global aggregation.
15.2.2 Compose transforms
Transform composition is where you win orders of magnitude: many pipelines are slow because they apply 8 operators in sequence that could be 2.
15.2.2.1 Operator fusion
If you have operators (A, B) and can apply (B(A(X))) without writing the intermediate:
you save a full read+write of (X)
you reduce memory traffic and cache churn
Fusion rules must respect:
numerical stability,
invariant monitoring boundaries (don’t fuse across points where you need checkpoints).
15.2.2.2 Kernel rewriting
Rewrite operators into:
separable forms,
low-rank approximations,
preconditioned forms,
mixed-domain forms (spatial↔spectral)
Example pattern:
replace a global dense operator by (FFT → diagonal multiply → iFFT)
or replace repeated small convolutions by a fused stencil.
15.2.2.3 Cache-aware scheduling
Schedule transforms to:
reuse hot tiles,
avoid ping-ponging between representations,
keep data resident for as long as possible.
In hierarchies, schedule coarse updates before expensive fine updates when possible (multigrid logic).
15.2.2.4 Complexity reduction
Identify:
separability,
stationarity,
symmetry,
sparsity structure,
shared subexpressions.
Typical outcome:
reduce (O(N^3P \log N)) to “almost linear” in the active regions for sparse/hierarchical cases,
or reduce constant factors enough to fit real-time constraints.
15.2.3 Invert transforms
“Inversion” includes exact inversion, approximate inversion, and inverse problems.
15.2.3.1 Direct inversion
Use direct inversion when:
operator is orthonormal/unitary (FFT, wavelets)
operator is diagonalizable efficiently
Ensure correct scaling:
unitary FFT conventions prevent norm drift.
15.2.3.2 Iterative inversion
Iterative inversion is the default for holographic inverse problems:
CG / PCG (symmetric positive definite)
GMRES (general)
gradient descent / Landweber
ADMM / proximal splitting (with priors)
Data structure implications:
need fast “apply operator” and “apply adjoint”
sparse/hierarchical can dramatically reduce iteration cost
streaming enables out-of-core iterative methods with checkpointing
15.2.3.3 Regularized inversion
Regularization is not optional when:
measurements are incomplete/noisy,
sparsity truncation is used,
streaming chunking introduces boundary artifacts.
Common regularizers:
Tikhonov ((\lambda|x|^2))
TV / edge-preserving
sparsity priors (L1 / soft-thresholding in wavelet domain)
15.2.3.4 Partial inversion
Partial inversion is the workhorse for streaming and hierarchies:
invert coarse first, refine only where needed
invert local regions with boundary conditions from a coarse global pass
compute partial adjoints for chunk-local updates
15.2.4 Monitor invariants online
Invariant monitoring is the “control plane” that makes holographic computation robust.
15.2.4.1 Streaming estimators
Maintain estimators for:
norm/energy proxy (E(X))
residual norm (|Ax-b|)
constraint violation measures (corridor metrics)
Use incremental statistics:
running mean/variance,
exponentially weighted moving averages,
robust quantiles for spike detection.
15.2.4.2 Early abort triggers
Abort (or roll back) when you detect:
monotonic divergence in residual,
exploding norm drift,
repeated boundary mismatch spikes in streaming.
Abort is not failure; it is a safety mechanism.
15.2.4.3 Repair triggers
Repair actions:
renormalize energy (when appropriate)
reproject onto constraints (corridor projection)
increase precision temporarily
refine hierarchy locally
expand halos / overlap in streaming if boundary artifacts dominate
15.2.4.4 Logging formats
Log everything needed to reproduce and audit:
operator graph + parameters
data model choice + chunking parameters
seeds
invariants over time
checkpoint hashes (15.4.4)
Prefer structured logs (JSON Lines / protobuf) with stable schemas.
15.3 Cloud — Guarantees and Failure Modes
15.3.1 Numerical stability per structure
15.3.1.1 Dense stability issues
accumulation error over many passes
FFT scaling conventions
mixed precision pitfalls
boundary condition sensitivity
Mitigations:
compensated summation where needed
stable normalization and explicit scaling audits
deterministic reduction order (for reproducibility)
15.3.1.2 Sparse stability issues
irregular sampling → ill-conditioning
truncation breaks unitarity → energy drift
aliasing under transforms
Mitigations:
regularization
stable preconditioners
periodic reprojection / corridor enforcement
15.3.1.3 Hierarchical stability issues
coarse/fine mismatch
interpolation/prolongation artifacts
thresholding instability (wavelets)
Mitigations:
conservative restriction/prolongation pairs
level-consistent invariant accounting
hysteresis in refinement thresholds (prevents flip-flop)
15.3.1.4 Streaming stability issues
chunk boundary artifacts
state drift due to local approximations
asynchronous ordering differences
Mitigations:
overlap and halo exchange
boundary packet consistency checks
checkpoint+rollback strategy
15.3.2 Error propagation
15.3.2.1 Chunk boundary artifacts
Errors concentrate at seams when:
overlap is too small,
halos are inconsistent,
operators have longer spatial support than your chunk boundary assumptions.
Treat boundary mismatch as a first-class invariant.
15.3.2.2 Sparse aliasing artifacts
Sparse sampling interacts with transforms:
false high-frequency structure
phantom interference patterns
instability in inverse steps
Mitigate by:
multiresolution sparsity (store coarse everywhere),
regularized inversion,
consistency checks in transform domains.
15.3.2.3 Multiscale mismatch artifacts
Occurs when coarse fields cannot represent the boundary conditions required by fine fields.
Mitigate via:
better coupling operators,
coarse correction passes more often,
local refinement triggered by mismatch metrics.
15.3.2.4 Certified error bounds
A practical “certified bound” approach:
define a measurable residual (r = Ax-b)
track (|r|) plus operator norm estimates (or conservative upper bounds)
derive a bound on solution error under assumptions (condition number/preconditioner)
Even when you can’t produce tight proofs, consistent conservative bounds are extremely valuable operationally.
15.3.3 Memory-time trade-offs
15.3.3.1 Lower bounds on memory for accuracy
For many reconstructions, memory must store enough degrees of freedom to represent the target resolution and phase information—aggressive compression forces bias.
Practical interpretation:
if you want fine detail everywhere, dense or near-dense is unavoidable
if you can accept locality assumptions, sparse/hierarchical can win
15.3.3.2 Lower bounds on time for accuracy
