Time lower bound is often:
“you must at least read the relevant data”
“you must apply the operator at least once per iteration”
So asymptotically:
out-of-core streaming cannot beat the I/O floor
sparse cannot beat index traversal + compute floor
15.3.3.3 Pareto curves
You should explicitly produce Pareto curves for:
memory vs residual error
latency vs error
throughput vs stability (drift)
This is a chapter-level engineering deliverable: it tells you which model is justified.
15.3.3.4 Optimal regimes
Rule-of-thumb regimes:
Dense: when signal is everywhere or transforms are global and repeated
Sparse: when support is naturally small and stable under your operators
Hierarchical: when content is multiscale and you need fast inversion
Streaming: when data doesn’t fit or latency demands online processing
15.3.4 Corridor integration
“Corridor” here is the constraint envelope: invariants, stability bounds, physical plausibility constraints, and safety thresholds.
15.3.4.1 Corridor enforcement in streaming
Streaming corridor enforcement must be:
local (per chunk),
composable (aggregates globally),
checkpoint-compatible.
Use:
boundary mismatch corridors,
chunk energy corridors,
monotonic improvement corridors (where applicable).
15.3.4.2 Corridor enforcement in hierarchies
Corridors must be consistent across levels:
coarse corridor cannot permit states that fine corridor forbids
fine corridor violations should propagate back to coarse refinement triggers
15.3.4.3 Corridor enforcement in sparsity
Sparsity corridors include:
max sparsity ratio constraints,
minimum coarse coverage constraints,
anti-aliasing constraints (e.g., store low-frequency base densely)
15.3.4.4 Audit and reporting
Corridor enforcement is meaningless without:
logs,
deterministic replay,
summarized compliance reports (pass/fail + drift stats),
artifacts tied to hashable builds (15.4.4).
15.4 Fractal — Engineering Blueprint
15.4.1 Reference code architecture
15.4.1.1 Core data classes
A minimal, clean core often looks like:
DenseField(N, P, layout, dtype)
SparseField(index_format, block_shape, P, dtype)
HierField(levels, node_indexing, coupling_ops)
StreamField(chunk_shape, halo, schedule, checkpointing)
Plus a single shared abstraction:
FieldView: a view into any field (dense block, sparse block, hierarchical node, stream chunk)
15.4.1.2 Operator kernels
Kernels should be written against FieldView and dispatched:
dense backend (SIMD / GPU)
sparse backend (blocked sparse)
hierarchy backend (node-wise + coupling)
streaming backend (chunk-wise)
15.4.1.3 Verification modules
Verification is not just unit tests; it’s runtime monitors:
invariant monitors
corridor checkers
deterministic replay harness
numerical stability probes (precision toggles, scaling checks)
15.4.1.4 Benchmark modules
Benchmarks must cover:
bandwidth (read/write passes)
arithmetic throughput
end-to-end pipeline latency
stability metrics under stress (drift, mismatch spikes)
15.4.2 Performance profiling
15.4.2.1 CPU profiling
Track:
cache misses
vectorization
branch mispredicts (sparse/hierarchical often suffer)
NUMA effects for big volumes
15.4.2.2 GPU profiling
Track:
memory coalescing
occupancy
shared memory usage
kernel launch overhead (streaming pipelines can be launch-heavy)
15.4.2.3 Memory profiling
Track:
peak resident set
allocator fragmentation (especially with dynamic sparsity/hierarchies)
copy counts between host/device
15.4.2.4 IO profiling
For streaming:
throughput
chunk scheduling stalls
checkpoint write amplification
compression overhead
15.4.3 Test harness
15.4.3.1 Unit tests
indexing correctness
layout correctness (strides, alignment)
kernel correctness on tiny fixtures
15.4.3.2 Property tests
Randomized tests asserting invariants like:
round-trip transform stability
monotonic residual decrease (for specific algorithms)
determinism given fixed seed
15.4.3.3 Stress tests
extreme sparsity
adversarial chunk boundaries
multiscale refinement oscillations
worst-case operators (ill-conditioned inversions)
15.4.3.4 Regression tests
frozen golden outputs
golden invariant traces
performance regression thresholds (latency, bandwidth)
15.4.4 Reproducible builds
15.4.4.1 Deterministic seeds
global seed + per-module seed derivation
log seeds into checkpoints
deterministic reduction order where possible
15.4.4.2 Environment pinning
pinned compiler/toolchain versions
pinned GPU driver/runtime (where feasible)
pinned dependencies
15.4.4.3 Artifact hashing
Hash:
code commit
build config
operator graph + parameters
checkpoint content
So every output has a provenance chain.
15.4.4.4 Publication packaging
A publishable package should include:
exact build recipe
minimal runnable reproduction
benchmark scripts
invariant/corridor reports
representative datasets or synthetic generators
Continuity note
For continuity with earlier project notes and framing that connect “operator graphs,” “corridors,” and structured transition systems into a single design language, see the reference notes here:
Chapter 16 — Core Algorithms: Apply, Invert, Optimize, Learn Transformations
16.1 Square — Algorithmic Problem Statements
16.1.1 Forward problem
16.1.1.1 Compute (H' = T(H))
Let ((\mathcal{H}, d_{\mathcal{H}})) be a complete separable metric space of states (also called hypotheses, representations, or structures). Let ((\mathcal{Y}, d_{\mathcal{Y}})) be a complete separable metric space of outputs. A transformation is a (possibly partial) map[T:\operatorname{dom}(T)\subseteq \mathcal{H}\to \mathcal{Y}.]When (\mathcal{Y}=\mathcal{H}), (T) is an endomorphism; when additionally (T) is bijective with measurable inverse, (T) is an automorphism.
Parameterized families. Frequently (T) is drawn from a hypothesis class (\mathcal{T}) with parameter (\theta\in\Theta), written (T_\theta). Typical regimes include:
Finite-dimensional parametric: (\Theta\subset\mathbb{R}^p), (T_\theta) continuous in (\theta).
Operator-valued: (\Theta) indexes linear operators on a Hilbert space, (T_\theta\in\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H},\mathcal{Y})).
Programmatic (gate programs): (\Theta) indexes programs over a gate library; see §16.2.3.
Semantics via composition. For a gate library (\mathcal{G}) of primitive maps (g:\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{H}) (or typed variants (g:\mathcal{H}a\to\mathcal{H}b)), a program is a finite sequence (p=(g_1,\dots,g_L)) whose semantics is[T_p ;\coloneqq; g_L\circ\cdots\circ g_1,]defined on the set of inputs for which all intermediate states are in-domain:[\operatorname{dom}(T_p)=\left{H\in\mathcal{H}:; H{k}\in\operatorname{dom}(g{k+1});\forall k\in{0,\dots,L-1}\right},\quad H_0=H,; H_k=g_k(H_{k-1}).]
Forward computation task. Given (H\in\mathcal{H}) and a representation of (T), compute (\widehat{H'}) such that (\widehat{H'}\approx T(H)) under a specified accuracy model (see §16.1.1.3), and optionally return a certificate of correctness (see §16.1.1.4).
Admissible representations of (T). A forward solver must specify an admissible encoding of (T), such as:
Explicit closed form: (T(H)=\phi(H)) with computable (\phi).
Oracle access: (T) is accessible only through an evaluation oracle; complexity is counted in oracle calls.
Program representation: (T) is given as a gate program (p).
Implicit definition: (T) is defined as the solution map of an equation (F(H,H')=0), i.e. (H'=\arg\min_Y F(H,Y)) or (G(H,H')=0) (then forward evaluation is itself an inner solve).
Stochastic transformation: (T(H)) is a random variable; output is an estimator with probabilistic guarantees.
16.1.1.2 Complexity measures
Forward complexity must be stated with respect to an explicit computational model. Let (n) denote the problem size (dimension, number of nodes/edges, tensor order, sample count, bit-length, or composite descriptor). Let (\kappa) denote a conditioning parameter (when relevant).
Time complexity. Worst-case time ( \mathsf{Time}(n)) and expected time ( \mathbb{E}[\mathsf{Time}(n)]) for randomized methods. For oracle models, report query complexity (\mathsf{Q}(n)).
Space complexity. Peak memory ( \mathsf{Mem}(n)), including intermediate buffers.
Arithmetic complexity and bit complexity. In numerical settings, separate:
arithmetic operation count (floating-point ops),
and bit complexity when exact arithmetic or high precision is required.
Gate complexity. For gate programs (p=(g_1,\dots,g_L)), define:[\mathsf{GateLen}(p)=L,\qquad\mathsf{GateCost}(p)=\sum_{k=1}^L c(g_k; n_k),]where (n_k) is the size of the (k)-th intermediate representation and (c(\cdot)) is a gate-specific cost model.
Sample complexity. If forward evaluation uses sampling (Monte Carlo, randomized sketching), report the sample size (m) required for target tolerance (\varepsilon), typically (m=\Theta(\varepsilon^{-2}\log(1/\delta))) under sub-Gaussian assumptions.
Latency/throughput regimes. When (T) is applied repeatedly (batch inference, streaming transforms), report amortized cost:[\mathsf{Time}_{\text{amort}} = \frac{\mathsf{Precompute} + B\cdot \mathsf{PerItem}}{B},]and memory footprint of cached precomputations.
16.1.1.3 Accuracy requirements
Accuracy must be specified as a contract between the algorithm and the user. The contract includes: (i) an error metric, (ii) determinism vs probability, (iii) a domain of validity, and (iv) rounding/finite precision assumptions.
Normed error. When (\mathcal{Y}) is a normed space ((\mathcal{Y},|\cdot|)), common requirements include:
Absolute error: (|\widehat{H'}-T(H)|\le \varepsilon).
Relative error: (|\widehat{H'}-T(H)|\le \varepsilon |T(H)|) (with conventions if (T(H)=0)).
Mixed: (|\widehat{H'}-T(H)|\le \varepsilon_{\text{abs}}+\varepsilon_{\text{rel}}|T(H)|).
Metric error. In general metric spaces:[d_{\mathcal{Y}}(\widehat{H'}, T(H))\le \varepsilon.]
Probabilistic error. For randomized outputs:[\mathbb{P}\big(d_{\mathcal{Y}}(\widehat{H'}, T(H))\le \varepsilon\big)\ge 1-\delta.]
Distributional accuracy. When (H\sim \mathsf{P}), require:[\mathbb{E}{H\sim\mathsf{P}}\big[d{\mathcal{Y}}(\widehat{H'}(H), T(H))\big]\le \varepsilon,\quad\text{or}\quad\mathbb{P}\big(d_{\mathcal{Y}}(\widehat{H'},T(H))\le \varepsilon\big)\ge 1-\delta.]
Stability and backward error. In numerical linear algebra style, a forward method is often specified by backward error:[\widehat{H'} = T(H+\Delta H)\quad\text{with}\quad |\Delta H|\le \eta |H|.]Backward stability is preferred when the direct forward error depends on conditioning of (T).
Constraint-respecting accuracy. When outputs must satisfy invariants (\mathcal{I}\subseteq\mathcal{Y}) (e.g., positivity, normalization, graph validity), accuracy must include constraint satisfaction:[\widehat{H'}\in \mathcal{I}\quad\text{and}\quad d_{\mathcal{Y}}(\widehat{H'},T(H))\le\varepsilon,]or a quantified violation bound ( \operatorname{dist}(\widehat{H'},\mathcal{I})\le \tau).
16.1.1.4 Verification requirements
Verification requirements specify what must be checked, when, and with what strength.
Types of verification.
Semantic verification (functional correctness): show (\widehat{H'}) equals (T(H)) in exact settings or meets an error contract in approximate settings.
Invariant verification: check (\widehat{H'}\in\mathcal{I}) and/or intermediate states satisfy invariants.
Corridor verification: check that the computation remained within an allowed corridor (\mathcal{K}) of intermediate states (defined below).
Certificate production: produce a succinct witness (C) allowing an independent verifier to validate correctness faster than recomputation.
Corridors. A corridor is a subset (\mathcal{K}\subseteq \mathcal{H}) of admissible intermediate states. For a gate program (p) with intermediate states (H_k), corridor compliance is:[H_k\in \mathcal{K}\quad\forall k\in{0,\dots,L}.]More generally, a corridor predicate is (\Omega:\mathcal{H}\to{0,1}) or (\Omega:\mathcal{H}\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}) (violation score), and compliance is (\Omega(H_k)=1) or (\Omega(H_k)\le \tau).
Verification modes.
Online: verify at each step (k), enabling early termination and fault localization.
Offline: verify only final output and/or a sampled subset of steps.
Certificates. A certificate format is a triple ((C,\mathsf{Verify},\mathsf{Size})) such that:
(C=\mathsf{Prove}(H,T,\widehat{H'})) is generated during or after computation,
(\mathsf{Verify}(H,T,\widehat{H'},C)\in{\mathsf{accept},\mathsf{reject}}),
soundness: if (\mathsf{Verify}) accepts, then the stated accuracy/invariant conditions hold,
completeness: if conditions hold, (\mathsf{Verify}) accepts (possibly with probability (1-\delta)),
succinctness: (\mathsf{Size}(C)) and verification time are substantially smaller than full recomputation.
Typical certificates include: residual bounds (|F(H,\widehat{H'})|), interval arithmetic enclosures, dual certificates for convex programs, KKT residuals, and cryptographic commitments/hashes for reproducibility (§16.4.4).
16.1.2 Inverse problem
16.1.2.1 Solve (T(H)=H') for (H) or (T^{-1})
The inverse problem appears in two principal forms.
(A) State inversion. Given (T) and an observation (Y\in\mathcal{Y}) with model[Y = T(H) + \eta,]recover (H\in\mathcal{H}), with (\eta) representing noise, truncation, quantization, or adversarial perturbation.
(B) Operator inversion / identification. Given pairs ({(H_i, Y_i)}_{i=1}^N) with (Y_i\approx T(H_i)), estimate (T) or an approximation to (T^{-1}). This is an identification problem that may be cast as learning (§16.1.4), optimization (§16.1.3), or constrained program search (§16.2.3).
Inverse maps and generalized inverses. When (T) is not bijective, the relevant notion is a selection of preimages:[\mathcal{S}(Y)\subseteq {H\in\operatorname{dom}(T): T(H)=Y}.]In linear settings on Hilbert spaces, generalized inverses include the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse (T^\dagger), and regularized inverses (T_\lambda^{-1}) defined via penalized least squares.
Well-posedness (Hadamard). An inverse problem is well-posed if:
existence: at least one solution exists for admissible data,
uniqueness: the solution is unique (or an agreed selection rule is unique),
stability: the solution depends continuously on data:[d_{\mathcal{H}}(H^\star(Y),H^\star(\widetilde{Y}))\le L, d_{\mathcal{Y}}(Y,\widetilde{Y}).]
16.1.2.2 Ill-posedness taxonomy
Ill-posedness is classified by which Hadamard condition fails, and by the mechanism of failure.
(I) Non-existence.
Model infeasibility: (Y\notin \operatorname{range}(T)).
Domain violation: true (H\notin\operatorname{dom}(T)) under modeling assumptions.
Constraint infeasibility: additional constraints (\mathcal{C}(H)\le 0) incompatible with data.
(II) Non-uniqueness.
Nullspace ambiguity: there exist (\Delta H\ne 0) with (T(H+\Delta H)=T(H)).
Symmetry/gauge ambiguity: group action (G\curvearrowright \mathcal{H}) with (T(g\cdot H)=T(H)).
Underdetermined parametrization: (\dim(\mathcal{H})\gg \dim(\mathcal{Y})) or insufficient measurements.
(III) Instability.
Ill-conditioning: small perturbations in (Y) cause large changes in solutions.
Compact operators: in infinite dimensions, inverse typically unbounded.
Discretization instability: stable continuous inverse becomes unstable after discretization (or vice versa).
(IV) Structural mismatch.
Model mismatch: true map is (T^\star\ne T); inversion under (T) is biased.
Approximation mismatch: using simplified (T_\text{approx}) yields systematic error.
Nonlinear regime mismatch: local linearization is invalid; multiple basins.
(V) Adversarial / worst-case perturbations.
Outliers: sparse gross errors in (Y).
Adaptive noise: perturbations correlated with (H) or with the algorithm.
A complete inverse specification must state which regimes are assumed and which are guarded by corridor constraints and certificates.
16.1.2.3 Regularization definitions
Regularization enforces identifiability and stability by introducing prior structure, constraints, or selection principles.
Variational regularization. Define data-fidelity (\mathcal{D}:\mathcal{Y}\times\mathcal{Y}\to \mathbb{R}{\ge 0}) and regularizer (\mathcal{R}:\mathcal{H}\to\mathbb{R}{\ge 0}\cup{+\infty}). The canonical form is:[\widehat{H}\in\arg\min_{H\in\mathcal{H}} ; \mathcal{D}(T(H),Y);+;\lambda,\mathcal{R}(H).]Typical (\mathcal{D}): squared norm (|T(H)-Y|^2), KL divergence, Huber loss. Typical (\mathcal{R}): (|H|_2^2) (Tikhonov), (|H|1) (sparsity), total variation, nuclear norm, group norms, or indicator of a constraint set (I{\mathcal{K}}(H)).
Constrained regularization.[\min_{H};\mathcal{D}(T(H),Y)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad H\in\mathcal{K},]where (\mathcal{K}) encodes priors, feasibility, and invariants (positivity, monotonicity, topology, grammar constraints for structured objects).
Bayesian/MAP equivalence. If (Y|H\sim p(Y|H)) and (H\sim p(H)), then MAP estimation is:[\widehat{H}\in\arg\max_H \log p(Y|H)+\log p(H),]equivalent to the variational form with (\mathcal{D}=-\log p(Y|H)), (\mathcal{R}=-\log p(H)).
Source condition and regularization parameter. Parameter (\lambda) must be selected by a rule (discrepancy principle, cross-validation, evidence maximization, stability selection, or risk bounds). A rigorous spec includes:
the selection rule (\lambda=\Lambda(Y)),
measurability/stability of (\Lambda),
and sensitivity (\partial \widehat{H}/\partial \lambda) when differentiable.
Corridor-regularized inversion. For programmatic inverses, regularization may constrain intermediate iterates:[H_{k+1}=\mathsf{Update}(H_k;Y,T)\quad\text{with}\quad H_k\in\mathcal{K};\forall k,]or penalize corridor violations (\sum_k \rho(\Omega(H_k))).
16.1.2.4 Certification requirements
Inverse certification must handle approximation, non-uniqueness, and stability explicitly.
Residual-based certificates. For a candidate (\widehat{H}), certify small residual:[r\coloneqq \mathcal{D}(T(\widehat{H}),Y)\le \tau_r.]Residual alone is insufficient under non-uniqueness and ill-conditioning; therefore certificates must be paired with identifiability/regularization evidence.
Optimality certificates for convex formulations. If the inverse is posed as a convex program[\min_H f(H)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad H\in\mathcal{K},]then primal-dual certificates provide:
feasibility: (\operatorname{dist}(\widehat{H},\mathcal{K})\le\tau_{\text{feas}}),
dual feasibility and complementary slackness,
duality gap: (f(\widehat{H})-d(\widehat{\nu})\le \tau_{\text{gap}}).
Stability/conditioning certificates. Provide a bound linking perturbations in data to perturbations in solution. For locally smooth problems, one can certify a local Lipschitz bound using the inverse function theorem: if (T) is differentiable and (DT(\widehat{H})) is injective with smallest singular value (\sigma_{\min}>0), then locally[| \Delta H | \le \frac{1}{\sigma_{\min}} |\Delta Y| + \text{higher order}.]A certificate may include an estimate or lower bound on (\sigma_{\min}(DT(\widehat{H}))) (or a restricted variant) computed by power iteration or randomized SVD with failure probability (\delta).
Selection certificates under non-uniqueness. When multiple solutions exist, certify that (\widehat{H}) is the chosen selection (e.g., minimal (\mathcal{R})-norm solution):[\widehat{H}\in\arg\min{\mathcal{R}(H): T(H)=Y},]with accompanying KKT/dual witness.
Uncertainty quantification as certification. When full determinism is not attainable, provide a calibrated confidence region (\mathcal{U}(Y)\subseteq\mathcal{H}) such that[\mathbb{P}(H^\star\in \mathcal{U}(Y))\ge 1-\delta,]and report (\operatorname{diam}(\mathcal{U}(Y))) as a measure of ill-posedness.
16.1.3 Optimization problem
16.1.3.1 Minimize (\mathcal{C}(T)) subject to goals
Optimization targets transformations (operators/programs) rather than states, or targets states under transformations. The general form is:
Operator optimization.[\min_{T\in\mathcal{T}} ;\mathcal{C}(T)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad \mathsf{Goal}(T)\le 0,;; T\in\mathcal{T}_{\text{admissible}}.]
State optimization under a transform.[\min_{H\in\mathcal{H}} ; \mathcal{J}(H)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad \mathsf{Goal}(T(H))\le 0,;; H\in\mathcal{K}.]
Program optimization. If (T) is a gate program (p), optimization is over discrete objects:[\min_{p\in\mathcal{P}} ; \mathcal{C}(p)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad \mathsf{Goal}(p)\le 0.]Here (\mathcal{C}) may include length penalties, resource usage, and semantic costs, and (\mathsf{Goal}) encodes correctness, invariants, and corridor compliance.
Composite objectives. A typical decomposed cost is:[\mathcal{C}(T)=\alpha,\mathcal{C}{\text{perf}}(T) + \beta,\mathcal{C}{\text{resource}}(T) + \gamma,\mathcal{C}{\text{risk}}(T) + \zeta,\mathcal{C}{\text{verif}}(T),]with explicit definitions of each term and the measurement protocol.
16.1.3.2 Constraint handling methods
Let constraints be written as:[g_i(\theta)\le 0,\quad i=1,\dots,m,\qquad h_j(\theta)=0,\quad j=1,\dots,r,]for continuous parameters (\theta), or analogously for program variables.
Penalty methods. Solve unconstrained surrogates:[\min_\theta; f(\theta) + \mu \sum_{i} \phi(g_i(\theta)) + \mu\sum_j \psi(h_j(\theta)),]where (\phi,\psi) are nonnegative penalties (quadratic, hinge, exact (\ell_1)-style). Exact penalty choices admit finite (\mu) such that global minimizers coincide with constrained minimizers under constraint qualification.
Barrier methods. For inequality constraints:[\min_\theta; f(\theta) - \mu\sum_i \log(-g_i(\theta)),]defined on strictly feasible sets. Barrier methods maintain feasibility and converge to KKT points as (\mu\downarrow 0).
Projection and proximal operators. For convex (\mathcal{K}\subseteq\Theta), projected gradient:[\theta_{k+1}=\Pi_{\mathcal{K}}(\theta_k - \eta_k \nabla f(\theta_k)),]or proximal gradient for (f(\theta)=u(\theta)+v(\theta)) with (v) prox-friendly:[\theta_{k+1}=\operatorname{prox}_{\eta_k v}(\theta_k-\eta_k\nabla u(\theta_k)).]
Lagrangian and augmented Lagrangian. Define:[\mathcal{L}(\theta,\lambda,\nu)=f(\theta)+\sum_i \lambda_i g_i(\theta)+\sum_j \nu_j h_j(\theta),]with (\lambda_i\ge 0). Augmented Lagrangian adds quadratic penalties to improve conditioning and enable ADMM-like splitting.
Feasibility-first (corridor enforcement). For corridor predicates (\Omega(\cdot)), enforce hard constraints at each step:[\theta_{k+1}=\mathsf{Repair}\Big(\theta_k - \eta_k \nabla f(\theta_k)\Big),]where (\mathsf{Repair}) returns a feasible (\theta) and optionally a repair certificate (distance moved, constraints satisfied).
Discrete constraints for program search. Constraints on programs (typing, grammar, corridor admissibility) are enforced by:
typed grammar generation (only generate feasible programs),
pruning (discard partial programs that violate constraints),
repair (rewrite partial programs to restore feasibility),
verification-guided search (proof obligations gate expansions).
16.1.3.3 Multi-objective formalisms
Let objectives be a vector (F(\theta)=(f_1(\theta),\dots,f_q(\theta))) to be minimized.
Pareto dominance. (\theta) dominates (\theta') if (f_i(\theta)\le f_i(\theta')) for all (i) and strict inequality for at least one (i). The Pareto front is the set of nondominated feasible points.
Scalarization.
Weighted sum: minimize (\sum_i w_i f_i(\theta)), (w_i>0).
(\varepsilon)-constraint: minimize (f_1(\theta)) subject to (f_i(\theta)\le \varepsilon_i) for (i\ge 2).
Chebyshev: minimize (\max_i w_i|f_i(\theta)-z_i|) for a reference point (z).
Lexicographic optimization. Prioritize objectives by ordering (f_{\pi(1)},\dots,f_{\pi(q)}) and solve sequentially with feasibility sets restricted by earlier optima (or near-optima).
Programmatic multi-objective. In gate programs, objectives often include:
semantic error,
program length/latency,
memory footprint,
verification cost,
robustness margins.A complete specification must state the selection rule for returning a single program: e.g., minimal semantic error among programs under resource caps, with a secondary tie-breaker by verification strength.
16.1.3.4 Termination criteria
Termination must be tied to a certificate, not merely iteration count.
Continuous optimization termination.
Gradient norm: (|\nabla f(\theta_k)|\le \varepsilon_g) (for unconstrained smooth).
Projected gradient: (|\theta_k-\Pi_{\mathcal{K}}(\theta_k-\nabla f(\theta_k))|\le \varepsilon_{pg}).
KKT residual: combined primal feasibility, dual feasibility, complementary slackness residual norms (\le \varepsilon_{\text{KKT}}).
Duality gap (convex): (f(\theta_k)-d(\lambda_k,\nu_k)\le \varepsilon_{\text{gap}}).
Discrete/program search termination.
Optimality by shortest path: termination when the best open node has cost (\ge) best found solution (Dijkstra/A* conditions).
Budgeted termination: time/memory cap with best-so-far program and its verification certificate.
Proof-complete termination: termination when all proof obligations for candidate are discharged and no lower-cost candidate remains under admissible heuristics.
Stochastic termination.
Confidence-based: stop when estimated error/variance falls below threshold with probability (1-\delta).
Sequential tests: stop when improvement is statistically insignificant under a chosen test.
16.1.4 Learning problem
16.1.4.1 Learn generators from data
A generator is a parameterized mapping (G_\theta:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{H}) producing states or transformations. Learning generators covers regression, classification, structured prediction, and generative modeling.
Empirical risk minimization. Given data (\mathcal{D}={(x_i,y_i)}{i=1}^N), loss (\ell), and hypothesis class ({G\theta}), learn:[\widehat{\theta}\in \arg\min_{\theta\in\Theta};\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \ell(G_\theta(x_i), y_i);+;\lambda,\mathcal{R}(\theta).]
Operator learning. When learning a transformation (T_\theta:\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{Y}) from paired data:[\widehat{\theta}\in \arg\min_{\theta};\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \mathcal{D}(T_\theta(H_i),Y_i)+\lambda \mathcal{R}(\theta).]
Conditional generative learning. For stochastic generators (G_\theta(x,\xi)) with (\xi\sim \mathsf{P}_\xi), specify:
the target distribution (p(\cdot|x)),
divergence (D(\cdot,\cdot)) to minimize (KL, JS, Wasserstein),
and training objective (maximum likelihood, score matching, adversarial, diffusion, flow).
Constrained generator learning. If outputs must satisfy invariants (\mathcal{I}), enforce by:
architectural constraints (parameterization guaranteeing (\mathcal{I})),
projection/post-processing with error accounting,
constrained optimization with certificates.
16.1.4.2 Learn corridors from data
A corridor is a formalization of “allowed intermediate states.” Learning corridors means learning a predicate (\Omega) or set (\mathcal{K}) such that:
normal/valid behavior lies in (\mathcal{K}),
invalid/unsafe behavior lies outside (\mathcal{K}),
and (\mathcal{K}) is computationally enforceable.
Binary corridor learning. Given labeled states ({(H_i, z_i)}) with (z_i\in{0,1}), learn (\Omega_\phi:\mathcal{H}\to{0,1}) or score (s_\phi:\mathcal{H}\to\mathbb{R}) with thresholding.
One-class corridor learning. Given only valid states, learn a set estimate (\widehat{\mathcal{K}}) via density level sets, support estimation, energy-based models, or reconstruction error bounds. Specify the false rejection rate target (\alpha) and calibration protocol.
Corridor learning under shift. Under distribution shift, corridor learning must be robust:[\sup_{\mathsf{Q}\in \mathcal{B}(\mathsf{P})}\mathbb{E}{H\sim \mathsf{Q}}[\mathbf{1}{\Omega\phi(H)=0}]\le \alpha,]where (\mathcal{B}(\mathsf{P})) is an uncertainty set (e.g., Wasserstein ball). This links directly to distributionally robust optimization.
Reachability corridors. When intermediate states arise from dynamics (H_{k+1}=g_k(H_k)), corridors are invariant sets:[H_0\in \mathcal{K}\implies H_k\in \mathcal{K};\forall k.]Learning (\mathcal{K}) as an invariant requires coupling with the gate library: (\mathcal{K}) must be closed (or repairable) under gates.
16.1.4.3 Learn canonicalization rules
Canonicalization selects a unique representative from an equivalence class induced by symmetries, compressions, or invariants.
Equivalence relation. Let (\sim) be an equivalence relation on (\mathcal{H}). A canonicalization map is (C:\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{H}) such that:
(C(H)\sim H),
(C(C(H))=C(H)) (idempotence),
(H\sim H'\implies C(H)=C(H')) (class invariance).
Group-action canonicalization. If a group (G) acts on (\mathcal{H}), and equivalence is orbit equivalence (H\sim H'\iff \exists g\in G:; H'=g\cdot H), canonicalization chooses a section of the orbit space (\mathcal{H}/G).
Learning canonicalization. Learn (C_\phi) such that it approximates the above axioms:[\mathbb{E}\big[d(C_\phi(H),H)\big]\ \text{small under symmetry constraints},\quad\mathbb{E}\big[d(C_\phi(H),C_\phi(g\cdot H))\big]\ \text{small}.]A rigorous learning spec must include:
an enforcement mechanism for idempotence (architectural or penalty),
constraints for class invariance,
and a verification step that detects canonicalization failures.
16.1.4.4 Learn gate programs
Gate program learning synthesizes discrete compositions (p\in\mathcal{P}) from data. Let (\mathcal{G}) be a gate library and (\llbracket p\rrbracket=T_p) denote semantics.
Supervised program synthesis. Given pairs ((H_i, Y_i)) and optionally traces of intermediate steps, solve:[\min_{p\in\mathcal{P}};\sum_{i=1}^N \mathcal{D}(\llbracket p\rrbracket(H_i), Y_i);+;\beta,\mathcal{C}_{\text{len}}(p),]subject to typing and corridor constraints.
Neural-guided search. Learn a policy (\pi_\phi(g,|,\text{state})) that proposes gates, and use it to guide beam/A*/MCTS search (§16.2.3).
Reinforcement learning. Treat partial programs as states, gates as actions, and terminal reward as negative loss with penalties for corridor violations and resource budgets:[R(p)= -\mathcal{D}(\llbracket p\rrbracket(H),Y) - \beta \mathcal{C}(p) - \gamma \sum_k \rho(\Omega(H_k)).]Specify exploration, off-policy correction, and a verification stage.
Differentiable relaxation. For certain gate libraries, discrete selection can be relaxed by mixtures:[H_{k+1}=\sum_{g\in\mathcal{G}} \alpha_{k,g},g(H_k),\quad \alpha_{k,\cdot}\in\Delta,]followed by discretization. Correctness requires bounding the discretization gap and certifying the final discrete program.
16.2 Flower — Algorithm Families
16.2.1 Direct solvers
Direct solvers compute forward or inverse maps in a finite number of algebraic operations without iterative refinement (beyond fixed formulas). They are preferred when available due to predictability, exactness (in exact arithmetic), and well-characterized numerical stability.
16.2.1.1 Closed-form kernels
Linear maps. For (T(H)=AH) with explicit matrix (A), forward is (O(\mathrm{nnz}(A))) for sparse (A), or (O(n^2)) dense. Inverse uses:
direct factorization (LU/Cholesky/QR), complexity (O(n^3)) dense, or (O(\mathrm{fill})) sparse,
pseudoinverse via SVD when rank-deficient.
Convolutional kernels. When (T) is convolution on (\mathbb{Z}^d),[(Tx)[k]=(h*x)[k]=\sum_j h[j],x[k-j],]direct spatial computation is (O(n\cdot |h|_0)). Stability is controlled by kernel norms; invertibility depends on frequency response (see FFT kernels).
Integral transforms. For (T) defined by integral kernels (K):[(Tf)(y)=\int K(y,x) f(x),dx,]closed forms exist for certain (K) (Gaussian, polynomial) yielding explicit transforms or low-rank expansions.
Exact symbolic transforms. When (\mathcal{H}) is algebraic (formal expressions, graphs with rewrite rules), direct solvers may be terminating rewrite systems, with correctness by confluence + termination proofs.
16.2.1.2 FFT-based kernels
When (T) is diagonalizable by Fourier transform:[T = \mathcal{F}^{-1},\Lambda,\mathcal{F},]forward evaluation is:
compute (\widehat{x}=\mathcal{F}x),
multiply (\widehat{y}=\Lambda \widehat{x}),
invert (y=\mathcal{F}^{-1}\widehat{y}).
Complexity. (O(n\log n)) time, (O(n)) memory (up to constants and dimensions).
Inverse. If (\Lambda) has no zeros, inverse is immediate:[x = \mathcal{F}^{-1},\Lambda^{-1},\mathcal{F},y.]If (\Lambda) is near-zero, inversion is ill-conditioned; regularized inverse uses:[\Lambda_{\lambda}^{-1} = \frac{\overline{\Lambda}}{|\Lambda|^2+\lambda}.]
Accuracy contract. FFT-based methods require explicit rounding error models: numerical error depends on floating precision, condition number of (\Lambda), and normalization conventions.
16.2.1.3 Wavelet-based kernels
Wavelets provide sparse representations for localized, multi-scale structures. If (W) denotes a wavelet transform, and (T) is well-approximated by a sparse operator in wavelet coordinates:[T \approx W^{-1} S W,\quad S\ \text{sparse},]then forward is (O(n)) to (O(n\log n)) depending on sparsity.
Inverse/denoising. Regularization naturally acts in wavelet domain via thresholding:[\widehat{w}=\operatorname{shrink}(Wy;\lambda),\quad \widehat{x}=W^{-1}\widehat{w}.]Certification can include bounds on reconstruction error under known noise models.
16.2.1.4 Hybrid kernels
Hybrid methods combine:
FFT for global convolutional structure,
wavelets for local irregularities,
low-rank approximations for global correlations,
and direct sparse solvers for constraints.
Hybridization must specify interface contracts between components, including:
boundary conditions and padding conventions,
coordinate transforms and their inverses,
error propagation between stages,
and corridor checks at each stage.
16.2.2 Iterative solvers
Iterative solvers are required when (T) is implicit, high-dimensional, nonlinear, or when direct inversion is infeasible.
16.2.2.1 Gradient descent
For minimizing a differentiable objective (f(\theta)), gradient descent:[\theta_{k+1}=\theta_k-\eta_k\nabla f(\theta_k).]
Step size regimes.
Constant (\eta) under Lipschitz gradient for convex (f).
Diminishing (\eta_k) for stochastic objectives.
Line search (Armijo/Wolfe) for robust descent.
Convergence contract. Must specify:
assumptions (Lipschitz gradient, convexity, PL inequality),
target (global optimum vs stationary point),
rate (e.g., (O(1/k)), linear),
and stopping certificate (gradient norm, gap).
Numerical issues. Gradient computation may use automatic differentiation. For programmatic transforms (T_p), differentiability may require continuous relaxations or surrogate losses.
16.2.2.2 Proximal methods
For (f(\theta)=u(\theta)+v(\theta)) with smooth (u) and possibly nonsmooth convex (v), proximal gradient:[\theta_{k+1}=\operatorname{prox}{\eta_k v}(\theta_k-\eta_k\nabla u(\theta_k)).]The proximal operator is:[\operatorname{prox}{\eta v}(z)=\arg\min_\theta \left{ v(\theta)+\frac{1}{2\eta}|\theta-z|^2\right}.]
Proximal methods are central for sparse recovery, TV regularization, nuclear norm minimization, and constrained projections (where (v=I_\mathcal{K})).
16.2.2.3 ADMM variants
For problems:[\min_{x,z}; f(x)+g(z)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad Ax+Bz=c,]ADMM alternates updates and dual ascent.
Guarantee regime. Convexity plus mild regularity yields convergence of primal residuals and objective. Nonconvex ADMM variants require explicit assumptions and often only guarantee convergence to stationary points.
Corridor integration. Corridor checks can be applied after each primal update, with repair maps that preserve dual feasibility within bounded error.
16.2.2.4 Multigrid variants
Multigrid accelerates solves for structured linear systems (often arising from PDE discretizations) by transferring error correction across scales.
Key contract.
Define hierarchy of discretizations ({n_\ell}),
define restriction/prolongation operators,
define smoothing steps and coarse correction,
certify convergence factors under standard assumptions (ellipticity, smoothing property).
Multigrid can also be generalized to graph multilevel methods and hierarchical program search over compressed representations.
16.2.3 Program search
Program search treats transformations as paths in a graph defined by admissible gate compositions. This is the canonical approach when the transformation space is discrete or mixed discrete-continuous.
16.2.3.1 Gate-graph shortest path
Define a directed labeled multigraph (\mathcal{G!G}=(V,E)) where:
(V) are abstract states or canonicalized state classes,
(E\subseteq V\times \mathcal{G}\times V) edges correspond to applying a gate,
each edge (e=(u,g,v)) has cost (w(e)\ge 0),
corridor feasibility is encoded as constraints on vertices and edges.
A program corresponds to a path (u_0\to u_1\to\cdots\to u_L). The optimization objective is:[\min_{p:;u_0\to u_L} \sum_{k=1}^L w(u_{k-1},g_k,u_k),]subject to semantic correctness constraints (checked by verifier or predicted by heuristics).
Algorithms:
Dijkstra (nonnegative weights) for exact shortest path,
dynamic programming on DAGs,
bidirectional search when reversible.
16.2.3.2 Beam search and pruning
Beam search maintains a set of top-(B) partial programs by score. Let (\mathcal{S}(p)) be a score for partial program (p) (lower better). At each depth (k), expand and retain best (B).
Pruning rules.
Type pruning: discard ill-typed programs.
Corridor pruning: discard programs violating corridor at any prefix.
Dominance pruning: if two partial programs map to the same canonicalized intermediate state, retain only the one with lower cost (or lower cost-to-go bound).
Proof-guided pruning (§16.2.3.4): discard if proof obligations already fail.
Beam search is incomplete but practical for large spaces; completeness can be recovered by iterative widening (increase (B) until solution found under bounded-depth assumptions).
16.2.3.3 A* with corridor heuristics
A* minimizes (f(n)=g(n)+h(n)), where:
(g(n)) is cost so far,
(h(n)) is a heuristic lower bound on remaining cost to goal.
Admissibility. (h(n)\le \text{true remaining cost}) ensures A* returns optimal solutions.
Corridor-aware heuristic. Let (\Omega) be violation score and (\mathcal{K}) feasible corridor. Corridor-aware heuristics incorporate feasibility distance:[h(n) ;\coloneqq; h_{\text{semantic}}(n) + \lambda_h,\operatorname{dist}(H_n,\mathcal{K}),]with (\lambda_h) chosen so admissibility is preserved (often requiring (\operatorname{dist}) to be a lower bound on minimal repair cost).
16.2.3.4 Proof-guided pruning
Proof-guided pruning couples search with formal or semi-formal verification conditions.
Proof obligations. Associate each partial program (p_{1:k}) with obligations (\Pi(p_{1:k})) that must be satisfiable for any completion to reach the goal. If (\Pi(p_{1:k})) is unsatisfiable, prune.
Examples:
invariant preservation constraints,
domain/range constraints for future gates,
resource feasibility bounds,
symbolic constraints (e.g., required commutations, canonicalization constraints).
Proof-guided pruning yields exponential reductions when obligations are informative and cheap to check. It is the primary route to correct-by-construction synthesis.
16.2.4 Learning methods
16.2.4.1 Supervised generator fitting
Supervised learning of transforms is appropriate when paired data ((H,Y)) is available and the transform class is well-specified.
A rigorous training pipeline includes:
dataset split protocol (train/validation/test),
hyperparameter selection rule,
calibration of probabilistic outputs,
and evaluation under both in-distribution and stress-test perturbations.
16.2.4.2 Self-supervised constraints learning
When labels are unavailable, constraints can be learned from structure:
consistency constraints (cycle-consistency, invertibility checks),
invariance constraints (group actions),
reconstruction constraints (autoencoding),
contrastive objectives.
Self-supervised corridor learning must include explicit failure detection for shortcut solutions and degenerate minima (see mode collapse in §16.3.4.1).
16.2.4.3 Reinforcement learning for programs
RL is used to learn search policies, gate selection, and adaptive control of iterative solvers.
A rigorous RL spec must include:
state representation and Markov assumptions,
reward design with penalties for corridor violations,
off-policy evaluation (to avoid reward hacking),
and final verification of the synthesized program.
16.2.4.4 Generalization guarantees (when possible)
Guarantees depend on function class capacity, stability, and margin conditions. A learning method should specify:
hypothesis class complexity measure (VC dimension, Rademacher complexity, covering numbers),
stability (uniform stability bounds for SGD),
and an evaluation guarantee:[\mathbb{E}[R(\widehat{\theta})]-\inf_{\theta}R(\theta)\le \text{(estimation)}+\text{(optimization)}+\text{(approximation)}.]For program synthesis, generalization is often empirical; when formalizable, it relies on structural priors (grammar, invariants) and compression arguments (short programs generalize better under MDL-style principles).
16.3 Cloud — Guarantees and Limits
16.3.1 Convergence guarantees
16.3.1.1 Convex regimes
Assume (f:\mathbb{R}^p\to\mathbb{R}) is convex with (L)-Lipschitz gradient.
Gradient descent. With step size (\eta\in(0,2/L)):[f(\theta_k)-f(\theta^\star)\le \frac{|\theta_0-\theta^\star|^2}{2\eta k}.]If (f) is (\mu)-strongly convex, then linear convergence:[|\theta_k-\theta^\star|^2 \le (1-\eta\mu)^k |\theta_0-\theta^\star|^2.]
Proximal gradient. For (f=u+v) with (u) convex (L)-smooth and (v) convex proper lsc, proximal gradient yields (O(1/k)) objective convergence; accelerated variants yield (O(1/k^2)) under standard conditions.
ADMM. Under convexity and constraint qualifications, ADMM converges to a primal-dual solution; residual norms converge to zero.
Guarantees must explicitly specify which quantities are bounded and what is returned as a certificate (duality gap, KKT residual).
16.3.1.2 Nonconvex regimes
In nonconvex problems, global optimality is generally intractable; guarantees focus on stationarity and avoidance of pathological points.
Smooth nonconvex GD. Under (L)-smoothness, gradient descent satisfies:[\min_{0\le t<k}|\nabla f(\theta_t)|^2 \le \frac{2L(f(\theta_0)-f_{\inf})}{k}.]Thus an (\varepsilon)-stationary point requires (O(1/\varepsilon)) iterations in this bound.
Saddle escape. With noise (explicit perturbations or stochastic gradients), algorithms can escape strict saddles under appropriate conditions, yielding convergence to approximate second-order stationary points.
Nonconvex proximal/ADMM. Require explicit assumptions (Kurdyka–Łojasiewicz property, coercivity, boundedness of iterates). Guarantees typically ensure subsequence convergence to critical points.
A rigorous spec must state: stationarity notion (first-order, second-order), tolerance, and failure probability if randomized.
16.3.1.3 Stochastic regimes
Let (f(\theta)=\mathbb{E}[\ell(\theta;\xi)]) with stochastic gradient (g(\theta;\xi)), (\mathbb{E}[g]=\nabla f), (\mathbb{E}|g-\nabla f|^2\le \sigma^2).
SGD (nonconvex). With appropriate diminishing step sizes:[\min_{t<k}\mathbb{E}|\nabla f(\theta_t)|^2 = O!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{k}}\right)]under standard bounded variance assumptions.
Variance reduction. SVRG/SAGA-style methods improve rates for finite-sum objectives, requiring explicit memory/time trade-offs.
Stochastic regimes require the algorithm to specify confidence bounds, logging of variance estimates, and reproducibility of randomness seeds (§16.4.4).
16.3.1.4 Certified convergence diagnostics
Certified diagnostics provide evidence that the algorithm reached the claimed regime.
Examples:
bounds on gradient norms computed with verified arithmetic,
duality gap bounds for convex programs,
monotonic energy descent with certified line search,
stability diagnostics: small perturbations in initialization/data do not change solution beyond tolerance (local stability check),
corridor satisfaction logs with cryptographic hashes of the trace.
A certified diagnostic is a certificate (C) such that verification time is sublinear in the total runtime or uses aggregated sufficient statistics.
16.3.2 Complexity lower bounds
16.3.2.1 Data requirements
Data lower bounds are information-theoretic: given a model class and noise model, specify the minimum sample size (N) such that any estimator achieves error (\le\varepsilon) with probability (1-\delta).
Typical forms:[N \ge \Omega!\left(\frac{\mathrm{complexity}(\mathcal{F})+\log(1/\delta)}{\varepsilon^2}\right)]for parametric regression under sub-Gaussian noise, and more elaborate bounds for structured recovery (sparsity, low-rank).
A manuscript-level specification must state the assumed noise model, adversary model, and identifiability conditions.
16.3.2.2 Time requirements
Optimization oracle lower bounds state that under first-order oracle access, certain convergence rates are optimal (e.g., (O(1/k)) for smooth convex GD without acceleration constraints, (O(1/k^2)) for accelerated methods). For nonconvex, stationarity may require (\Omega(1/\varepsilon)) steps in worst case under Lipschitz gradient assumptions.
For program search, worst-case time is exponential in minimal program length absent strong structure; lower bounds may be stated in terms of branching factor and indistinguishability of partial programs under limited observations.
16.3.2.3 Memory requirements
Memory lower bounds arise in:
streaming models (must store enough to represent sufficient statistics),
sketching models (dimension reduction constraints),
RL/off-policy evaluation (buffering trajectories).
A rigorous spec states whether the algorithm operates in:
single-pass streaming,
multi-pass,
or random access model,and reports memory required for the target guarantee.
16.3.2.4 Trade-off curves
Trade-offs must be stated explicitly, typically as Pareto frontiers between:
accuracy vs runtime,
accuracy vs memory,
accuracy vs sample size,
robustness vs bias.
A canonical form is:[\varepsilon(\mathsf{Time},\mathsf{Mem},N) \le \varepsilon_0]or its inverse (minimum resources to achieve (\varepsilon_0)). For complex systems, empirical trade-off curves are benchmarked (§16.4.2) and paired with theoretical constraints where available.
16.3.3 Stability under perturbation
Let (Y = T(H) + \eta) with perturbation (\eta), and let (\mathsf{Alg}(Y)) produce (\widehat{H}).
Stability describes bounds of the form:[d_{\mathcal{H}}(\mathsf{Alg}(Y), \mathsf{Alg}(\widetilde{Y})) \le \Psi(d_{\mathcal{Y}}(Y,\widetilde{Y})),]with explicit (\Psi).
16.3.3.1 Noise sensitivity
For linear inverse problems (Y=AH+\eta), sensitivity depends on conditioning of (A). For regularized solutions:[\widehat{H}_\lambda = \arg\min_H |AH-Y|^2+\lambda |H|^2,]noise amplification is bounded by (|(A^\top A+\lambda I)^{-1}A^\top|), which decreases as (\lambda) increases at the cost of bias.
For nonlinear problems, local sensitivity is governed by Jacobians and curvature; robust design requires either global regularization or corridor restrictions preventing entry into high-sensitivity regions.
16.3.3.2 Truncation sensitivity
Truncation (limited resolution, finite basis expansion, early stopping) induces bias. Stability requires explicit truncation error bounds, e.g.,[|T(H)-T_m(H)|\le \tau(m),]where (T_m) is an (m)-term approximation. Early stopping in iterative methods acts as implicit regularization; its stability must be stated as a function of iteration count and noise level.
16.3.3.3 Model mismatch sensitivity
If the true operator is (T^\star) but inversion uses (T), the reconstruction error decomposes into:[| \widehat{H}-H^\star| \le \text{(estimation error under }T);+;\text{(bias from }T^\star-T).]A robust spec must explicitly quantify mismatch bounds (|T^\star-T|) in an operator norm or task-specific metric, and include mitigation (domain adaptation, robust losses, uncertainty sets).
16.3.3.4 Robust algorithm design
Robust design includes:
robust losses (Huber, Tukey, quantile),
distributionally robust objectives,
adversarial training,
certified bounds via interval/convex relaxations,
corridor design to avoid unstable regimes,
and verification of robustness margins (e.g., certify (\widehat{H}) remains valid for all (\eta) in a set (\mathcal{E})).
16.3.4 Algorithm failure modes
16.3.4.1 Mode collapse
In generative learning, mode collapse occurs when the generator maps many inputs/noise values to a limited set of outputs, failing to represent the target distribution.
Detection requires:
coverage metrics (precision/recall for generative models),
diversity measures conditional on inputs,
and stress tests for rare modes.
Mitigation includes:
alternative divergences (Wasserstein, f-divergences with gradient penalties),
explicit diversity regularizers,
ensemble methods,
and corridor constraints enforcing minimum entropy or coverage.
16.3.4.2 Divergence
Iterative methods may diverge due to:
step sizes violating stability conditions,
non-Lipschitz gradients,
numerical overflow/underflow,
or accumulation of approximation error in nested solvers.
A robust implementation must include:
safeguard line searches or trust regions,
gradient clipping and overflow checks,
monotonicity checks and rollback,
and termination-on-instability with diagnostic certificates.
16.3.4.3 Overfitting noise
Inverse solutions may fit noise when regularization is insufficient or when optimization is run too long.
Mitigation includes:
explicit regularization with parameter selection rules,
early stopping calibrated by validation or discrepancy principle,
and uncertainty quantification with rejection options when identifiability is low.
16.3.4.4 Corridor violations
Corridor violations occur when intermediate states leave admissible regimes, causing:
semantic invalidity (illegal structures),
numerical instability,
or safety violations.
Mitigation includes:
online corridor enforcement hooks (§16.4.3.1),
repair operators with bounded distortion,
proofs of invariance of (\mathcal{K}) under gates,
and audit logs of violations with replayable traces.
16.4 Fractal — Reference Implementations and Benchmarks
16.4.1 Minimal algorithm library
A minimal library defines stable interfaces for forward evaluation, inversion, optimization, and learning, with consistent typing, reproducibility, and verification hooks.
16.4.1.1 Forward API
Core interface. Let (\mathsf{Transform}) be an object representing (T), and (\mathsf{State}) represent (H).
A reference typed interface:
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Protocol, Generic, TypeVar, Optional, Sequence, Mapping, Any
H = TypeVar("H") # state type
Y = TypeVar("Y") # output type
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class AccuracySpec:
abs_tol: float
rel_tol: float = 0.0
failure_prob: float = 0.0 # δ; 0 => deterministic contract
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class VerifySpec:
require_invariants: bool = True
require_corridor: bool = False
require_certificate: bool = False
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ForwardResult(Generic[Y]):
value: Y
certificate: Optional[bytes] = None
diagnostics: Optional[Mapping[str, Any]] = None
class Transform(Protocol[H, Y]):
def apply(self, h: H, *, acc: AccuracySpec, verify: VerifySpec) -> ForwardResult[Y]:
...
Required semantics.
apply must be referentially transparent relative to explicit randomness seeds (no hidden nondeterminism).
If failure_prob>0, diagnostics must report empirical checks supporting the probability contract (e.g., sample variance, concentration bound parameters).
If require_certificate=True, certificate must be sufficient for an independent verifier.
16.4.1.2 Inverse API
Inverse solvers must expose:
regularization choice and parameters,
solution selection rules under non-uniqueness,
and certificates of feasibility/optimality/stability.
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RegularizationSpec:
kind: str # e.g. "tikhonov", "l1", "tv", "constraint"
lam: float
extra: Mapping[str, Any] = None
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class InverseResult(Generic[H]):
estimate: H
certificate: Optional[bytes] = None
diagnostics: Optional[Mapping[str, Any]] = None
class InverseSolver(Protocol[H, Y]):
def invert(
self,
transform: Transform[H, Y],
y: Y,
*,
reg: RegularizationSpec,
acc: AccuracySpec,
verify: VerifySpec
) -> InverseResult[H]:
...
16.4.1.3 Optimization API
Optimization APIs must support:
constraints and corridor enforcement,
multiobjective selection rules,
termination certificates.
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TerminationSpec:
max_iters: int
time_budget_s: float | None = None
grad_tol: float | None = None
kkt_tol: float | None = None
dual_gap_tol: float | None = None
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class OptimizeResult(Generic[Any]):
solution: Any
certificate: Optional[bytes] = None
diagnostics: Optional[Mapping[str, Any]] = None
class Optimizer(Protocol):
def optimize(self, *, term: TerminationSpec, verify: VerifySpec) -> OptimizeResult:
...
16.4.1.4 Learning API
Learning APIs must fix:
dataset interface,
training randomness controls,
evaluation metrics,
and artifact tracking for reproducibility.
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TrainSpec:
seed: int
epochs: int
batch_size: int
lr: float
extra: Mapping[str, Any] = None
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TrainResult:
params: bytes # serialized θ
metrics: Mapping[str, float]
artifacts: Mapping[str, str] # paths or hashes
certificate: Optional[bytes] = None
class Learner(Protocol):
def fit(self, data: Any, *, spec: TrainSpec, verify: VerifySpec) -> TrainResult:
...
16.4.2 Benchmarks
Benchmarks must be defined as protocols, not merely datasets.
16.4.2.1 Accuracy benchmarks
Accuracy benchmarks specify:
input distribution (\mathsf{P}) or test set construction,
ground truth definition (exact, high-precision, or consensus),
metric (d_{\mathcal{Y}}) or task loss,
reporting: mean, quantiles, worst-case, and failure rates.
Include:
unit tests for known identities,
property-based tests (invariants preserved),
adversarial stress tests (worst-case perturbations within a set).
16.4.2.2 Runtime benchmarks
Runtime benchmarks specify:
hardware and software environment,
warm-up protocol,
measurement method (wall-clock vs CPU cycles),
batching and parallelism settings,
and variability reporting (median, IQR, tail latencies).
16.4.2.3 Memory benchmarks
Memory benchmarks specify:
peak resident set size,
GPU memory if applicable,
per-sample vs batch scaling,
and memory-time trade-offs (checkpointing, recomputation).
16.4.2.4 Robustness benchmarks
Robustness benchmarks specify perturbation models:
additive noise with specified distribution,
structured corruption (occlusion, missing data, outliers),
model mismatch scenarios,
and distribution shift.
Report robustness curves:[\varepsilon \mapsto \text{performance under perturbation magnitude }\varepsilon,]and certify where possible (bounds or verified envelopes).
16.4.3 Verification integration
Verification must be integrated as a first-class runtime feature, not an afterthought.
16.4.3.1 Corridor enforcement hooks
A corridor enforcement hook is a function called on intermediate states:[\mathsf{Hook}_{\text{corr}}(k, H_k)\to (\mathsf{ok}, H_k', C_k),]returning:
whether the state is admissible,
an optional repaired state (H_k'),
and a per-step certificate (C_k).
The corridor contract must specify:
admissibility predicate (\Omega),
repair map properties (bounded distortion, idempotence),
and aggregation of per-step certificates into a final certificate.
16.4.3.2 Invariant monitoring hooks
Invariant hooks evaluate algebraic or structural invariants (mass conservation, normalization, graph validity, typing):[\mathsf{Inv}(H_k)=\text{(value, violation)}.]Monitoring must log violations, trigger corrective actions, and optionally halt execution if invariants are safety-critical.
16.4.3.3 Reconstruction monitoring hooks
For inverse and learning systems, reconstruction monitors evaluate:
data-fit residuals,
regularization contributions,
stability indicators (Jacobian condition estimates),
and out-of-distribution alarms.
Monitoring must be defined with explicit thresholds and statistical calibration.
16.4.3.4 Certificate generation hooks
Certificate hooks produce:
primal-dual witnesses,
residual bounds,
trace hashes,
and audit logs.
Certificates must be:
versioned (format stability),
independently verifiable,
and robust to floating-point nondeterminism via deterministic computation modes or verified arithmetic.
16.4.4 Reproducibility packaging
16.4.4.1 Deterministic configs
A deterministic configuration includes:
algorithm identifier and version,
full hyperparameter set,
random seeds (per component),
hardware determinism flags (where supported),
and explicit control of parallelism and nondeterministic kernels.
16.4.4.2 Artifact hashes
All artifacts must be content-addressed. For each artifact (A), store:[\mathsf{hash}(A)=\mathrm{Hash}(\text{bytes}(A)),]and include a manifest mapping logical names to hashes. Manifests must be signed or otherwise protected if used as verification anchors.
16.4.4.3 Environment lockfiles
Environment specification must include:
language runtime versions,
dependency lockfiles,
OS/library versions for numerical reproducibility (BLAS, CUDA),
and compiler flags when relevant.
16.4.4.4 Publication bundle
A publication bundle must contain:
source code (including exact versions),
configuration manifests,
benchmark scripts and datasets or dataset generation code,
trained model checkpoints (if any) with hashes,
certificates and verification scripts,
and a minimal reproduction guide specifying the command sequence required to regenerate key tables/figures.
Chapter 16 — Core Algorithms: Apply, Invert, Optimize, Learn Transformations
17 — Verification, Certificates, and Reproducible Builds
17.1 Square — What Must Be Proven and What Must Be Tested
Verification is the disciplined production of checkable evidence that a constructed artifact satisfies a specification under explicitly declared assumptions. This chapter fixes (i) a contract for what constitutes correctness, (ii) certificate objects that carry the evidence, (iii) deterministic evaluation rules that make verification reproducible, and (iv) pipeline designs that remain reliable under numerical and adversarial stress.
17.1.1 Kernel correctness obligations
Let a kernel be the minimal trusted evaluator that validates certificates.
Definition 17.1 (Kernel). A kernel is a tuple[\mathcal{K} ;=; (\mathsf{Spec},;\mathsf{Artifact},;\mathsf{Cert},;\mathsf{Verify}),]where:
(\mathsf{Spec}) is a specification language with a denotation map (\llbracket\cdot\rrbracket) into semantic objects (constraints, transforms, invariants, reconstruction tasks, and tolerances).
(\mathsf{Artifact}) is the artifact domain to be verified (programs, models, datasets, compiled binaries, proofs, or composite bundles).
(\mathsf{Cert}) is a certificate domain (structured evidence objects).
(\mathsf{Verify}(\sigma,a,c)\in{\mathsf{ACCEPT},\mathsf{REJECT}}) is a deterministic verification function.Determinism is a correctness requirement: the same ((\sigma,a,c)) must yield the same output for all conforming implementations of (\mathcal{K}).
Kernel correctness decomposes into four obligations, each with a “provable core” and a “testable surface.” The provable core is the part of the obligation that can be verified by finite, machine-checkable certificates. The testable surface is the part that must be supported by statistical or empirical evidence when the provable core is infeasible or undecidable.
17.1.1.1 Constraint correctness
Constraints formalize admissible states, solutions, or outputs.
Definition 17.2 (Constraint system). A constraint system is a pair[\mathcal{C} = (g,h)]with equality constraints (g:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^p) and inequality constraints (h:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^q). The feasible set is[\mathrm{Feas}(\mathcal{C})={x\in\mathbb{R}^n: g(x)=0,; h(x)\le 0}.]
Definition 17.3 (Residual vector and compliance). For a candidate (x), define the residual[r_{\mathcal{C}}(x)=\begin{bmatrix}g(x)\\max(h(x),0)\end{bmatrix},\quad|r_{\mathcal{C}}(x)|{\star}]for a declared norm (|\cdot|{\star}) (must be specified). Constraint correctness under tolerance (\varepsilon\ge 0) is[|r_{\mathcal{C}}(x)|_{\star}\le \varepsilon.]
Kernel obligation (Constraint correctness). A certificate must either:
carry a proof object establishing exact feasibility (when exact arithmetic is used), or
carry a verified bound (\overline{\rho}) such that (|r_{\mathcal{C}}(x)|_{\star}\le \overline{\rho}\le \varepsilon) (when floating-point is used), or
carry a statistically qualified bound with explicit uncertainty labeling when neither (1) nor (2) is achievable.
The kernel must never “assume feasibility by construction.” Every acceptance must be traceable to checkable evidence plus declared uncertainty.
17.1.1.2 Reconstruction correctness
Reconstruction covers the situation where only partial or corrupted observations are available, and the system outputs a reconstructed object intended to match a latent truth.
Definition 17.4 (Reconstruction problem). A reconstruction problem is a tuple[\mathcal{R}=(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y},A,M,\Pi,\mathcal{L},\varepsilon),]where:
(\mathcal{X}) is the latent space, (\mathcal{Y}) the observation space,
(A:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{Y}) is a forward operator,
(M:\mathcal{Y}\to\mathcal{Y}) is a mask/corruption operator (possibly random but deterministically generated under the certificate’s RNG rules),
(\Pi:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{X}) is a feasibility projector or regularizer-induced constraint,
(\mathcal{L}:\mathcal{Y}\times\mathcal{Y}\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}) is a loss,
(\varepsilon) is the required error envelope.
Given observation (y), a reconstruction algorithm outputs (\hat{x}\in\mathcal{X}). The reconstruction error is measured by a declared metric (d_{\mathcal{X}}) and/or a task loss (\mathcal{L}):[d_{\mathcal{X}}(\hat{x},x)\le \varepsilon\quad \text{or} \quad\mathcal{L}(M(A(\hat{x})),y)\le \varepsilon.]
Kernel obligation (Reconstruction correctness). A certificate must:
specify the mask (M) via a deterministic protocol (seed + generation algorithm + version),
specify the solver configuration and stopping criteria,
provide a checkable optimality/witness condition (e.g., KKT residuals or dual certificates) sufficient to bound reconstruction error, and
include an error envelope derivation: analytic, verified-numeric, or statistically qualified with uncertainty.
Reconstruction correctness is not solely “low loss”; it is “low loss plus a defensible bound connecting loss to latent error under declared assumptions.”
17.1.1.3 Invariant correctness
Invariants are properties required to remain stable under permitted transformations, across time, and across implementations.
Definition 17.5 (Invariant suite). An invariant suite is a finite family[\mathcal{I}={I_j}_{j=1}^m,]where each (I_j:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{V}_j) maps a state to a value in a metric space ((\mathcal{V}_j,d_j)). An invariant may be exact (discrete) or approximate (continuous).
Definition 17.6 (Invariant preservation). For a transformation (T:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{X}), invariants are preserved within tolerance vector (\delta\in\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^m) if[d_j!\left(I_j(T(x)),,I_j(x)\right)\le \delta_j\quad \forall j.]
Kernel obligation (Invariant correctness). A certificate must:
declare the invariant suite and the metrics (d_j),
identify the transformation(s) under which invariants must hold,
provide checkable invariant computations (with verified numeric bounds if floating-point is used),
provide drift controls and attribution hooks when invariants are tracked over time.
Invariant correctness is a system property: it constrains both artifacts and the verification tools themselves.
17.1.1.4 Admissibility correctness
Admissibility governs whether a transformation is permitted: semantic equivalence, stability, invertibility requirements, and domain/range conformity.
Definition 17.7 (Admissibility). Let (\llbracket\cdot\rrbracket) map artifacts to semantics (e.g., program behavior, model function, theorem statement). A transformation (T) is admissible for specification (\sigma) if it satisfies all required predicates:[\mathsf{Admissible}\sigma(T) ;\equiv;\mathsf{Domain}\sigma(T)\wedge\mathsf{SemEq}\sigma(T)\wedge\mathsf{Stability}\sigma(T)\wedge\mathsf{Invert}\sigma(T)\wedge\mathsf{InvariantPres}\sigma(T).]
Typical components:
Domain/range correctness: (T) maps well-formed artifacts to well-formed artifacts.
Semantic equivalence: (\llbracket T(a)\rrbracket=\llbracket a\rrbracket) (exact) or equals within specified observational equivalence.
Stability: small input perturbations cannot cause unbounded output perturbations beyond declared limits.
Invertibility: required when (T) must be reversible or auditable via inverse witnesses.
Invariant preservation: as in §17.1.1.3.
Kernel obligation (Admissibility correctness). A certificate must supply evidence for each required predicate at the strength demanded by (\sigma), using the evidence hierarchy of §17.1.4. When full proof is infeasible, the certificate must carry explicitly labeled test evidence and its statistical limitations.
17.1.2 Certificate formats
Certificates are first-class artifacts: structured, versioned, hash-addressable objects designed for deterministic validation.
17.1.2.1 Human-readable proof objects
A human-readable proof object is a structured narrative that mirrors machine-checkable content and provides auditability.
Requirements.
Alignment: every human-readable claim must reference a machine-checkable artifact identifier (hash) or a formally stated lemma whose proof is included elsewhere.
Non-ambiguity: all tolerances, norms, and environments must be explicit; no reliance on unstated defaults.
Stable rendering: proofs must be renderable deterministically (fixed ordering, fixed numeric formatting rules, fixed symbol resolution).
Human-readable proof objects are not a substitute for machine-checkable logs; they are an audit lens over them.
17.1.2.2 Machine-checkable logs
Machine-checkable logs are linear or DAG-structured sequences of verification events that a verifier can replay.
Definition 17.8 (Verification log). A log is a sequence of typed records ((e_i)_{i=1}^N) where each event has:
a type tag,
a canonical payload,
a parent reference (for DAG logs),
a content hash.
Replay property. For a log (\mathcal{L}), verification is replayable if:[\mathsf{Replay}(\sigma,a,\mathcal{L})=\mathsf{ACCEPT};\Rightarrow;\mathsf{Verify}(\sigma,a,c)=\mathsf{ACCEPT}]for the certificate (c) whose log root is (\mathrm{root}(\mathcal{L})). Replay must be deterministic and independent of wall-clock time, machine scheduling, and locale.
Machine-checkable logs must support:
local verification (each step checkable with bounded context),
progressive disclosure (ability to truncate or stream logs while maintaining integrity),
selective auditing (Merkle proofs for subcomponents).
17.1.2.3 Hashable artifact structure
Certificates and all referenced artifacts must be hash-addressed to prevent substitution attacks and to support caching and reproducibility.
Definition 17.9 (Content-addressed DAG). A certificate bundle is a DAG where each node payload (P) is serialized canonically to bytes (\mathrm{ser}(P)) and addressed by[h(P)=H(\mathrm{ser}(P)),]for a declared cryptographic hash (H). Node references use hashes only.
Integrity invariant.
Any change to payload bytes changes (h(P)).
The bundle’s root hash commits to the entire transitive closure.
Canonicality requirement. Canonical serialization is mandatory; otherwise hashing is not stable. Canonicality is enforced by §17.1.3.4.
17.1.2.4 Versioned certificate schemas
Certificates are long-lived and must remain verifiable across toolchain evolution.
Definition 17.10 (Schema ID). Every certificate carries:
(\mathsf{schema_name}),
(\mathsf{schema_semver}=(M,m,p)) (major/minor/patch),
(\mathsf{schema_hash}) (hash of the schema definition),
(\mathsf{kernel_compat}) (minimum kernel version that can verify it).
Compatibility rules.
Patch versions may clarify constraints without changing semantics.
Minor versions may add optional fields; verifiers must ignore unknown optional fields.
Major versions may change semantics; verification requires an upgraded kernel or an explicit migration certificate.
Migration certificates. If a certificate must be translated to a new major schema, the translation itself is an admissible transformation and must be certified under §17.2.4.
17.1.3 Determinism rules
Determinism is a verification invariant: acceptance must not depend on hidden nondeterminism.
Definition 17.11 (Deterministic verification environment). An environment descriptor (E) includes:
OS and kernel ABI constraints (when relevant),
CPU/ISA constraints and floating-point mode constraints,
compiler/toolchain versions,
library versions and build flags,
RNG and scheduling discipline configuration,
serialization canonicalization configuration.
A certificate must bind (E) by hash and must declare whether verification is:
environment-independent (portable determinism), or
environment-scoped (determinism guaranteed only within declared constraints).
17.1.3.1 RNG discipline
Randomness must be explicit, reproducible, and auditably derived.
Rules.
No ambient entropy: no use of system RNG, time-based seeds, or nondeterministic hardware RNG during verification.
Seed derivation: seeds must be derived from certificate content and explicit labels:[\mathsf{seed}(label)=\mathrm{PRF}(\mathsf{root_seed},,label),]with a declared PRF and root seed.
Stream discipline: each randomized subroutine consumes a dedicated stream identified by a label; streams may not share state.
Sampling commitments: masks, subsets, or random challenges used in verification must be either fully materialized in the certificate or regenerable exactly from committed seeds and algorithms.
Deterministic mask protocol (example requirement).
The certificate specifies: distribution family, stratification, rejection rules, and the exact iteration order used to draw samples.
The verifier must reproduce the same sample sequence bit-for-bit.
17.1.3.2 Floating-point discipline
Floating-point verification is permitted only with explicit stability controls.
Floating-point contract.
Type contract: all numeric types are fixed (e.g., IEEE-754 binary64) and declared.
Rounding contract: rounding mode is fixed; flush-to-zero and denormal behavior are declared.
Operation ordering: reductions must be deterministic; parallel reductions must be prohibited or replayed from recorded order.
Library determinism: BLAS/LAPACK/GPU kernels may be used only when deterministic mode is available and enabled; otherwise verification must fall back to deterministic CPU routines.
Verified numerics option: critical bounds must be computed using interval arithmetic, exact rationals, or correctly-rounded libraries.
Certified numeric bound requirement. Any acceptance based on a floating-point inequality must either:
prove a separation margin (\gamma>0) such that the computed value is at least (\gamma) away from the threshold under a conservative error bound, or
label the result as uncertain and downgrade evidence level.
17.1.3.3 Scheduling discipline
Concurrency is a major source of nondeterminism and must be controlled.
Rules.
Single-threaded verification is the default.
Deterministic parallelism is permitted only if:
task graphs are explicitly defined,
reduction orders are fixed,
scheduling is either deterministic by construction or recorded in the certificate.
No data races: verification code must be free of races; any shared mutable state must be prohibited or protected under deterministic ordering.
If schedule recording is used, the schedule log becomes part of the hash-addressed certificate DAG.
17.1.3.4 Serialization discipline
Canonical serialization is required for hashing, replay, and cross-implementation consistency.
Requirements.
Canonical map ordering: keys sorted by a declared order (e.g., UTF-8 byte order).
Canonical numeric encoding: fixed endianness; fixed NaN payload policy; fixed representation for (-0).
No locale dependence: no locale-sensitive formatting.
Stable timestamps: timestamps are prohibited in certificates except as declared metadata that does not influence verification; any such metadata must be excluded from hash commitments.
Serialization discipline applies uniformly to:
certificate payloads,
logs,
proof objects,
environment descriptors,
benchmark outputs.
17.1.4 Evidence hierarchies
Evidence must be labeled by strength; verification acceptance is conditioned on required evidence levels.
Definition 17.12 (Evidence levels). Evidence levels are partially ordered:
Theorem proofs (Level P): machine-checked proofs in a sound logic; acceptance is proof-theoretic.
Verified numerics (Level N): bounds certified with correct rounding / interval methods; acceptance is numeric-theoretic.
Statistical evidence (Level S): probabilistic guarantees with explicit confidence and power; acceptance is inferential.
Empirical experiments (Level E): observational evidence without formal confidence bounds; acceptance is heuristic.
The order is: (P \succ N \succ S \succ E). A specification (\sigma) declares, per obligation type, the minimum acceptable level.
Composition rules.
Evidence must not be silently upgraded: (E) cannot masquerade as (S), and (S) cannot masquerade as (N).
Mixed evidence is permitted only with explicit aggregation logic and failure modes (e.g., “accept if (P holds) OR (N holds) OR (S holds with (\alpha\le 10^{-6})”).
Certified uncertainty labeling.
When evidence is below the required level, the verifier must produce a rejection or a labeled “conditionally accepted for exploratory use” result if and only if (\sigma) permits it.
Uncertainty labels are part of the certificate and are hash-committed.
17.2 Flower — Verification Pipelines
Verification must be engineered as a pipeline that transforms artifacts into certificates and rejects or repairs failures with full traceability.
Definition 17.13 (Pipeline). A verification pipeline is a composition of stages:[\mathsf{Pipe}=\mathsf{Ingest}\circ \mathsf{Normalize}\circ \mathsf{Check}\circ \mathsf{Summarize}\circ \mathsf{Commit},]where:
Ingest loads artifacts and specs and pins environment descriptors.
Normalize canonicalizes serialization, ordering, and numeric formats.
Check runs obligation-specific verifiers, producing logs and witnesses.
Summarize produces human-readable proof objects consistent with logs.
Commit builds the hash-addressed bundle and emits a root hash.
The pipeline must be idempotent under determinism rules: rerunning it yields identical root hashes for identical inputs.
17.2.1 Constraint verification
Constraint verification certifies feasibility and provides diagnostics when infeasibility occurs.
17.2.1.1 Residual computation
Residual computation must be stable and auditably bounded.
Residual algorithm contract.
The certificate must declare evaluation strategy for (g(x)) and (h(x)): exact, interval, or floating-point with error bounds.
The verifier must compute a bound (\overline{\rho}) such that:[|r_{\mathcal{C}}(x)|_{\star} \le \overline{\rho}.]
Verified residual bound (interval method).Let (\mathbf{g}(x)) denote an interval enclosure of (g(x)), and (\mathbf{h}(x)) an interval enclosure of (h(x)). Define:[\mathbf{r}{\mathcal{C}}(x)=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{g}(x)\\max(\mathbf{h}(x),0)\end{bmatrix}.]Then (\overline{\rho}) can be taken as an upper bound on (|\mathbf{r}{\mathcal{C}}(x)|_{\star}) computed via interval arithmetic. Acceptance requires (\overline{\rho}\le \varepsilon).
Floating-point residual bound (separation margin).If interval methods are not used, the certificate must include:
a bound (\eta) on evaluation error of (r_{\mathcal{C}}(x)),
a computed residual (\hat{\rho}),such that (\hat{\rho}+\eta\le \varepsilon).
17.2.1.2 Projection checks
Projection checks verify feasibility by comparing the candidate to a projection onto the feasible set (or a relaxable approximation).
Let (P_{\mathcal{C}}:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathrm{Feas}(\mathcal{C})) be a projector (exact or approximate). Define:[d_{\mathcal{C}}(x)=|x-P_{\mathcal{C}}(x)|.]A projection-based check is acceptable only if the certificate also provides a theorem or bound connecting (d_{\mathcal{C}}(x)) to residual (|r_{\mathcal{C}}(x)|_{\star}). Without this connection, projection distance is diagnostic but not certifying.
Projection certificate requirements.
explicit projector definition and version,
stopping criteria,
proof/bound: (d_{\mathcal{C}}(x)\le \delta\Rightarrow |r_{\mathcal{C}}(x)|_{\star}\le \varepsilon) under declared regularity assumptions (e.g., constraint qualification).
17.2.1.3 Sensitivity checks
Sensitivity checks assess how fragile feasibility is to perturbations.
Let (J_g(x)) and (J_h(x)) denote Jacobians. Define a conditioning indicator, for example:[\kappa_g(x)=|J_g(x)^{\dagger}|,\quad\kappa_{\text{act}}(x)=|J_{h_{\text{act}}}(x)^{\dagger}|,]where (\dagger) is the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse and (h_{\text{act}}) are active inequalities.
Sensitivity certificate requirements.
method used to compute (\kappa) (SVD, QR with pivoting, interval-SVD),
numeric stability flags,
thresholds that trigger trust-boundary warnings (§17.3.1.4),
local linearization error model or explicit disclaimer if not computed.
Sensitivity checks do not typically reject; they gate trust and produce certified warnings.
17.2.1.4 Repair verification
Repair is a controlled transformation producing a new candidate (x') intended to satisfy constraints while preserving admissibility requirements.
Repair protocol.
Produce a repair proposal (x'=\mathsf{Repair}(x)).
Verify constraint correctness of (x') per §17.2.1.1.
Verify admissibility of repair transformation (domain, semantics, invariants) per §17.2.4.
Repair certificates must include both the pre-repair and post-repair hashes, the repair algorithm identity, and the differential witness (what changed).
17.2.2 Reconstruction verification
Reconstruction verification ensures that the reconstruction procedure is reproducible and that its output is boundedly correct under declared assumptions.
17.2.2.1 Mask sampling protocols
Mask sampling must be deterministic and auditable.
Mask protocol requirements.
RNG root seed and derivation labels (§17.1.3.1),
mask family (uniform, stratified, block, adversarial stress masks),
sampling order, rejection rules, and termination bounds,
materialization policy: either store the mask explicitly or store a regenerable recipe.
Mask protocols must support replay across implementations; no dependence on unordered hash maps, thread interleavings, or floating-point RNGs.
17.2.2.2 Reconstruction solvers
Reconstruction solvers must have checkable stopping and correctness witnesses.
Consider a canonical convex reconstruction form:[\hat{x}\in\arg\min_{x\in\mathcal{X}} ;; \mathcal{L}(M(A(x)),y) + \lambda R(x),]with regularizer (R).
Solver certificate requirements.
algorithm identity and version (e.g., proximal gradient, ADMM, interior-point),
deterministic hyperparameters and stopping criteria,
primal residual, dual residual, and complementary slackness residuals where applicable,
objective gap bound (\overline{\Delta}) or KKT residual bound (\overline{\kappa}) sufficient to certify an error envelope.
If the solver is nonconvex, the certificate must state the evidence level downgrade and provide either multiple restarts with deterministic seeds or a verified local optimality check when available.
17.2.2.3 Error envelope estimation
Error envelopes connect observed fit to latent error.
Envelope types.
Analytic envelope: derived from identifiability and stability assumptions (e.g., restricted isometry, Lipschitz constants).
Verified-numeric envelope: uses interval methods to bound Lipschitz constants, operator norms, or condition numbers.
Statistical envelope: uses resampling or concentration bounds with declared (\alpha), (\beta) and multiple-testing corrections.
Envelope certificate requirements.
a formal statement of the envelope theorem:[\mathcal{L}(M(A(\hat{x})),y)\le \varepsilon_L ;\Rightarrow; d_{\mathcal{X}}(\hat{x},x)\le \varepsilon]under explicit assumptions,
evidence that assumptions hold (proof, verified numeric check, or statistically qualified check),
explicit failure modes and triggers (§17.3.1.4).
17.2.2.4 Regression tests
Regression tests ensure that reconstruction pipelines remain stable across code changes.
Requirements.
