fixed, versioned test suites and golden certificates,
deterministic tolerances: acceptance must not depend on nondeterministic floating-point fluctuations,
stored failure-minimizing counterexamples for any detected regression,
continuous integration gates that require identical certificate root hashes for unchanged inputs.
17.2.3 Invariant verification
Invariant verification maintains continuity across transformations and time.
17.2.3.1 Multi-scale invariant computation
Multi-scale invariants are computed at progressively refined resolutions to detect both gross and subtle drift.
Let (S_k) be a scale parameter (e.g., discretization level). Invariant evaluation produces:[I^{(k)}_j(x), \quad k=0,1,\dots,K.]Multi-scale preservation requires:[d_j!\left(I^{(k)}_j(T(x)),,I^{(k)}_j(x)\right)\le \delta^{(k)}_j \quad \forall j,k.]
Certificate requirements.
explicit scale ladder definition,
verified numeric stability of refinement operators,
aggregation rule from multi-scale results to a single accept/reject decision.
17.2.3.2 Drift monitoring
Drift is a time-series phenomenon in invariant space.
Let (t\mapsto x_t) be a sequence of artifacts or states. Define invariant traces:[v_{j,t}=I_j(x_t).]
Monitoring requirements.
a declared drift statistic (D_{j,t}) (e.g., moving z-score, CUSUM, EWMA),
declared thresholds (T_j) and actions: warn, tighten corridor, reject,
deterministic computation and storage of sufficient statistics.
Drift monitoring must produce certificate increments that are composable into a long-horizon audit log.
17.2.3.3 Drift attribution
Attribution identifies causes of drift: which transformation(s), input shifts, or numerical regimes contributed.
Attribution contract.
transformations must be logged with hash identities and declared domains,
sensitivity analysis (Jacobian influence, ablations, or counterfactual replays) must be deterministic,
attribution must output a ranked list of causes with evidence levels.
17.2.3.4 Drift correction
Correction applies admissible transformations to restore invariants.
Correction protocol.
Detect drift and produce an attribution report.
Propose correction transformation (C).
Verify admissibility of (C) (§17.2.4).
Verify invariants after correction (§17.2.3.1).
Emit a correction certificate that links pre- and post-correction roots and includes the rationale chain.
17.2.4 Transform verification
Transform verification certifies that transformations are admissible, stable, invertible where required, and semantically equivalent.
17.2.4.1 Admissibility tests
Admissibility tests are the concrete realization of §17.1.1.4 predicates.
Minimum admissibility record.
transform identity and version,
preconditions and postconditions (typed),
domain/range checks (well-formedness),
invariant suite obligations,
any required semantic equivalence conditions.
Admissibility tests must produce machine-checkable witnesses (e.g., typed derivations, SMT witnesses, or verified numeric bounds).
17.2.4.2 Stability tests
Stability tests certify that numerical and semantic behavior does not explode under small perturbations.
A typical stability condition is Lipschitz continuity:[d_{\mathcal{Y}}(F(x),F(x'))\le L, d_{\mathcal{X}}(x,x')]for a declared constant (L) (global or local).
Stability certificate requirements.
definition of metrics (d_{\mathcal{X}},d_{\mathcal{Y}}),
bound on (L) (proof or verified numeric bound),
regime detectors for instability (floating-point cancellation, ill-conditioning),
stress tests with deterministic adversarial perturbations (§17.3.2.1).
17.2.4.3 Invertibility tests
Invertibility is required when transformations must be undone or audited.
Invertibility obligations.
existence: (T) is injective on the admissible domain or admits a left-inverse (T^{-1}),
reconstruction: compute (x=T^{-1}(T(x))) within tolerance,
stability: inverse mapping is not catastrophically ill-conditioned.
Certificate requirements.
inverse algorithm identity and determinism declaration,
residual and envelope checks for inversion,
explicit ill-conditioning warnings when applicable (§17.3.1.2).
17.2.4.4 Program equivalence tests
Program equivalence and semantic preservation are central but often undecidable.
Two-tier strategy.
Translation validation (local proof): for each transformation, prove equivalence for the specific input artifact rather than for all programs. This yields checkable witnesses (e.g., proof-carrying compilation).
Property-based relational testing (fallback): when proof is infeasible, execute paired tests ((p, T(p))) under deterministic generators and report statistical limitations.
Equivalence certificate requirements.
equivalence relation definition (observational equivalence, bisimulation, refinement),
proof object or test suite specification,
counterexample minimization: any failure must output a deterministic minimal counterexample trace.
17.3 Cloud — Trust, Limits, and Adversarial Conditions
Verification is meaningful only when its trust boundaries are explicit. This section defines regimes where correctness cannot be certified at the desired strength and mandates warning and uncertainty labeling.
17.3.1 Numerical trust boundaries
17.3.1.1 Floating-point instability regimes
Floating-point instability regimes include:
catastrophic cancellation,
subtractive loss of significance,
overflow/underflow transitions,
non-associative reductions affecting thresholds.
Instability detectors.
ulp-distance checks near thresholds,
interval vs float divergence checks,
condition-number-triggered escalation.
A verifier must escalate from Level E/S to Level N when thresholds are tight and numeric risk is high, or emit certified uncertainty.
17.3.1.2 Ill-conditioning regimes
Ill-conditioning undermines the reliability of residuals, inverses, and sensitivity estimates.
Certified conditioning indicators.
singular value lower bound (\underline{\sigma}_{\min}),
condition number upper bound (\overline{\kappa}),
rank deficiency witnesses.
If (\overline{\kappa}) exceeds declared trust bounds, acceptance must either:
require verified numerics, or
emit certified warnings and downgrade evidence.
17.3.1.3 Discretization mismatch regimes
Discretization mismatch arises when the artifact is evaluated under one discretization but claimed correct under another.
Mismatch controls.
discretization parameters must be certificate-committed,
refinement comparisons must be provided for multi-scale invariants,
acceptance must be scoped to the declared discretization unless a refinement theorem is included.
17.3.1.4 Certified warning triggers
Warnings must be deterministic, structured, and hash-committed.
Warning schema requirements.
warning code,
severity (info / caution / critical),
triggering inequality and values,
recommended escalation (e.g., require interval bounds),
affected obligations and evidence level downgrades.
Warnings are not free text; they are machine-actionable objects.
17.3.2 Adversarial robustness
Robustness treats the artifact and verification process as targets of adversarial manipulation.
17.3.2.1 Adversarial perturbations
Define a perturbation set (\Delta) (input-level, observation-level, code-level):[x' = x + \delta,\quad \delta\in\Delta.]Perturbation sets must be explicit: norm-balls, structured corruptions, targeted mask patterns, or semantic-preserving but numerically hostile transforms.
Certificates must declare:
perturbation model,
attack generator determinism,
evaluation metrics under attack.
17.3.2.2 Robust invariant suites
A robust invariant suite strengthens §17.1.1.3 by requiring stability under perturbations:[\sup_{\delta\in\Delta} d_j!\left(I_j(T(x+\delta)),,I_j(x)\right)\le \delta_j.]Bounds may be certified via verified numerics (interval propagation) or conservative relaxations.
17.3.2.3 Certified robustness bounds
Robustness bounds must be certified, not merely observed.
Bound forms.
interval bounds (exact enclosures),
convex relaxations with certified optimality gaps,
worst-case Lipschitz bounds.
The certificate must include the derivation path and any relaxations used; relaxations must be admissible transformations (§17.2.4).
17.3.2.4 Failure reporting
Failures must be reproducible and actionable.
Failure certificate requirements.
minimal counterexample artifact hash,
full deterministic replay log,
classification: constraint failure / invariant failure / admissibility failure / numeric trust failure,
suggested triage path: which stage and which assumption failed.
17.3.3 Verification impossibility
Certain regimes cannot be fully verified at Level P/N.
17.3.3.1 Undecidable regimes (when applicable)
Undecidability arises in general program equivalence, termination, and rich semantic properties.
Kernel rule.
If the obligation is undecidable in the general case, the specification must state the restricted fragment under which Level P is expected, or must permit a lower evidence level with explicit uncertainty labeling.
17.3.3.2 Incomplete evidence regimes
Evidence can be incomplete due to missing assumptions, unavailable bounds, or unverified dependencies.
Requirement.
Any dependency not verified at the required level must be listed in the certificate dependency graph with its evidence level; acceptance is conditioned on policy.
17.3.3.3 Statistical limitations
Statistical evidence must explicitly report:
confidence level ((1-\alpha)),
power ((1-\beta)),
multiple comparison corrections (when relevant),
distributional assumptions and diagnostics.
No acceptance may silently rely on underpowered tests.
17.3.3.4 Certified uncertainty labeling
Uncertainty is a first-class output.
Uncertainty label requirements.
obligation affected,
evidence level achieved,
reason for downgrade,
quantitative uncertainty bounds where possible,
policy disposition (reject / warn / accept-for-exploration).
17.3.4 Corridor governance
Corridor governance formalizes budgets and adaptive tightening/loosening mechanisms that keep verification reliable and cost-controlled.
17.3.4.1 Budget design principles
Budgets are constraints on:
computational cost (time, memory),
numerical risk (conditioning, ulp margins),
statistical risk (false accept probability),
operational risk (adversarial exposure).
Budgets must be declared in (\sigma) and enforced by the pipeline.
17.3.4.2 Automatic tightening/loosening
Adaptive governance adjusts tolerances and evidence requirements based on observed stability.
Policy form.
tighten when drift increases or numeric risk escalates,
loosen only when long-run evidence supports it at the required level.
All policy transitions must be logged and certified.
17.3.4.3 Auditing policies
Auditing requires:
periodic re-verification under updated kernels,
random spot checks of certificate subtrees via Merkle proofs,
adversarial red-team runs against perturbation models.
Audit outcomes are published as certificate bundles.
17.3.4.4 Public reporting formats
Public reports must be derived deterministically from certificates and must include:
artifact root hashes,
environment descriptors,
evidence level summaries,
warning and uncertainty summaries,
reproducibility instructions (exact commands and inputs pinned by hash).
17.4 Fractal — Tooling and Build System
Verification must be supported by tooling that makes correctness and reproducibility routine rather than heroic.
17.4.1 Verification tooling
17.4.1.1 Unit test frameworks
Unit tests validate local, deterministic properties.
Must run under deterministic scheduling.
Must forbid ambient randomness.
Must fail with minimal reproducible counterexamples.
Unit tests are insufficient alone for acceptance but are mandatory to protect the kernel and pipeline.
17.4.1.2 Property test frameworks
Property tests validate invariants across wide input distributions.
Requirements.
deterministic generators with labeled RNG streams,
shrinking to minimal counterexamples,
coverage metrics committed to logs,
explicit statistical interpretation when used for acceptance.
17.4.1.3 Benchmark frameworks
Benchmarks validate performance claims without corrupting determinism.
Requirements.
benchmark inputs pinned by hash,
CPU frequency scaling controls or environment scoping,
separated “measurement artifacts” from “verification artifacts” (performance is not correctness).
Benchmarks may be included as Level E/S evidence for performance corridors but must not be confused with correctness.
17.4.1.4 Certificate frameworks
Certificate frameworks provide:
schema definitions and validators,
canonical serialization libraries,
Merkle DAG construction and verification,
replay engines for logs,
human-readable renderers linked to machine evidence.
The framework itself must be part of the trusted computing base and must be heavily unit- and property-tested.
17.4.2 Build reproducibility
Build reproducibility ensures that binaries and artifacts can be regenerated identically.
17.4.2.1 Environment pinning
Environment pinning requires:
exact toolchain versions (compiler, linker, interpreter),
exact dependency versions (including transitive),
hermetic build inputs (no network fetches during build),
declared platform constraints.
Environment descriptors are hash-committed as part of certificate bundles.
17.4.2.2 Deterministic builds
Deterministic builds require:
fixed timestamps (or stripped timestamps),
stable file ordering,
stable symbol tables where applicable,
deterministic code generation options enabled.
Any nondeterministic build step must be eliminated or its output must be normalized deterministically.
17.4.2.3 Artifact hashing
All build outputs are content-addressed.
The hash algorithm is declared and versioned.
Hashes are computed over canonical bytes (post-normalization).
The final release artifact is identified by a root hash over a DAG including binaries, sources, schemas, and verification logs.
17.4.2.4 Release process
A release is valid only if:
it is reproducibly buildable from pinned sources,
it includes verification certificates for all required obligations,
it includes audit metadata and evidence summaries,
it is signed under a declared signing policy (signature policy itself is versioned and hash-committed).
17.4.3 Documentation generation
Documentation is part of the verification surface: it must be derived from sources and certificates deterministically.
17.4.3.1 Auto-generated theorem index
A theorem index is generated from proof objects and includes:
theorem identifiers,
statement hashes,
proof artifact hashes,
dependencies.
Any rendered theorem must map to machine-checkable proof content.
17.4.3.2 Auto-generated API docs
API docs must be generated from:
source code annotations,
schema definitions,
verification contracts embedded in types.
Rendered docs must include versioned references and hashes.
17.4.3.3 Auto-generated benchmark reports
Benchmark reports must:
separate raw measurements from derived summaries,
include environment descriptors,
include statistical methodology and confidence intervals when claimed.
17.4.3.4 External extraction bundle
An extraction bundle provides:
minimal sources required to reproduce verification,
schemas and validators,
deterministic scripts for replay.
The extraction bundle is itself hash-addressed and referenced by release certificates.
17.4.4 Publication artifacts
17.4.4.1 Reproducible notebooks/scripts
Notebooks/scripts must:
be parameterized with inputs by hash,
record environment descriptors,
produce outputs that are content-addressed and verifiable.
Interactive outputs must never be the sole evidence; they must link to certificates.
17.4.4.2 Data bundles
Data bundles must:
include provenance,
include integrity hashes for all files,
include schema and type declarations,
include licensing metadata without affecting verification hashes.
17.4.4.3 Proof bundles
Proof bundles must include:
machine-checkable proofs and logs,
proof toolchain pinning,
proof replay instructions,
dependency DAG hashes.
17.4.4.4 Versioned releases
Each release is a versioned, content-addressed snapshot that includes:
artifacts,
certificates,
environment descriptors,
documentation outputs,
audit summaries.
A release is verified only when the kernel accepts all required certificates under the release’s declared policy.
Chapter 17 — Verification, Certificates, and Reproducible Builds
18 Predictive Modeling on (H^4): Forecasting, Simulation, Inference
18.0 Preliminaries and Problem Setting
Let ((\Omega,\mathcal{F},\mathbb{P})) be a probability space. Let time be indexed by (t\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}) (discrete-time), with an optional step size (\Delta t>0). The predictive state space (H^4) is defined as a structured product space[H^4 ;\coloneqq; \mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{U}\times\mathcal{G}\times\Sigma,]where:
(\mathcal{X}) is the hologram state manifold (typically a finite-dimensional differentiable manifold embedded in (\mathbb{R}^d) or a function space discretized to (\mathbb{R}^d)); elements are denoted (x\in\mathcal{X}).
(\mathcal{U}) is the invariant/summary space; elements are denoted (u\in\mathcal{U}) (e.g., conserved quantities, topological signatures, energy-like scalars, or learned invariants).
(\mathcal{G}) is a Lie group (or semigroup) of transforms/operators acting on (\mathcal{X}); elements are denoted (T\in\mathcal{G}). The action is written (T\cdot x).
(\Sigma) is a space of certificates, i.e., machine-checkable witnesses that constraints, invariants, or safety conditions hold (or fail with a quantified counterexample); elements are denoted (\sigma\in\Sigma).
An (H^4)-state is therefore[h_t ;=; (x_t,u_t,T_t,\sigma_t)\in H^4.]
Observation model
Let (\mathcal{Y}) be an observation space. Observations (y_t\in\mathcal{Y}) arise from a partial, noisy measurement channel[y_t ;=; \mathcal{O}(x_t; \eta_t),\qquad \eta_t\sim \nu,]where (\mathcal{O}) may include occlusions, subsampling, sensor nonlinearity, quantization, and adversarial corruption.
Engine dynamics and parameters
Let (p\in\mathcal{P}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^k) denote hidden parameters. The engine (\mathcal{Q}p) defines stochastic dynamics on (\mathcal{X}):[x{t+1} ;=; \mathcal{Q}p(x_t, a_t, \xi_t),\qquad \xi_t\sim \mu,]where (a_t\in\mathcal{A}) denotes an optional control/transform selection action. Operator evolution may be modeled as[T{t+1} ;=; \Phi(T_t, x_t, a_t, \zeta_t), \qquad \zeta_t\sim \rho,]and invariants evolve via a known or learned invariant dynamics operator (\mathcal{I}):[u_t ;=; \iota(x_t),\quad\text{or}\quad u_{t+1} ;=; \mathcal{I}(u_t, x_t, \xi_t).]
Constraints, corridors, and certificates
Let constraints be represented by a family (\mathfrak{C}={C_\alpha}{\alpha\in\mathcal{A}C}) where each (C\alpha) induces a feasible set (\mathcal{F}\alpha\subseteq \mathcal{X}) and/or a predicate (\chi_\alpha:\mathcal{X}\to{0,1}). A corridor is a time-indexed feasible tube (\mathcal{K}_{0:T} = {\mathcal{K}t\subseteq \mathcal{X}}{t=0}^T) expressing allowable evolution (state constraints, invariant bands, safety envelopes, or performance bounds). A certificate map[\mathrm{Cert}:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{U}\times\mathfrak{C}\to \Sigma]produces (\sigma=\mathrm{Cert}(x,u,C)) encoding either a proof of compliance or a structured violation witness, optionally with quantitative margins.
18.1 Square — Predictive Problem Definitions
18.1.1 Forecasting tasks
Forecasting on (H^4) is the prediction of future components of (h_{t+\tau}) given history (\mathcal{H}t). Define history[\mathcal{H}t ;=; {(y_s, a_s)}{s=0}^{t}\cup {m},]where (m) denotes metadata (sensor specs, known transforms applied, known constraint IDs, environment labels). A forecasting model outputs a distribution over future objects:[\widehat{\mathbb{P}}\theta(\cdot \mid \mathcal{H}_t) \approx \mathbb{P}(\cdot \mid \mathcal{H}_t).]
18.1.1.1 Predict future hologram states
The state-forecasting task is[\widehat{x}{t+\tau} \sim \widehat{\mathbb{P}}\theta(x_{t+\tau}\mid \mathcal{H}_t), \quad \tau\in{1,\dots,H}.]Outputs may be:
point estimates (\widehat{x}_{t+\tau}),
predictive distributions (e.g., Gaussian, mixture, diffusion),
sets (\widehat{\mathcal{X}}_{t+\tau}) with coverage guarantees.
Losses are defined in (\mathcal{X}) by a task-appropriate discrepancy (d_{\mathcal{X}}):[\mathcal{L}{\text{state}}(\theta);=;\sum{\tau=1}^H \mathbb{E}\big[d_{\mathcal{X}}(x_{t+\tau},\widehat{x}_{t+\tau})\big].]
18.1.1.2 Predict invariants trajectories
Given invariant map (\iota:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{U}), the task is[\widehat{u}{t+\tau} \sim \widehat{\mathbb{P}}\theta(u_{t+\tau}\mid \mathcal{H}t),\qquad u{t+\tau}=\iota(x_{t+\tau})\ \text{or}\ u_{t+\tau}=\mathcal{I}(u_{t+\tau-1},x_{t+\tau-1},\xi_{t+\tau-1}).]Invariant forecasting is evaluated by (d_{\mathcal{U}}) and by constraint satisfaction over invariant corridors:[u_{t+\tau}\in \mathcal{U}^{\text{safe}}_{t+\tau}.]
18.1.1.3 Predict reconstruction outcomes
Reconstruction produces (\widehat{x}t = \mathcal{R}\theta(y_{0:t})), often under partial observation. The reconstruction outcome forecasting task predicts how reconstruction quality evolves under future observation patterns or corruption:[\widehat{q}{t+\tau} \sim \widehat{\mathbb{P}}\theta\big(q(x_{t+\tau}, \mathcal{R}(y_{0:t+\tau})) \mid \mathcal{H}_t\big),]where (q) is a quality functional (e.g., PSNR, Chamfer distance, topological agreement, constraint-violation count). This task is essential for planning and sensor management.
18.1.1.4 Predict transform programs
Let transform programs be finite sequences (or trees) of operators from a library (\mathcal{L}\subseteq \mathcal{G}):[\pi_{t:t+H-1} ;=; (T_t^{(1)},T_t^{(2)},\dots,T_t^{(L_t)})\in \mathcal{L}^*.]The program-forecasting task predicts the distribution over future programs:[\widehat{\pi}{t:t+H-1} \sim \widehat{\mathbb{P}}\theta(\pi_{t:t+H-1}\mid \mathcal{H}t),]possibly conditioned on a target corridor (\mathcal{K}{t+1:t+H}). Program prediction may be posed as sequence modeling, structured prediction, or inference in probabilistic grammars with hard constraints.
18.1.2 Simulation tasks
Simulation tasks generate plausible trajectories under hypothesized parameters, transforms, noise, and repair operators. A simulator defines a joint rollout distribution:[(x_{0:T}, u_{0:T}, T_{0:T-1}, y_{0:T}) \sim \mathbb{S}(\cdot \mid x_0,p,\Pi,\nu,\mu),]where (\Pi) is an operator policy or program distribution.
18.1.2.1 Simulate engine (\mathcal{Q}) dynamics
Given (p) and initial state (x_0), simulate[x_{t+1}=\mathcal{Q}_p(x_t,a_t,\xi_t).]Key requirements:
stable numerical integration if (\mathcal{Q}_p) discretizes a continuous-time system,
reproducibility via explicit RNG seeding,
traceability: log ((a_t,\xi_t)) for replay.
18.1.2.2 Simulate operator families
Operators may be sampled from parameterized families (T(\cdot;\phi)\in\mathcal{G}):[\phi_t\sim \pi_\Phi(\cdot\mid \mathcal{H}t),\qquad x{t}^{\text{op}} = T(x_t;\phi_t).]Operator-family simulation supports stress testing, augmentation, and identifiability experiments.
18.1.2.3 Simulate noise and corruption
Noise models include:
additive: (y_t = \mathcal{O}(x_t)+\eta_t),
multiplicative: (y_t = \mathcal{O}(x_t)\odot(1+\eta_t)),
structured missingness: masks (M_t) with (y_t = M_t\odot \mathcal{O}(x_t)+\eta_t),
adversarial corruption within a budget (|\delta_t|\le \epsilon): (y_t = \mathcal{O}(x_t)+\delta_t).
Simulation must support matched (training-like) and shifted (OOD) noise regimes.
18.1.2.4 Simulate repair and healing
Repair operators (\mathcal{H}\psi) map corrupted states back toward feasible corridors:[x_t^{\text{heal}} = \mathcal{H}\psi(x_t^{\text{corr}}, y_{0:t}, C).]Healing simulation evaluates robustness and the effectiveness of constraint-aware correction policies; it also supplies counterfactual training trajectories.
18.1.3 Inference tasks
Inference tasks recover latent objects given observations, typically via posterior approximation.
18.1.3.1 Infer hidden parameters (p)
Parameter inference is[\widehat{p} \sim \widehat{\mathbb{P}}\theta(p\mid y{0:t}, a_{0:t-1}, m).]In Bayesian form:[\mathbb{P}(p\mid y_{0:t}) \propto \mathbb{P}(y_{0:t}\mid p),\mathbb{P}(p),]where (\mathbb{P}(y_{0:t}\mid p)) integrates over latent states. Approximations include variational inference, sequential Monte Carlo, amortized inference networks, and likelihood-free (simulation-based) inference.
18.1.3.2 Infer transforms (T) from observations
Transform inference estimates (T_{t-1}) such that[x_t \approx T_{t-1}\cdot x_{t-1},]from (y_{t-1},y_t) or reconstructed (\widehat{x}_{t-1},\widehat{x}t). If (\mathcal{G}) is Lie, local parameterization (\phi\in\mathbb{R}^r) yields[T(\phi) = \exp\Big(\sum{i=1}^r \phi_i A_i\Big),]with generators (A_i). Inference can be performed via maximum likelihood, differentiable alignment losses, or group-equivariant encoders.
18.1.3.3 Infer constraints (C) (model selection)
Constraint inference selects (C\in\mathfrak{C}) explaining observed trajectories with minimal complexity:[\widehat{C} \in \arg\min_{C\in\mathfrak{C}} \Big{ -\log \mathbb{P}C(y{0:t}) + \lambda,\mathrm{Complexity}(C)\Big}.]This includes:
selecting which invariants are conserved,
selecting admissible operator families,
selecting corridor envelopes consistent with data.
18.1.3.4 Infer latent patterns
Latent patterns (z_t\in\mathcal{Z}) capture regimes, discrete modes, or motif structure:[z_t \sim \mathbb{P}(z_t\mid y_{0:t}),\quad x_t \sim \mathbb{P}(x_t\mid z_t, y_{0:t}).]Patterns may be:
Markov switching dynamics,
latent graph structure controlling operator composition,
symbolic motifs (program fragments) explaining repeated sequences.
18.1.4 Evaluation metrics
Metrics must evaluate (i) accuracy, (ii) uncertainty, (iii) robustness, and (iv) certificate alignment.
18.1.4.1 Predictive accuracy metrics
Let (\widehat{\mathbb{P}}\theta) be a predictive distribution and (x{t+\tau}) ground truth.
Proper scoring rules for distributions:[\mathrm{NLL} = -\log \widehat{p}\theta(x{t+\tau}\mid \mathcal{H}_t).]
Point accuracy:[\mathrm{MSE}=\mathbb{E}|x_{t+\tau}-\widehat{x}_{t+\tau}|^2.]
Structure-aware distances (d_{\mathcal{X}}): Chamfer/EMD for point sets, spectral losses for fields, topology-aware penalties for invariants.
For invariants:[\mathrm{InvErr}=\mathbb{E},d_{\mathcal{U}}(u_{t+\tau},\widehat{u}_{t+\tau}).]
For program prediction (sequence):
token-level cross entropy,
edit distance,
semantic equivalence under execution and corridor satisfaction.
18.1.4.2 Calibration metrics
For predictive sets (\widehat{\mathcal{X}}{t+\tau}(\alpha)) with nominal coverage (1-\alpha),[\mathrm{Cov}(\alpha) = \mathbb{P}(x{t+\tau}\in \widehat{\mathcal{X}}_{t+\tau}(\alpha)).]Calibration error:[\mathrm{CalErr} = \int_0^1 \big|\mathrm{Cov}(\alpha)-(1-\alpha)\big|,d\alpha,]or discrete binning variants. For scalar targets, evaluate reliability diagrams and expected calibration error (ECE).
18.1.4.3 Robustness metrics
Robustness is evaluated under perturbation families (\Delta) of observation corruption and model shift:[\mathrm{RobErr}(\epsilon)=\sup_{\delta\in\Delta(\epsilon)} \mathbb{E},d_{\mathcal{X}}\big(x_{t+\tau}, \widehat{x}{t+\tau}(y{0:t}+\delta)\big).]Also evaluate:
worst-case corridor violation rate,
performance under distribution shift (OOD splits),
stability under iterative closed-loop rollouts.
18.1.4.4 Certificate-aware metrics
Let (\sigma_{t+\tau}) be the ground-truth certificate and (\widehat{\sigma}{t+\tau}) the predicted/generated certificate. Define a verifier (\mathcal{V}) that checks certificates:[\mathcal{V}(x,C,\sigma)\in{\text{PASS},\text{FAIL}}\times \mathbb{R}{\ge 0},]returning a pass/fail plus a margin (or counterexample magnitude). Certificate-aware scoring includes:
Certificate validity rate:[\mathrm{CertValid} = \mathbb{P}\big(\mathcal{V}(\widehat{x}{t+\tau},\widehat{C},\widehat{\sigma}{t+\tau})=\text{PASS}\big).]
Sound violation reporting: if violation is claimed, it must be verifiable with a witness.
Tightness: measure margin conservatism vs empirical satisfaction.
Corridor compliance: fraction of predicted trajectories with verifiable membership in (\mathcal{K}_{t+\tau}).
18.2 Flower — Predictive Models
18.2.1 Model families
Predictive models on (H^4) factor into state models, invariant models, operator models, and certificate models, with coupling constraints.
18.2.1.1 Linear dynamical models
Assume a latent linear state (z_t\in\mathbb{R}^n) with observation (y_t):[z_{t+1} = A z_t + B a_t + w_t,\qquad y_t = C z_t + v_t,]with (w_t,v_t) noise. A decoder (D) maps to hologram state: (x_t=D(z_t)). Constraints can be enforced by projecting (D(z_t)) onto feasible sets.
For operator effects, incorporate bilinear forms:[z_{t+1} = A z_t + \sum_{i=1}^r \phi_{t,i} A_i z_t + w_t,]where (\phi_t) parameterizes (T_t).
18.2.1.2 Nonlinear dynamical models
Nonlinear state-space models:[z_{t+1}=f_\theta(z_t,a_t)+w_t,\qquad y_t=g_\theta(z_t)+v_t,\qquad x_t=D_\theta(z_t).]Implementations include recurrent models, neural ODE discretizations, diffusion-based transitions, and energy-based dynamics. Constraint satisfaction is addressed via:
constrained decoding,
penalty/barrier terms,
projection layers,
differentiable verifiers.
18.2.1.3 Operator-learning models
Operator learning targets maps between function spaces:[\mathcal{Q}p:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{X},\qquad x{t+1}=\mathcal{Q}p(x_t).]Learn (\mathcal{Q}p) from data using neural operators (Fourier/operator kernels, graph operators, attention operators) with explicit equivariance to (\mathcal{G}) when applicable:[\mathcal{Q}\theta(T\cdot x) \approx T\cdot \mathcal{Q}\theta(x).]Operator learning is preferred when (\mathcal{X}) is large (fields) and dynamics are structured.
18.2.1.4 Program-learning models
Program learning produces symbolic or differentiable programs implementing operator compositions:[x_{t+1} = \mathrm{Exec}(\pi_t, x_t),\qquad \pi_t\sim \mathbb{P}_\theta(\pi_t\mid \mathcal{H}_t).]Programs may be:
discrete (grammar-based) with verification,
neural-guided search over operator libraries,
differentiable programs with typed interfaces.
Certificates naturally couple to program learning via proof-carrying transforms: the program emits ((x_{t+1},\sigma_{t+1})) where (\sigma_{t+1}) proves corridor compliance.
18.2.2 Training objectives
Training is multi-objective with accuracy, constraints, invariants, and robustness.
Let (\theta) denote all model parameters. A general objective is[\min_\theta;\mathbb{E}\Big[\mathcal{L}{\text{pred}} + \lambda_C \mathcal{L}{\text{con}} + \lambda_I \mathcal{L}{\text{inv}} + \lambda_R \mathcal{L}{\text{rob}} + \lambda_\Sigma \mathcal{L}_{\text{cert}}\Big].]
18.2.2.1 Likelihood objectives
For probabilistic models with density (\widehat{p}\theta):[\mathcal{L}{\text{pred}} = -\sum_{\tau=1}^H \log \widehat{p}\theta(x{t+\tau}\mid \mathcal{H}t),]possibly using latent-variable ELBOs:[\mathcal{L}{\text{ELBO}} = -\mathbb{E}{q\theta(z\mid y)}[\log p_\theta(y\mid z)] + \mathrm{KL}(q_\theta(z\mid y),|,p(z)).]
18.2.2.2 Constraint-aware objectives
For constraints (\chi_\alpha(x)=1) feasible, define a differentiable violation (v_\alpha(x)\ge 0) with (v_\alpha(x)=0) iff feasible. Then[\mathcal{L}{\text{con}} = \sum{\tau=1}^H \mathbb{E},v_{\widehat{C}}(\widehat{x}{t+\tau}).]Hard constraints are handled via projection:[\widehat{x}{t+\tau} \leftarrow \Pi_{\mathcal{F}{\widehat{C}}}(\widehat{x}{t+\tau}),]or via constrained optimization layers.
18.2.2.3 Invariant-aware objectives
If invariants must be preserved or obey known evolution laws:[\mathcal{L}{\text{inv}} = \sum{\tau=1}^H \mathbb{E}, d_{\mathcal{U}}\big(\iota(\widehat{x}{t+\tau}), \widehat{u}{t+\tau}\big);+;\sum_{\tau=1}^H \mathbb{E}, d_{\mathcal{U}}\big(\widehat{u}{t+\tau}, \mathcal{I}(\widehat{u}{t+\tau-1},\widehat{x}_{t+\tau-1})\big).]For conserved invariants, (\mathcal{I}(u,\cdot)=u).
18.2.2.4 Robust objectives
Robustness may be optimized by adversarial training:[\mathcal{L}{\text{rob}} = \sup{\delta\in\Delta(\epsilon)} \sum_{\tau=1}^H d_{\mathcal{X}}\big(x_{t+\tau}, \widehat{x}{t+\tau}(y{0:t}+\delta)\big),]or by distributional robustness (Wasserstein balls), heavy-tailed noise models, and consistency regularization across augmentations.
Certificate loss (\mathcal{L}_{\text{cert}}) includes:
invalid certificate penalty,
margin calibration penalties,
completeness penalties when certificates are missing or non-informative.
18.2.3 Calibration and uncertainty
18.2.3.1 Predictive uncertainty estimation
Uncertainty decomposes into:
aleatoric: observation/dynamics noise,
epistemic: model uncertainty,
specification: corridor/constraint ambiguity.
Methods:
probabilistic models with calibrated likelihoods,
ensembles,
Bayesian approximations,
conformal prediction producing set-valued forecasts (\widehat{\mathcal{X}}(\alpha)).
18.2.3.2 Coverage guarantees
For exchangeable data, conformal prediction provides finite-sample coverage:[\mathbb{P}\big(x_{t+\tau}\in \widehat{\mathcal{X}}_{t+\tau}(\alpha)\big)\ge 1-\alpha.]For time series, use block/conformal methods with appropriate dependence corrections and report empirical coverage under corridor regimes.
18.2.3.3 Out-of-distribution detection
OOD detection targets[\mathbb{I}{(y_{0:t},m)\not\sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}}.]Use:
predictive entropy,
likelihood-ratio tests with learned density models,
representation-space distance to training manifold,
certificate anomalies: increased constraint violation or verifier margin collapse.
18.2.3.4 Confidence reporting
Confidence is reported as a tuple:[\mathrm{Conf}_{t+\tau} = \big(\text{coverage level},\ \text{calibration score},\ \text{corridor margin},\ \text{OOD score}\big),]with strict separation between probability of correctness and verifier-confirmed compliance.
18.2.4 Closed-loop prediction
Closed-loop systems use predictions to select transforms that influence future states.
Let a policy (\pi) choose actions (a_t) based on history and predicted rollouts:[a_t = \pi(\mathcal{H}t,\widehat{\mathbb{P}}\theta(\cdot\mid \mathcal{H}_t)).]
18.2.4.1 Prediction (\rightarrow) transform selection
Define a utility functional (J) incorporating task reward and corridor penalties:[J(\pi;\theta)=\mathbb{E}\Big[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} r(x_t,a_t) - \lambda ,\mathrm{Viol}(x_{t+1},\mathcal{K}_{t+1})\Big].]Transform selection may be:
MPC over predicted trajectories,
program synthesis over operator libraries,
risk-sensitive optimization using distributional forecasts.
18.2.4.2 Transform (\rightarrow) updated state
Apply the selected transform and engine update:[x_{t+1} = \mathcal{Q}p\big(T_t\cdot x_t, a_t,\xi_t\big),\quad \sigma{t+1}=\mathrm{Cert}(x_{t+1},\iota(x_{t+1}),\widehat{C}).]Certificate generation is coupled to execution, not post hoc.
18.2.4.3 Monitoring (\rightarrow) correction
Define monitors:
invariant monitor (M_I(x_t)=d_{\mathcal{U}}(\iota(x_t),\widehat{u}_t)),
constraint monitor (M_C(x_t)=v_{\widehat{C}}(x_t)),
reconstruction monitor (M_R) tracking observation/model mismatch.
Correction mechanisms:
belief update (filtering),
transform rollback or alternate operator selection,
healing operator (\mathcal{H}_\psi),
corridor tightening/relaxation with explicit certificate updates.
18.2.4.4 Stability of closed loop
Closed-loop stability is formulated as boundedness of expected corridor violation and state deviation. Sufficient conditions include:
contraction of predictive dynamics in a suitable metric,
bounded model error with robust MPC,
certificate-enforced constraints guaranteeing invariant corridors.
A typical risk-sensitive stability criterion:[\sup_{t\ge 0}\mathbb{E},|x_t| < \infty,\qquad\sum_{t\ge 0}\mathbb{P}(x_t\notin \mathcal{K}_t) < \infty,]with explicit reporting when conditions fail.
18.3 Cloud — Limits and Failure Modes
18.3.1 Identifiability limits
18.3.1.1 Limits from partial observation
If (\mathcal{O}) is non-injective, multiple states map to the same observation. Define the observation fiber[\mathcal{F}(y)={x\in\mathcal{X}: \exists \eta,\ \mathcal{O}(x;\eta)=y}.]Identifiability requires the posterior over (\mathcal{F}(y)) to concentrate; otherwise, prediction must remain set-valued and certificate-aware.
18.3.1.2 Limits from gauge ambiguity
If (\mathcal{G}) acts as a gauge symmetry, the equivalence class ([x]={T\cdot x:T\in\mathcal{G}_0}) may be observationally indistinguishable. Models must:
predict gauge-invariant quantities,
report equivalence classes rather than canonical representatives,
emit certificates expressing gauge ambiguity.
18.3.1.3 Limits from noise
Noise enlarges posterior support and inflates irreducible uncertainty. For high noise regimes, any point forecast is unstable; robust approaches must revert to calibrated sets and corridor-level guarantees.
18.3.1.4 Certified ambiguity reports
A certified ambiguity report is a certificate (\sigma) that includes:
an explicit lower bound on posterior diameter in (d_{\mathcal{X}}),
a witness pair ((x,x')) with indistinguishable likelihoods,
a statement of which constraints/invariants remain identifiable.
18.3.2 Prediction impossibility
18.3.2.1 Chaos bounds
If the dynamics have positive Lyapunov exponents, long-horizon point forecasts are information-theoretically unstable. Proper outputs become:
distributional forecasts,
horizon-dependent widening corridors,
certificates indicating forecast horizon beyond which prediction is non-actionable.
18.3.2.2 Information-bounded bounds
With finite observation bandwidth and partial observability, mutual information bounds achievable accuracy:[I(x_{t+\tau}; y_{0:t}) ;\text{limits concentration of}; \mathbb{P}(x_{t+\tau}\mid y_{0:t}).]
18.3.2.3 Adversarial bounds
Under adversarial perturbations (\delta\in\Delta(\epsilon)), worst-case error lower bounds apply. Models must either:
restrict operating corridors to adversarially robust regions,
provide certificates of robustness margins,
declare infeasibility under the given adversary.
18.3.2.4 Certified uncertainty
Certified uncertainty is a verified lower bound on prediction error or an upper bound on achievable corridor tightness, emitted as (\sigma) so downstream systems do not treat uncertainty as optional metadata.
18.3.3 Model risk
18.3.3.1 Overfitting risks
Overfitting manifests as:
sharp likelihoods with poor calibration,
brittle behavior under small corruptions,
certificate invalidity spikes under minor shifts.
Mitigation: regularization, data augmentation, robust objectives, held-out corridor regimes, and verifier-guided validation.
18.3.3.2 Dataset shift risks
Shift types:
covariate shift in (\mathcal{Y}),
dynamics shift in (\mathcal{Q}_p),
constraint shift in (\mathfrak{C}).
Mitigation: shift detection, domain generalization, online recalibration, and explicit reporting of shift via OOD scores and certificate margins.
18.3.3.3 Objective mismatch risks
If optimization targets proxy losses misaligned with corridor safety, models can maximize accuracy while violating constraints. Mitigation:
constraint-first training schedules,
certificate-aware loss terms,
evaluation prioritized by corridor compliance.
18.3.3.4 Monitoring and mitigation
Operational monitoring includes:
drift metrics on representations,
calibration drift,
certificate validity drift,
violation trend analysis.
Mitigation includes:
automatic corridor tightening,
safe fallback models,
abstention policies: output sets/certified ambiguity rather than point guesses.
18.3.4 Corridor integration
18.3.4.1 Corridor-aware training
Corridor-aware training optimizes performance under constraints by sampling corridor conditions:[\min_\theta \mathbb{E}{(x,y),\mathcal{K}}\Big[\mathcal{L}{\text{pred}}(\theta) + \lambda,\mathrm{Viol}(\widehat{x},\mathcal{K})\Big],]with curriculum over corridor tightness.
18.3.4.2 Corridor-aware inference
Inference conditions on corridors:[\widehat{\mathbb{P}}\theta(x{t+\tau}\mid \mathcal{H}t,\mathcal{K}{t+\tau}),]or projects posterior samples into (\mathcal{K}_{t+\tau}) with explicit certificate tracking of projection-induced bias.
18.3.4.3 Corridor-aware reporting
Reports include:
predicted corridor membership probability,
verifier-confirmed membership (binary + margin),
certified ambiguity if neither can be established.
18.3.4.4 Corridor violation handling
When violation is detected:
emit a violation certificate (\sigma^{\text{viol}}) with witness,
trigger healing operators or safe fallback,
update corridor model selection (\widehat{C}) if violations indicate constraint mismatch.
18.4 Fractal — Predictive Tooling
18.4.1 Reference pipelines
18.4.1.1 Training pipelines
A reference training pipeline enforces determinism, auditability, and verifier integration:
Dataset assembly: ({(y_{0:t},x_{t+1:t+H},u_{t+1:t+H},T_{t:t+H-1},C)}).
Augmentation: corruption simulators for noise and missingness, operator perturbations.
Model training: multi-objective optimization with corridor sampling.
Verifier-in-the-loop: periodic certificate checks on held-out sets and adversarial regimes.
Calibration stage: conformal/temperature scaling for uncertainty outputs.
Artifact emission: checkpoint, config, seeds, dataset hash, verifier version.
18.4.1.2 Evaluation pipelines
Evaluation must be scenario-complete:
IID evaluation,
corridor-tight evaluation,
OOD shift suites,
adversarial corruption suites,
long-horizon rollout stability tests,
certificate validity and ambiguity reporting tests.
18.4.1.3 Deployment pipelines
Deployment is defined by a runtime contract:
inputs: (y_t, m),
outputs: (\widehat{x}{t+\tau}) or (\widehat{\mathcal{X}}{t+\tau}(\alpha)), (\widehat{u}{t+\tau}), (\widehat{T}{t:t+H-1}), (\widehat{\sigma}_{t+\tau}),
verifier step: (\mathcal{V}(\cdot)) executed with fixed versioning,
logging: trajectories, decisions, certificate outcomes, and margins.
18.4.1.4 Reproducibility pipelines
Reproducibility requires:
dataset snapshotting with content hashes,
deterministic seeds and exact dependency locks,
model and verifier versioning,
full provenance: preprocessing graphs, augmentation parameters, hardware determinism notes where applicable.
18.4.2 Benchmark suites
18.4.2.1 Forecast benchmarks
Benchmarks span:
short-horizon high-accuracy forecasting,
long-horizon distributional forecasting,
invariant preservation under rollout,
closed-loop planning benchmarks with corridor constraints.
18.4.2.2 Inference benchmarks
Inference benchmarks include:
parameter recovery accuracy for (p),
transform recovery accuracy for (T),
constraint selection accuracy for (C),
identifiability stress tests under partial observability.
18.4.2.3 Robustness benchmarks
Robustness suites include:
corruption ladders over (\epsilon),
missingness patterns,
adversarial attacks on observations,
dynamics shift scenarios for (\mathcal{Q}_p).
18.4.2.4 Calibration benchmarks
Calibration suites include:
coverage curves over (\alpha),
conditional coverage under corridor slices,
calibration under distribution shift,
certificate-margin calibration.
18.4.3 Verification integration
18.4.3.1 Invariant monitors
Invariant monitors compute:[M_I(t)=d_{\mathcal{U}}(\iota(\widehat{x}_t),\widehat{u}_t),]and verify invariant corridor membership with certificate output.
18.4.3.2 Constraint monitors
Constraint monitors compute violation magnitudes (v_C(\widehat{x}_t)) and request certificates from (\mathrm{Cert}) or a proof-producing decoder.
18.4.3.3 Reconstruction monitors
Reconstruction monitors quantify observation-model mismatch:[M_R(t)=d_{\mathcal{Y}}\big(y_t,\mathcal{O}(\widehat{x}_t)\big),]and trigger abstention/healing when mismatch exceeds calibrated thresholds.
18.4.3.4 Certificate outputs
Certificate outputs are first-class outputs of predictive models:[(\widehat{x}{t+\tau},\widehat{u}{t+\tau},\widehat{T}{t:t+H-1},\widehat{\sigma}{t+\tau}),]with (\widehat{\sigma}) structured for automated verification, margin extraction, and witness-based debugging.
18.4.4 Publication artifacts
18.4.4.1 Trained models
Release:
model weights,
architecture spec,
training logs,
calibration parameters,
verifier compatibility matrix.
18.4.4.2 Dataset snapshots
Release:
immutable dataset versions,
hashes and schemas,
generation scripts for synthetic components,
split definitions for IID/OOD/corridor regimes.
18.4.4.3 Reproducible reports
Provide:
executable evaluation notebooks/scripts,
full metric tables and confidence intervals,
certificate validity and ambiguity summaries,
failure-mode casebooks with witness certificates.
18.4.4.4 Versioned releases
Versioning is defined over the full predictive stack:[\text{Release} = (\text{Model},\text{Data},\text{Verifier},\text{Constraints},\text{Pipelines}),]with semantic versioning rules:
patch: metric-neutral bugfix, identical verifier semantics,
minor: improved performance with backward-compatible certificates,
major: changes to certificate schema, corridor semantics, or constraint families requiring downstream migration.
All releases include a compatibility contract specifying:
accepted input schemas,
guaranteed output formats,
verifier version constraints,
deprecation timeline.
Chapter 18 — Predictive Modeling on ((H^4)): Forecasting, Simulation, Inference
Chapter 19 — Mapping to Physics: Spacetime, Fields, Constraint-Respecting Couplings
Let the kernel be the structured object[\mathcal{K};:=;\bigl(H,;\mathcal{C},;\mathcal{I},;\mathcal{G},;\mathcal{O}\bigr),]where:
(H) is the kernel state space (finite- or infinite-dimensional), modeled as a smooth Banach manifold or Hilbert manifold when differential structure is required.
(\mathcal{G}) is a (Lie) group acting smoothly on (H) (kernel gauge/symmetry), written (h\mapsto g\cdot h).
(\mathcal{C}) is a set of constraints on (H), decomposed as equality and inequality components[\mathcal{C}(h)=0,\qquad \mathcal{C}\le(h)\le 0,]with (\mathcal{C}:H\to \mathbb{R}^{m}), (\mathcal{C}\le:H\to\mathbb{R}^{m_\le}) (or more general codomains, e.g. distributions for PDE constraints).
(\mathcal{I}={I_\alpha}{\alpha\in A}) is a family of invariants (functions or functionals) on (H); typically each (I\alpha:H\to\mathbb{R}) is (\mathcal{G})-invariant.
(\mathcal{O}) is the designated set of kernel observables (predictive outputs) intended to be mapped to measurable quantities.
A physical model is a tuple[\mathfrak{P};:=;\bigl(M,;g,;\mathfrak{B},;\Gamma(\mathfrak{B}),;\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{phys}},;\mathcal{S},;\mathcal{M}\bigr),]where:
(M) is an (n)-dimensional spacetime manifold (typically (n=4)).
(g) is a Lorentzian metric on (M) (or a class of metrics if dynamical).
(\mathfrak{B}\to M) is a field bundle (possibly a product of bundles, including principal bundles).
(\Gamma(\mathfrak{B})) is the configuration space of sections (with specified regularity class, e.g. Sobolev (H^s)).
(\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{phys}}) is the physical gauge group acting on (\Gamma(\mathfrak{B})) and (when appropriate) on (g).
(\mathcal{S}) is the action functional (or, more generally, a generator of dynamics: action, Hamiltonian, or stochastic generator).
(\mathcal{M}) is a measurement/observation map (an operational interface from physical states to data).
The objective of this chapter is to construct embedding and coupling maps[\mathsf{E}:;H \to \mathcal{F},\qquad \mathcal{F}:=\bigl{(g,\Phi);|; g\in\mathrm{Met}(M),\ \Phi\in\Gamma(\mathfrak{B})\bigr},]together with constraint-respecting dynamics and falsifiable prediction pipelines.
19.1 Square — Embedding the Kernel into Physical Models
19.1.1 Field embeddings
19.1.1.1 Map (H) to field configurations
A field embedding is a map (\mathsf{E}\Phi:H\to \Gamma(\mathfrak{B})) assigning to each kernel state (h\in H) a physical field configuration (\Phi=\mathsf{E}\Phi(h)). The embedding must be specified together with:
Regularity class. Fix (s) such that (\Gamma(\mathfrak{B})\subset H^s(M,\mathfrak{B})) (or (C^k), (C^\infty)) and require (\mathsf{E}_\Phi(H)\subset H^s). If dynamics require products of fields, pick (s>\frac{n}{2}) to ensure Sobolev multiplication.
Locality structure (optional). If the kernel is intended to embed locally, require existence of a local functional form:[(\mathsf{E}_\Phi(h))(x) ;=; F\bigl(x,;j^r h,;\theta(h)\bigr),]where (j^r h) denotes a finite jet (when (h) has a spacetime interpretation) and (\theta(h)) are global parameters. If (h) is not spacetime-indexed, locality is expressed by factoring through latent fields (\eta(h)\in \Gamma(\mathfrak{E})) on (M).
Equivariance (gauge compatibility). Let (\pi_H:H\to H/\mathcal{G}) be the quotient map and let (\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{phys}}) act on (\Gamma(\mathfrak{B})). A field embedding is equivariant if there exists a homomorphism (\rho:\mathcal{G}\to \mathcal{G}{\mathrm{phys}}) such that[\mathsf{E}\Phi(g\cdot h);=;\rho(g)\cdot \mathsf{E}\Phi(h)\qquad \forall g\in\mathcal{G},\ h\in H.]Equivariance ensures kernel gauge or symmetry redundancy does not generate physically distinct configurations unless intended.
Observational soundness. If kernel observables (\mathcal{O}) are designated as predictive, require that each (O\in\mathcal{O}) factors through the physical measurement map:[O(h);=;\widetilde{O}\bigl(\mathcal{M}(g,\Phi)\bigr)\quad\text{for}\quad (g,\Phi)=(\mathsf{E}g(h),\mathsf{E}\Phi(h))]for some (\widetilde{O}), or equivalently that (\mathcal{M}\circ \mathsf{E}) contains sufficient information to evaluate (\mathcal{O}) up to declared equivalences.
Configuration-space formulation. Let (\mathcal{F}\Phi:=\Gamma(\mathfrak{B})) and (\mathcal{F}\Phi/\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{phys}}) its physical quotient. If (\mathsf{E}\Phi) is equivariant, it induces a well-defined map on quotients:[\overline{\mathsf{E}}\Phi:\ H/\mathcal{G}\ \to\ \mathcal{F}\Phi/\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{phys}},\qquad \overline{\mathsf{E}}\Phi([h])=[\mathsf{E}_\Phi(h)].]
19.1.1.2 Constraint mapping ((\mathcal{C})) to physical constraints
A constraint mapping is a construction that transfers kernel constraints to physical admissibility conditions. The physically admissible set is typically of the form[\mathcal{A}{\mathrm{phys}};:=;{(g,\Phi)\in\mathcal{F};:;\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{phys}}(g,\Phi)=0,\ \mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{phys},\le}(g,\Phi)\le 0}.]
A constraint mapping is defined by operators[\mathsf{T}{\mathcal{C}}:\ \mathcal{C}\mapsto \mathcal{C}{\mathrm{phys}},\qquad\mathsf{T}{\mathcal{C}\le}:\ \mathcal{C}\le\mapsto \mathcal{C}{\mathrm{phys},\le},]satisfying the implication[\mathcal{C}(h)=0,\ \mathcal{C}\le(h)\le 0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{phys}}(\mathsf{E}(h))=0,\ \mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{phys},\le}(\mathsf{E}(h))\le 0.]
Structural classification (Dirac-style). If the kernel carries a Poisson structure ({\cdot,\cdot}) and constraints define a submanifold (\mathcal{C}^{-1}(0)), classify constraints into:
First-class: generate gauge directions; require closure under Poisson bracket up to constraints.
Second-class: require explicit stabilization (Dirac bracket or explicit solving).
A physically faithful embedding preserves the constraint class: first-class constraints map to gauge constraints (e.g., Gauss laws), and second-class constraints map to eliminable conditions (e.g., constitutive relations, gauge fixing, or algebraic constraints).
Constraint preservation under evolution. If physical dynamics are generated by (\mathcal{S}) or a PDE flow (\partial_t \Phi = \mathcal{F}(\Phi)), impose[\frac{d}{dt},\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{phys}}(\Phi(t)) = 0\quad\text{whenever}\quad\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{phys}}(\Phi(0))=0,]either exactly or via a stabilizing mechanism (constraint damping/projection). The mapping (\mathsf{T}_{\mathcal{C}}) is admissible only if it yields constraints that are (i) compatible and (ii) maintainable under the selected dynamics.
19.1.1.3 Invariant mapping to conserved quantities
An invariant mapping takes kernel invariants (I_\alpha\in\mathcal{I}) to physically meaningful conserved quantities (Q_\alpha) or conserved currents (J_\alpha^\mu).
Assume the physical model has an action (\mathcal{S}[g,\Phi]) and a continuous symmetry group (G_{\mathrm{sym}}) acting on ((g,\Phi)) with infinitesimal generator (\delta_\xi). When (\delta_\xi \mathcal{S}=0), Noether’s theorem yields a conserved current (J^\mu_\xi) satisfying[\nabla_\mu J^\mu_\xi = 0](on-shell, with appropriate boundary conditions). The conserved charge is[Q_\xi(\Sigma) = \int_\Sigma J^\mu_\xi,n_\mu,d\Sigma,]for spacelike hypersurface (\Sigma).
A kernel invariant (I_\alpha) is realized as a Noether charge if there exists (\xi_\alpha) such that, for admissible configurations,[I_\alpha(h);=;\mathcal{N}\alpha!\left(Q{\xi_\alpha}\bigl(\mathsf{E}(h)\bigr)\right)]for a specified normalization map (\mathcal{N}_\alpha) (e.g., unit conversion, additive constant, or scaling).
Topological invariants. If the physical embedding includes a connection (A) with curvature (F), invariants may map to characteristic classes (Chern numbers, Pontryagin numbers):[Q = \int_M \mathrm{tr}(F\wedge F),\qquadQ = \int_{\Sigma} \mathrm{tr}!\left(A\wedge dA + \tfrac{2}{3}A\wedge A\wedge A\right),]depending on dimension and bundle. Kernel invariants intended to be robust against perturbation should map to topological charges or protected indices.
Invariant completeness requirement. If two kernel states are physically distinguishable under intended measurements, then invariants and mapped observables must separate them up to gauge:[\bigl(\forall \alpha,\ I_\alpha(h_1)=I_\alpha(h_2)\bigr)\ \wedge\ [h_1]=[h_2]\ \text{in}\ H/\mathcal{G}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad[\mathsf{E}(h_1)]=[\mathsf{E}(h_2)]\ \text{in}\ \mathcal{F}/\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{phys}}.]Failure indicates either missing invariants or an embedding that collapses physically distinct structure.
19.1.1.4 Gauge mapping to physical gauge
A gauge mapping aligns kernel gauge redundancy with physical gauge redundancy. Formally, specify:
Kernel gauge action: (\mathcal{G}\curvearrowright H).
Physical gauge action: (\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{phys}}\curvearrowright \mathcal{F}).
A gauge mapping is admissible if there exists (\rho:\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{phys}}) such that (\mathsf{E}) is (\rho)-equivariant. Additionally, gauge mapping must satisfy:
Faithfulness criterion (when required). If gauge directions represent pure redundancy, require that (\ker\rho) acts trivially on all kernel observables and invariants; otherwise, kernel redundancy would not align with physical redundancy.
Anomaly exclusion criterion. If the embedding induces quantum dynamics, require that the gauge symmetry is anomaly-free in the chosen matter content; otherwise the gauge mapping fails at the quantized level.
Gauge-fixing compatibility. If computations use gauge fixing, specify a gauge condition (\chi(g,\Phi)=0) such that (\chi\circ \mathsf{E}) is solvable and stable. For differential gauge conditions, require ellipticity/hyperbolicity properties consistent with the intended solver.
19.1.2 Spacetime embeddings
19.1.2.1 Metric interpretations as model choices
A metric embedding is a map (\mathsf{E}g:H\to \mathrm{Met}(M)) that assigns a metric (g{\mu\nu}) to each kernel state. There are two principal regimes.
(i) Background-metric regime. Choose a fixed (g=g_0) and embed only into fields:[\mathsf{E}g(h)=g_0,\qquad \mathsf{E}(h)=(g_0,\mathsf{E}\Phi(h)).]This is appropriate when the kernel is interpreted as matter/interaction structure on a predetermined spacetime.
(ii) Dynamical-metric regime. Define (g=\mathsf{E}_g(h)) as a functional of kernel state. Admissibility requires:
Signature constraint: (g) has Lorentzian signature ((-,+,+,+)) everywhere.
Nondegeneracy: (\det g \neq 0) everywhere.
Regularity: (g\in H^{s}) with (s) sufficient for curvature terms used in (\mathcal{S}).
A constructive template uses a base metric plus a kernel-dependent perturbation:[g_{\mu\nu}(x;h)=g^{(0)}{\mu\nu}(x)+\Delta{\mu\nu}\bigl(x;\eta(h)\bigr),]where (\eta(h)) is an induced latent field on (M) (or a finite parameter set), and (\Delta_{\mu\nu}) is constrained so that signature is preserved (e.g., via tetrad/ADM parameterization).
Discrete harmonic backbones (sexagesimal/sextile invariance). If the kernel encodes a fundamental (6)-fold phase-locking structure (e.g., a preferred subgroup (C_6\subset SO(2)) arising from a base-(60) discretization), a metric embedding may enforce symmetry constraints such as[\mathcal{L}_{\xi}g = 0 \quad \text{for generators } \xi \text{ of } C_6\text{-invariant isometries},]or restrict to geometries admitting a hexagonal lattice or (60^\circ) rotational invariance in a specified submanifold (e.g., spatial slices). This is a model choice: it encodes a discrete symmetry constraint as a physical symmetry or as a discretization-invariant prior.
19.1.2.2 Connection interpretations
A connection embedding assigns a connection (\nabla) (or connection coefficients (\Gamma^\rho_{\mu\nu})) and, when relevant, a gauge connection (A) on a principal bundle. Three standard interpretations occur.
Levi–Civita connection from (g). If (g) is defined, set (\nabla=\nabla^{(g)}). This ensures torsion-free metric compatibility:[\nabla_\lambda g_{\mu\nu}=0,\qquad \Gamma^\rho_{\mu\nu}=\Gamma^\rho_{\nu\mu}.]
Affine connection as independent structure. Define (\mathsf{E}\Gamma:H\to \mathrm{Conn}(TM)) independently from (\mathsf{E}g), allowing torsion (T^\rho{\mu\nu}) and/or non-metricity (Q{\lambda\mu\nu}:=\nabla_\lambda g_{\mu\nu}). Constraints must specify admissible torsion/non-metricity and ensure well-posed dynamics.
Gauge connections. Embed kernel states into a principal (G)-bundle connection (A\in\Omega^1(M,\mathfrak{g})). Require gauge covariance:[A \mapsto uAu^{-1} + u,du^{-1},\qquad u:M\to G.]Kernel gauge group (\mathcal{G}) must map coherently to the induced (G)-gauge transformations.
19.1.2.3 Curvature interpretations
Curvature is derived from a connection:
Spacetime curvature:[R^\rho{}{\sigma\mu\nu}=\partial\mu \Gamma^\rho_{\nu\sigma}-\partial_\nu \Gamma^\rho_{\mu\sigma}+\Gamma^\rho_{\mu\lambda}\Gamma^\lambda_{\nu\sigma}-\Gamma^\rho_{\nu\lambda}\Gamma^\lambda_{\mu\sigma}.]
Gauge curvature:[F = dA + A\wedge A.]
A curvature interpretation map assigns kernel invariants or constraint expressions to curvature scalars or tensors. Standard templates include:
Scalar curvature coupling:[I_\alpha(h);\leadsto;\int_M \sqrt{|g|},f_\alpha(\eta(h)),R,d^nx.]
Quadratic curvature invariants:[\int_M \sqrt{|g|},\bigl(a,R^2 + b,R_{\mu\nu}R^{\mu\nu} + c,R_{\mu\nu\rho\sigma}R^{\mu\nu\rho\sigma}\bigr),d^nx,]with coefficients constrained for stability (ghost avoidance, EFT cutoff specification).
Topological densities (dimension-dependent), used when invariants are intended to be robust.
Curvature interpretations are admissible only if they preserve constraint compatibility and yield a well-posed evolution problem in the selected formulation (covariant, ADM, tetrad, etc.).
19.1.2.4 Limits of interpretation
Any embedding from (\mathcal{K}) into (\mathfrak{P}) faces unavoidable non-uniqueness and potential degeneracy. The following limits are structural and must be explicitly bounded.
Equivalence-class ambiguity. If kernel gauge quotient (H/\mathcal{G}) is non-Hausdorff or singular, induced physical quotients may be ill-defined. Remedy: restrict to strata where the quotient is well-behaved or work with stacks/groupoids rather than naive quotients.
Identifiability failure. If multiple distinct (h) map to observationally indistinguishable physical states ((g,\Phi)), the embedding is not identifiable. Remedy: restrict parameterization, add observables, or redesign coupling.
Effective-field limitation. If couplings include higher-derivative operators or nonrenormalizable terms, the model is valid only below a declared cutoff (\Lambda). The embedding must specify its domain of validity and provide internal consistency checks for extrapolation.
Topology and global obstructions. Global existence of bundles, spin structures, or gauge fields may fail for some (M). Admissibility includes topological feasibility conditions.
19.1.3 Coupling definitions
A coupling specifies how kernel-induced structures influence spacetime geometry, matter content, and observables. In action-based models, write
[
\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{tot}}[g,\Phi;h]
\mathcal{S}{\mathrm{grav}}[g]+\mathcal{S}{\mathrm{mat}}[g,\Phi]+\mathcal{S}{\mathrm{ker}}[h]+\mathcal{S}{\mathrm{int}}[g,\Phi;h],]with the understanding that (h) may be promoted to induced fields (\eta(h)) on (M) (via (\mathsf{E})) to maintain locality.
19.1.3.1 Coupling ((H\leftrightarrow g_{\mu\nu})) templates
Template A: Minimal stress–energy coupling. If the kernel induces a matter Lagrangian density (\mathcal{L}H(g,\eta,\nabla\eta)), define[\mathcal{S}{\mathrm{int}}[g;h]=\int_M \sqrt{|g|},\mathcal{L}H\bigl(g,\eta(h),\nabla\eta(h)\bigr),d^nx.]Then the induced stress–energy tensor is[T{\mu\nu}^{(H)}:=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{|g|}}\frac{\delta \mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{int}}}{\delta g^{\mu\nu}},]and appears in the metric field equations.
Template B: Conformal coupling. Let (\Omega(h;x)>0). Define[g_{\mu\nu}(h;x)=\Omega(h;x)^2,g^{(0)}_{\mu\nu}(x).]Constraints enforce positivity and regularity of (\Omega). This template is appropriate when kernel degrees of freedom represent effective scaling.
Template C: Disformal coupling. For a scalar induced field (\eta=\eta(h)),[g_{\mu\nu}=A(\eta),g^{(0)}{\mu\nu}+B(\eta),\nabla\mu \eta,\nabla_\nu \eta,]with admissibility constraints on (A,B) to preserve Lorentzian signature and causality.
Template D: Derivative coupling to curvature. For selected kernel-induced fields,[\int_M \sqrt{|g|},f(\eta),R,d^nx,\qquad\int_M \sqrt{|g|},f(\eta),G_{\mu\nu}\nabla^\mu \eta\nabla^\nu \eta,d^nx,]subject to well-posedness and stability checks.
Template E: Topological coupling. Couple kernel invariants to topological terms (dimension permitting), to produce robust quantized responses.
19.1.3.2 Coupling ((H\leftrightarrow)) matter fields templates
Let (\Phi) denote matter fields not already induced by the kernel. Standard portals include:
Template A: Source/current coupling.[\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{int}}=\int_M \sqrt{|g|},\langle J(h),\Phi\rangle,d^nx,]where (J(h)) is a kernel-induced source with the correct representation type and dimension.
Template B: Mass/parameter modulation.[m\ \mapsto\ m(h)=m_0,F\bigl(\eta(h)\bigr),]implemented by replacing (m_0\Phi^2) with (m(h)\Phi^2) in the Lagrangian.
Template C: Yukawa/interaction portal.[\mathcal{L}{\mathrm{int}}=\lambda,\eta(h),\bar\psi\psi,\qquad\mathcal{L}{\mathrm{int}}=\lambda,\eta(h)^2,\Phi^2,]with admissibility constraints (boundedness below, perturbative regime specification or nonperturbative control).
Template D: Gauge-charge assignment.Assign kernel-induced fields to representations of the gauge group, enforcing gauge invariance of (\mathcal{S}{\mathrm{tot}}). This requires compatibility between kernel symmetry (\mathcal{G}) and physical gauge (\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{phys}}).
19.1.3.3 Coupling ((H\leftrightarrow)) observers templates
An observer coupling is an operational specification of how physical configurations become data. Let the data space be (\mathcal{D}) and define[\mathcal{M}:\ \mathcal{F}\times \mathcal{A}\to \mathcal{P}(\mathcal{D}),]where (\mathcal{A}) are apparatus settings and (\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{D})) is the space of probability measures on (\mathcal{D}).
Template A: Deterministic readout.[d=\mathcal{M}_0(g,\Phi;a),\qquad d\in\mathcal{D}.]
Template B: Stochastic measurement channel.[d\sim \mathcal{M}(g,\Phi;a),\quad \text{e.g.}\quad d=\mathcal{M}_0(g,\Phi;a)+\varepsilon,\ \varepsilon\sim \mathcal{N}(0,\Sigma(a)).]
Template C: Local detector coupling.For a worldline (\gamma) and detector kernel (K),[d = \int K(\tau),\Phi(\gamma(\tau)),d\tau,]extended to field derivatives and tensor contractions with (u^\mu=\dot\gamma^\mu) as required.
Admissibility requirement (observer locality and causality). If the physical model is relativistic, (\mathcal{M}) must not introduce superluminal dependence: dependence on ((g,\Phi)) must be restricted to the causal past of the measurement event, given the chosen causal structure.
19.1.3.4 Admissibility constraints on couplings
A coupling set is admissible if it lies in the intersection of:
Covariance: invariance under diffeomorphisms and gauge transformations as declared.
Locality (or declared nonlocality class): if nonlocal, specify kernel, decay, and causal support.
Well-posedness: the resulting field equations admit existence/uniqueness in an appropriate function space for admissible initial/boundary data.
Stability: Hamiltonian bounded below (or dissipative generator bounded), absence of ghosts/tachyons within the declared regime.
Constraint preservation: constraints remain satisfied under evolution (exactly or with certified stabilization).
Energy/corridor constraints: respects specified physical admissibility corridors (Section 19.3.3).
Observability: model yields measurable differences under at least one realizable protocol; otherwise it is not falsifiable.
Define the admissible parameter/coupling set (\Theta_{\mathrm{adm}}\subseteq \Theta) by explicit inequalities and structural conditions. A model instance is valid only if it includes a certificate that (\theta\in\Theta_{\mathrm{adm}}).
19.1.4 Dimensional consistency
19.1.4.1 Units and scaling
Fix base dimensions ([L],[T],[M]) (and optionally ([Q]), ([\Theta]) for charge and temperature). Assign each field (\Phi) a physical dimension ([\Phi]). Require:
The action is dimensionless in units where (\hbar=1), or has dimension of action otherwise.
Each term in the Lagrangian density has the same dimension:[[\sqrt{|g|},\mathcal{L}];=;[{\rm action}], [L]^{-n}.]
If the kernel parameters are dimensionless by construction, define a scaling map (\Pi:\Theta_{\mathrm{ker}}\to \Theta_{\mathrm{phys}}) that introduces physical units via reference scales (masses, lengths, times) explicitly declared.
19.1.4.2 Dimensional analysis rules
For any proposed coupling term, enforce dimensional consistency via:
Field dimension constraints (e.g., in (n=4), scalar kinetic term fixes ([\phi]=[M])).
Operator dimension constraints (derivatives add ([L]^{-1})).
Coupling dimension constraints (determine relevance/irrelevance in EFT sense).
When multiple terms appear, define dimensionless groups via Buckingham–(\Pi) construction. Declare which (\Pi)-groups are fit parameters versus derived predictions.
19.1.4.3 Parameter identification
Let (\theta\in \Theta) denote the physically parameterized model and (d\in\mathcal{D}) the data. Identification requires that the forward map[\mathcal{F}\theta:\ \theta \mapsto \mathbb{P}\theta(\cdot)=\mathcal{M}(\mathrm{Solve}(\theta))]is injective on (\Theta_{\mathrm{adm}}) up to declared equivalences, at least locally for parameters intended to be inferred.
A quantitative identifiability certificate may be based on Fisher information (for likelihood-based models):[\mathcal{I}(\theta)=\mathbb{E}\theta!\left[\nabla\theta \log p_\theta(d)\ \nabla_\theta \log p_\theta(d)^\top\right].]Ill-conditioning of (\mathcal{I}(\theta)) indicates non-identifiability or sloppiness; admissibility may require a lower bound on the smallest eigenvalue over the intended operating region.
19.1.4.4 Experimental mapping requirements
A mapping to physics is not complete without explicit experimental referents. Require:
A declared set of measurable observables ({Y_i}) with units and protocols.
A forward simulator producing predictions (Y_i^{\mathrm{pred}}(\theta)) with uncertainty quantification.
A falsification rule (Section 19.3.1) defining what constitutes a failure of the embedding.
A reproducibility package specification (Section 19.4.4).
19.2 Flower — Dynamics, Geodesics, and Engineering Programs (Formal)
19.2.1 Geodesic programs
19.2.1.1 Action functionals
Let ((\mathcal{X},G)) be a configuration manifold, where (\mathcal{X}) may be:
spacetime ((M,g)) for geodesics of test particles,
field configuration space (\Gamma(\mathfrak{B})),
kernel manifold (H),
or a product (\mathcal{F}).
A geodesic program specifies an action[\mathcal{A}[\gamma]=\int_{t_0}^{t_1} L(\gamma(t),\dot\gamma(t),t),dt]whose stationary curves are the desired trajectories.
Metric geodesics. If (L=\tfrac{1}{2}G_{\gamma(t)}(\dot\gamma,\dot\gamma)), the Euler–Lagrange equations yield[\nabla^{(G)}_{\dot\gamma}\dot\gamma=0.]
Relativistic particle template. On spacetime, use[\mathcal{A}[\gamma]=\int \sqrt{-g_{\mu\nu}(\gamma)\dot\gamma^\mu\dot\gamma^\nu},dt,]or the equivalent quadratic action with an einbein for numerical stability.
19.2.1.2 Constraint-aware geodesics
Let constraints be (\mathcal{C}_{\mathcal{X}}(\gamma(t))=0). Define the constrained action using Lagrange multipliers (\lambda(t)):[\mathcal{A}c[\gamma,\lambda]=\int \Bigl(L(\gamma,\dot\gamma,t)+\lambda(t)^\top \mathcal{C}{\mathcal{X}}(\gamma)\Bigr),dt.]Stationarity yields a differential-algebraic system (DAE). Admissible discretization must preserve:
constraint satisfaction (up to tolerance),
constraint consistency (hidden constraints),
stability (no drift blow-up).
For nonholonomic constraints (A(\gamma)\dot\gamma=0), use d’Alembert–Lagrange projection or a constrained variational integrator.
19.2.1.3 Numerical geodesic solvers
Let (\gamma:[0,1]\to\mathcal{X}) with boundary conditions (\gamma(0)=x_0), (\gamma(1)=x_1).
Algorithm 19.1 (Shooting method with constraint projection).
Input: x0, x1, initial guess v0, step size h, tolerance ε
Output: γ(t) approximating constrained geodesic from x0 to x1
1. repeat
2. Integrate ODE/DAE for (γ, v) forward from t=0 to t=1 using v(0)=v0
3. After each step: project γ onto constraint manifold via Π(γ)
4. Compute endpoint error e = Log_{x1}(γ(1)) or coordinate difference
5. Update v0 ← v0 - J^{-1} e (Newton) or v0 ← v0 - α J^T e (gradient)
6. until ||e|| ≤ ε and constraint residual ≤ ε
7. return γ
Here (J) is the sensitivity (endpoint Jacobian) computed by tangent linearization or automatic differentiation. The projection (\Pi) must be specified (exact solve, Newton, or proximal step).
Algorithm 19.2 (Discrete variational integrator).Construct a discrete Lagrangian (L_d(\gamma_k,\gamma_{k+1})\approx \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} L,dt) and solve the discrete Euler–Lagrange equations, enforcing constraints by discrete multipliers. This yields symplectic structure preservation when applicable.
19.2.1.4 Stability
Define the second variation (\delta^2\mathcal{A}[\gamma]). Stability of a geodesic segment is characterized by absence of conjugate points; numerically, track Jacobi fields (J(t)) satisfying the linearized geodesic equation:[\nabla_{\dot\gamma}\nabla_{\dot\gamma}J + R(J,\dot\gamma)\dot\gamma=0.]A practical certificate uses bounds on the smallest eigenvalue of the Hessian of the discretized action along the computed trajectory, together with constraint qualification conditions.
19.2.2 Field evolution programs
19.2.2.1 PDE templates
A field evolution program is specified by an operator equation[\mathcal{E}(g,\Phi)=0,]with (\mathcal{E}) derived from:
Euler–Lagrange equations of (\mathcal{S}),
Hamiltonian evolution with constraints,
dissipative/gradient flow:[\partial_t \Phi = -\mathcal{G}^{-1}\frac{\delta \mathcal{F}}{\delta \Phi},]
stochastic evolution (SPDE) with declared noise model.
A kernel-to-physics embedding is dynamically coherent if, for kernel-consistent states (h), the induced fields (\Phi=\mathsf{E}_\Phi(h)) lie in the solution set (or approximate solution set) of (\mathcal{E}) under the intended regime.
19.2.2.2 Operator-split evolution
When the generator decomposes (\mathcal{E}=\mathcal{E}_A+\mathcal{E}_B), implement Lie–Trotter or Strang splitting.
Algorithm 19.3 (Strang splitting with constraint handling).
Input: Φ0, timestep Δt, operators A and B, projection Π, tolerance ε
Output: ΦN approximating evolution at time NΔt
for n = 0..N-1:
Φ ← Flow(A, Φ, Δt/2)
Φ ← Π(Φ) (enforce constraints)
Φ ← Flow(B, Φ, Δt)
Φ ← Π(Φ)
Φ ← Flow(A, Φ, Δt/2)
Φ ← Π(Φ)
record invariants and residuals
return Φ
The flows (\mathrm{Flow}(A,\cdot)) and (\mathrm{Flow}(B,\cdot)) must be stable in the chosen norms; projection (\Pi) must be consistent with PDE constraints (e.g., divergence cleaning for Maxwell-like constraints).
19.2.2.3 Conservation monitoring
Let (Q_j(\Phi)) be conserved quantities (energy, momentum, charges). For a discrete solver, define monitored drift:[\Delta Q_j^{(n)} := Q_j(\Phi_n)-Q_j(\Phi_0).]A solver is conservation-certified over a horizon if (|\Delta Q_j^{(n)}|\le \epsilon_j) for all (n) and all monitored (j), with (\epsilon_j) derived from truncation error analysis and floating-point bounds.
19.2.2.4 Repair loops
A repair loop is a controlled modification applied when constraints or invariants drift beyond tolerance.
Common repair operators:
Projection repair: (\Phi\leftarrow \Pi(\Phi)) where (\Pi) solves (\min_{\Phi'\in\mathcal{A}} |\Phi'-\Phi|).
Penalty/augmented Lagrangian repair: add terms (\mu|\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{phys}}(\Phi)|^2) and adjust (\mu).
Constraint damping: modify evolution to (\partial_t \Phi = \mathcal{F}(\Phi)-\kappa,\nabla \mathcal{C}(\Phi)^\top \mathcal{C}(\Phi)).
A repair loop is admissible only if it preserves the declared physical meaning of the dynamics (e.g., does not introduce nonphysical dissipation unless the model is dissipative).
19.2.3 Engineering primitives
19.2.3.1 Boundary manipulation operators
Let (\Omega\subset M) (or spatial domain) with boundary (\partial\Omega). Boundary manipulation specifies operators that set or modify boundary conditions:[\mathcal{B}(\Phi,u)=0 \quad \text{on } \partial\Omega,]where (u) is the boundary control.
Standard forms:
Dirichlet: (\Phi|_{\partial\Omega}=u).
Neumann: (\nabla_n\Phi|_{\partial\Omega}=u).
Robin: (\alpha\Phi+\beta\nabla_n\Phi=u).
Absorbing/radiation: tailored to hyperbolic PDE to reduce reflections.
Admissibility requires well-posedness of the resulting initial-boundary value problem.
19.2.3.2 Source insertion operators
Define distributed sources (J(u)) or point sources (q,\delta(x-x_0)). Source insertion must respect conservation constraints (e.g., continuity equations):[\nabla_\mu J^\mu = 0]when required, or otherwise explicitly declare the conservation violation as part of the engineered intervention.
19.2.3.3 Constraint-respecting control
Let dynamics be (\partial_t \Phi = \mathcal{F}(\Phi,u)) with constraints (\mathcal{C}(\Phi)=0). The control (u) is admissible if it preserves constraints:[\frac{d}{dt}\mathcal{C}(\Phi(t)) = D\mathcal{C}(\Phi),\mathcal{F}(\Phi,u)=0](or is stabilized by certified projection). This yields a constrained control problem in which feasible controls lie in an affine subspace (or manifold) defined by the above condition.
19.2.3.4 Cost-minimizing control
Define a cost functional[J(u)=\int_{0}^{T} \ell(\Phi(t),u(t),t),dt + \Psi(\Phi(T)),]subject to PDE constraints. Adjoint-state methods provide gradients.
Algorithm 19.4 (Adjoint-based PDE-constrained optimization).
Input: initial control u, tolerance ε
Output: optimized control u*
repeat:
1) Forward solve: ∂t Φ = F(Φ,u), Φ(0)=Φ0
2) Backward solve adjoint: -∂t Λ = (∂Φ F)^T Λ + ∂Φ ℓ, Λ(T)=∂Φ Ψ
3) Gradient: g(t) = ∂u ℓ(Φ,u,t) + (∂u F)^T Λ
4) Update: u ← Proj_U ( u - α g ) (step α chosen by line search or trust region)
5) Enforce constraints via Π in forward solves if required
until ||g|| ≤ ε and feasibility residual ≤ ε
return u
Provide a certificate consisting of: (i) final gradient norm bound, (ii) feasibility residual bound, and (iii) objective value with uncertainty (numerical + modeling).
19.2.4 Model selection
19.2.4.1 Competing embeddings
Let ({\mathfrak{P}k}{k=1}^K) be candidate physical realizations (different (\mathsf{E}), different couplings, different dynamics). Each induces a forward predictive distribution (\mathbb{P}_{k,\theta}(d)).
19.2.4.2 Evidence comparison criteria
For data (d), compare models using:
Likelihood-based criteria (maximum likelihood, cross-validated predictive score),
Bayesian evidence:[p(d\mid \mathfrak{P}_k)=\int p(d\mid \theta,\mathfrak{P}_k),p(\theta\mid \mathfrak{P}_k),d\theta,]
Information criteria (AIC/BIC) where justified,
Robust scoring rules when likelihood is misspecified.
Admissible selection requires explicit declaration of the evaluation criterion and its statistical assumptions.
19.2.4.3 Falsification criteria
A model instance ((\mathfrak{P},\theta)) is falsified by an experiment (E) if observed data (d_E) violates the predicted corridor:[d_E \notin \mathcal{R}\alpha(\theta),]where (\mathcal{R}\alpha(\theta)) is a specified ((1-\alpha))-credible (or confidence) region under (\mathbb{P}_\theta). The falsification protocol must specify (\alpha), multiplicity correction if multiple tests, and the decision rule for rejection.
19.2.4.4 Selection certificates
A selection certificate includes:
The candidate set ({\mathfrak{P}_k}) and parameterizations.
The evaluation functional (S(\mathfrak{P}_k)) with uncertainty decomposition (statistical + numerical + systematic).
A dominance statement, e.g.[S(\mathfrak{P}_{k^\star}) \ge S(\mathfrak{P}_k) - \epsilon\quad \forall k,]with an explicit (\epsilon) bound.
Reproducible code and data artifacts (Section 19.4.4).
19.3 Cloud — Limits and Falsifiability
19.3.1 Non-falsifiable traps and avoidance
19.3.1.1 Define what counts as prediction
A prediction is a map from declared inputs to distributions over measurable outputs:[\mathrm{Pred}:\ (\theta,a)\mapsto \mathbb{P}_{\theta,a}(\cdot)\in\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{D}),]where (a\in\mathcal{A}) are experimental settings. A model that does not define (\mathrm{Pred}) on a nontrivial subset of ((\theta,a)) is not predictive.
19.3.1.2 Define what counts as falsification
A falsification rule is a measurable decision functional (\varphi:\mathcal{D}\to{0,1}) (reject/accept) with controlled error rates under the model. Falsifiability requires existence of an experiment family ({a}) such that (\mathrm{Pred}(\theta,a)) is not invariant across (\Theta_{\mathrm{adm}}), i.e. predictions vary with parameters in a testable way.
19.3.1.3 Guard against unconstrained mapping
Unconstrained embeddings can be made to fit arbitrary data by adding latent degrees of freedom. Prevention requires:
explicit bounds on model capacity (parameter dimension, function class norms, smoothness constraints),
identifiability checks (Section 19.1.4.3),
fixed prior structure for latent fields (if Bayesian),
explicit restriction of embedding class (\mathsf{E}\in \mathcal{E}_{\mathrm{adm}}) with certified constraints.
19.3.1.4 Require measurable outputs
Every claim of physical realization must be reducible to a measurable output family ({Y_i}) with defined units, protocol, uncertainty model, and decision thresholds. Claims without ({Y_i}) are inadmissible.
19.3.2 Stability limits in embeddings
19.3.2.1 Ill-posed embeddings
An embedding (\mathsf{E}) is ill-posed if it fails continuity or stability: there exist sequences (h_n\to h) with (|\mathsf{E}(h_n)-\mathsf{E}(h)|\not\to 0) in the relevant physical norm. Quantify with a condition number:[\kappa(h):=\limsup_{\delta\to 0}\ \sup_{|u|\le \delta}\frac{|\mathsf{E}(h+u)-\mathsf{E}(h)|}{|u|}.]Admissibility may require (\kappa(h)\le \kappa_{\max}) on the operating domain.
19.3.2.2 Overparameterized embeddings
Overparameterization occurs when the Jacobian (D\mathsf{E}(h)) has deficient rank relative to the parameter dimension needed for prediction, yielding non-identifiability. Detect via singular value thresholds of the sensitivity matrix and by measuring posterior/prior collapse in inference.
19.3.2.3 Sensitivity bounds
For a predicted observable (Y(h)=\mathcal{M}(\mathsf{E}(h))), compute local Lipschitz bounds:[|Y(h+u)-Y(h)|\le L(h),|u|.]Large (L(h)) indicates fragile predictions; admissibility may enforce (L(h)\le L_{\max}) or require stability margins in corridor definitions.
19.3.2.4 Certified warning conditions
A certified warning condition is a computable predicate (W(h)\in{0,1}) that guarantees entry into an unstable regime. For example:[W(h)=\mathbf{1}{\kappa(h)\ge \kappa_{\mathrm{warn}}}\ \vee\ \mathbf{1}{\lambda_{\min}(\mathcal{I}(\theta))\le \tau}\ \vee\ \mathbf{1}{\text{solver residual blow-up}}.]Warnings must be included in any prediction report (Section 19.4.4.2).
19.3.3 Physical admissibility corridors
Define the corridor (\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{corr}}\subseteq \Theta) by constraints expressing physical permissibility. Examples:
19.3.3.1 Energy constraints
