Impose boundedness below, positive-energy conditions (model-dependent), or explicit energy budgets for engineered interventions:[E(\Phi)\le E_{\max},\qquad \mathcal{H}(\Phi)\ge -C,]with declared norms and boundary assumptions.
19.3.3.2 Causality constraints (model dependent)
Require hyperbolicity of PDE operators, absence of closed timelike curves in allowed metric configurations, and restriction against superluminal signal propagation in effective metrics. In coupled models, enforce that characteristic cones remain inside the metric null cone unless explicitly intended and justified.
19.3.3.3 Observational constraints
Hard constraints from existing measurements define feasible parameter sets:[|Y_i^{\mathrm{pred}}(\theta)-Y_i^{\mathrm{obs}}|\le \Delta_i](or probabilistic analogues). The admissibility corridor is the intersection of all such constraints.
19.3.3.4 Safety constraints
Safety constraints restrict regimes associated with uncontrolled amplification, runaway instability, or hazardous energy densities. These must be explicit inequalities and include solver-triggered shutdown predicates.
19.3.4 No-go results
19.3.4.1 Nonexistence theorems (model dependent)
A nonexistence theorem asserts that no admissible (\theta) yields a physical realization under specified constraints. Examples include: incompatibility of symmetries with topology, impossibility of certain stress–energy tensors under energy conditions, or obstruction to global gauge structure on (M).
19.3.4.2 Lower bounds on required information
If the task is inference of (\theta), provide information-theoretic lower bounds on sample complexity or reconstruction error (e.g., via Cramér–Rao or minimax bounds), within a declared measurement model.
19.3.4.3 Lower bounds on required energy
For control/engineering problems, provide lower bounds of the form[\inf_{u\in\mathcal{U}{\mathrm{adm}}} J(u)\ \ge\ J{\min},]where (J) contains energy or action-like costs, derived via duality or variational lower bounding methods.
19.3.4.4 Certified impossibility in chosen embedding
A certified impossibility statement consists of a machine-checkable infeasibility proof, e.g.:
convex dual infeasibility certificate,
sum-of-squares certificate for polynomial constraints,
interval-arithmetic verified contradiction for nonlinear constraints,
formal proof artifact for discrete models.
19.4 Fractal — Experimental Protocols
19.4.1 Synthetic experiments
19.4.1.1 Simulated physical fields
Provide reference simulators for each candidate model, including discretization scheme, stability constraints, and error estimates. Simulators must output: predicted observables, constraint residuals, invariant drifts, and computational cost metrics.
19.4.1.2 Controlled boundary experiments
Design experiments where boundary data (u) is chosen and controlled; specify which boundary operators are realizable and how uncertainty enters. This is the natural regime for validating couplings and constraint preservation mechanisms.
19.4.1.3 Measurement-reconstruction experiments
Define a reconstruction operator (\mathcal{R}:\mathcal{D}\to \widehat{\mathcal{F}}) and test whether reconstructed fields satisfy constraints and invariants:[|\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{phys}}(\widehat{\Phi})|\le \epsilon,\qquad |Q(\widehat{\Phi})-Q(\Phi{\mathrm{true}})|\le \epsilon_Q.]
19.4.1.4 Reproducible outputs
All synthetic experiments must include fixed seeds (where stochastic), deterministic solver settings, and saved intermediate states sufficient to reproduce every figure and table.
19.4.2 Data-driven experiments
19.4.2.1 Dataset requirements
Declare: data provenance, measurement protocol, unit conventions, calibration, sampling cadence, missingness model, and bias sources. Provide explicit inclusion/exclusion rules.
19.4.2.2 Preprocessing pipelines
Preprocessing must be a deterministic pipeline with versioned dependencies. Provide: filtering, detrending, normalization, coordinate transforms, and uncertainty propagation.
19.4.2.3 Evaluation pipelines
Evaluation must separate training/validation/test (where applicable) and define metrics that correspond to falsifiable claims (not merely fit quality). Include uncertainty decomposition and sensitivity analysis.
19.4.2.4 Publication artifacts
Publication artifacts include: dataset hashes, code hashes, environment specification, and run manifests.
19.4.3 Verification integration
19.4.3.1 Constraint verification
Implement runtime checks for constraint residuals with thresholds, escalation levels, and automatic recording of violations.
19.4.3.2 Invariant verification
Monitor conserved quantities and provide certified drift bounds. Where possible, use structure-preserving integrators to reduce drift and then verify residual drift against theoretical truncation error.
19.4.3.3 Reconstruction verification
For inverse problems, verify that reconstructions satisfy forward consistency:[d \approx \mathcal{M}(\widehat{\Phi})]and feasibility:[\widehat{\Phi}\in \mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{phys}}.]
19.4.3.4 Certificate generation
Each run produces a certificate bundle containing:
parameter/coupling admissibility proof ((\theta\in\Theta_{\mathrm{adm}})),
solver residual and stability logs,
constraint/invariant verification logs,
falsification test outputs and thresholds,
reproducibility manifest.
19.4.4 Reporting
19.4.4.1 Prediction/falsification reports
A prediction report must include: predicted distributions, experimental settings, predicted corridors, and explicit rejection criteria. A falsification report must include: observed data, test statistic values, decision outcomes, and multiplicity corrections.
19.4.4.2 Stability reports
Include: condition numbers (embedding and inference), solver stability diagnostics, sensitivity measures, and warning predicates (W(h)) with timestamps/indices.
19.4.4.3 Corridor compliance reports
Report the corridor constraints and whether they are satisfied. Violations must include quantitative severity and causal attribution (parameter, solver, data, or modeling assumption).
19.4.4.4 Reproducibility bundles
A reproducibility bundle is complete only if it enables an independent rerun producing identical outputs (within declared numerical tolerance) and includes a verification toolchain that checks certificates.
Chapter 19 — Mapping to Physics: Spacetime, Fields, Constraint-Respecting Couplings
Chapter 20 — Higher-Dimensional Extensions: ((D^n)), Category Lifts, Functorial Structure
This chapter generalizes the kernel architecture ((H^4, D^5)) to an indexed family ({(H^k, D^{k+1})}_{k\ge 1}) together with (i) constraint operators ({C_k}), (ii) gauge groups ({\mathcal G_k}) and quotient pattern spaces ({\mathrm{PATT}_k}), (iii) invariant suites ({\mathrm{Inv}_k}), and (iv) representation-changing functors linking continuous and finite realizations. The goal is a functorial, implementation-faithful system in which:(1) each level (k) is a precise constrained state space;(2) transformations at level (k) form a well-typed admissible morphism class;(3) discretizations and basis changes commute with canonicalization and verification; and(4) higher-dimensional constructions admit computable approximations with certified error, stability, and corridor governance.
20.1 Square — Generalization of the Kernel
20.1.1 Define ((H^k)) and ((D^{k+1})) families
20.1.1.1 Recursive definitions
Ambient spaces. For each integer (k\ge 1), fix a separable complex Hilbert space (\mathfrak H_k) (“ambient”) equipped with inner product (\langle \cdot,\cdot\rangle_k) and norm (|\cdot|_k). The ambient choice must support:(i) a dense test subspace (\mathcal D_k\subset \mathfrak H_k) (e.g., smooth compactly supported functions in an (L^2) realization),(ii) a distributional dual (\mathcal D_k') to support weak formulations,(iii) a regularity ladder (\mathfrak H_k^{(s)}) (Sobolev-type scale) for stability and truncation analysis.
State objects. A level-(k) state is an element (H\in \mathfrak H_k) subject to constraints and gauge identification. The “kernel” space is defined via constraint operators (C_k) below.
Transform objects. Define a transformation ambient (\mathfrak D_{k+1}) as a class of (possibly unbounded) operators acting on (\mathfrak H_k), with a distinguished admissible subcollection (D^{k+1}\subset \mathfrak D_{k+1}) satisfying corridor and computability axioms.
Recursive compatibility. The family ({(\mathfrak H_k,\mathfrak D_{k+1})}) is required to admit level-changing maps (lifts/collapses) (\mathrm{Lift}{k\to \ell}) and (\mathrm{Red}{\ell\to k}) for (1\le k\le \ell), such that:
(\mathrm{Lift}{k\to \ell}:\mathfrak H_k \to \mathfrak H\ell) is linear and bounded on the regularity classes used for computation;
(\mathrm{Red}{\ell\to k}:\mathfrak H\ell \to \mathfrak H_k) is a bounded linear map (or a closed densely-defined map with certified domain);
(\mathrm{Red}{\ell\to k}\circ \mathrm{Lift}{k\to \ell} = I) on (\mathfrak H_k) (exact embedding) or (= I + \mathcal E_{k,\ell}) with a certified embedding error (|\mathcal E_{k,\ell}|\le \varepsilon_{k,\ell}).
These maps define the minimum coherence required to interpret “higher-dimensional extension” as a controlled refinement rather than an unrelated new model.
20.1.1.2 Constraint operator families ((C_k))
Constraint codomains. For each (k), fix a Banach space (\mathfrak C_k) (often a Hilbert space) with norm (|\cdot|_{\mathfrak C_k}). Constraints are represented as operators[C_k:\mathrm{Dom}(C_k)\subseteq \mathfrak H_k \to \mathfrak C_k]with (\mathcal D_k\subseteq \mathrm{Dom}(C_k)) dense in (\mathfrak H_k).
Kernel definition. The constrained state space at level (k) is[H^k ;:=; \ker(C_k);=;{H\in \mathrm{Dom}(C_k): C_k(H)=0}.]
Closedness and stability. The constraint operator (C_k) must be closed (or closable with specified closure) so that (\ker(C_k)) is closed in (\mathfrak H_k) under the graph norm. Sufficient conditions include:
(C_k) closed and (\mathfrak C_k) Hausdorff (\Rightarrow \ker(C_k)) closed.
If (C_k) is bounded, (\ker(C_k)) is automatically closed.
Compatibility across levels. For (k\le \ell), the constraint family is coherent if there exists a bounded linear “constraint lift” (\Lambda_{k\to \ell}:\mathfrak C_k\to \mathfrak C_\ell) such that the diagram commutes on a dense core:[C_\ell\circ \mathrm{Lift}{k\to \ell} ;=; \Lambda{k\to \ell}\circ C_k.]This yields constraint-preserving lifts:[H\in H^k ;\Rightarrow; \mathrm{Lift}_{k\to \ell}(H)\in H^\ell.]
Constraint conditioning. Define the local (or global) constraint conditioning at a state (H\in \mathrm{Dom}(C_k)) by:[\kappa_{C_k}(H) ;:=; \sup_{\delta H\neq 0}\frac{|DC_k[H](\delta H)|_{\mathfrak C_k}}{|\delta H|k},]when Fréchet differentiable; otherwise use a Lipschitz constant of (C_k) on admissible neighborhoods. Certified corridors depend on uniform bounds of (\kappa{C_k}) over the feasible tube.
20.1.1.3 Pattern quotient families
Gauge groups. For each (k), fix a topological group (\mathcal G_k) acting on (H^k) by homeomorphisms (and isometries when required):[\cdot:\mathcal G_k\times H^k\to H^k,\qquad (g,H)\mapsto g\cdot H.]The action is required to preserve constraints: if (H\in H^k) then (g\cdot H\in H^k).
Equivalence classes. Define pattern equivalence:[H\sim_k H' \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \exists g\in \mathcal G_k:; H'=g\cdot H.]Define the pattern space:[\mathrm{PATT}_k ;:=; H^k/\mathcal G_k]equipped with the quotient topology (and quotient metric when a gauge-invariant metric is specified).
Regular quotient conditions. The quotient is required to be well-behaved for canonicalization and numerical stability. Sufficient structural assumptions include:
proper group action (so orbit space is Hausdorff),
existence of local slices (so canonical representatives exist locally),
existence of a measurable selector when global slices fail (for deterministic canonicalization).
Gauge-invariant observables. Any functional (\Phi:H^k\to \mathbb R^m) used as an invariant must satisfy (\Phi(g\cdot H)=\Phi(H)) for all (g\in\mathcal G_k).
20.1.1.4 Invariant suite families
Invariant suite at level (k). Fix a measurable map[\mathrm{Inv}_k: H^k \to \mathbb R^{m_k}]or to a product of structured spaces (vectors, spectra, measures). The suite is admissible if:
(Gauge invariance) (\mathrm{Inv}_k(g\cdot H)=\mathrm{Inv}_k(H)) for all (g\in\mathcal G_k).
(Stability) (|\mathrm{Inv}_k(H)-\mathrm{Inv}_k(H')|\le L_k|H-H'|_k) on corridor neighborhoods (local Lipschitz).
(Computability) there exists a finite estimator (\widehat{\mathrm{Inv}}_{k,N}) on truncated representations with certified error bounds.
Cross-level invariants. For (k\le \ell), invariants are coherent if there exists (\Xi_{k\to \ell}) such that[\mathrm{Inv}\ell(\mathrm{Lift}{k\to \ell}(H)) ;=; \Xi_{k\to \ell}(\mathrm{Inv}_k(H))]for all (H\in H^k) within the applicable regularity class (exact), or within a certified error tolerance (approximate).
Minimal generating suites. A suite (\mathrm{Inv}_k) is minimal (relative to a specified admissible transformation family) if for any other invariant suite (\widetilde{\mathrm{Inv}}_k) satisfying the same invariance requirements, there exists a map (F) with[\widetilde{\mathrm{Inv}}_k(H) = F(\mathrm{Inv}_k(H)).]Practical minimality is certified via redundancy diagnostics (rank tests, mutual information tests, or sensitivity analysis) on a reference ensemble.
20.1.2 Category-theoretic framing
20.1.2.1 Objects as constrained spaces
Define a category (\mathbf{HKer}) (“Holographic Kernels”) whose objects are tuples[\mathbb K_k ;:=;(\mathfrak H_k,; C_k,; H^k,; \mathcal G_k,; \mathrm{PATT}_k,; d_k,; \mathrm{Inv}_k,; \mathbb B_k)]where:
(\mathfrak H_k) is the ambient Hilbert space,
(C_k) is the constraint operator with (H^k=\ker(C_k)),
(\mathcal G_k) is the gauge group acting on (H^k),
(\mathrm{PATT}_k) is the quotient,
(d_k) is a chosen metric on (H^k) (typically gauge-invariant or defined on the quotient),
(\mathrm{Inv}_k) is the invariant suite,
(\mathbb B_k) is a corridor/budget structure (a specified feasible tube with tolerances and audit rules).
Object validity axioms. An object (\mathbb K_k) is valid if:
(H^k) is nonempty and closed in (\mathfrak H_k);
the gauge action is well-defined on (H^k) and preserves feasibility tubes;
(d_k) is complete on the feasible tube (or completeness is replaced by a certified compactness substitute on bounded subsets used in proofs);
(\mathrm{Inv}_k) is defined and stable on the feasible tube;
(\mathbb B_k) defines measurable admissibility predicates for algorithms.
20.1.2.2 Morphisms as admissible transforms
A morphism in (\mathbf{HKer}) from (\mathbb K_k) to itself is an operator (T:H^k\to H^k) satisfying:
Constraint preservation.[H\in H^k ;\Rightarrow; T(H)\in H^k.]
Gauge equivariance (or gauge compatibility). There exists a map (\Gamma_T:\mathcal G_k\to \mathcal G_k) such that[T(g\cdot H);=;\Gamma_T(g)\cdot T(H)]for all (g\in\mathcal G_k), (H\in H^k). When (\Gamma_T=\mathrm{id}), (T) is strictly equivariant.
Corridor admissibility. If (\mathbb B_k) specifies budgets (\delta) for invariants and residuals, then (T) must satisfy the admissibility predicate:[\mathrm{Adm}_k(T; \delta)=\mathrm{true},]meaning (at minimum) bounded invariant drift, bounded reconstruction degradation (when reconstruction operators are part of the kernel package), and bounded operator sensitivity under the chosen metric.
Computability. (T) admits a finite executable representation on truncations, with determinism rules.
Denote by (D^{k+1}) the class of all such admissible morphisms. Composition is function composition; identity is (I). Closure under composition is required for the admissibility predicate under corridor composition rules.
20.1.2.3 Functors between representations
Define representation categories:
(\mathbf{Cont}_k): continuous realizations (infinite-dimensional, analytic objects).
(\mathbf{Disc}_{k,N}): discrete realizations at truncation level (N) (finite arrays, sparse structures, or hierarchical representations).
A discretization functor[\mathcal F_{k,N}:\mathbf{Cont}k \to \mathbf{Disc}{k,N}]maps a continuous object (\mathbb K_k) to its finite realization (\mathbb K_{k,N}) by specifying:
basis/truncation parameters,
discrete constraint operator (C_{k,N}),
discrete gauge representation (\mathcal G_{k,N}),
discrete invariant estimators (\widehat{\mathrm{Inv}}_{k,N}),
discrete corridor and tolerance translations.
A refinement naturality requirement: for (N<M), there exist projection maps (\pi_{M\to N}) such that[\pi_{M\to N}\circ \mathcal F_{k,M} ;\approx; \mathcal F_{k,N}]with explicitly stated approximation error and determinism guarantees.
A change-of-basis functor (\mathcal U:\mathbf{Cont}_k\to \mathbf{Cont}_k) is induced by a unitary (or bounded invertible) map (U:\mathfrak H_k\to \mathfrak H_k), transporting constraints and invariants by:[C_k^{(U)} := C_k\circ U^{-1},\qquad \mathrm{Inv}_k^{(U)} := \mathrm{Inv}_k\circ U^{-1},]with gauge action conjugated appropriately.
20.1.2.4 Natural transformations as rewrite rules
A compiler or rewrite system is formalized as a natural transformation between functors mapping “high-level transform specifications” to “executable programs”.
Let (\mathcal P_k) be a category of programs (typed operator graphs with serialization), and let:
(\mathcal GSpec_k:\mathbf{HKer}\to \mathcal P_k) map a transform specification to a program in a chosen normal form,
(\mathcal GExec_k:\mathcal P_k\to \mathrm{End}(H^k)) interpret a program as an operator.
A rewrite rule system is a natural transformation (\eta:\mathcal GSpec_k\Rightarrow \mathcal GSpec_k) such that for each object (\mathbb K_k),[\mathcal GExec_k(\eta_{\mathbb K_k}(P)) ;=; \mathcal GExec_k(P)]or equality holds up to certified corridor tolerances (approximate rewriting). Soundness requires that rewritten programs preserve admissibility certificates; completeness is not assumed but search must expose failure certificates when rewriting cannot attain a target.
20.1.3 Limits and colimits
20.1.3.1 Projective limits (infinite refinements)
Consider a refinement ladder ({\mathbb K_{k,N}}{N\in\mathbb N}) with projection maps[\pi{N+1\to N}: \mathbb K_{k,N+1}\to \mathbb K_{k,N}]that are compatible with constraints, invariants, and canonicalization (see §20.1.4). The family ((\mathbb K_{k,N},\pi_{N+1\to N})) defines an inverse system.
The projective limit object (\varprojlim_N \mathbb K_{k,N}) is the set of consistent sequences ((x_N)N) such that (\pi{N+1\to N}(x_{N+1})=x_N). Under standard completeness assumptions, the projective limit embeds into (\mathbf{Cont}_k) and provides a constructive route to the continuous space via consistent refinement.
Constraint-limit compatibility. Require:[\pi_{M\to N}(\ker(C_{k,M}))\subseteq \ker(C_{k,N}),]and, in the limit, the constraint residual tends to (0) as (N\to\infty) for any representable state.
20.1.3.2 Inductive limits (expansion)
Let ({\mathbb K_{k,N}}) be an expansion ladder where (N) adds degrees of freedom (larger basis support), with injection maps[\iota_{N\to N+1}:\mathbb K_{k,N}\to \mathbb K_{k,N+1}.]The inductive limit (\varinjlim_N \mathbb K_{k,N}) forms the union modulo the equivalence induced by the injections, representing “growth to the full object.” This captures progressive model enrichment and is the natural setting for implementing higher-dimensional lifts as basis expansions or added coordinate dimensions.
Admissibility across expansions. If a transform (T_N) is admissible on (\mathbb K_{k,N}), the expanded transform (T_{N+1}) must satisfy:[T_{N+1}\circ \iota_{N\to N+1} ;\approx; \iota_{N\to N+1}\circ T_N]with certified defect bounded by expansion-induced tolerance scaling.
20.1.3.3 Consistency conditions
Commutative diagram requirements. For any combination of:
discretization/refinement,
basis change,
gauge action,
canonicalization,
invariant estimation,
the resulting diagrams must commute exactly (symbolic) or commute up to certified tolerances (numerical). Typical required commutations include:
Constraint functoriality:(C_{k,N}\circ \mathcal F_{k,N} \approx \mathcal F_{C,k,N}\circ C_k).
Gauge action transport:(\mathcal F_{k,N}(g\cdot H) = g_N\cdot \mathcal F_{k,N}(H)) for a representation homomorphism (g\mapsto g_N).
Invariant estimation compatibility:(\widehat{\mathrm{Inv}}{k,N}(\mathcal F{k,N}(H)) \approx \mathrm{Inv}_k(H)).
Canonicalization naturality:(\mathrm{Can}{k,N}(\mathcal F{k,N}(H)) \approx \mathcal F_{k,N}(\mathrm{Can}_k(H))).
Each approximation symbol (\approx) must be replaced in implementation by an explicit inequality bound and a deterministic error-reporting rule.
20.1.3.4 Computable approximations
A computable approximation scheme for (\mathbb K_k) consists of:
a truncation family ({\mathbb K_{k,N}}),
a reconstruction map (\mathcal R_{k,N}:\mathbb K_{k,N}\to \mathfrak H_k),
error estimators (E_{k,N}) such that for admissible (H\in H^k),[|H - \mathcal R_{k,N}(\mathcal F_{k,N}(H))|k ;\le; E{k,N}(H),]with (E_{k,N}(H)\to 0) as (N\to\infty) for states in the target regularity class.
Certified operator approximation. For admissible transforms (T\in D^{k+1}), there must exist executable approximations (T_N) such that:[|T(H)-\mathcal R_{k,N}(T_N(\mathcal F_{k,N}(H)))|k ;\le; E^{(T)}{k,N}(H),]with explicit decomposition of error into truncation, numerical, and model-mismatch components.
20.1.4 Canonicalization in higher dimensions
20.1.4.1 Gauge families
The gauge family ({\mathcal G_k}) is required to be coherent across dimension changes. For (k\le \ell), a gauge lift homomorphism (\Upsilon_{k\to \ell}:\mathcal G_k\to \mathcal G_\ell) must satisfy:[\mathrm{Lift}{k\to \ell}(g\cdot H) ;=; \Upsilon{k\to \ell}(g)\cdot \mathrm{Lift}_{k\to \ell}(H).]Similarly, reductions must be compatible with gauge actions, either strictly or up to a certified defect map capturing information loss.
Gauge families admit additional structure when (\mathcal G_k) is a Lie group: one may define Lie algebras (\mathfrak g_k), enabling differential gauge fixing and gauge-covariant derivatives in higher-dimensional geometry.
20.1.4.2 Canon map families
A canonicalization map at level (k) is a function[\mathrm{Can}_k: H^k \to H^k]satisfying:
Idempotence on canonical set: (\mathrm{Can}_k(\mathrm{Can}_k(H))=\mathrm{Can}_k(H)).
Orbit selection: (\mathrm{Can}k(H)\in [H]{\sim_k}) (same gauge orbit).
Gauge invariance: (\mathrm{Can}_k(g\cdot H)=\mathrm{Can}_k(H)).
Stability: on corridor neighborhoods,[|\mathrm{Can}_k(H)-\mathrm{Can}_k(H')|k \le L{\mathrm{Can},k}|H-H'|_k.]
When global selectors are impossible, (\mathrm{Can}_k) is defined by a deterministic tie-breaking rule among multiple valid representatives.
Compatibility with lifts. Require canonicalization commutes with lift/reduction up to certified tolerance:[\mathrm{Can}\ell(\mathrm{Lift}{k\to \ell}(H)) ;\approx; \mathrm{Lift}_{k\to \ell}(\mathrm{Can}_k(H)).]
20.1.4.3 Hashing and identity
A canonical identity for a state is derived from deterministic serialization. Define:
a serialization map (\mathrm{Ser}_k:\mathrm{Im}(\mathrm{Can}_k)\to {0,1}^*) (byte string),
a cryptographic hash (\mathrm{Hash}:{0,1}^*\to {0,1}^{256}) (or specified output length).
Then the canonical identifier is:[\mathrm{ID}_k(H);:=;\mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}_k(\mathrm{Can}_k(H))).]
Determinism requirements. Serialization must be independent of:
floating-point nondeterminism (by quantization and rounding policy),
thread scheduling (by fixed evaluation order),
platform endianness (by specified byte ordering),
basis representation (by portability constraints below).
Perturbation robustness (optional). If stable identities under small perturbations are required, define a robust ID as a hash of quantized invariants and/or multiscale summaries:[\mathrm{RID}_k(H) := \mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Quant}(\mathrm{Inv}_k(\mathrm{Can}_k(H));\tau)),]where (\tau) is a corridor-controlled tolerance and (\mathrm{Quant}) is deterministic.
20.1.4.4 Portability conditions
Portability requires that identities and canonical forms are invariant under admissible re-encodings. For any basis-change functor (\mathcal U) induced by a unitary (U), require:[\mathrm{ID}k^{(U)}(U H) ;=; \mathrm{ID}k(H)]or equality holds after mapping both to a shared canonical representation. Equivalent portability is expressed by commutative diagrams between canonicalization, discretization, and basis change:[\mathcal F{k,N}\circ \mathrm{Can}k ;\approx; \mathrm{Can}{k,N}\circ \mathcal F{k,N},\qquad\mathcal F_{k,N}\circ U ;\approx; U_N\circ \mathcal F_{k,N}.]Portability is validated by regression suites that run canonicalization/hashing across multiple implementations and confirm identical IDs for the same abstract state.
20.2 Flower — Higher-Dimensional Operators
20.2.1 Generalized quaternary engines
20.2.1.1 (k)-stage cycle definitions
A (k)-stage engine at level (k) is a tuple of admissible transforms[Q^{(k)} := (Q_1,\dots,Q_k),\qquad Q_i\in D^{k+1},]with the cycle operator defined by composition:[\mathcal Q^{(k)} ;:=; Q_k\circ Q_{k-1}\circ \cdots \circ Q_1.]
Each stage must be well-typed on the feasible tube:[Q_i:\mathcal T_k(\delta)\subseteq H^k \to H^k]where (\mathcal T_k(\delta)) is a corridor-defined feasible neighborhood.
A (k)-stage engine is admissible if:
every stage is admissible under (\delta), and
the composed cycle satisfies a controlled closure property (§20.2.1.2).
20.2.1.2 Closure properties
The engine has ((\varepsilon,\Delta))-closure if there exists a bounded operator (\Delta:H^k\to \mathfrak H_k) and a scalar (\varepsilon\ge 0) such that:[\mathcal Q^{(k)}(H) ;=; H ;+; \varepsilon,\Delta(H)]for all (H) in the specified feasible tube, with[|\Delta(H)|k \le B\Delta(1+|H|_k).]
Increment classification. The increment operator (\Delta) is classified by:
order (linear/nonlinear),
regularity mapping ((\mathfrak H_k^{(s)}\to \mathfrak H_k^{(s-r)})),
constraint tangency (whether (\Delta(H)) lies in the tangent cone of feasibility),
invariant drift vector (induced change in (\mathrm{Inv}_k)).
When the closure is approximate, the residual[R_{\mathrm{cl}}(H):=\mathcal Q^{(k)}(H)-H-\varepsilon\Delta(H)]must satisfy (|R_{\mathrm{cl}}(H)|_k\le \eta(\delta,N)) with explicit dependence on corridor and truncation.
20.2.1.3 Resonance properties
A state (H\in H^k) is in (n)-resonance with (\mathcal Q^{(k)}) if:[(\mathcal Q^{(k)})^n(H) ;=; g\cdot H]for some (g\in\mathcal G_k). If (g) is the identity, (H) is a strict periodic point. Resonance is measured on the quotient (\mathrm{PATT}_k) by:[[(\mathcal Q^{(k)})^n(H)] = [H] \quad \text{in } \mathrm{PATT}_k.]
Linearized resonance spectrum. If (\mathcal Q^{(k)}) is differentiable at (H), define the Jacobian (D\mathcal Q^{(k)}[H]). Resonance neighborhoods are described by eigenvalues of the Jacobian restricted to a gauge-fixed slice; neutral stability corresponds to eigenvalues on the unit circle.
Resonance invariants. Define a resonance signature map:[\mathrm{ResSig}_k(H;n) := \big(|\mathrm{Inv}_k((\mathcal Q^{(k)})^n(H))-\mathrm{Inv}_k(H)|,; \mathrm{Hol}_k(H;n)\big),]where (\mathrm{Hol}_k) is a holonomy-like quantity induced by noncommutativity and gauge transport (formalized in §20.2.3).
20.2.1.4 Drift control
Given corridor budgets (\delta) for invariant drift, constraint residual drift, and reconstruction error (when applicable), define the drift vector:[\Delta_{\mathrm{inv}}(H) := \mathrm{Inv}k(\mathcal Q^{(k)}(H))-\mathrm{Inv}k(H),\qquad\Delta{\mathrm{con}}(H) := |C_k(\mathcal Q^{(k)}(H))|{\mathfrak C_k}.]
The engine is corridor-stable if:[|\Delta_{\mathrm{inv}}(H)|\le \delta_{\mathrm{inv}},\qquad\Delta_{\mathrm{con}}(H)\le \delta_{\mathrm{con}}]for all (H) in the feasible tube, and if repeated iteration preserves a feasible tube for the specified iteration horizon.
Projection-based drift control. Define a feasibility projection (\Pi_k:\mathfrak H_k\to H^k) (exact projection where computable, or approximate projection via constrained optimization). Drift-controlled iteration uses:[H_{n+1} := \Pi_k(\mathcal Q^{(k)}(H_n)).]A certified bound requires (\Pi_k) to be nonexpansive or Lipschitz on the tube, and to reduce constraint residual deterministically.
20.2.2 Higher-order gate systems
20.2.2.1 Basis selection in higher dimensions
For each level (k), define a parameterized generator family ({E^{(k)}a}{a\in \mathcal A_k}) (possibly finite or countable) such that admissible transforms admit approximations by exponentials and compositions of these generators. A minimal requirement is dense reachability in a topology relevant to the cost function:[\overline{\langle \exp(\theta E^{(k)}a)\rangle{a,\theta}}^{;|\cdot|} \supseteq D^{k+1}_{\mathrm{target}}]for a specified target subfamily.
Typed generators. Each generator (E^{(k)}_a) includes:
domain (\mathrm{Dom}(E^{(k)}_a)\subseteq H^k),
constraint tangency condition (infinitesimal constraint preservation),
gauge compatibility condition.
A generator is constraint-aware if (DC_kH=0) for feasible (H), ensuring first-order feasibility preservation.
20.2.2.2 Program graphs
A gate program is a typed directed acyclic graph (or a graph with certified cycle semantics) whose nodes are gates (G_j) and edges encode composition order. A program (P) defines an operator:[T_P := G_m\circ \cdots \circ G_1.]
Each gate (G_j) is either:
exponential gate (G_j=\exp(\theta_j E^{(k)}_{a_j})),
primitive gate (closed-form kernel),
projection/repair gate (corridor enforcement).
Program validity. A program is valid if:
all intermediate states remain in the declared domain of subsequent gates (domain safety),
each gate is admissible or the program contains repair gates that restore admissibility with certification,
serialization is deterministic.
20.2.2.3 Rewrite rules
A rewrite rule is a relation (P \Rightarrow P') on programs such that (T_P=T_{P'}) (exact) or (d_k(T_P(H),T_{P'}(H))\le \eta) (approximate, with (\eta) certified). Rewrite rules include:
cancellation ((G\circ G^{-1}\Rightarrow I)),
commutation where commutators are certified small,
BCH compression where higher-order terms are bounded,
fusion of adjacent linear gates into a single gate.
Soundness requirement. Every rewrite must either preserve existing certificates or output updated certificates with explicit drift/stability recalculation.
Termination and normal forms. Define a well-founded measure (program length, cost, or lexicographic index tuple) that strictly decreases under “normalizing rewrites,” ensuring termination to a canonical normal form when exact equivalence is used. When approximate equivalence is used, termination is governed by a tolerance floor and monotone cost descent.
20.2.2.4 Optimization
Define a cost functional (\mathcal C_k(P)) incorporating:
runtime proxy (gate count, FLOP estimate),
memory proxy (peak footprint),
corridor penalties (invariant drift, constraint residual),
approximation error penalties (BCH truncation, discretization error).
The optimization problem is:[\min_{P\in \mathcal P_k};\mathcal C_k(P)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad \mathrm{Adm}k(T_P;\delta)=\mathrm{true},;; T_P\in \mathcal T{\mathrm{goal}}.]
Graph search formulation. With a finite gate library, compile a weighted directed graph where vertices are intermediate “macro-states” (or equivalence classes of transforms) and edges are gates. Then:
Dijkstra/A* yields shortest cost programs under admissibility heuristics,
multi-objective search yields Pareto-front programs.
Certified heuristics. A heuristic (h) used in A* must be a certified lower bound on remaining cost to preserve optimality claims.
20.2.3 Higher-dimensional geometry
20.2.3.1 Metrics and connections
State-space geometry. For each (k), choose a metric tensor (Riemannian or Finsler) on the feasible manifold (H^k) (or on its quotient (\mathrm{PATT}k)). In infinite dimensions, define a Riemannian structure via a bounded, coercive, self-adjoint operator (G_k(H)) on the tangent space:[\langle u,v\rangle{G_k(H)} := \langle u,, G_k(H)v\rangle_k,]with uniform bounds (m|v|_k^2\le \langle v,G_k(H)v\rangle_k\le M|v|_k^2) on the feasible tube.
Gauge quotient metric. If the gauge group acts by isometries, define the quotient metric on (\mathrm{PATT}k) by:[d{\mathrm{PATT}k}([H],[H']) := \inf{g\in \mathcal G_k} d_k(H,g\cdot H').]
Connections. If (H^k\to \mathrm{PATT}_k) is treated as a principal bundle, define a connection by specifying horizontal subspaces ( \mathcal H_H\subset T_H H^k) complementary to vertical (gauge) directions. This enables gauge-covariant differentiation and well-defined parallel transport of perturbations and features.
20.2.3.2 Curvature objects
Curvature arises from:
noncommutativity of admissible transformation flows,
geometric curvature of the feasible manifold,
gauge holonomy.
Define an operator-curvature measure for two infinitesimal generators (X,Y) by:[\mathcal K(X,Y)[H] := D X[H]\cdot Y(H);-;D Y[H]\cdot X(H);-;X,Y,]interpreted in the chosen coordinate chart and projected to the horizontal subspace (gauge-fixed curvature).
For finite gate loops, define holonomy as the deviation of a loop composition from identity in the quotient:[\mathrm{Hol}k(\gamma;H) := d{\mathrm{PATT}k}\big([H],[T\gamma(H)]\big),]where (T_\gamma) is the composed transform along the loop (\gamma).
Measurable curvature invariants are obtained by integrating curvature densities across scales and reporting them as part of (\mathrm{Inv}_k) when stability permits.
20.2.3.3 Geodesic programs
Define a path of transforms (T(t)) in (D^{k+1}) acting on states (H(t)=T(t)(H_0)). A geodesic synthesis problem is:[\min_{T(\cdot)} \int_0^1 | \dot T(t)|_{\mathsf g}^2,dt\quad\text{s.t.}\quadH(0)=H_0,;H(1)=H_1,;\mathrm{Adm}k(T(t);\delta);\forall t.]Here (|\cdot|{\mathsf g}) is a metric on transformation space (operator norm metric, cost-induced metric, or information-weighted metric).
The Euler–Lagrange equations define a geodesic flow in transformation space; in practice, numerical solvers compute discrete geodesics as shortest programs in a gate graph (compatibility with Chapter 7–10 structures).
20.2.3.4 Computation
Computational differential geometry at level (k) requires:
discretized metrics (G_{k,N}) and their stable application/inversion,
curvature estimators for commutators and holonomy with truncation error bounds,
geodesic solvers (shooting, gradient descent) with corridor projections.
A minimal curvature estimator for a pair of small-step gates (A=\exp(\epsilon X)), (B=\exp(\epsilon Y)) uses the commutator loop:[L := A^{-1}B^{-1}AB,]and measures (\mathrm{Hol}_k(L;H)) as an empirical curvature proxy with certified bounds under BCH truncation assumptions.
20.2.4 Cross-dimensional couplings
20.2.4.1 Coupling operators between ((H^k)) spaces
A cross-dimensional coupling from level (k) to level (\ell) is a pair ((\Phi_{k\to \ell},\Psi_{\ell\to k})) with:[\Phi_{k\to \ell}:H^k\to H^\ell,\qquad \Psi_{\ell\to k}:H^\ell\to H^k,]interpreted as lift and reduction. These operators must be:
well-typed on feasible tubes,
computable on discrete realizations,
stable with explicit Lipschitz bounds.
When (\Phi_{k\to \ell}) is an embedding and (\Psi_{\ell\to k}) is a left inverse, the coupling is lossless; otherwise, reduction induces information loss quantified by invariant drift and reconstruction residual.
20.2.4.2 Constraint compatibility
Constraints must commute with coupling. There must exist (\Lambda_{k\to \ell}) such that:[C_\ell(\Phi_{k\to \ell}(H))=\Lambda_{k\to \ell}(C_k(H)).]This implies feasibility preservation under lifts. For reductions, require either:[C_k(\Psi_{\ell\to k}(H^\ell))=0 \quad \text{whenever } C_\ell(H^\ell)=0,]or a certified repair projection (\Pi_k) after reduction.
20.2.4.3 Invariant transfer
Invariant transfer is formalized by pullback/pushforward maps:
pullback along lift: ((\mathrm{Inv}k) \mapsto (\mathrm{Inv}\ell\circ \Phi_{k\to \ell})),
pushforward along reduction: ((\mathrm{Inv}_\ell)\mapsto (\mathrm{Inv}k\circ \Psi{\ell\to k})).
A coupling is invariant-compatible if there exist deterministic maps (\Xi,\Upsilon) such that:[\mathrm{Inv}\ell(\Phi{k\to \ell}(H))=\Xi(\mathrm{Inv}k(H)),\qquad\mathrm{Inv}k(\Psi{\ell\to k}(\widetilde H))=\Upsilon(\mathrm{Inv}\ell(\widetilde H))]on feasible sets (or with certified tolerance). This enables corridor governance across levels: budgets at one level translate to budgets at another.
20.2.4.4 Stability
Define coupling condition numbers on feasible tubes:[\kappa(\Phi_{k\to \ell}) := \sup_{H\neq H'} \frac{|\Phi_{k\to \ell}(H)-\Phi_{k\to \ell}(H')|\ell}{|H-H'|k},\quad\kappa(\Psi{\ell\to k}) := \sup \frac{|\Psi{\ell\to k}(\widetilde H)-\Psi_{\ell\to k}(\widetilde H')|k}{|\widetilde H-\widetilde H'|\ell}.]Cross-level algorithms must include these constants in error propagation and corridor scaling laws. If (\kappa) is large, coupling is ill-conditioned and requires either regularization, reduced operating domain, or no-go certification (§20.3.4).
20.3 Cloud — Bounds and Complexity Growth
20.3.1 Curse of dimensionality analysis
20.3.1.1 Sample complexity growth
For a feasible tube (\mathcal T_k(\delta)\subset H^k) with effective dimension (d_{\mathrm{eff}}(k,\delta)) (e.g., metric entropy dimension, manifold dimension, or Kolmogorov width), the number of samples required to achieve uniform approximation error (\epsilon) under Lipschitz observation maps typically scales like:[N_{\mathrm{samp}}(\epsilon);\gtrsim; \mathcal N(\epsilon;\mathcal T_k,d_k),]where (\mathcal N) is the covering number. Without structure, (\log \mathcal N) grows proportionally to (d_{\mathrm{eff}}\log(1/\epsilon)), yielding exponential growth in naive dimension.
Structural mitigation. If feasible sets have low intrinsic dimension (sparse, low-rank, or manifold structure), then (d_{\mathrm{eff}}) replaces ambient dimension and yields tractable scaling. The framework’s responsibility is to (i) define such structure as constraints and invariants, and (ii) certify that transforms preserve it.
20.3.1.2 Memory complexity growth
A truncation (\mathbb K_{k,N}) carries representation size (S_{k}(N)). In tensorized models, naive dense size scales as (O(N^{\alpha(k)})) for some exponent (\alpha(k)) depending on coordinate factorization. This is unacceptable at large (k) unless:
sparsity reduces stored coefficients to (O(s(N))\ll N^{\alpha(k)}),
hierarchical compression yields (O(N \log N)) or similar near-linear scaling,
streaming reduces peak memory via chunking.
Memory must be reported with deterministic accounting, including overhead for indices and certificates.
20.3.1.3 Time complexity growth
Let (T_{k,N}) be the runtime of applying an admissible transform in the discrete representation. Naively:
dense kernels scale as (O(S_k(N))),
spectral transforms may scale as (O(S_k(N)\log N)),
iterative solvers scale as (O(\mathrm{Iter}\cdot \mathrm{CostPerIter})).
Higher (k) amplifies costs via:
increased operator bandwidth,
worse conditioning requiring more iterations,
more expensive invariant monitoring.
Time budgets become part of admissibility when real-time or bounded-time operation is required.
20.3.1.4 Mitigation via structure
Mitigation requires explicit structure axioms that are preserved by admissible transforms:
Sparsity structure: constraint enforces compressibility; transforms preserve sparsity class up to bounded inflation.
Low-rank structure: states lie near low-rank manifolds; transforms preserve rank or nuclear norm within budgets.
Symmetry/gauge structure: quotient reduces effective dimension; canonicalization prevents redundant exploration.
Multiscale structure: wavelet/hierarchical representations reduce cost by locality.
Each mitigation must include: (i) formal definition, (ii) preservation theorem template, (iii) computable diagnostic, (iv) failure mode taxonomy.
20.3.2 Expressivity bounds
20.3.2.1 Lower bounds on basis size
For a target accuracy (\epsilon) in approximating states in (\mathcal T_k(\delta)), define the Kolmogorov (n)-width:[d_n(\mathcal T_k) := \inf_{\dim V=n}\sup_{H\in\mathcal T_k}\inf_{v\in V}|H-v|_k.]If (d_n(\mathcal T_k)>\epsilon), then no (n)-dimensional truncation can achieve error (\epsilon). This yields certified lower bounds on required basis size (n(\epsilon)).
20.3.2.2 Lower bounds on program length
Let (\mathcal L(P)) be program length (gate count) and let (\mathcal D) be a metric on (D^{k+1}) (e.g., induced by operator norm or cost metric). If each gate step changes the transform by at most (\eta) in (\mathcal D), then any program implementing a transform (T) must satisfy:[\mathcal L(P) ;\ge; \frac{\mathcal D(I,T)}{\eta}.]When (\mathcal D) is geodesic distance under a cost-induced metric, this lower bound becomes a path-length bound.
20.3.2.3 Approximation bounds
For finite gate libraries, approximation error bounds take the form:[\inf_{P:\mathcal L(P)\le L}\sup_{H\in\mathcal T_k}|T(H)-T_P(H)|_k ;\le; \mathcal E_k(L),]where (\mathcal E_k(L)) decreases with (L) depending on generator richness and commutator structure. Conversely, adversarial lower bounds show that some transforms require exponentially many gates unless additional structure is exploited.
20.3.2.4 Certified limits
A certificate of expressivity failure consists of:
a target transform (T) or target mapping goal,
a proven lower bound on required resources (basis size, program length, runtime),
a verification that available resources are below the bound,
a deterministic report encoding assumptions and proof objects (human-readable + machine-checkable).
Certified limits integrate into corridor governance: when infeasibility is certified, systems must reject the requested transformation and optionally propose nearest feasible alternatives under a specified metric.
20.3.3 Stability bounds
20.3.3.1 Conditioning growth
Define transform conditioning at level (k):[\kappa(T) := |DT|,|DT^{-1}|]when differentiable and invertible on the feasible tube, or replace by Lipschitz constants for (T) and a right inverse. In higher dimensions, (\kappa(T)) tends to grow under composition and under coupling. Stability analysis must bound:
(\kappa) growth with (k),
(\kappa) growth with truncation refinement (N),
(\kappa) growth with program length (L).
20.3.3.2 Error amplification growth
Let (\widetilde H = H + e). Then:[|T(\widetilde H)-T(H)|_k \le L_T |e|_k]for Lipschitz (T). Under repeated application,[|T^n(\widetilde H)-T^n(H)|_k \le (L_T)^n |e|_k,]so stability requires (L_T\le 1) (contractive regime) or additional Lyapunov structure controlling growth. Higher-dimensional extensions must include regimes where contraction is preserved, and a taxonomy of noncontractive but bounded dynamics (e.g., unitary-like transforms).
20.3.3.3 Corridor scaling laws
Corridors must scale across (k) and across discretization (N). A corridor scaling law specifies:[\delta_k = \mathcal S_k(\delta_{k-1}),\qquad\delta_{k,N} = \mathcal S_{k,N}(\delta_k),]where (\mathcal S) accounts for:
increased estimator variance at higher dimension,
increased conditioning and numerical drift,
accumulation across program length.
Scaling laws must be monotone and conservative: if a transform is admissible at level (k) under (\delta_k), then its lifted representation remains admissible at level (\ell) under (\delta_\ell) after applying scaling.
20.3.3.4 Certified thresholds
A certified stability threshold is a bound of the form:[\text{If } \delta \le \delta^\star(k,N,L)\text{ and }\kappa(T)\le \kappa^\star(k,N,L),\text{ then execution is stable and admissible.}]These thresholds must be produced by:
analytic proofs where possible (operator bounds, energy estimates),
verified numerics where required (interval arithmetic, validated residuals),
deterministic test harness outputs otherwise.
20.3.4 No-go results
20.3.4.1 Nonexistence under constraints
Nonexistence occurs when the constraint set (H^k=\ker(C_k)) is empty or incompatible with required boundary/initial conditions, invariants, or coupling conditions. A formal nonexistence proof may proceed via:
contradiction (constraints imply incompatible invariants),
functional-analytic obstruction (no solution to PDE constraints),
topological obstruction (required mapping class absent),
spectral obstruction (operator spectrum disallows target behavior).
20.3.4.2 Infeasibility under budgets
Even when existence holds, corridors may forbid feasible execution. Infeasibility arises when any admissible transform achieving a goal must violate:
invariant budgets,
reconstruction budgets,
stability thresholds,
resource constraints (time/memory).
A budget infeasibility certificate includes a lower bound on required drift/resources exceeding the corridor allowance.
20.3.4.3 Undecidability regimes (if any)
Certain equivalence and verification questions for program graphs may be undecidable in full generality (e.g., exact equivalence of arbitrary operator programs or termination of general rewrite systems). The framework therefore requires:
restricted program languages where critical checks are decidable, or
conservative semi-decision procedures that return “unknown” with certified uncertainty labels.
Any invocation of undecidability must be accompanied by a precise statement of the language class and the decision problem, and must integrate into corridor governance as a certified uncertainty condition rather than an untracked failure.
20.3.4.4 Certified impossibility
A certified impossibility output is a structured object containing:
target statement (S) (goal, property, or transform existence),
assumptions (A) (constraints, corridor budgets, representation limits),
proof object (P) or validated evidence (E) establishing (\neg S) under (A),
reproducibility metadata (hashes, versions, deterministic seeds),
recommended relaxations (minimal changes to (A) that could make (S) feasible).
20.4 Fractal — Engineering Higher-Dimensional Systems
20.4.1 Data structures
20.4.1.1 Hierarchical generalizations
For higher (k), hierarchical structures replace dense tensors:
hyperoctrees / wavelet trees for multiscale spatial–parameter partitioning,
multigrid ladders for solver acceleration,
nested block decompositions aligned with constraint locality.
A hierarchical node stores:
coefficient block (dense or sparse),
local invariant summaries (for monitoring),
local constraint residual estimates,
deterministic child indexing.
Operations must be defined as tree traversals with bounded error accumulation and deterministic ordering.
20.4.1.2 Sparse generalizations
Sparse representations encode only significant coefficients. Required components:
sparse coordinate format with deterministic ordering,
block-sparse for structured sparsity,
multiresolution sparsity (coarse-to-fine significance).
Sparsity must be paired with certified truncation envelopes:
guarantee that dropped mass/energy is below a known bound,
propagate this bound through transforms and invariants.
20.4.1.3 Streaming generalizations
Streaming enables operation when full state does not fit memory:
chunking along coordinate axes or along hierarchical partitions,
online reconstruction and invariant estimation,
checkpointing policies with hash-chained logs.
Streaming execution must include boundary artifact control:
deterministic overlap regions,
consistent stitching operators,
certified chunk-boundary error bounds.
20.4.1.4 Deterministic indexing
Deterministic indexing is required across platforms:
canonical multi-index ordering (lexicographic on ((k,N))-dependent indices),
stable hashing of index sets,
deterministic serialization of sparse/hierarchical structures.
Indexing must be compatible with canonicalization and portability: identical abstract states must yield identical serialized forms after canonicalization, regardless of internal storage permutations.
20.4.2 Algorithm libraries
20.4.2.1 Forward libraries
A forward library provides kernels to compute:[H' = T(H)]for (T\in D^{k+1}), across dense/sparse/hierarchical/streaming backends. Required API properties:
typed interfaces: (T:\mathbb K_{k,N}\to \mathbb K_{k,N}),
explicit precision and rounding policies,
invariant monitoring hooks,
constraint residual reporting.
20.4.2.2 Inverse libraries
Inverse libraries provide solvers for:
reconstructing (H) from partial observations,
approximating (T^{-1}) or right-inverses on feasible tubes,
coupling inverses ((\Psi_{\ell\to k}) approximations) with repair projections.
Each inverse method must output:
solution estimate,
residual and conditioning diagnostics,
certificate or “unknown” label if conditions are insufficient.
20.4.2.3 Optimization libraries
Optimization libraries solve:[\min_T \mathcal C_k(T)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad \mathrm{Adm}_k(T;\delta)=\mathrm{true},;; \text{goal constraints}.]Supported families include:
gradient-based optimization with adjoint kernels,
proximal methods for sparsity or constraints,
augmented Lagrangian for hard constraints,
program search with admissibility-aware heuristics.
All optimizers must be instrumented with:
determinism controls,
monotone descent checks (when required),
corridor enforcement (projection/repair),
convergence certificates.
20.4.2.4 Learning libraries
Learning libraries fit:
generators (E^{(k)}_a) from data,
corridor models (predicting safe budgets),
canonicalization rules (selectors trained but executed deterministically),
gate programs (policy search).
Learning must be embedded in verification: trained components are not admissible unless they pass corridor tests and produce reproducible artifacts.
20.4.3 Verification scaling
20.4.3.1 Certificate scaling
Certificates must scale with dimension without becoming intractably large. Requirements:
hierarchical certificates (local proofs aggregated to global),
hash-chained logs for streaming,
summarized invariants with drill-down pointers,
machine-checkable schemas with versioning.
A certificate must encode:
constraint residual statistics across partitions,
invariant drift across scales,
stability/conditioning witnesses,
reproducibility manifests.
20.4.3.2 Test harness scaling
Test harnesses must include:
unit tests for each kernel,
property-based tests for invariance and equivariance,
randomized stress tests across (k,N,L),
regression tests with gold states and canonical IDs.
Scaling strategies:
test at multiple truncations (N) to validate convergence,
test across multiple backends (dense/sparse/hierarchical) for portability,
test cross-dimensional couplings for commutativity defects.
20.4.3.3 Benchmark scaling
Benchmark suites must report:
runtime scaling curves in ((k,N)),
memory scaling curves by backend,
accuracy scaling and truncation envelopes,
robustness scaling under noise and perturbations.
Benchmarks are required to be reproducible: pinned environments, deterministic seeds, stable serialization, and artifact hashing.
20.4.3.4 Audit scaling
Auditing policies must remain feasible at scale:
automated alert thresholds for corridor violations,
tiered logging (summary vs full),
sampling-based audits with certified confidence intervals,
public reporting formats that preserve determinism and verifiability.
20.4.4 Reference deployments
20.4.4.1 Minimal demos
Minimal deployments demonstrate:
constructing (\mathbb K_k) for a small (k) and small (N),
implementing a tiny admissible gate set,
verifying constraint preservation and invariant drift under one transform and one coupling,
producing reproducible IDs and certificates.
20.4.4.2 Research demos
Research deployments demonstrate:
multi-stage generalized engines (Q^{(k)}),
program search and rewrite with certificates,
cross-dimensional coupling experiments,
curvature/holonomy estimation on noncommuting families.
These demos must include full audit artifacts and regression baselines.
20.4.4.3 Stress demos
Stress deployments demonstrate:
high (k) or large (N) behavior,
worst-case conditioning and stability regimes,
adversarial perturbation robustness and failure reporting,
corridor tightening/loosening policies under controlled degradation.
Stress demos must output certified “unknown” or “impossible” labels rather than silent failure.
20.4.4.4 Reproducible bundles
A reproducible bundle includes:
source code with pinned dependencies,
deterministic configs (seeds, precision, serialization rules),
reference datasets and gold states,
certificates and proof objects,
benchmark reports,
hash manifest for every artifact.
The bundle must support end-to-end verification that recomputes canonical IDs and validates that all corridor and admissibility conditions are met under the published settings.
Chapter 20 — Higher-Dimensional Extensions: ((D^n)), Category Lifts, Functorial Structure
21.1 Square — Boundary Statements of the Theory
Let (\mathcal{X}) be a real separable Hilbert space (the state space) with norm (|\cdot|). Let (\mathcal{Y}) be an observation space. The kernel is defined by the tuple[\mathbf{K} ;\equiv; (\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y},\mathcal{A},\mathcal{C},\Omega,\Sigma;;\Pi,;\mathcal{J}),]where:
(\mathcal{A}) is a (possibly noncommutative) operator algebra acting on (\mathcal{X}) (generators, gates, cycles).
(\mathcal{C}:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{Z}) is a constraint operator with feasible set[\mathcal{F};\equiv;{x\in\mathcal{X}: \mathcal{C}(x)\le 0}](interpreting (\le 0) componentwise in (\mathcal{Z}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^p) or via an order cone).
(\Pi:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{F}) is a projection / repair map, assumed to satisfy (\Pi(x)=x) for (x\in\mathcal{F}) and to be nonexpansive on its domain when invoked by the kernel (formalized below).
(\Omega) is a guard (runtime safety/validity gate) with acceptance predicate (\Omega(x,\theta)\in{\mathrm{PASS},\mathrm{FAIL}}) and an associated cost/barrier (\omega(x,\theta)\in[0,\infty]) such that (\Omega=\mathrm{PASS}\Rightarrow \omega<\infty).
(\Sigma) is an attestation operator producing machine-checkable certificates for claims the kernel is permitted to make.
(\mathcal{J}(x;y,\theta)) is an objective (data-fit + regularization + barrier) minimized over (x\in\mathcal{F}) given observations (y\in\mathcal{Y}) and hyperparameters (\theta).
A forward map (\mathcal{M}:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{Y}) (implicitly part of (\mathcal{J})) defines the observation model.
A canonical kernel instance returns[\widehat{x} ;=; \mathbf{K}(y;\theta)\quad\in\quad \mathcal{F}]together with a certificate (\sigma=\Sigma(y,\widehat{x};\theta)) whenever (\Omega(\widehat{x},\theta)=\mathrm{PASS}).
The square principle is the boundary theorem: every correctness statement is conditional on explicit hypotheses stated below; every excluded claim is excluded for explicit structural reasons.
21.1.1 What the kernel proves
21.1.1.1 Proven reconstruction conditions
Problem class (constrained inverse problem). Given (y\in\mathcal{Y}), solve[\min_{x\in\mathcal{F}} ;\mathcal{J}(x;y,\theta)\quad\text{where}\quad\mathcal{J}(x;y,\theta);=;\ell(\mathcal{M}(x),y);+;r(x;\theta);+;\omega(x;\theta).]Assume:
(A1) Data term regularity. (\ell(\cdot,y)) is proper, lower semicontinuous, and convex in its first argument; if differentiable, (\nabla_x \ell(\mathcal{M}(x),y)) is (L)-Lipschitz on the relevant set.
(A2) Constraint regularity. (\mathcal{F}) is nonempty, closed; the repair (\Pi) is nonexpansive:[|\Pi(u)-\Pi(v)|\le |u-v|,\quad \forall u,v \text{ in the kernel’s domain of repair.}]
(A3) Identifiability on (\mathcal{F}). There exists a subset (\mathcal{F}\star\subseteq\mathcal{F}) (the target class) such that (\mathcal{M}) is injective on (\mathcal{F}\star), or satisfies a restricted strong convexity property:[\langle \nabla \ell(\mathcal{M}(x),y)-\nabla \ell(\mathcal{M}(x'),y),; x-x'\rangle;\ge; \mu|x-x'|^2,\quad \forall x,x'\in\mathcal{F}_\star]for some (\mu>0) (or an equivalent restricted invertibility statement).
(A4) Noise model. Observations satisfy (y=\mathcal{M}(x_\star)+\eta) with (\eta) bounded or subgaussian in an appropriate norm.
Theorem 21.1 (Stable reconstruction on constrained class).Under (A1)–(A4), if (\widehat{x}) is a global minimizer of (\mathcal{J}(\cdot;y,\theta)) over (\mathcal{F}) and (x_\star\in\mathcal{F}\star) is the data-generating state, then there exist constants (c_1,c_2\ge 0) (depending only on (\mu,L) and regularizer parameters) such that[|\widehat{x}-x\star|;\le; c_1,|\eta|{\mathcal{Y}} ;+; c_2,\mathrm{bias}(\theta;\mathcal{F}\star),]where (\mathrm{bias}(\theta;\mathcal{F}_\star)) quantifies model mismatch induced by (r(\cdot;\theta)) and constraint relaxation in (\omega).
Corollary 21.2 (Exact recovery in the noiseless realizable case).If (\eta=0) and (\mathrm{bias}(\theta;\mathcal{F}\star)=0), then (\widehat{x}=x\star).
Admissible “holographic” regime. In boundary-measurement settings, let (\mathcal{M}) be a trace / boundary restriction map and (\mathcal{C}) encode interior PDE/variational structure. Reconstruction is provable when (\mathcal{C}) induces a uniqueness class and (\mathcal{M}) is injective over that class; the kernel proves reconstruction only to the extent that the chosen ((\mathcal{C},\mathcal{M})) pair implies such uniqueness.
21.1.1.2 Proven admissibility conditions
A transform (T:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{X}) is kernel-admissible if it satisfies all of:
(B1) Feasibility preservation (strong).[x\in\mathcal{F};\Rightarrow;T(x)\in\mathcal{F}.](Or feasibility preservation up to repair: (T(x)\in \mathrm{dom}(\Pi)) and (\Pi(T(x))\in\mathcal{F}).)
(B2) Guard compatibility.[\Omega(x,\theta)=\mathrm{PASS};\Rightarrow;\Omega(T(x),\theta)=\mathrm{PASS}\quad\text{or}\quad\omega(T(x),\theta)\le \omega(x,\theta)+\kappa_T]for a bounded slack (\kappa_T).
(B3) Controlled distortion. (T) is Lipschitz on (\mathcal{F}) with constant (L_T<\infty):[|T(x)-T(x')|\le L_T|x-x'|,\quad x,x'\in\mathcal{F}.]
(B4) Certificate liftability. There exists a certificate transformer (\Sigma_T) such that whenever (\sigma) certifies property (P(x)), then (\Sigma_T(\sigma)) certifies (P_T(T(x))) as required by the kernel contract.
Proposition 21.3 (Closure under composition).If (T_1,T_2) are kernel-admissible with certificate transformers (\Sigma_{T_1},\Sigma_{T_2}), then (T_2\circ T_1) is kernel-admissible with transformer (\Sigma_{T_2}\circ\Sigma_{T_1}), and Lipschitz constant (L_{T_2\circ T_1}\le L_{T_2}L_{T_1}).
Definition 21.4 (Admissible gate basis).A generating set (\mathcal{G}\subset\mathcal{A}) is an admissible gate basis if every finite word (T=g_k\circ\cdots\circ g_1) with (g_i\in\mathcal{G}) is kernel-admissible (possibly after an explicit repair (\Pi) at specified checkpoints) and admits certificate liftability.
21.1.1.3 Proven stability conditions
Stability is stated at three levels: solution stability, iteration stability, and certification stability.
(i) Solution stability. Theorem 21.1 gives Lipschitz-type dependence of (\widehat{x}) on observation perturbations when restricted convexity holds.
(ii) Algorithmic stability under perturbations. Let the kernel’s iterative update be[x^{t+1};=;\Pi!\left(\mathcal{T}\theta(x^t;y)\right),]where (\mathcal{T}\theta) is a (possibly stochastic) update map (e.g., gradient/prox step, primal-dual, alternating minimization), and (\Pi) enforces feasibility.
Assume:
(S1) Contractive update on feasible set. There exists (\rho\in[0,1)) such that for all feasible (u,v\in\mathcal{F}),[|\Pi(\mathcal{T}\theta(u;y))-\Pi(\mathcal{T}\theta(v;y))|;\le;\rho|u-v|.]
(S2) Bounded stochasticity. If (\mathcal{T}_\theta) is stochastic, its noise is a martingale difference with bounded conditional second moment.
Theorem 21.5 (Iteration stability).Under (S1)–(S2), the iteration is stable in the sense that for any two initializations (x^0,\tilde{x}^0\in\mathcal{F}),[\mathbb{E}|x^t-\tilde{x}^t|;\le;\rho^t|x^0-\tilde{x}^0| ;+; \frac{1}{1-\rho},\sup_{s<t}\mathbb{E}|\delta^s|,]where (\delta^s) is the per-step perturbation induced by observation noise or stochastic gradients.
(iii) Certification stability. A certificate family (\Sigma) is stable if its verification predicate (\mathrm{Verify}(\sigma)) is continuous with respect to the computed witness (e.g., residuals, dual gaps) under bounded numerical error. Formally, if (\sigma) encodes inequalities of the form (a\le b), then floating-point verification must include explicit tolerances derived from deterministic error bounds on computed quantities.
21.1.1.4 Proven convergence conditions
The kernel’s convergence guarantee is stated for the following broad algorithmic class:
Algorithm schema (Guarded Projected Descent / Proximal Kernel).[\begin{aligned}&x^0\in\mathcal{F},\&u^{t+1} \leftarrow \mathrm{Step}(x^t;y,\theta)\quad\text{(gradient/prox/primal-dual step)},\&x^{t+1} \leftarrow \Pi(u^{t+1}),\&\text{if }\Omega(x^{t+1},\theta)=\mathrm{FAIL}\text{ then }x^{t+1}\leftarrow \mathrm{Rollback}(x^t,u^{t+1};\theta),\&\text{output }\widehat{x}=x^T,;\sigma=\Sigma(y,\widehat{x};\theta).\end{aligned}]
Assume (\mathcal{J}) is (\mu)-strongly convex over (\mathcal{F}\star) or satisfies a Polyak–Łojasiewicz (PL) condition on (\mathcal{F}\star):[\frac{1}{2}|\nabla \mathcal{J}(x;y,\theta)|^2 ;\ge; \mu(\mathcal{J}(x;y,\theta)-\mathcal{J}^\star),\quad x\in\mathcal{F}_\star.]
Theorem 21.6 (Linear convergence under PL + nonexpansive feasibility).If the step map implements gradient descent with step size (\alpha\in(0,2/L)) on a differentiable (\mathcal{J}) satisfying the PL condition over (\mathcal{F}_\star), and (\Pi) is nonexpansive and preserves the PL region, then[\mathcal{J}(x^t;y,\theta)-\mathcal{J}^\star ;\le; (1-\alpha\mu)^t\big(\mathcal{J}(x^0;y,\theta)-\mathcal{J}^\star\big).]
Theorem 21.7 (Sublinear convergence in convex nonsmooth case).If (\ell) is convex with (L)-Lipschitz gradient, (r) is convex proper (possibly nonsmooth), (\omega) is a convex indicator/barrier of (\mathcal{F}), and (\Pi) corresponds to the proximal operator of (\omega), then proximal gradient yields[\mathcal{J}(x^t;y,\theta)-\mathcal{J}^\star ;=; O(1/t).]
Guard soundness. If (\Omega) enforces conditions sufficient for the PL region (or sufficient for contraction), then the convergence rate above holds on the maximal contiguous interval of iterations where (\Omega=\mathrm{PASS}); rollback must be defined so that the sequence remains in (\mathcal{F}) and does not increase (\mathcal{J}) beyond a bounded envelope.
21.1.2 What the kernel cannot claim
21.1.2.1 Not every transform is admissible
No-go 21.8 (Constraint violation by generic transforms).Let (\mathcal{F}\subsetneq\mathcal{X}) be a proper closed set with nonempty interior boundary. For a generic continuous (T:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{X}), feasibility preservation fails:[\exists x\in\mathcal{F};\text{ such that };T(x)\notin\mathcal{F}.]In particular, if (\mathcal{F}) encodes invariants (mass, positivity, causality, monotonicity, safety constraints), then any transform not explicitly constructed to preserve those invariants is inadmissible. Admissibility is therefore a design property, not a generic property.
No-go 21.9 (Certificate non-functoriality).There exist admissible transforms (T) (in the feasibility sense) for which no computable (\Sigma_T) can transform certificates of (P(x)) into certificates of (P_T(T(x))) without re-verification. This occurs whenever (P_T) depends on information erased or conflated by (T), or when the proof system requires witnesses not preserved under (T).
21.1.2.2 Not every target is reachable
Define a gate set (\mathcal{G}\subset\mathcal{A}) and the reachable set from (x\in\mathcal{F}):[\mathcal{R}(x;\mathcal{G}) ;\equiv; {\Pi(g_k\circ\cdots\circ g_1(x)):;k\in\mathbb{N},;g_i\in\mathcal{G}}.]
No-go 21.10 (Invariant obstruction).If there exists a nonconstant invariant (I:\mathcal{F}\to\mathbb{R}) such that (I(\Pi(g(x)))=I(x)) for all (g\in\mathcal{G}) and (x\in\mathcal{F}), then (\mathcal{R}(x;\mathcal{G})\subseteq I^{-1}(I(x))), hence (\mathcal{R}(x;\mathcal{G})\neq \mathcal{F}) unless (I) is constant on (\mathcal{F}).
No-go 21.11 (Rank obstruction in smooth control).If (\mathcal{X}) is a smooth manifold and gates correspond to flows of vector fields ({V_i}), then reachability of a neighborhood requires the Lie algebra rank condition. Failure implies the orbit is a lower-dimensional immersed submanifold, hence most targets are unreachable.
Computational obstruction. Even when reachable in principle, finding a word in (\mathcal{G}) of length (\le k) mapping (x) near (x_\mathrm{tar}) can be NP-hard or worse, depending on (\mathcal{G}) and the metric on (\mathcal{F}). The kernel cannot claim polynomial-time reachability without a gate-system-specific proof.
21.1.2.3 Not every state is identifiable
Let (x,x'\in\mathcal{F}). Define observational equivalence:[x \sim_{\mathcal{M}} x' ;\Longleftrightarrow; \mathcal{M}(x)=\mathcal{M}(x').]
No-go 21.12 (Nonidentifiability from symmetries).If there exists a nontrivial group (G) acting on (\mathcal{F}) such that (\mathcal{M}(g\cdot x)=\mathcal{M}(x)) for all (g\in G), then at best the kernel can identify the orbit ([x]={g\cdot x:g\in G}). Point identification is impossible without symmetry breaking constraints or additional measurements.
No-go 21.13 (Dimension counting obstruction for generic linear sensing).If (\mathcal{F}\star) contains a (d)-dimensional embedded submanifold and (\mathcal{M}) is a generic smooth map into (\mathbb{R}^m) with (m<2d), then global injectivity on (\mathcal{F}\star) fails generically. Any claim of universal identifiability must therefore specify (m) relative to the effective intrinsic dimension of the constrained class.
21.1.2.4 Not every embedding is falsifiable
An embedding (\varphi:\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}^k) (or a representation map) is falsifiable relative to a test family (\mathcal{T}) if for any two hypotheses (H_0,H_1) expressed as disjoint properties on (\mathcal{X}), there exists (t\in\mathcal{T}) whose evaluation on (\varphi(x)) separates (H_0) from (H_1) with bounded error.
No-go 21.14 (Falsifiability obstruction under representation collapse).If (\varphi) is non-injective on a hypothesis boundary (i.e., there exist (x\in H_0, x'\in H_1) with (\varphi(x)=\varphi(x'))), then no downstream test operating only on (\varphi(x)) can falsify (H_0) vs (H_1) in general. Thus falsifiability requires either injectivity on the relevant hypothesis class or access to supplementary witnesses beyond (\varphi).
No-go 21.15 (Verifier incompleteness under adversarial distributions).If adversaries can choose inputs from a distribution family (\mathcal{D}) broad enough to realize indistinguishable representations while violating a target property, then any fixed finite test suite is incomplete. Completeness requires formal certificates or quantified assumptions narrowing (\mathcal{D}).
21.1.3 Canonical open conjectures
21.1.3.1 Minimal constraint families for holography
Let (\mathcal{F}(\Gamma)) be a feasible set indexed by a constraint family (\Gamma={\gamma_i}{i\in I}), and let boundary measurements be (y=\mathcal{M}\partial(x)).
Conjecture 21.16 (Holographic minimality).There exists a minimal family (\Gamma^\star) such that:
(\mathcal{M}_\partial) is injective on (\mathcal{F}(\Gamma^\star)) up to a known gauge group (G);
for any strict subfamily (\Gamma'\subsetneq \Gamma^\star), injectivity fails on (\mathcal{F}(\Gamma'));
(\Gamma^\star) is closed under certificate generation: each (\gamma\in\Gamma^\star) admits a (\Sigma)-witness verifiable in polynomial time in the representation size.
21.1.3.2 Minimal invariant suites for safety
Let a safety corridor be defined by invariants (\mathcal{I}={I_j}_{j=1}^q) with bounds (I_j(x)\in[a_j,b_j]).
Conjecture 21.17 (Invariant basis for safety corridors).There exists a finite basis (\mathcal{I}^\star) such that:
If (\mathcal{I}^\star) holds along a trajectory produced by admissible gates, then all safety properties in a target specification class (\mathfrak{S}) hold.
Any strict subset of (\mathcal{I}^\star) is insufficient: there exists a trajectory satisfying the subset while violating some (S\in\mathfrak{S}).
21.1.3.3 Minimal gate bases for expressivity
Let (\mathcal{G}\subset\mathcal{A}) be a gate set and (\overline{\langle \mathcal{G}\rangle}) the closure of its generated semigroup (in an operator topology appropriate to the application).
Conjecture 21.18 (Admissible universality threshold).There exists a smallest cardinality (k^\star) such that an admissible gate basis (\mathcal{G}) with (|\mathcal{G}|=k^\star) achieves (\overline{\langle \mathcal{G}\rangle}) dense in the admissible transformation monoid (\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{adm}}) on (\mathcal{F}), while any admissible (\mathcal{G}) with (|\mathcal{G}|<k^\star) fails to be dense.
21.1.3.4 Minimal cost forms for optimality
Let (\mathcal{J}) be selected from a design family ({\mathcal{J}_\lambda}).
Conjecture 21.19 (Cost-form minimality under certificate constraints).Among costs admitting polynomial-time certificates of near-optimality (e.g., duality gaps, residual bounds), there exists a minimal structural form (\mathcal{J}^\star) achieving the Pareto frontier of (i) reconstruction accuracy, (ii) stability margins, (iii) certificate tightness, and (iv) computational complexity. Any strictly simpler form loses at least one frontier point.
21.1.4 Final glossary of “ultimate objects”
21.1.4.1 Fixed points
A fixed point of the kernel iteration is (x^\infty\in\mathcal{F}) such that[x^\infty=\Pi(\mathcal{T}_\theta(x^\infty;y)).]A fixed point is certified if (\Sigma) produces a proof of (approximate) stationarity:[\mathrm{Verify}\big(\Sigma(y,x^\infty;\theta)\big)=\mathrm{TRUE}\quad\Rightarrow\quad|\mathcal{G}(x^\infty)|\le \varepsilon,]where (\mathcal{G}) is the relevant generalized gradient mapping.
21.1.4.2 Attractors
An attractor (\mathcal{A}\subseteq\mathcal{F}) for the kernel dynamics is a compact invariant set such that for a basin (\mathcal{B}\subseteq\mathcal{F}),[\mathrm{dist}(x^t,\mathcal{A})\to 0\quad \forall x^0\in\mathcal{B}.]Attractors classify asymptotic behavior under fixed ((y,\theta)); they are good if they consist of minimizers or certified near-minimizers, and bad if they include spurious fixed points, limit cycles, or chaotic sets.
21.1.4.3 Absolute bounds
An absolute bound is a statement of the form[\inf_{\widehat{x}=\mathcal{E}(y)}\sup_{x_\star\in\mathcal{F}\star}\mathbb{E}|\widehat{x}-x\star|;\ge; \mathcal{B}(\text{noise},\text{dimension},\text{constraints}),]where (\mathcal{E}) ranges over all estimators computable under a specified resource model. Absolute bounds separate what is information-theoretically impossible from what is merely algorithmically difficult.
21.1.4.4 Certified impossibilities
A certified impossibility is a pair ((\mathcal{H},\pi)) where (\mathcal{H}) is an impossibility hypothesis (e.g., “injectivity cannot hold for this (\mathcal{M}) and (\mathcal{F}_\star)”), and (\pi) is a verifiable witness (dimension contradiction, symmetry witness, adversarial pair (x\neq x') with (\mathcal{M}(x)=\mathcal{M}(x')), or a reduction). Certified impossibilities are first-class outputs of (\Sigma) when (\Omega) detects a “do not proceed” condition.
21.2 Flower — Research Program and Expansion Paths
21.2.1 Expanding the constraint operator ((\mathcal{C}))
21.2.1.1 Additional structural constraints
Structural constraints define an intrinsic model class (\mathcal{F}) by encoding geometry, sparsity, compositionality, or invariance. Typical expansions include:
Manifold constraints: (x\in\mathcal{M}\subset\mathcal{X}) (embedded manifold) with a computable retraction (\Pi).
Convex structural constraints: cone constraints, monotonicity, total variation, nuclear norm surrogates with proximal maps.
Equivariance constraints: (x) constrained to satisfy (x=g\cdot x) for specified (g\in G) (symmetry reduction).
Operator-consistency constraints: (\mathcal{C}(x)=0) encodes commutation relations or conservation laws induced by (\mathcal{A}).
Each new constraint must be accompanied by: (i) a repair map (\Pi) with quantified distortion; (ii) a certificate schema (\Sigma) validating satisfaction within numerical tolerance.
21.2.1.2 Additional physical constraints
Physical constraints encode causality, locality, conservation, and bounded propagation. Formal forms include:
Causality cones: forbidden influence relations represented as constraints on kernels/graphs.
Energy/mass conservation: invariants (I(x)) preserved under admissible transforms.
Stability margins: constraints ensuring spectral radius bounds or Lyapunov inequalities.
A physical constraint is kernel-integrable only if the resulting feasible set remains closed under admissible gate application or if repair does not destroy identifiability.
21.2.1.3 Learned constraints
Let (\mathcal{C}_\phi) be a parameterized constraint (e.g., neural implicit constraint). Learned constraints are admissible only under a verification wrapper:
(\Omega) must enforce conservative acceptance regions (outer approximations).
(\Sigma) must certify either: (i) constraint satisfaction under Lipschitz bounds for (\mathcal{C}_\phi); or (ii) robust satisfaction via interval propagation / abstract interpretation.
21.2.1.4 Verified constraints
Verified constraints are those for which (\Sigma) produces a machine-checkable proof object (e.g., formally verified inequalities, certified reachability bounds). A verified constraint layer requires:
exact arithmetic or verified numerics for critical inequalities,
explicit rounding models,
proof-carrying artifacts integrated into the release pipeline (see §21.4.3).
21.2.2 Expanding the operator algebras
21.2.2.1 New generator families
Introduce generators (\mathcal{G}) that enlarge expressivity while preserving admissibility:
Local generators (sparse/structured updates),
Spectral generators (diagonalizable families with controlled spectra),
Symplectic/unitary generators (norm-preserving families),
Stochastic generators with certified concentration bounds.
Each generator family requires: (i) admissibility proof, (ii) certificate liftability, (iii) compositional stability bounds under repeated application.
21.2.2.2 New gate systems
A gate system is a presentation of (\mathcal{A}) with relations. Research targets:
Minimal admissible presentations,
Normal forms enabling canonicalization and equivalence checking,
Efficient compilation from high-level operator programs to gate sequences with bounded error.
21.2.2.3 New cycles and engines
Define engines as higher-level programs:[E ;=; g_{i_k}\circ\cdots\circ g_{i_1}\quad\text{with checkpoints}\quad\Pi,\Omega,\Sigma]inserted at certified boundaries. Engines are admissible if they preserve feasibility and satisfy explicit stability margins (contractivity or Lyapunov decrease) in the corridor of operation.
21.2.2.4 New optimization structures
Research expansions include:
Primal–dual splitting with certified dual feasibility,
Monotone operator theory formulations enabling resolvent-based guarantees,
Nonconvex but tame regimes (PL/Łojasiewicz) with certificateable descent conditions,
Bilevel designs where (\theta) is optimized subject to certificates.
21.2.3 Expanding computational performance
21.2.3.1 Parallelization strategies
Partition (\mathcal{J}) into separable blocks; use ADMM / consensus with certificateable residual bounds.
Parallelize (\Omega) checks (independent invariants) and (\Sigma) proofs (independent lemmas).
Use deterministic scheduling for reproducible concurrency.
21.2.3.2 Hardware acceleration
Provide fused kernels for the dominant linear operators in (\mathcal{M}) and gate primitives in (\mathcal{A}).
Maintain numerically stable accumulation (Kahan/Neumaier) for certificate-critical quantities.
Separate “fast path” (float) from “proof path” (verified numerics) with explicit interfaces.
21.2.3.3 Streaming strategies
Online updates (y_t) with incremental reconstruction:[x^{t+1}\leftarrow \Pi(\mathcal{T}_\theta(x^t;y_t)),]with time-uniform safety corridor enforcement.
Certified drift bounds to decide whether to continue streaming or trigger reinitialization.
21.2.3.4 Compression strategies
Compress operator sequences via normal forms and relation reductions.
Compress state representations with certified error envelopes so that (\Omega) and (\Sigma) remain sound under compression distortion.
21.2.4 Expanding verification rigor
21.2.4.1 Stronger certificates
Dual certificates for optimality,
Counterexample certificates for impossibilities,
Lipschitz envelopes for learned constraints,
Certified bounds for numerical error accumulation.
21.2.4.2 Formal proof checking integration
Export (\Sigma) objects in a proof-assistant-friendly format.
Maintain a verified core: critical lemmas checked by a proof kernel, with the main runtime invoking verified lemmas by hash.
21.2.4.3 Stronger adversarial testing
Construct adversarial pairs ((x,x')) with (\mathcal{M}(x)=\mathcal{M}(x')) to test identifiability boundaries.
Stress-test corridor enforcement by searching for gate sequences that maximize (\omega) while remaining feasible.
21.2.4.4 Public reproducibility standards
Deterministic seeds, pinned dependencies, hermetic builds,
Reproducible hardware reports,
Verifiable data provenance and immutable artifact hashes (§21.4.3).
21.3 Cloud — Boundaries and Risk Maps
21.3.1 Risk taxonomy
21.3.1.1 Numerical risks
Floating-point drift corrupting (\Omega) decisions or (\Sigma) proofs.
Ill-conditioning in (\mathcal{M}) or gate compositions leading to catastrophic amplification.
Non-associativity effects invalidating certificate thresholds without verified error models.
Mitigation requires certified rounding envelopes and audit logs of critical scalars.
21.3.1.2 Model risks
Mis-specified constraint families (\Gamma) causing bias or false feasibility.
Over-regularization collapsing identifiability.
Under-regularization permitting nonidentifiable or unsafe solutions.
Model risk is quantified by sensitivity of (\widehat{x}) and (\Sigma)-verified properties to perturbations in (\theta) and constraint definitions.
21.3.1.3 Data risks
Distribution shift violating (A4) and invalidating bounds.
Corrupted observations creating spurious minima.
Missingness patterns that destroy injectivity on (\mathcal{F}_\star).
Mitigation requires explicit drift detectors and “do not proceed” triggers (§21.3.2.4).
21.3.1.4 Adversarial risks
Adaptive inputs designed to pass (\Omega) while violating unmodeled constraints.
Attacks exploiting certificate gaps (soundness vs completeness).
Gate-sequence attacks producing slow corridor drift.
Mitigation requires conservative (\Omega), adversarial search, and stronger certificates (§21.2.4.3).
21.3.2 Boundaries of controllability
Let the controlled dynamics be[x^{t+1}=\Pi(g_t(x^t)),\quad g_t\in\mathcal{G},\quad x^t\in\mathcal{F}.]
21.3.2.1 Uncontrollable drift regimes
A drift regime exists if there is a direction (v) in which all admissible controls induce a systematic bias:[\mathbb{E}[x^{t+1}-x^t]\cdot v \ge \delta>0]while (\Omega) remains permissive. Drift becomes uncontrollable when no gate sequence can reverse the drift without violating feasibility or triggering (\Omega=\mathrm{FAIL}).
21.3.2.2 Uncontrollable chaos regimes
Let (F(x)=\Pi(\mathcal{T}\theta(x;y))). If (F) is expansive on a set (U\subset\mathcal{F}) with positive Lyapunov exponent, then small perturbations induce exponential divergence, defeating stability and reliable certification. The kernel cannot claim control in regimes where (\sup{x\in U}|DF(x)|>1) persistently and no guard-enforced contraction region exists.
21.3.2.3 Uncontrollable ambiguity regimes
Ambiguity regimes occur when observational equivalence classes have large diameter:[\sup_{x\sim_{\mathcal{M}} x'}|x-x'| \ge \Delta,]so that reconstruction is fundamentally set-valued at scale (\Delta). In such regimes, safety must be defined over equivalence classes rather than points; otherwise certification is ill-posed.
21.3.2.4 Certified “do not proceed” conditions
The kernel must halt or refuse action when (\Sigma) certifies any of:
nonidentifiability witness: (x\neq x') with (\mathcal{M}(x)=\mathcal{M}(x')) in the target class;
corridor violation inevitable under all admissible controls (invariant obstruction);
numerical verification unsound (error envelope exceeds decision margins);
