adversarial susceptibility witness (found counterexample passing (\Omega) but violating a safety property).
These are first-class outputs: ((\mathrm{HALT},\sigma_\mathrm{no\mbox{-}go})).
21.3.3 Safety corridors (formal)
Let corridor constraints be (\mathcal{S}={S_j}_{j=1}^q) with[S_j(x)\le 0,\quad j=1,\dots,q.]Define the safety corridor:[\mathcal{K}\equiv {x\in\mathcal{F}:; S_j(x)\le 0,;\forall j}.]
21.3.3.1 Corridor definition recap
A corridor is forward-invariant under admissible dynamics if[x\in\mathcal{K},; g\in\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{adm}};\Rightarrow; \Pi(g(x))\in\mathcal{K}.]
21.3.3.2 Corridor enforcement recap
Corridor enforcement is realized by:
barrier terms in (\omega) (soft),
projection/repair (\Pi) (hard),
guard (\Omega) (runtime rejection + rollback),
and certificates (\Sigma) verifying corridor membership after each critical transition.
21.3.3.3 Auditability recap
Auditability requires that each transition (x^t\to x^{t+1}) produces:
a hash-linked log entry including gate ID, parameters, residuals,
(\Omega) decision evidence,
(\Sigma) certificate references.
21.3.3.4 Transparency recap
Transparency is formalized as the availability of:
a replayable execution trace,
deterministic regeneration of (\widehat{x}) from ((y,\theta)) and seeds,
and independently verifiable (\Sigma) objects.
21.3.4 Final no-go catalog
21.3.4.1 Identifiability no-go catalog
Symmetry invariance without gauge fixing (No-go 21.12).
Insufficient measurement dimension (No-go 21.13).
Learned constraints collapsing equivalence classes without detection (representation collapse).
21.3.4.2 Reachability no-go catalog
Invariant obstruction (No-go 21.10).
Lie rank failure (No-go 21.11).
Resource-bounded reachability hardness.
21.3.4.3 Stability no-go catalog
Expansive dynamics without guard-enforced contraction (chaos regime).
Ill-conditioning and numerical error overwhelming corridor margins.
Nonconvex landscapes with unavoidable spurious attractors under allowed steps.
21.3.4.4 Verification no-go catalog
Certificates requiring witnesses not preserved under admissible transforms (No-go 21.9).
Finite test suites incomplete under adversarial families (No-go 21.15).
Verified numerics infeasible at required precision under resource constraints (absolute bound on verification throughput).
21.4 Fractal — Deliverables and Publication Package
21.4.1 Reference implementation deliverables
21.4.1.1 Core library
A reference implementation SHALL include:
State module: typed state objects with explicit norm/metric interfaces.
Operator module: gate primitives implementing (\mathcal{A}), with:
compositional semantics,
explicit Lipschitz bounds (or conservative envelopes),
deterministic serialization.
Constraint module: (\mathcal{C}), feasibility predicates, and repair (\Pi).
Kernel module: optimization engines implementing (\mathrm{Step}), rollback logic, and termination criteria.
Guard module: (\Omega) with explicit evidence objects.
Attestation module: (\Sigma) and verifiers.
Minimum API surface (illustrative contract):
apply_gate(gate_id, x, params) -> x'
project(x) -> x_feasible
guard(x, context) -> (pass_fail, evidence)
attest(y, x, context) -> certificate
verify(certificate) -> bool
solve(y, theta, x0) -> (x_hat, certificate, logs)
All API calls SHALL be pure with respect to explicit inputs, except for controlled randomness whose seed is part of the call signature.
21.4.1.2 Verification suite
Unit proofs: verify each (\Sigma) schema on synthetic witnesses.
Soundness checks: inject controlled numerical error; verify decisions remain conservative.
Regression harness: freeze golden certificates for canonical problems.
21.4.1.3 Benchmark suite
Benchmarks SHALL cover:
identifiability regimes (recoverable vs nonrecoverable),
reachability regimes (reachable vs invariant-blocked),
stability regimes (contractive vs expansive),
adversarial regimes (counterexample search).
Each benchmark SHALL include:
data generator with fixed seeds,
oracle or lower bound,
success criteria including certificate verification.
21.4.1.4 Documentation suite
Documentation SHALL be produced from code annotations and include:
formal specification of admissibility,
corridor definitions,
certificate formats,
deterministic reproduction instructions.
21.4.2 Reference dataset deliverables
21.4.2.1 Synthetic sets
Provide synthetic families with tunable intrinsic dimension, noise, and constraint tightness; include ground truth (x_\star), observations (y), and metadata describing the generating parameters.
21.4.2.2 Mask sets
Mask sets define missingness/occlusion operators (\mathcal{P}) applied to (\mathcal{M}(x)). Each mask family includes certified properties (e.g., coverage lower bounds) enabling theoretical interpretability.
21.4.2.3 Noise sets
Noise sets define (\eta) families: bounded, Gaussian, heavy-tailed, structured. Each set includes documented norms (|\eta|_{\mathcal{Y}}) and expected tails to match the assumptions of reconstruction theorems.
21.4.2.4 Gold states
Gold states are canonical ((x_\star,y)) instances used for regression and audit trails. Each gold instance includes a frozen certificate (\sigma_\star) and verification transcript.
21.4.3 Reference certificate deliverables
21.4.3.1 Proof objects
Proof objects SHALL be machine-verifiable and include:
claim identifier,
assumptions (explicit),
witnesses (residuals, dual variables, invariants),
verification procedure reference,
and numerical tolerance model.
21.4.3.2 Logs
Logs SHALL be:
append-only,
hash-chained,
reproducible (deterministic ordering),
and include all (\Omega) decisions and (\Sigma) verification outcomes.
21.4.3.3 Hash manifests
A manifest SHALL enumerate:
dataset hashes,
code commit hash,
build environment identifiers,
benchmark configuration hashes,
certificate schema versions.
21.4.3.4 Release signatures
Releases SHALL be signed. Signature verification MUST be part of the reproducibility checklist and CI gates.
21.4.4 Final publication artifacts
21.4.4.1 Typeset manuscript
The manuscript SHALL include:
theorem statements with explicit hypotheses,
proof outlines sufficient for formalization,
explicit no-go results and certified impossibilities,
and a reproducibility appendix mapping claims to certificates.
21.4.4.2 Code repository snapshot
Publish an immutable snapshot with:
tagged version,
vendor-locked dependencies or lockfiles,
deterministic build scripts,
and verification harnesses.
21.4.4.3 Reproducible experiment bundle
Provide:
container images or hermetic build descriptors,
scripts to regenerate all figures/tables,
cached artifacts with hash checking,
and end-to-end verification output.
21.4.4.4 Versioned releases
Each release SHALL specify:
semantic version,
compatibility notes,
migrated certificate schema versions,
deprecation policy for constraints/gates/guards.
Chapter 21 — Ultimate Limits & Open Problems: Bounds, No-Go Results, Research Agenda
Appendix A — Notation, Symbols, and Index Conventions
A.1 Indices and spaces
A.1.1 Index sets, ordering, and typography
Primitive index sets
For any integer (n\ge 1), define the half-open index set[[n] ;\coloneqq; {0,1,\dots,n-1}.]
Define the cyclic group (integers modulo (n))[\mathbb{Z}_n ;\coloneqq; \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z},]written additively; representatives are taken in ([n]) unless stated otherwise.
When a 1-based human-facing index is required, define (\overline{s}\coloneqq s+1) for (s\in[n]), and explicitly state the convention.
Canonical ordering conventions
A canonical order is required whenever a set must be serialized or iterated deterministically.
For integers and integer tuples, canonical order is lexicographic on representatives:
Scalars: ascending order on ([n]).
Tuples: ((a_1,\dots,a_k) < (b_1,\dots,b_k)) iff (\exists,t) minimal with (a_t\neq b_t) and (a_t<b_t).
For finite sets (S\subseteq\mathbb{Z}^k), the canonical list form is (\mathrm{sort}(S)) under this lexicographic order.
Typography and symbol classes
Scalars: (a,b,c,\dots) (italic).
Vectors: (\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) (bold).
Matrices/linear maps: (A,B,M) (uppercase roman/italic by context).
Sets/spaces: (\mathcal{S},\mathcal{V},\mathbb{Z}_n) (calligraphic / blackboard bold).
Functions/operators: (f,g,\Phi,\tau,\Omega,\Sigma) (italic/Greek; operator symbols are treated as named maps).
Indicator: (\mathbf{1}[\text{predicate}]\in{0,1}).
Disjoint union: (\bigsqcup). Cartesian product: (\times). Quotient: (/\sim).
Equality vs definitional equality:
(=) denotes equality in the underlying set.
(\coloneqq) introduces a definition.
Graph and sequence indexing
A finite directed graph is (G=(V,E)) with (E\subseteq V\times V). For undirected graphs, treat (E\subseteq {{u,v}:u,v\in V}) or enforce symmetry ( (u,v)\in E \Leftrightarrow (v,u)\in E).
A finite path (p) of length (L) is a sequence (p=(v_0,v_1,\dots,v_L)) with ((v_{t-1},v_t)\in E) for (t=1,\dots,L).
For sequences, (x_t) indexes the (t)-th element (0-based unless stated), (x_{0:L}) denotes the prefix ((x_0,\dots,x_{L-1})).
A.1.2 Element–mode coding and state parameterizations
Four-base coding (2-bit basis)
A canonical 4-state basis (\mathbb{B}) is fixed:[\mathbb{B} ;\coloneqq; {00,01,10,11}.]A bijection (\beta:\mathbb{B}\to [4]) is fixed by[\beta(00)=0,\quad \beta(01)=1,\quad \beta(10)=2,\quad \beta(11)=3.]The inverse map is (\beta^{-1}:[4]\to \mathbb{B}). When convenient, identify (\mathbb{B}\cong[4]) via (\beta).
A semantic labeling of (\mathbb{B}) is assumed fixed throughout the manuscript:[00\mapsto \mathrm{Air},\quad 01\mapsto \mathrm{Fire},\quad 10\mapsto \mathrm{Water},\quad 11\mapsto \mathrm{Earth}.]This semantic labeling is not required for the algebra, but is used to attach domain meaning. The algebraic object is the 4-state basis itself.
Three active modes and one grounding mode
Define the active mode set[\mathbb{M} ;\coloneqq; {00,01,10}\subset \mathbb{B}.]Modes are interpreted as a 3-current system:
(01) (initiate / cardinal / ignition),
(10) (hold / fixed / binding),
(00) (shift / mutable / translation).
The excluded mode (11) is reserved as the grounding (collapse/stabilization) operator-mode and is never used as an active mode label unless explicitly stated.
Write (\mu:\mathbb{M}\to [3]) for the canonical indexing[\mu(00)=0,\quad \mu(01)=1,\quad \mu(10)=2,]and (\mu^{-1}) for the inverse.
The 12-state product space
Define the 12-state space as a product:[\mathcal{S}{12} ;\coloneqq; \mathbb{B}\times \mathbb{M},\qquad |\mathcal{S}{12}|=4\cdot 3=12.]An element (x\in\mathcal{S}_{12}) is written[x=(b,m),\quad b\in\mathbb{B},; m\in\mathbb{M}.]
A fixed bijection (\varphi:\mathcal{S}{12}\to \mathbb{Z}{12}) defines the canonical station index (s\in\mathbb{Z}{12}):[s ;\coloneqq; \varphi(b,m).]All algorithms that operate on “stations” must specify whether they act on (s\in\mathbb{Z}{12}) or on ((b,m)\in\mathcal{S}_{12}). If both are used, (\varphi) and (\varphi^{-1}) must be applied explicitly, never implicitly.
Zodiac-order station indexing (canonical within this manuscript)
A canonical presentation order for (\mathbb{Z}{12}) is fixed by the sequence[s=0,1,\dots,11\quad\longleftrightarrow\quad(\mathrm{Aries},\mathrm{Taurus},\mathrm{Gemini},\mathrm{Cancer},\mathrm{Leo},\mathrm{Virgo},\mathrm{Libra},\mathrm{Scorpio},\mathrm{Sagittarius},\mathrm{Capricorn},\mathrm{Aquarius},\mathrm{Pisces}).]Define a label map (\mathrm{Name}{12}:\mathbb{Z}_{12}\to {\text{strings}}) implementing this correspondence.
Independently, define the fixed element–mode decomposition for these 12 stations as a function[\mathrm{EM}:\mathbb{Z}_{12}\to \mathbb{B}\times\mathbb{M},\qquad \mathrm{EM}(s)=(b(s),m(s)).]The explicit table is:
Fire-base: (\mathrm{Aries}=(\mathrm{Fire}:\mathrm{init})), (\mathrm{Leo}=(\mathrm{Fire}:\mathrm{hold})), (\mathrm{Sagittarius}=(\mathrm{Fire}:\mathrm{shift})).
Earth-base: (\mathrm{Capricorn}=(\mathrm{Earth}:\mathrm{init})), (\mathrm{Taurus}=(\mathrm{Earth}:\mathrm{hold})), (\mathrm{Virgo}=(\mathrm{Earth}:\mathrm{shift})).
Water-base: (\mathrm{Cancer}=(\mathrm{Water}:\mathrm{init})), (\mathrm{Scorpio}=(\mathrm{Water}:\mathrm{hold})), (\mathrm{Pisces}=(\mathrm{Water}:\mathrm{shift})).
Air-base: (\mathrm{Libra}=(\mathrm{Air}:\mathrm{init})), (\mathrm{Aquarius}=(\mathrm{Air}:\mathrm{hold})), (\mathrm{Gemini}=(\mathrm{Air}:\mathrm{shift})).
This decomposition is treated as definitional for any statement that references a station’s base or mode.
A.1.3 Angles, sexagesimal backbone, and quantizers
Angle space and normalization
Angles are represented either:
in degrees as real numbers (\theta\in\mathbb{R}), or
in modular form (\theta\in\mathbb{R}/360\mathbb{Z}).
The canonical normalization map is[\mathrm{norm}{360}(\theta);\coloneqq;\theta - 360\left\lfloor \frac{\theta}{360}\right\rfloor \in [0,360).]Whenever (\theta) is used as input to a quantizer, it must be normalized by (\mathrm{norm}{360}) first.
Sexagesimal backbone index
Define the sextine (sexagesimal) backbone as the partition of the circle into 6 sectors of (60^\circ). Define:[h ;\coloneqq; Q_6(\theta) ;\coloneqq; \left\lfloor \frac{\mathrm{norm}_{360}(\theta)}{60}\right\rfloor \in [6]\cong \mathbb{Z}_6.]The sextine boundaries are the angles (\theta\equiv 60k\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 360)), (k\in[6]).
12-grid and 18-grid quantizers
Define the 12-part quantizer:[z ;\coloneqq; Q_{12}(\theta) ;\coloneqq; \left\lfloor \frac{\mathrm{norm}{360}(\theta)}{30}\right\rfloor \in [12]\cong \mathbb{Z}{12}.]Define the 18-part quantizer:[u ;\coloneqq; Q_{18}(\theta) ;\coloneqq; \left\lfloor \frac{\mathrm{norm}{360}(\theta)}{20}\right\rfloor \in [18]\cong \mathbb{Z}{18}.]
2/3 refinement law and sextile nexus points
The refinement relation is:
One sextine sector ([60k,60(k+1))) decomposes into:
two 12-bins of width (30^\circ), and
three 18-bins of width (20^\circ).
The least common boundary spacing between the 12-grid and 18-grid is[\mathrm{LCM}(30,20)=60,]so the strict boundary alignment points (nexus points) are precisely the sextine boundaries.
At a sextine boundary (\theta=60k), the boundary indices satisfy[z = 2k \pmod{12},\qquad u = 3k \pmod{18}.]Equivalently, a sextile-step update in the product index space (\mathbb{Z}{12}\times\mathbb{Z}{18}) is[(z,u)\mapsto(z+2,;u+3).]
Joint tiling induced by overlay
Overlaying the 12-grid and 18-grid partitions yields a deterministic 24-tile segmentation of the circle. In each sextine sector, the sorted boundary set is[{0,20,30,40,60}](relative to the sector start), producing four subinterval tiles with widths (20^\circ,10^\circ,10^\circ,20^\circ). Over 6 sectors, this yields (6\cdot 4=24) joint tiles. Each joint tile is labeled by a unique pair ((z,u)) and can be treated as an element of a subset of (\mathbb{Z}{12}\times\mathbb{Z}{18}) determined by the quantizers.
A.1.4 Product spaces, tensors, and adjacency representations
Product coordinate spaces
Whenever two indexing schemes are used simultaneously, the combined state is represented in a product space:
Tandem angular index:[\mathcal{T} ;\coloneqq; \mathbb{Z}{12}\times\mathbb{Z}{18}.]
Base–mode station index:[\mathcal{S}_{12} ;\coloneqq; \mathbb{B}\times\mathbb{M}.]
Full evaluation state (typical):[\mathcal{X} ;\coloneqq; \mathcal{S}{12}\times \mathcal{T}\times \mathbb{Z}{\ge 0}]with the last factor indexing discrete time/step count when required.
A point in a product space is always written as an explicit tuple; destructuring must preserve component order.
Matrices and adjacency
For a finite graph (G=(V,E)) with (|V|=n) and a fixed vertex ordering (v_0,\dots,v_{n-1}), define the adjacency matrix[A\in{0,1}^{n\times n},\qquad A_{ij}=\mathbf{1}[(v_i,v_j)\in E].]For weighted graphs, define a weight function (w:E\to\mathbb{R}) and its matrix extension[W_{ij}=\begin{cases}w(v_i,v_j), & (v_i,v_j)\in E,\+\infty, & \text{otherwise},\end{cases}]with (+\infty) treated as an absorbing element for min-plus path algebra.
Tensor index conventions
If tensors are used, the following conventions apply:
Lower indices denote covariant slots, upper indices contravariant slots; if no ambient manifold is specified, indices are purely symbolic for bookkeeping.
Repeated index summation (Einstein convention) is used only when explicitly declared for a section; otherwise all sums are written explicitly.
All tensor components are indexed in 0-based form unless a domain reason forces 1-based presentation.
A.2 Operators and transforms
A.2.1 Primitive cyclic transforms
Translation on (\mathbb{Z}_n)
For (k\in\mathbb{Z}n), define the shift (rotation) operator[T_k:\mathbb{Z}n\to\mathbb{Z}n,\qquad T_k(x)=x+k.]Properties:[T_0=\mathrm{id},\qquad T_k\circ T\ell = T{k+\ell},\qquad T_k^{-1}=T{-k}.]
Reflection (mirror) about a pivot
For a pivot (p\in\mathbb{Z}_n), define the reflection[R_p:\mathbb{Z}_n\to\mathbb{Z}n,\qquad R_p(x)=2p-x.]Properties:[R_p\circ R_p=\mathrm{id},\qquad R_p\circ T_k = T{-k}\circ R_p.]
Opposition (half-turn) on even cycles
For even (n), define the polarity inversion (half-turn)[P:\mathbb{Z}n\to\mathbb{Z}n,\qquad P(x)=x+\frac{n}{2}.]In particular, on (\mathbb{Z}{12}),[P(x)=x+6.]Properties:[P\circ P=\mathrm{id},\qquad P\circ T_k = T_k\circ P,\qquad P\circ R_p = R{p+6}\circ P.]
Bitwise complement on (\mathbb{B})
On the 2-bit basis (\mathbb{B}), define complement (\neg:\mathbb{B}\to\mathbb{B}) by (\neg b = b\oplus 11) (bitwise XOR with (11)). This is an involution. Use (\neg) only when operating at the (\mathbb{B}) layer; do not conflate with opposition (P) on (\mathbb{Z}_{12}).
A.2.2 Structural transforms: embedding, projection, and grounding
Base and mode projections
For (x=(b,m)\in\mathcal{S}{12}), define projections[\pi{\mathbb{B}}(x)=b,\qquad \pi_{\mathbb{M}}(x)=m.]
Grounding operator
Define the grounding operator (\Gamma) as a stabilization map that collapses an active mode into an Earth-mode checkpoint without changing base unless stated:[\Gamma:\mathbb{B}\times\mathbb{M}\to\mathbb{B}\times\mathbb{B},\qquad \Gamma(b,m)=(b,11).]When the system requires returning to the active mode set (\mathbb{M}), grounding is paired with a reactivation (selection) operator (\rho) whose admissible outputs are restricted:[\rho:\mathbb{B}\times\mathbb{B}\to\mathbb{B}\times\mathbb{M},\qquad \rho(b,11)=(b,m')\text{ for some }m'\in\mathbb{M}.]Any algorithm using (\rho) must specify how (m') is selected (deterministic rule, cost minimization, or externally supplied parameter).
Angular embedding of discrete indices
Define the canonical angle embedding of a discrete index:
For (z\in\mathbb{Z}{12}), the canonical cell is ([30z,30(z+1))), and a canonical representative angle is[\theta{12}(z);\coloneqq;30\left(z+\tfrac12\right).]
For (u\in\mathbb{Z}{18}), the canonical representative is[\theta{18}(u);\coloneqq;20\left(u+\tfrac12\right).]
For (h\in\mathbb{Z}_6),[\theta_6(h);\coloneqq;60\left(h+\tfrac12\right).]When an algorithm requires a representative point of a bin, it must use the canonical midpoint embeddings above unless otherwise stated.
Quantization as an operator
Quantization is treated as an operator (Q_n:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{Z}_n) as defined in A.1.3. Any composition (Q_n\circ f) must state whether (f) outputs degrees or radians; this manuscript uses degrees unless explicitly labeled.
A.2.3 Routing operators: tunnels, guards, and attestations
Corridor move sets on (\mathbb{Z}_{12})
Define standard offset classes (as subsets of (\mathbb{Z}{12})):[\Delta{60} \coloneqq {\pm 2},\quad\Delta_{120}\coloneqq {\pm 4},\quad\Delta_{90}\coloneqq {\pm 3},\quad\Delta_{180}\coloneqq {6}.]Define the tunnel offsets and forbidden offsets:[\Delta_{\mathrm{tunnel}} \coloneqq \Delta_{60}\cup \Delta_{120}={\pm 2,\pm 4},\qquad\Delta_{\mathrm{nono}} \coloneqq \Delta_{90}\cup \Delta_{180}={\pm 3,6}.]
Tunnel operator on stations
Let (s\in\mathbb{Z}{12}). A tunnel operator is a partial map parameterized by an offset (\delta\in\Delta{\mathrm{tunnel}}):[\tau_{\delta}:\mathbb{Z}{12}\rightharpoonup\mathbb{Z}{12},\qquad\tau_{\delta}(s)=s+\delta,]defined only when a guard predicate (below) holds; otherwise it is undefined (or returns a distinguished failure value (\bot) in implementations).
Define the tunnel relation (edge set)[E_{\mathrm{tunnel}};\coloneqq;{(s,s+\delta): s\in\mathbb{Z}{12},\ \delta\in\Delta{\mathrm{tunnel}}},]and the induced tunnel graph[G_{\mathrm{tunnel}}=(\mathbb{Z}{12},E{\mathrm{tunnel}}).]
Extended tunnel operator on tandem indices
On the tandem index space (\mathcal{T}=\mathbb{Z}{12}\times\mathbb{Z}{18}), define the sextine-step operator[\tau_{60}^{\times}:\mathcal{T}\to\mathcal{T},\qquad\tau_{60}^{\times}(z,u)=(z+2,\ u+3).]This is the unique primitive step that respects the 2/3 refinement law at sextine nexus points and extends sextile motion to tandem coordinates.
Define the trine-step operator (two sextine steps)[\tau_{120}^{\times}(z,u) ;=; (\tau_{60}^{\times})^{\circ 2}(z,u)=(z+4,\ u+6).]
Guard functional and feasibility predicate
Let (\Omega_q) be a real-valued coherence cost functional on transitions:[\Omega_q:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}\cup{+\infty}.]The qualitative guard is the predicate[\Omega:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{X}\to{0,1},\qquad\Omega(x,y)=\mathbf{1}[\Omega_q(x,y)\le \kappa],]for a fixed threshold (\kappa\in\mathbb{R}).
A transition (x\to y) is admissible iff (\Omega(x,y)=1). In all routing algorithms, admissibility is evaluated before committing to a transition; if (\Omega=0), the transition is rejected.
Attestation operator
Attestation provides a reproducible certification that an object (graph, path, adjacency list, serialization) is canonical under the conventions of A.4. Let (\mathrm{ser}(\cdot)) be a canonical serialization function. Define[\Sigma(\mathcal{O}) ;\coloneqq; H(\mathrm{ser}(\mathcal{O})),]where (H) is a fixed cryptographic hash (or deterministic checksum) function declared in A.4.3. Attestation values are treated as immutable identifiers for the serialized object.
A.2.4 Composition laws, normal forms, and implementation signatures
Composition and domain discipline
Operators are composed right-to-left:[(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x)).]Partial operators (f:\mathcal{A}\rightharpoonup\mathcal{B}) are composed only when (g(x)) is defined and lies in (\mathrm{dom}(f)). Implementations must represent undefinedness explicitly (exception, option type, or distinguished value) and must never silently coerce undefined results.
Normal forms on (\mathbb{Z}_{12})
Any integer offset (\delta\in\mathbb{Z}) applied to (\mathbb{Z}{12}) is reduced by[\mathrm{mod}{12}(\delta);\coloneqq;\delta - 12\left\lfloor \frac{\delta}{12}\right\rfloor\in[12].]When offsets are compared for class membership (e.g., sextile vs trine), they must be reduced to ([12]) first, then mapped to the canonical signed representative in ({-6,-5,\dots,5,6}) when needed.
Reference function signatures (typed)
A minimal typed interface (language-agnostic) is assumed:
Indices:
Z12 = {0..11}, Z18 = {0..17}, Z6 = {0..5}.
AngleDeg = real with explicit normalization.
Quantizers:
Q12: AngleDeg -> Z12
Q18: AngleDeg -> Z18
Q6: AngleDeg -> Z6
Operators:
T12(k): Z12 -> Z12
R12(p): Z12 -> Z12
P12: Z12 -> Z12
tau60x: (Z12,Z18) -> (Z12,Z18) as ((z,u)\mapsto(z+2,u+3))
Guard:
Omega_q: State x State -> RealPlusInfinity
Omega: State x State -> Bool
Attestation:
ser: Object -> ByteString
Sigma: Object -> Digest
Implementations must enforce the canonical ordering of indices and the canonical serialization rules of A.4.
A.3 Invariants and corridors
A.3.1 Invariants: definitions, equivalence, and canonical representatives
Invariant definition
Let (G) be a group acting on a set (\mathcal{Y}) via (\cdot:G\times \mathcal{Y}\to\mathcal{Y}). A function (I:\mathcal{Y}\to\mathcal{I}) is an invariant under the action if[I(g\cdot y)=I(y)\quad\forall g\in G,\ \forall y\in\mathcal{Y}.]
Typical actions used in this manuscript:
Rotational symmetry on (\mathbb{Z}_n): (g=T_k) for (k\in\mathbb{Z}_n).
Reflection symmetry: (g=R_p) for (p\in\mathbb{Z}_n).
Product actions on (\mathbb{Z}{12}\times\mathbb{Z}{18}): apply component-wise.
Equivalence relations induced by invariants
An invariant (I) induces an equivalence relation (\sim_I) on (\mathcal{Y}) by:[y\sim_I y' \iff I(y)=I(y').]A canonical representative of an equivalence class ([y]_{\sim_I}) is defined as[\mathrm{canon}I(y);\coloneqq;\min {y'\in[y]{\sim_I}}]under a specified canonical order on (\mathcal{Y}). The order must be explicitly stated; absent a domain-specific alternative, the lexicographic order on integer encodings is used.
Station-class invariants
For (s\in\mathbb{Z}_{12}), the following invariants are frequently used:
Base invariant:[I_{\mathrm{base}}(s);\coloneqq; b(s)\in\mathbb{B}.]
Mode invariant:[I_{\mathrm{mode}}(s);\coloneqq; m(s)\in\mathbb{M}.]
Sextine sector invariant (angle-embedded):[I_6(s);\coloneqq; Q_6(\theta_{12}(s))\in\mathbb{Z}_6.]
A.3.2 Coherence functional (\Omega_q) and feasibility
Coherence cost
Coherence is represented by a cost functional (\Omega_q). At minimum, it must satisfy:
Nonnegativity: (\Omega_q(x,y)\ge 0) whenever finite.
Identity: (\Omega_q(x,x)=0).
Inadmissible transitions are encoded by (+\infty).
If paths are scored, the path cost is additive:[\Omega_q(p)=\sum_{t=1}^{L}\Omega_q(v_{t-1},v_t),]with the convention that any (+\infty) term forces (\Omega_q(p)=+\infty).
Feasibility (guard) threshold
Given (\kappa\in\mathbb{R}), define the feasibility predicate[\Omega(x,y)=\mathbf{1}[\Omega_q(x,y)\le \kappa].]A path (p) is feasible iff (\Omega(v_{t-1},v_t)=1) for all steps.
Monotone refinement property (recommended)
When multiple coordinate systems are used (e.g., (\mathbb{Z}{12}) vs (\mathbb{Z}{12}\times\mathbb{Z}_{18})), define (\Omega_q) such that refinement does not decrease cost information:[\Omega_q^{\times}((z,u),(z',u')) ;\ge; \Omega_q^{12}(z,z')]whenever ((z,u)) and ((z',u')) project to (z) and (z'). This guarantees that adding the second clock cannot falsely certify infeasible transitions as feasible.
A.3.3 Corridors: sextile backbone, tunnel classes, and forbidden classes
Offset corridors on (\mathbb{Z}_{12})
Define corridor classes by offset magnitude (in degrees) as in A.2.3:
Sextile corridor: (\Delta_{60}={\pm 2}).
Trine corridor: (\Delta_{120}={\pm 4}).
Square corridor: (\Delta_{90}={\pm 3}).
Opposition corridor: (\Delta_{180}={6}).
Define the admissible tunnel corridor set[\Delta_{\mathrm{tunnel}}={\pm 2,\pm 4},]and forbidden (hard) corridor set[\Delta_{\mathrm{nono}}={\pm 3,6}.]
A corridor graph may be parameterized by an allowed offset set (\Delta\subseteq \mathbb{Z}{12}):[E{\Delta};\coloneqq;{(s,s+\delta): s\in\mathbb{Z}{12},\ \delta\in\Delta},\qquadG{\Delta}=(\mathbb{Z}{12},E{\Delta}).]
Sextine backbone corridor (sexagesimal invariance)
Define the sextine backbone as the subgroup of (\mathbb{Z}{12}) generated by (+2):[\langle 2\rangle ;=;{0,2,4,6,8,10}\subseteq\mathbb{Z}{12}\cong \mathbb{Z}_6.]Movement by sextile offsets preserves alignment with the sextine nexus structure in the tandem system. In corridor constructions that must remain phase-locked across multiple partitions, sextile edges are treated as the primitive coherence-preserving moves.
Tandem corridor on (\mathbb{Z}{12}\times\mathbb{Z}{18})
Define the sextine-step operator[\tau_{60}^{\times}(z,u)=(z+2,u+3).]The directed corridor graph induced by this operator is[E_{60}^{\times};\coloneqq;{((z,u),\tau_{60}^{\times}(z,u)) : (z,u)\in\mathcal{T}},\qquadG_{60}^{\times}=(\mathcal{T},E_{60}^{\times}).]This corridor is the minimal shared-step corridor of the 12- and 18-quantizers under the 2/3 refinement law.
A.3.4 Path metrics, routing, and anomaly detection in tandem coordinates
Distance and shortest paths
For a directed weighted graph ((V,E,w)), define the directed shortest-path distance[d(u,v)=\inf{\sum_{e\in p} w(e): p\text{ is a directed path from }u\text{ to }v}.]If no path exists, set (d(u,v)=+\infty). In finite graphs with nonnegative weights, the infimum is attained and standard algorithms (Dijkstra, A(^*), Bellman–Ford for specific constraints) compute (d).
When corridor moves are unweighted, use hop distance (each edge weight (1)).
Corridor-consistent routing
Given a corridor graph (G_{\Delta}), a route from (s) to (t) is corridor-consistent iff every step uses an offset in (\Delta). The set of corridor-consistent endpoints reachable from (s) is[\mathrm{Reach}{\Delta}(s)={t\in\mathbb{Z}{12}:\exists \text{ path in }G_{\Delta}\text{ from }s\text{ to }t}.]
Tandem consistency checks (two-clock handshake)
Given an observed angle (\theta), compute[(z,u)=(Q_{12}(\theta),Q_{18}(\theta)).]A sextine nexus consistency test checks whether (\theta) lies sufficiently close to a sextine boundary (\theta\equiv 60k) under a tolerance (\varepsilon>0):[\mathrm{Nexus}(\theta)=\mathbf{1}\Big[\min_{k\in[6]} |\mathrm{wrap}{360}(\theta-60k)|\le \varepsilon\Big],]where (\mathrm{wrap}{360}) returns the signed minimal circular difference in ((-180,180]).
Additionally, at nexus points the index relation ((z,u)\approx (2k,3k)) holds. A discrete nexus check is:[\mathrm{NexusIdx}(z,u)=\mathbf{1}\big[\exists k\in[6]\text{ such that }z\equiv 2k\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 12)\ \wedge\ u\equiv 3k\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 18)\big].]
Anomaly detection by mismatch
Define the mismatch score[\delta(z,u);\coloneqq;\min_{k\in[6]} \Big(\mathrm{dist}{12}(z,2k)+\mathrm{dist}{18}(u,3k)\Big),]where (\mathrm{dist}_{n}) is the cyclic distance on (\mathbb{Z}_n):[\mathrm{dist}_n(a,b)=\min{(a-b)\bmod n,\ (b-a)\bmod n}.]Large (\delta(z,u)) indicates disagreement between the two clocks relative to the sextine backbone and can be used as a generic anomaly signal upstream of any interpretive layer.
A.4 Serialization and determinism
A.4.1 Canonical ordering, tie-breaking, and stable iteration
Canonical ordering requirements
Any object (\mathcal{O}) intended for hashing, attestation, reproducible computation, or cross-implementation equivalence must have a unique canonical form (\mathrm{canon}(\mathcal{O})). Canonicalization must:
Remove irrelevant representation degrees of freedom (ordering of sets, adjacency list permutations, equivalent encodings).
Fix a total order over components.
Fix formatting details (number formats, whitespace, key order) in serialization.
Stable sorting and deterministic tie-breaking
Whenever a selection is made by minimizing a value (e.g., (\arg\min)), ties must be resolved deterministically:
Primary key: the minimized scalar objective.
Secondary key: canonical order of the candidate object (lexicographic on index tuples).Thus, for a finite candidate set (C),[\arg\min_{c\in C} f(c)]returns the unique (c^\in C) such that ((f(c^),c^*)) is lexicographically minimal among ({(f(c),c):c\in C}).
Graph iteration determinism
For a graph (G=(V,E)) with (V\subseteq \mathbb{Z}^k):
Vertices iterate in (\mathrm{sort}(V)).
Edges iterate in (\mathrm{sort}(E)) where each edge is a tuple ((u,v)) (directed) or ((\min(u,v),\max(u,v))) (undirected canonicalization).
A.4.2 Numeric determinism: modular arithmetic, floating-point, and tolerances
Modular arithmetic determinism
All modular operations must:
use integer arithmetic,
reduce to canonical representatives in ([n]),
avoid mixed-sign ambiguities by applying (\mathrm{mod}_n) normalization immediately after each operation.
Define:[\mathrm{mod}_n(x)=x-n\left\lfloor\frac{x}{n}\right\rfloor\in[0,n)]for real (x), and restrict to integers when constructing (\mathbb{Z}_n) indices.
Floating-point usage policy
Floating-point arithmetic is permitted only for:
angle inputs and outputs,
tolerance comparisons ((\varepsilon)-neighborhoods),
cost scoring in (\Omega_q) when explicitly declared.
All discrete indices must be derived by quantization functions that are specified in exact mathematical form (A.1.3) and implemented using a consistent rounding policy.
Quantizer boundary convention
All quantizers (Q_n) are left-closed/right-open by definition:[Q_n(\theta)=\left\lfloor \frac{\mathrm{norm}_{360}(\theta)}{360/n}\right\rfloor.]Thus boundary angles (\theta\equiv k\cdot 360/n) quantize to bin (k) (mod (n)). Implementations must not use “round-to-nearest” for bin assignment.
Tolerance comparisons
Whenever comparing angles to boundaries or nexus points, use a declared tolerance (\varepsilon) and a declared wrap convention ((\mathrm{wrap}_{360})). No implicit tolerances are permitted.
A.4.3 Canonical serialization format, hashing, and versioning
Canonical serialization
Define a canonical byte-level serialization (\mathrm{ser}(\cdot)) satisfying:
Deterministic key order for maps/dictionaries (lexicographic on UTF-8 key bytes).
Deterministic list order (pre-sorted by canonical ordering rules).
Explicit numeric formats:
integers in base-10 ASCII with no leading zeros (except “0”),
real numbers either forbidden or encoded via a declared scheme (recommended: scaled integers or IEEE-754 binary64 with mandated endianness and NaN policy).
No insignificant whitespace.
If JSON is used, canonical JSON rules must be fixed and treated as part of the specification. If CBOR is used, canonical CBOR (RFC canonical form) must be enforced; endianness must be stated if raw binary fields exist.
Hash function and digest format
Define a fixed hash function (H) (e.g., SHA-256) producing a digest (d\in{0,1}^{256}). The displayed form is lowercase hex of the digest bytes. The attestation operator is:[\Sigma(\mathcal{O}) = H(\mathrm{ser}(\mathcal{O})).]
Versioning
Every serialized top-level object must include:
a schema version (v\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}),
a declared index convention identifier (0-based vs 1-based, station ordering name),
a declared numeric policy identifier (float policy, tolerance policy),so that (\mathrm{ser}) is unambiguous across time and across implementations.
A.4.4 Deterministic reference construction algorithms
Deterministic construction of corridor graphs on (\mathbb{Z}_{12})
Given an allowed offset set (\Delta\subseteq\mathbb{Z}_{12}), define:
Vertex set:[V=\mathrm{sort}(\mathbb{Z}_{12})=[12].]
Edge set (directed):[E=\mathrm{sort}\big({(s,(s+\delta)\bmod 12): s\in[12],\ \delta\in\Delta}\big).]
Adjacency list canonicalization:[\mathrm{Adj}[s] = \mathrm{sort}({t:(s,t)\in E}).]
Attestation:[\Sigma(G_{\Delta}) = H(\mathrm{ser}(V,E,\mathrm{Adj})).]
This construction is deterministic by design; the only degrees of freedom are (\Delta) and the declared serialization schema.
Deterministic sextine-tandem operator graph
On (\mathcal{T}=\mathbb{Z}{12}\times\mathbb{Z}{18}), define:
Vertex list:[V^{\times}=\mathrm{sort}\big({(z,u): z\in[12],\ u\in[18]}\big)]under lexicographic order.
Edge list:[E_{60}^{\times}=\mathrm{sort}\big({((z,u),(z+2\bmod 12,u+3\bmod 18)) : (z,u)\in V^{\times}}\big).]
Adjacency:[\mathrm{Adj}^{\times}[(z,u)] = [(z+2\bmod 12,\ u+3\bmod 18)].]
This yields a functional directed graph (outdegree 1 at every vertex), suitable as the canonical “phase-lock corridor” backbone in tandem computations.
Deterministic route selection with guards
Given:
a state space (V),
candidate transitions (C(x)\subseteq V),
guard (\Omega(x,y)),
cost (\Omega_q(x,y)),
a deterministic next-step selector is:
Filter admissible candidates:[C_{\mathrm{ok}}(x)={y\in C(x):\Omega(x,y)=1}.]
If (C_{\mathrm{ok}}(x)=\varnothing), return failure.
Select[y^*=\arg\min_{y\in C_{\mathrm{ok}}(x)} \big(\Omega_q(x,y),\ \mathrm{canon}(y)\big)]using lexicographic tie-breaking.
This rule is deterministic provided (\Omega_q) is deterministic and (\mathrm{canon}) is fixed.
Reproducible checksum of a computed path
For a path (p=(v_0,\dots,v_L)), define the canonical serialization (\mathrm{ser}(p)) as the serialization of the list ([v_0,\dots,v_L]) with each vertex in canonical tuple form. The path attestation is[\Sigma(p)=H(\mathrm{ser}(p)).]If paths carry weights or annotations, these must be included in a canonical per-step record.
Appendix A — Notation, Symbols, and Index Conventions
Appendix B — Core Theorem Templates and Proof Schemas (4×4)
Let ((\mathcal{X},|\cdot|{\mathcal{X}})) denote a real or complex Banach space and let ((\mathcal{H},\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle)) denote a real or complex Hilbert space. Let (C:\mathrm{Dom}(C)\subseteq \mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{Y}) be a (possibly unbounded) constraint operator into a Banach space (\mathcal{Y}), and define the feasible (holographic) subspace[\mathcal{H}^{4};:=;\ker(C);\subseteq;\mathcal{H},]assumed closed unless otherwise stated. Let (\mathcal{G}) be a gauge group acting on (\mathcal{H}^{4}) via maps (g:\mathcal{H}^{4}\to\mathcal{H}^{4}) that are isometries for the metric induced by (|\cdot|{\mathcal{H}}) (or, more generally, uniformly bi-Lipschitz with constants independent of (g)). Let (\pi:\mathcal{H}^{4}\to \mathcal{PATT}:=\mathcal{H}^{4}/\mathcal{G}) be the quotient map, and define the gauge-invariant (quotient) distance[\bar d(\pi(H),\pi(K)) ;:=;\inf_{g\in \mathcal{G}} |g\cdot H - K|{\mathcal{H}}.]Let (\mathrm{Inv}:\mathcal{H}^{4}\to\mathbb{R}^{m}) be an invariant (or invariant-like) vector and let (\delta\in\mathbb{R}^{m}{\ge 0}) denote a corridor budget. A canonical corridor centered at a reference (H_{\mathrm{ref}}\in\mathcal{H}^{4}) is[\mathrm{Corr}(H_{\mathrm{ref}},\delta);:=;\Big{H\in \mathcal{H}^{4}:;|\mathrm{Inv}{j}(H)-\mathrm{Inv}{j}(H_{\mathrm{ref}})|\le \delta_{j};;\forall j=1,\dots,m\Big}.]A transform (T) is constraint-admissible if (T(\mathcal{H}^{4})\subseteq \mathcal{H}^{4}), and corridor-admissible (relative to ((H_{\mathrm{ref}},\delta))) if (T(\mathrm{Corr}(H_{\mathrm{ref}},\delta))\subseteq \mathrm{Corr}(H_{\mathrm{ref}},\delta)). A canonicalization map (\mathrm{Can}:\mathcal{H}^{4}\to\mathcal{H}^{4}) is assumed gauge-selecting in the sense that (\pi(\mathrm{Can}(H))=\pi(H)) and (\mathrm{Can}(\mathrm{Can}(H))=\mathrm{Can}(H)).
Each schema below is written as a reusable template with: (i) hypotheses, (ii) statement, (iii) proof skeleton, (iv) algorithmic instantiation and certificate object.
B.1 Fixed point schemas (B.1.1–B.1.4)
B.1.1 Banach contraction schema (unique fixed point + certified iteration)
Hypotheses.Let ((\mathcal{X},d)) be a complete metric space. Let (F:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{X}) satisfy a global contraction bound: there exists (q\in[0,1)) such that[d(F(x),F(y))\le q,d(x,y)\quad\forall x,y\in\mathcal{X}.]Optionally, (\mathcal{X}\subseteq \mathcal{H}^{4}) may be a closed subset and (F) may be constraint-admissible and corridor-admissible.
Statement.There exists a unique (x^{\star}\in\mathcal{X}) such that (F(x^{\star})=x^{\star}). For any (x_{0}\in\mathcal{X}), the Picard iterates (x_{n+1}:=F(x_{n})) satisfy (x_{n}\to x^{\star}) and the a priori error bound[d(x_{n},x^{\star}) \le \frac{q^{n}}{1-q},d(x_{1},x_{0}).]If (F) preserves feasibility/corridors, then (x^{\star}) is feasible/corridor-resident.
Proof skeleton.
(Cauchy property) Use the contraction inequality inductively to show (d(x_{n+k},x_{n})\le q^{n}\frac{1-q^{k}}{1-q}d(x_{1},x_{0})).
(Existence) Completeness of ((\mathcal{X},d)) implies ({x_{n}}) converges to some (x^{\star}\in\mathcal{X}).
(Fixed point) Continuity implied by contraction yields (F(x^{\star})=\lim F(x_{n})=\lim x_{n+1}=x^{\star}).
(Uniqueness) If (F(x)=x) and (F(y)=y), then (d(x,y)=d(F(x),F(y))\le q d(x,y)) forces (x=y).
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A contraction certificate for (F) is a tuple[\mathsf{Cert}{\mathrm{contr}}(F);=;\big(\mathcal{X},d,q,\mathsf{Witness}{q}\big),]where (\mathsf{Witness}{q}) is either (a) a proven operator norm bound (e.g. (|DF| \le q) in a Fréchet sense), or (b) a verified Lipschitz estimate on a cover of (\mathcal{X}) (interval arithmetic or certified sampling with proof obligations). A run certificate records ((x{0},n_{\max},\tau)), the stopping tolerance (\tau), and the final inequality[d(x_{n+1},x_{n})\le (1-q)\tau \quad\Rightarrow\quad d(x_{n},x^{\star})\le \tau.]This schema is the canonical “existence-by-contraction” template and is compatible with standard fixed-point theorems referenced in the underlying framework.
B.1.2 Schauder compact-map schema (existence on compact convex sets)
Hypotheses.Let (\mathcal{X}) be a Banach space. Let (K\subseteq \mathcal{X}) be nonempty, convex, closed, and compact in the norm topology (or compact in a topology in which (F) is continuous and (K) is compact, with a separate argument that a fixed point lies in (K)). Let (F:K\to K) be continuous.
Statement.There exists (x^{\star}\in K) such that (F(x^{\star})=x^{\star}).
Proof skeleton.
(Finite-dimensional approximation) Construct a sequence of continuous maps (P_{n}:\mathcal{X}\to \mathcal{X}{n}) where (\mathcal{X}{n}) is finite-dimensional and (P_{n}\to \mathrm{Id}) uniformly on (K).
(Brouwer on (\mathcal{X}{n})) Define (F{n}:=P_{n}\circ F|{K}) mapping (K) into a compact convex subset of (\mathcal{X}{n}). Apply Brouwer fixed point theorem to obtain (x_{n}\in K) with (F_{n}(x_{n})=x_{n}).
(Compactness) Use compactness of (K) to extract a convergent subsequence (x_{n_{k}}\to x^{\star}\in K).
(Pass to the limit) Use continuity of (F) and uniform convergence (P_{n}\to \mathrm{Id}) on (K) to show (F(x^{\star})=x^{\star}).
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A Schauder certificate for (F) is a tuple[\mathsf{Cert}{\mathrm{sch}}(F);=;\big(K,\mathsf{Convexity}(K),\mathsf{Compactness}(K),\mathsf{Continuity}(F),\mathsf{Invariance}(F(K)\subseteq K)\big),]where (\mathsf{Compactness}(K)) is witnessed either by (a) a direct compactness proof, or (b) a coercive bound (K={x:|x|\le R}) plus compact embedding into a weaker topology used for the limit passage.A computational witness is an approximation ladder ({P{n}}) and a sequence ({x_{n}}) produced by a finite-dimensional fixed point solver with certified residuals (|P_{n}(F(x_{n}))-x_{n}|\le \eta_{n}) and (\eta_{n}\to 0), together with a compactness extraction argument.
This schema matches the “compact operator + convex compact set” existence pattern explicitly used in the framework.
B.1.3 Monotone-operator / variational schema (existence via maximal monotonicity)
Hypotheses.Let (\mathcal{H}) be a Hilbert space. Let (A:\mathcal{H}\rightrightarrows \mathcal{H}) be a maximal monotone operator. Optionally assume strong monotonicity: there exists (\mu>0) such that[\langle u-v, x-y\rangle \ge \mu|x-y|^{2}\quad\forall u\in A(x),,v\in A(y).]Consider the inclusion (0\in A(x)) or the fixed point equation (x = J_{\lambda A}(x)) where (J_{\lambda A}:=(I+\lambda A)^{-1}) is the resolvent.
Statement.If (A) is maximal monotone and satisfies standard coercivity conditions (e.g. (\langle u,x\rangle\to+\infty) as (|x|\to\infty)), then there exists (x^{\star}\in\mathcal{H}) such that (0\in A(x^{\star})). If (A) is strongly monotone, the solution is unique and (J_{\lambda A}) is a contraction for suitable (\lambda), yielding a Banach-type uniqueness and linear convergence of the proximal point iteration.
Proof skeleton.
(Minty parametrization) Use maximal monotonicity to characterize the range of (I+\lambda A) and show resolvent (J_{\lambda A}) is single-valued and firmly nonexpansive.
(Coercivity (\Rightarrow) surjectivity) Invoke Browder–Minty or direct coercivity arguments to conclude existence of a zero.
(Uniqueness under strong monotonicity) Strong monotonicity implies strict contraction behavior for the resolvent, hence uniqueness.
(Iterative convergence) Proximal point: (x_{n+1}=J_{\lambda A}(x_{n})); firm nonexpansiveness yields Fejér monotonicity to (\mathrm{Fix}(J_{\lambda A})); strong monotonicity yields a geometric rate.
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A monotonicity certificate contains: (i) a representation of (A) (subdifferential (A=\partial \phi), KKT system, or constraint normal cone), (ii) a proof or verified numeric witness of maximal monotonicity, and (iii) coercivity/strong monotonicity parameters.A run certificate includes per-iterate residuals (r_{n}\in A(x_{n})) with (|r_{n}|\to 0), and (if applicable) contraction/Fejér bounds:[|x_{n+1}-x^{\star}|\le q|x_{n}-x^{\star}|,\quad q<1.]This schema is the standard template underlying projection-to-feasibility, proximal decoding, and corridor-repair steps that can be expressed as monotone inclusions.
B.1.4 Nonexpansive/averaged-map schema (existence + weak convergence in Hilbert space)
Hypotheses.Let (\mathcal{H}) be a Hilbert space and let (K\subseteq \mathcal{H}) be nonempty, closed, and convex. Let (F:K\to K) be nonexpansive:[|F(x)-F(y)|\le |x-y|\quad\forall x,y\in K,]and assume (\mathrm{Fix}(F)\neq\varnothing). More generally, assume (F) is (\alpha)-averaged: (F=(1-\alpha)I+\alpha G) for some nonexpansive (G) and (\alpha\in(0,1)).
Statement.The Krasnosel’skiĭ–Mann iteration[x_{n+1};:=;(1-\lambda_{n})x_{n}+\lambda_{n}F(x_{n}),\qquad \lambda_{n}\in(0,1),]converges weakly to a point in (\mathrm{Fix}(F)) under standard step-size conditions (e.g. (\sum_{n}\lambda_{n}(1-\lambda_{n})=+\infty)). If (F) is a strict contraction (or if additional regularity/strong monotonicity holds in an associated inclusion), convergence is strong with a rate.
Proof skeleton.
(Fejér monotonicity) Show (|x_{n+1}-x^{\star}|^{2}\le |x_{n}-x^{\star}|^{2}-\lambda_{n}(1-\lambda_{n})|x_{n}-F(x_{n})|^{2}) for any (x^{\star}\in\mathrm{Fix}(F)).
(Asymptotic regularity) Deduce (|x_{n}-F(x_{n})|\to 0).
(Demiclosedness) Use demiclosedness of (I-F) at (0) to identify weak cluster points as fixed points.
(Uniqueness (\Rightarrow) strong convergence) Add a strict contractivity or strong convexity hypothesis to upgrade weak to strong convergence.
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.This schema is the canonical template for alternating projections, averaged repair operators, and projection-based decoders. A nonexpansive certificate records (i) a proof that (F) is nonexpansive/averaged on (K), (ii) convexity/closedness of (K), and (iii) a feasibility witness that (\mathrm{Fix}(F)\neq\varnothing) (often provided indirectly by existence arguments elsewhere). A run certificate logs the Fejér decrease and the residual (|x_{n}-F(x_{n})|) as the primary convergence diagnostic.
B.2 Stability schemas (B.2.1–B.2.4)
B.2.1 Lyapunov / energy-method schema (continuous and discrete stability)
Hypotheses.Let (S(t):\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{X}) be a continuous-time evolution (semiflow) generated by an equation (\dot x = F(x)) on (\mathcal{X}\subseteq \mathcal{H}), or let ({x_{n}}) be a discrete-time recursion (x_{n+1}=T(x_{n})). Let (V:\mathcal{X}\to \mathbb{R}{\ge 0}) be a Lyapunov functional and (W:\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}{\ge 0}) a dissipation functional.
Statement.If along trajectories (t\mapsto x(t)) one has[\frac{d}{dt}V(x(t))\le -\alpha, W(x(t))\quad(\alpha>0),]then (V) is nonincreasing and (\int_{0}^{\infty} W(x(t)),dt<\infty). If additionally (W(x)\ge c,\mathrm{dist}(x,\mathcal{E})^{2}) for an equilibrium set (\mathcal{E}), then trajectories are stable and converge toward (\mathcal{E}) in the appropriate sense.In discrete time, if[V(x_{n+1})-V(x_{n})\le -\alpha, W(x_{n}),]then (V(x_{n})) is decreasing, (\sum_{n}W(x_{n})<\infty), and convergence follows under standard coercivity/regularity assumptions.
Proof skeleton.
Integrate the differential inequality (or sum the discrete inequality) to obtain monotonicity and finiteness of total dissipation.
Use coercivity of (V) to obtain boundedness (precompactness under compact embeddings when needed).
Identify limit points via LaSalle invariance principle (continuous time) or via asymptotic regularity (discrete time).
Derive quantitative rates using strengthened inequalities (e.g. (W\ge \beta V) yields exponential decay).
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A Lyapunov certificate consists of symbolic derivations (or machine-checked inequalities) establishing (dV/dt\le -\alpha W) or (V(Tx)-V(x)\le -\alpha W(x)) over a declared domain (\mathcal{X}). A numerical certificate logs (V(x_{n})), (W(x_{n})), step sizes, and verifies the inequality to within declared tolerances, labeling any violations as corridor breaches requiring repair.
B.2.2 Semigroup / linearization schema (spectral stability near equilibria)
Hypotheses.Let (x^{\star}\in\mathcal{X}\subseteq \mathcal{H}) be an equilibrium of (\dot x = F(x)) (i.e. (F(x^{\star})=0)). Assume (F) is Fréchet differentiable at (x^{\star}) with derivative (A:=DF(x^{\star})), and (A) generates a (C_{0})-semigroup (e^{tA}). Suppose the nonlinear remainder satisfies a local Lipschitz bound: (|F(x)-F(x^{\star})-A(x-x^{\star})|\le L|x-x^{\star}|^{1+\eta}) near (x^{\star}).
Statement.If the spectral bound satisfies (\sup{\mathrm{Re},\lambda:\lambda\in\sigma(A)}\le -\gamma<0) and the nonlinear remainder is sufficiently small in a neighborhood, then (x^{\star}) is locally exponentially stable: there exist (M\ge 1), (\alpha>0) such that[|x(t)-x^{\star}|\le M e^{-\alpha t}|x(0)-x^{\star}|]for initial data near (x^{\star}).In discrete time (x_{n+1}=T(x_{n})), if (\rho(DT(x^{\star}))<1) (spectral radius), then (x^{\star}) is locally exponentially stable.
Proof skeleton.
(Variation of constants) Write (x(t)-x^{\star}=e^{tA}(x(0)-x^{\star})+\int_{0}^{t} e^{(t-s)A}R(x(s)),ds).
(Semigroup bound) Use (|e^{tA}|\le M e^{-\gamma t}).
(Bootstrap) Apply Grönwall-type estimates to control the integral term via the smallness of the remainder.
(Conclude stability) Deduce exponential decay with a reduced rate (\alpha\in(0,\gamma)).
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A spectral certificate records a computed/verified spectral bound for (A) (analytic proof, interval arithmetic on resolvents, or certified Gershgorin-type bounds in finite truncations together with convergence arguments). A local remainder certificate records Lipschitz/Hölder bounds on the nonlinear remainder in a declared neighborhood. Execution logs report whether iterates remain inside the neighborhood; leaving it invalidates the certificate and triggers corridor governance.
B.2.3 Perturbation / conditioning schema (robust stability under implementation error)
Hypotheses.Let (A:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{X}) be a bounded linear operator with bounded inverse. Let (\Delta:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{X}) be a perturbation (discretization error, truncation error, floating-point roundoff model). Assume[|A^{-1}\Delta| < 1.]
Statement.Then (A+\Delta) is invertible and[(A+\Delta)^{-1} = (I + A^{-1}\Delta)^{-1}A^{-1} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(-A^{-1}\Delta)^{k}A^{-1},]with the norm bound[|(A+\Delta)^{-1}|\le \frac{|A^{-1}|}{1-|A^{-1}\Delta|}.]Consequently, stability properties controlled by (|A^{-1}|) are robust under perturbations smaller than (1/|A^{-1}|).
Proof skeleton.
Define (B:=A^{-1}\Delta).
Since (|B|<1), the Neumann series ((I+B)^{-1}=\sum_{k\ge 0}(-B)^{k}) converges in operator norm.
Multiply out to verify ((A+\Delta)^{-1}(A+\Delta)=I).
Derive the inverse norm bound by summing the geometric series.
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A conditioning certificate is (\mathsf{Cert}_{\mathrm{cond}}=(|A|,|A^{-1}|,|\Delta|,\text{bound check})) with explicit inequality (|A^{-1}||\Delta|<1). The corresponding runtime system must report: (i) estimated or certified (|\Delta|) from truncation/roundoff policies, (ii) updated stability margins, and (iii) an escalation when the margin approaches zero.
B.2.4 Corridor stability schema (invariant-budget stability and barrier certificates)
Hypotheses.Let (T:\mathcal{H}^{4}\to\mathcal{H}^{4}) be constraint-admissible. Let (\mathrm{Inv}:\mathcal{H}^{4}\to\mathbb{R}^{m}) be measurable and locally Lipschitz. Fix ((H_{\mathrm{ref}},\delta)). Suppose there exists a drift bound function (\mathbf{b}:\mathrm{Corr}(H_{\mathrm{ref}},\delta)\to\mathbb{R}^{m}{\ge 0}) such that for all (H\in\mathrm{Corr}(H{\mathrm{ref}},\delta)),[|\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)|\le \mathbf{b}(H),]and suppose a guard condition holds:[\mathbf{b}(H)\le \delta-\big|\mathrm{Inv}(H)-\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{ref}})\big|\quad\text{(componentwise)}.]
Statement.Then (T) is corridor-admissible: (T(\mathrm{Corr}(H_{\mathrm{ref}},\delta))\subseteq \mathrm{Corr}(H_{\mathrm{ref}},\delta)). If, in addition, a repair operator (\mathcal{R}) is applied on violations and satisfies (\mathrm{Inv}(\mathcal{R}(H))=\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{ref}})) (or reduces drift by a quantified amount), then repeated application of (T) with (\mathcal{R}) yields forward invariance of the corridor and bounded drift over arbitrarily long executions.
Proof skeleton.
For (H\in\mathrm{Corr}), expand (|\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{ref}})|\le |\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)|+|\mathrm{Inv}(H)-\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{ref}})|).
Substitute the drift bound and apply the guard condition to conclude (|\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{ref}})|\le \delta).
Iterate to obtain forward invariance for repeated application.
When repair is present, treat it as a reset map and prove invariance of a composite transition system by case analysis.
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A corridor certificate must include: (i) the explicit invariant definitions and scaling conventions, (ii) the drift bound witness (\mathbf{b}) (analytic inequality, Lipschitz envelope, or verified empirical upper bound with stated confidence labeling), (iii) guard conditions and triggers, and (iv) a runtime log schema that records per-step invariant vectors, drift magnitudes, and repair invocations, producing an audit-trace that is hashable and replayable.
B.3 Convergence schemas (B.3.1–B.3.4)
B.3.1 Certified fixed-point convergence schema (rate, stopping, and correctness)
Hypotheses.Let (F:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{X}) be a contraction on a complete metric space ((\mathcal{X},d)) with constant (q\in[0,1)), or let (F) be averaged/nonexpansive on a closed convex subset of a Hilbert space with (\mathrm{Fix}(F)\neq\varnothing).
Statement.Convergence holds (strong, geometric) for contraction; (weak, Fejér) for averaged/nonexpansive. A posteriori stopping criteria can be stated in terms of the residual (r(x):=d(F(x),x)). For contraction,[d(x,x^{\star})\le \frac{1}{1-q},r(x).]
Proof skeleton.
Use contraction inequality to relate (d(x,x^{\star})) to (d(F(x),F(x^{\star}))).
Bound (d(x,x^{\star})\le d(x,F(x))+d(F(x),x^{\star})\le r(x)+q,d(x,x^{\star})).
Rearrange to obtain the residual-to-error bound.
For averaged maps, use Fejér monotonicity and demiclosedness to identify limit points.
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A convergence certificate couples (i) a map-level property certificate (contraction or averagedness) with (ii) an execution log providing monotone decrease of residuals and a final bound guaranteeing correctness at termination. The certificate must encode: the metric (d), the constant (q) (if applicable), and the numerical tolerance used in residual evaluation.
B.3.2 Optimization convergence schema (convex and proximal regimes)
Hypotheses.Let (J:\mathcal{H}\to\mathbb{R}\cup{+\infty}) be proper, lower semicontinuous, and convex. Let (J= f + g) where (f) is differentiable with (L)-Lipschitz gradient and (g) is convex (possibly nonsmooth). Consider the proximal-gradient iteration[x_{n+1} = \mathrm{prox}{\lambda g}\big(x{n} - \lambda \nabla f(x_{n})\big),\qquad 0<\lambda<2/L.]Optionally incorporate feasibility by setting (g = g_{0} + \iota_{\mathcal{H}^{4}}) where (\iota) is the indicator of the constraint set (or an exact penalty surrogate).
Statement.The sequence ({x_{n}}) converges to a minimizer of (J) (when minimizers exist). If (J) is strongly convex with modulus (\mu>0), then linear convergence holds:[|x_{n}-x^{\star}|\le \rho^{n}|x_{0}-x^{\star}|,\quad \rho\in(0,1).]
Proof skeleton.
Use descent lemma for (f) and firm nonexpansiveness of (\mathrm{prox}) to derive a one-step decrease inequality.
Prove summability of (|x_{n+1}-x_{n}|^{2}) and asymptotic stationarity (subgradient residual (\to 0)).
Use convexity to conclude convergence of objective values and weak/strong convergence of iterates (strong when strongly convex).
Extract explicit rates via strong convexity or Polyak–Łojasiewicz-type inequalities.
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A proximal convergence certificate includes: the step size rule, the Lipschitz constant bound (L), a coercivity/lower-bound witness ensuring existence of minimizers, and runtime logs of objective decrease and stationarity residuals. When feasibility is required, logs must include constraint residuals (|C(x_{n})|) and/or distance-to-feasible estimates, with a declared tolerance policy.
B.3.3 Discretization / truncation convergence schema (consistency + stability ⇒ convergence)
Hypotheses.Let (\mathcal{H}) be a Hilbert space and let ({\mathcal{H}{N}}{N\in\mathbb{N}}) be a sequence of finite-dimensional approximation spaces with linear maps (P_{N}:\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{H}{N}) (projection) and (I{N}:\mathcal{H}{N}\to\mathcal{H}) (injection/reconstruction). Assume approximation consistency:[|I{N}P_{N}x - x|{\mathcal{H}}\to 0\quad\text{for all }x\in\mathcal{D}\subseteq\mathcal{H},]for some dense domain (\mathcal{D}). Let (T:\mathcal{H}^{4}\to\mathcal{H}^{4}) be a target operator and let (T{N}:\mathcal{H}{N}\to\mathcal{H}{N}) be a discrete operator. Assume operator consistency:[|I_{N}T_{N}P_{N}x - T(x)|{\mathcal{H}}\to 0\quad\forall x\in\mathcal{D}\cap\mathcal{H}^{4}.]Assume stability: there exists (M\ge 1) independent of (N) such that[|I{N}T_{N}y|{\mathcal{H}}\le M|I{N}y|{\mathcal{H}}\quad\forall y\in\mathcal{H}{N}.]
Statement.Then (I_{N}T_{N}P_{N}x \to T(x)) in (\mathcal{H}) for all (x\in\mathcal{D}\cap\mathcal{H}^{4}). If iterated dynamics are considered, additional uniform stability (e.g. power-boundedness) yields convergence of trajectories on finite time horizons, with explicit error decompositions into truncation and stability-amplified consistency terms.
Proof skeleton.
Decompose the error:[|I_{N}T_{N}P_{N}x - T(x)|\le |I_{N}T_{N}P_{N}x - I_{N}T_{N}P_{N}T^{-1}T(x)| + \text{(consistency remainder)},]or use direct triangle inequality:[|I_{N}T_{N}P_{N}x - T(x)|\le |I_{N}T_{N}P_{N}x - I_{N}T_{N}P_{N}x_{0}| + |I_{N}T_{N}P_{N}x_{0}-T(x_{0})| + |T(x_{0})-T(x)|,]with (x_{0}\in\mathcal{D}) approximating (x).
Use stability to control the first term by (|x-x_{0}|).
Use consistency for the middle term.
Use continuity of (T) for the last term, then send (N\to\infty) followed by (x_{0}\to x).
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A discretization certificate must report: the approximation ladder ((P_{N},I_{N})), the declared dense domain (\mathcal{D}), measured/verified consistency rates, and stability constants (M). A runtime convergence report logs observed error scaling versus (N) and flags nonuniform stability (e.g. growth of (M) or condition numbers) as a certified limitation.
B.3.4 Compactness / weak-limit schema (existence and convergence of approximate solutions)
Hypotheses.Let ({x_{n}}\subseteq \mathcal{X}) be a sequence of approximate solutions to a problem (\mathcal{F}(x)=0) (equation, inclusion, variational condition). Assume:
(Uniform bounds) ({x_{n}}) is bounded in a reflexive Banach space (\mathcal{X}).
(Compactness) (\mathcal{X}\hookrightarrow \mathcal{Z}) compactly for some Banach space (\mathcal{Z}).
(Consistency) (\mathcal{F}(x_{n})\to 0) in a suitable dual space, and (\mathcal{F}) is weakly sequentially closed in the sense required by the formulation.
Statement.There exists a subsequence (x_{n_{k}}\rightharpoonup x^{\star}) in (\mathcal{X}) and (x_{n_{k}}\to x^{\star}) strongly in (\mathcal{Z}). The limit (x^{\star}) is a solution (\mathcal{F}(x^{\star})=0) (or satisfies the limiting variational inequality), and lower semicontinuity yields energy/invariant bounds inherited by the limit.
Proof skeleton.
Use reflexivity to extract a weakly convergent subsequence.
Use compact embedding to upgrade to strong convergence in (\mathcal{Z}).
Pass to the limit in the weak formulation using test functions and weak/strong convergence pairings.
Apply weak lower semicontinuity to carry inequalities (e.g. corridor bounds, energy estimates) to the limit.
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A compactness certificate records: the uniform bound source (Lyapunov inequality, coercive functional), the embedding theorem invoked (with parameter regimes), and the weak formulation used for limit passage. A numerical implementation produces approximate solutions along a refinement ladder and reports: boundedness, residual decay, and compactness indicators (e.g. equicontinuity or multi-scale tail bounds) that justify extraction in practice.
B.4 No-go schemas (B.4.1–B.4.4)
B.4.1 Identifiability no-go schema (non-injectivity under observation and gauge)
Hypotheses.Let (\mathcal{X}\subseteq \mathcal{H}^{4}) be a feasible set and let (\mathcal{G}) act on (\mathcal{X}). Let (O:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{Z}) be an observation map (e.g. restriction, mask sampling, boundary extraction) that is gauge-invariant: (O(g\cdot x)=O(x)). Let (\bar O:\mathcal{PATT}\to\mathcal{Z}) be the induced map on the quotient.
Statement.If (\bar O) fails to be injective, then reconstruction from observations is impossible as a well-defined function on patterns: there exist distinct ([x]\neq [y]\in\mathcal{PATT}) such that (O(x)=O(y)). Consequently, no algorithm can guarantee unique recovery on (\mathcal{PATT}) from (O) without adding further constraints, priors, or auxiliary measurements.
Proof skeleton.
Non-injectivity means (\exists [x]\neq [y]) with (\bar O([x])=\bar O([y])).
Choose representatives (x,y\in\mathcal{X}). Gauge-invariance implies (O(x)=O(y)).
Any purported reconstruction operator (R:\mathcal{Z}\to\mathcal{PATT}) must satisfy (R(O(x))=[x]) and (R(O(y))=[y]), contradiction.
Therefore uniqueness cannot be certified; at best one can output an ambiguity class.
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A non-identifiability certificate is a pair ((x,y)\in \mathcal{X}^{2}) with ([x]\neq [y]) and a verified equality (O(x)=O(y)) (within declared tolerances). In finite truncations, this is typically produced via nullspace computation (singular value (\approx 0)) or by explicit adversarial construction. The certificate must include a proof that the difference is not removable by gauge: (\inf_{g\in\mathcal{G}}|g\cdot x-y|>0) (or a discrete surrogate bound).
B.4.2 Reachability no-go schema (invariant obstruction and corridor infeasibility)
Hypotheses.Let (\mathcal{T}) be a class of admissible transforms on (\mathcal{H}^{4}) satisfying exact invariance:[\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))=\mathrm{Inv}(H)\quad\forall T\in\mathcal{T},;\forall H\in\mathcal{H}^{4},]or corridor invariance:[|\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)|\le \delta_{T}\quad\text{with declared budgets}.]Let (H_{\mathrm{src}},H_{\mathrm{tgt}}\in\mathcal{H}^{4}).
Statement.If (\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{src}})\neq \mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{tgt}})) under exact invariance, then (H_{\mathrm{tgt}}) is unreachable from (H_{\mathrm{src}}) by transforms in (\mathcal{T}). Under corridor budgets, if[|\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{tgt}})-\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{src}})| > \sum_{k=1}^{N}\delta_{T_{k}}]for any admissible program length (N) and per-step budgets, then (H_{\mathrm{tgt}}) is unreachable within the declared corridor governance.
Proof skeleton.
Under exact invariance, invariants are constant along any orbit (H_{n+1}=T_{n}(H_{n})).
Therefore any reachable state must share the invariant vector with the source.
Under budgets, invariants can drift at most by the cumulative budget; apply triangle inequality along the program.
If target mismatch exceeds cumulative budget, reachability contradicts the drift bound.
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A reachability-impossibility certificate consists of: (i) the invariant definition and its proof of invariance/budgeted drift for all admissible transforms in scope, (ii) computed invariant vectors for source and target with uncertainty bounds, and (iii) the inequality demonstrating mismatch beyond allowable drift. The output object is an explicit “unreachable within corridor” report that is reproducible and hashable.
B.4.3 Ill-posed inverse no-go schema (unbounded inverse and noise amplification)
Hypotheses.Let (A:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{Z}) be a bounded linear operator between infinite-dimensional normed spaces, injective but with non-closed range (equivalently: (A^{-1}) exists on (\mathrm{Ran}(A)) but is unbounded). A canonical sufficient condition is that (A) is compact and injective with infinite-dimensional domain.
Statement.No continuous inverse exists on (\mathrm{Ran}(A)). Consequently, for any reconstruction procedure (\widehat x=\mathcal{R}(z)) attempting to solve (A x=z), arbitrarily small measurement noise can produce arbitrarily large reconstruction error on some instances. In particular, there is no uniform stability bound (|x-\widehat x|\le K|z-\widehat z|) valid over (\mathrm{Ran}(A)).
Proof skeleton.
Since (A^{-1}) is unbounded, there exists a sequence (x_{n}) with (|x_{n}|=1) but (|A x_{n}|\to 0).
Let (z_{n}:=A x_{n}). Then (z_{n}\to 0) while the corresponding solutions have unit norm.
Any continuous inverse would imply (|x_{n}|=|A^{-1}z_{n}|\to 0), contradiction.
Noise amplification follows by considering perturbed data (z_{n}+\eta) with (|\eta|) comparable to (|z_{n}|).
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.An ill-posedness certificate is typically a singular-sequence witness: a sequence ({x_{n}}) and ({z_{n}}) with (|x_{n}|=1), (z_{n}=A x_{n}), (|z_{n}|\to 0). In finite truncations, this corresponds to rapidly decaying singular values; the certificate reports the smallest singular values, their scaling with resolution, and a declared warning boundary beyond which stable inversion is not certified.
B.4.4 Finite-program / complexity no-go schema (lower bounds and search infeasibility)
Hypotheses.Let (\mathcal{S}) be a finite generating set (gates, primitives) for a program space and let (\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{S})) denote all finite-length programs over (\mathcal{S}) under a composition semantics (\circ). Let (\mathrm{Cost}:\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{S})\to\mathbb{R}{\ge 0}) be a cost functional and let (\mathcal{T}{\mathrm{goal}}) be a target transform (or equivalence class up to gauge). Consider the decision problem: determine whether there exists a program (p) such that (|T_{p}-\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{goal}}|\le \varepsilon) and (\mathrm{Cost}(p)\le B).
Statement.Without additional structure (convexity, commutativity, restricted gate graphs, or explicit normal forms), one cannot generally guarantee polynomial-time search for globally optimal programs. Moreover, in the presence of noncommutativity and equivalence relations, minimal-length representations need not be unique, and proof obligations for optimality can require enumerating exponentially many candidates in the worst case.
Proof skeleton.
Identify the combinatorial growth of candidate programs: (|\mathcal{S}|^{L}) at length (L).
Show that equivalence under rewriting can create long-range dependencies, obstructing greedy search.
Establish lower bounds on program length for approximation by metric entropy/covering number arguments: if the (\varepsilon)-covering number of the reachable set grows faster than (|\mathcal{S}|^{L}), then (L) must scale at least like (\log N(\varepsilon)).
For specific instances, provide reductions from known hard problems (when explicitly formalized) or restrict claims to certified lower bounds derived from invariants and Lipschitz envelopes.
Algorithmic instantiation and certificate.A program no-go certificate is either:(i) an expressivity lower bound: a proven inequality (L\ge L_{\min}(\varepsilon)) derived from covering-number or invariant-resolution arguments, or(ii) an infeasibility certificate under corridor/admissibility constraints showing that all programs up to a declared depth violate constraints or budgets, with the depth bound justified by monotone pruning rules that are themselves certified.A runtime system must produce a search-governance log that records pruning criteria, admissibility failures, and the formal reason why extending the search is not certified under the declared computational budget.
Appendix B — Core Theorem Templates and Proof Schemas (4×4)
Appendix C — Algorithm Catalog (Pseudocode + Complexity) (4×4)
C.0 Preliminaries and shared conventions
Let (\mathfrak{H}) denote the ambient (real or complex) Hilbert space of states (holograms), with inner product (\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle) and induced norm (|\cdot|). Let (H^4\subseteq \mathfrak{H}) denote the feasible (constrained) holographic state space, typically expressed as a kernel:[H^4 ;=; \ker(C),]for a (possibly vector-valued) constraint operator (C:\mathfrak{H}\to \mathcal{Y}). In discrete models (finite truncations), (\mathfrak{H}_N\cong \mathbb{K}^N) ((\mathbb{K}\in{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C}})) with constraint operator (C_N:\mathfrak{H}_N\to \mathcal{Y}_N) and feasible set (H_N^4:=\ker(C_N)).
Let (\mathcal{G}) denote a gauge group acting on states (e.g., phase or coordinate gauge), and (\mathrm{Can}:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathfrak{H}) a canonicalization operator selecting a representative in each gauge class (when definable). Let (\mathrm{Inv}:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathbb{R}^q) denote an invariant suite (multi-scale, structural, informational), and let (\Omega) denote corridor budgets (tolerances) constraining allowable drift:[\big|\mathrm{Inv}(H') - \mathrm{Inv}(H)\big|;\le;\Omega\quad\text{(componentwise, or under a specified norm).}]
Observations
An observation model is a (typically linear) operator (O:\mathfrak{H}\to\mathbb{K}^m) with data (y\in\mathbb{K}^m):[y ;=; O(H) + \eta,]where (\eta) is noise. Region restriction and masking/sampling are treated as special cases of (O).
Complexity conventions
All algorithmic complexity statements are in terms of discrete representations:
(N := \dim(\mathfrak{H}_N)) total degrees of freedom of the state.
(m := \dim(\mathrm{range}(O))) number of measurements.
(s :=) sparsity level in a chosen basis (when applicable).
(T :=) iteration count for iterative methods.
(\mathrm{nnz}(\cdot)) denotes number of nonzeros.
For operator applications, (\mathrm{Apply}(A,x)) is assumed to cost (O(\mathrm{nnz}(A))) for sparse (A), (O(N^2)) for dense (A), and (O(N\log N)) for FFT-like structured operators (when available).
In all pseudocode, deterministic execution means: fixed seeds; fixed iteration ordering; fixed reduction order; fixed serialization of certificates and hashes.
C.1 Reconstruction algorithms (C.1.1–C.1.4)
C.1.1 Constrained least-squares reconstruction (linear feasibility)
Problem. Given (O\in\mathbb{K}^{m\times N}), (y\in\mathbb{K}^m), and linear constraints (C_N\in\mathbb{K}^{p\times N}), compute[\widehat{H};\in;\arg\min_{H\in\mathbb{K}^N}\ \frac12|OH-y|_2^2\quad\text{s.t.}\quad C_N H=0,]optionally followed by gauge canonicalization (\widehat{H}\leftarrow \mathrm{Can}(\widehat{H})).
Algorithm C.1.1 (KKT / nullspace solve).
Input:
O ∈ 𝕂^{m×N}, y ∈ 𝕂^m
C ∈ 𝕂^{p×N} (discrete constraint operator)
solver tolerances: tol_ls, tol_kkt
optional canonicalizer Can(·)
Output:
H_hat ∈ 𝕂^N, certificate Cert
Procedure:
1) Compute (or implicitly represent) an orthonormal basis Z ∈ 𝕂^{N×r} for ker(C),
where r = N - rank(C). (If too large, represent Z via an implicit projector Π.)
2) Reduce to unconstrained LS in coordinates u ∈ 𝕂^r:
minimize ½‖ O(Zu) - y ‖_2^2
Solve for u_hat using a numerically stable method:
- if m,r moderate: QR / SVD
- else: iterative LS (LSQR / CGNR) using matvecs with O and Z
3) Set H_hat ← Z u_hat.
4) (Optional) Canonicalize: H_hat ← Can(H_hat).
5) Build certificate:
residual_obs = ‖O H_hat - y‖_2 / (‖y‖_2 + ε)
residual_con = ‖C H_hat‖_2 / (‖H_hat‖_2 + ε)
inv_vector = Inv(H_hat) (if available)
hash = Hash(Serialize(H_hat), Serialize(metadata))
Cert ← {residual_obs, residual_con, inv_vector, hash, solver logs}
6) Return (H_hat, Cert).
Complexity.
Nullspace basis construction (explicit): (O(Np\min{N,p})) worst-case (SVD); sparse structured constraints can reduce to (O(\mathrm{nnz}(C)\cdot k)) for iterative nullspace discovery.
Reduced LS:
QR/SVD: (O(mr^2)) (QR) or (O(mr\min{m,r})) (SVD).
LSQR with (T) iterations: (O(T(\mathrm{Apply}(O,\cdot)+\mathrm{Apply}(O^,\cdot)+\mathrm{Apply}(Z,\cdot)+\mathrm{Apply}(Z^,\cdot)))).
Memory: store (O) and (C) (sparse) plus iterates: (O(\mathrm{nnz}(O)+\mathrm{nnz}(C)+N)); explicit (Z) costs (O(Nr)).
Correctness. If (C) is linear and (Z) spans (\ker(C)), step (2) solves the constrained LS exactly (up to numerical tolerance). Residual certificates validate feasibility and data fit.
C.1.2 Regularized reconstruction (Tikhonov / sparsity priors)
Problem. Recover (\widehat{H}) by solving:[\widehat{H}\in\arg\min_{H\in\mathbb{K}^N}\ \frac12|OH-y|_2^2 + \lambda \mathcal{R}(H)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad C_N H=0,]with (\mathcal{R}) convex (e.g., (|WH|_1) for sparsity in basis (W), or (|LH|_2^2) for smoothness).
Algorithm C.1.2 (Proximal projected reconstruction).
Input:
O, y, C
regularizer R(H) with proximal operator prox_{τR}(·)
step size τ > 0, iterations T
feasibility projector Π ≈ projection onto ker(C) (exact or iterative)
Output:
H_hat, certificate Cert
Procedure:
1) Initialize H ← 0 (or warm start from C.1.1).
2) For t = 1..T:
g ← O^*(O H - y) // gradient of data term
H ← H - τ g // gradient step
H ← prox_{τλR}(H) // regularization proximal step
H ← Π(H) // enforce constraints (hard projection)
(Optional) early stop if relative decrease < tol
3) Canonicalize if required: H_hat ← Can(H).
4) Certificate:
residual_obs, residual_con, objective history, Inv(H_hat), hash.
5) Return.
Complexity.
Per iteration: (O(\mathrm{Apply}(O,\cdot)+\mathrm{Apply}(O^*,\cdot)+\mathrm{Cost}(\mathrm{prox})+\mathrm{Cost}(\Pi))).
If (O) is FFT-structured: (\mathrm{Apply}(O,\cdot)=O(N\log N)).
If (\Pi) is implemented by solving (\min|H-\tilde H|^2) s.t. (CH=0): cost depends on constraint solve; with (p\ll N), using (C C^*) solves can be efficient.
Convergence. For convex (\mathcal{R}) and exact projection (\Pi), proximal gradient converges under standard step size conditions (e.g., (\tau\le 1/L), (L=|O|_2^2)). Approximate projection yields inexact proximal iterations; certificates must report projection residuals.
C.1.3 Multiscale reconstruction via restriction/prolongation (Fractal ladder)
Problem. Reconstruct (H) at fine resolution (k) using a coarse-to-fine schedule with restriction (S_{k\leftarrow k+1}), prolongation (R_{k\to k+1}), and projector (\Pi_k = R S) (coarse-grid correction). This structure is explicitly part of the multiscale toolkit.
Algorithm C.1.3 (Coarse-to-fine constrained reconstruction).
Input:
Levels ℓ = 0..L (ℓ=0 coarse, ℓ=L fine)
For each level ℓ: observation operator O_ℓ, data y_ℓ, constraints C_ℓ
Restriction S_ℓ: ℓ+1 → ℓ, prolongation R_ℓ: ℓ → ℓ+1
Smoother SolveLocal(·) for level problems (few iterations)
Output:
H_L_hat, certificate Cert
Procedure:
1) Initialize coarse guess H_0 ← 0.
2) For ℓ = 0..L:
// (a) Solve / smooth on current level
H_ℓ ← SolveLocal(O_ℓ, y_ℓ, C_ℓ, init=H_ℓ)
// (b) If not finest, prolongate to next level
If ℓ < L:
H_{ℓ+1} ← R_ℓ(H_ℓ)
// optional: inject residual correction
r_{ℓ+1} ← y_{ℓ+1} - O_{ℓ+1} H_{ℓ+1}
H_{ℓ+1} ← H_{ℓ+1} + (fine_correction from r_{ℓ+1})
3) Final fine polishing: run SolveLocal at ℓ=L to meet tolerances.
4) Canonicalize: H_L_hat ← Can(H_L_hat).
5) Certificate includes per-level residuals, constraint residuals, invariant drift by level, hash.
Complexity.
If each level halves degrees of freedom per axis, total work for V-cycle style solvers is typically (O(N)) to (O(N\log N)) depending on smoother and operator structure.
Memory: store level operators or generate on the fly; typically (O(N)) total for multilevel hierarchies.
Correctness. Under standard multigrid assumptions (smoothing + coarse correction effectiveness), the method converges to the constrained solution of the fine-level reconstruction problem. Certificates must include per-level constraint residuals.
C.1.4 Reconstruction with corridor enforcement and invariant monitoring
Problem. Reconstruct (\widehat{H}) while enforcing:
feasibility (C_N\widehat{H}=0);
gauge canonicalization (\widehat{H}=\mathrm{Can}(\widehat{H}));
corridor constraints on invariants (|\mathrm{Inv}(\widehat{H})-\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\mathrm{ref}})|\le \Omega) for a reference (H_{\mathrm{ref}}) or target invariant vector.
Algorithm C.1.4 (Monitor–repair reconstruction loop).
Input:
Observation model O,y ; constraints C ; canonicalizer Can ; invariants Inv ; corridor Ω
Base reconstructor BaseRecon (e.g., C.1.1 or C.1.2)
Repair operator Repair(·) that reduces corridor violations
Max outer iterations K, tolerances tol_obs, tol_con, tol_inv
Output:
H_hat, certificate Cert
Procedure:
1) Initialize H ← BaseRecon(O,y,C) // inner certificate retained
2) For k = 1..K:
H ← Can(H)
r_obs ← ‖O H - y‖ / (‖y‖ + ε)
r_con ← ‖C H‖ / (‖H‖ + ε)
v_inv ← Inv(H)
if (r_obs ≤ tol_obs) and (r_con ≤ tol_con) and (|v_inv - v_target| ≤ Ω + tol_inv):
break
// Repair step: adjust H to reduce invariant drift without breaking constraints
H ← Repair(H, target=v_target, budgets=Ω)
H ← ProjectToKernel(H,C) // enforce feasibility
3) Return H_hat=H with certificate including full loop logs, invariant traces, hashes.
Complexity.
Outer loop (K) multiplies the cost of (canonicalize + invariant compute + repair + projection).
Invariant computation cost depends on estimator suite (spectral, wavelet, information), commonly (O(N\log N)) if FFT/wavelet-based.
Soundness of certificates. The certificate is valid if it (i) logs every corridor check and repair action, and (ii) records invariant computation versions and tolerances so that drift claims are reproducible.
C.2 Constraint projection algorithms (C.2.1–C.2.4)
C.2.1 Exact projection onto (\ker(C_N)) for linear constraints
Problem. Given (\tilde{H}\in\mathbb{K}^N), compute:[\Pi_{\ker(C)}(\tilde{H}) := \arg\min_{H\in\mathbb{K}^N}\ |H-\tilde{H}|_2^2 \quad\text{s.t.}\quad C H=0.]
Algorithm C.2.1 (Normal-equation projection).
Input:
C ∈ 𝕂^{p×N}, vector H_tilde ∈ 𝕂^N
solver for (C C^*) λ = C H_tilde
Output:
H_proj, certificate Cert
Procedure:
1) Solve (C C^*) λ = C H_tilde for λ ∈ 𝕂^p
2) Set H_proj ← H_tilde - C^* λ
3) Cert: residual = ‖C H_proj‖/(‖H_proj‖+ε), condition-estimate of (C C^*), hash.
Complexity.
Building (C C^*): (O(p\cdot \mathrm{nnz}(C))) if sparse; solving:
direct (Cholesky) (O(p^3)) for dense;
iterative (O(T\cdot \mathrm{Apply}(C,\cdot)+T\cdot \mathrm{Apply}(C^*,\cdot))) for large sparse.
Memory: (O(\mathrm{nnz}(C)+p^2)) (or (O(\mathrm{nnz}(C))) with matrix-free iterative solves).
C.2.2 Alternating projections for multiple constraint families
Problem. Constraints often decompose into families:[H\in \bigcap_{j=1}^J \mathcal{K}_j,]where each (\mathcal{K}_j) admits an efficient projector (\Pi_j) (linear or nonlinear). Compute an approximate feasible point via alternating projections, with Dykstra correction if needed.
Algorithm C.2.2 (Dykstra alternating projections).
Input:
Projectors Π_j for sets 𝒦_j, j=1..J
Initial H ← H0, correction vectors q_j ← 0
Iterations T
Output:
H_feas, certificate Cert
Procedure:
For t = 1..T:
For j = 1..J:
x ← H - q_j
p ← Π_j(x)
q_j ← p - x
H ← p
Cert: per-set residuals dist(H,𝒦_j), final max residual, iteration log, hash.
Complexity.
Per outer iteration: (\sum_{j=1}^J \mathrm{Cost}(\Pi_j)).
Memory: (O(JN)) to store (q_j) (can be reduced when (q_j) structured/sparse).
Convergence. For closed convex (\mathcal{K}_j) in Hilbert space, Dykstra converges to the Euclidean projection onto (\cap_j \mathcal{K}_j). Nonconvex cases require certificates that report failure modes (cycling, stagnation).
C.2.3 Augmented Lagrangian / ADMM feasibility enforcement
Problem. Solve[\min_H\ \mathcal{R}(H)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad C(H)=0,]where (C) may be nonlinear, and (\mathcal{R}) is a regularizer enforcing smoothness, sparsity, or proximity to a target.
Algorithm C.2.3 (ADMM on split constraints).
Input:
Split variables: enforce z = H, constraint C(z)=0
Penalty parameter ρ>0, iterations T
Output:
H_feas, certificate Cert
Procedure:
Initialize H,z,u (scaled dual) ← 0
For t = 1..T:
// H-step: proximal on R + quadratic
H ← argmin_H R(H) + (ρ/2)‖H - z + u‖^2
// z-step: projection onto constraint manifold
z ← argmin_z 𝟙_{C(z)=0}(z) + (ρ/2)‖H - z + u‖^2
= Π_{C(z)=0}(H + u) // exact or approximate
// dual update
u ← u + (H - z)
Return H_feas ← z
Cert: primal residual ‖H-z‖, constraint residual ‖C(z)‖, dual residual, hash.
Complexity.
