Dominated by the two subproblems per iteration: prox for (R) and projection onto (C(z)=0).
If both are FFT/wavelet-structured: typically (O(T N\log N)).
Notes on robustness. Certificates must log (\rho), stopping rules, and the exact projection tolerance used in the (z)-step, because feasibility hinges on it.
C.2.4 Feasibility repair transforms with certification
Problem. When exact feasibility is expensive, apply a repair transform (\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{rep}}) that decreases constraint residual and provides an auditable trail.
Algorithm C.2.4 (Residual-descent repair with projection).
Input:
H0, constraint residual function r(H)=‖C(H)‖
Step schedule α_t, max iterations T
Optional linearized projector Π_lin around current iterate
Output:
H_rep, certificate Cert
Procedure:
H ← H0
For t = 1..T:
g ← ∇(½‖C(H)‖^2) // via adjoint/Jacobian: g = J_C(H)^* C(H)
H ← H - α_t g
If Π_lin available: H ← Π_lin(H)
If ‖C(H)‖ ≤ tol: break
Cert: residual trace {‖C(H_t)‖}, step sizes, linearization logs, final hash.
Complexity.
Per iteration: one evaluation of (C(H)) and one adjoint-Jacobian application (J_C(H)^*), cost depends on constraint structure; sparse local constraints often yield (O(N)) or (O(N\log N)).
C.3 Transform optimization algorithms (C.3.1–C.3.4)
C.3.1 Adjoint-based gradient optimization of parameterized transforms
Problem. Let (T_\theta) be a differentiable family of admissible transforms on (\mathfrak{H}N). Optimize:[\min\theta\ \mathcal{C}(\theta) := \mathcal{L}(T_\theta(H_{\mathrm{in}}), H_{\mathrm{target}}) + \lambda,\mathcal{R}(\theta)\quad\text{s.t. corridor/feasibility constraints.}]
Algorithm C.3.1 (Adjoint gradient descent with corridor checks).
Input:
H_in, H_target
Transform family T_θ, and adjoint sensitivities to compute ∇_θ 𝓛(T_θ(H_in),H_target)
Step schedule η_t, iterations T
Constraint checker CheckAdmissible(θ), corridor checker CheckCorridor(H,Ω)
Output:
θ_hat, certificate Cert
Procedure:
θ ← θ0
For t = 1..T:
H_out ← T_θ(H_in)
if not CheckCorridor(H_out, Ω): θ ← RepairParams(θ); continue
g ← ∇_θ [ 𝓛(H_out,H_target) + λ𝓡(θ) ] // computed via adjoint
θ ← θ - η_t g
if not CheckAdmissible(θ): θ ← ProjectParamsToAdmissible(θ)
Cert: objective trace, corridor violations, admissibility repairs, final θ hash.
Complexity.
Per iteration: one forward application (T_\theta(H_{\mathrm{in}})) plus one adjoint pass (often comparable cost).
Total: (O(T \cdot (\mathrm{CostForward} + \mathrm{CostAdjoint}))).
C.3.2 Projected proximal optimization for composite costs
Problem. Optimize over (T) or (\theta) with composite cost (\mathcal{C}=\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{smooth}}+\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{nonsmooth}}) and hard admissibility set (\Theta_{\mathrm{adm}}).
Algorithm C.3.2 (Projected proximal gradient).
Input:
θ0, step τ, iterations T
smooth gradient ∇C_smooth(θ)
prox_{τ C_nonsmooth}(·)
projector Π_Θadm(·)
Output:
θ_hat, certificate Cert
Procedure:
θ ← θ0
For t = 1..T:
θ ← θ - τ ∇C_smooth(θ)
θ ← prox_{τ C_nonsmooth}(θ)
θ ← Π_Θadm(θ)
Cert: stationarity metrics, projection residuals, hashes.
Complexity.
Per iteration: gradient evaluation + prox + projection. If all are cheap relative to transform execution, this is dominated by whatever operator applications appear inside (\nabla C_{\mathrm{smooth}}).
C.3.3 Alternating minimization over program structure and parameters
Problem. Let a transform be expressed as a structured program:[T ;=; T_{\theta_L}^{(L)}\circ \cdots \circ T_{\theta_1}^{(1)},]where the structure (which primitives/gates appear) and parameters (\theta_i) are both decision variables.
Algorithm C.3.3 (Structure–parameter alternating descent).
Input:
Initial program structure P = (op_1,...,op_L), parameters θ
Rewrite rule set Rewrite(·) producing candidate structures
Parameter optimizer ParamOpt(·) (e.g., C.3.1 on fixed structure)
Iterations K
Output:
Best program (P*,θ*), certificate Cert
Procedure:
(P_best,θ_best) ← (P,θ)
For k = 1..K:
// 1) Structure step
Candidates ← Rewrite(P_best) // local rewrites, swaps, merges
Choose P_candidate minimizing estimated lower bound + corridor heuristics
// 2) Parameter step
θ_candidate ← ParamOpt(P_candidate, θ_init=θ_best)
// 3) Accept/reject by true cost + corridor
if FeasibleAndBetter(P_candidate,θ_candidate):
(P_best,θ_best) ← (P_candidate,θ_candidate)
Cert: rewrite log, acceptance decisions, corridor checks, hashes.
Complexity.
Let (R) be number of rewrite candidates evaluated per iteration; parameter optimization uses (T_p) inner iterations; cost is roughly[O!\big(K\cdot (R\cdot \mathrm{EvalHeuristic} + T_p\cdot \mathrm{CostForward/Adjoint})\big).]
Memory depends on caching partial evaluations of program prefixes/suffixes.
C.3.4 Multi-objective Pareto optimization with corridor constraints
Problem. Minimize vector cost ((\mathcal{C}_1,\dots,\mathcal{C}_q)) under corridor budgets and feasibility.
Algorithm C.3.4 (ε-constraint Pareto sweep).
Input:
Objectives C_1,...,C_q; primary objective C_1
Bounds ε_2,...,ε_q defining feasible objective region
Solver SolveSingleObjective(·) (e.g., C.3.2)
Sweep schedule over ε vectors
Output:
Pareto set 𝒫, certificate Cert
Procedure:
𝒫 ← ∅
For each ε in SweepGrid:
Solve: minimize C_1(θ) s.t. C_i(θ) ≤ ε_i for i=2..q and θ ∈ Θ_adm
If feasible:
Add solution to 𝒫; store certificates
Cert: ε-grid definition, per-solve logs, dominance checks, hashes for all members.
Complexity.
If (S) ε-points are evaluated and each solve costs (K), total (O(SK)).
Provides an auditable Pareto frontier with explicit feasibility logs.
C.4 Gate-program search algorithms (C.4.1–C.4.4)
Let (\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{gate}}={g_1,\dots,g_M}) be a finite gate library, and a program be a sequence (P=(g{i_1},\dots,g_{i_L})) with composed transform (T(P)=g_{i_L}\circ\cdots\circ g_{i_1}). A target may be a transform (T_\star) or an input-output specification ((H_{\mathrm{in}},H_{\mathrm{target}})). Costs include runtime, length, and corridor penalties.
C.4.1 A* search on the gate graph with admissibility heuristics
Algorithm C.4.1 (A gate-program search).*
Input:
Gate set {g_j}, cost per gate c(g_j) ≥ 0
Start transform T0 = Identity
Goal predicate Goal(T): true if Dist(T, T*) ≤ tol and Admissible(T)
Heuristic h(T): lower bound on remaining cost (corridor-aware)
Max expansions B
Output:
Program P*, certificate Cert
Procedure:
PriorityQueue Q keyed by f = g_cost + h
Insert node (T0, P=[]) with g_cost=0
While Q not empty and expansions < B:
Pop node with minimal f: (T,P,g_cost)
If Goal(T): return (P, certificate)
For each gate g_j:
T' ← g_j ∘ T
If not CorridorPass(T'): continue
g_cost' ← g_cost + c(g_j)
Push (T', P+[g_j], g_cost') with f' = g_cost' + h(T')
Return failure certificate (best-so-far, logs)
Complexity.
Worst-case (O(M^L)) expansions; with A* and good heuristics, expansions reduce to near-optimal.
Each expansion requires composing transforms or applying them to a test set; practical cost dominated by evaluation ( \mathrm{Eval}(T') ).
C.4.2 Beam search with canonicalization and pruning
Algorithm C.4.2 (Beam search).
Input:
Beam width W, depth limit L_max
Scoring score(T,P) = estimated total cost + corridor penalties
Canonicalization CanonProg(P) for program normal forms
Output:
Best program P*, certificate Cert
Procedure:
Beam ← {(T0, P=[])}
For depth = 1..L_max:
Candidates ← ∅
For each (T,P) in Beam:
For each gate g:
P' ← CanonProg(P+[g])
T' ← Compose(T,g)
If CorridorFail(T'): continue
Add (T',P') to Candidates
Beam ← select W elements of Candidates with smallest score
If any Goal(T) in Beam: return best Goal element
Return best Beam element with certificate
Complexity.
Per depth: (O(W\cdot M)) candidate expansions; total (O(L_{\max}WM)).
Canonicalization cost depends on rewrite engine; typically (O(L)) per candidate.
C.4.3 Meet-in-the-middle with hashing of partial compositions
Algorithm C.4.3 (Bidirectional meet-in-the-middle).
Input:
Depth split L = L1 + L2
Gate set {g_j} with inverses available (or approximate inverses)
Target transform T*
Output:
Program P*, certificate Cert
Procedure:
// Forward table
Table F: map Hash(T) → best program P achieving T with length ≤ L1
Enumerate all programs P_f of length ≤ L1:
T_f ← T(P_f)
If CorridorFail(T_f): continue
Store if better than existing entry for Hash(T_f)
// Backward table using inverses
Table B: map Hash(T) → best program P achieving T with length ≤ L2
Enumerate all programs P_b of length ≤ L2:
T_b ← T(P_b)
If CorridorFail(T_b): continue
Store in B
// Match: T_b ∘ T_f ≈ T*
For each entry (T_f,P_f) in F:
Need T_b ≈ T* ∘ (T_f)^{-1}
Query B by hash buckets / nearest-neighbor in transform metric
If match found:
P* ← P_b ∘ P_f
Verify exact corridor + goal; return with certificate
Return failure certificate with best approximate matches
Complexity.
Time: (O(M^{L1} + M^{L2} + \text{matching})); typically choose (L1\approx L2\approx L/2) yielding (O(M^{L/2})) scaling.
Memory: (O(M^{L/2})) to store hash tables.
Requires robust hashing / bucketing under approximate equality; certificates must include match tolerances and verification replay steps.
C.4.4 Proof-guided pruning and certificate-driven search
Goal. Reduce search by using invariants, corridor budgets, and admissibility theorems as pruning lemmas. The framework supports explicit corridor thresholds and audit logs; e.g., measurable defect thresholds and lift-stability checks are treated as admissibility gates.
Algorithm C.4.4 (Certificate-guided branch-and-bound).
Input:
Gate set {g_j}, target T*
Bounding function LB(node) giving provable lower bound on remaining cost
Pruning predicates:
- InvariantMismatch(T) (provable impossibility under Inv)
- CorridorViolation(T) (budget exceeded)
- StabilityBoundFail(T) (condition number bounds exceeded)
Search budget B
Output:
Best feasible program P*, certificate Cert
Procedure:
Best ← ∞, P* ← None
DFS/BFS with priority on smallest LB:
pop node (T,P)
if LB(T,P) ≥ Best: prune
if InvariantMismatch(T): prune
if CorridorViolation(T): prune
if Goal(T): Best ← Cost(P); P* ← P; store proof logs
else expand by appending gates, but only those whose local certificates pass
Return P* with full certificate:
includes all pruning reasons used, bounds, invariant checks, and replay hashes.
Complexity.
Worst-case still exponential, but empirical reductions can be dramatic when invariants and corridor bounds are strong.
Certificate size: (O(#\text{expanded nodes})) unless compressed; deterministic hashing and canonical serialization are required for reproducibility.
Appendix C — Algorithm Catalog (Pseudocode + Complexity) (4×4)
Appendix D — Verification & Testing Handbook (4×4)
This appendix specifies the mandatory verification, testing, benchmarking, and certification standards for all publication-grade artifacts (states, transforms, invariants, reconstructions, and programs) defined in the manuscript, and operationalizes the determinism, verification, and reproducibility doctrines.
D.1 Unit testing standards (D.1.1–D.1.4)
D.1.1 Unit test scope, granularity, and coverage obligations
(A) Unit under test (UUT) definition.A unit is the smallest executable component that claims a stable semantic contract under the manuscript’s typing rules. A unit may be:
a pure function (e.g., invariant evaluator, serialization encoder, canonicalizer),
a kernel (e.g., discrete operator, projection step, sampling functional),
a method implementing a typed operator interface (e.g., apply, adjoint, serialize, check),
a deterministic composition wrapper whose semantics are fixed by a schema (e.g., a program node evaluation rule).
(B) Contract-first unit tests.Every unit test MUST reference an explicit contract consisting of:
Type contract: declared domain/codomain and required regularity rung (continuous or discrete type tags).
Determinism contract: identical inputs + identical configuration ⇒ identical outputs and identical artifact IDs.
Numerical contract: equality notion (exact, a.e., gauge-equivalent, or within tolerance) and the metric/norm used.
Side-effect contract: allowed effects (none by default), including explicit prohibition of hidden randomness, system time, network I/O, and nondeterministic parallel reduction.
(C) Coverage obligations by artifact class.Unit tests MUST cover, at minimum, the following classes and their boundary behaviors:
State objects: storage layout invariants, norm/inner product evaluation, zero-state handling, canonicalization behavior, serialization round-trips.
Constraint operators: residual evaluation correctness, threshold logic, localized residual extraction, conditioning diagnostics and failure modes.
Invariant suite components: correctness of estimator outputs, invariance under declared gauge actions, and sane ranges (e.g., nonnegativity where applicable).
Transform primitives and stages: typing checks, pre/post feasibility residuals, invariant drift computation, and deterministic parameter canonicalization.
Reconstruction operators: observation restriction correctness, solver determinism, and reconstruction-residual reporting consistency.
(D) Boundary and degenerate cases are mandatory.Each unit test module MUST include explicit tests for:
degenerate or near-degenerate inputs (e.g., zero norm, near-zero denominators, empty masks, fully masked observations),
tie-boundary scenarios (rank selections, top-K, threshold equality, discrete ordering ties),
domain boundary conditions (grid boundary stencils, periodic padding, trace extraction conventions),
numerical extremes (overflow/underflow guards, NaN/Inf rejection).
D.1.2 Determinism and numeric correctness standards for unit tests
(A) Deterministic execution requirement.A unit test is valid only if it is fully deterministic under fixed:
code version + dependency versions,
precision policy (float32/float64/mixed),
deterministic reduction policy (fixed summation order),
seed lineage (including “no randomness” where required).
Any nondeterministic pass/fail outcome invalidates the test suite until determinism is restored.
(B) Tolerance doctrine (ledger form).When a test asserts approximate equality, it MUST declare a tolerance ledger:[\tau_{\mathrm{total}} = \tau_{\mathrm{float}}+\tau_{\mathrm{disc}}+\tau_{\mathrm{est}}+\tau_{\mathrm{algo}},]where:
(\tau_{\mathrm{float}}) bounds floating-point rounding and reduction order,
(\tau_{\mathrm{disc}}) bounds discretization/truncation mismatch,
(\tau_{\mathrm{est}}) bounds estimator bias/variance for computed statistics,
(\tau_{\mathrm{algo}}) bounds algorithmic approximation (iteration stopping, solver tolerance, truncated series).
Each component MUST be justified by a stored witness (analytic bound, calibration run, or gold-state envelope) and must be versioned.
(C) Absolute/relative mixed tolerance standard.For scalar comparisons ((a,b)), use:[|a-b| \le \tau_{\mathrm{abs}} + \tau_{\mathrm{rel}}(|a|+|b|),]with fixed ((\tau_{\mathrm{abs}},\tau_{\mathrm{rel}})) recorded in the suite configuration. For vector/tensor comparisons, the norm must be declared (default: induced (\ell^2) or the typed state norm).
(D) Fail-closed numeric safety.Unit tests MUST assert:
NaN/Inf prohibition in all serialized numeric fields,
explicit handling for undefined operations (e.g., reject or map to a declared null-invariant profile),
stable behavior under precision changes within declared envelopes (if multi-precision support is claimed).
D.1.3 Gold vectors, regression anchors, and minimal reproducers
(A) Gold vectors and gold states.A gold vector is a canonical, immutable test input with:
canonical serialization bytes,
content hash ID,
full type metadata (basis, ((N,P,\epsilon)), constraint IDs, suite IDs, precision policy).
Gold states MUST cover the diversity regime required by the manuscript’s verification doctrine (smooth, sparse/interface, high-frequency, near-degenerate, boundary-dominant, adversarial). All gold state updates are versioned and never overwrite prior versions.
(B) Expected outputs as content-addressed artifacts.Unit tests MUST validate outputs via one of:
exact content hash match (preferred for deterministic pure functions),
equality within tolerance in a declared metric with stored tolerance ledger,
equivalence under canonicalization/gauge (then compare canonical hashes).
(C) Minimal reproducer requirements.Every failing unit test MUST output a minimal reproducer bundle containing:
input artifact IDs and serialized inputs,
unit/version identifiers,
configuration (precision policy, determinism policy, tolerance ledger),
the exact failing predicate, threshold, and observed value,
a deterministic seed lineage record (even when “no randomness”: record seed as null/zero).
(D) Reproducer stability across environments.A reproducer is valid only if it replays identically under the pinned environment manifest used by CI. Any environment drift requires either:
a pinned migration with equivalence tests and bounded envelopes, or
explicit revocation of the prior reproducer’s portability scope.
D.1.4 CI gating, structure, and failure reporting for unit tests
(A) Gating policy.
Unit tests are hard gates for publication-grade builds.
Any failure blocks release unless resolved by (i) bug fix, or (ii) explicit version bump plus validated migration.
(B) Runtime budget.Unit tests MUST be fast and isolated:
no external network or service dependencies,
bounded runtime per module, with deterministic timeouts,
bounded memory use consistent with minimal reproducibility.
Extended correctness validation belongs in property tests or benchmarks; unit tests must remain minimal and surgical.
(C) Failure taxonomy and triage fields.Every failure report MUST classify into:
typing/domain violation,
determinism violation,
tolerance envelope violation,
serialization/hash mismatch,
numerical instability (NaN/Inf/overflow),
boundary condition mismatch,
regression (gold mismatch) with version scope.
(D) Change impact discipline.If a unit test failure arises from an intentional semantic change, the change is admissible only if:
the affected mathematical definition is versioned,
all dependent certificates are regenerated,
all downstream suites re-pass under the new versions,
a migration note exists establishing equivalence or controlled drift on declared domains.
D.2 Property testing standards (D.2.1–D.2.4)
D.2.1 Generator standards and admissible sampling of objects
(A) Deterministic generators as first-class artifacts.Every property test MUST be driven by generators that are deterministic under a seed and serialize into a generator artifact ID. A generator specifies:
distribution family (or deterministic enumeration policy),
parameter bounds and admissible domain predicates,
coverage targets (edge-case weights),
shrink strategy (deterministic).
(B) Generators for states.State generators MUST support:
random coefficient sampling in a declared basis with deterministic indexing,
optional band-limiting and sparsity controls,
feasibility enforcement via deterministic projection/repair,
canonicalization to ensure gauge-stable identity.
The generator MUST produce typed states (H^{(N,P,\epsilon)}) with attached residual and invariant baselines for immediate checks.
(C) Generators for transforms and programs.Transform generators MUST support:
admissible primitive gates with parameter domains,
composition into typed program graphs with deterministic ordering,
optional inclusion of repair/canonicalization nodes at declared checkpoints,
explicit restriction to admissible corridors unless the test targets failure detection.
(D) Generators for observers and reconstructions.Observer generators MUST deterministically generate:
mask families and sampling designs,
noise models (if included) with seeded draws,
reconstruction operator configurations (solver IDs, tolerances) as artifacts.
All observer/reconstruction objects must be serializable and content-addressed.
D.2.2 Property classes and required metamorphic laws
(A) Typing and closure properties.For any generated object instance, the following properties MUST hold (or be expected to fail when explicitly testing rejection paths):
well-typedness of each operation and intermediate node,
domain predicate propagation correctness,
closure of feasible sets under declared admissible transforms (hard) or corridor invariance (soft).
(B) Determinism and idempotence properties.Mandatory properties:
serialization determinism: (\mathrm{Ser}(A)) identical across reruns,
canonicalization idempotence: (\mathrm{Can}(\mathrm{Can}(H))=\mathrm{Can}(H)),
canon-hash stability under gauge actions: (\mathrm{ID}(g\cdot H)=\mathrm{ID}(H)) where (g) is declared gauge.
(C) Operator and reconstruction metamorphic properties.Where applicable, property suites MUST include:
identity neutrality: (T\circ \mathrm{Id} \equiv T) and (\mathrm{Id}\circ T \equiv T),
approximate associativity domain correctness for partial operators (same maximal domain),
adjointness consistency for linear operators: inner-product identity holds within tolerance,
reconstruction consistency: (R_A(\mathcal{O}_A(H))) satisfies declared reconstruction envelope on the test domain,
corridor drift bounds: (|\mathrm{Inv}(T(H))-\mathrm{Inv}(H)|\preceq \delta) within the test scope.
(D) Non-commutativity and rewrite safety properties.If a rewrite system is present, property tests MUST validate:
rewrite preserves typing and determinism,
rewrite preserves corridor bounds (or updates budgets conservatively and records the update),
commutation defects are detected and bounded where reordering is permitted,
reordering is rejected where commutator diagnostics exceed declared thresholds.
D.2.3 Shrinking, counterexample capture, and no-go witness discipline
(A) Deterministic shrinking.Every property failure MUST be shrinkable to a minimal counterexample under a deterministic shrinking strategy. Shrinking must preserve:
type validity up to the point of failure,
reproducibility (same seed ⇒ same shrunk result),
complete provenance (generator ID, shrink steps, final artifact IDs).
(B) Counterexample artifact bundle.The minimal counterexample bundle MUST include:
input state ID(s), transform/program ID(s), observer/reconstructor ID(s),
pre/post invariants, residuals, and reconstruction residuals (if relevant),
exact failing property statement and threshold(s),
determinism configuration (precision policy, reduction order),
environment manifest ID.
(C) No-go witnesses as regression assets.When a failure demonstrates a structural impossibility (e.g., observational null mode, non-identifiability, irreducible aliasing), the counterexample is promoted to a no-go witness and added to a dedicated regression suite that must remain failing under unchanged observer/constraint configurations.
(D) Scope labeling.All counterexamples MUST explicitly label scope:
the declared domain (resolution, regularity, corridor budgets),
the object versions (constraints, invariants, canonicalizers),
the observation model (mask family, sampling design, noise).
Counterexamples are invalid evidence outside their labeled scope.
D.2.4 Statistical policy, CI integration, and flakiness prohibition
(A) Two-tier execution policy.
CI tier: bounded sample counts and bounded runtime, targeting broad coverage with strict determinism.
Extended tier: nightly or release-candidate tier with increased sample counts, adversarial seeds, and larger resolution coverage.
(B) Fixed sample sizes and deterministic seed schedules.Property tests MUST not rely on “run until failure” randomness. They MUST use:
deterministic seed sequences derived from suite IDs and run IDs,
fixed sample counts per property,
fixed time budgets with deterministic cutoffs.
(C) Fail-closed behavior for guard invariants.If a guard invariant becomes indeterminate (domain violation, numeric instability, tie boundary outside margin), the property test MUST treat this as a failure unless the suite explicitly defines a deterministic repair/robustification step that remains within the invariant suite ID.
(D) Flakiness is a correctness failure.Any nondeterministic property outcome is classified as a determinism defect and blocks publication-grade claims until resolved.
D.3 Benchmark standards (D.3.1–D.3.4)
D.3.1 Benchmark taxonomy and admissible benchmark objects
(A) Benchmark classes.
Microbenchmarks: kernel-level costs (FFT, projection step, invariant estimator).
Mesobenchmarks: single stage execution (one transform stage with checks).
Macrobenchmarks: full pipelines (observe → reconstruct → canonicalize → certify).
Stress benchmarks: performance under controlled noise/truncation/adversarial inputs.
(B) Correctness-gated benchmarks.A benchmark is admissible only if it is paired with correctness checks:
constraint residual checks pre/post,
invariant sanity checks,
deterministic hash stability checks for outputs,
reconstruction checks when measuring reconstruction systems.
(C) Benchmark artifacts are typed and content-addressed.Each benchmark run MUST reference:
input artifact IDs (gold states or generator IDs),
program/transform IDs,
suite IDs (invariants, corridor policies),
environment manifest ID.
(D) Benchmark scope constraints.Benchmarks must declare:
resolution ladder ((N,P,\epsilon)) coverage,
which metrics are measured (time, memory, ops),
what compute backend is used (CPU/GPU) and determinism policy.
D.3.2 Methodology: measurement discipline and reproducibility
(A) Environment pinning is mandatory.Benchmarks MUST run under a pinned environment manifest specifying:
OS/toolchain/library versions,
deterministic settings (thread counts, deterministic kernel modes),
precision policies and rounding conventions.
(B) Deterministic workload construction.Benchmark workloads must be fully deterministic:
fixed input artifacts or fixed generator seeds,
fixed execution ordering,
fixed warmup policy and fixed iteration counts.
(C) Timing methodology.Benchmarks MUST report both:
deterministic operation counts where meaningful (preferred for comparability),
wall-time measurements under controlled settings, reported with a deterministic summary statistic and dispersion (min/median/max or fixed quantiles) derived from fixed repeats.
(D) Memory methodology.Memory reporting MUST include:
peak resident set size (or equivalent deterministic proxy),
peak live buffer bytes under declared allocation model,
serialization/log sizes for certificates and monitoring.
D.3.3 Metrics, reporting format, and accuracy–cost trade-offs
(A) Mandatory metrics per benchmark run.
runtime (or op count) per stage and per pipeline,
memory footprint (peak and steady),
number of iterations and solver convergence flags (if applicable),
conditioning diagnostics (where relevant),
correctness deltas (residuals, invariant drift, reconstruction residual).
(B) Scaling laws and parametric reporting.Benchmarks MUST include scaling curves as functions of:
(N) (spatial resolution),
(P) (parameter resolution),
ladder size (K) (multi-scale invariants),
mask sparsity/geometry, and constraint complexity.
(C) Pareto reporting standard.When alternatives exist (solver choice, basis choice, thresholding strategy), benchmark reports MUST include Pareto frontiers over:
accuracy (reconstruction error, drift),
stability (conditioning, Lipschitz proxy),
cost (time, memory).
(D) Benchmark report schema (canonical).Reports MUST serialize deterministically and include:
IDs (input, program, suite, environment),
metric vectors,
correctness gate results,
deterministic links to raw logs.
D.3.4 Performance regression policy and acceptance thresholds
(A) Baseline management.Every benchmark suite MUST maintain baselines as content-addressed reports. Any baseline update requires:
explicit version bump,
explanation of intended change,
revalidation of correctness gates.
(B) Regression thresholds.Thresholds MUST be explicit and deterministic:
time/op count increase caps,
memory increase caps,
“no-worse” constraints on key correctness metrics (residuals, drift, reconstruction error).
(C) Triage discipline.Regression failures MUST be accompanied by:
minimal reproduction manifest,
comparison to last baseline (IDs + delta),
classification (algorithmic change vs environment drift vs nondeterminism).
(D) Fail-closed for determinism.Any benchmark whose outputs or IDs are not stable across reruns under the same manifest is invalid and blocks publication-grade claims.
D.4 Certificate standards (D.4.1–D.4.4)
D.4.1 Certificate semantics, scope, and admissibility classes
(A) Certificate definition.A certificate is a structured, content-addressed evidence artifact that attests to one or more claims about an object (state, transform, program, observer, reconstruction, invariant suite) under explicitly declared scope.
(B) Claim classes.
Typing certificate: domain/codomain, regularity requirements, and domain predicates.
Feasibility certificate: constraint residuals and thresholds, including localized residuals where defined.
Invariant certificate: invariant suite ID, computed invariant vector, drift budgets, pass/fail outcomes.
Stability certificate: operator norm bounds, Lipschitz bounds, conditioning diagnostics, and scope of validity.
Reconstruction certificate: reconstruction residuals, envelope bounds, observer IDs and mask family IDs.
Determinism certificate: serialization determinism, hash stability, seed lineage and determinism settings.
Benchmark certificate: performance metrics with correctness gates under a pinned environment.
(C) Scope is mandatory.Every certificate MUST declare:
versions of all dependencies (constraints, invariants, canonicalizers, solvers),
resolution and discretization parameters,
precision policy and determinism policy,
the domain of validity (test set, analytic assumptions, or both).
(D) Admissibility level labels.Certificates MUST label whether the claim is:
analytic (proof-based under stated assumptions),
verified computational (deterministic evaluation with exact IDs),
empirical (tested on a declared finite suite, with explicit coverage limits).
D.4.2 Canonical serialization, hashing, and signature requirements
(A) Canonical serialization requirement.Every certificate MUST serialize to canonical bytes under:
deterministic ordering of fields,
fixed numeric encoding and rounding policy,
stable text normalization,
prohibition of NaNs and platform-dependent encodings.
(B) Content hash identity.A certificate’s identity is:[\mathrm{CID} := \mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(\mathrm{Cert})),]and MUST be referenced by all downstream reports. Any certificate without (\mathrm{CID}) is non-admissible.
(C) Linkage fields.Certificates MUST include content-hash links to:
subject artifact IDs (state ID, program ID, suite IDs),
input artifacts used to compute the claim (gold states, generator IDs),
environment manifest ID,
logs and intermediate evidence artifacts.
(D) Optional signatures.If cryptographic signing is used, signatures MUST sign the canonical bytes (\mathrm{Ser}(\mathrm{Cert})) and include key identifiers. Signature validity does not replace content-addressing; it augments it.
D.4.3 Evidence completeness and code-to-math traceability
(A) Evidence completeness standard.A certificate is complete only if it contains sufficient information to reproduce the claim without external implicit knowledge:
exact inputs (by hash),
exact code/configuration (by manifest),
exact thresholds and tolerance ledgers,
deterministic seeds and sampling designs.
(B) Traceability mapping requirement.Each certificate field MUST map to:
a manuscript definition (identifier) or theorem (identifier) when the field corresponds to a formal quantity,
a verification test (identifier) when the field is empirically certified,
an implementation function (identifier) for audit and refactor safety.
(C) Analytic-empirical split discipline.If a claim mixes analytic bounds and empirical verification, the certificate MUST separate:
the analytic statement and assumptions,
the empirical coverage suite and measured results,
the derived conservative bound used operationally.
(D) Change impact coupling.Any change to a mathematical definition, invariant, constraint, solver semantics, or serialization schema MUST invalidate affected certificates by construction and require regeneration under new IDs.
D.4.4 Certificate lifecycle: issuance, renewal, revocation, and migration
(A) Issuance requirements.A certificate may be issued only when:
all required unit and property tests pass under the pinned environment,
all required benchmarks (if any) pass correctness gates,
determinism checks pass for the relevant artifact classes,
the certificate is serialized, hashed, and stored immutably.
(B) Renewal requirements.Certificates MUST be renewed when any dependency version changes, including:
constraint operator version,
invariant suite version,
canonicalization policy,
solver tolerance policy,
environment manifest.
Renewal produces a new (\mathrm{CID}); prior certificates remain immutable.
(C) Revocation requirements.A certificate MUST be revoked (marked invalid) when:
a counterexample is discovered within its declared scope,
determinism is found violated under the declared manifest,
a claimed bound is falsified by verified computation.
Revocation records MUST include the revoking witness artifact IDs and scope details.
(D) Migration discipline.When migrating certificates across versions, migration is admissible only with:
explicit migration theorem or equivalence argument (analytic or tested),
re-execution of dependent verification suites,
conservative envelope updates where equivalence is approximate,
explicit statement of what claims remain valid and what scope changed.
Appendix D — Verification & Testing Handbook (4×4)
Appendix E — Data Structures Reference (4×4)
This appendix defines the canonical in-memory and serialized representations used by the holographic kernel for finite models of holographic states and transforms. Each structure is specified by: (i) a typed data model, (ii) deterministic indexing and ordering rules, (iii) asymptotic memory/time costs for core operations, and (iv) stability and corridor-integration obligations.
Throughout, let (\mathbb{K}\in{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C}}). A finite holographic state is represented as a rank-4 tensor[X \in \mathbb{K}^{N\times N\times N\times P},]where ((N,N,N)) are spatial degrees of freedom (or three primary axes) and (P) is the channel/parameter axis (feature, wavelength, phase plane, time-slice, basis band, or any explicitly typed parameter dimension). The linear index set is[\mathcal{I}_{N,P} ;=; [N]\times[N]\times[N]\times[P],]with canonical element ordering lexicographic in ((x,y,z,p)) unless otherwise declared by a layout descriptor.
A transform (T) is represented either as:
an explicit operator (dense/sparse matrix, convolution kernel, spectral multiplier),
a program (graph of gates),
or an implicit solver (defined by an optimization problem).
All structures below must carry a Type Header with:
basis ID and truncation ID,
constraint family ID,
invariant suite ID,
corridor budget ID,
canonical serialization schema ID,
numeric policy ID (precision, rounding, reduction determinism).
E.1 Dense structures (E.1.1–E.1.4)
E.1.1 Dense tensor model
E.1.1.1 Canonical dense state object
A dense state is a tuple[\mathrm{DenseField} ;=; (N,P,\mathrm{dtype},\mathrm{layout},\mathrm{buf},\mathrm{meta}),]where:
(N\in\mathbb{Z}{\ge 1}), (P\in\mathbb{Z}{\ge 1}),
(\mathrm{dtype}\in{\text{fp32},\text{fp64},\text{complex64},\text{complex128}}) (or fixed-point types if specified),
(\mathrm{layout}) specifies the stride order and contiguity (see E.1.2),
(\mathrm{buf}) is a contiguous memory buffer of length (N^3P) values (or (2N^3P) for split real/imag representation),
(\mathrm{meta}) is the Type Header plus optional cached statistics (norms, band energies) with explicit invalidation rules.
The canonical indexing function is defined by a stride vector (s=(s_x,s_y,s_z,s_p)) and base offset (o):[\mathrm{idx}(x,y,z,p);=;o + s_x x + s_y y + s_z z + s_p p,]with the requirement that (\mathrm{idx}) is a bijection from (\mathcal{I}_{N,P}) to ([N^3P]).
E.1.1.2 Complex representation
If (\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{C}), the dense buffer must choose exactly one of:
Interleaved complex: (\mathrm{buf}[i]\in\mathbb{C}) (native complex type),
Split real/imag: (\mathrm{buf}\Re,\mathrm{buf}\Im\in\mathbb{R}) with identical layout,
Packed AoS: (\mathrm{buf}[2i],\mathrm{buf}[2i+1]) for real/imag, with explicit alignment guarantees.
The complex format is part of (\mathrm{meta}) and is hash-committed.
E.1.1.3 Dense operator representation
A dense operator acting on (\mathrm{DenseField}) is represented by one of:
explicit dense matrix (A\in\mathbb{K}^{(N^3P)\times(N^3P)}) (rare; only for small problems),
structured operator descriptor (FFT diagonal multiplier, separable operator, stencil),
program IR (sequence/graph of primitive kernels).
Dense matrices must be avoided unless (N^3P) is small; otherwise structured descriptors are required.
E.1.1.4 Dense metadata invariants
DenseField may cache derived metadata:
(|X|_2) (energy/norm),
band energies (E_b(X)),
constraint residual norms (|C_N(X)|),
invariant vector (\mathrm{Inv}_N(X)).
Caching is valid only if:
caches are recomputed deterministically,
caches are invalidated on every mutation,
caches do not enter canonical serialization unless explicitly declared as part of the state identity.
E.1.2 Memory layouts
E.1.2.1 Layout descriptor
A layout is specified by[\mathrm{layout} = (\mathrm{order},\mathrm{strides},\mathrm{tile},\mathrm{align}),]where:
(\mathrm{order}) is a permutation of axes ({x,y,z,p}) defining contiguity,
(\mathrm{strides}) are implied by order and element size unless overridden,
(\mathrm{tile}) specifies optional blocked tiling (see E.1.2.3),
(\mathrm{align}) specifies alignment in bytes for base pointer and inner strides.
Two canonical dense orders are permitted unless a chapter explicitly chooses otherwise:
Layout D-Pfast (channel-contiguous):[(x,y,z,p)\ \text{with } p \text{ fastest}.]Index map:[\mathrm{idx}(x,y,z,p)=(((xN+y)N+z)P+p).]
Layout D-Xfast (spatial-contiguous):[(p,x,y,z)\ \text{with } z \text{ fastest inside spatial blocks}.]Index map:[\mathrm{idx}(p,x,y,z)=(((pN+x)N+y)N+z).]
Any nonstandard layout must declare:
the precise stride vector,
the intended dominant kernel class (FFT vs per-voxel mixing),
and conversion costs (transposes) as part of the performance model.
E.1.2.2 AoS vs SoA for channels
Let (P) be channel count.
AoS channel pack: store (P) channels contiguously per voxel (good for per-voxel mixing).
SoA channel split: store each channel as an independent 3D field (good for per-channel FFT and streaming).
A layout must specify:
whether channels are stored as AoS or SoA,
the canonical mapping between AoS and SoA forms (deterministic reshape/transposition),
and whether conversion is permitted as an admissible transform (it is permitted only if accompanied by a certificate of invariance preservation and deterministic ordering).
E.1.2.3 Blocked tiling and cache locality
If tiling is enabled, define a tile size (B\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 1}) and represent the volume as[\left(\frac{N}{B}\right)\times\left(\frac{N}{B}\right)\times\left(\frac{N}{B}\right)\ \text{tiles},]with each tile containing a dense (B\times B\times B\times P) subarray stored contiguously.
Tiling must satisfy:
(B\mid N) (or declare padding policy),
canonical tile ordering lexicographic in tile coordinates ((i,j,k)),
canonical intra-tile ordering consistent with (\mathrm{order}).
E.1.2.4 Deterministic transposes and layout conversions
Any conversion between layouts is defined as a pure permutation operator (\mathcal{P}) on indices. The conversion algorithm must:
iterate indices in canonical order,
write into a fresh buffer (no aliasing),
be deterministic under fixed threading policy (single-threaded by default for verification),
produce a conversion certificate containing:
source/destination layout IDs,
hash of source and destination buffers,
invariants before/after (must match within tolerance).
E.1.3 Access patterns
E.1.3.1 Stencil neighborhood access
Stencil kernels access local neighborhoods:[(\mathcal{L}X)[x,y,z,p] = \sum_{(\Delta x,\Delta y,\Delta z)\in \mathcal{S}} w_{\Delta},X[x+\Delta x,y+\Delta y,z+\Delta z,p],]with stencil set (\mathcal{S}) and weights (w_{\Delta}). Boundary handling must be explicit (periodic, Dirichlet, Neumann, absorbing). Dense layouts should favor spatial contiguity for stencils; tiling is recommended.
E.1.3.2 Spectral/global access (FFT and diagonal multipliers)
FFT-based transforms apply:[X \xrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \widehat{X} \xrightarrow{\Lambda} \widehat{Y} \xrightarrow{\mathcal{F}^{-1}} Y,]typically independently per channel (p), or jointly with channel mixing in frequency domain. The data structure must declare:
whether FFT is per channel or batched,
frequency indexing conventions,
normalization convention (unitary vs nonunitary FFT),
deterministic FFT mode requirements for verification runs.
E.1.3.3 Per-voxel channel mixing (small dense linear algebra)
A common kernel is per-voxel mixing:[Y[x,y,z,:] = M[x,y,z];X[x,y,z,:],]where (M[x,y,z]\in\mathbb{K}^{P\times P}) is a small matrix (possibly constant over space). AoS (channel-contiguous) layouts minimize cache misses and maximize vectorization for this class.
E.1.3.4 Gather/scatter and masked operations
Masked observation or update uses:[Y = M\odot X,\quad M\in{0,1}^{N\times N\times N\times P},]or sparse masks acting on a subset of indices. Dense structures must provide:
fast contiguous iteration for full masks,
optional sparse mask acceleration (delegating to sparse structures in E.2).
E.1.4 Performance bounds
E.1.4.1 Memory cost
Dense field memory is:[\mathrm{Mem}(\mathrm{DenseField}) = N^3P\cdot \mathrm{bytes}(\mathrm{dtype})](or doubled for split real/imag).
For tiled layouts, additional overhead includes tile metadata (negligible compared to (N^3P) unless tiles are extremely small) and padding if (B\nmid N).
E.1.4.2 Time cost of core operations
One full pass read+write is (\Theta(N^3P)) element operations and is bandwidth-limited.
FFT per channel is (\Theta(P\cdot N^3\log N)) (3D FFT).
Stencil of width (|\mathcal{S}|) is (\Theta(|\mathcal{S}|\cdot N^3P)).
E.1.4.3 Determinism costs
Deterministic reductions require fixed summation order; parallel reductions are disallowed in verification mode unless the reduction tree is recorded and hash-committed. Deterministic FFT mode may cost additional runtime relative to nondeterministic high-performance kernels; the determinism mode is part of the environment manifest.
E.1.4.4 Dense failure modes
Dense failure modes include:
boundary handling mismatch (off-by-one, wrong padding),
hidden transposes breaking invariants (FFT normalization drift),
floating-point drift from nondeterministic reductions,
cache thrash in layouts mismatched to kernel locality.
Each failure mode must be detectable by unit and property tests (Appendix D), and by invariant drift monitors.
E.2 Sparse structures (E.2.1–E.2.4)
E.2.1 Sparse encodings (COO/CSR-like)
E.2.1.1 Sparse voxel–channel model
A sparse hologram is represented as nonzeros over spatial indices with dense channel vectors:[\mathrm{SparseField} = (\mathrm{coords},\mathrm{vals},N,P,\mathrm{format},\mathrm{meta}),]where:
(\mathrm{coords}) is an array of length (M) of integer triples ((x,y,z)\in[N]^3),
(\mathrm{vals}) is an array of shape (M\times P) (AoS for channels within each nonzero),
(\mathrm{format}\in{\mathrm{COO},\mathrm{CSR3},\mathrm{CSC3},\mathrm{Hash}}).
The canonical linear index for a voxel is[\ell(x,y,z)=xN^2+yN+z.]Canonical ordering of coordinates is ascending in (\ell); ties are forbidden (duplicate coordinates) unless explicitly represented by a deterministic reduction rule.
E.2.1.2 COO format
COO stores:
sorted (\mathrm{coords}) by (\ell),
corresponding (\mathrm{vals}),
optional (\mathrm{rowptr}) for faster range queries (if provided, it upgrades to CSR-like).
COO is admissible for:
construction,
streaming ingestion,
sparse mask operations.
COO is not preferred for repeated neighborhood queries unless augmented with indexing.
E.2.1.3 CSR-like format for 3D
CSR-like for 3D uses a two-level compression:
compress by (\ell) directly (monolithic CSR on flattened space),or
compress by a chosen major axis (e.g., (x)-slices), with per-slice CSR for ((y,z)).
Monolithic CSR fields:[\mathrm{rowptr}\in\mathbb{Z}^{(N^3+1)},\quad \mathrm{colind}\equiv \text{trivial},\quad \mathrm{vals}.]This is efficient for sparse matrix–vector style operators but may be heavy for very large (N) unless sparsity is high and index arrays compress well.
E.2.1.4 Hash sparse format
Hash sparse stores a map (\ell\mapsto v\in\mathbb{K}^P). It is admissible only when:
deterministic iteration order is enforced (keys must be sorted for serialization and for any reduction),
hashing is not used for order-sensitive computations in verification mode (hash maps are inherently nondeterministic unless iterated by sorted keys).
Hash sparse is used for dynamic updates, then converted to sorted COO/CSR for stable computation.
E.2.2 Block sparsity
E.2.2.1 Block partition
Choose block size (B) and partition voxels into blocks indexed by[(i,j,k)\in\left[\frac{N}{B}\right]^3,\qquad\text{block } (i,j,k)\text{ covers } x\in[iB,(i+1)B),\ \text{etc.}]A block key is the Morton code (Z-order) or lexicographic triple; deterministic ordering must be specified.
E.2.2.2 Block-sparse representation
A block sparse field is[\mathrm{BlockSparseField} = (\mathrm{blockKeys},\mathrm{blockVals},B,N,P,\mathrm{meta}),]where each block value is dense (B^3\times P) stored contiguously. Block keys are sorted in canonical order; duplicates are forbidden.
E.2.2.3 Complexity of block operations
Applying local transforms within blocks is dense and vectorizable:
per block cost (O(B^3P)) per pass,
total cost (O(MB^3P)) for (M) active blocks.
Neighborhood operations require accessing adjacent blocks; halo handling is reduced to block-neighbor halo exchange.
E.2.2.4 Block sparsity stability
Block sparsity preserves locality better than point sparsity. However, truncation introduces bias; therefore block activation/deactivation must be governed by:
a deterministic thresholding policy,
a certified truncation envelope (upper bound on dropped energy),
and corridor checks that detect drift induced by sparsification.
E.2.3 Multi-resolution sparsity
E.2.3.1 Pyramid model
A multi-resolution sparse field stores:
a dense coarse base (X^{(0)}) at resolution (N_0),
sparse residuals (R^{(\ell)}) at higher resolutions (N_\ell), (\ell=1,\dots,L).
Reconstruction is:[X^{(L)} = \mathrm{Up}(X^{(0)}) + \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} \mathrm{Up}{\ell}(R^{(\ell)}),]where (\mathrm{Up}\ell) are deterministic prolongation operators.
E.2.3.2 Wavelet coefficient tree
Alternatively, store wavelet coefficients in a tree with thresholding:[X ;\leftrightarrow; {c_{\ell,i}}{\ell,i},]and store only coefficients satisfying (|c{\ell,i}|\ge \tau_\ell). Thresholds (\tau_\ell) must be deterministic and recorded.
E.2.3.3 Indexing and serialization
Multi-resolution structures require:
level IDs,
per-level basis IDs,
deterministic ordering of coefficients by (level, index),
explicit encoding of absent coefficients (implicitly zero).
Serialization must commit to:
level count (L),
all thresholds (\tau_\ell),
prolongation/restriction operator IDs.
E.2.3.4 Truncation envelopes
A certified envelope for a thresholded wavelet representation is:[|X - \widehat{X}|2^2 = \sum{(\ell,i)\ \text{dropped}} |c_{\ell,i}|^2 ;\le; E_{\mathrm{drop}},]where (E_{\mathrm{drop}}) is computed exactly from dropped coefficients if available, or upper-bounded by conservative estimates when coefficients are not retained.
E.2.4 Stability implications
E.2.4.1 Sparse aliasing and non-unitarity
Sparse truncation breaks exact orthogonality/unitarity of many transforms. If (U) is unitary and (\Pi_s) a sparsifying projector, then (\Pi_s U) is not unitary; repeated application can drift invariants (energy, phase coherence). Sparse pipelines must therefore:
monitor energy drift,
enforce periodic reprojection to feasible sets,
limit iteration counts or apply dampening.
E.2.4.2 Conditioning under sparse observation
Sparse observation operators (O) can be ill-conditioned. Reconstruction stability requires either:
restricted isometry conditions on the effective sensing map,
explicit regularization,
or certified ambiguity reporting when identifiability fails.
E.2.4.3 Corridor integration for sparsity
Sparsity must be integrated into corridor governance with:
a sparsity budget (maximum support size or maximum block count),
a drop budget (E_{\mathrm{drop}}) (maximum allowable truncation energy),
a repair policy that increases resolution/support when drift exceeds thresholds.
E.2.4.4 Sparse failure modes
Sparse failure modes include:
phantom high-frequency artifacts from undersampling,
instability from support oscillation (support flips between iterations),
loss of phase coherence under aggressive pruning,
nondeterministic key ordering in hash-based formats.
All are required to be detectable by property tests and to emit certificates identifying the failure class.
E.3 Hierarchical structures (E.3.1–E.3.4)
E.3.1 Octrees / wavelet trees
E.3.1.1 Octree node schema
An octree node represents an axis-aligned cube region. A node stores:[\mathrm{Node}=(\mathrm{key},\ell,\mathrm{bbox},\mathrm{payload},\mathrm{childrenMask},\mathrm{meta}),]where:
(\mathrm{key}) is a deterministic Morton code,
(\ell) is level (root (\ell=0)),
(\mathrm{bbox}) is the spatial region,
(\mathrm{payload}) is either:
constant/linear fit parameters,
dense block (B^3\times P),
sparse block representation,
(\mathrm{childrenMask}\in{0,1}^8) indicates present children.
E.3.1.2 Wavelet tree schema
A wavelet tree stores coefficients at nodes aligned with multiresolution partitions. Node payload is coefficient vector(s), plus:
threshold policy,
parent/child relationships,
local error contribution estimates.
E.3.1.3 Deterministic traversal and ordering
Traversal order must be fixed:
nodes sorted by (level, Morton key),
children visited in fixed child index order ({0,\dots,7}),
any pruning decisions recorded deterministically with thresholds committed.
E.3.1.4 Hierarchical metadata and local invariants
Each node may store local summaries:
local energy,
local constraint residual proxy,
local invariant contributions,
local conditioning proxies.
These local values enable:
adaptive refinement,
corridor checks at multiple scales,
certificate compression (aggregate local certificates into global).
E.3.2 Multigrid ladders
E.3.2.1 Level definition
A multigrid ladder is a finite sequence of levels (\ell=0,\dots,L) with:
grid size (N_\ell) (typically (N_{\ell+1}=2N_\ell)),
state space (\mathfrak{H}{N\ell}),
operator (A_\ell) (constraint, normal equations, or PDE operator),
restriction (S_\ell:\mathfrak{H}{N{\ell+1}}\to\mathfrak{H}{N\ell}),
prolongation (R_\ell:\mathfrak{H}{N\ell}\to\mathfrak{H}{N{\ell+1}}).
All maps must have deterministic implementations and stable normalization.
E.3.2.2 Data structure for operators
Each level stores operators in the most efficient representation:
stencils for local PDE-like (A_\ell),
FFT diagonals for convolutional (A_\ell),
sparse matrices for irregular constraints.
Operator representation IDs must be stored per level; mixed representations are permitted but must be explicit.
E.3.2.3 Smoothers and residual buffers
Each level stores:
current iterate (x_\ell),
residual (r_\ell=b_\ell-A_\ell x_\ell),
temporary buffers for smoothing steps,
optional preconditioner metadata.
Buffers must be allocated deterministically and sized from ((N_\ell,P)).
E.3.2.4 Multigrid certificates
Multigrid runs must emit:
per-level residual norms,
per-level invariant drift proxies (when defined),
iteration counts and stopping reasons,
defect measures for restriction/prolongation consistency.
These form the multilevel evidence that the hierarchy is coherent.
E.3.3 Coarse/fine coupling
E.3.3.1 Restriction and prolongation operators
Restriction (S) and prolongation (R) must satisfy a consistency identity on the intended model class:[S(R(x)) = x \quad\text{(exact on coarse space)},]or[|S(R(x))-x|\le \varepsilon_{\mathrm{SR}}]with certified (\varepsilon_{\mathrm{SR}}). These defects must be tracked as part of corridor budgets.
E.3.3.2 Residual correction and defect tracking
A standard correction is:[x_{\ell+1}\leftarrow x_{\ell+1} + R_\ell(e_\ell),\qquad A_\ell e_\ell = r_\ell.]Defects arise from approximate solves and from mismatch between (A_{\ell+1}) and lifted (A_\ell). Defect measures must be recorded and bounded to certify convergence.
E.3.3.3 Boundary and interface coupling
Hierarchies interacting with chunking or octrees must define interface coupling:
halo values,
flux matching,
stitching operators.
Interface coupling must be deterministic and must include a measurable mismatch metric used by corridor enforcement.
E.3.3.4 Coupling failure modes
Coupling failure modes include:
coarse level missing modes needed for fine stability,
prolongation injecting artifacts,
restriction losing critical phase structure,
oscillatory refinement decisions.
Failures must be detectable via defect logs and invariant drifts.
E.3.4 Deterministic indexing
E.3.4.1 Morton codes and canonical keying
For hierarchical nodes, use Morton (Z-order) or lexicographic keys. The key function must be declared:[\mathrm{Morton}:[N]^3\to \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0},]with explicit bit-interleaving order and endianness.
E.3.4.2 Canonical serialization of hierarchies
Serialize nodes as a sorted list by (level, key). Each node’s payload is serialized using its own schema (dense block, sparse block). Absent children are implicit by childrenMask; no implicit map iteration is permitted.
E.3.4.3 Deterministic parallelism policy
Hierarchies often invite parallel traversal. Verification mode must either:
disable parallel traversal,or
record traversal schedule and reduction trees into the certificate log.
E.3.4.4 Hashable hierarchy identity
Hierarchy ID is:[\mathrm{ID} = \mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(\mathrm{Tree})),]where (\mathrm{Tree}) includes node ordering, payload schemas, thresholds, and basis IDs. Any non-canonical serialization invalidates identity stability and therefore invalidates certificates.
E.4 Streaming structures (E.4.1–E.4.4)
E.4.1 Chunking strategies
E.4.1.1 Chunk schema
A streaming representation defines a chunked field:[\mathrm{StreamField} = (\mathrm{chunkShape},\mathrm{halo},\mathrm{schedule},\mathrm{store},\mathrm{meta}),]where:
(\mathrm{chunkShape}=(B_x,B_y,B_z,B_p)),
(\mathrm{halo}=(h_x,h_y,h_z)) boundary width,
(\mathrm{schedule}) defines deterministic chunk traversal order,
(\mathrm{store}) is an out-of-core backing store with deterministic read/write API.
Chunks are identified by a canonical chunk ID:[\mathrm{cid}(i,j,k) \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0},]derived deterministically from chunk coordinates and layout schema.
E.4.1.2 Slabs, bricks, and frequency chunks
Permitted chunk families:
Slabs: stream by one axis (e.g., contiguous z-slices),
Bricks: cubic or rectangular tiles (best for locality),
Frequency chunks: chunk in spectral domain (requires deterministic FFT staging).
Each chunk family must declare:
halo rules,
overlap-add vs halo exchange policy,
boundary condition enforcement.
E.4.1.3 Deterministic scheduling
The schedule must be a deterministic total order over chunk IDs. Default is lexicographic in chunk coordinates. Any adaptive schedule must be generated by deterministic rules and committed to the log.
E.4.1.4 Chunk-level typing
Each chunk carries:
local layout descriptor,
local basis ID (if transformed),
local invariant estimates,
local constraint residual proxies.
Chunk metadata must not be permitted to drift silently; it must be recalculated or invalidated deterministically.
E.4.2 Online reconstruction support
E.4.2.1 Streaming reconstruction state
Online reconstruction maintains:
a rolling window of chunks in memory,
boundary packets (summaries of interface conditions),
solver state (iterative method iterates or sufficient statistics),
checkpoints at declared intervals.
E.4.2.2 Overlap-add and halo exchange
For convolutional and FFT-like operations, overlap-add is used:
read chunk with overlap,
apply local transform,
add into output buffer with deterministic accumulation order.
For stencil-like operations, halo exchange is used:
read neighbor halos deterministically,
apply local update,
write updated chunk and updated halo packets.
E.4.2.3 Incremental solvers
Streaming inverse problems often use incremental gradient/proximal updates:[x \leftarrow \Pi\bigl(x - \eta,O_{\mathrm{chunk}}^*(O_{\mathrm{chunk}}x-y_{\mathrm{chunk}})\bigr),]applied chunk-wise. Chunk-wise updates must include:
deterministic step-size schedule,
deterministic chunk order,
explicit projection/repair after each chunk or after each pass.
E.4.2.4 Online certificates
Online runs must emit:
per-chunk residuals,
per-chunk invariant summaries,
boundary mismatch metrics,
checkpoint hashes and seeds.
Certificates must be composable: the full run certificate is the hash of the concatenated, hash-chained chunk certificates.
E.4.3 Online invariant monitoring
E.4.3.1 Streaming estimators
Streaming estimators compute invariants without storing full state. Examples:
running energy (\sum |X_{\mathrm{chunk}}|^2),
band energies via streaming FFT blocks,
entropy estimates via streaming histograms,
drift detectors via EWMA.
Each estimator must specify:
update equations,
numeric stability policy (compensated summation where needed),
bias/variance envelope (if statistical).
E.4.3.2 Early abort triggers
Abort triggers must be deterministic predicates on monitor outputs:
monotone increase in residual beyond threshold,
invariant drift beyond corridor,
boundary mismatch spikes beyond safety margin,
numeric instability flags (NaN/Inf, overflow, ill-conditioning warnings).
E.4.3.3 Repair triggers
Repair actions include:
local re-solve on problematic chunks,
increased overlap/halo,
increased precision mode for critical steps,
projection to constraint manifold,
corridor tightening or fallback to coarser model.
All repair triggers must be logged and their effects measured.
E.4.3.4 Logging formats
Logs are structured records containing:
chunk IDs,
operation IDs and versions,
monitor outputs and thresholds,
decisions (continue/repair/abort),
hashes of chunk inputs/outputs.
Logs must be canonicalized and hash-chained.
E.4.4 Checkpointing
E.4.4.1 Checkpoint schema
A checkpoint is a content-addressed bundle:[\mathrm{Chkpt} = (\mathrm{stateSlice},\mathrm{solverState},\mathrm{boundaryPackets},\mathrm{monitors},\mathrm{meta}),]where (\mathrm{stateSlice}) is the set of chunks currently resident (or a compact sketch), (\mathrm{solverState}) is the exact state of the iterative method, and (\mathrm{meta}) includes seeds and environment manifest IDs.
E.4.4.2 Hash chaining and replay
Checkpoints are chained:[h_{t} = \mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(\mathrm{Chkpt}t, h{t-1})),]so each checkpoint commits to all prior checkpoints. Replay is valid only if:
the environment manifest matches,
the chunk schedule and seeds match,
hashes match at each checkpoint boundary.
E.4.4.3 Retention and pruning policy
Retention policies must be deterministic:
periodic retention (every (K) chunks),
event-based retention (on drift spikes),
fixed window retention (keep last (W) checkpoints),
archival of failure checkpoints (retain those that triggered abort).
Pruning must never remove the ability to reproduce a published claim; published claims must reference retained checkpoint roots.
E.4.4.4 Failure recovery
Recovery is defined as:
load last checkpoint,
replay from that point deterministically,
optionally modify only parameters explicitly allowed by the corridor governance policy (e.g., increase precision, change overlap),
reissue certificates with explicit versioning and policy annotation.
Recovery must not silently change algorithm semantics; any changes must be recorded as an admissible repair transform with its own certificate.
Appendix E — Data Structures Reference (4×4)
Appendix F — Reference Datasets and Gold States (4×4)
This appendix defines the canonical dataset families used for (i) correctness proofs by example, (ii) regression stability, (iii) benchmarking, and (iv) falsifiable stress tests. A “dataset” here is not merely a list of arrays; it is a typed, deterministic generator producing tuples ((H_{\star}, O, M, \eta, y, \mathsf{meta})) together with content-addressed identifiers and certificates.
F.0 Dataset object model and determinism contract
F.0.1 Canonical dataset sample tuple
A dataset sample at resolution ((N,P)) is the tuple[\mathbf{s};=;\big(H_{\star},;O,;M,;\eta,;y,;\mathsf{meta}\big)]with:
(H_{\star}\in H^{4}_{(N,P)}) the ground-truth feasible state (discrete feasible model at ((N,P))).
(O:\mathfrak{H}_{(N,P)}\to \mathbb{K}^{m}) the observation operator (linear or nonlinear; typed).
(M) the mask operator (possibly absorbed into (O); recorded explicitly for reproducibility).
(\eta\in \mathbb{K}^{m}) the noise realization.
(y = O(M(H_{\star}))+\eta\in \mathbb{K}^{m}) the observed measurement.
(\mathsf{meta}) the metadata record including: generator IDs, parameters, seeds, schema versions, basis IDs, constraint IDs, invariant suite IDs, corridor budget IDs, numeric policy IDs.
A dataset family is a deterministic generator[\mathsf{Gen}:\ (\mathsf{root_seed},\ \mathsf{params}) \mapsto {\mathbf{s}i}{i=1}^{S}]with explicit sample indexing and deterministic ordering.
F.0.2 Deterministic seed derivation
All randomness is derived from a root seed (\mathsf{root_seed}) and a label string (\ell) using a fixed PRF:[\mathsf{seed}(\ell) ;=; \mathrm{PRF}(\mathsf{root_seed},\ell)\in {0,1}^{64}.]Label strings are canonicalized (UTF-8, no locale dependence). Every generator call records the exact (\ell) used for seed derivation.
F.0.3 Canonical IDs and content addressing
Each sample (\mathbf{s}) has:
SampleID: (\mathrm{SID}=\mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(\mathsf{meta})))
StateID: (\mathrm{HID}=\mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(\mathrm{Can}(H_{\star}))))
ObsID: (\mathrm{OID}=\mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(O,M)))
NoiseID: (\mathrm{NID}=\mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(\eta)))
DataID: (\mathrm{DID}=\mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(y)))
Serialization (\mathrm{Ser}) is canonical per Appendix A and Appendix D. Hash function and schema versions are pinned.
F.0.4 Feasibility and certificate payload
Each sample includes a feasibility certificate:[\mathsf{Cert}{\mathrm{feas}}(H{\star})=\big(|C_{N}(H_{\star})|,\ \mathrm{Inv}(H_{\star}),\ \mathrm{CanOK},\ \mathrm{HID}\big),]with explicit tolerances. If (H_{\star}) is generated by projection onto (\ker(C_N)), the projection logs and residual bounds are included.
F.1 Synthetic hologram families (F.1.1–F.1.4)
Synthetic families are deterministic generators producing feasible states with known structure, parameter metadata, and optional closed-form invariants. Every synthetic family must provide: (i) a parametric description, (ii) a discretization map, (iii) a feasibility enforcement path, (iv) invariant and canonicalization witnesses.
F.1.1 Smooth analytic families
F.1.1.1 Band-limited Fourier sums
Definition (Fourier-sum family).Fix integer bandlimit (K) with (K\ll N). Define coefficients (a_{k,p}\in\mathbb{C}) for (k\in[-K,K]^3\subset \mathbb{Z}^3), (p\in[P]), and define on the discrete grid:[H_{\mathrm{raw}}[x,y,z,p];=;\sum_{k\in[-K,K]^3} a_{k,p},e^{2\pi i (k_x x + k_y y + k_z z)/N}.]Coefficients are generated deterministically by a seed (\mathsf{seed}(\ell)), then normalized to satisfy a chosen energy target (E_0).
Feasibility.If the constraint (C_N) is spectral (band restriction, divergence-free, etc.), pick (a_{k,p}) to satisfy (C_N(H_{\mathrm{raw}})=0) by construction; otherwise project: (H_{\star}=\Pi_{H^4_N}(H_{\mathrm{raw}})).
Primary uses.
FFT-kernel correctness
spectral invariants correctness
truncation/convergence benchmarks (vary (K) and (N))
F.1.1.2 Gaussian wavepackets and Gabor atoms
Definition (Gabor family).Let centers (\mu_j\in[0,N)^3), widths (\sigma_j>0), carrier frequencies (\omega_j\in\mathbb{R}^3), and channel weights (b_{j,p}\in\mathbb{C}). Define:[H_{\mathrm{raw}}[x,y,z,p]=\sum_{j=1}^{J} b_{j,p},\exp!\left(-\frac{|[x,y,z]-\mu_j|^2}{2\sigma_j^2}\right),\exp!\left(i,\omega_j\cdot [x,y,z]\right).]All parameters ((\mu_j,\sigma_j,\omega_j,b_{j,p})) are generated deterministically from labeled seeds.
Feasibility.If (C_N) encodes smoothness or PDE-like constraints, use manufactured-solution enforcement (F.1.1.4) or project onto (\ker(C_N)). If constraints are purely algebraic (energy, symmetry), enforce by parameter tying and normalization.
Primary uses.
locality-sensitive reconstructions
wavelet/sparse-vs-dense comparisons
stability under small perturbations
F.1.1.3 Separable products across ((x,p))
Definition (separable family).Generate (f[x,y,z]\in\mathbb{C}) and (g[p]\in\mathbb{C}) and define:[H_{\mathrm{raw}}[x,y,z,p]=f[x,y,z]\cdot g[p].]More generally, rank-(R) CP form:[H_{\mathrm{raw}}[x,y,z,p]=\sum_{r=1}^{R} f_r[x,y,z]\cdot g_r[p],]with deterministic generation and energy normalization.
Feasibility.Constraints that factor along (p) (e.g., per-channel constraints) can be satisfied by choosing (f_r,g_r) from constrained families; otherwise project.
Primary uses.
low-rank inference baselines
cross-dimensional coupling tests
channel-mixing transform tests
F.1.1.4 Manufactured solutions for constraint operators
Definition (manufactured family).Given a constraint operator (C_N) derived from a continuous operator (C), select an analytic (H_{\mathrm{raw}}) and define a forcing term (b) so that:[C(H_{\mathrm{raw}})=b.]Then define a dataset that tests whether the discrete operator satisfies:[C_N(\Pi_N H_{\mathrm{raw}})\approx \Pi_N b]with convergence rate in (N).
Feasibility option.If the goal is to produce feasible states, choose (b=0) and manufacture (H_{\mathrm{raw}}\in\ker(C)) when possible; otherwise, set (H_{\star}=\Pi_{H^4_N}(H_{\mathrm{raw}})) and record the projection residual trace as part of the gold artifact.
Primary uses.
discrete-to-continuous consistency tests
stability implies convergence test harness
constraint kernel regression baselines
F.1.2 Sparse and structured families
F.1.2.1 Sparse scatterer (spike) family
Definition (spike family).Select (S) voxel locations ({v_j}{j=1}^{S}\subset[N]^3) and channel vectors (c_j\in\mathbb{C}^{P}). Define:[H{\mathrm{raw}}[x,y,z,p] =\sum_{j=1}^{S} \mathbf{1}[(x,y,z)=v_j]; (c_j)_p.]Locations and coefficients are seeded and canonically sorted by linear voxel index.
Feasibility.If constraints require smoothness, the spike family is intentionally infeasible; it becomes a stress family for repair/healing (Chapter 13) and for robustness/no-go certificates. If feasibility is required, apply a controlled smoothing operator (S_\epsilon) and then project:[H_{\star}=\Pi_{H^4_N}(S_\epsilon(H_{\mathrm{raw}})).]
Primary uses.
compressed sensing masks
sparse reconstruction stress tests
adversarial aliasing detection
F.1.2.2 Piecewise-smooth / edge (BV) family
Definition (BV family).Define regions (\Omega_a\subset[N]^3) (e.g., half-spaces, spheres, polyhedra) and assign constant channel vectors (u_a\in\mathbb{C}^{P}). Define:[H_{\mathrm{raw}}[x,y,z,p]=\sum_{a=1}^{A} \mathbf{1}[(x,y,z)\in\Omega_a],(u_a)_p.]Edges are intentionally present; this family is wavelet-sparse and stresses boundary artifacts.
Feasibility.If constraints encode conservation or divergence-free structure, enforce by constructing (\Omega_a) and (u_a) in compatible pairs (e.g., vector fields built from potentials); otherwise project and record residuals.
Primary uses.
wavelet truncation envelopes
TV regularization baselines
chunk boundary artifact tests
F.1.2.3 Low-rank tensor families (CP / Tucker)
Definition (low-rank family).For CP rank (R), generate factor matrices:[A\in\mathbb{C}^{N^3\times R},\qquad B\in\mathbb{C}^{P\times R},]and set (H_{\mathrm{raw}}=\mathrm{reshape}(A B^\top)) into (N\times N\times N\times P). Tucker variants define a small core tensor and mode factors. All factors are deterministic under seed and normalized to fixed spectral energy in each mode.
Feasibility.If constraints are linear, project low-rank state by solving for factors under constraints where possible; otherwise project in ambient space and optionally re-factor with bounded error.
Primary uses.
operator-learning baselines
transform inversion tests
memory/time Pareto baselines
F.1.2.4 Wavelet-tree sparse families
Definition (wavelet sparse family).Choose a wavelet basis (\mathcal{W}) and generate coefficients (c_{\ell,i,p}) with a tree sparsity pattern:
parent coefficients nonzero imply children possibly nonzero;
coefficients decay by scale.
Construct:[H_{\mathrm{raw}}=\mathcal{W}^{-1}(c),]with deterministic coefficient tree generation and a certified drop-energy envelope.
Feasibility.Wavelet-sparse families are compatible with multiresolution invariants; feasibility projection is applied after inverse transform if required.
Primary uses.
multi-scale invariant computation
hierarchical data structures benchmarks
wavelet-based reconstruction correctness
F.1.3 Periodic, resonant, and cycle-structured families
F.1.3.1 Multi-frequency phase-locked families
Definition (phase-locked family).For each channel (p), generate a set of frequencies ({\omega_{p,j}}) with rational relations and define:[H_{\mathrm{raw}}[x,y,z,p]=\sum_{j=1}^{J} a_{p,j},e^{i(\omega_{p,j}\cdot [x,y,z]+\phi_{p,j})}.]Construct frequencies so that within a specified tolerance the resonance relations hold (exact in discrete lattice). This family is used to validate resonance detectors and commutator curvature diagnostics where applicable.
F.1.3.2 Log-periodic / fractal modulation families
Definition (log-periodic family).Introduce a modulation in a parameter coordinate (often (p) interpreted as time or scale index):[H_{\mathrm{raw}}[x,y,z,p]=F[x,y,z]\cdot \exp!\big(i,\alpha \log(p+p_0)\big)]or discrete self-similar modulations across (p). This family stresses fractal-time invariants and multi-scale coupling diagnostics.
F.1.3.3 Engine-cycle (Q) closure families
Definition (cycle family).Given a declared engine (Q=(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3,Q_4)), generate an initial state (H_0) and enforce approximate closure:[Q_4(Q_3(Q_2(Q_1(H_0)))) ;\approx; H_0]with controlled defect (\epsilon\Delta). Dataset instances include:
(H_0),
stage outputs (H_i),
closure residual (|H_4-H_0|),
drift in (\mathrm{Inv}) across stages.
This family is used for deterministic regression of engine implementations and for stability diagnostics under iteration.
F.1.3.4 Ambiguity witness families (non-identifiability stress)
Definition (ambiguity family).Construct pairs (H^{(1)}{\star}\neq H^{(2)}{\star}) in the feasible set such that:[O(M(H^{(1)}{\star})) ;=; O(M(H^{(2)}{\star}))]exactly (or within declared tolerance), while the canonicalized state distance remains bounded away from zero:[|\mathrm{Can}(H^{(1)}{\star})-\mathrm{Can}(H^{(2)}{\star})|\ge \gamma>0.]These are no-go witnesses used to validate certified impossibility outputs. Their construction must be deterministic and include a full witness package.
F.1.4 Gold-state packaging, invariants, and canonicalization
F.1.4.1 Parameter manifests
Every synthetic instance must include a parameter manifest:[\mathsf{Param}=\big(\mathsf{familyID},;N,;P,;\theta_{\mathrm{geom}},;\theta_{\mathrm{spec}},;\theta_{\mathrm{corr}},;\mathsf{seedLabels}\big),]with:
geometry parameters (centers, widths, regions),
spectral parameters (bandlimits, frequencies),
corridor parameters (target invariants, allowable drift),
explicit seed labels used for generation.
F.1.4.2 Feasibility enforcement trace
If feasibility is enforced by projection, the gold state includes the trace:
projection method ID,
solver tolerances,
constraint residual history,
final residual (|C_N(H_{\star})|),
optional dual witness for constraint satisfaction (when available).
This trace is hash-addressed and referenced by (\mathrm{HID}).
F.1.4.3 Invariant vector precomputation
Gold states must include:
(\mathrm{Inv}(H_{\star})) evaluated under fixed estimator suite ID,
multi-scale components if the suite is multi-resolution,
numerical policy (precision, FFT normalization, thresholding rules),
tolerance ledger for invariant estimation.
Invariant values are not part of the state serialization unless explicitly declared, but are part of the gold-state metadata and certificates.
F.1.4.4 Canonicalization and gold-state hashing
Gold states must be canonicalized:[H_{\star}\leftarrow \mathrm{Can}(H_{\star}),]then serialized deterministically and hashed:[\mathrm{HID}=\mathrm{Hash}(\mathrm{Ser}(H_{\star})).]A gold state is admissible only if:
canonicalization is idempotent on it,
its hash is stable under reruns with the same manifest,
feasibility residual and invariant suite values pass the declared corridor checks.
F.2 Mask families (F.2.1–F.2.4)
Mask families define sampling and occlusion patterns. Every mask family must support deterministic generation, content-addressed IDs, and coverage/identifiability diagnostics.
F.2.1 Random masks
F.2.1.1 IID Bernoulli masks
Define a Bernoulli mask (M\in{0,1}^{m}) (or as a selection set (S\subset[m])) with retain probability (p\in(0,1]). Deterministic generator:
seed label (\ell=) mask/bernoulli/{p}/{sample_id}
PRF seed → pseudorandom bits → mask
optional stratification constraints (below)
Required metadata:
(p),
mask dimension (m),
RNG algorithm ID,
seed label and derived seed.
F.2.1.2 Stratified masks by scale/band
Partition indices into strata (S_1,\dots,S_L) (e.g., frequency bands, wavelet levels, spatial shells). Assign per-stratum retain probabilities (p_\ell). Generate Bernoulli masks within each stratum with deterministic seeds per stratum:[\ell=\texttt{mask/stratified/}\ell\texttt{/sample_id}.]Stratified masks are required for stable calibration of multi-scale reconstruction and for fair comparisons across basis choices.
F.2.1.3 Block masks
Choose block size (B) and generate a block-level Bernoulli mask; all indices inside a block share the same keep/drop decision. Block masks stress spatial occlusions and support chunked/streaming evaluations.
F.2.1.4 Paired masks for cross-validation
Define paired masks ((M_{\mathrm{train}},M_{\mathrm{val}})) with controlled overlap (\rho). A canonical method:
generate a global random ordering of indices,
assign the first fraction (p_{\mathrm{train}}) to train,
the next fraction to val,
ensure disjointness unless overlap is explicitly required.
Paired masks are mandatory for overfitting detection in reconstruction and self-healing loops.
F.2.2 Structured occlusions and boundary masks
F.2.2.1 Contiguous spatial occlusions
Define an occlusion region (R\subset[N]^3) (box, sphere, polytope). Mask drops all voxels in (R). Parameters ((R)) are deterministic from seed labels and stored explicitly (center, radius, bounds).
F.2.2.2 Boundary-only sampling (holographic boundary masks)
Define boundary shell of thickness (t):[\partial_t\Omega = {(x,y,z): \min{x,y,z,N-1-x,N-1-y,N-1-z}<t}.]Boundary mask keeps only indices in (\partial_t\Omega), optionally with additional thinning by Bernoulli sampling. This family is used to test boundary reconstruction conditions.
F.2.2.3 Sector/radial masks aligned to sextine partitions
Define angular sector masks in a 2D projection (or in a chosen plane) aligned with (60^\circ) sectors. This provides deterministic structured missingness aligned with sextine backbone tests and stresses rotation/phase invariance.
F.2.2.4 Multi-region masks
Define a finite set of regions (A_1,\dots,A_J) and a mask family that selects combinations of them. Used to test multi-region reconstruction and redundancy-driven correction.
F.2.3 Adversarial masks
F.2.3.1 Worst-case subframe degradation masks
If reconstruction uses a frame ({\varphi_k}), define masks designed to minimize the lower frame bound (A_\Lambda) of the retained subset (\Lambda). Practical generation:
greedy removal maximizing condition number of restricted operator,
deterministic tie-breaking by canonical ordering.
F.2.3.2 Aliasing-inducing masks
Masks that produce spectral aliasing by sampling at periodic sublattices. Used to validate that the system emits ambiguity certificates rather than false-confidence reconstructions.
F.2.3.3 Streaming/chunk boundary masks
Masks aligned with chunk boundaries to maximize boundary artifacts. Used to validate overlap/halo policies and seam repair.
F.2.3.4 Identifiability witness masks
Masks paired with ambiguity witness states (F.1.3.4) to produce exact collisions (O(M(H_1))=O(M(H_2))). These masks are stored as gold no-go instances.
F.2.4 Mask IDs, determinism, and certificates
F.2.4.1 Mask schema
A mask artifact stores:
mask family ID,
dimensions and index space description,
parameters (retain rates, region descriptors),
seed lineage labels and derived seeds,
canonical serialization and hash (\mathrm{MID}).
F.2.4.2 Deterministic generation algorithm
Mask generation must be implemented as:
normalize parameters to canonical form,
derive seeds by PRF,
generate mask in canonical index order,
canonicalize mask representation (sorted indices or canonical bitmap),
compute (\mathrm{MID}).
F.2.4.3 Coverage and identifiability diagnostics
Each mask must include:
retain fraction,
per-stratum retain counts (if stratified),
connectivity measures for contiguous masks (optional),
condition proxies (e.g., smallest singular value estimates of restricted operator) where applicable.
F.2.4.4 Replay manifest
A replay manifest includes:
(\mathrm{MID}),
generator schema ID and version,
parameter manifest,
seed labels,
deterministic reproduction instructions.
F.3 Noise families (F.3.1–F.3.4)
Noise families generate (\eta) and/or corrupted observations. Noise is part of the dataset, not an external nuisance.
F.3.1 Additive noise
F.3.1.1 White Gaussian noise
[\eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2 I_m).]Noise is generated by deterministic PRF-derived seeds and a fixed RNG algorithm ID. Store (\sigma) and the target SNR mapping (F.3.4).
F.3.1.2 Colored Gaussian noise
Define a filter (K) (convolution kernel or spectral multiplier) and generate:[\eta = K \xi,\quad \xi\sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2 I).](K) is stored as a typed operator artifact ID. Colored noise stresses spectral stability and estimator robustness.
F.3.1.3 Heavy-tailed noise
Generate Laplace or Student-t noise with parameters ((b,\nu)) to stress robustness:[\eta_i \sim \mathrm{Laplace}(0,b)\quad \text{or}\quad\eta_i \sim t_\nu.]Deterministic RNG and parameter manifests are mandatory.
F.3.1.4 Bounded adversarial noise
Generate (\eta) constrained by a norm bound:[|\eta|2 \le \epsilon\quad \text{or}\quad |\eta|\infty \le \epsilon.]A canonical deterministic construction:
generate (\xi) from a fixed seed,
set (\eta=\epsilon \xi/|\xi|) (for (\ell_2)) or (\eta=\epsilon,\mathrm{sign}(\xi)) (for (\ell_\infty)),with explicit tie-breaking when (\xi=0).
F.3.2 Multiplicative and phase noise
F.3.2.1 Multiplicative amplitude noise
Define multiplicative noise (a) and apply:[y = (1+a)\odot O(M(H_{\star})) + \eta.]Typical (a) distributions: lognormal, Gaussian small amplitude. Record both (a) and (\eta) as artifacts.
F.3.2.2 Poisson noise (counting regime)
For nonnegative intensity observations (\lambda = \max(0, O(M(H_{\star})))), generate:[y_i \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_i).]Record whether intensity clipping was applied and the deterministic rounding rule for (\lambda).
F.3.2.3 Phase noise
When observations are complex, apply:[y = e^{i\theta}\odot O(M(H_{\star})) + \eta,]where (\theta) is a deterministic random field with specified smoothness (e.g., low-pass filtered Gaussian). This family tests phase unwrapping, gauge handling, and resonance stability.
F.3.2.4 Phase wrapping stress
Generate phase fields that cross branch cuts frequently. This family is used to validate:
canonical phase fixing,
gauge-invariant distances,
impossibility detection when phase information is insufficient.
F.3.3 Corruption and outliers
